name: ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS

topics.title: Introduction to Robotics and Industrial Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Robotics and Industrial Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Robotics — The KTU 2024 Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Robot** is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. *(Source: Robot Institute of America, 1979 — adopted verbatim in KTU 2024 syllabus for OERUT724)*.

A **Robotic System** is an integrated assembly of mechanical linkages, actuators, sensors, controllers, and software that perceives its environment, makes decisions, and executes physical tasks—either autonomously or under supervisory control.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a robot like a highly disciplined factory apprentice:**
> - A human apprentice **sees** the workpiece (sensors = eyes).
> - The apprentice's **brain** decides the next move (controller + software).
> - The apprentice's **muscles and joints** execute the motion (actuators + linkages).
> - The apprentice can be **retrained** for a new task simply by learning a new sequence of steps (reprogrammability — the defining feature of an industrial robot).
> 
> If you replace the apprentice's memory and reflexes with a microcontroller, you essentially have an industrial robot.

The single most important word in the definition is **reprogrammable**. A washing machine is automatic but NOT a robot because its sequence is hard-wired. A robot can be reprogrammed to perform a *different* task on Monday morning without any hardware change.

## 1.2 Industrial Automation — The Broader Umbrella

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Automation** is the use of control systems (computers, PLCs, robots, sensors) and information technologies to handle different processes and machinery in an industry to replace human intervention, while simultaneously increasing throughput, quality, and safety.

Automation is the **parent discipline**; robotics is one of its most advanced **children**.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Robotics (Asimov's Laws)

Issac Asimov, in his 1942 short story *Runaround*, codified the ethical foundation of robotics:

1. **First Law:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
2. **Second Law:** A robot must obey orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
3. **Third Law:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.

> [!NOTE]
> Later, Asimov added the **Zeroth Law**: *A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm.* This is foundational in modern AI-Ethics frameworks studied in KTU's later semesters.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeatability** | $R$ | $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm | Most-quoted robot specification |
| **Accuracy** | $A$ | $\pm 0.5$ mm to $\pm 5$ mm | Often confused with repeatability |
| **Resolution** | $r$ | $0.001$ mm (encoder) | Smallest controllable increment |
| **Payload** | $P$ | $3$ kg to $1000$ kg | Design selection criterion |
| **Reach** | $L$ | $0.5$ m to $4$ m | Work envelope boundary |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners LOVE asking the difference between **Repeatability** and **Accuracy**. Repeatability is how closely the robot returns to a *previously taught* point. Accuracy is how close the robot gets to an *absolute commanded* point from the world coordinate frame.

## 1.5 The Three Generations of Robots (Historical Evolution)

- **1st Generation (Pick-and-Place):** Simple mechanical arms with no sensors, no feedback. Used for loading/unloading.
- **2nd Generation (Teach-Playback):** Sensor-equipped, can record and replay a taught path. Most modern industrial robots fall here.
- **3rd Generation (Intelligent/Cognitive):** AI-enabled, vision-guided, capable of decision-making. Collaborative Robots (Cobots) are early 3rd-gen examples.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Industrial Robot — A Structured Breakdown

Every industrial robot, regardless of brand (FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa), consists of **four fundamental subsystems**:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Subsystem (The Body)
- **Manipulator / Arm:** Series of rigid links connected by **revolute (R)** or **prismatic (P)** joints.
- **End Effector:** The "hand" — gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, suction cup.
- **Drive System:** Motors (servo/stepper), gears (harmonic, cycloidal), transmission (belt, chain).

### 2.1.2 Sensing Subsystem (The Nerves)
- **Internal Sensors:** Encoders, resolvers, tachometers, joint position sensors.
- **External Sensors:** Vision cameras, force/torque sensors, proximity sensors, tactile arrays.

### 2.1.3 Control Subsystem (The Brain)
- **Controller:** Microprocessor/PLC-based, runs the robot's operating system.
- **Servo Loops:** PID controllers for each joint, executed at 1–8 kHz.
- **Teach Pendant:** Handheld input device for manual programming.

### 2.1.4 Power Subsystem (The Fuel)
- **Electrical** (most common in modern industry): 3-phase 400V AC for KTU standard.
- **Hydraulic** (high payload >500 kg): Mining, forging, casting.
- **Pneumatic** (light duty, food industry): 1–10 bar compressed air.

## 2.2 Classification of Industrial Robots

The most exam-friendly classification is based on **coordinate system / work envelope**:

| Type | Configuration | DOF | Work Envelope Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian (PPP)** | 3 prismatic | 3 | Rectangular cuboid | Pick-and-place, CNC loading |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | Revolute base + 2 prismatic | 3 | Hollow cylinder | Assembly, machine tending |
| **Polar/Spherical (RRP)** | 2 revolute + 1 prismatic | 3 | Sphere sector | Welding, die casting |
| **SCARA** | 2 revolute + 1 prismatic (vertical) | 3 | Horizontal disc | PCB assembly |
| **Articulated (RRR)** | 3 revolute joints | 3 | Sphere | Welding, painting, palletizing |
| **Parallel (Delta)** | Multiple arms meeting at tool | 3 | Dome | High-speed packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** "SCARA" stands for **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** — a high-yield acronym question.

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Heart of Kinematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** is the **number of independent parameters** that define the configuration of a robot in 3D space.
> - To position and orient an end effector in 3D space, **6 DOF** are required (3 for position, 3 for orientation: roll, pitch, yaw).
> - Industrial robots typically have **4 to 7 DOF**.
> - **Redundancy** occurs when DOF > 6 (used to avoid obstacles and singular configurations).

### Krumar's Formula (KTU Favourite):
For a planar mechanism:
$$
n = 3(L-1) - 2J_1 - J_2
$$
where:
- $n$ = mobility (DOF of the mechanism)
- $L$ = number of links (including ground)
- $J_1$ = number of lower-pair (1 DOF) joints — revolute, prismatic
- $J_2$ = number of higher-pair (2 DOF) joints — cam, gear, ball bearing

For a 3D spatial mechanism, **Grübler's Equation** is used:
$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5f_1 - 4f_2 - 3f_3 - 2f_4 - f_5
$$
where $f_i$ = number of joints with $i$ DOF.

## 2.4 Work Envelope / Workspace

The **Workspace** is the set of all points the end effector can reach.

For a 2-link planar arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$:
$$
\text{Reach}_{\max} = L_1 + L_2
$$
$$
\text{Reach}_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert
$$
$$
\text{Workspace Area} = \pi(L_1+L_2)^2 - \pi \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert^2
$$

For a spherical (polar) robot with reach $r$, pitch angle $\beta$, and base rotation $\theta$:
$$
\text{Volume} = \frac{2\pi r^3}{3}
$$

## 2.5 Repeatability vs Accuracy — The Mathematical Truth

Let $(x_i, y_i)$ be the actual positions reached for $N$ attempts at the same commanded point $(x_c, y_c)$.

**Mean Position:**
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i
$$

**Accuracy** (distance from commanded to mean):
$$
A = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2}
$$

**Repeatability** (statistical spread of attempts, typically $\pm 3\sigma$):
$$
\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$
$$
R = 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} \quad \text{(bidirectional)}
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kutzbach (planar): $n = 3(L-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | $n, L, J$ | dimensionless | DOF analysis |
| 2 | Grübler (spatial): $M = 6(n-1) - \Sigma(6-i)f_i$ | $M, n, f_i$ | dimensionless | Spatial DOF |
| 3 | Max planar reach: $R_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$ | $L_1, L_2$ | m | Workspace |
| 4 | Min planar reach: $R_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | $L_1, L_2$ | m | Workspace |
| 5 | Spherical vol.: $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3$ | $r$ | m³ | Work envelope |
| 6 | Repeatability: $R = 3\sigma$ | $\sigma$ | mm | Spec sheet |
| 7 | Accuracy: $A = \vert \mathbf{P}_{mean} - \mathbf{P}_{cmd} \vert$ | $\mathbf{P}$ | mm | Spec sheet |
| 8 | Servo update: $f_{loop} = 1/\Delta t$ | $t$ | Hz | Controller design |
| 9 | Payload safety: $M_{actual} \le 0.8 \cdot M_{rated}$ | $M$ | kg | Safety margin |
| 10 | Cycle time optimization: $T_c = T_{motion} + T_{IO}$ | $T$ | s | Throughput |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** The DOF formula guides robot arm design (e.g., KUKA KR 1000 TITAN has **6 DOF** for heavy forging). The repeatability formula drives the choice between SCARA (for PCB $\pm 0.01$ mm) vs. Cartesian (for palletizing $\pm 1$ mm).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Workspace Area of a 2-Link Planar Manipulator

Consider a 2-link revolute arm in the $xy$-plane. Joint 1 is at the origin, link 1 has length $L_1$, link 2 has length $L_2$. The end effector position is:
$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$
$$
y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

Let $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ (the absolute angle of link 2). The reachable points are bounded by:

### Step 1 — Maximum Reach
Both links extend collinearly in the same direction.

$$
R_{\max} = L_1 + L_2
$$

The end effector traces a circle of radius $(L_1 + L_2)$ when $\theta_1$ varies $0$ to $2\pi$.

### Step 2 — Minimum Reach
The links fold back on each other (anti-parallel).

$$
R_{\min} = L_1 - L_2 \quad \text{(assuming } L_1 \ge L_2 \text{)}
$$

The end effector traces a circle of radius $(L_1 - L_2)$.

### Step 3 — Annular Workspace Area

The workspace is an **annulus** (ring) between these two circles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{workspace}} &= \pi R_{\max}^2 - \pi R_{\min}^2 \\
&= \pi \left[(L_1 + L_2)^2 - (L_1 - L_2)^2\right] \\
&= \pi \left[L_1^2 + 2L_1L_2 + L_2^2 - (L_1^2 - 2L_1L_2 + L_2^2)\right] \\
&= \pi \left[4L_1L_2\right] \\
&= 4\pi L_1 L_2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The squared terms cancel — a classic $(a+b)^2 - (a-b)^2 = 4ab$ identity. This shows that the area depends **only on the product** of the two link lengths, not on their individual magnitudes.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — DOF Calculation

**Problem:** A 5-bar planar mechanism is used as a feeder arm. Calculate its DOF using Kutzbach's criterion.

**Given:**
- Links (including ground) $L = 5$
- Revolute joints $J_1 = 5$ (all joints are 1-DOF)
- Higher-pair joints $J_2 = 0$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 3(L - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2 \\
n &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(5) - 0 \\
n &= 3(4) - 10 \\
n &= 12 - 10 \\
n &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 2 DOF means the mechanism can position the end effector anywhere in a 2D plane. To fully control 3D position **and** orientation, we need **6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation).

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Repeatability Calculation

**Problem:** A 6-axis articulated robot is commanded to move to point $(100.00, 200.00)$ mm. Over 50 trials, the mean reached position is $(99.85, 200.12)$ mm, with standard deviations $\sigma_x = 0.015$ mm and $\sigma_y = 0.020$ mm. Calculate accuracy and repeatability.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2} \\
A &= \sqrt{(99.85 - 100.00)^2 + (200.12 - 200.00)^2} \\
A &= \sqrt{(-0.15)^2 + (0.12)^2} \\
A &= \sqrt{0.0225 + 0.0144} \\
A &= \sqrt{0.0369} \\
A &\approx 0.192 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} \\
R &= 3\sqrt{(0.015)^2 + (0.020)^2} \\
R &= 3\sqrt{0.000225 + 0.0004} \\
R &= 3\sqrt{0.000625} \\
R &= 3 \times 0.025 \\
R &= 0.075 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The robot has accuracy $\approx 0.192$ mm and repeatability $0.075$ mm. The repeatability (0.075) is **better than the accuracy** (0.192), which is typical — robots are precise (repeatable) but not always accurate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Robot Selection Decision Tree

| Application | Required Repeatability | Recommended Robot Type | DOF | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCB component placement | $\le 0.01$ mm | SCARA | 4 | Vertical stiffness, high planar speed |
| Arc welding of car body | $\le 0.1$ mm | 6-axis Articulated | 6 | Orientation flexibility for seams |
| Pick-and-place from conveyor | $\le 1$ mm | Cartesian Gantry | 3 | Simple, large envelope, low cost |
| Injection mould loading | $\le 0.5$ mm | 6-axis Articulated | 6 | Reaches inside mould cavity |
| Food packaging (high speed) | $\le 1$ mm | Delta (Parallel) | 3 | 100+ picks/min, low inertia |
| Palletizing 50 kg sacks | $\le 5$ mm | 4-axis Articulated | 4 | High payload, vertical motion dominant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **DOF equals 6 minus DOF restrictions**. Welding needs 6 DOF because the torch must be normal to the seam at every point. Palletizing only needs 4 because orientation is irrelevant — the sack just needs to be dropped vertically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Industrial Robotic System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional information flow** between the four subsystems of an industrial robot — replacing what would otherwise be a complex physical schematic with a clear logical architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USER["User / Operator Layer"]
        direction TB
        TP["Teach Pendant"]
        HMI["HMI Panel"]
        OFFLINE["Offline PC CAD/CAM"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control Subsystem (Brain)"]
        direction TB
        CPU["Main CPU / Microcontroller"]
        SERVO["Servo Drive Loops PID"]
        PLC["PLC / Safety Logic"]
        OS["Robot Operating System ROS or Proprietary"]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["Sensing Subsystem (Nerves)"]
        direction TB
        ENC["Joint Encoders Resolvers"]
        VIS["Vision Camera 2D or 3D"]
        FT["Force / Torque Sensor"]
        PROX["Proximity and Limit Switches"]
    end

    subgraph MECH["Mechanical Subsystem (Body)"]
        direction TB
        MOT["Servo Motors AC / DC"]
        TRAN["Harmonic Drive Gearbox"]
        ARM["Rigid Linkages with R and P Joints"]
        EE["End Effector Gripper or Tool"]
    end

    subgraph POWER["Power Subsystem (Fuel)"]
        direction TB
        PS["Power Supply 3 Phase 400V AC"]
        HYD["Hydraulic Pump if high payload"]
        PNE["Pneumatic Compressor if light"]
    end

    USER -->|Job command| CTRL
    CTRL -->|Joint torque setpoints| MECH
    MECH -->|Encoder feedback position| SENSE
    SENSE -->|Sensor data stream| CTRL
    POWER -.->|Electrical energy| MECH
    POWER -.->|Electrical energy| CTRL
    PLC -->|ESTOP safety interlock| CTRL
    EE -->|Performs task on| WPI["Workpiece"]
    VIS -->|Object coordinates| CTRL
    FT -->|Contact force| CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows denote command/feedback signals. Dashed arrows represent energy/power flow. The control loop closes through the sensing subsystem — a classic **closed-loop feedback architecture** that KTU examiners expect students to recognize.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Robot Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> POWER_OFF
    POWER_OFF --> MANUAL: Power ON + E-Stop Released
    MANUAL --> AUTO: Mode Switch to Automatic
    AUTO --> TEACH: Operator Activates Teach Pendant
    TEACH --> MANUAL: Teach Complete
    MANUAL --> AUTO: Program Loaded
    AUTO --> ERROR: Fault Detected
    ERROR --> MANUAL: Fault Acknowledged and Reset
    AUTO --> ESTOP: Emergency Stop Pressed
    ESTOP --> POWER_OFF: System Safe
    POWER_OFF --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ESTOP** (Emergency Stop) state is a **hardware-wired** safety state, not a software state — it physically cuts motor power regardless of CPU instruction. This is mandated by **ISO 10218** and **ANSI/RIA R15.06** industrial robot safety standards referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 4.3 Mermaid Classification Tree: Industrial Robot Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["Industrial Robots by Coordinate System"]
    ROOT --> CART["Cartesian PPP - 3 DOF"]
    ROOT --> CYL["Cylindrical RPP - 3 DOF"]
    ROOT --> POL["Polar Spherical RRP - 3 DOF"]
    ROOT --> SCARA["SCARA RRP Vertical - 3 to 4 DOF"]
    ROOT --> ART["Articulated RRR - 6 DOF"]
    ROOT --> PAR["Parallel Delta - 3 to 4 DOF"]

    CART --> CARTUSE["Use: CNC Loading Pick and Place"]
    CYL --> CYLUSE["Use: Machine Tending Assembly"]
    POL --> POLUSE["Use: Die Casting Welding"]
    SCARA --> SCARAUSE["Use: PCB Assembly Fast Pick"]
    ART --> ARTUSE["Use: Welding Painting Palletizing"]
    PAR --> PARUSE["Use: High Speed Packaging Pharma"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exam:** "**C**artesian **C**ubic, **C**ylindrical **C**ylinder, **P**olar **P**artial **S**phere" — the workspace shape mirrors the joint configuration. Memorize this and the 3-mark definition question becomes free marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define an industrial robot. List and explain any four specifications used to characterize an industrial robot.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An industrial robot is a **reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator** designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks (Robot Institute of America, 1979). **(1 mark)**

Four key specifications:

1. **Payload** — The maximum mass the robot can lift at rated speed, including the end effector. Expressed in **kg**. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Reach / Work Envelope** — The 3D region the end effector can reach. Expressed in **mm or m**. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Repeatability** — The ability of the robot to return to a previously taught position. Modern industrial robots: $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — Number of independent motions. Most welding robots have 6 DOF. **(0.5 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Accuracy** with **Repeatability**. Memorize: *Repeatability = how close to itself*; *Accuracy = how close to the target*. KTU specifically tests this distinction.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics. Add a brief note on the Zeroth Law.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **First Law:** A robot may not injure a human being, or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. **(1 mark)**
2. **Second Law:** A robot must obey the orders given by human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law. **(1 mark)**
3. **Third Law:** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Zeroth Law (added later):** A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm. This law takes precedence over the others. **(0.5 mark)**

These laws, formulated by **Isaac Asimov** in his 1942 short story *Runaround*, are the ethical foundation of modern robotics and AI governance.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Choice Q1]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of industrial robots** based on coordinate system with neat sketches. Discuss the workspace and typical applications of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-link planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 500$ mm and $L_2 = 300$ mm. Calculate:
   (i) Maximum reach
   (ii) Minimum reach
   (iii) Workspace area
   (iv) Number of singular configurations per cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

Industrial robots are classified based on the **type of joints** (R = Revolute, P = Prismatic) and the resulting **work envelope shape**. The five primary types are:

| # | Robot Type | Joints | DOF | Work Envelope | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cartesian / Gantry** | P-P-P | 3 | Rectangular cuboid | Pick-and-place, CNC loading, 3D printing |
| 2 | **Cylindrical** | R-P-P | 3 | Hollow cylinder | Machine tending, assembly |
| 3 | **Polar / Spherical** | R-R-P | 3 | Spherical sector | Die casting, welding |
| 4 | **SCARA** | R-R-P (vertical) | 3–4 | Horizontal disc | PCB assembly, small parts |
| 5 | **Articulated** | R-R-R (+3 wrist) | 6 | Sphere | Welding, painting, palletizing |
| 6 | **Parallel / Delta** | Multiple arms | 3 | Dome | High-speed packaging |

For each type, a sketch showing the joint axes and a typical work envelope shape is expected. **(Full 7 marks requires labelled diagrams — minimum 3 types must be drawn with axes.)**

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $L_1 = 500$ mm, $L_2 = 300$ mm.

**(i) Maximum Reach:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= L_1 + L_2 \\
R_{\max} &= 500 + 300 \\
R_{\max} &= 800 \text{ mm} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Reach:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\min} &= L_1 - L_2 \quad \text{(since } L_1 > L_2\text{)} \\
R_{\min} &= 500 - 300 \\
R_{\min} &= 200 \text{ mm} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Workspace Area** (annulus formula):
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 4\pi L_1 L_2 \\
A &= 4 \times \pi \times 500 \times 300 \\
A &= 600{,}000\pi \\
A &\approx 1{,}884{,}956 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A &\approx 1.885 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Singular Configurations:**

A 2-link planar arm has **2 singular configurations** per joint-1 cycle:
- $\theta_2 = 0°$ (fully extended — boundary singularity)
- $\theta_2 = 180°$ (fully folded — interior singularity)

At singularities, the Jacobian becomes rank-deficient, and infinite joint velocities are required for small end-effector velocities. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Choice Q2]`

**(a)** Explain the **four main subsystems of an industrial robot** with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of internal and external sensors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial robot is commanded to move to position $(250.00, 350.00)$ mm. Over 30 trials, the mean reached position is $(250.25, 349.80)$ mm, with $\sigma_x = 0.025$ mm and $\sigma_y = 0.030$ mm. Calculate the **accuracy** and **repeatability** of the robot. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

An industrial robot consists of four interconnected subsystems:

**1. Mechanical Subsystem (Body):** Includes the manipulator (links and joints), end effector (gripper, tool), and the drive system (motors, gearboxes, transmissions). **(1.5 marks)**

**2. Sensing Subsystem (Nerves):**
- **Internal Sensors:** Encoders/resolvers measure joint angles; tachometers measure joint velocities. These are essential for closed-loop position control. **(1 mark)**
- **External Sensors:** Vision cameras (for object recognition and localization), force/torque sensors (for assembly force feedback), proximity sensors, and tactile arrays. **(1.5 marks)**

**3. Control Subsystem (Brain):** Contains the CPU, memory, servo drive loops, safety PLC, and the teach pendant. Executes the robot program at high frequency (1–8 kHz). **(1.5 marks)**

**4. Power Subsystem (Fuel):** Provides electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy. Most modern robots use 3-phase 400V AC. **(1.5 marks)**

*(A neat block diagram showing arrow flow between subsystems is expected — 0.5 mark reserved for diagram.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Commanded point $(x_c, y_c) = (250.00, 350.00)$ mm, mean reached $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}) = (250.25, 349.80)$ mm, $\sigma_x = 0.025$ mm, $\sigma_y = 0.030$ mm.

**Accuracy Calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2} \\
A &= \sqrt{(250.25 - 250.00)^2 + (349.80 - 350.00)^2} \\
A &= \sqrt{(0.25)^2 + (-0.20)^2} \\
A &= \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.0400} \\
A &= \sqrt{0.1025} \\
A &= 0.320 \text{ mm} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Repeatability Calculation** (using the $3\sigma$ rule):
$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2} \\
R &= 3\sqrt{(0.025)^2 + (0.030)^2} \\
R &= 3\sqrt{0.000625 + 0.000900} \\
R &= 3\sqrt{0.001525} \\
R &= 3 \times 0.03905 \\
R &= 0.117 \text{ mm} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The accuracy ($0.320$ mm) is **much larger** than the repeatability ($0.117$ mm). This indicates a **systematic error** in calibration (the robot consistently misses the commanded point in a specific direction). Repeatability is good, so re-calibration of the world frame will improve accuracy without hardware change. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes on This Topic:**
> 1. Confusing **DOF** with **axes** — A SCARA has 4 axes but only 3 DOF (3 for position, 1 redundant for obstacle avoidance).
> 2. Forgetting the **units** in workspace calculations — KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Sign error** in accuracy calculation — $(\bar{x} - x_c)$ and $(x_c - \bar{x})$ give the same squared result, but students often write the wrong term in the difference of means.
> 4. In classification questions, students draw diagrams without **labelled joint axes (R/P)** — full marks require both.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Robotics & Industrial Automation**

- [ ] **Robot** = reprogrammable + multifunctional + manipulator. The word *reprogrammable* is non-negotiable.
- [ ] **Automation** is the parent discipline; **robotics** is a branch of it.
- [ ] **Asimov's Laws** — 3 original + 1 Zeroth Law. Memorize the order of precedence.
- [ ] **DOF** = 6 for full 3D position + orientation control. Industrial robots: 3 to 7.
- [ ] **Kutzbach (planar)**: $n = 3(L-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$. **Grübler (spatial)**: $M = 6(n-1) - \Sigma(6-i)f_i$.
- [ ] **Five Robot Types**: Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, SCARA, Articulated (+ Parallel/Delta).
- [ ] **SCARA** = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm. Vertical stiffness + planar compliance.
- [ ] **Repeatability $\neq$ Accuracy**. Repeatability is better than accuracy on most industrial robots.
- [ ] **Repeatability** uses the $3\sigma$ rule: $R = 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2}$.
- [ ] **Accuracy** uses the distance from the mean position to the commanded position.
- [ ] **Workspace of 2-link planar arm** is an annulus: $A = 4\pi L_1 L_2$.
- [ ] **Singularities** occur when the Jacobian is rank-deficient — infinite joint velocities needed.
- [ ] **Three Generations**: Pick-and-Place → Teach-Playback → Intelligent/Cognitive.
- [ ] **Four Subsystems**: Mechanical, Sensing, Control, Power.
- [ ] **Safety standard**: ISO 10218 + ANSI/RIA R15.06 govern industrial robot safety.
- [ ] **ESTOP** is hardware-wired, not software-controlled.
- [ ] **Six values to memorize**: DOF, Payload, Reach, Repeatability, Accuracy, Resolution.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Good luck with your KTU Board Examination!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Robotics: Definition and History of Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Robotics: Definition and History of Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Robotics** is the interdisciplinary branch of mechanical engineering, electronics, computer science, and information engineering that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of **robots**. It integrates the disciplines of **mechatronics**, **automation engineering**, **artificial intelligence**, and **control systems** to produce programmable, multifunctional manipulators capable of performing tasks in place of human beings.

A **Robot** (from the Czech word *robota*, meaning "forced labour" or "drudgery") is formally defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** as:

> *"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."* — **ISO 8373:2021**

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (Course Code: OERUT724), a robot is understood as an **integrated mechatronic system** composed of four essential subsystems:
1. **Mechanical Structure** (manipulator + end-effector)
2. **Drive System** (actuators + power transmission)
3. **Sensing System** (sensors + transducers)
4. **Control System** (controller + software)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The syllabus explicitly defines robotics as an *Open Elective* course, meaning emphasis is placed on the **practical application of robotic systems in assembly lines and material handling** rather than deep theoretical computer science. Mastering the basic taxonomy of robots and their historical evolution forms the foundation for all later modules on kinematics, end-effectors, and industrial automation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you walk into a modern car manufacturing plant. A robotic arm, looking like a giant mechanical "steel octopus," is meticulously welding car frames with millimeter precision. Another robot is gliding along a track, picking up heavy engine blocks and placing them onto an assembly conveyor without a single bead of sweat. These are not science-fiction fantasies — they are **industrial robots** working tirelessly 24/7.

Think of a robot as a **human arm with a digital brain**:
- **Bones** → Mechanical links (rigid structural members)
- **Joints** → Revolute or prismatic actuators (allow rotation/translation)
- **Muscles** → Servo motors, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinders (provide power)
- **Skin** → Sensors (force, vision, tactile — perceive the environment)
- **Brain** → Microcontroller/PLC (processes information and issues commands)
- **Hand** → End-effector / gripper (interacts with the physical world)

Just as a human worker must be *trained* to perform a task, a robot must be *programmed*. The beauty lies in the fact that once programmed, a robot can repeat that task **infinitely** without fatigue, with *consistent precision*, and in *hazardous environments* where humans cannot survive.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** | Typically **3 to 6** for industrial robots | Number of independent movements |
| **Payload Capacity** | **3 kg to 1000+ kg** | Maximum load the robot can manipulate |
| **Reach / Workspace** | **0.5 m to 5+ m** | Maximum radial distance of end-effector |
| **Repeatability** | **±0.02 mm to ±0.5 mm** | Precision of returning to a taught position |
| **Number of Axes** | **3, 4, 5, or 6** | Independent motion axes (joints) |
| **Operating Speed** | **1 m/s to 10 m/s** | Maximum TCP (Tool Center Point) velocity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Repeatability vs. Accuracy**: These are often confused but are fundamentally different. **Repeatability** is the robot's ability to return to a previously taught position (statistical measure of *consistency*). **Accuracy** is the ability to move to a *new* commanded position in space. Industrial robots typically have **high repeatability but moderate accuracy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition: Anatomy of an Industrial Robot

Every robotic manipulator, regardless of its manufacturer or configuration, consists of **four tightly integrated subsystems**. Understanding each is critical for the KTU board examination.

#### A. Mechanical Subsystem (The Skeleton)
- **Base**: Anchors the robot to the floor/work surface.
- **Links**: Rigid members connecting joints (analogous to bones).
- **Joints**: Allow relative motion between consecutive links.
  - **Revolute (R) joint**: Rotational motion about an axis (like a hinge).
  - **Prismatic (P) joint**: Linear sliding motion along an axis.
- **End-Effector (EOAT — End of Arm Tooling)**: The "hand" of the robot (gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, suction cup, etc.).

#### B. Drive / Actuation Subsystem (The Muscles)
Three principal actuator types power industrial robots:
1. **Electric Servo Motors (DC/AC/BLDC)**: Most common; offer high precision, ease of control, and cleanliness. Used in **~70%** of industrial robots.
2. **Hydraulic Actuators**: Provide high force/payload capabilities (used in heavy-duty applications like forging).
3. **Pneumatic Actuators**: Compressed air-driven; simple, low-cost; used for pick-and-place operations requiring only 2-3 DOF.

#### C. Sensing Subsystem (The Nerves)
- **Internal Sensors (Proprioceptive)**: Encoders, resolvers, tachometers — monitor *internal* state (joint position, velocity).
- **External Sensors (Exteroceptive)**: Vision cameras, force/torque sensors, proximity sensors, tactile sensors — perceive the *external* environment.

#### D. Control Subsystem (The Brain)
- **Controller Hardware**: PLC, microcontroller, or industrial PC.
- **Programming**: Teach-pendant programming, offline programming (OLP), or AI-based learning.
- **Feedback Loop**: Closed-loop PID control ensures the robot reaches the desired setpoint.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Robot Classifications

**Why classify robots?** Because different industrial applications demand different kinematic structures, payloads, and precision. A robotic arm designed to assemble microchips cannot lift a car engine, and vice versa.

**How are they classified?**

**(a) By Mechanical Configuration (Coordinate System)**
- **Cartesian Robot (PPP)** — Rectilinear motions along X, Y, Z axes. Best for pick-and-place, CNC-like operations.
- **Cylindrical Robot (RPP)** — One rotational joint at the base + two prismatic joints.
- **Polar/Spherical Robot (RRP)** — Two rotational joints + one prismatic.
- **SCARA Robot (RRP)** — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm; ideal for vertical assembly.
- **Articulated Robot (RRR)** — All-rotational joints; resembles a human arm (e.g., **6-axis KUKA, ABB IRB, FANUC**).

**(b) By Control Method**
- **Non-Servo (Open-loop)**: Limited sequence control (pick-and-place machines).
- **Servo-Controlled (Closed-loop)**: Continuous path control with feedback (most modern industrial robots).

**(c) By Application Domain**
- **Industrial Robots**: Assembly, welding, painting, palletizing.
- **Service Robots**: Domestic, medical, agricultural, defense.
- **Mobile Robots**: AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots), drones.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL RULE — Markdown Table Pipe Symbol**: In all formulas below, absolute values and dividers are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) instead of the literal pipe character `\vert` so they do not break the markdown table syntax.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom (Planar Mechanism) | $\text{DOF} = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$ | $N$ = number of links, $J_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $J_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| Degrees of Freedom (Spatial Mechanism) | $\text{DOF} = 6(N-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$ | Kutzbach–Grübler criterion |
| Joint Variable | $\theta_i$ (revolute) or $d_i$ (prismatic) | Generalized coordinate at joint $i$ |
| Workspace Reach | $R_{\max} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert L_i \vert$ | Sum of link lengths for fully extended arm |
| Repeatability | $\pm 3\sigma$ (statistical) | $\sigma$ = standard deviation of position error |
| Resolution | $\text{Res} = \dfrac{\text{Encoder bits}}{2\pi}$ | Smallest incremental joint motion |
| Cartesian Position | $(x, y, z) = (r\cos\phi, \, r\sin\phi, \, z)$ | Cylindrical-to-Cartesian conversion |
| Spherical Position | $(x, y, z) = (R\sin\theta\cos\phi, \, R\sin\theta\sin\phi, \, R\cos\theta)$ | Spherical-to-Cartesian conversion |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Degrees of Freedom Using Kutzbach–Grübler Equation

The **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** is the foundational formula used to compute the mobility (degrees of freedom) of any mechanical linkage — including robotic manipulators.

#### Step 1: Set Up the Generic Formula

For a planar mechanism (motion in 2D plane):

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(N - 1) - 2J_1 - J_2
$$

**Conversion Logic**:
- $3(N - 1)$ accounts for the fact that each of the $N$ rigid bodies (excluding the fixed ground link) has **3 planar degrees of freedom** (translation along X, translation along Y, rotation about Z).
- $2J_1$ represents the constraint imposed by each **1-DOF joint** (pin/hinge/slider) — it removes 2 DOF.
- $J_2$ represents the constraint of each **2-DOF joint** (e.g., cam, multiple-pin link) — it removes 1 DOF.

#### Step 2: Apply the Formula to a Standard 6-Axis Articulated Robot

Consider a 6-axis industrial robot (such as an ABB IRB 1200):

$$
N = 7 \quad (6 \text{ moving links} + 1 \text{ fixed ground link})
$$

$$
J_1 = 6 \quad (6 \text{ revolute joints, each a 1-DOF joint})
$$

$$
J_2 = 0 \quad (\text{no 2-DOF joints present})
$$

Substituting into the planar formula (we use spatial for real accuracy, but the principle is identical):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF}_{\text{planar}} &= 3(7 - 1) - 2(6) - 0 \\
&= 3 \cdot 6 - 12 - 0 \\
&= 18 - 12 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic**:
- Each of the 6 moving links contributes 3 planar DOF → total **18 DOF** available.
- Each of the 6 revolute joints removes 2 DOF → total **12 DOF** constrained.
- Net mobility = **6 DOF**, which exactly matches the robot's 6 independent axes (yaw, pitch, roll of end-effector + 3 base rotations).

#### Step 3: Spatial Kutzbach–Grübler Generalization

For a 3D robot operating in space:

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5
$$

**Conversion Logic**:
- Each spatial rigid body has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- Joints of higher class remove proportionally fewer DOF.

Applying to the same 6-axis robot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF}_{\text{spatial}} &= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

The result confirms a **6-DOF spatial manipulator**, exactly as required.

### 3.2 Historical Timeline Derivation: Evolution of Robotics

The evolution of robotics can be mathematically abstracted as a **generational step function**, where each generation represents a leap in capability:

$$
\text{Generation}_n = \text{Generation}_{n-1} + \Delta C_n
$$

where $\Delta C_n$ represents the **capability delta** introduced by generation $n$ (sensors, AI, mobility, etc.).

| Generation | Era | Capability Delta $\Delta C_n$ | Landmark System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gen 0** | Pre-1950 | Mechanical automata (no programmability) | Al-Jazari's *Programmable* flute player (1206) |
| **Gen 1** | 1950–1960 | Pick-and-place, single-task automation | Unimate #001 (1961), Devol & Engelberger |
| **Gen 2** | 1970–1980 | Servo control + microprocessors | Cincinnati Milacron T3 (1973), IRB-6 (ABB, 1974) |
| **Gen 3** | 1980–1990 | Vision-guided, sensor-integrated | SCARA (Hirata, 1978; commercialized 1981) |
| **Gen 4** | 1990–2010 | Mobile robots, networked control | Honda ASIMO (2000), Roomba (2002) |
| **Gen 5** | 2010–Present | AI-driven collaborative robots (Cobots) | Universal Robots UR5 (2008), Boston Dynamics Spot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU**: Questions on "history of robotics" frequently test the student's ability to **chronologically arrange milestones** and to associate a *year* with a *pioneer*. Mastering this table guarantees 7+ marks on any history-based question.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pioneer–Contribution Mapping

| Pioneer / Organization | Year | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Al-Jazari** | 1206 | First *programmable* humanoid automaton (muscle-and-pulley driven) |
| **Leonardo da Vinci** | 1495 | Mechanical Knight (analog computing via cables/pulleys) |
| **Karel Čapek** | 1920 | Coined the term *Robot* in play *R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)* |
| **Isaac Asimov** | 1942 | Coined term *Robotics*; formulated the **Three Laws of Robotics** |
| **George Devol** | 1954 | Filed patent for *Programmable Article Transfer* (first industrial robot patent) |
| **Joseph Engelberger** | 1956 | Founded *Unimation* — world's first robot manufacturing company |
| **Victor Scheinman** | 1969 | Invented the **Stanford Arm** (first all-electric 6-axis articulated robot) |
| **Hirata (Yamanashi Univ.)** | 1978 | Invented the **SCARA** robot |
| **Victor Scheinman** | 1980 | Invented the **MIT-Arm** (precursor to PUMA) |
| **Unimation (with GM)** | 1961 | First commercial installation at GM Ternstedt plant |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Robot

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop interaction between the four subsystems of any robotic manipulator:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Operator / Programmer] --> B[Controller Unit CPU and Memory]
    B --> C[Drive Signal Amplifier]
    C --> D[Actuators Servo Hydraulic Pneumatic]
    D --> E[Mechanical Linkage Joints and Links]
    E --> F[End Effector Gripper or Tool]
    F --> G[Workpiece External Environment]
    G --> H[External Sensors Vision Force Tactile]
    E --> I[Internal Sensors Encoders Resolvers]
    H --> B
    I --> B
    B --> A
```

**Reading the Diagram**:
- The **forward path** (top half) represents command flow: Operator → Controller → Amplifier → Actuator → Linkage → End-Effector → Environment.
- The **feedback path** (bottom half) represents sensory feedback: Internal sensors and external sensors send real-time data back to the controller.
- The controller compares **desired setpoint vs. actual state** and adjusts the drive signal — this is **closed-loop PID control**, the heart of every modern industrial robot.

### 4.2 Mermaid Timeline: Evolution of Robotics (Generational Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Gen 0 Pre 1950 Mechanical Automata] --> A1[Gen 1 1950 1960 Pick and Place Unimate]
    A1 --> A2[Gen 2 1970 1980 Servo and Microprocessor]
    A2 --> A3[Gen 3 1980 1990 Vision Guided SCARA]
    A3 --> A4[Gen 4 1990 2010 Mobile and Networked ASIMO]
    A4 --> A5[Gen 5 2010 Present AI Cobots Universal Robots]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Classification Tree: Robot Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Robot Classification]
    root --> byConfig[By Mechanical Configuration]
    root --> byControl[By Control Method]
    root --> byApp[By Application]

    byConfig --> cart[Cartesian PPP]
    byConfig --> cyl[Cylindrical RPP]
    byConfig --> pol[Polar RRP]
    byConfig --> scara[SCARA RRP]
    byConfig --> artic[Articulated RRR]

    byControl --> nonServo[Non Servo Open Loop]
    byControl --> servo[Servo Closed Loop]

    byApp --> ind[Industrial Assembly Welding]
    byApp --> serv[Service Medical Domestic]
    byApp --> mob[Mobile AGV AMR Drone]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
**Define the term "Robot" as per ISO 8373. List any two distinguishing features that differentiate a robot from a conventional automated machine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to **ISO 8373:2021**, a robot is defined as *"an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications."*

**Distinguishing features** (any two, 1.5 marks each):
1. **Reprogrammability**: A robot can be reprogrammed to perform multiple different tasks without hardware modification. A conventional automated machine is hard-wired for a single task.
2. **Multipurpose capability with ≥3 axes**: Robots possess three or more programmable axes of motion. A simple pick-and-place conveyor has only 1–2 DOF and hence is not classified as a robot.
3. **Multipurpose functionality**: Robots can perform varied tasks (welding, painting, assembly) by changing the end-effector, while dedicated automation machines cannot.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
**State and explain Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Isaac Asimov, in his 1942 short story *Runaround*, formulated the **Three Laws of Robotics** (1 mark for each law):

1. **First Law**: *"A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm."*
2. **Second Law**: *"A robot must obey orders given by human beings except where such orders would conflict with the First Law."*
3. **Third Law**: *"A robot must protect its own existence as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law."*

Later, Asimov added the **Zeroth Law**: *"A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip**: Asimov's laws are philosophical/ethical guidelines, NOT engineering constraints. Examiners may ask for the difference — make it clear these are *fictional ethical postulates*, not implemented in any real controller.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q.A (i). [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**  
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the classification of robots based on **mechanical configuration**. With neat diagrams, describe the workspace of each type.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-DOF **Cartesian robot** has link lengths $L_1 = 500\,\text{mm}$ along X, $L_2 = 400\,\text{mm}$ along Y, and $L_3 = 300\,\text{mm}$ along Z. Calculate its **maximum reach** and **total cubic workspace volume**.

---

**Model Answer for Q.A:**

**(a) Classification by Mechanical Configuration (7 marks):**

| # | Robot Type | Joint Sequence | Workspace Shape | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cartesian (Gantry)** | P-P-P | Rectangular cuboid | Pick-and-place, CNC, 3D printing |
| 2 | **Cylindrical** | R-P-P | Hollow cylinder | Loading/unloading CNC lathes |
| 3 | **Polar / Spherical** | R-R-P | Spherical sector | Forging, die casting |
| 4 | **SCARA** | R-R-P | Cylindrical sector (horizontal) | Vertical assembly, PCB insertion |
| 5 | **Articulated (Anthropomorphic)** | R-R-R | Irregular sphere | Welding, painting, complex assembly |

For each configuration, the student should mention:
- **Joint arrangement** (sequence of R and P joints).
- **Workspace geometry** (the volume accessible to the TCP).
- **Typical industrial application**.

A detailed diagram showing each workspace shape (cuboid, cylinder, sphere, irregular sphere) is expected for full marks.

**(b) Solution — Cartesian Robot Reach & Volume (7 marks):**

Given:
- $L_1 = 500\,\text{mm}$
- $L_2 = 400\,\text{mm}$
- $L_3 = 300\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1: Maximum Reach (Diagonal Distance)**

The maximum reach is the straight-line distance from the base to the farthest corner of the cuboid workspace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + L_3^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(500)^2 + (400)^2 + (300)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{250000 + 160000 + 90000} \\
&= \sqrt{500000} \\
R_{\max} &\approx 707.11\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Total Workspace Volume**

The Cartesian robot's workspace is a rectangular cuboid (prism) with dimensions $L_1 \times L_2 \times L_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{workspace}} &= L_1 \times L_2 \times L_3 \\
&= 500 \times 400 \times 300 \\
&= 60{,}000{,}000\,\text{mm}^3 \\
V_{\text{workspace}} &= 0.06\,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer**: $R_{\max} \approx 707.11\,\text{mm}$ and $V = 0.06\,\text{m}^3$.

---

**Q.B (i). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **historical evolution of robotics**, highlighting at least six major milestones from pre-1950 to the modern era.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a **4-axis SCARA robot** using the Kutzbach–Grübler equation. The robot has 5 links (including ground) and 4 revolute joints, with one prismatic joint at the end-effector for vertical insertion.

---

**Model Answer for Q.B:**

**(a) Historical Evolution (7 marks):**

Six major milestones (chronologically ordered):

1. **1206 — Al-Jazari's Programmable Automaton**: Muslim polymath Al-Jazari built a programmable humanoid robot using pegs and cams.
2. **1495 — Leonardo da Vinci's Mechanical Knight**: First humanoid automaton driven by cables and pulleys.
3. **1920 — Karel Čapek coined "Robot"**: Czech playwright introduced the word in *R.U.R.* (Rossum's Universal Robots).
4. **1942 — Asimov's Three Laws**: Science-fiction author coined *robotics* and proposed ethical laws.
5. **1954 — George Devol's Patent**: Filed the first industrial robot patent (*Programmable Article Transfer*).
6. **1961 — Unimate Installed at GM**: First commercial industrial robot deployed at the GM Ternstedt plant, New Jersey, USA.
7. **1978 — SCARA Robot Invented**: Prof. Hiroshi Makino at Yamanashi University invented the SCARA — revolutionized PCB assembly.
8. **2008 — Universal Robots UR5**: First commercial *collaborative robot (cobot)* opened the era of human-robot collaboration.

**(b) DOF Computation for 4-axis SCARA Robot (7 marks):**

Given:
- $N = 5$ links (4 moving + 1 ground)
- 4 revolute joints (1-DOF each): $J_1 = 4$
- 1 prismatic joint at the end-effector (1-DOF): $J_1 = 5$ total 1-DOF joints
- $J_2 = 0$ (no 2-DOF joints)

Using the **spatial Kutzbach–Grübler equation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5 \\
&= 6(5 - 1) - 5(5) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
&= 6 \cdot 4 - 25 \\
&= 24 - 25 \\
&= -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this result is mathematically inconsistent because SCARA has 4 DOF in practice. This happens because **SCARA has parallel joint constraints** (the prismatic is in-line with the revolute). For serial chains, the simpler rule applies:

**For a serial kinematic chain**:
$$
\text{DOF}_{\text{serial}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i
$$

where $f_i$ is the DOF of joint $i$.

For the SCARA: 4 revolute + 1 prismatic → wait, a 4-axis SCARA has only **4 joints**: 3 revolute + 1 prismatic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF}_{\text{serial}} &= 3(1) + 1(1) \\
&= 3 + 1 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer**: The 4-axis SCARA robot has **4 degrees of freedom** (X, Y translation + Z rotation + Z translation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: A common mistake is applying the planar Kutzbach formula to a spatial robot. **Planar** equations ($\text{DOF} = 3(N-1) - 2J_1 - J_2$) are used **only for mechanisms confined to a 2D plane**. For a spatial robot, **always use the 6-DOF spatial formula**, OR for a simple serial chain, simply **count the number of joints** (each revolute or prismatic joint contributes 1 DOF). Students lose 2-3 marks for confusing these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Definition and History of Robotics**

- [ ] **Robot Definition (ISO 8373)**: Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose, ≥3 axes.
- [ ] **Etymology**: "Robota" (Czech) = forced labour; coined by Karel Čapek (1920); popularized by Asimov.
- [ ] **Four Subsystems**: Mechanical + Drive + Sensing + Control (all mandatory).
- [ ] **Three Actuator Types**: Electric (most common), Hydraulic (heavy duty), Pneumatic (simple).
- [ ] **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy**: Repeatability = returning to known point; Accuracy = reaching new commanded point.
- [ ] **Five Mechanical Configurations**: Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, SCARA, Articulated.
- [ ] **Joint Types**: Revolute (R, rotational) and Prismatic (P, linear).
- [ ] **DOF Formula (Spatial)**: $\text{DOF} = 6(N-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$.
- [ ] **DOF for Serial Chain**: Sum of joint DOFs (each R or P joint = 1 DOF).
- [ ] **Key Historical Dates**: 1920 (term coined), 1942 (Asimov), 1954 (Devol's patent), 1961 (Unimate at GM), 1978 (SCARA), 2008 (first cobot).
- [ ] **Key Pioneers**: Čapek, Asimov, Devol, Engelberger, Scheinman, Makino.
- [ ] **Asimov's Three Laws**: 1) No harm to humans, 2) Obey humans, 3) Self-preservation (in order of priority).
- [ ] **Generational Evolution**: Gen 1 (Pick-Place) → Gen 2 (Servo) → Gen 3 (Vision) → Gen 4 (Mobile) → Gen 5 (AI Cobots).
- [ ] **Application Domains**: Industrial (welding, assembly, painting), Service (medical, domestic), Mobile (AGV, drone).
- [ ] **Cartesian Robot Workspace**: Rectangular cuboid = $L_1 \times L_2 \times L_3$.
- [ ] **Maximum Reach Formula**: $R_{\max} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + L_3^2}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Robots and Their Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots and Their Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU / ISO Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO 8373:2021 Definition:** An *industrial robot* is an **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes**, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.

A **robot** in the context of robotic assembly and material handling is therefore a **re-programmable mechatronic device** consisting of:
- A **manipulator mechanism** (links + joints forming a kinematic chain),
- A **controller** (servo + PLC / industrial PC),
- **End-of-arm tooling (EOAT)** or gripper, and
- **Sensors and actuators** that enable autonomous or semi-autonomous execution of tasks such as pick-and-place, welding, palletizing, and assembly.

### Key Metrics Used to Classify Robots (KTU Board Exam Favourites)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | **DOF** | 3 – 6 (occasionally 7) |
| Payload Capacity | $m_p$ | 3 kg – 1000 kg |
| Reach / Radius | $R$ | 0.3 m – 3.5 m |
| Repeatability | $\pm \rho$ | $\pm 0.02$ mm – $\pm 0.1$ mm |
| Maximum Joint Speed | $\omega_{max}$ | 90 – 360 deg/s |
| Cycle Time | $t_c$ | 0.3 s – 4.0 s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Repeatability vs Accuracy — Board Favourite Distinction**
> *Repeatability* = how closely the robot returns to a **pre-taught** position. *Accuracy* = how close it gets to an **absolute commanded** position from a 3D model. Industrial robots typically have **high repeatability but lower absolute accuracy**, hence they are **lead-through or teach-pendant programmed**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine your **own human arm**:
- Your **shoulder**, **elbow**, and **wrist** are analogous to robot **revolute joints** (R).
- Your ability to **extend/retract** the arm (like a sliding drawer) is analogous to a **prismatic joint** (P).
- Your **clenched fist gripping a cup** is analogous to the **end-effector / gripper**.
- Your **brain + nerves + muscles** are analogous to the **controller + servo drives + actuators**.

A robot is essentially a **"manufactured, mathematically-modeled arm"** — every motion of every joint is described by a precise equation (forward / inverse kinematics), unlike the biological approximation your nervous system performs.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump for DOF:** A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF** (3 translations along X, Y, Z + 3 rotations about X, Y, Z). To *fully position and orient* a part in space, the manipulator must therefore possess **at least 6 DOF** — this is why most industrial robots are 6-axis.

## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Snapshot — Why This Topic Matters

This topic forms the **foundational classification** for every subsequent module:
- Module 2 (Kinematics & Dynamics) → builds on **DOF and joint types**.
- Module 3 (Grippers & EOAT) → builds on **application-specific robot selection**.
- Module 4 (Material Handling Systems) → builds on **payload / reach / speed trade-offs**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Classification of Robots

Industrial robots are classified primarily by their **kinematic structure** (joint arrangement and link geometry), which directly determines the **shape of their workspace envelope**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workspace Envelope = The volume of 3D space physically reachable by the robot's end-effector (tool centre point, TCP).** This is *the single most important board-exam concept* because **each robot type has a unique, geometrically-distinct envelope**.

### 2.1.1 Articulated Robot (RRR-RRR or 6R)

- **Structure:** All **revolute joints** — looks like a human arm.
- **Workspace:** Approximately a **partial sphere** (irregular "kidney" or "donut" shape).
- **DOF:** Typically **6** (3 for positioning the wrist + 3 for orienting the tool).
- **Typical Applications:** Welding, painting, machining tending, complex assembly.

### 2.1.2 Cartesian / Rectilinear / Gantry Robot (PPP)

- **Structure:** Three **prismatic joints** arranged mutually perpendicular along X, Y, Z.
- **Workspace:** A perfect **rectangular cuboid** of dimensions $L_x \times L_y \times L_z$.
- **DOF:** **3** (positioning only — no orientation unless extra wrist is added).
- **Typical Applications:** CNC loading, pick-and-place, 3D printing, PCB assembly, palletizing.

### 2.1.3 Cylindrical Robot (RPP)

- **Structure:** One **revolute base joint** + two **prismatic joints** (vertical + radial).
- **Workspace:** A **cylindrical volume** of radius $R$ and height $H$.
- **DOF:** **3** (extendable to 4 with a wrist roll axis).
- **Typical Applications:** Machine tending, die-casting, injection moulding unload, simple assembly.

### 2.1.4 Polar / Spherical Robot (RRP)

- **Structure:** Two **revolute joints** (base rotation + shoulder pitch) + one **prismatic joint** (radial extension).
- **Workspace:** A **partial spherical shell** (bowl-shaped).
- **DOF:** **3** (commonly extended to 6).
- **Typical Applications:** Forging, foundry, heavy forging tending, early-generation Unimate-style robots.

### 2.1.5 SCARA Robot (RRR + vertical PR)

- **Structure:** **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** — two revolute joints in the horizontal plane giving compliance in X-Y but stiffness in Z, plus a vertical prismatic joint and a wrist rotation.
- **Workspace:** A **horizontal cylindrical disc** of radius $L_1 + L_2$ and small height.
- **DOF:** **4** (X-Y positioning + Z insertion + wrist roll).
- **Typical Applications:** PCB assembly, screw-driving, vertical insertion tasks.

### 2.1.6 Delta / Parallel Robot

- **Structure:** Three (or more) parallel kinematic chains connecting a fixed base to a moving platform via revolute joints and parallelograms.
- **Workspace:** A **shallow dome / cylindrical disc** above the base.
- **DOF:** **3 translational** (no rotation, unless extra wrist).
- **Typical Applications:** Ultra-high-speed pick-and-place (toffee, pharma packaging) — cycle times under **0.3 s**.

### 2.1.7 Collaborative Robot (Cobot)

- **Structure:** Same kinematics as articulated, but with **force / torque sensors**, **rounded geometry**, and **low-inertia drives**.
- **Standards:** ISO/TS 15066 + ISO 10218 (power and force limiting).
- **Applications:** Human-robot collaborative assembly, machine tending in SMEs, laboratory automation.

### 2.1.8 Mobile Robots — AGV & AMR

- **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle):** Follows fixed paths (magnetic tape, wires, QR codes).
- **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot):** Uses **SLAM** (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) with LiDAR/cameras — no fixed infrastructure.
- **Applications:** Warehouse logistics, intra-factory material movement (handled later in Module 4).

### 2.1.9 Humanoid and Service Robots

- Bipedal locomotion, 20+ DOF, multi-modal sensing. Currently outside mainstream KTU board numerical problems but appear as **theory short-answer** items.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — every formula here is a likely Part-A or Part-B question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF of open-chain serial manipulator | $\text{DOF} = N_{joints}$ | dimensionless | True for all serial industrial arms |
| 2 | Kutzbach–Grübler DOF (spatial, 3-D) | $F = 6(N-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$ | dimensionless | N = total links, $J_i$ = number of joints with i DOF |
| 3 | Workspace — Cartesian robot | $V_{cart} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Rectangular cuboid |
| 4 | Workspace — Cylindrical robot | $V_{cyl} = \pi R^2 H$ | $\text{m}^3$ | R = radial reach, H = vertical stroke |
| 5 | Workspace — Spherical robot | $V_{sph} = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi R^3 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta\theta_{polar}}{180°}$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Fraction of full sphere |
| 6 | Workspace — SCARA robot | $V_{SCARA} = \pi (L_1 + L_2)^2 \cdot H_z$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Disc + vertical lift |
| 7 | Repeatability vs Accuracy | $\rho \ll \sigma_{acc}$ | mm | Repeatability ≤ ±0.02 mm for SCARA |
| 8 | Tip speed (cylindrical robot) | $v_{tip} = R \cdot \omega + v_{prismatic}$ | $\text{m/s}$ | Vector addition |
| 9 | Payload force at joint | $\tau_i = \sum_{j} \left[ m_j g \cdot r_{ij} \right]$ | N·m | Static torque on joint *i* |
| 10 | Cycle time estimate | $t_c = \dfrac{2 d_{avg}}{v_{avg}} + t_{acc/dec}$ | s | Distance + acceleration overhead |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Welding cells (automotive):** Articulated 6-axis robots dominate due to dexterity for seam tracking.
- **Pharma packaging:** Delta robots dominate due to **sub-second cycle time** (toffees, blister packs).
- **PCB assembly:** SCARA dominates due to **stiffness in Z** (vertical insertion force) + compliance in X-Y (compensating for part tolerance).
- **Warehouse fulfilment:** AMR fleets (Amazon Kiva-style) have replaced conveyor-centric AGVs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **All derivations below are intentionally exhaustive.** Skipping steps in the exam script is the **#1 reason** students lose marks on Part-B 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Degrees of Freedom Using Kutzbach–Grübler Formula

### 3.1.1 The General Spatial Formula

We begin with the Kutzbach criterion adapted for **3-dimensional spatial mechanisms**:

$$
F = 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5
$$

**Meaning of every term (KTU board examiners expect you to define these):**

- $F$ = Mobility (number of independent inputs the mechanism needs to fully specify its configuration).
- $N$ = Total number of **rigid links**, including the fixed ground link.
- $J_1$ = Number of **lower pairs** with 1 DOF (revolute R, prismatic P, helical H).
- $J_2$ = Number of pairs with 2 DOF (universal U, cylindrical C, planar in 3D).
- $J_3$ = Number of pairs with 3 DOF (spherical S, ball-and-socket).
- $J_4, J_5$ = Higher-pair joints (4 and 5 DOF respectively — rare in industrial robots).

The **factor of 6** arises because a **free rigid body in 3D space has 6 independent motions**: 3 translations (along $\hat{X}, \hat{Y}, \hat{Z}$) and 3 rotations (about $\hat{X}, \hat{Y}, \hat{Z}$).

Each revolute / prismatic joint removes 5 DOF (constrains 5 of the 6 freedoms). A spherical joint removes only 3 DOF (allows all 3 rotations).

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — 6-DOF Articulated Robot (PUMA-type)

A 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., PUMA 560, ABB IRB 120, KUKA KR 6) consists of 6 revolute joints connecting 6 moving links to a fixed base.

**Step 1 — Count the links:**

$$
N = \underbrace{1}_{\text{ground}} + \underbrace{6}_{\text{moving links}} = 7
$$

**Step 2 — Count the joints:**

Each revolute joint is a 1-DOF lower pair, so:

$$
J_1 = 6, \quad J_2 = J_3 = J_4 = J_5 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Kutzbach–Grübler:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5 \\
  &= 6(7 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 0 \\
  &= 6(6) - 30 \\
  &= 36 - 30 \\
  &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The articulated 6R robot has exactly **6 DOF**, which is sufficient to **fully position and orient** an arbitrary rigid body in 3D space — confirming it is a **general-purpose manipulator**.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — SCARA Robot (RRP + R)

A typical SCARA has:
- 2 horizontal revolute joints (shoulder, elbow)
- 1 vertical prismatic joint (Z insertion)
- 1 wrist revolute joint (roll)

**Step 1 — Count the links:**

$$
N = 1_{\text{ground}} + 4_{\text{moving links}} = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Count the joints:**

$$
J_1 = 4 \; (\text{three R + one P}), \quad J_2 = J_3 = J_4 = J_5 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 6(5 - 1) - 5(4) - 0 \\
  &= 6(4) - 20 \\
  &= 24 - 20 \\
  &= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** SCARA has **4 DOF** — exactly right for planar assembly + insertion tasks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workspace Volume of a Cartesian Robot

A Cartesian robot has 3 mutually-perpendicular prismatic joints with strokes $L_x, L_y, L_z$.

Each joint independently sweeps a 1-D line segment. The combination sweeps a **rectangular cuboid**.

**Derivation (from first principles):**

The TCP position is:

$$
\vec{r}_{TCP} = x \, \hat{X} + y \, \hat{Y} + z \, \hat{Z}, \quad \text{where } 0 \le x \le L_x,\; 0 \le y \le L_y,\; 0 \le z \le L_z
$$

The number of *differential* volume elements in each axis is $dx, dy, dz$. Integrating over the full stroke:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cart} &= \int_{0}^{L_x}\!\int_{0}^{L_y}\!\int_{0}^{L_z} dx\, dy\, dz \\
         &= \left[\int_{0}^{L_x} dx\right]\left[\int_{0}^{L_y} dy\right]\left[\int_{0}^{L_z} dz\right] \\
         &= L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $L_x, L_y, L_z$ are *independent* — adding reach in one axis does **not** compromise another. This is the chief advantage of Cartesian robots.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Workspace Volume of a Cylindrical Robot

A cylindrical robot has:
- One revolute joint at the base sweeping an angle $\theta$ (typically $0 \le \theta \le 360°$).
- One radial prismatic joint extending from $R_{min}$ to $R_{max}$.
- One vertical prismatic joint lifting from $z = 0$ to $z = H$.

**Position of TCP in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$
\vec{r}_{TCP} = r\cos\theta \, \hat{X} + r\sin\theta \, \hat{Y} + z \, \hat{Z}
$$

The differential volume element in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$
dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

Integrating over the reachable domain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cyl} &= \int_{0}^{H}\!\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\int_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz \\
        &= \left[\int_{0}^{H} dz\right] \left[\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta\right] \left[\int_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} r \, dr\right] \\
        &= H \cdot 2\pi \cdot \left[\frac{r^2}{2}\right]_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} \\
        &= H \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2}{2} \\
        &= \pi \, H \, \left(R_{max}^2 - R_{min}^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special case — full radial extension starting from the central axis ($R_{min} = 0$):**

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi \, R^2 \, H
$$

This recovers the standard textbook formula and explains *why* it appears so often in exams.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tip Speed of a Cylindrical Robot (Composite Motion)

A cylindrical robot's TCP simultaneously experiences:
1. Tangential velocity from base rotation $v_{tan} = R \cdot \omega$
2. Radial velocity from prismatic extension $v_{rad}$

These two are mutually perpendicular, so the **resultant speed** is their Pythagorean sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{resultant} &= \sqrt{v_{tan}^2 + v_{rad}^2} \\
              &= \sqrt{(R\omega)^2 + v_{rad}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example (board style):** $R = 0.8$ m, $\omega = 60°/\text{s} = \pi/3$ rad/s, $v_{rad} = 0.25$ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{tan} &= 0.8 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{0.8\pi}{3} \approx 0.838 \, \text{m/s} \\
v_{resultant} &= \sqrt{(0.838)^2 + (0.25)^2} = \sqrt{0.702 + 0.0625} \\
              &= \sqrt{0.7645} \approx 0.874 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 The KTU Analytical Matrix — Robot Selection at a Glance

| Selection Criterion | Cartesian | Cylindrical | Spherical | Articulated | SCARA | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best for | Heavy payloads, large work envelope | Compact cells | Heavy forging | General welding | Vertical assembly | High-speed packaging |
| Workspace shape | Cuboid | Cylinder | Partial sphere | Irregular sphere | Disc + column | Dome |
| Typical payload | Up to **1000 kg** | Up to **50 kg** | Up to **100 kg** | Up to **1000 kg** | Up to **20 kg** | Up to **8 kg** |
| Repeatability | ±0.05 mm | ±0.05 mm | ±0.10 mm | ±0.02 mm | ±0.01 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| Max speed | 1 m/s linear | 5 m/s linear | 3 m/s | 10 m/s TCP | 8 m/s TCP | 10 m/s TCP, **30 picks/min** |
| Cost index | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium | Very high |
| Orientation control | Poor (need wrist) | Limited | Limited | **Excellent (6 DOF)** | Limited | None (translation only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection rule of thumb taught in KTU lectures:**
> 1. Need **3-D positioning only** → Cartesian.
> 2. Need **speed + 3-D positioning** → Delta.
> 3. Need **vertical insertion force** → SCARA.
> 4. Need **arbitrary orientation in tight cells** → Articulated 6R.
> 5. Need **heavy load in compact floor area** → Cylindrical or Gantry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, **physical sketches of robot geometries** are rendered here as **Mermaid block diagrams** showing the **functional architecture and joint topology** rather than pictorial drawings, ensuring maximum clarity and zero rendering errors.

---

## 4.1 Master Classification of Robots (Mermaid Mind-Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[ROBOT TAXONOMY] --> rootB[By Kinematic Structure]
    rootA --> rootC[By Application Domain]
    rootA --> rootD[By Mobility]

    rootB --> b1[Articulated 6R]
    rootB --> b2[Cartesian PPP]
    rootB --> b3[Cylindrical RPP]
    rootB --> b4[Polar RRP]
    rootB --> b5[SCARA RRP plus R]
    rootB --> b6[Delta Parallel]

    rootC --> c1[Industrial]
    rootC --> c2[Service]
    rootC --> c3[Medical]
    rootC --> c4[Defence]
    rootC --> c5[Space]

    rootD --> d1[Fixed Base Manipulator]
    rootD --> d2[AGV Guided Vehicle]
    rootD --> d3[AMR SLAM Mobile]
    rootD --> d4[Humanoid Bipedal]

    b1 --> b1a[Welding Painting Assembly]
    b2 --> b2a[Pick Place 3D Printing]
    b3 --> b3a[Machine Tending Die Casting]
    b4 --> b4a[Forging Foundry]
    b5 --> b5a[PCB Assembly Screwdriving]
    b6 --> b6a[Pharma Food Packaging]

    c1 --> c1a[Manufacturing Logistics]
    c2 --> c2a[Cleaning Hospitality]
    c3 --> c3a[Surgery Rehabilitation]
    c4 --> c4a[EOD Reconnaissance]
    c5 --> c5a[Rovers Manipulator Arms]

    d1 --> d1a[Single Cell Operation]
    d2 --> d2a[Fixed Path Follower]
    d3 --> d3a[Dynamic Path Planner]
    d4 --> d4a[Human Interaction]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Industrial Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    teachA[Teach Pendant or HMI] --> ctrlA[Robot Controller Servo PLC]
    progA[Offline Program] --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> driveA[Servo Drive Units]
    driveA --> motorA[Brushless AC Motors]
    motorA --> mechA[Kinematic Chain Links and Joints]
    mechA --> eoatA[End of Arm Tooling Gripper Welder]
    eoatA --> taskA[Workpiece Assembly or Material Move]

    sensA[Encoder Feedback] --> driveA
    sensB[Force Torque Sensor] --> ctrlA
    sensC[Vision System] --> ctrlA
    sensD[Safety Scanner] --> ctrlA

    safA[E Stop Safety PLC] --> ctrlA
```

---

## 4.3 Robot-Selection Decision Flow (Engineer's Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start New Automation Task] --> q1{Does the workpiece need to be oriented in arbitrary directions}
    q1 -- YES --> a1[Choose 6 Axis Articulated Robot]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{Is the cycle time below 1 second}
    q2 -- YES --> a2[Choose Delta Parallel Robot]
    q2 -- NO --> q3{Does the task require vertical insertion force}
    q3 -- YES --> a3[Choose SCARA Robot]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{Is the work envelope larger than 3 m cube}
    q4 -- YES --> a4[Choose Cartesian Gantry Robot]
    q4 -- NO --> q5{Is the load above 50 kg in a compact cell}
    q5 -- YES --> a5[Choose Cylindrical Robot]
    q5 -- NO --> a6[Choose Articulated 4 Axis or SCARA based on cost]
```

---

## 4.4 Joint-Topology Comparison (ASCII-Approximation via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseA[Ground Base] --> j1a[Revolute Joint J1]
    j1a --> l1a[Link 1]
    l1a --> j2a[Revolute Joint J2]
    j2a --> l2a[Link 2]
    l2a --> j3a[Revolute Joint J3]
    j3a --> l3a[Link 3]
    l3a --> j4a[Revolute Joint J4]
    j4a --> l4a[Link 4]
    l4a --> j5a[Revolute Joint J5]
    j5a --> l5a[Link 5]
    l5a --> j6a[Revolute Joint J6]
    j6a --> tcpA[Tool Centre Point]

    baseB[Ground Base] --> p1b[Prismatic X]
    p1b --> p2b[Prismatic Y]
    p2b --> p3b[Prismatic Z]
    p3b --> tcpB[Tool Centre Point]

    baseC[Ground Base] --> r1c[Revolute Base Theta]
    r1c --> p1c[Prismatic Radial R]
    p1c --> p2c[Prismatic Vertical Z]
    p2c --> tcpC[Tool Centre Point]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **three parallel pipelines** above represent the kinematic chain of an **articulated 6R (top)**, a **Cartesian PPP (middle)** and a **cylindrical RPP (bottom)**. Students should practice drawing similar block-chain diagrams for SCARA, Polar, and Delta robots during revision.

---

## 4.5 Application-to-Robot Mapping Matrix (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app1[Welding] --> r1[6 Axis Articulated]
    app2[PCB Assembly] --> r2[SCARA]
    app3[Pharma Packing] --> r3[Delta]
    app4[Heavy Palletizing] --> r4[Gantry Cartesian]
    app5[Die Casting Unload] --> r5[Cylindrical]
    app6[Forging Tending] --> r6[Spherical Polar]
    app7[Warehouse Transport] --> r7[AMR Mobile]
    app8[Human Collaboration] --> r8[Cobot Articulated]
    app9[PCB Inspection] --> r9[SCARA plus Vision]
    app10[3D Printing Large] --> r10[Cartesian Gantry]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are styled per **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Marks shown are as per the **2-tier KTU internal-choice pattern for Part B**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q1.** Define **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot manipulator. Why is a **6-DOF articulated robot** considered the most versatile configuration for general-purpose industrial automation?

**Model Answer:**

> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot manipulator is the **number of independent joint motions** required to completely specify the position and orientation of its end-effector (TCP) in 3-dimensional space.

Since a rigid body in 3D space possesses **6 independent motions** — 3 translations along $\hat{X}, \hat{Y}, \hat{Z}$ and 3 rotations about $\hat{X}, \hat{Y}, \hat{Z}$ — a manipulator needs **at least 6 DOF** to fully position *and* orient an arbitrary workpiece.

A **6-DOF articulated robot** has 6 revolute joints arranged serially, providing **maximum dexterity** because:
- 3 DOF are used to **position** the wrist centre at any point within its spherical workspace.
- 3 DOF are used to **orient** the end-effector at any desired roll-pitch-yaw combination.

Hence, it is the **most versatile** configuration — capable of welding complex 3D seams, spray painting curved surfaces, and assembling parts at arbitrary orientations. **(3 marks — definition 1, formula justification 1, versatility explanation 1)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q2.** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, differentiate between a **SCARA robot** and a **Cartesian robot** in terms of **(i) joint configuration, (ii) workspace shape, and (iii) any one industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SCARA Robot | Cartesian Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Joint Configuration | RR + P (vertical) + R (wrist) | PPP (three mutually perpendicular prismatic) |
| DOF | 4 | 3 |
| Workspace Shape | Horizontal disc + small vertical column | Rectangular cuboid |
| Compliance | Compliant in X-Y, **rigid in Z** | Rigid in all 3 axes |
| Typical Application | **PCB component insertion / screw-driving** | **CNC machine loading / 3D printing** |

The key functional difference is that **SCARA's selective compliance** allows it to absorb small horizontal misalignments during vertical peg-in-hole insertions, while the Cartesian robot offers a **larger predictable cuboid work envelope** but no in-built orientation control. **(3 marks — joint 1, workspace 1, application + comment 1)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Both questions are independently framed on the same module topic.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Classify industrial robots based on their kinematic structure. Explain any three types in detail with neat sketches of their workspace envelopes and state two typical industrial applications for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (1 mark):** Industrial robots are classified by **joint type and arrangement** into six major families — Articulated (6R), Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical/Polar (RRP), SCARA (RRP + R), and Parallel/Delta.

**Type 1 — Articulated Robot (2 marks):**
- **Structure:** All revolute joints in a serial chain (like a human arm).
- **Workspace:** Partial spherical / irregular "kidney" shape.
- **Applications:** (1) Arc welding in automobile body shops, (2) Spray painting car bodies.

**Type 2 — SCARA Robot (2 marks):**
