- **Structure:** Two parallel revolute joints in horizontal plane + vertical prismatic + wrist roll.
- **Workspace:** Horizontal disc of radius $(L_1 + L_2)$ + small vertical column.
- **Applications:** (1) Pick-and-place of SMD components on PCBs, (2) Vertical screw-driving in assembly lines.

**Type 3 — Delta / Parallel Robot (2 marks):**
- **Structure:** Three parallelograms connecting fixed base to moving platform via revolute joints.
- **Workspace:** Shallow dome / cylindrical disc above the base.
- **Applications:** (1) High-speed sorting of toffees in food packaging, (2) Picking pharmaceutical vials at 100+ picks/min.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner expects **labelled ASCII block diagram or Mermaid chain** for each type — students who skip the diagram lose **1 mark per type** despite correct text.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**A manufacturing cell requires assembly of small aluminium housings (mass 12 kg, longest dimension 600 mm) onto a conveyor moving at 0.4 m/s. Compare the suitability of a 6-axis articulated robot versus a 4-axis SCARA for this task, justifying with reference to DOF, workspace, payload, cycle time, and cost.**

**Model Answer:**

| Selection Parameter | 6-Axis Articulated | SCARA (4-axis) | Better Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| DOF | 6 (full orientation) | 4 (planar + Z + roll) | Articulated |
| Workspace | ~ partial sphere, R ≈ 0.7 m | Disc, R ≈ 0.5 m, H ≈ 0.2 m | Articulated |
| Payload | 12 kg ⇒ well within 6 kg–20 kg class | 12 kg ⇒ within typical 6 kg–20 kg class | Tied |
| Cycle time | ~ 1.5 s for simple pick-place | ~ 0.5 s for planar pick-place | SCARA |
| Orientation | Any roll-pitch-yaw | Limited (no pitch, no yaw) | Articulated |
| Cost | ₹ 8–15 lakh | ₹ 4–8 lakh | SCARA |

**Justification (3 marks):**
- If the **housing orientation varies** (e.g., flipped upside down before insertion) → **6-axis articulated** is mandatory.
- If the housing is always presented in a **fixed orientation on the conveyor** (single orientation, vertical insertion only) → **SCARA** wins on **cost + speed**.
- Given the conveyor is at 0.4 m/s and spacing is constant, a SCARA at 0.5 s cycle time comfortably keeps up; therefore for a **fixed-orientation assembly cell, SCARA is the rational choice**, saving ≈ 50 % capital cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]`

**Explain the working principle, joint configuration, and key applications of a SCARA robot. Compute its DOF using the Kutzbach–Grübler formula and verify.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) exploits a unique mechanical anisotropy: it is **rigid (stiff) along the vertical Z-axis** so that it can apply strong downward insertion forces (e.g., peg-in-hole), while being **slightly compliant in the X-Y plane** so that small positional misalignments between the peg and the hole are absorbed by the parallelogram linkages — preventing jamming or part damage.

**Joint Configuration (2 marks):** R-R (horizontal) + P (vertical) + R (wrist roll). Total DOF = 4.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- PCB SMD component placement.
- Vertical screw-driving in mobile-phone assembly.
- Vial-capping in pharmaceutical lines.

**DOF Computation using Kutzbach–Grübler (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total links } N &= 1_{\text{ground}} + 4_{\text{moving}} = 5 \\
\text{1-DOF joints } J_1 &= 4 \; (\text{three R + one P}) \\
F &= 6(N - 1) - 5J_1 \\
  &= 6(5 - 1) - 5(4) \\
  &= 24 - 20 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence DOF = 4, which matches the direct count. ✓

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**A cylindrical coordinate robot has a maximum radial reach of 800 mm and a vertical stroke of 600 mm. Calculate:**

**(i)** The cylindrical workspace volume it can cover.
**(ii)** The maximum linear velocity of the TCP if the rotary joint sweeps at 60°/s and the prismatic joint extends at 250 mm/s simultaneously.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $R = 0.800$ m, $H = 0.600$ m, $\omega = 60°/\text{s} = \pi/3$ rad/s, $v_{rad} = 0.250$ m/s.

**(i) Workspace Volume (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cyl} &= \pi R^2 H \\
        &= \pi \times (0.800)^2 \times 0.600 \\
        &= \pi \times 0.64 \times 0.600 \\
        &= 0.384 \pi \\
        &\approx 1.206 \, \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum TCP Velocity (4 marks):**

Tangential velocity from base rotation:

$$
v_{tan} = R \cdot \omega = 0.800 \times \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{0.800\pi}{3} \approx 0.8378 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Radial velocity from prismatic joint:

$$
v_{rad} = 0.250 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Since $v_{tan}$ and $v_{rad}$ are perpendicular, resultant magnitude:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{resultant} &= \sqrt{v_{tan}^2 + v_{rad}^2} \\
              &= \sqrt{(0.8378)^2 + (0.250)^2} \\
              &= \sqrt{0.7019 + 0.0625} \\
              &= \sqrt{0.7644} \\
              &\approx 0.8743 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Volume $\approx 1.206 \, \text{m}^3$; Maximum TCP speed $\approx 0.874 \, \text{m/s}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the ground link** in the link count $N$ for the Kutzbach formula — costs **2 marks** per derivation question.
> 2. **Confusing DOF with number of axes** — these are usually the same in serial manipulators but differ in parallel manipulators (Delta has 3 axes but technically 3 DOF translational).
> 3. **Mixing up repeatability and accuracy** — repeating wrong definitions in Part A costs the *definition mark*.
> 4. **Drawing the wrong joint symbol** in SCARA diagrams — examiners expect **R-R-P-R** configuration clearly visible in the chain diagram.
> 5. **Forgetting the integration limits** in workspace derivations — $R_{min}$ vs $R_{max}$ distinction in the cylindrical volume formula is a **favourite trick**.
> 6. **Not stating the ISO reference** (ISO 8373:2021) when asked to *define a robot* — loses the formal-definition mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before walking into the KTU exam hall.**

- An **industrial robot** is an **automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator with ≥ 3 axes** — ISO 8373:2021 definition (must be quoted verbatim if asked).
- **Six classical robot types** by kinematic structure: **Articulated (6R), Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Polar/Spherical (RRP), SCARA (RRP+R), Delta/Parallel**.
- **DOF of serial open chain** = number of joints = number of moving links + ground included in N for Kutzbach formula.
- **Kutzbach–Grübler spatial formula:** $F = 6(N-1) - 5J_1 - 4J_2 - 3J_3 - 2J_4 - J_5$.
- **Workspace volumes:**
  - Cartesian: $L_x L_y L_z$
  - Cylindrical: $\pi R^2 H$
  - SCARA: $\pi (L_1 + L_2)^2 H_z$
  - Spherical (full): $\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$; (partial) scale by fraction $\Delta\theta/360°$.
- **Maximum DOF** for full 3-D positioning + orientation = **6**.
- **Repeatability** is much tighter than **accuracy** in industrial robots (e.g., ±0.02 mm vs ±1 mm).
- **SCARA** = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm — rigid in Z, compliant in X-Y — used for **vertical insertion** tasks.
- **Delta robot** = parallel kinematic chain — used for **ultra-high-speed** pick-and-place (food, pharma).
- **Cartesian** = best for **heavy payloads + large predictable envelopes** (gantry palletizing, 3D printing).
- **Cylindrical** = best for **compact cells + medium-payload machine tending**.
- **Articulated 6R** = best for **welding, painting, complex assembly** requiring arbitrary orientation.
- **Cobot** = power-and-force-limited robot per **ISO/TS 15066** + **ISO 10218** for human-robot collaboration.
- **AMR uses SLAM** (LiDAR + vision); **AGV follows fixed paths** (magnetic tape / QR / wire).
- **Tip speed of cylindrical robot** = Pythagorean sum of tangential and radial components: $v_{res} = \sqrt{(R\omega)^2 + v_{rad}^2}$.
- Always convert **degrees/s to rad/s** in numerical problems: $\omega_{rad/s} = \omega_{deg/s} \times \pi/180$.
- Always state the **joint notation (R, P, R-R-P, etc.)** when describing any robot type — examiners explicitly allocate marks for this.

---

    m1Start --> m1B[Classify by Kinematics]

topics.title: Overview of Robotic Systems in Industrial Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Robotic Systems in Industrial Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

According to the **ISO 8373:2021** standard (the international reference adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus), an **Industrial Robot** is defined as:

> An **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator**, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.

The three cardinal properties that distinguish an industrial robot from a conventional automated machine are:
1. **Automatic Control** – it operates without continuous human intervention.
2. **Re-programmability** – its task sequence and motion can be modified without physical alteration.
3. **Multipurpose Use** – it is adaptable across a range of tasks, not hard-wired to a single job.

**Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems** is the integrated discipline that combines the manipulator (robot arm), end-effector (gripper/tool), sensors, controllers, and material transport sub-systems to perform **picking, placing, sorting, palletizing, joining, and assembling** operations on a factory floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pay close attention to the phrase "**re-programmable, multipurpose**". KTU examiners frequently test whether students can differentiate an industrial robot from a *Hard-Automation machine* (which is purpose-built and non-reprogrammable). A CNC machine, for example, is NOT classified as an industrial robot by ISO 8373, even though it is automated.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Human Arm" Model

Imagine you are working on an assembly line, picking up a gear from a conveyor and pressing it onto a shaft. What do you use?

- Your **arm** (the mechanical structure) provides reach and positioning.
- Your **hand and fingers** (the end-effector) grasp the gear.
- Your **eyes and skin** (sensors) detect the gear's position, weight, and whether it has snapped in place.
- Your **brain** (the controller) sends signals to muscles (the actuators) telling them how fast, how far, and how forcefully to move.
- The **workspace** around you is the **envelope** within which your hand can reach.

An industrial robot is essentially a **metallic, tireless, and precise twin** of this human assembly worker. The mapping is exact:

| Human Worker | Industrial Robot Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Skeleton + Joints | Links + Revolute / Prismatic Joints |
| Muscles | Servo Motors / Pneumatic / Hydraulic Actuators |
| Hand | Gripper, Suction Cup, Welding Torch (End-Effector) |
| Eyes / Skin | Vision, Force/Torque, Proximity Sensors |
| Brain | PLC / Microcontroller-based Robot Controller |
| Reach Envelope | Robot's Workspace / Working Volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Tip:** Whenever you get stuck on a robotic concept, mentally map it to the human arm. Almost every parameter (degrees of freedom, payload, repeatability) has a human-equivalent meaning. This analogy is a favourite KTU examiner hook in Part A questions.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Performance Metrics

When evaluating or specifying an industrial robot, KTU 2024 expects familiarity with these standard ISO/ANSI/RIA metrics (all defined in **bold**):

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** – The number of independent joint motions required to fully specify the position and orientation of the end-effector in 3D space. A free rigid body in 3D requires **6 DOF** (3 for position, 3 for orientation).
- **Payload (kg)** – The maximum mass the robot can manipulate while maintaining rated performance, including the weight of the end-effector.
- **Reach (mm)** – The maximum distance from the base centre to the centre of the end-effector mounting flange.
- **Repeatability (±mm)** – The radius of the smallest sphere within which the end-effector will return to a taught position over many cycles. **Note:** Repeatability is NOT the same as accuracy.
- **Accuracy (mm)** – The closeness of a commanded position to the actual position achieved.
- **Working Envelope (m³)** – The 3D spatial volume reachable by the end-effector, bounded by the mechanical reach limits.
- **Cycle Time (s)** – The time taken to complete a standard pick-and-place cycle (often the "200 mm up, 400 mm across, 200 mm down" benchmark).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic for Robot Metrics — "P-R-A-W-C-D":**
> **P**ayload, **R**each, **A**ccuracy, **W**orking envelope, **C**ycle time, **D**OF.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of an Industrial Robot System

A complete robotic cell is **not** just a robot arm. The KTU 2024 module framing demands a systems-level understanding. A robotic work-cell is decomposed into **four functional layers**:

### Layer 1 — Mechanical Subsystem (Manipulator)
- **Links** (rigid members) connected by **Joints**.
- **Joint Types:**
  - **Revolute (R) joint:** Rotary motion (rotation about an axis) — analogous to a human elbow.
  - **Prismatic (P) joint:** Linear (sliding) motion — analogous to a human shoulder telescoping.
- **Common Configurations (named by joint sequence):**
  - **Articulated (RRR) — 6 DOF:** Most versatile; resembles human arm. Industry workhorse (e.g., FANUC M-2000, KUKA KR series).
  - **SCARA (RRP) — 4 DOF:** Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm; ideal for vertical assembly and PCB pick-place.
  - **Cartesian (PPP) — 3 DOF:** Three mutually perpendicular prismatic axes; used in gantry systems, pick-place, 3D printing.
  - **Cylindrical (RPP) — 4 DOF:** One revolute base + two prismatic; used in simple loading/unloading.
  - **Polar / Spherical (RRP) — 4 DOF:** Older design; used in forging and die-casting.
  - **Delta (Parallel) — 3 to 4 DOF:** Three arms in parallelogram arrangement; ultra-fast pick-and-place for food/pharma packaging.

### Layer 2 — Actuation Subsystem
- **Electric Servo Motors** (most common): High precision, clean, easy to control via PWM/encoder feedback.
- **Hydraulic Actuators:** High power-to-weight ratio; used in heavy-payload robots (>500 kg) like forging manipulators.
- **Pneumatic Actuators:** Low cost, low precision; used in simple pick-and-place, "bang-bang" cycles.

### Layer 3 — End-Effector (EOAT – End of Arm Tooling)
- **Mechanical Grippers:** Two or three-finger jaw grippers for prismatic parts.
- **Vacuum Grippers:** Suction cups for smooth, flat surfaces (glass, sheet metal, cardboard).
- **Magnetic Grippers:** For ferrous parts.
- **Tool-Type End-Effectors:** Welding torch, paint sprayer, drilling spindle, dispensing nozzle.

### Layer 4 — Sensory & Control Subsystem
- **Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors:** Encoders on each joint (position), tachometers (velocity), current sensors (torque).
- **External (Exteroceptive) Sensors:** Vision cameras (2D/3D), proximity (inductive, capacitive), force/torque sensors at the wrist, tactile sensor arrays.
- **Controller:** The "brain". Accepts the program (e.g., teach pendant input, RAPID, KRL, VAL3 language), executes trajectory planning, and closes the feedback loop at typically **1 kHz to 4 kHz** servo update rate.

## 2.2 Industrial Automation Pyramid (ISA-95 / KTU Context)

Industrial robots never operate in isolation — they are a layer inside the **Automation Hierarchy (ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model)**:

| Level | Layer | Function in Robotic Context |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **L4** | Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) | Order scheduling, production targets |
| **L3** | Manufacturing Execution System (MES) | Work-order dispatch to the cell |
| **L2** | Supervisory Control (SCADA / HMI) | Cell-level monitoring, recipe selection |
| **L1** | Cell / Robot Controller | Trajectory execution, I/O handling |
| **L0** | Field Devices (Sensors, Actuators) | Physical I/O — the robot itself |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO1** (Fundamentals of Robotic Systems). Examiners reward answers that explicitly link the robot to its place in the automation pyramid — a common 7-mark question stem.

## 2.3 The "Why" – Engineering Rationale Behind Robotic Adoption

A KTU-quality answer must go beyond *what* a robot is and explain *why* industries deploy them. The drivers are:

1. **Repeatability & Quality:** A robot can repeat a motion cycle with $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$ precision, **24/7** without fatigue, vastly outperforming a human's $\pm 0.25 \text{ mm}$ and 8-hour endurance.
2. **Hazardous Environment Operation:** Painting, welding, foundry work, nuclear decontamination, chemical handling — tasks with toxic fumes, extreme heat, or ionizing radiation.
3. **Throughput Optimisation:** A Delta robot can perform up to **300 picks/minute** in packaging lines — economically impossible for human labour.
4. **Labour Cost Arbitrage:** In high-wage economies, a $50{,}000$ robot amortised over 5 years can replace three shift workers, with a payback period often under **18 months**.
5. **Data Traceability:** Modern robots log cycle times, torque traces, and vision captures — feeding Industry 4.0 analytics.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> The table below is your one-stop revision reference for all quantitative aspects of this topic.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning / Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Spatial DOF of a free rigid body | $n = 6$ | 3 translation + 3 rotation | Fixed constant in 3D Euclidean space |
| 2 | Gruebler's Criterion (planar) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_{1} - j_{2}$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints | Lower bound on mobility for planar linkage |
| 3 | Gruebler's Criterion (spatial) | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_{1} - 4j_{2} - 3j_{3} - 2j_{4} - j_{5}$ | $j_k$ = joints with $k$ DOF | Used to verify 6-DOF industrial arms |
| 4 | Workspace Reach (cylindrical) | $V \approx \pi r^{2} h$ | $r$ = radial reach, $h$ = vertical stroke | Approx. for cylindrical robots; $r,h$ in metres |
| 5 | Workspace Reach (spherical) | $V \approx \frac{2}{3} \pi r^{3}$ | $r$ = max reach | Approx. for polar/spherical robots |
| 6 | Pick-and-Place Cycle Throughput | $N = \frac{3600}{T_{c}}$ | $T_c$ = cycle time in seconds | $N$ = picks per hour |
| 7 | Payload-Rated Torque at Joint $i$ | $\tau_{i} = \sum_{k=i}^{n} (m_{k} g \cdot d_{k} + I_{k}\alpha_{k})$ | $m_k$ = link mass, $d_k$ = moment arm, $I_k$ = inertia, $\alpha_k$ = angular accel. | Sum of gravity + inertial torques |
| 8 | Repeatability vs. Accuracy | $R \ll A$ in well-calibrated robots | $R$ = repeatability, $A$ = accuracy | $R$ is sensor/encoder-limited; $A$ includes calibration error |
| 9 | Gear Reduction (Servo) | $N = \frac{\omega_{motor}}{\omega_{joint}} = \frac{\tau_{joint}}{\tau_{motor}}$ | $N$ = gear ratio, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\tau$ = torque | $N > 1$ trades speed for torque |
| 10 | Resolution per Encoder Count | $\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{N_{ppr} \cdot N_{gear}}$ | $N_{ppr}$ = encoder pulses per rev, $N_{gear}$ = gear ratio | Smallest measurable joint increment in radians |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):** **Repeatability** = closeness of repeated measurements to *each other*. **Accuracy** = closeness of a measurement to the *true value*. A robot can be highly repeatable but inaccurate if poorly calibrated. The reverse is generally impossible.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The concepts above are not abstract — they form the backbone of:
- **Automotive Body Shops:** Articulated 6-DOF robots perform $\approx 80\%$ of spot-welding tasks in a modern car plant.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** Delta robots pick-and-place vials at 200+ cycles/min on GMP-certified clean lines.
- **E-commerce Warehouses:** Mobile robots (Kiva/Amazon Robotics) using LiDAR + SLAM bring shelves to pickers.
- **PCB Assembly:** SCARA robots populate components on printed circuit boards with placement rates exceeding 30{,}000 components per hour.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> This section provides **fully worked analytical derivations** that are commonly expected in KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) answers. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Verification of a 6-DOF Industrial Arm Using Gruebler's Criterion

**Problem Statement:** Verify that the popular **FANUC M-16iB articulated robot** (an R-R-R-R-R-R chain, often called a 6R robot) achieves the standard 6 DOF using Gruebler's spatial mobility formula.

### Given
- Number of moving links: $n = 6$ (base link is fixed/ground, not counted in the $n-1$ formulation — the moving chain has 6 links connected in series).
- Number of 1-DOF joints: $j_1 = 6$ (all six are revolute joints, each contributing 1 DOF).
- Number of higher-DOF joints: $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$.

### Step 1 — State the Gruebler Spatial Equation

The general Gruebler-Kutzbach mobility formula for a spatial mechanism is:

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

where $M$ is the **mobility (DOF)** of the mechanism in free space.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Values

Insert $n = 6$, $j_1 = 6$, and all other $j_k = 0$:

$$
M = 6(6 - 1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 1(0)
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Parenthesised Term

$$
6 - 1 = 5
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by 6

$$
6 \times 5 = 30
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Joint Penalty Term

$$
5 \times 6 = 30
$$

### Step 6 — Subtract to Obtain Mobility

$$
M = 30 - 30 - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0
$$

$$
M = 6
$$

### Conclusion

The 6R articulated robot configuration yields exactly **6 degrees of freedom** — the maximum required to position and orient a rigid body (end-effector) arbitrarily in 3D Cartesian space. This is why the articulated arm is the *de facto* standard for general industrial assembly and material handling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Workspace Volume of a Cylindrical Robot

**Problem Statement:** A cylindrical-coordinate robot has a maximum radial reach $r = 1.5 \text{ m}$ and a vertical stroke $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$. Compute its working envelope volume, assuming the radial reach describes a full circle ($\theta = 0$ to $2\pi$).

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometric Solid Formed

The end-effector can sweep:
- A radial excursion from the central axis producing a **cylinder** of radius $r$.
- A vertical excursion from the floor to height $h$ producing a **height** $h$.
- A rotational sweep about the base axis producing a **full $2\pi$ rev**.

The resulting envelope is a **right circular cylinder** of radius $r$ and height $h$.

### Step 2 — Write the Standard Volume Formula

$$
V = \pi r^{2} h
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

Substitute $r = 1.5 \text{ m}$ and $h = 2.0 \text{ m}$:

$$
V = \pi \times (1.5)^{2} \times 2.0
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Square Term

$$
(1.5)^{2} = 2.25
$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by 2.0

$$
2.25 \times 2.0 = 4.50
$$

### Step 6 — Multiply by $\pi$

$$
V = 4.50 \pi
$$

$$
V \approx 4.50 \times 3.1416
$$

$$
V \approx 14.137 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

### Final Result (with Units)

$$
\boxed{V \approx 14.14 \text{ m}^{3}}
$$

### Physical Interpretation

This is the spatial region within which the end-effector flange can be positioned. Any work-piece or fixture must lie **entirely within** this envelope, otherwise the robot cannot reach it. In a KTU layout-design question, this calculation is the first step to validating cell feasibility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pick-and-Place Throughput from Cycle Time

**Problem Statement:** A SCARA robot executes a single pick-and-place cycle in $T_c = 1.2 \text{ seconds}$. Determine the number of parts the robot can handle per **hour** and per **8-hour shift**.

### Step 1 — Define the Throughput Relationship

Throughput $N$ in parts-per-hour is the total seconds in an hour divided by the cycle time per part:

$$
N = \frac{3600 \text{ s/h}}{T_c \text{ s/part}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Value

$$
N = \frac{3600}{1.2}
$$

### Step 3 — Perform the Division

$$
\frac{3600}{1.2} = \frac{36000}{12} = 3000
$$

### Step 4 — State the Hourly Result

$$
N = 3000 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Per-Shift Result

An 8-hour shift yields:

$$
N_{\text{shift}} = 3000 \times 8 = 24{,}000 \text{ parts/shift}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Real-World Efficiency Correction

A KTU-quality answer accounts for the **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** loss factor. Industrial cells typically run at $85\%$ efficiency due to changeover, micro-stops, and quality checks. Adjusted throughput:

$$
N_{\text{effective}} = 24{,}000 \times 0.85 = 20{,}400 \text{ parts/shift}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{N \approx 20{,}400 \text{ parts per 8-hour shift at 85\% OEE}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Robot Configurations

> The following analytical table is high-yield for KTU 2024 questions comparing robot types. Memorise the joint sequences and the DOF counts.

| Configuration | Joint Sequence (from base) | DOF | Typical Workspace | Strength | Weakness | Industrial Use |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cartesian (Gantry) | P — P — P | 3 | Rectangular box | High rigidity, large envelope | Low dexterity, floor space | CNC loading, pick-place, 3D printing |
| Cylindrical | R — P — P | 4 | Hollow cylinder | Simple kinematics | Limited angular reach | Machine tending, simple loading |
| Spherical / Polar | R — R — P | 4 | Spherical sector | Heavy payload capability | Bulky structure | Forging, die-casting |
| SCARA | R — R — P | 4 | Cylindrical (horizontal disk + vertical) | Fast, rigid in vertical direction | Limited to vertical assembly | PCB assembly, screwdriving |
| Articulated (6R) | R — R — R — R — R — R | 6 | Near-spherical, complex | Maximum dexterity | Complex kinematics, larger footprint | Welding, painting, assembly |
| Delta (Parallel) | P — P — P + passive links | 3 or 4 | Dome-shaped, flat | Ultra-high speed | Limited payload & reach | Food, pharma packaging |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Joint Torque Estimation Under Gravity (Static)

**Problem Statement:** A 2-DOF planar arm has a forearm of length $L_2 = 0.5 \text{ m}$ and carries a payload $m_p = 2 \text{ kg}$ at its tip. The forearm mass is $m_2 = 1.5 \text{ kg}$ (uniform). The arm is held horizontally ($\theta_2 = 0$). Compute the gravitational torque at the **elbow joint** ($q_2$).

### Step 1 — Free-Body Identification

Two contributions to the elbow torque:
1. The **payload** $m_p$ acting at the tip, distance $L_2$ from the elbow.
2. The **forearm's own weight** $m_2 g$ acting at its centre of mass, distance $L_2/2$ from the elbow.

### Step 2 — State the Static Torque Equation

$$
\tau_2 = m_p g L_2 + m_2 g \frac{L_2}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$:

$$
\tau_2 = (2)(9.81)(0.5) + (1.5)(9.81)\left(\frac{0.5}{2}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the First (Payload) Term

$$
2 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 = 9.81 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Half-Length

$$
\frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Second (Forearm) Term

$$
1.5 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 3.67875 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 7 — Sum the Two Contributions

$$
\tau_2 = 9.81 + 3.67875
$$

$$
\tau_2 = 13.48875 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 8 — Apply Engineering Safety Factor

Standard industrial practice multiplies the static torque by a safety factor $S = 1.5$ to $2.0$ for dynamic loading. Taking $S = 1.5$:

$$
\tau_{\text{rated}} = 13.49 \times 1.5 = 20.24 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\text{rated}} \approx 20.2 \text{ N·m (with 1.5× safety factor)}}
$$

### Engineering Insight

The motor selected for joint $q_2$ must supply a continuous stall torque of at least $20.2 \text{ N·m}$ after the gear reduction. This is the foundational step in *motor sizing* for a robotic joint — a question KTU examiners love in 14-mark problems.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> The following questions are modelled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** patterns. The 14-mark questions include **internal choice** as per KTU 2024 module policy: students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`
**Define an industrial robot as per ISO 8373:2021. List any two distinguishing features that separate it from a hard-automated special-purpose machine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **industrial robot** is an **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator**, programmable in **three or more axes**, which can be fixed in place or mobile, designed for use in industrial automation applications (ISO 8373:2021).

Two distinguishing features over hard automation:
1. **Re-programmability** — The robot can be re-taught a new task (e.g., welding path) by altering software, with **no mechanical retooling**. A hard-automated machine, in contrast, requires physical re-fixturing and re-wiring.
2. **Multipurpose Use** — The same robot arm can be redeployed to assembly, painting, palletising, or inspection by changing only the **end-effector** and the program. A hard-automated machine is purpose-built for a single product.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two distinguishing features.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Retest]`
**Differentiate between "Repeatability" and "Accuracy" of an industrial robot. Why is the former more important in a pick-and-place cell?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Definition | Importance in Pick-and-Place |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Repeatability** | The ability of the robot to return to a *previously taught* position consistently over many cycles. Measured as the radius of a sphere within which the end-effector will land. | **Highly important** — the robot is taught a fixed "pick" point and a fixed "place" point; it must revisit these *same* points thousands of times, not different absolute points. |
| **Accuracy** | The ability of the robot to move to a *commanded* point in absolute world coordinates. Depends on calibration. | Less critical — the points are taught, not commanded absolutely. |

**Numerical example:** A SCARA robot with $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$ repeatability but $\pm 1 \text{ mm}$ accuracy is *ideal* for pick-and-place because it will reliably return to the taught point every time. Hence, repeatability dominates.

*(1.5 marks for definitions, 1.5 marks for the explanation.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) **and** (b), typically 7 marks each. **Internal choice**: you answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**"Robotic systems are a cornerstone of modern industrial automation." In the context of this statement:**

#### (a) **(7 marks)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a robotic work-cell**, clearly identifying the role of the **manipulator, controller, end-effector, and sensor subsystem**. Explain how this cell fits into the **ISA-95 automation pyramid**.

#### (b) **(7 marks)** Compare the **Articulated (6R), SCARA, Cartesian, and Delta** robot configurations under the following heads: (i) joint sequence and DOF, (ii) typical workspace shape, (iii) one industrial application. Justify which configuration is best suited for **high-speed pharmaceutical vial pick-and-place**.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Functional Architecture & Automation Pyramid (7 marks)

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                    │   LEVEL 4: ERP (SAP/Oracle)      │   (1.5 mark)
                    │   Order & Production Scheduling  │
                    └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                     │
                    ┌────────────────▼─────────────────┐
                    │   LEVEL 3: MES                  │
                    │   Work-order Dispatch            │   (0.5 mark)
                    └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                     │
                    ┌────────────────▼─────────────────┐
                    │   LEVEL 2: SCADA / HMI           │
                    │   Cell Monitoring & Recipe       │   (0.5 mark)
                    └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                     │
                    ┌────────────────▼─────────────────┐
                    │   LEVEL 1: ROBOT CONTROLLER      │   (0.5 mark)
                    │   CPU + Trajectory + Servo Loop  │
                    └────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                     │
        ┌────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┐
        │                       LEVEL 0: ROBOT CELL               │
        │  ┌────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐    │
        │  │ MANIPULATOR│  │ END-EFFECTOR │  │   SENSORS    │    │ (3 marks)
        │  │ (Arm+Links │  │  (Gripper /  │  │  (Vision,    │    │
        │  │  +Joints)  │  │  Tool/Sucker)│  │  Force,      │    │
        │  │            │  │              │  │  Encoders)   │    │
        │  └────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘    │
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**2. Role of Each Subsystem (3 marks):**
- **Manipulator:** Provides the mechanical structure (links + joints) that physically moves the end-effector through the workspace. Contains the actuators (servo motors) and gear trains.
- **Controller:** The "brain" — executes the user program, performs forward/inverse kinematics, generates trajectory setpoints, and closes the position/velocity servo loop typically at 1–4 kHz.
- **End-Effector (EOAT):** The "hand" — directly interfaces with the work-piece. Grippers, suction cups, welding torches, or specialised tools.
- **Sensor Subsystem:** Provides feedback. *Proprioceptive* (encoders, tachometers) for internal state; *exteroceptive* (vision, force) for external environment.

**3. ISA-95 Pyramid Fit (1 mark):**
The robot cell operates at **L0 (Field Devices)** under the supervision of L1 (Controller), which receives recipes from L2 (SCADA), work-orders from L3 (MES), and business schedules from L4 (ERP). This hierarchical structure is the modern Industry 4.0 paradigm.

---

#### Part (b) — Robot Configuration Comparison (7 marks)

| Configuration | Joint Sequence | DOF | Workspace Shape | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Articulated (6R)** | R-R-R-R-R-R | **6** | Near-spherical, complex | Welding, painting, general assembly |
| **SCARA** | R-R-P (with passive vertical compliance) | **4** | Cylindrical, horizontally flat | PCB assembly, screwdriving |
| **Cartesian** | P-P-P | **3** | Rectangular box | Gantry pick-place, 3D printing |
| **Delta** | 3 × parallelogram linkages driven by P joints | **3 or 4** | Dome-shaped, flat work area | Ultra-fast packaging, pharma |

*(2 marks for the table, 1 mark for joint sequences and DOF.)*

**Justification for High-Speed Pharmaceutical Vial Pick-and-Place (4 marks):**

The **Delta robot** is the optimal choice for the following reasons:
1. **Speed:** The parallelogram linkage keeps the heavy actuators mounted on the **fixed frame above** the work area, leaving only lightweight carbon-fibre arms to move. This dramatically reduces inertia and enables cycle times as low as **0.15 s per pick** — up to 240 picks/minute.
2. **Hygiene:** No lubricants drip onto the work area; the open-frame top is easy to sterilise — a critical GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) requirement in pharmaceuticals.
3. **Repeatability:** Typical $\pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$ repeatability — sufficient for vial handling where tolerances are loose.
4. **Workspace:** A flat, dome-shaped work envelope of ~1 m diameter is ideal for indexing conveyors below.

The **6R articulated arm is rejected** because its higher inertia and longer reach cycle yield cycle times of 1–2 s — too slow. The **SCARA is acceptable** for moderate speeds but cannot match Delta throughput. The **Cartesian gantry** is bulky and offers lower acceleration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1, Alternative Choice]`

**"The performance of an industrial robot is quantified by standardised metrics." Critically analyse this statement with reference to the metrics of payload, reach, repeatability, accuracy, and cycle time.**

#### (a) **(7 marks)** Define each of the five metrics, give their **SI / engineering units**, and state **one typical numerical value** for a contemporary 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., KUKA KR 16 R2010).

#### (b) **(7 marks)** A cylindrical robot has a radial reach of $r = 1.2 \text{ m}$ and vertical stroke $h = 2.5 \text{ m}$. Compute (i) the working envelope volume, and (ii) the hourly throughput if the pick-and-place cycle time is $T_c = 1.5 \text{ s}$. Comment on the engineering significance of the result.

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Five Performance Metrics (7 marks)

| # | Metric | Definition | Units | Typical Value (KUKA KR 16 R2010) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Payload** | Max mass the robot can carry at rated performance, including EOAT | kg | **16 kg** |
| 2 | **Reach** | Max distance from base centre to flange centre | mm | **2013 mm (≈ 2.0 m)** |
| 3 | **Repeatability** | Smallest sphere radius within which the robot returns to a taught pose | mm | **±0.04 mm** |
| 4 | **Accuracy** | Closeness of a commanded position to true world position | mm | **±0.5 mm (varies with calibration)** |
| 5 | **Cycle Time** | Time to complete a benchmark pick-and-place cycle (often VDI 2860) | s | **≈ 0.7 s for standard cycle** |

*(1 mark per row, with 2 marks extra for full-table coherence and unit correctness.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> Students frequently **swap accuracy and repeatability** in definitions. Remember: *Repeatability* is a self-referencing property (return to a taught point), while *Accuracy* is an absolute property (return to a *true* point). Also, cycle time is often quoted without specifying the **VDI 2860 benchmark** (a 25 mm up, 305 mm across, 25 mm down motion at 100% payload) — writing a number without the benchmark loses **0.5 marks** in a careful valuation.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**(i) Working Envelope Volume (3 marks):**

For a cylindrical robot, the envelope is a right circular cylinder of radius $r$ and height $h$:

$$
V = \pi r^{2} h
$$

Substitute $r = 1.2 \text{ m}$ and $h = 2.5 \text{ m}$:

$$
V = \pi \times (1.2)^{2} \times 2.5
$$

$$
V = \pi \times 1.44 \times 2.5
$$

$$
V = \pi \times 3.60
$$

$$
V \approx 3.60 \times 3.1416 \approx 11.31 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**(ii) Hourly Throughput (3 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{1.5} = 2400 \text{ picks/hour}
$$

**Engineering Significance (1 mark):**

The envelope of **11.31 m³** is the spatial region in which the end-effector can be placed; any work-piece or fixture must lie entirely within this volume. The throughput of **2400 picks/hour** corresponds to a part-handling capacity suitable for medium-scale manufacturing (e.g., machine tending of a small CNC cell). However, since this is a *theoretical* value, in practice an OEE factor of 80–85% should be applied, yielding ~1920–2040 effective picks per hour — a realistic figure for a small-scale industrial deployment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Numerical Questions:**
> - **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing $11.31$ without "$\text{m}^{3}$") loses 0.5 marks.
> - **Using $\pi = 3.14$ vs. $3.1416$** — be consistent; KTU accepts either, but do not switch mid-calculation.
> - **Not applying OEE** in throughput problems — the question says "comment on significance," so an unmodified number is a lost opportunity for a higher cognitive-level (Analyse) mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> Use this checklist as your **last-hour revision summary** before the KTU exam.

- [ ] **ISO 8373:2021 Definition:** automatically controlled, re-programmable, multipurpose manipulator in **3 or more axes** — this exact phrasing earns full marks.
- [ ] **Three Cardinal Properties of an Industrial Robot:** *automatic, reprogrammable, multipurpose.* All three must be present; missing one = it's NOT a robot.
- [ ] **The 5 Robot Configurations:** **C**artesian (PPP), **C**ylindrical (RPP), **P**olar (RRP), **S**CARA (RRP), **A**rticulated (RRRRRR). Mnemonic: *"Can Cats Play Sleeping Artfully?"*
- [ ] **DOF for free rigid body in 3D = 6** (3 translation + 3 rotation). Any robot that cannot arbitrarily position AND orient its end-effector has < 6 DOF.
- [ ] **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy.** Repeatability is taught-point-to-taught-point consistency; accuracy is commanded-point-to-true-point closeness.
- [ ] **Gruebler's Spatial Formula:** $M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{k=1}^{5} (6-k)j_k$ — verify any linkage's DOF.
- [ ] **Pick-and-Place Throughput:** $N = 3600 / T_c$ parts/hour, with OEE factor ~85% in real-world answers.
- [ ] **Workspace volume formulas:** Cylindrical robot $V = \pi r^{2} h$, Spherical robot $V \approx \frac{2}{3} \pi r^{3}$.
- [ ] **Delta robot** = high-speed packaging; **SCARA** = vertical assembly; **6R articulated** = general-purpose (welding, painting); **Cartesian** = gantry, 3D printing.
- [ ] **ISA-95 Pyramid:** ERP (L4) → MES (L3) → SCADA (L2) → Controller (L1) → Robot + Sensors (L0). Always mention the pyramid in a "system-level" question.
- [ ] **Joint Torque Equation:** $\tau = m g d + I \alpha$ — gravity + inertial contributions. Multiply by a safety factor of 1.5–2.0 for motor sizing.
- [ ] **Servo loop update rate** is typically **1 kHz to 4 kHz** in modern industrial controllers.
- [ ] **"Re-programmable" is the keyword** that distinguishes a robot from a CNC machine — KTU examiners love this distinction.
- [ ] **Actuator types:** Electric (precision), Hydraulic (heavy payload), Pneumatic (simple/low-cost). Choose based on payload and accuracy needs.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam-Day Strategy:** For 14-mark answers, **always draw the block diagram first** (it secures 2–3 marks even if your text is incomplete), then bullet-point the textual explanation, and end with a real-world application example. KTU valuators follow a structured marking scheme — give them the keywords in a clear visual hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotic Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Components** refer to the integrated set of mechanical, electrical, electronic, and computational subsystems that together constitute an industrial robotic system. As per KTU 2024 syllabus, these components are classified into **five primary functional blocks**: (1) **Manipulator / Mechanical Structure**, (2) **End Effector**, (3) **Actuators & Drive System**, (4) **Sensors**, and (5) **Controller with Power Supply**.

A robotic assembly and material handling system is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks. The architecture is functionally analogous to a **human arm with a brain**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Human Robot Metaphor)

Imagine a human worker performing a task on an assembly line — say, picking up a bolt and inserting it into a hole:

| Human Part | Robotic Equivalent | Function |
|------------|-------------------|----------|
| Arm + Joints | Manipulator (Links + Joints) | Reach and positioning |
| Hand | End Effector / Gripper | Grasping and holding the object |
| Muscles | Actuators (Pneumatic / Hydraulic / Electric) | Provide force and motion |
| Eyes / Skin | Sensors (Vision, Force, Tactile) | Perceive environment |
| Brain + Nerves | Controller + Bus Network | Process information & issue commands |
| Heart (blood) | Power Supply + Energy Lines | Distribute power throughout |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Beginners:** A robot is essentially a **digital arm** that can be told what to do, can sense its surroundings, and can adjust its actions accordingly. The five components above act in a closed loop — sensor data flows to the controller, which commands the actuators, which move the manipulator, which repositions the end effector.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Metrics for Robotic Components:**
> - **Payload Capacity:** Typically ranges from **3 kg** (light-duty) to **1000+ kg** (heavy-duty) in industrial robots.
> - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Standard industrial manipulators have **4 to 6 DOF**.
> - **Repeatability:** High-precision robots achieve **±0.02 mm**; standard industrial robots achieve **±0.1 mm**.
> - **Operating Speed:** Reach speeds of **up to 2 m/s** for Cartesian or gantry robots.
> - **Reach Envelope:** Articulated robots typically have **0.5 m to 3.0 m** reach radius.

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlight Callout

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Weightage Focus:** Questions on Robotic Components typically appear as **Part A (3 marks)** for naming/definition of components, and as **Part B (7–14 marks)** for detailed comparison (e.g., Hydraulic vs. Pneumatic actuators) and block diagram explanations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Robotic Architecture

### 2.1.1 Manipulator (Mechanical Structure)
- **Definition:** A series of rigid links connected by joints, forming a kinematic chain that terminates at the end effector.
- **Joint Types:**
  - **Revolute (R) Joint:** Rotational motion about an axis (1 DOF).
  - **Prismatic (P) Joint:** Linear sliding motion along an axis (1 DOF).
- **Common Configurations:**
  - **Cartesian (PPP)** — 3 prismatic joints, used in pick-and-place.
  - **Cylindrical (RPP)** — one revolute + two prismatic.
  - **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** — two revolute + one prismatic.
  - **Articulated (RRR)** — three revolute joints (e.g., **6-axis ABB / KUKA arms**).
  - **SCARA (RRP + vertical R)** — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm, optimized for vertical assembly.
  - **Delta** — Parallel robot using three parallel arms, very high speed.
  - **Gantry (3P + 1R)** — Used in material handling over large areas.

### 2.1.2 End Effector
- **Definition:** The device attached to the wrist of the manipulator that interacts with the workpiece.
- **Two main categories:**
  - **Grippers:** Mechanical jaws, vacuum cups, magnetic grippers, adhesive grippers.
  - **Tools:** Welding torches, paint sprayers, drills, cutting tools.
- **Selection depends on:** part geometry, weight, surface condition, and cycle time.

### 2.1.3 Actuators & Drive System
- **Actuator:** A device that converts energy (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic) into mechanical motion.
- **Three Industrial Types:**

| Type | Energy Source | Strength | Limitation |
|------|---------------|----------|------------|
| **Electric** (Servo/Stepper) | Electricity | Precise, clean, controllable | Limited payload |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized oil | High force, heavy duty | Leaks, noisy, maintenance |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | Fast, simple, cheap | Low precision, compressible |

- **Drive Mechanisms:** Harmonic drives, planetary gearheads, rack-and-pinion, ball screws, lead screws, cycloidal drives.

### 2.1.4 Sensors
- **Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors:**
  - Encoders (position, velocity)
  - Resolvers
  - Potentiometers
  - Tachometers
  - Current/Voltage sensors
- **External (Exteroceptive) Sensors:**
  - Vision cameras (2D / 3D)
  - Proximity sensors (inductive, capacitive, ultrasonic)
  - Force/Torque sensors
  - Tactile sensors
  - Laser range finders

### 2.1.5 Controller & Power Supply
- **Controller:** The "brain" — typically a microcomputer / PLC / industrial PC running real-time OS. Handles trajectory planning, inverse kinematics, sensor fusion, and I/O.
- **Power Supply:** Provides regulated DC and AC voltages to motors, sensors, and logic circuits. Often includes UPS / battery backup for safe shutdown.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Formulas for Robotic Components**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= I \alpha + b \omega + T_L \quad &\text{(Motor torque equation)} \\
\theta(t) &= \frac{1}{2}\alpha t^2 + \omega_0 t + \theta_0 \quad &\text{(Angular displacement)} \\
J_{eq} &= J_m + \frac{J_{load}}{n^2} \quad &\text{(Reflected inertia at motor shaft)} \\
T_{eq} &= T_m + \frac{T_{load}}{n \cdot \eta} \quad &\text{(Reflected torque, n = gear ratio, } \eta \text{ = efficiency)} \\
V_{max} &= \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t} \quad &\text{(End effector max velocity)} \\
F_{grip} &= \mu \cdot N \quad &\text{(Gripper holding force, } \mu = \text{ friction coef.)} \\
P_{hyd} &= \frac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{overall}} \quad &\text{(Hydraulic power requirement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Cheat-Sheet Table (NO pipe characters in math — using `\vert` / `\mid`)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Notes for KTU |
|--------|----------|--------------|----------------|
| $\tau$ | Torque | N·m | Required motor torque |
| $I$ | Moment of inertia | kg·m² | Reflected to motor shaft |
| $\alpha$ | Angular acceleration | rad/s² | From trajectory profile |
| $n$ | Gear reduction ratio | dimensionless | $n \geq 1$ for reducers |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | dimensionless | Gripper-object interface |
| $\eta$ | Efficiency | $\vert 0,1 \mid$ | Gear / motor efficiency |
| $DOF$ | Degrees of freedom | count | Manipulator DOF |
| $P$ | Payload | kg | Manufacturer rated |
| $R$ | Repeatability | mm | ± value |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Articulated 6-axis robots with electric servo actuators for welding, painting, and assembly.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** SCARA robots with vision-guided pick-and-place for PCB assembly.
- **E-commerce Warehousing:** Cartesian gantry robots for parcel sorting and material handling.
- **Food & Pharma:** Delta robots with vacuum end effectors for high-speed packaging.
- **Heavy Forging:** Hydraulic-actuated robots for handling red-hot billets weighing **hundreds of kg**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Motor Torque Required to Drive a Joint

**Problem Setup (KTU Style):**
A robotic joint must accelerate a load of moment of inertia $J_L$ (about the joint axis) from rest to angular velocity $\omega$ in time $t_a$, and then hold it against a constant load torque $T_L$. The motor is connected to the joint through a gear reducer with ratio $n$ and efficiency $\eta$. Derive the **required motor torque** during the acceleration phase.

**Given:**
- Load moment of inertia: $J_L = 0.5 \text{ kg·m}^2$
- Gear ratio: $n = 50$
- Gear efficiency: $\eta = 0.9$
- Required joint speed: $\omega = 1.5 \text{ rad/s}$
- Acceleration time: $t_a = 0.2 \text{ s}$
- Load torque at joint: $T_L = 20 \text{ N·m}$
- Motor inertia: $J_m = 0.002 \text{ kg·m}^2$

### Step 1 — Write the Equation of Motion at the Joint
The torque required at the joint is the sum of inertia torque and load torque.

$$
T_{joint} = J_L \alpha + T_L
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Angular Acceleration
The joint accelerates from $\omega_0 = 0$ to $\omega$ in time $t_a$.

$$
\alpha = \frac{\omega - \omega_0}{t_a} = \frac{1.5 - 0}{0.2} = 7.5 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Inertia Torque at the Joint

$$
T_{inertia} = J_L \alpha = 0.5 \times 7.5 = 3.75 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Joint Torque

$$
T_{joint} = 3.75 + 20 = 23.75 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 5 — Reflect to the Motor Shaft
Because the gear reducer with ratio $n$ (n:1 reducer) multiplies speed by $n$ and divides torque by $n$, while losses are accounted for by $\eta$:

$$
T_m = \frac{T_{joint}}{n \cdot \eta} = \frac{23.75}{50 \times 0.9} = 0.5278 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 6 — Add Motor Inertia Reflected at the Motor Shaft

$$
J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{n^2} = 0.002 + \frac{0.5}{2500} = 0.002 + 0.0002 = 0.0022 \text{ kg·m}^2
$$

### Step 7 — Motor Acceleration (Reflected $\alpha$ at motor)

$$
\alpha_m = n \cdot \alpha = 50 \times 7.5 = 375 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

### Step 8 — Total Motor Torque (during acceleration phase)

$$
T_{m,total} = J_{eq} \cdot \alpha_m + T_m = 0.0022 \times 375 + 0.5278
$$

$$
T_{m,total} = 0.825 + 0.5278 = 1.3528 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 9 — Convert to mN·m (typical motor spec units)

$$
T_{m,total} = 1352.8 \text{ mN·m} \approx 1.35 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The motor must deliver **~1.35 N·m of continuous torque** during acceleration, but the **joint sees 23.75 N·m** — this is the role of the gear reducer: torque amplification at the cost of speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gripper Holding Force for a Vertical Lifting Application

**Problem Setup:**
A vacuum-cup gripper is used to lift a flat glass plate of mass $m = 5$ kg vertically. The coefficient of static friction between the cup seal and glass is $\mu = 0.4$. The safety factor is $SF = 2.5$. Find the required normal holding force $N$ and the minimum cup area $A$ if the available vacuum pressure is $P_v = 60$ kPa (gauge).

### Step 1 — Weight of the Object

$$
W = m g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Safety Factor

$$
F_{req} = SF \times W = 2.5 \times 49.05 = 122.625 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3 — Relation Between Friction and Normal Force

$$
F_{req} = \mu \cdot N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = \frac{F_{req}}{\mu} = \frac{122.625}{0.4} = 306.56 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 4 — Cup Area from Vacuum Pressure

$$
P_v = \frac{N}{A} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A = \frac{N}{P_v} = \frac{306.56}{60 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
A = 5.11 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 51.1 \text{ cm}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Equivalent Circular Cup Diameter

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad d = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 51.1}{\pi}} \approx 8.07 \text{ cm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A single vacuum cup of diameter **~8 cm** at **60 kPa** vacuum can safely lift the 5 kg glass plate with a 2.5× safety factor.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Actuator Selection)

| Selection Criterion | Electric (Servo) | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---------------------|------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Position Accuracy** | High ($\pm 0.01$ mm) | Medium | Low |
| **Force/Power Density** | Medium | Very High | Low |
| **Speed** | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Cleanliness** | Clean | Risk of oil leak | Clean |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Cost (initial)** | High | Very High | Low |
| **Typical Application** | Assembly, welding, painting | Heavy forging, presses | Pick-and-place, packaging |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Robotic System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply Unit] --> B[Controller CPU + Memory]
    B --> C[Drive Electronics]
    C --> D[Actuators Servo Hydraulic Pneumatic]
    D --> E[Manipulator Links and Joints]
    E --> F[End Effector Gripper Tool]
    G[Internal Sensors Encoders Resolvers] --> B
    H[External Sensors Vision Force Proximity] --> B
    I[Operator Teach Pendant HMI Panel] --> B
    J[Workpiece Environment] --> H
    E --> J
    F --> J

    classDef power fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef motion fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef sense fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    classDef user fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef world fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#424242,color:#000

    class A power
    class B logic
    class C,D,E,F motion
    class G,H sense
    class I user
    class J world
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Manipulator Kinematic Chain (6-DOF Articulated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base1[Base Mount] --> joint1[J1 Revolute Waist]
    joint1 --> link1[Link 1 Shoulder]
    link1 --> joint2[J2 Revolute Shoulder]
    joint2 --> link2[Link 2 Upper Arm]
    link2 --> joint3[J3 Revolute Elbow]
    joint3 --> link3[Link 3 Forearm]
    link3 --> joint4[J4 Revolute Wrist Roll]
    joint4 --> joint5[J5 Revolute Wrist Pitch]
    joint5 --> joint6[J6 Revolute Wrist Yaw]
    joint6 --> tool1[End Effector TCP]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Sensor Fusion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    world1[Robot Workspace Environment] --> vision1[2D 3D Vision Camera]
    world1 --> prox1[Proximity Inductive Capacitive Ultrasonic]
    world1 --> force1[Force Torque Sensor 6 axis]
    world1 --> tactile1[Tactile Sensor Array]

    manip1[Manipulator Internal State] --> enc1[Optical Encoders]
    manip1 --> res1[Resolvers]
    manip1 --> tacho1[Tachometers]
    manip1 --> current1[Current Sensors]

    vision1 --> fusion1[Sensor Fusion Module Kalman Filter]
    prox1 --> fusion1
    force1 --> fusion1
    tactile1 --> fusion1
    enc1 --> fusion1
    res1 --> fusion1
    tacho1 --> fusion1
    current1 --> fusion1

    fusion1 --> ctrl1[Controller Trajectory Planning Inverse Kinematics]
    ctrl1 --> drive1[Drive Electronics]
    drive1 --> act1[Actuators Move Robot]
    act1 --> manip1
```

## 4.4 Component Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start Component Selection] --> q1{Payload of Workpiece}
    q1 -->|Less than 5 kg| e1[Electric Servo or Pneumatic]
    q1 -->|5 to 50 kg| e2[Electric Servo with Harmonic Drive]
    q1 -->|50 to 500 kg| e3[Hydraulic Actuator]
    q1 -->|More than 500 kg| e4[Heavy Hydraulic with Counterbalance]

    e1 --> q2{Cycle Time}
    e2 --> q2
    e3 --> q2
    e4 --> q2

    q2 -->|Less than 1 s| f1[Delta or SCARA Parallel Kinematics]
    q2 -->|1 to 5 s| f2[Articulated 6 axis]
    q2 -->|More than 5 s| f3[Cartesian or Gantry]

    f1 --> q3{Workpiece Geometry}
    f2 --> q3
    f3 --> q3

    q3 -->|Flat Smooth| g1[Vacuum Cup Gripper]
    q3 -->|Cylindrical| g2[Mechanical 3 jaw Gripper]
    q3 -->|Irregular| g3[Adaptive Fingers with Tactile Feedback]
    q3 -->|Magnetic Material| g4[Electromagnetic Gripper]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "End Effector" in the context of industrial robotics. List any four types of end effectors. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An end effector is a device attached to the wrist of a robotic manipulator that interacts with the workpiece or environment. It is the "hand" of the robot.
>
> **Four types (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Mechanical gripper (two-jaw / three-jaw)
> 2. Vacuum cup gripper
> 3. Electromagnetic gripper
> 4. Welding torch / Tool-type end effector

### Question 2: Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with two examples each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Proprioceptive Sensors (1.5 marks):** These measure the internal state of the robot — joint position, velocity, and motor current. Examples: **optical encoders** and **resolvers**.
>
> **Exteroceptive Sensors (1.5 marks):** These perceive the external environment — distance, force, images. Examples: **vision camera** and **force/torque sensor**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): Robotic Component Architecture and Actuator Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major components of an industrial robotic system used in material handling. State the function of each component.**

**Model Solution:**

A robotic material handling system consists of **five major functional blocks** (Block Diagram — refer Section 4.1).

1. **Manipulator / Mechanical Structure (1.5 marks):**
   A series of rigid links connected by joints (revolute/prismatic). It provides the physical reach and positioning of the end effector. Common configurations: Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Delta.

2. **End Effector (1 mark):**
   The device mounted on the wrist (TCP — Tool Centre Point) that directly handles the workpiece. Examples: grippers, vacuum cups, magnetic grippers.

3. **Actuators and Drive System (1.5 marks):**
   Convert electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into mechanical motion. Includes servo motors, harmonic drives, planetary gearheads, ball screws.

4. **Sensors (1.5 marks):**
   Provide feedback to the controller. Internal sensors (encoders) measure joint state. External sensors (vision, force) measure environment.

5. **Controller and Power Supply (1.5 marks):**
   Controller runs real-time algorithms (inverse kinematics, trajectory planning) and issues commands to drives. Power supply distributes regulated energy to all subsystems.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare hydraulic, pneumatic, and electric actuators in industrial robots. Mention one typical application for each.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Energy source** | Pressurized oil | Compressed air | Electrical |
| **Force / Power** | Very high | Low | Medium |
| **Precision** | Medium (with servo valves) | Low | High |
| **Speed** | Medium | Very high | High |
| **Cleanliness** | Oil leak risk | Clean | Clean |
| **Maintenance** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Cost (initial)** | Very high | Low | High |
| **Typical application** | Heavy forging, die casting, large press tending (1.5 marks) | Pick-and-place, packaging, light assembly (1.5 marks) | Welding, painting, precision assembly, PCB handling (1.5 marks) |

**Selection logic (1 mark):**
For payload above 50 kg with high force → Hydraulic. For high-speed packaging with low precision → Pneumatic. For high precision with clean operation → Electric.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Sensor Systems and End Effector Design `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the classification of sensors used in industrial robots. Discuss the working principle of a 6-axis force/torque sensor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Sensors (4 marks):**
Sensors are broadly classified into two categories:

**A. Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors:**
- Optical/Incremental Encoders — measure joint angular position using slotted disk and photodiode.
- Resolvers — measure absolute angular position using electromagnetic coupling.
- Potentiometers — convert angular position to voltage.
- Tachometers — measure angular velocity (V/1000 rpm).
- Current / Voltage sensors — monitor motor electrical state.

**B. External (Exteroceptive) Sensors:**
- 2D / 3D Vision cameras — capture image data for object recognition and pose estimation.
- Proximity sensors — inductive (metal), capacitive (any material), ultrasonic (distance).
- Force / Torque sensors — measure interaction forces.
- Tactile sensor arrays — detect contact pressure distribution.

**Working Principle of 6-axis Force/Torque Sensor (3 marks):**
A 6-axis F/T sensor mounted between the robot wrist and the end effector uses a **cross-beam strain-gauge structure**. Four strain gauges arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** are bonded to each of the internal beams. When the end effector experiences any external force $F_x, F_y, F_z$ or moment $M_x, M_y, M_z$, the beams deform, and the strain gauges produce proportional voltage outputs. These six outputs are decoded via a calibration matrix to give the full wrench vector. Typical sensing range: ±500 N force, ±50 N·m torque, with **1–2 % nonlinearity**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A robot gripper is to lift a steel block of mass 12 kg using a mechanical jaw gripper. The coefficient of friction between jaw and block is 0.3. The safety factor is 2.0. Find (i) the minimum gripping force, and (ii) the minimum jaw contact area if the allowable contact pressure is 1.5 MPa. Assume gravity g = 9.81 m/s².**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Minimum Gripping Force (3.5 marks):**

Weight of the block:
$$
W = m g = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N}
$$

Required friction force (with safety factor):
$$
F_{fric,req} = SF \times W = 2.0 \times 117.72 = 235.44 \text{ N}
$$

From $F_{fric} = \mu \cdot N$, the normal gripping force per jaw pair:
$$
2N = \frac{F_{fric,req}}{\mu} = \frac{235.44}{0.3} = 784.8 \text{ N}
$$
$$
N = 392.4 \text{ N per jaw}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Contact Area (3.5 marks):**

Allowable contact pressure:
$$
\sigma_{allow} = 1.5 \text{ MPa} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

Contact area on one jaw:
$$
A = \frac{N}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{392.4}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 2.616 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A \approx 2.62 \text{ cm}^2 \text{ per jaw}
$$

Total contact area (both jaws): $2A \approx 5.24 \text{ cm}^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Safety Factor (SF):** Many students compute $F_{grip} = W/\mu$ directly. In KTU papers, **SF is almost always applied** — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Single vs. Pair of Jaws:** $N$ is the force on **one jaw pair** — both jaws together provide $2\mu N$. Confusing this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit Conversion:** Mixing N/mm² with MPa, or using $g = 10$ instead of 9.81 — losing 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Misnaming "Load Torque" as "Motor Torque":** In actuator derivations, students often forget the gear ratio $n$ and efficiency $\eta$ — losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Block Diagram without proper labelling:** Drawing the block diagram of robot components without arrow directions and functional labels loses **1 full mark** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Robotic Components (Module 1)**
> - [x] **Five main components:** Manipulator, End Effector, Actuators + Drives, Sensors, Controller + Power Supply.
> - [x] **Joint types:** Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P).
> - [x] **Manipulator configurations:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA, Delta, Gantry.
> - [x] **Industrial DOF range:** 4 to 6 for most robotic arms.
> - [x] **Actuator trio:** Hydraulic (high force), Pneumatic (high speed, low precision), Electric (high precision, clean).
> - [x] **Sensors — 2 types:** Proprioceptive (encoders, resolvers) and Exteroceptive (vision, force, proximity).
> - [x] **Key formula 1:** $\tau = I\alpha + b\omega + T_L$ (motor torque equation).
> - [x] **Key formula 2:** $F_{grip} = \mu \cdot N$ and apply $SF$ (usually 1.5 to 3.0).
> - [x] **Key formula 3:** $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L / n^2$ and $T_m = T_L / (n \cdot \eta)$.
> - [x] **Key formula 4:** $P_{hyd} = F \cdot v / \eta_{overall}$.
> - [x] **Block diagram must include:** arrow directions, power flow, sensor feedback loop.
> - [x] **Selection rule of thumb:** Payload > 50 kg → Hydraulic; high speed packaging → Pneumatic; precision assembly → Electric.
> - [x] **Always apply Safety Factor (SF)** in gripper and lifting calculations.
> - [x] **Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** unless the problem explicitly states $g = 10$.
> - [x] **Use $\eta$ for efficiency** in any gear / drive calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators, Sensors, and Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Actuators, Sensors, and Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Actuators, Sensors, and Controllers — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 The Triad of Robotics: Definition and Context

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **OERUT724 — Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems**, the three pillars that bring a robot from a static mechanical structure to an intelligent, moving, decision-making machine are **Actuators**, **Sensors**, and **Controllers**. Together, they form the *Perception–Decision–Action* loop that defines modern robotic automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> - **Actuator:** A power-conversion device that transforms electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or other forms of energy into mechanical motion (linear or rotary) to execute a robotic task.
> - **Sensor:** A transducer that detects a physical quantity (position, force, light, temperature) and converts it into an electrical signal readable by the controller.
> - **Controller:** The "brain" of the robotic system — a hardware/software unit that processes sensor inputs, compares them to desired references, and issues corrective commands to the actuators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine the human body as a robot:

- **Sensors** = the **five senses** (eyes, skin, ears). They gather information about the world.
- **Controller** = the **brain + spinal cord**. It processes the sensed information and decides what to do.
- **Actuators** = the **muscles**. They actually *move* the limbs, fingers, and joints to perform actions.

Without sensors, the robot is *blind*; without a controller, it is *brain-dead*; without actuators, it is *paralyzed*. All three must work in harmony.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric to Remember:**
> - A typical industrial robot arm has **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, each driven by its own actuator.
> - Sensor feedback resolution in modern robots is on the order of **micrometers (μm)** for position and **millinewtons (mN)** for force.

## 1.3 The Perception–Decision–Action Loop

The complete operating cycle of a robotic material-handling system follows this canonical loop:

1. **Perceive** — Sensors measure the current state (joint angles, gripper force, object position).
2. **Decide** — Controller computes the error between the measured state and the desired state.
3. **Act** — Controller sends signals to actuators to reduce the error.
4. **Repeat** at high frequency (typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz** in industrial controllers).

This closed-loop operation is the very essence of *feedback control*, a topic deeply tied to KTU's B.Tech Control Systems and Mechatronics courses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Actuators — The "Muscles" of the Robot

Actuators are broadly classified by the energy domain they use.

### 2.1.1 Electric Actuators (Most Common in KTU Lab Settings)

Electric actuators use electromagnetic force to produce motion.

**Types:**
- **DC Servo Motors** — Excellent speed control via PWM; used in small-to-medium robots.
- **AC Servo Motors / Brushless DC (BLDC)** — Industrial standard; high torque-to-weight ratio.
- **Stepper Motors** — Open-loop position control; discrete angular steps (commonly **1.8° per step**, i.e., **200 steps/revolution**).
- **Servo Motors with Encoders** — Closed-loop position/velocity control.
- **Piezoelectric Actuators** — Sub-micrometer precision for micro-robotics.

**Key Equations:**

The torque produced by a DC servo motor is proportional to armature current:

$$\tau_m = K_t \cdot i_a$$

where $\tau_m$ is motor torque (N·m), $K_t$ is the torque constant (N·m/A), and $i_a$ is armature current (A).

The back-EMF generated when the motor rotates is:

$$e_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

where $K_e$ is the back-EMF constant (V·s/rad) and $\omega_m$ is the motor's angular velocity (rad/s).

> [!NOTE]
> **SI Units Rule:** $K_t$ (SI) and $K_e$ (SI) are numerically equal when consistent SI units are used. This is a frequently tested KTU short-answer concept.

### 2.1.2 Hydraulic Actuators

Hydraulic actuators use incompressible fluid (typically oil) under high pressure to produce very large forces — ideal for heavy material handling in automotive and aerospace assembly lines.

**Force produced by a hydraulic cylinder:**

$$F = P \cdot A$$

where $P$ is the fluid pressure (Pa) and $A$ is the piston cross-sectional area (m²).

**Typical operating pressure:** **70 bar to 350 bar** (7 MPa to 35 MPa).

**Advantages:** Very high power-to-weight ratio, self-lubricating, holds position without energy.
**Disadvantages:** Leakage, noisy, requires pump and reservoir.

### 2.1.3 Pneumatic Actuators

Pneumatic actuators use compressed air (typically at **6 bar** standard pressure). They are clean, fast, and cheap — perfect for pick-and-place material handling.

**Force equation (same form as hydraulic):**

$$F = P \cdot A$$

The fundamental difference from hydraulics is the *compressibility* of air, which makes pneumatic systems less precise but much lighter.

### 2.1.4 Comparison Matrix (MUST memorize for KTU)

| Parameter | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Power Source | Electrical energy | Pressurized oil | Compressed air |
| Typical Pressure/Voltage | 12 V – 480 V | 70 – 350 bar | 4 – 8 bar |
| Force Output | Low–High | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Positioning Accuracy | High (μm) | Medium (mm) | Low (mm) |
| Speed | Medium–High | Medium | High |
| Maintenance | Low | High (leakage) | Low |
| KTU Application | Precision assembly | Heavy payload handling | Pick-and-place, packaging |

## 2.2 Sensors — The "Senses" of the Robot

Sensors convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.

### 2.2.1 Position Sensors

- **Potentiometer:** Resistive element; output voltage proportional to slider position.
- **Optical Encoder (Incremental):** Pulses counted to give relative position.
- **Optical Encoder (Absolute):** Unique Gray code for every angular position; survives power loss.
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer):** Inductive sensor for linear displacement with very high accuracy.

**Encoder Resolution Formula:**

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{360°}{N}$$

where $N$ is the number of pulses per revolution (PPR) on the encoder disc.

### 2.2.2 Velocity Sensors

- **Tachogenerator:** A small DC generator whose output voltage is proportional to rotational speed.
- **Incremental encoder with frequency measurement:** Counts pulses per unit time.

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt}, \quad \omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

### 2.2.3 Force / Torque Sensors

- **Strain Gauge:** Resistance changes with deformation; arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**.
- **Piezoelectric Force Sensor:** Generates charge when force is applied.

**Gauge factor (G):**

$$G = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the strain, $R$ is the nominal resistance, and $\Delta R$ is the resistance change.

### 2.2.4 Proximity and Range Sensors

- **Inductive Proximity Sensor:** Detects metallic objects only.
- **Capacitive Proximity Sensor:** Detects both metallic and non-metallic objects.
- **Ultrasonic Sensor:** Uses time-of-flight of sound waves (typically **40 kHz**).
- **Infrared / LIDAR:** For distance measurement and mapping.

**Time-of-flight distance formula:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v$ is the speed of the wave in the medium and $t$ is the round-trip time.

### 2.2.5 Vision Sensors

- **CCD / CMOS Cameras** for 2D and 3D object recognition.
- Used in assembly line quality inspection and bin-picking applications.

## 2.3 Controllers — The "Brain" of the Robot

The controller implements the control law that drives the actuators based on sensor feedback.

### 2.3.1 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop

- **Open-Loop Control:** Controller sends commands without feedback. Example: Stepper motor driven by a fixed pulse count. *Simple but inaccurate if load changes.*
- **Closed-Loop Control:** Controller uses sensor feedback to correct errors continuously. Example: Servo motor with encoder feedback. *Accurate and robust.*

### 2.3.2 The PID Controller (High-Yield KTU Topic)

The **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID)** controller is the workhorse of industrial robotics.

The control signal is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = control signal sent to actuator
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ = error (reference minus measured output)
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = proportional, integral, derivative gains

| Term | Effect on Response | Effect on Stability |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ (Proportional) | Reduces rise time | Too high → oscillation |
| $K_i$ (Integral) | Eliminates steady-state error | Too high → overshoot, instability |
| $K_d$ (Derivative) | Improves damping, reduces overshoot | Sensitive to noise |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to "design a PID controller," always state the three tuning goals explicitly: **fast response, zero steady-state error, and minimum overshoot**.

### 2.3.3 Industrial Controller Hardware

- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Rugged, ladder-logic-based; used in material-handling conveyor systems.
- **Microcontroller (Arduino, STM32):** Used in academic and low-cost robotic arms.
- **Industrial Robot Controller (e.g., FANUC, ABB, KUKA):** Proprietary, multi-axis real-time controllers running at **≥ 1 kHz** loop rate.
- **DSP / FPGA-based controllers:** For high-speed vision processing and motion control.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Ready)

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Motor Torque | $\tau_m = K_t \cdot i_a$ | $K_t, i_a$ | N·m, N·m/A, A |
| Back-EMF | $e_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$ | $K_e, \omega_m$ | V, V·s/rad, rad/s |
| Hydraulic/Pneumatic Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | $P, A$ | N, Pa, m² |
| Encoder Resolution | $\theta_{res} = 360° / N$ | $N$ | degrees, pulses |
| Gauge Factor | $G = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | $\Delta R, R, \varepsilon$ | dimensionless, Ω, – |
| Time-of-Flight Distance | $d = v \cdot t / 2$ | $v, t$ | m, m/s, s |
| PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot e$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ | unitless, 1/s, s |
| Steady-state Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$ | – | – |
| Transfer Function (1st order) | $G(s) = K / (\tau s + 1)$ | $K, \tau$ | – , s |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The actuator–sensor–controller triad is used in:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (welding, painting) — hydraulic + electric hybrid actuators.
- **E-commerce warehouses** (Amazon Kiva robots) — BLDC motors + LIDAR + onboard microcontrollers.
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** — Piezoelectric actuators + force sensors + PID/adaptive controllers.
- **CNC machines** — Servo motors + linear encoders + DSP controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of a DC Servo Motor (Armature-Controlled)
