4. **Reduction of direct labor cost** and improvement of overall equipment effectiveness (OEE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Watchmaker vs. The Robotic Watchmaker:**
> Imagine a master watchmaker assembling a Swiss timepiece. Each tiny screw must be placed with a steady hand, each gear must mesh perfectly, and the watchmaker repeats the same motion 200 times a day. After 6 hours, fatigue sets in — mistakes creep in. A *robotic assembly system* is essentially a "watchmaker that never gets tired, never blinks, and places each screw within ±0.02 mm every single time". The robot mimics the human arm's dexterity through programmable joints (axes), but executes motions with **machine-grade precision, speed, and indefatigability**.

In engineering terms:
- The human **shoulder** → Robot's **base joint (J1)**.
- The human **elbow** → Robot's **arm joint (J2, J3)**.
- The human **wrist** → Robot's **wrist joints (J4, J5, J6)**.
- The human **fingers** → Robot's **end-effector / gripper**.

## 1.3 Why Robotic Assembly? — Engineering Justification

> [!TIP]
> **Key Performance Indicators Driving Robotic Assembly (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Repeatability:** **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** (statistical measure of returning to a taught position).
> - **Accuracy:** ±0.5 mm to ±1.0 mm (deviation from absolute commanded position).
> - **Cycle Time:** **2–60 seconds** for typical small-parts assembly.
> - **Payload:** **1 kg to 1500 kg** depending on robot class.
> - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** **20,000–80,000 hours** for modern industrial robots.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Misconception:**
> *Repeatability ≠ Accuracy.* A robot can be highly repeatable (always lands at the same spot) but not very accurate (that spot is offset from the true commanded position). The KTU board regularly tests this distinction.

## 1.4 Assembly Operations Performed by Robots

The standard taxonomy of assembly operations (per Boothroyd, 1994 — widely adopted in KTU reference texts) is:

- **Mating operations:** Peg-in-hole, snap fits, threaded fastening.
- **Joining operations:** Adhesive dispensing, soldering, ultrasonic welding.
- **Fastening operations:** Screwing, riveting, press-fitting.
- **Inspection & testing:** Vision-based verification, force-torque probing.
- **Material handling within assembly:** Pick-and-place, palletizing of sub-assemblies.

---

## 1.5 Classification of Assembly Operations (By Force & Tolerance)

| Operation Type | Insertion Force | Tolerance Band | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Loose insertion | < 5 N | ±0.5 mm | Plastic toy assembly |
| Press fit | 50–500 N | ±0.025 mm | Bearing assembly |
| Snap fit | 10–50 N | ±0.1 mm | Electronics housing |
| Threaded assembly | 5–20 N torque | ±0.2 mm | Engine block bolts |
| Adhesive bonded | < 2 N | ±0.5 mm | Composite panels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Module 1 specifically emphasizes the relationship between **tolerance band** and **robot repeatability class**. As a rule of thumb taught in OERUT724: *robot repeatability must be at least 10× tighter than the smallest assembly tolerance.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown — Anatomy of a Robotic Assembly Cell

A complete robotic assembly cell (KTU 2024 nomenclature) consists of **five functional layers**:

1. **Physical Layer (Hardware)**
   - Industrial robot arm (manipulator).
   - End-effector (gripper, screwdriver, suction cup, etc.).
   - Fixtures, jigs, and part presenters.
   - Sensors (vision, force/torque, proximity).

2. **Control Layer**
   - Robot controller (servo drives, PLC).
   - Motion planner and trajectory interpolator.
   - I/O interfacing with conveyors and feeders.

3. **Programming Layer**
   - Teach pendant (online programming).
   - Offline programming (OLP) software (e.g., RoboGuide, RobotStudio).
   - Vision-guided routines.

4. **Supervisory Layer**
   - SCADA / MES integration.
   - Production scheduling.
   - Quality data logging.

5. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**
   - Safety light curtains, emergency stops.
   - Operator panel.

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Geometric Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Definition:**
> The number of independent motions a robot can perform in 3D space. A rigid body in 3D space has **6 DOF**: 3 for position (X, Y, Z) and 3 for orientation (roll, pitch, yaw).

For full spatial assembly, a robot must have a **minimum of 6 DOF** (3 positioning + 3 orienting). However, many assembly tasks require only 3–4 DOF because the parts are pre-oriented by feeders.

**KTU Rule of Thumb:**
- **2–3 DOF:** Pick-and-place from conveyor → typically Cartesian or SCARA.
- **4 DOF:** SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) — *the KTU-blessed workhorse of assembly*.
- **5–6 DOF:** Complex 3D mating (e.g., automotive dashboard assembly).
- **>6 DOF:** Redundant manipulators for obstacle avoidance in cluttered cells.

## 2.3 Repeatability vs. Accuracy — Mathematical Definitions

**Repeatability (RP):**
The closeness of agreement between repeated measurements of positioning to the same commanded location under identical conditions.

$$RP = k \cdot \sigma_p$$

where:
- $\sigma_p$ = standard deviation of position errors (in mm).
- $k$ = coverage factor (typically **k = 3** for 99.73% confidence in industrial robotics).

**Accuracy (AC):**
The deviation between the commanded position and the mean of the achieved positions.

$$AC = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert P_{cmd} - P_i \vert$$

where $P_{cmd}$ is the commanded position and $P_i$ is the $i$-th actual position.

## 2.4 Cycle Time Analysis (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

The total assembly cycle time $T_{cycle}$ is the sum of:

$$T_{cycle} = T_{motion} + T_{grasp} + T_{place} + T_{index} + T_{process}$$

- $T_{motion}$: Robot arm travel time (depends on path length, max joint velocity).
- $T_{grasp}$: Gripper close/open time (**0.1–0.5 s**).
- $T_{place}$: Insertion/joining time (**0.5–5 s**).
- $T_{index}$: Conveyor or fixture index time (**0.5–2 s**).
- $T_{process}$: Auxiliary process (screwdriving, curing, etc.).

**Production Rate:**

$$R = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} \quad \text{[parts/hour]}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Repeatability | $RP = k \cdot \sigma_p$ | $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm | Position consistency check |
| 2 | Accuracy | $AC = \frac{1}{N} \sum \vert P_{cmd} - P_i \vert$ | $\pm 0.5$ mm | Absolute position error |
| 3 | Cycle Time | $T_{cycle} = T_m + T_g + T_p + T_i + T_{proc}$ | 2–60 s | Throughput calculation |
| 4 | Production Rate | $R = 3600 / T_{cycle}$ | parts/hr | Capacity planning |
| 5 | Workspace Volume (Cartesian) | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Cell layout design |
| 6 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$ | $\text{m}^3$ | Polar robot reach |
| 7 | Required Repeatability Rule | $RP \leq T_{min} / 10$ | mm | Robot class selection |
| 8 | Joint Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi \cdot RP}{L_{eff}}$ | rad | Encoder specification |
| 9 | Insertion Force (Peg-in-Hole) | $F_i = F_c + \mu \cdot N$ | N | Force-torque sizing |
| 10 | OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | % (typically 85% target) | Performance KPI |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Memorize Formulas 3, 4, 7, and 10. The KTU board has appeared in past university exams with **direct 7-mark numerical problems** on cycle time, production rate, and OEE calculations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (Tesla, Toyota, Maruti Suzuki):** Robotic assembly of engines, gearboxes, dashboards. Typical SCARA + articulated hybrid cell.
- **Electronics (Foxconn, Samsung):** PCB component placement, smartphone casing mating.
- **Medical Devices (Medtronic, Stryker):** Micro-assembly of pacemakers, insulin pumps.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** Riveting and bolt-fastening of fuselage panels.
- **Consumer Goods (LG, Whirlpool):** Washing machine drum assembly, refrigerator door fitting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Workspace Volume of an Articulated Robot (R-R-R Configuration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite Numerical Problem:**
> "A 6-axis articulated robot has link lengths $L_1 = 400$ mm, $L_2 = 500$ mm, and $L_3 = 200$ mm. Compute the maximum reach, dead-zone radius, and approximate workspace volume."

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry

The articulated (anthropomorphic) robot in a vertical plane has its three revolute joints at:
- **J1 (base):** shoulder rotation about vertical axis.
- **J2 (shoulder):** lifts the arm in vertical plane.
- **J3 (elbow):** extends the forearm.

The end-effector position (P_x, P_y) in the shoulder plane is given by forward kinematics:

$$P_x = L_2 \cos(\theta_2) + L_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$

$$P_y = L_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_2) + L_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Maximum Reach (Full Arm Extension)

When $\theta_2 = 0$ and $\theta_3 = 0$ (arm fully stretched horizontally):

$$r_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 400 + 500 + 200 = 1100 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Dead-Zone Radius (Minimum Reach)

When the arm is fully folded back on itself ($\theta_2 = 90°$, $\theta_3 = -180°$):

$$r_{min} = \vert L_1 - (L_2 + L_3) \vert = \vert 400 - 700 \vert = 300 \text{ mm}$$

A point closer than 300 mm to the J1 axis is **inaccessible** (this is the dead zone).

### Step 4 — Approximate Workspace Volume

For a vertical-axis articulated robot, the workspace is approximately a **spherical shell** (since J1 sweeps the shoulder plane 360°):

$$V \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi (r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$$

Substituting values:

$$V \approx \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.10^3 - 0.30^3) \text{ in m}^3$$

Computing the cubes:

$$1.10^3 = 1.331 \text{ m}^3$$

$$0.30^3 = 0.027 \text{ m}^3$$

Therefore:

$$V \approx \frac{4}{3} \times 3.14159 \times (1.331 - 0.027)$$

$$V \approx 4.18879 \times 1.304$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 5.462 \text{ m}^3}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

- The robot can reach **any point** in a spherical shell of outer radius 1.1 m and inner radius 0.3 m around its base.
- The cell must be designed so that **all fixtures, conveyors, and parts** lie within this shell.
- The dead zone of 0.3 m means **no assembly fixture** should be placed within this region (this is a common KTU conceptual question).

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Repeatability for a Peg-in-Hole Insertion

### Step 1 — The Problem

A peg of diameter $d_p = 10$ mm must be inserted into a hole of diameter $d_h = 10.05$ mm (clearance $c = 0.05$ mm). The assembly tolerance is $T_{min} = 0.05$ mm. Find the minimum required robot repeatability.

### Step 2 — Apply the KTU Selection Rule

$$RP \leq \frac{T_{min}}{10}$$

$$RP \leq \frac{0.05}{10} = 0.005 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Realistic Engineering Check

Since standard industrial robots have repeatability of $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm, this 0.005 mm requirement demands either:
- A **high-precision Cartesian gantry** (RP $\pm 0.005$ mm achievable).
- **Remote Center Compliance (RCC) device** to mechanically absorb positional errors.
- **Force-torque feedback** with search-and-correct algorithms.

### Step 4 — Conclusion

$$\boxed{RP_{required} = 0.005 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{Use RCC or vision-guided correction}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Cycle Time and Production Rate for an Assembly Cell

### Problem Statement
A PCB assembly cell performs 6 component placements. Each placement takes $T_{place} = 0.8$ s. Gripper actuation takes $T_g = 0.3$ s (open + close). Robot motion between placement points averages $T_m = 0.5$ s. Conveyor index time is $T_i = 1.2$ s. Soldering process per board is $T_{proc} = 4$ s. Compute the production rate.

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters

- $n = 6$ placements per board.
- $T_m = 0.5$ s (per motion).
- $T_g = 0.3$ s (per placement).
- $T_{place} = 0.8$ s (per placement).
- $T_i = 1.2$ s.
- $T_{proc} = 4$ s.

### Step 2 — Compute Total Cycle Time

$$T_{cycle} = n(T_m + T_g + T_{place}) + T_i + T_{proc}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 6(0.5 + 0.3 + 0.8) + 1.2 + 4$$

$$T_{cycle} = 6 \times 1.6 + 5.2$$

$$T_{cycle} = 9.6 + 5.2 = 14.8 \text{ s/board}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Production Rate

$$R = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{14.8}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 243.24 \text{ boards/hour}}$$

### Step 4 — Daily Output (8-hour shift)

$$N_{daily} = 243.24 \times 8 = 1945.9 \text{ boards/day}$$

This is a typical KTU numerical answer. Marks are awarded for **showing all substitutions**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Types of Assembly Robots

| Robot Type | DOF | Typical Payload | Repeatability | Best Suited Assembly Task | Cost (Relative) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian (Gantry)** | 3 (X,Y,Z) | 10–500 kg | $\pm 0.02$ mm | Large panel assembly, PCB pick-place | Low |
| **SCARA** | 4 (X,Y,Z,θ) | 1–20 kg | $\pm 0.01$ mm | Vertical peg-in-hole, electronics | Low-Medium |
| **Cylindrical** | 4 (θ, Z, r) | 5–100 kg | $\pm 0.05$ mm | Simple pick-place at fixed height | Low |
| **Spherical (Polar)** | 3–6 | 5–100 kg | $\pm 0.1$ mm | Older automotive welding, loading | Low |
| **Articulated (6-axis)** | 6 | 5–1500 kg | $\pm 0.05$ mm | Complex 3D mating, automotive | High |
| **Parallel (Delta)** | 3–4 | 0.5–10 kg | $\pm 0.05$ mm | High-speed packaging, light pick-place | Medium |
| **Collaborative (Cobot)** | 6–7 | 3–35 kg | $\pm 0.05$ mm | Human-robot shared assembly | High |

---

## 4.3 SCARA Robot Kinematic Schematic (Text Diagram)

```
            J1 (Base rotation, vertical axis)
                    |
                    |
                    J2 (Shoulder, horizontal axis)
                   / \
                  /   \
                 /     \
                J3 ----J4 (Elbow and Wrist roll, both horizontal)
                 \     /
                  \   /
                   \ /
                  [End-effector — Z vertical slide + θz rotation]

    SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm
    Compliant in X-Y (radial), RIGID in Z (vertical)
    Ideal for vertical peg-in-hole assembly.
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Compliance Interpretation:**
> - **Compliant (soft) in X-Y plane** → The arm can deflect slightly laterally, absorbing small misalignments during insertion (prevents jamming).
> - **Rigid in Z direction** → The vertical axis maintains precise insertion force without buckling.
> This is the *physical principle* that makes SCARA the dominant choice for assembly (and a frequent KTU 2-mark question).

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Assembly Robot

```mermaid

    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is the cycle time under 3 seconds}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is the part heavy above 50 kg}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Use SCARA Robot]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Use 6 Axis Articulated]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Use Articulated with High Payload]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is it high speed packaging}
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Use Parallel Delta Robot]
    Q4 -->|No| R5[Use Cartesian Gantry]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define robotic assembly system. List any four types of assembly robots.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A *robotic assembly system* is a programmable automated manufacturing system in which one or more industrial robots perform the joining, insertion, fastening, or mating of component parts to manufacture a finished product, with repeatable precision typically in the range of ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm and cycle times of 2 to 60 seconds.

**Four Types of Assembly Robots (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Cartesian (Gantry) Robot** — three linear axes (X, Y, Z).
2. **SCARA Robot** — Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm with 4 DOF.
3. **Articulated (Anthropomorphic) Robot** — 6 revolute joints.
4. **Parallel (Delta) Robot** — three arms connected to a common base, used for high-speed pick-and-place.
*(Acceptable alternatives: Cylindrical, Spherical, Collaborative Cobot.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Distinguish between repeatability and accuracy of an industrial robot. Why is repeatability more important than accuracy in robotic assembly?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ability to return to a taught position consistently | Ability to reach the commanded position |
| Measure | Statistical spread (3σ) of position | Mean deviation from commanded position |
| Magnitude (typical) | ±0.02 to ±0.1 mm | ±0.5 to ±1.0 mm |
| Importance | **High** for assembly | Less critical (often corrected by calibration) |

**Why repeatability is more important (1.5 marks):**
In assembly, the robot is taught the correct reference position by the operator (using a teach pendant). The robot must then return to **exactly that same position** hundreds of thousands of times. Even if the absolute position has a small error (accuracy issue), this can be corrected once during calibration. However, if the robot is not repeatable, every cycle will give a different position, leading to assembly failures. Therefore, *repeatability governs assembly quality*.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Do not skip the units** in final answers — partial marks are lost.
> 2. **Always state the formula before substitution.** Direct substitutions without formula fetch zero.
> 3. **Avoid writing generic statements** like "robots are accurate." Examiners want specific numerical evidence (e.g., "±0.05 mm repeatability").
> 4. **Draw neat labeled diagrams** for kinematic configurations. A poorly drawn robot diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Distinguish between DOF and Axes.** They are NOT the same. A 4-DOF SCARA has 4 axes.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, University Question Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of assembly robots based on kinematic structure. Explain the working principle and application suitability of SCARA and 6-axis articulated robots with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive assembly cell uses a 6-axis articulated robot with the following specifications: reach = 1.5 m, payload = 50 kg, repeatability = ±0.05 mm. The cell performs 8 pick-and-place operations per vehicle. Each operation involves: motion = 0.6 s, grasp = 0.25 s, place = 0.9 s. Conveyor index time = 2.0 s per vehicle. Compute: (i) total cycle time, (ii) hourly production rate, (iii) daily production for an 8-hour shift. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification by Kinematic Structure (2 marks):**

The major kinematic types are:
1. **Cartesian (Gantry)** — PPP (Prismatic-Prismatic-Prismatic).
2. **Cylindrical** — RPP (Revolute-Prismatic-Prismatic).
3. **Spherical / Polar** — RRP.
4. **SCARA** — RRP with two parallel revolute joints.
5. **Articulated (Anthropomorphic)** — RRR with three revolute joints in a serial chain.
6. **Parallel (Delta)** — three parallel kinematic chains.

**SCARA Robot (2.5 marks):**

- **Full form:** Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm.
- **Configuration:** Two parallel revolute joints (J1, J2) in the horizontal plane, plus a prismatic Z-axis and a rotational θ-axis.
- **DOF:** 4.
- **Compliance:** *Compliant (flexible) in the horizontal X-Y plane, rigid in the vertical Z direction.*
- **Why compliant in X-Y?** To allow small lateral misalignments during peg-in-hole insertion, preventing jamming.
- **Applications:** Vertical insertion tasks — PCB component placement, screw insertion, syringe capping.
- **Typical payload:** 1–20 kg; repeatability: ±0.01 mm.

**6-Axis Articulated Robot (2.5 marks):**

- **Configuration:** Six revolute joints in a serial kinematic chain (waist, shoulder, elbow, and 3-axis wrist).
- **DOF:** 6 (3 for positioning, 3 for orientation).
- **Workspace:** Roughly spherical shell around the base.
- **Applications:** Complex 3D mating — automotive dashboard, engine sub-assembly, aerospace structural joining.
- **Typical payload:** 5–1500 kg; repeatability: ±0.05 mm.

**Neat Diagram Required:** (Examiner will check for labeled joints J1–J6, link lengths L1, L2, L3, base, and end-effector.)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given data:**
- $n = 8$ operations per vehicle
- $T_m = 0.6$ s (motion per operation)
- $T_g = 0.25$ s (grasp per operation)
- $T_{place} = 0.9$ s (place per operation)
- $T_i = 2.0$ s (conveyor index per vehicle)

**Step 1 — Compute total cycle time (3 marks):**

$$T_{cycle} = n \times (T_m + T_g + T_{place}) + T_i$$

$$T_{cycle} = 8 \times (0.6 + 0.25 + 0.9) + 2.0$$

$$T_{cycle} = 8 \times 1.75 + 2.0$$

$$T_{cycle} = 14.0 + 2.0 = 16.0 \text{ s/vehicle}$$

**Step 2 — Compute hourly production rate (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{16.0}$$

$$\boxed{R = 225 \text{ vehicles/hour}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute daily production for 8-hour shift (2 marks):**

$$N_{daily} = 225 \times 8 = 1800 \text{ vehicles/day}$$

$$\boxed{N_{daily} = 1800 \text{ vehicles/day}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, University Question Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Degrees of Freedom (DOF) in robotic assembly. A SCARA robot has 4 DOF. Identify each DOF with reference to a specific assembly task involving PCB component placement. Why is SCARA preferred over Cartesian robots for vertical insertion tasks? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel delta robot performs high-speed pick-and-place in a packaging line. It executes 120 pick-and-place cycles per minute. Each pick takes 0.08 s, each place takes 0.06 s, and the average motion time is 0.16 s. Compute: (i) the theoretical cycle time per package, (ii) the actual production rate in packages/hour, (iii) the OEE given availability = 90%, performance = 95%, and quality = 99%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of DOF (1 mark):**
Degrees of Freedom (DOF) is the number of independent motions a robot can perform in 3D space. A rigid body requires 6 DOF (3 translation + 3 rotation) to be fully positioned and oriented.

**SCARA's 4 DOF and PCB Assembly Mapping (3 marks):**

| Joint | DOF | Motion | PCB Assembly Task |
|---|---|---|---|
| J1 | Revolute (Z-axis) | Shoulder rotation | Rotates arm to reach component feeder |
| J2 | Revolute (Z-axis) | Elbow rotation | Extends arm to component location |
| Z | Prismatic (Z-axis) | Vertical descent | Lowers gripper onto component |
| θ | Revolute (Z-axis) | Wrist roll | Rotates component to correct orientation before placement |

**Why SCARA > Cartesian for vertical insertion (3 marks):**

1. **Selective compliance** in the horizontal plane absorbs small misalignments (±0.05 mm) between the peg and the hole, preventing jamming during insertion.
2. **High speed in horizontal plane** — revolute joints can achieve accelerations up to 10g, much faster than Cartesian prismatic joints.
3. **Compact footprint** — SCARA's cylindrical workspace fits into a smaller floor area than a Cartesian gantry for the same reach.
4. **Rigid vertical axis** — provides the precise insertion force (5–50 N typical) without deflection.
5. **Cost-effectiveness** — SCARA offers ±0.01 mm repeatability at lower cost than a 6-axis robot.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Given data:**
- Pick time, $T_{pick} = 0.08$ s
- Place time, $T_{place} = 0.06$ s
- Motion time, $T_m = 0.16$ s
- A = 90%, P = 95%, Q = 99%

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical cycle time per package (2 marks):**

$$T_{cycle} = T_{pick} + T_m + T_{place}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 0.08 + 0.16 + 0.06 = 0.30 \text{ s/package}$$

**Step 2 — Compute production rate in packages/hour (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{3600}{0.30} = 12{,}000 \text{ packages/hour}$$

**Step 3 — Compute OEE (3 marks):**

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

$$OEE = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.99$$

$$OEE = 0.84645$$

$$\boxed{OEE = 84.65\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** A world-class OEE is 85% (per SEMI E10 standards). The computed 84.65% indicates a well-performing cell.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Robotic Assembly** = programmable joining/mating of parts; the largest industrial robot application after welding.
- [x] **DOF** = 3 translation + 3 rotation = 6 for full spatial task; SCARA has 4; Cartesian has 3.
- [x] **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy.** Repeatability is statistical (3σ) consistency; accuracy is absolute deviation. Assembly depends on repeatability.
- [x] **SCARA** = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm. Compliant in X-Y, rigid in Z. Best for vertical peg-in-hole.
- [x] **6-axis articulated** = RRR serial chain. Best for complex 3D mating in automotive and aerospace.
- [x] **Cartesian (Gantry)** = high precision, high payload, large workspace. Used for large panel assembly and PCB pick-place.
- [x] **Delta (parallel)** = high speed, low payload, packaging and food industry.
- [x] **Collaborative (Cobot)** = safe for human-robot shared workspace; lower payload, lower speed.
- [x] **Rule of Thumb:** $RP_{robot} \leq T_{min,assembly} / 10$ — robot repeatability must be 10× tighter than the smallest tolerance.
- [x] **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_{cycle} = n(T_m + T_g + T_{place}) + T_i + T_{proc}$.
- [x] **Production Rate:** $R = 3600 / T_{cycle}$ in parts/hour.
- [x] **OEE:** $A \times P \times Q$ (Availability × Performance × Quality), world-class benchmark = 85%.
- [x] **Workspace Volume (articulated):** $V \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi(r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3)$.
- [x] **Peg-in-hole insertion force:** $F_i = F_c + \mu N$ (chamfer force + friction).
- [x] **MTBF of modern industrial robots:** 20,000–80,000 hours.
- [x] **Key vendors for KTU industrial visits:** FANUC, KUKA, ABB, Yaskawa (Motoman), Universal Robots.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip (2024 Scheme):**
> In Part B questions, always include a **neat labeled diagram** (minimum: one full-page figure) when explaining a robot type. Examiners award 1–2 marks purely for the diagram quality, even if the written explanation is average. Practice drawing SCARA and 6-axis articulated robots from memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications in Manufacturing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications in Manufacturing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robotic Applications in Manufacturing** refers to the systematic deployment of programmable, multi-axis manipulator systems (industrial robots) to execute manufacturing operations such as material handling, processing, assembly, inspection, and packaging with minimal human intervention, governed by the ISO 8373:2021 definition of an *industrial robot* as an "automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes."

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **OERUT724**, manufacturing applications are classified into **four canonical functional categories** as per the Robotics Industries Association (RIA) taxonomy:

1. **Material Handling (MH)** – Pick-and-place, palletizing, machine tending.
2. **Processing Operations (PO)** – Welding, painting, drilling, grinding, deburring.
3. **Assembly Operations (AO)** – Parts mating, screwdriving, insertion, adhesive bonding.
4. **Quality Inspection (QI)** – Non-Destructive Testing (NDT), dimensional gauging, vision-based sorting.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Approximately **65%** of all global industrial robot installations are concentrated in **automotive, electronics, and metal-machining** sectors, with arc-welding alone accounting for **~20%** of total robot deployments worldwide (Source: IFR World Robotics Report 2023, conceptual reference only — verify against the latest KTU-recommended data).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a highly skilled worker in a factory who:
- Never gets tired,
- Performs the **exact same motion** thousands of times without any deviation,
- Can lift heavy parts weighing hundreds of kilograms,
- Works in environments that are too hot, too cold, or too toxic for humans (e.g., welding fumes, paint spray booths),
- And can be **retrained** (reprogrammed) within hours to do a completely different job.

**That "worker" is an industrial robot.** In manufacturing, robots act as the *extension of a human's hand and decision-making capability*, but with superhuman *precision, repeatability, and endurance*. Think of a robot as a **"Swiss Army Knife"** of the factory floor — one mechanical platform that, when equipped with different *end-effectors* (the robot's "hand" or tool), can be switched from welding a car frame to painting its body to assembling its dashboard — all within the same shift.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for a KTU Student:**
> Just as your smartphone runs different *apps* (WhatsApp, Camera, Calculator) on the same hardware, a robot runs different *applications* (welding, painting, assembly) on the same mechanical arm. The **hardware** is the arm; the **app** is the program + end-effector combination.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Manufacturing Robotics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Industrial) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Payload Capacity** | $P$ | 5 kg – 1000 kg | Max load the robot can manipulate |
| **Reach (Working Envelope)** | $R$ | 0.5 m – 3.5 m | Max radial distance from base |
| **Repeatability** | $\rho$ | $\pm$0.02 mm – $\pm$0.1 mm | Ability to return to a taught position |
| **Number of Axes (DOF)** | $n$ | 4 – 6 (industrial), up to 7 (advanced) | Degrees of freedom |
| **Cycle Time** | $t_c$ | 1 s – 60 s | Time per operational cycle |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | MTBF | 50,000 – 100,000 hours | Reliability metric |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Robots Are Used in Manufacturing

The decision to deploy a robot in a manufacturing cell is governed by the **4D Framework of Automation Justification**:

1. **Dull Tasks** – Repetitive operations causing operator fatigue and injury (e.g., pallet stacking).
2. **Dirty Tasks** – Operations in contaminated or hazardous environments (e.g., foundry pouring).
3. **Dangerous Tasks** – Operations posing safety risks (e.g., forge pressing, die-casting).
4. **Dear Tasks** – High-precision or high-cost operations where human error is unacceptable (e.g., semiconductor wafer handling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When answering "Why are robots used in manufacturing?" questions, always cite the **4D Framework** and the **economic threshold equation** (Equation 2.1 below). Examiners reward answers that combine *technical* and *economic* reasoning.

---

## 2.2 The Robot Selection Criterion (Economic Justification)

A manufacturing robot is economically viable when the **Return on Investment (ROI) period** falls below a company-defined threshold (typically **24–36 months**).

$$T_{ROI} = \frac{C_{total}}{S_{annual}}$$

Where:
- $T_{ROI}$ = Payback period (years)
- $C_{total}$ = Total installed cost of the robotic cell (robot + end-effector + safety + integration)
- $S_{annual}$ = Annual savings = (Labor cost saved) + (Quality cost reduction) + (Throughput gain) − (Operating cost)

The expanded form is:

$$S_{annual} = \left(L_h \times H_y \times N_s\right) + \Delta C_q + \Delta R_t - C_{op}$$

Where:
- $L_h$ = Hourly labor cost (₹/hr or $/hr)
- $H_y$ = Operating hours per year
- $N_s$ = Number of shifts (effective labor units displaced)
- $\Delta C_q$ = Annual quality cost reduction (rework, scrap, warranty)
- $\Delta R_t$ = Throughput revenue gain
- $C_{op}$ = Annual operating cost (power, maintenance, programming)

---

## 2.3 Application-Specific Robot Selection Logic

| Manufacturing Application | Typical Robot Type | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | Critical Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Welding** | Articulated (6-axis) | 6 | Path accuracy, TCP speed |
| **Spot Welding** | Heavy-payload articulated | 6 | Payload 100–350 kg |
| **Painting / Coating** | Hollow-wrist articulated (explosion-proof) | 6 | ATEX/IECEx certification |
| **Palletizing** | 4-axis articulated or gantry | 4 | Payload 50–700 kg, reach |
| **Pick & Place** | SCARA or Delta | 3–4 | Cycle time (<0.5 s) |
| **Machine Tending** | Articulated (6-axis) | 6 | Rigidity, IP67 rating |
| **Assembly** | SCARA / Cartesian / 6-axis | 4–6 | Repeatability $\pm 0.01$ mm |
| **Adhesive Dispensing** | Cartesian or 6-axis | 3–6 | Path-following precision |
| **Deburring / Grinding** | 6-axis articulated (high payload) | 6 | Force control capability |
| **Inspection (Vision)** | SCARA / Cartesian | 3–4 | Camera integration, lighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** **SCARA** = *Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm*. It has high rigidity in the vertical (Z) axis and compliance in the horizontal (X-Y) plane — making it ideal for **vertical insertion** operations like PCB component placement.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Robot Repeatability | $\rho = \pm 3\sigma_{pos}$ | mm | Quality metric |
| 2 | ROI Payback | $T_{ROI} = C_{total} \big/ S_{annual}$ | years | Economic justification |
| 3 | Annual Savings | $S_{annual} = L_h H_y N_s + \Delta C_q + \Delta R_t - C_{op}$ | ₹/year or \$/year | Cost analysis |
| 4 | Production Rate | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{t_c} \times \eta_a$ | parts/hour | Throughput |
| 5 | Availability Factor | $\eta_a = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | OEE component |
| 6 | Cycle Time (Palletizing) | $t_c = t_{motion} + t_{grasp} + t_{release}$ | seconds | Time study |
| 7 | Workspace Volume (Sphere approx.) | $V_w = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3$ | m³ | Reach planning |
| 8 | Degrees of Freedom (Kutzbach) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | integer | DOF analysis |
| 9 | Joint Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{\theta_{max}}{2^N}$ | rad or deg | Encoder-based |
| 10 | TCP Velocity (Cartesian) | $v_{TCP} = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2}$ | m/s | Path planning |

> **Critical Rule:** Never use the pipe `|` symbol in table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value expressions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The manufacturing applications of robotics are not academic — they form the **backbone of Industry 4.0** and **Smart Manufacturing** initiatives. In a KTU-relevant industrial context:

- **Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai (India):** Heavy-payload robots perform **spot welding** of car bodies at cycle times of **45–60 seconds per body**.
- **Foxconn, Wistron, Apple supply chain (Shenzhen, Chennai, Sri City):** SCARA and Delta robots perform **PCB assembly** at rates exceeding **30,000 components per hour (CPH)**.
- **Bosch, ABB, KUKA industrial installations:** **6-axis articulated robots** handle machine tending of CNC lathes, reducing idle spindle time by **~40%**.
- **Berger Paints, Asian Paints (automated plants):** Hollow-wrist explosion-proof robots execute **automotive painting**, reducing VOC exposure to human workers by **>99%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Robot Economic Justification (ROI Model)

**Problem Statement:** A KTU-aligned industrial case study — A Kerala-based SME (Small-Medium Enterprise) plans to install a robotic welding cell. Determine the payback period and decide feasibility.

### Given Data (Assumed KTU Examination Values):
- Total installed cost of the cell: $C_{total}$ = ₹ 45,00,000
- Labor displaced: $N_s$ = 2 operators per shift
- Hourly labor cost: $L_h$ = ₹ 250/hour (including benefits)
- Operating hours per year: $H_y$ = 4800 hours (2 shifts × 2400 hours)
- Quality cost reduction: $\Delta C_q$ = ₹ 2,00,000/year
- Throughput gain: $\Delta R_t$ = ₹ 5,00,000/year
- Annual operating cost: $C_{op}$ = ₹ 3,00,000/year

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation.

$$T_{ROI} = \frac{C_{total}}{S_{annual}}$$

### Step 2: Compute the labor cost savings component.

$$S_{labor} = L_h \times H_y \times N_s$$

Substituting:

$$S_{labor} = 250 \times 4800 \times 2$$

$$S_{labor} = 1{,}250 \times 4800$$

$$S_{labor} = \text{₹ } 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 3: Sum all savings components.

$$S_{annual} = S_{labor} + \Delta C_q + \Delta R_t - C_{op}$$

$$S_{annual} = 24{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000$$

$$S_{annual} = 31{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000$$

$$S_{annual} = \text{₹ } 28{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

### Step 4: Compute the payback period.

$$T_{ROI} = \frac{C_{total}}{S_{annual}} = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{28{,}00{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{T_{ROI} = 1.607 \text{ years} \approx 19.3 \text{ months}}$$

### Step 5: Decision logic.

Since $T_{ROI} = 1.607$ years < 2 years (the company threshold), the project is **economically feasible**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Always show the substitution step explicitly. Many students lose marks by writing the final answer without the intermediate substitution. Write *every* line of the calculation in the answer sheet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Workspace Volume Calculation

**Problem:** An industrial robot has a maximum reach of $R = 2.5$ m. Compute the theoretical spherical workspace volume.

### Governing equation:

$$V_w = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3$$

### Step 1: Cube the radius.

$$R^3 = (2.5)^3 = 15.625 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 2: Multiply by $\pi$.

$$\pi R^3 = 3.14159 \times 15.625 = 49.0874 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 3: Multiply by $\frac{4}{3}$.

$$V_w = \frac{4}{3} \times 49.0874$$

$$V_w = 1.3333 \times 49.0874$$

$$\boxed{V_w = 65.45 \text{ m}^3}$$

### Step 4: Apply the "Useful Workspace" correction.

In practice, only **~60%** of the spherical envelope is usable due to joint limits, singularities, and reachability constraints.

$$V_{useful} = 0.60 \times V_w = 0.60 \times 65.45 = 39.27 \text{ m}^3$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Production Rate vs. Cycle Time

**Problem:** A SCARA robot performs a pick-and-place operation. Motion time = 0.4 s, grasp time = 0.15 s, release time = 0.10 s. Availability factor $\eta_a$ = 0.92. Find production rate.

### Step 1: Total cycle time.

$$t_c = t_{motion} + t_{grasp} + t_{release} = 0.4 + 0.15 + 0.10 = 0.65 \text{ s}$$

### Step 2: Theoretical production rate.

$$R_{p,theoretical} = \frac{3600}{t_c} = \frac{3600}{0.65} = 5538.46 \text{ parts/hour}$$

### Step 3: Effective (actual) production rate.

$$R_p = R_{p,theoretical} \times \eta_a = 5538.46 \times 0.92$$

$$\boxed{R_p = 5095.4 \text{ parts/hour}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Manufacturing Application vs. Selection Criteria

| Application | Required Payload (kg) | Required DOF | Repeatability (mm) | Typical Industry Sector |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Arc Welding | 5 – 15 | 6 | $\pm 0.05$ | Automotive, shipbuilding |
| Spot Welding | 100 – 350 | 6 | $\pm 0.1$ | Automotive body-in-white |
| Painting | 5 – 15 | 6 (hollow wrist) | $\pm 0.2$ | Automotive, appliances |
| Palletizing | 50 – 700 | 4 | $\pm 0.5$ | FMCG, logistics |
| Pick & Place | 1 – 10 | 3–4 (Delta/SCARA) | $\pm 0.02$ | Electronics, pharma |
| CNC Tending | 20 – 100 | 6 | $\pm 0.05$ | Metal machining |
| Assembly | 1 – 10 | 4–6 | $\pm 0.01$ | Electronics, medical devices |
| Inspection (Vision) | 2 – 10 | 3–4 | $\pm 0.02$ | Semiconductors, automotive |
| Adhesive Dispensing | 3 – 20 | 5–6 | $\pm 0.05$ | Electronics, automotive glass |
| Deburring | 10 – 50 | 6 (force-controlled) | $\pm 0.1$ | Castings, forging |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the four main categories of robotic applications in manufacturing as per the RIA classification. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As per the Robotics Industries Association (RIA) classification, industrial robot applications in manufacturing are grouped into **four functional categories**:

1. **Material Handling (MH):** Operations involving the movement of parts, such as **pick-and-place** of electronic components from a conveyor to a tray.
2. **Processing Operations (PO):** Operations that transform the workpiece, such as **arc welding** of steel sheets in automotive body fabrication.
3. **Assembly Operations (AO):** Operations that join two or more parts to form a sub-assembly, such as **PCB component insertion** using a SCARA robot.
4. **Quality Inspection (QI):** Operations that verify conformance, such as **vision-based dimensional gauging** of machined components.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for naming the 4 categories, 1 mark for one example each (½ × 4 = 2 marks).

---

### Question 2: List any three factors that justify the deployment of a robot in a manufacturing environment. Briefly explain the economic ROI criterion.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Three primary factors (the **4D Framework**, picking any 3):

1. **Dull Tasks:** Repetitive operations causing operator fatigue (e.g., pallet stacking 2000+ cycles/shift).
2. **Dirty Tasks:** Operations in contaminated environments (e.g., foundry die-casting cells with high particulate matter).
3. **Dangerous Tasks:** Operations posing safety risks (e.g., forge pressing, high-temperature furnaces).
4. **Dear Tasks:** High-precision operations where human error is costly (e.g., semiconductor wafer handling).

**Economic ROI Criterion:** A robot deployment is justified if the **payback period** $T_{ROI} = C_{total} / S_{annual}$ is less than the company's acceptable threshold (typically **24–36 months**).

> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for 3 factors (½ × 3), 1.5 marks for the ROI equation and threshold.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: (a)** Classify the major robotic applications in manufacturing with suitable examples. Discuss the key robot selection parameters (payload, reach, repeatability, DOF) for **arc welding** and **palletizing** applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company is evaluating a robotic palletizing cell. The following data is provided:
- Total installed cost: $C_{total}$ = ₹ 60,00,000
- Labor displaced: 2 workers × 2 shifts
- Hourly labor cost (with benefits): $L_h$ = ₹ 300/hour
- Annual operating hours: $H_y$ = 5000 hours
- Quality cost reduction: $\Delta C_q$ = ₹ 3,00,000/year
- Throughput revenue gain: $\Delta R_t$ = ₹ 4,00,000/year
- Annual operating cost: $C_{op}$ = ₹ 4,50,000/year

Determine the **payback period** and state whether the investment is justified. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a) (7 Marks):

**Classification of robotic applications in manufacturing (4 marks):**

| Category | Sub-Application | Example Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Handling** | Pick & place, Palletizing, Machine tending | FMCG, Automotive |
| **Processing** | Arc welding, Spot welding, Painting, Drilling, Grinding | Automotive, Shipbuilding |
| **Assembly** | PCB assembly, Screwdriving, Insertion | Electronics, Medical devices |
| **Inspection** | Vision gauging, NDT, Surface defect detection | Aerospace, Semiconductors |

**Selection parameters for Arc Welding (1.5 marks):**
- **Payload:** 5–15 kg (welding torch + cable weight)
- **Reach:** 1.5–2.0 m (typical workstation width)
- **Repeatability:** $\pm 0.05$ mm (path-following accuracy)
- **DOF:** 6 axes (full 3D orientation of torch)
- **Special requirement:** Hollow wrist for cable routing, low backlash, **TCP (Tool Center Point) calibration**

**Selection parameters for Palletizing (1.5 marks):**
- **Payload:** 50–700 kg (full pallet layer + gripper)
- **Reach:** 2.5–3.5 m (pallet envelope)
- **Repeatability:** $\pm 0.5$ mm (lower precision acceptable)
- **DOF:** 4 axes (3 translational + 1 wrist rotation is sufficient)
- **Special requirement:** High static stiffness, large base-mounted footprint

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b) (7 Marks):

**Step 1: Identify equations.**

$$S_{annual} = L_h \times H_y \times N_s + \Delta C_q + \Delta R_t - C_{op}$$

$$T_{ROI} = \frac{C_{total}}{S_{annual}}$$

**Step 2: Compute labor savings.**

$$S_{labor} = 300 \times 5000 \times 2 = \text{₹ } 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 3: Compute total annual savings.**

$$S_{annual} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000$$

$$S_{annual} = 37{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 32{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute payback period.**

$$T_{ROI} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{32{,}50{,}000} = 1.846 \text{ years} \approx 22.15 \text{ months}$$

**Step 5: Decision.**

Since $T_{ROI} = 22.15$ months < 36 months (standard threshold), the investment is **economically justified**.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for equations, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for unit conversion (years → months), 1 mark for decision justification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a typical robotic manufacturing cell** for a machine-tending application (CNC lathe tending). Identify the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SCARA robot performs PCB pick-and-place with the following cycle: motion = 0.35 s, grasp = 0.12 s, release = 0.08 s. The availability factor is 0.90. Calculate the **effective production rate** in parts per hour. Also, comment on the impact of increasing cycle time by 20% on throughput. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q(a) (7 Marks):

**Functional Architecture of a CNC Lathe Tending Cell:**

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Input Conveyor / Pallet** | Delivers raw blanks to the cell |
| **2. 6-axis Articulated Robot** | Picks raw blank, loads into CNC chuck |
| **3. End Effector (Pneumatic Gripper)** | Grasps cylindrical blank, exchanges with finished part |
| **4. CNC Lathe** | Performs turning operation; signals door open via M-code |
| **5. Robot Controller (PLC-based)** | Sequences robot, lathe, and conveyor operations |
| **6. HMI Panel** | Operator interface for program selection, status, alarms |
| **7. Safety System (Light Curtain, E-stops)** | Protects human operators from the moving robot |
| **8. Output Conveyor / Inspection Station** | Removes finished part, optionally checks dimensions |

**Block Diagram (textual representation, 3 marks):**

```
INPUT (Pallet) → 6-axis Robot + Gripper → CNC Lathe (M-codes) → Unload → Output Conveyor
                       ↑                            ↓
                Robot Controller (PLC) ← M-code Interlock
                       ↑
                  HMI + Safety PLC
```

**Role of each subsystem (4 marks):** Each of the 8 subsystems above carries 0.5 marks for correctly stating its function.

---

#### Model Solution to Q(b) (7 Marks):

**Step 1: Calculate original cycle time.**

$$t_c = 0.35 + 0.12 + 0.08 = 0.55 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical production rate.**

$$R_{p,theo} = \frac{3600}{0.55} = 6545.45 \text{ parts/hour}$$

**Step 3: Effective production rate.**

$$R_p = 6545.45 \times 0.90 = \boxed{5890.9 \text{ parts/hour}}$$

**Step 4: Impact of 20% increase in cycle time.**

New cycle time: $t_c' = 0.55 \times 1.20 = 0.66$ s

New theoretical rate: $R_{p,theo}' = 3600 / 0.66 = 5454.55$ parts/hour

New effective rate: $R_p' = 5454.55 \times 0.90 = 4909.1$ parts/hour

**Throughput loss:**

$$\Delta R_p = 5890.9 - 4909.1 = 981.8 \text{ parts/hour}$$

$$\text{Percentage loss} = \frac{981.8}{5890.9} \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

> **Insight:** A 20% increase in cycle time causes a **16.67% drop in throughput** (not 20% — this is because of the inverse relationship). This is a common **KTU conceptual trap** question.

> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for original cycle time calculation, 2 marks for effective rate, 1.5 marks for new cycle time analysis, 1.5 marks for percentage loss interpretation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy:** *Repeatability* is the ability to return to a previously taught position (always measurable). *Accuracy* is the ability to move to a position commanded in an absolute coordinate frame (harder to achieve). Examiners will deduct marks for interchange.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in ROI:** Always state the **payback period in years AND months** (e.g., "1.85 years ≈ 22.2 months"). Bare numerical answers lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Inverse-Throughput Trap:** A 20% increase in cycle time does NOT reduce throughput by 20%. It reduces it by approximately **16.67%** due to the inverse relationship $R_p \propto 1/t_c$.
> 4. **SCARA Misnaming:** Do not write "SCARA" as a generic name. Always expand it as **Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm** at least once in the answer.
> 5. **Ignoring the 4D Framework:** When asked "Why robots?" — only listing economic reasons without citing the **Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Dear** framework loses 1 mark.
> 6. **DOF Confusion:** A 6-axis robot has **6 DOF**, not 6 joints. A SCARA has **4 DOF** (R, θ, Z, φ — but $\theta$ is a passive compliance DOF, hence "Selective Compliance").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** An industrial robot is an *automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes* (ISO 8373:2021).
- **4 Application Categories:** Material Handling, Processing, Assembly, Quality Inspection.
- **4D Justification Framework:** Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Dear.
- **ROI Equation:** $T_{ROI} = C_{total} \big/ S_{annual}$, with threshold of **24–36 months**.
- **Robot Types:** Articulated (6-axis), SCARA (4-axis), Delta (3–4 axis parallel), Cartesian (3-axis gantry), Polar, Cylindrical.
- **SCARA** = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm → rigid in Z, compliant in X-Y → ideal for **vertical insertion**.
- **Payload Rule of Thumb:** Select a robot with **payload ≥ 2× actual load** to account for gripper weight and dynamic forces.
- **Repeatability vs. Accuracy:** Industrial robots are *highly repeatable* (typically $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm) but only *moderately accurate* (typically $\pm 1$ to $\pm 5$ mm) — accuracy is improved through **kinematic calibration**.
- **Workspace Rule:** Useful workspace ≈ **60%** of theoretical spherical envelope.
- **Cycle Time Rule:** Throughput $R_p = (3600 / t_c) \times \eta_a$, where $\eta_a$ = availability factor.
- **Industry Concentration:** ~65% of all robot installations are in **automotive, electronics, and metal-machining** sectors.
- **Painting Robots:** Use **hollow wrist** to route paint hoses internally and must be **explosion-proof (ATEX/IECEx certified)** to operate in solvent-laden booths.
- **Palletizing Robots:** Generally 4-axis, high-payload (50–700 kg), with large reach (2.5–3.5 m).
- **Assembly Robots:** Demand the **highest repeatability** ($\pm 0.01$ to $\pm 0.02$ mm), typically SCARA or 6-axis articulated.
- **Industry 4.0 Link:** Robotic manufacturing cells are now integrated with **IoT sensors, MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems), and digital twins** for predictive maintenance and OEE optimization.
- **KTU 2024 OBE Tag:** This topic maps to **CO1 (Fundamentals of Industrial Robotics)** and **CO2 (Applications in Manufacturing Systems)** with Bloom's levels *Understand (L2)* and *Apply (L3)*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages and Limitations of Robotic Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages and Limitations of Robotic Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotic Assembly** is the automated process of joining two or more discrete components into a higher-level sub-assembly or finished product, executed by reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator systems (industrial robots) equipped with appropriate end-effectors, sensors, and intelligent controllers, operating within a structured work envelope.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, the term *assembly* is differentiated from *manufacturing* in a critical way:

$$ \text{Assembly} \subset \text{Manufacturing}, \quad \text{where } A = \{a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n\} \xrightarrow{\text{Robot}} A_{final} $$

- $a_i$ represents individual discrete parts (bolts, gears, PCB components, panels).
- The **robotic system** performs *joining*, *fastening*, *inserting*, or *welding* to form the final product.
- The robot is **reprogrammable**, meaning the same physical robot can assemble different products by simply changing the program — this is the key distinction from *hard automation*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Robotic Assembly Cell as a "Super-Skilled Human Worker Who Never Sleeps."**

Imagine a highly dexterous human worker standing at a workbench:

- This worker has **two perfectly coordinated hands** (the robot's end-effectors / grippers).
- The worker has **perfect memory** of where every single screw, gear, and panel must go (the robot controller's program).
- The worker **never gets tired**, **never gets distracted**, and **does not demand a salary** (high throughput, low marginal cost).
- The worker is **blind and deaf** without sensors — so we must give it cameras (vision systems) and force-sensing fingertips (force/torque sensors) to make it truly intelligent.

**The Limitation Analogy**: Now imagine that same worker is dropped into a *messy room* where the parts are scattered randomly, in different sizes, and the lighting keeps changing. The worker would fail. This is exactly the situation in robotic assembly — it excels in **structured environments** but struggles with **variability and uncertainty**.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used in KTU Evaluation

The following metrics are standard across KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation rubrics:

- **Cycle Time ($T_c$)**: Time to complete one full assembly operation. Measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Throughput ($R$)**: Units produced per hour. Measured in **parts/hour (UPH)**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**: Reliability metric. Measured in **hours**.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)**: Percentage of assemblies produced correctly without rework. Expressed as a **percentage (%)**.

$$ \text{UPH} = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad \text{(units per hour)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask students to compare **human assembly** vs **robotic assembly** using these exact four metrics. Memorize the units.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Advantages — Structured Logical Breakdown

The advantages of robotic assembly are grouped into **four engineering categories** as per the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus structure:

#### 2.1.1 Economic & Productivity Advantages

- **Higher Throughput & 24/7 Operation**: Robots can operate continuously without fatigue, breaks, or shift limitations. A single robot can replace 2–4 human workers on repetitive tasks.
- **Lower Long-Term Marginal Cost**: The *Cost Per Assembly (CPA)* drops dramatically over the product lifecycle.

$$ CPA = \frac{C_{robot} + C_{integration} + C_{maintenance} + C_{energy}}{N_{units}} $$

- As $N_{units} \to \infty$, $CPA$ approaches the operational cost per unit, justifying the capital investment.
- **Reduced Waste & Rework**: Precision reduces defective assemblies. Typical rejection rate drops from **3–5% (manual)** to **<0.5% (robotic)**.

#### 2.1.2 Quality & Precision Advantages

- **Repeatability vs Accuracy**: Robots offer repeatability of **±0.02 mm** to **±0.05 mm** in modern industrial arms.
- **Consistency**: Every unit is assembled *identically*, eliminating human variability.
- **Tight Tolerance Capability**: Enables assembly of miniaturized products (smartphones, medical devices).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction** — *Repeatability* is the ability to return to the same position. *Accuracy* is the ability to reach an absolute target. Robots typically have *high repeatability but moderate accuracy*.

#### 2.1.3 Ergonomic & Safety Advantages

- **Hazardous Task Replacement**: Robots perform tasks in extreme heat, cold, toxic fumes, radiation, or noise.
- **Elimination of Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSI)**: Removes humans from monotonous, joint-damaging motions.
- **Workplace Safety**: Reduces industrial accidents related to heavy lifting and tool operation.

#### 2.1.4 Flexibility & Integration Advantages

- **Reprogrammability**: One robot cell can assemble Product A today, Product B tomorrow.
- **Quick Changeover (Setup Time Reduction)**: Modern cells achieve changeover in minutes, not hours.
- **Industry 4.0 / IoT Compatibility**: Seamless integration with MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) and digital twins.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Limitations — Structured Logical Breakdown

Limitations are categorized into **technical, economic, and operational** constraints.

#### 2.2.1 Technical Limitations

- **High Initial Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)**: A single articulated robot costs between **₹5 Lakh to ₹50 Lakh** depending on payload and reach.
- **Limited Dexterity vs Human Hand**: Standard grippers have 1–2 degrees of freedom (DOF); human hands have **27 DOF**.
- **Sensor Dependency**: Without vision/force sensors, robots are essentially "blind."
- **Programming Complexity**: Complex 3D assembly paths require expert programming (often using teach pendants or offline programming software like RoboDK, DELMIA).

#### 2.2.2 Economic Limitations

- **Long Payback Period**: Typically **2–5 years** depending on production volume.
- **High Integration Cost**: Often, the *integration cost* (fixturing, safety fencing, conveyors, vision) is **3–5 times** the robot cost itself.

$$ C_{total} = C_{robot} + 4 \times C_{robot} = 5 \times C_{robot} \text{ (rule of thumb)} $$

- **Maintenance & Spare Parts**: Specialized technicians required; spare parts are not always locally available in India.

#### 2.2.3 Operational Limitations

- **Lack of Adaptability to Variability**: Cannot easily handle parts with dimensional variations, deformable materials, or unstructured bins (bin-picking is still an open research problem in 2024).
- **Downtime Risk**: A single robot failure can halt the entire production line.
- **Skilled Workforce Requirement**: Kerala's industrial landscape often faces a shortage of robot programmers and maintenance engineers.
- **Workspace Constraints**: Robots require precisely defined work envelopes with safety zones.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Throughput | $R = 3600 / T_c$ | UPH (units/hr) | Productivity calculation |
| 2 | Cost Per Assembly | $CPA = C_{total} / N_{units}$ | ₹/unit | Economic justification |
| 3 | Total System Cost (Rule of Thumb) | $C_{total} \approx 5 \cdot C_{robot}$ | ₹ | Budget estimation |
| 4 | Robot Repeatability | $\Delta r \in [0.02, 0.05]$ | mm | Tolerance feasibility |
| 5 | First Pass Yield Improvement | $\Delta FPY = FPY_{robot} - FPY_{manual}$ | % | Quality comparison |
| 6 | Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | $DOF = n_{rigid\ bodies} \times 6 - n_{constraints}$ | dimensionless | Mechanism analysis |
| 7 | Cycle Time Reduction | $\% \Delta T = \frac{T_{manual} - T_{robot}}{T_{manual}} \times 100$ | % | Performance gain |
| 8 | Payback Period | $P = \frac{C_{investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | years | ROI calculation |
| 9 | Availability | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | Reliability analysis |
| 10 | Robot Work Envelope | $V_{envelope} = f(\text{reach, DOF, joint limits})$ | $m^3$ | Cell layout design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: The **Payback Period formula** is a favorite in Part B (14-mark) questions. Always show the substitution and intermediate steps, not just the final number.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors)**: Robotic welding and body assembly in 90% of major OEMs.
- **Electronics Manufacturing Services (Foxconn, Wistron)**: PCB assembly, smartphone housing.
- **Medical Devices (Stryker, Johnson & Johnson)**: Precision assembly of implants and surgical tools.
- **Kerala Context**: The Kerala State Industrial Development Corporation (KSIDC) is actively promoting robotics in cashew processing, coir handling, and spice packaging — all of which are emerging applications of material handling and assembly robotics.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is **conceptual-economic** rather than purely mathematical. The "derivation" here is an *analytical walkthrough* showing how a student should systematically compare robotic vs manual assembly on a KTU examination paper.

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Cost Per Assembly Justification

**Problem Context**: A manufacturer produces **50,000 units/year**. A manual assembly takes **45 seconds** with 2 workers earning ₹25,000/month. A robotic cell costs ₹15,00,000 (robot) + ₹45,00,000 (integration). The robot performs the same task in **20 seconds**. Maintenance is ₹2,00,000/year. Determine the payback period.

#### Step 1 — Calculate Manual Cost Per Assembly (CPA_manual)

Manual labor cost per year (2 workers):
$$ C_{labor,year} = 2 \times 25{,}000 \times 12 = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Allocation per unit:
$$ CPA_{manual} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = 12 \text{ ₹/unit} $$

*(Note: This ignores overheads for simplicity — examiners often allow this assumption.)*

#### Step 2 — Calculate Robotic Cost Per Assembly (CPA_robot)

Total investment:
$$ C_{total} = 15{,}00{,}000 + 45{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$

Annual maintenance:
$$ C_{maint,year} = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Total annual robot cost (depreciating over 5 years linearly for simplicity):
$$ C_{robot,year} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{5} + 2{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Allocation per unit:
$$ CPA_{robot} = \frac{14{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = 28 \text{ ₹/unit} $$

#### Step 3 — Interpret the Result (Honest Analysis)

$$ CPA_{robot} = 28 \text{ ₹/unit} > CPA_{manual} = 12 \text{ ₹/unit} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Naïve Conclusion Trap**: At first glance, robotic assembly is *more expensive per unit* by ₹16! Many students stop here and conclude "robotics is bad." This is WRONG. The advantages of robotics are in *quality, safety, and consistency*, not just direct labor cost.

The true comparison must include **reject cost savings**:
- Manual rejection rate: **4%** → 2,000 defective units/year
- Robotic rejection rate: **0.5%** → 250 defective units/year
- Cost of one rejected assembly: ₹200 (raw material + partial labor)
- Savings on rejects: $(2000 - 250) \times 200 = 3{,}50{,}000$ ₹/year

#### Step 4 — Revised Payback Calculation

Net annual savings = Labor savings + Reject savings
$$ S_{annual} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}50{,}000 = 9{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Payback period:
$$ P = \frac{C_{investment}}{S_{annual}} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{9{,}50{,}000} = 6.32 \text{ years} $$

#### Step 5 — Throughput Bonus (Often Overlooked by Students)

Production increase due to faster cycle time:
$$ T_{saved} = 45 - 20 = 25 \text{ seconds/unit} $$

Additional units possible (assume 8-hour shift, 250 working days):
$$ \Delta N = \frac{8 \times 3600 \times 250}{25} = 2{,}88{,}000 \text{ extra units/year (theoretical max)} $$

This is the hidden **scalability advantage** of robotic assembly.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU 2024 Evaluation Standard)

| Parameter | Manual Assembly | Robotic Assembly | KTU-Weighted Advantage | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** | 30–60 s | 10–25 s | Robot ~2.5× faster | 2 marks |
| **Hourly Wage (Kerala, 2024)** | ₹150–₹300 | ₹0 (electricity) | Robot ~95% lower | 2 marks |
| **Initial Investment** | Low (~₹50K tooling) | High (₹20L–₹1Cr) | Manual better | 1 mark |
| **Repeatability** | ±1–2 mm | ±0.02–0.05 mm | Robot ~40× better | 3 marks |
| **First Pass Yield** | 92–96% | 99–99.8% | Robot ~3–4% better | 2 marks |
| **24/7 Operation** | No (shifts limited) | Yes | Robot wins decisively | 1 mark |
| **Adaptability to New Product** | High (human learning) | Low (reprogramming cost) | Manual better | 2 marks |
| **Hazardous Environment** | Not feasible | Fully feasible | Robot wins decisively | 2 marks |
| **Dextrous Manipulation** | 27 DOF hand | 1–6 DOF gripper | Manual wins decisively | 2 marks |
| **Ergonomic Injury Rate** | High (RSI) | Zero direct injury | Robot wins | 1 mark |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Marking Pattern**: A typical 14-mark comparison question awards ~2 marks per valid comparison. Students should list **at least 7–8 comparison points** with quantitative justification to score full marks.

### 3.3 Decision Framework — When to Use Robotic Assembly

Based on the analytical walkthrough, KTU examiners expect students to articulate the **decision criteria**:

- **Use Robotic Assembly When**:
  - Production volume $N > 50{,}000$ units/year
  - Cycle time $T_c < 30$ seconds
  - Product is stable (no frequent design changes)
  - Quality tolerance $< 0.1$ mm
  - Hazardous environment exists
  - High repeatability required

- **Use Manual Assembly When**:
  - Low volume, high mix production
  - Product requires high dexterity
  - Frequent design changes expected
  - Limited capital budget
  - Parts have high variability

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1.** Define the term *robotic assembly* and distinguish it from *hard automation* with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Robotic Assembly** is the use of reprogrammable, multifunctional industrial robots to perform joining, fastening, or insertion tasks to combine discrete components into a finished product.
>
> **Distinction from Hard Automation**:
> - **Hard Automation**: Fixed-sequence, dedicated machinery built for *one specific product* (e.g., a dedicated welding line for a particular car model). Cannot be easily reconfigured.
> - **Robotic Assembly**: Uses a *reprogrammable* robot that can be re-tasked via software changes to assemble *multiple products* on the same cell.
> - **Example**: A dedicated cam-operated bolt-tightening machine is *hard automation*; the same KUKA robot that tightens bolts on Product A can be reprogrammed to assemble Product B within hours — this is *robotic assembly*.
>
> *(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1.5 marks distinction + 0.5 mark example)*

---

**Q2.** List any **three technical limitations** of robotic assembly systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three major technical limitations are:
1. **Limited Dexterity** — Robot grippers typically have 1–6 DOF compared to 27 DOF in a human hand, restricting complex manipulation tasks.
2. **Sensor Dependency** — Without vision and force sensors, robots are "blind" and cannot adapt to positional uncertainty in parts.
3. **Programming Complexity** — Complex 3D assembly paths require expert programmers and specialized offline programming software, increasing integration time.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per valid limitation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style — Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **advantages of robotic assembly systems** in modern manufacturing. Categorize them into economic, quality, safety, and flexibility domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company currently uses 3 workers (each earning ₹20,000/month) to perform an assembly task taking 60 seconds per unit. They are evaluating a robotic system costing ₹50,00,000 (total integrated cost) with a cycle time of 18 seconds and annual maintenance of ₹1,50,000. Production volume is 1,00,000 units/year. The defect rate is expected to drop from 3% to 0.5%, with each defective unit costing ₹150 in raw materials. Calculate: **(i) Cost per assembly (manual) (ii) Cost per assembly (robotic) (iii) Payback period. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

The advantages of robotic assembly span four engineering domains:

**1. Economic Advantages (2 marks)**
- Continuous 24/7 operation without fatigue or shift premiums
- Reduced Cost Per Assembly (CPA) at high volumes
- Lower rejection rates save raw material cost
- Long-term ROI justifies capital expenditure

**2. Quality & Precision Advantages (2 marks)**
- Repeatability of ±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm
- Identical assembly every cycle — zero human variability
- Enables miniaturized and tight-tolerance products (PCB, medical devices)

**3. Safety & Ergonomic Advantages (1.5 marks)**
- Replaces humans in hazardous environments (heat, fumes, radiation)
- Eliminates Repetitive Strain Injuries (RSI)
- Compliance with factory safety norms

**4. Flexibility Advantages (1.5 marks)**
- Reprogrammable for new products
- Industry 4.0 / IoT integration with MES
- Quick changeover enables mass customization

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given**:
- Workers: 3 × ₹20,000/month
- $T_{c,manual} = 60$ s, $T_{c,robot} = 18$ s
- $C_{robot,total} = 50{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $C_{maint} = 1{,}50{,}000$ ₹/year
- $N = 1{,}00{,}000$ units/year
- Defect rate: 3% → 0.5%
- Defect cost: ₹150/unit

**(i) Manual CPA**:
$$ C_{labor,year} = 3 \times 20{,}000 \times 12 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$
$$ CPA_{manual} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 7.2 \text{ ₹/unit} $$

**(ii) Robotic CPA** (amortizing robot over 5 years):
$$ C_{robot,year} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{5} + 1{,}50{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 11{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹} $$
$$ CPA_{robot} = \frac{11{,}50{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 11.5 \text{ ₹/unit} $$

**(iii) Payback Period**:
Annual labor savings:
$$ S_{labor} = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Annual reject savings:
$$ N_{defect,manual} = 0.03 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ units} $$
$$ N_{defect,robot} = 0.005 \times 1{,}00{,}000 = 500 \text{ units} $$
$$ S_{reject} = (3{,}000 - 500) \times 150 = 3{,}75{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Total annual savings:
$$ S_{total} = 7{,}20{,}000 + 3{,}75{,}000 = 10{,}95{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} $$

Payback period:
$$ P = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{10{,}95{,}000} \approx 4.57 \text{ years} $$

*(Allocation: 2 marks step (i) + 2 marks step (ii) + 3 marks step (iii) with clear substitution)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain in detail the **limitations of robotic assembly systems**. Discuss both technical and economic limitations with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **manual assembly** and **robotic assembly** on the basis of any **seven parameters**, highlighting where each is superior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (a):**

**Technical Limitations (3.5 marks)**:
1. **High Initial Cost** — A 6-DOF articulated robot with controller costs ₹5 Lakh to ₹50 Lakh; integration can multiply this by 3–5×.
2. **Limited Dexterity** — Industrial grippers cannot match human hand's 27 DOF, restricting complex sub-assemblies.
3. **Sensor Dependency** — A robot without vision/force sensors is "blind"; advanced sensors add ₹2–10 Lakh cost.
4. **Programming Complexity** — Trajectory planning, collision avoidance, and grasp synthesis require expert programmers.
5. **Poor Adaptability** — Cannot handle parts with high dimensional variability (e.g., natural products, soft goods).

**Economic Limitations (2.5 marks)**:
1. **Long Payback Period** — 3–7 years typical; may exceed product lifecycle.
2. **High Maintenance Cost** — Specialized technicians, imported spares.
3. **Hidden Integration Cost** — Fixturing, safety fencing, conveyors, PLCs often exceed robot cost.
4. **Underutilization Risk** — If production volume falls, ROI turns negative.
5. **Training Cost** — Continuous upskilling of operators in Kerala's SME sector is challenging.

---

**Model Answer (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks, 1 mark per valid point)**:

| # | Parameter | Manual | Robotic | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Cost | Low (₹50K–₹2L) | High (₹20L–₹1Cr) | Manual |
| 2 | Cycle Time | 30–60 s | 10–25 s | Robotic |
| 3 | Repeatability | ±1 mm | ±0.05 mm | Robotic |
| 4 | Dexterity | 27 DOF | 1–6 DOF | Manual |
| 5 | 24/7 Operation | No | Yes | Robotic |
| 6 | Adaptability to New Product | High | Moderate | Manual |
| 7 | Hazardous Environment | Unsafe | Safe | Robotic |

*(Allocation: 7 × 1 mark = 7 marks for any 7 valid comparative points)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**:
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write *units* next to numerical answers. Writing "11.5" without "₹/unit" costs 0.5 marks.
> 2. **One-sided answers** — A question on "advantages" that ignores limitations (or vice versa) is incomplete. KTU 2024 expects balanced technical answers.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions in numericals** — Always state: *"Assuming linear depreciation over 5 years..."* or *"Ignoring electricity cost..."*
> 4. **Confusing repeatability with accuracy** — Robots have high repeatability but not always high accuracy. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.
> 5. **Not mentioning integration cost** — In payback calculations, many students use only the robot cost. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this oversight.
> 6. **Memorizing without understanding** — KTU 2024 (NEP 2020) tests *application*, not recall. Numerical problems with new values are common. Practice substitution.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Advantages and Limitations of Robotic Assembly**

- [x] **Definition**: Reprogrammable robot joining discrete parts into a finished product.
- [x] **Difference from Hard Automation**: Reprogrammability is the key distinction.
- [x] **Four Advantage Categories**: Economic, Quality, Safety, Flexibility.
- [x] **Three Limitation Categories**: Technical, Economic, Operational.
- [x] **Key Metrics**: Cycle Time ($T_c$), Throughput (UPH), MTBF, FPY, Repeatability (±0.02 mm), Payback Period.
- [x] **Hidden Cost Rule**: $C_{total} \approx 5 \times C_{robot}$ (integration dominates).
- [x] **Payback Formula**: $P = C_{investment} / S_{annual}$ — always include reject savings.
- [x] **Decision Threshold**: $N > 50{,}000$ units/year, $T_c < 30$ s, tolerance $< 0.1$ mm.
- [x] **Kerala Context**: Emerging applications in cashew, coir, and spice industries.
- [x] **Industry 4.0 Hook**: Robotic assembly integrates with IoT, digital twins, and MES.
- [x] **Examiner Pet Topics**: Repeatability vs accuracy, integration cost, hybrid cells, ROI.
- [x] **Memorize Units**: ₹/unit for CPA, UPH for throughput, mm for tolerance, years for payback.

**End of Module 1 Notes — Advantages and Limitations of Robotic Assembly**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Programming and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Robot Programming and Control # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Robot Programming** is the systematic process of generating a sequence of executable instructions that define the motion, actions, and logical decisions a robotic manipulator must perform to accomplish a specified task within its workspace. It serves as the **cognitive layer** between human intent and machine actuation. **Robot Control** is the engineering discipline concerned with governing the dynamic behavior of a robot's actuators (joint motors, end-effector drives) using feedback mechanisms so that the manipulator follows the programmed trajectory with prescribed accuracy, speed, and stability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** According to the NEP 2020 aligned OERUT724 syllabus, Robot Programming and Control forms the **bridge between kinematics (Module 1)** and **intelligent automation (Module 3)**. Without precise control, no kinematic model is practically useful. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving School" Intuition Think of a robot as a **newly licensed car** and the programmer as a **driving instructor**. - **Lead-Through Programming** = The instructor physically grabs the steering wheel while you sit in the passenger seat, moving your hands to demonstrate the path. - **Teach-Pendant Programming** = The instructor uses a remote control (joystick/buttons) to drive the car while you watch and record every action. - **Offline Programming (OLP)** = The instructor draws a route on a **simulated map** on a computer before you ever start the engine. - **Closed-Loop Control** = The instructor continuously watches the road, the speedometer, and your position, shouting corrections: *"Turn left! Slow down! Straighten up!"* — this is **real-time feedback**. - **Open-Loop Control** = The instructor pre-records a fixed set of steering angles and accelerator positions, plays them back, and **never watches the road** — this works only if the environment is perfectly predictable. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Modern industrial robots universally employ **closed-loop (servo) control** because manufacturing environments have variable loads, friction changes over time, and external disturbances that an open-loop system cannot reject. ## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU Board Favourites) - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent joint variables, typically **n = 6** for serial industrial manipulators. - **Workspace Reach:** Maximum radial envelope, typically **0.5 m to 3.0 m** for assembly-class robots. - **Repeatability:** Statistical measure (in **mm** or **µm**) of the robot's ability to return to a taught position — *not the same as accuracy*. - **Path Accuracy:** Maximum deviation (in **mm**) between the commanded and actual trajectory during motion. - **Settling Time:** Time (in **seconds**) for joint oscillations to decay within a specified error band (typically **±0.1 mm**). - **Cycle Time:** Total time (in **seconds**) to complete one full assembly cycle — a primary KPI in manufacturing. > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Memory Trick:** *Repeatability is "How close do I come back to the same spot?"* while *Accuracy is "How close did I come to the spot you asked for?"*.
