Most industrial robots are **highly repeatable but only moderately accurate**, which is why we TEACH them rather than program them numerically. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Robot Programming Methods Robot programming is classified into **three primary paradigms**, each representing a different level of abstraction and human-machine interaction. ### 2.1.1 Level 1 — Manual / Lead-Through Programming - **Mechanism:** The operator physically moves the robot arm through the desired sequence of positions; joint encoders record each pose. - **Variants:** * **Powered Lead-Through:** The robot is in a zero-gravity (gravity-compensated) mode; the operator moves the arm with minimal force. Used for large **paint spraying** and **welding** robots. * **Manual Lead-Through:** Operator moves each joint individually using a teach handle. - **Advantages:** Extremely intuitive, no programming knowledge required, ideal for simple paint/weld paths. - **Disadvantages:** No symbolic abstraction, cannot incorporate sensor logic, cumbersome for complex assemblies, accuracy depends on operator skill. - **Typical Industries:** Automotive body painting, arc welding seams, sealant application. ### 2.1.2 Level 2 — Teach-Pendant Programming (Point-to-Point / Walk-Through) - **Mechanism:** A **handheld pendant** with buttons, joystick, and a small LCD/LED display is used to jog the robot joint-by-joint or in Cartesian frames to desired waypoints. Each waypoint is **stored with a label** (e.g., `P1`, `P2`, `P3`). - **Programming Constructs Supported:** Variable definitions, IF-THEN-ELSE, FOR/WHILE loops, subroutines, I/O triggering, register arithmetic. - **Languages Backing This:** VAL II, AML, KAREL, RAPL-3, MML. - **Advantages:** Combines physical teaching with structured logical programming; portable across cells; supports sensor integration. - **Disadvantages:** Slow for large programs, requires the robot to be taken out of production during teaching (downtime loss), pendant screens are small. ### 2.1.3 Level 3 — Offline Programming (OLP) / Text-Based High-Level Languages - **Mechanism:** Programs are written on a **separate workstation** using either: (a) High-level textual languages (e.g., **VAL, AML, RAIL, PASRO, RCCL**), or (b) Graphical simulation environments (e.g., **ABB RobotStudio, KUKA.Sim, FANUC Roboguide, Yaskawa MotoSim**). - **Workflow:** CAD model → Cell layout → Path planning → Collision check → Code generation → Download to controller → Calibration correction. - **Advantages:** Zero production downtime, complex paths feasible, can simulate multi-robot cells, supports CAD-based path generation. - **Disadvantages:** Calibration drift between simulated and real world (typically **1–5 mm**); steep learning curve; high software cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis:** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the **trade-off triangle** — *Programming Speed vs. Program Complexity vs. Production Downtime*. A 14-mark question will almost certainly ask you to compare these three levels using a real industrial example. ## 2.2 Classification of Robot Control Systems ### 2.2.1 By Feedback Architecture | Control Type | Feedback | Typical Use | Cost | |--------------|----------|-------------|------| | **Open-Loop (Non-Servo)** | None | Toy robots, simple pick-and-place, low-cost mechanisms | Very Low | | **Closed-Loop (Servo)** | Position/Velocity | Industrial assembly, welding, painting, machining | High | | **Adaptive Control** | Real-time parameter estimation | Unknown payloads, flexible arms, space robotics | Very High | | **Hybrid Force/Position** | Force + Position | Deburring, polishing, assembly with contact | High | ### 2.2.2 By Control Hierarchy Modern robot controllers implement a **three-tier hierarchical architecture**: 1. **High-Level Planner:** Generates task-level commands (e.g., "pick bolt from tray, insert into hole"). 2. **Trajectory Level:** Converts planned waypoints into time-parameterized joint trajectories using **linear, parabolic, or cubic blends** (Law of Trapezoidal Velocity Profile). 3. **Servo Level:** Runs at **1–4 kHz** sampling rate, executes PID control on each joint motor, resolves kinematics in real time. ## 2.3 The Servo Control Loop — Step-by-Step Logic For each robot joint, a **closed-loop servo system** operates continuously: 1. **Reference Input:** The trajectory generator outputs a desired joint angle $\theta_d(t)$ at time $t$. 2. **Error Computation:** The controller subtracts the actual feedback $\theta_a(t)$ from the reference: $$e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta_a(t)$$ 3. **Control Law Application:** A PID controller produces a motor voltage $u(t)$: $$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ 4. **Actuation:** The DC servo motor / AC servomotor / harmonic-drive actuator receives voltage $u(t)$ and produces torque $\tau_m(t)$. 5. **Plant Dynamics:** The robot joint (with its inertia $J$, damping $b$, and gravity load $G(\theta)$) responds: $$J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} + G(\theta) = \tau_m$$ 6. **Feedback:** The optical/ magnetic encoder measures the new $\theta_a(t)$ and closes the loop. > [!NOTE] > **Why PID?** Proportional (P) gives fast response, Integral (I) eliminates steady-state error (critical for assembly accuracy), Derivative (D) damps overshoot and oscillation. The KTU board loves asking why we need **all three** terms in robotic joint control. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Physical Meaning | |---|-------------------|---------------|-------|------------------| | 1 | PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Volt | Motor command signal | | 2 | Position Error | $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta_a(t)$ | rad or deg | Instantaneous tracking error | | 3 | Joint Dynamics | $J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} + G(\theta) = \tau_m$ | N·m | Newton's 2nd law for rotation | | 4 | Trapezoidal Velocity Peak | $v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$ | m/s | Max speed for distance $d$ and accel $a$ | | 5 | Trapezoidal Cycle Time | $T = 2v_{max}/a_{max} + (d - v_{max}^2/a_{max})/v_{max}$ | s | Total motion time | | 6 | Cubic Polynomial Segment | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | rad | Smooth trajectory interpolation | | 7 | Jacobian Velocity Mapping | $\dot{x} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta}$ | m/s | Joint-to-Cartesian velocity | | 8 | Repeatability (3σ) | $R = \pm 3 \sigma_{pos}$ | mm | 99.7% confidence band | | 9 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | mm or rad | Final positioning inaccuracy | | 10 | Bandwidth | $\omega_b \approx \sqrt{K_p / J}$ | rad/s | Speed of servo response | > [!TIP] > **Critical Notation Rule for Tables:** Absolute value and determinant notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (e.g., $\vert J(\theta) \vert = \det J$) to prevent markdown table syntax breakage. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Assembly Lines:** ABB and KUKA robots use **closed-loop servo control with teach-pendant programming** to perform spot welding with cycle times of **60–90 seconds** per vehicle and repeatability of **±0.05 mm**. - **PCB Manufacturing:** Pick-and-place machines use **vision-servo hybrid control** to align components with **±25 µm** accuracy. - **Space Robotics (Canadarm2 on ISS):** Uses **adaptive control** because payload inertia varies as astronauts grip the end-effector. - **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Use **force-position hybrid control** so surgeons feel tissue resistance while scaling motions by 10:1. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — The Trapezoidal Velocity Profile for a Single Joint This is the **most-tested derivation** in the KTU 2024 scheme for this module. We will derive the peak velocity, acceleration time, and total cycle time for a point-to-point motion over a joint displacement $d$. ### Given: - Total joint displacement: $d$ (radians or meters) - Maximum allowable acceleration: $a_{max}$ - Maximum allowable velocity: $v_{max}$ (a constraint imposed by the motor) ### Assumed Profile: The velocity vs. time graph is a **trapezoid** with three phases: 1. **Acceleration phase** (duration $t_1$): velocity rises linearly from $0$ to $v_{max}$. 2. **Constant velocity phase** (duration $t_2$): velocity remains at $v_{max}$. 3. **Deceleration phase** (duration $t_3$): velocity falls linearly from $v_{max}$ to $0$. ### Step 1 — Acceleration Time During the acceleration phase, the velocity increases at a constant rate $a_{max}$: $$v_{max} = a_{max} \cdot t_1 \implies t_1 = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$ ### Step 2 — Deceleration Time By symmetry of the trapezoid (assuming symmetric acceleration and deceleration limits): $$t_3 = t_1 = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$ ### Step 3 — Distance Covered During Acceleration The area under the velocity-time triangle in phase 1 is: $$d_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot v_{max} \cdot t_1 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot v_{max} \cdot \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}}$$ ### Step 4 — Distance Covered During Deceleration By symmetry: $$d_3 = d_1 = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}}$$ ### Step 5 — Constant Velocity Distance The total distance is the sum of all three phases: $$d = d_1 + d_2 + d_3$$ $$d = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}} + d_2 + \frac{v_{max}^2}{2 a_{max}}$$ $$d_2 = d - \frac{v_{max}^2}{a_{max}}$$ ### Step 6 — Constant Velocity Duration $$t_2 = \frac{d_2}{v_{max}} = \frac{d}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$ ### Step 7 — Total Cycle Time $$T = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 2 \cdot \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \left( \frac{d}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} \right)$$ $$\boxed{T = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \frac{d}{v_{max}}}$$ ### Step 8 — Case Analysis: Is the Profile Truly Trapezoidal? The trapezoidal profile is **valid only if** the constant velocity phase actually exists, i.e., $d_2 \geq 0$: $$d - \frac{v_{max}^2}{a_{max}} \geq 0 \implies v_{max} \leq \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$$ > If the commanded velocity exceeds $\sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$, the profile degenerates into a **triangular velocity profile** (no constant phase). The KTU board often tests this conditional. ### Step 9 — Triangular Profile Total Time (Special Case) When $v_{max} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$ (boundary case), or when commanded $v_{max}$ is too high: $$T_{tri} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{d}{a_{max}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation II — Cubic Polynomial Trajectory for Smooth Blending For applications demanding **jerk-limited motion** (e.g., wafer handling, medical robotics), a cubic polynomial in time is used to interpolate between two waypoints. ### Boundary Conditions: At $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ and $\dot{\theta}(0) = v_0$ At $t = T$: $\theta(T) = \theta_f$ and $\dot{\theta}(T) = v_f$ ### Step 1 — Assume the Form $$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$ ### Step 2 — Differentiate to Get Velocity $$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Initial Conditions From $\theta(0) = \theta_0$: $a_0 = \theta_0$ From $\dot{\theta}(0) = v_0$: $a_1 = v_0$ ### Step 4 — Apply Final Conditions From $\theta(T) = \theta_f$: $a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = \theta_f$ From $\dot{\theta}(T) = v_f$: $a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = v_f$ ### Step 5 — Solve the Linear System Substitute $a_0$ and $a_1$ into the final two equations: $$\theta_0 + v_0 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = \theta_f$$ $$v_0 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = v_f$$ Solving simultaneously (using elimination): $$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2 v_0 + v_f}{T}$$ $$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2}$$ ### Step 6 — Final Compact Form $$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_0 + v_0 t + \left( \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2 v_0 + v_f}{T} \right) t^2 + \left( \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2} \right) t^3}$$ This ensures **C¹ continuity** (continuous position AND velocity) between waypoints — a strict requirement for high-speed robotic assembly. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Programming Method Selection | Application | Recommended Method | Justification | Controller Type | |-------------|--------------------|---------------|-----------------| | Car body painting | Lead-through | Smooth continuous free-form path | PTP + CP servo | | Spot welding (auto) | Teach-pendant | Discrete weld points, high repeatability | Closed-loop PTP | | Electronic PCB pick-and-place | Offline (vision-guided) | High density, frequent program changes | Vision-servo hybrid | | Palletizing (heavy boxes) | Teach-pendant | Few waypoints, payload-aware motion | Closed-loop PTP | | Additive manufacturing (3D print) | Offline (G-code) | Complex slicing paths, layered motion | Open-loop acceptable | | Deburring of castings | Force/position hybrid | Contact force must be regulated | Hybrid force control | | Surgical suturing | Offline + teleoperation | Safety-critical, high precision | Master-slave teleop | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Heuristic for KTU Exam:** > (a) **Continuous free-form path** → Lead-through. > (b) **Discrete waypoints with logic** → Teach-pendant. > (c) **High complexity, zero downtime** → Offline programming. > (d) **Variable environment** → Sensor-based adaptive control. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Three-Tier Robot Controller Architecture The following Mermaid block depicts the **hierarchical control architecture** of a modern industrial robot, from the highest task planner down to the joint-level servo loop. ```mermaid flowchart TB TaskPlanner[Task Level Planner
High Level Goal Logic] --> TrajGen[Trajectory Generator
Time Parameterized Path] TrajGen --> InvKin[Inverse Kinematics Solver
Joint Angle Computation] InvKin --> ServoLoop[Joint Servo Controller
PID Algorithm at 1 to 4 kHz] ServoLoop --> Motor[Servo Motor Driver
Power Amplifier Stage] Motor --> JointActuator[Robot Joint Actuator
Harmonic Drive or RV Reducer] JointActuator --> MechanicalLink[Mechanical Link and Payload] MechanicalLink --> Encoder[Optical or Magnetic Encoder
Position and Velocity Feedback] Encoder --> ServoLoop TrajGen --> FTSensor[Force Torque Sensor Monitor] FTSensor --> ServoLoop ExternalIO[External I/O Triggers
Conveyor and Gripper Signals] --> TrajGen VisionSys[Vision System Processor] --> TrajGen ``` **Reading Guide:** The blue feedback arrows (Encoder, Force Sensor) close the **inner servo loop**, while the red arrows from Vision and I/O represent **outer loop corrections** to the trajectory plan. ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Robot Programming Method Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([New Robotic Task Identified]) --> Q1{Path Complexity?} Q1 -->|Simple free form| Q2{Task Repetition Frequency?} Q1 -->|Discrete waypoints| Q3{Production Downtime Tolerance?} Q1 -->|Complex 3D path| OLP[Use Offline Programming
Simulation and Code Gen] Q2 -->|Few units| LeadThrough[Use Lead Through Teaching
Manual Demonstration] Q2 -->|Many units| Pendant[Use Teach Pendant
Structured Waypoint List] Q3 -->|Zero tolerance| Pendant Q3 -->|Downtime OK| Q4{Programmer Skill Level?} Q4 -->|Low| LeadThrough Q4 -->|High| Pendant OLP --> Calib[Perform Cell Calibration
Correct Sim to Real Drift] Pendant --> Test[Run Dry Cycle Test] LeadThrough --> Test Calib --> Test Test --> Deploy([Deploy to Production]) ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Closed-Loop Servo Control Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Ref[Reference Input
theta desired] --> SumJunction{Summing Junction} Fbk[Feedback Signal
theta actual] --> SumJunction SumJunction --> ErrSig[Error Signal e of t] ErrSig --> PIDBlock[PID Controller
Kp Ki Kd Tuning] PIDBlock --> Amp[Power Amplifier
Voltage or Current Driver] Amp --> MotorPlant[DC or AC Servo Motor
Plant Dynamics] MotorPlant --> JointOutput[Joint Output Position] JointOutput --> Load[Robot Link and Payload] Load --> Sensor[Encoder and Tachogenerator] Sensor --> Fbk Load --> Dist[Disturbance Torque
Gravity Friction External] Dist -.-> MotorPlant ``` > [!NOTE] > **Disturbance Rejection Insight:** The dashed arrow from the disturbance node to the motor plant represents **unmodeled effects** (e.g., a part slipping in the gripper, increasing the inertia). The PID loop must compensate for these in real time, which is why the **Integral term** is critical — it eliminates persistent steady-state errors caused by constant disturbances such as gravity loading. ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — State Machine for a Pick-and-Place Program The following diagram models the **logical flow** of a typical KTU-assignment robot program. It illustrates how conditional branching, I/O, and motion primitives interleave. ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Initialize Initialize --> HomePosition : Drive to Home HomePosition --> WaitPart : Sensor READY equals TRUE WaitPart --> GripperOpen : Send OUTPUT 1 equals ON GripperOpen --> ApproachP1 : Move LINEAR to P1 ApproachP1 --> DescendP2 : Move LINEAR to P2 DescendP2 --> GripperClose : Send OUTPUT 2 equals ON GripperClose --> WaitGrip : DELAY 0.5 seconds WaitGrip --> AscendP3 : Move LINEAR to P3 AscendP3 --> MoveToPlace : Move JOINT to P4 MoveToPlace --> ReleasePart : Send OUTPUT 2 equals OFF ReleasePart --> ReturnHome : Move LINEAR to P5 ReturnHome --> HomePosition WaitPart --> AlarmState : Sensor READY equals FALSE for 10 s AlarmState --> Halt : STOP ALL and SIGNAL TOWER RED Halt --> [*] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] **Define the term "Teach Pendant" and list its three primary functions in industrial robot programming.** **Model Answer:** A teach pendant is a **handheld, programmable control device** connected to the robot controller via a cable or wireless link. It combines a joystick, push buttons, and a small graphical display to enable on-site programming of the manipulator. Its three primary functions are: 1. **Manual Jogging:** Moving individual joints or Cartesian axes of the robot to desired positions. 2. **Waypoint Recording:** Saving each target position with a label (e.g., `P1`, `P2`) into the controller memory. 3. **Program Editing:** Writing logical constructs (IF-THEN, FOR loops), inserting I/O commands, and saving the complete task program for playback. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Open-Loop and Closed-Loop control in robotic systems with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop Control | |---------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Feedback** | Absent | Position/velocity sensor feedback | | **Accuracy** | Low to moderate | High | | **Cost** | Very low | High (sensors + processor) | | **Example** | Toy robot arm using stepper motors | Industrial welding robot with encoders | | **Error Correction** | Not possible | Continuous via PID controller | | **Disturbance Rejection** | None | Robust | > **Example:** A **toy robotic arm** is open-loop (driven by stepper motors with no encoder), while a **KUKA KR 16 welding robot** is closed-loop (servo motors with optical encoders and a PID loop running at 4 kHz). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted] **(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, explain the **three levels of robot programming** and give one industrial example for each level. **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **total cycle time** for a trapezoidal velocity profile and state the condition under which the profile degenerates into a triangular profile. --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution The three levels of robot programming, in increasing order of abstraction, are: **Level 1 — Lead-Through Programming:** The operator physically grasps the robot end-effector (in a gravity-compensated mode for powered lead-through) and moves it along the desired trajectory. Joint encoders record the entire continuous path at high sampling rates. * **Industrial Example:** Automotive **body painting** at a Ford plant using a FANUC paint robot. The operator traces the contours of the car door once, and the robot replays the path millions of times with paint-flow control synchronized to the speed. **Level 2 — Teach-Pendant Programming:** A handheld pendant with a joystick and keypad is used to jog the robot to discrete waypoints, which are saved with numeric labels. A high-level language (VAL II, KAREL) constructs the motion and logic flow. * **Industrial Example:** **Spot welding** on a Toyota assembly line. The operator teaches 12–20 weld points per station, programs the weld current and time via the pendant, and the robot executes the sequence every 60 seconds. **Level 3 — Offline Programming (OLP):** Programs are written on a remote PC using either text-based languages (RAIL, AML) or graphical simulation software (ABB RobotStudio). The complete cell is simulated, paths are collision-checked, and the code is downloaded to the controller. * **Industrial Example:** **Aerospace wing drilling** at Airbus. Hundreds of thousands of rivet holes are programmed offline from CATIA CAD data, achieving 99% reduction in programming time compared to teach-pendant. **Comparison Block Diagram (textual representation):** ``` LEVEL 1 ── Lead Through ── Continuous Path Only ── No Logic │ ▼ LEVEL 2 ── Teach Pendant ── Discrete Waypoints + Logic ── Most Common │ ▼ LEVEL 3 ── Offline / Text ── Full Task + Simulation ── Zero Downtime ``` --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution Consider a point-to-point motion with displacement $d$, maximum acceleration $a_{max}$, and a commanded peak velocity $v_{max}$. The trapezoidal velocity profile has three phases: acceleration ($t_1$), constant velocity ($t_2$), and deceleration ($t_3$). **Step 1:** During acceleration, $v = a_{max} \cdot t_1$, so: $$t_1 = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$ **Step 2:** By symmetry, $t_3 = t_1 = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$. **Step 3:** Distance covered during acceleration and deceleration (triangular area): $$d_1 + d_3 = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot v_{max} \cdot t_1 = \frac{v_{max}^2}{a_{max}}$$ **Step 4:** Constant velocity distance: $$d_2 = d - \frac{v_{max}^2}{a_{max}}$$ **Step 5:** Constant velocity time: $$t_2 = \frac{d_2}{v_{max}} = \frac{d}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$ **Step 6:** Total cycle time: $$T = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = 2 \cdot \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \frac{d}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$$ $$\boxed{T = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \frac{d}{v_{max}}}$$ **Step 7 — Degeneration Condition:** A trapezoidal profile exists only if $d_2 \geq 0$: $$d \geq \frac{v_{max}^2}{a_{max}} \implies v_{max} \leq \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$$ If the commanded $v_{max}$ exceeds $\sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$, the constant phase vanishes and the profile becomes **triangular**, with peak velocity: $$v_{peak} = \sqrt{a_{max} \cdot d}$$ and total time: $$T_{tri} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{d}{a_{max}}}$$ --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted — Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PID control algorithm** for a single robot joint and discuss the effect of each gain ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) on the system's response. Mention one limitation of pure PID in robotics. **(b) [7 Marks]** A robot joint must move from $\theta_0 = 10°$ to $\theta_f = 70°$ in $T = 4$ seconds with initial and final velocities of $v_0 = 5°/s$ and $v_f = 2°/s$. Compute the coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ of the cubic trajectory $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ and find the joint angle at $t = 2$ s. --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller** is the workhorse of robotic joint servos. The control law is: $$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ where $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta_a(t)$ is the position error. **Effect of $K_p$ (Proportional Gain):** - Increases the speed of response (higher bandwidth). - Reduces (but does not eliminate) steady-state error. - Too high $K_p$ causes **overshoot and oscillation**; can destabilize the system. - Acts on the **present** error. **Effect of $K_i$ (Integral Gain):** - Eliminates **steady-state error** completely (essential for assembly accuracy). - Accumulates past errors; acts as a "memory" of persistent offsets. - Too high $K_i$ causes **integrator wind-up** and slow oscillation. - Acts on the **accumulated past** error. **Effect of $K_d$ (Derivative Gain):** - Provides a **braking action** proportional to the rate of change of error. - Damps overshoot, improves stability, sharpens transient response. - Sensitive to measurement noise (the derivative of noise is large). - Acts as a prediction of **future** error. **Limitation of Pure PID in Robotics:** Pure PID is a **linear, single-input single-output** control law. Robot joints, however, are **multi-variable, non-linear, and coupled** (motion of joint 2 affects joint 1's dynamics through reaction torques). Pure PID tuned at one operating point performs poorly at another. Therefore, modern robot controllers add: - **Feed-forward torque compensation** (gravity, Coriolis, inertia terms), or - **Computed torque control** using the full dynamic model, or - **Adaptive / sliding-mode control** for high-performance tasks. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Given:** $\theta_0 = 10°$, $\theta_f = 70°$, $T = 4$ s, $v_0 = 5°/s$, $v_f = 2°/s$. **Step 1 — Coefficient $a_0$:** From $\theta(0) = \theta_0$: $$a_0 = \theta_0 = 10°$$ **Step 2 — Coefficient $a_1$:** From $\dot{\theta}(0) = v_0$: $$a_1 = v_0 = 5°/s$$ **Step 3 — Coefficient $a_2$:** Using the standard cubic formula: $$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^2} - \frac{2 v_0 + v_f}{T}$$ $$a_2 = \frac{3 \cdot (70 - 10)}{4^2} - \frac{2 \cdot 5 + 2}{4}$$ $$a_2 = \frac{3 \cdot 60}{16} - \frac{12}{4}$$ $$a_2 = \frac{180}{16} - 3 = 11.25 - 3 = 8.25°/s^2$$ **Step 4 — Coefficient $a_3$:** $$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{T^2}$$ $$a_3 = \frac{-2 \cdot 60}{64} + \frac{5 + 2}{16}$$ $$a_3 = \frac{-120}{64} + \frac{7}{16}$$ $$a_3 = -1.875 + 0.4375 = -1.4375°/s^3$$ **Step 5 — Joint Angle at $t = 2$ s:** $$\theta(2) = 10 + 5 \cdot 2 + 8.25 \cdot (2)^2 + (-1.4375) \cdot (2)^3$$ $$\theta(2) = 10 + 10 + 8.25 \cdot 4 - 1.4375 \cdot 8$$ $$\theta(2) = 10 + 10 + 33 - 11.5 = 41.5°$$ $$\boxed{\theta(2) = 41.5°}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Errors in $a_3$:** Students frequently forget the negative sign in $a_3 = -2(\Delta\theta)/T^3$. The cubic coefficient is almost always negative for forward decelerating motion. > 2. **Forgetting the Derivative Boundary Conditions:** Many students use only position boundary conditions, yielding only TWO equations for FOUR unknowns. Both **position AND velocity** boundary conditions at $t = 0$ and $t = T$ are mandatory. > 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing degrees and radians in the same equation is the #1 cause of zero marks. Ensure all angles and angular velocities share a single unit. > 4. **Skipping the Trapezoidal Derivation Steps:** For 7-mark questions, you must show the **distance computation $d_2$** explicitly. The examiner will not award partial credit for jumping to the final answer. > 5. **Confusing Repeatability with Accuracy:** A classic 2-mark trap. Repeatability = consistency; Accuracy = closeness to target. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Module 2 Revision — Robot Programming and Control** - **Three programming levels** in increasing abstraction: **Lead-Through → Teach-Pendant → Offline Programming (OLP)**. - **Lead-through** is best for **continuous free-form paths** (painting, welding seams). - **Teach-pendant** is the **industry default** for discrete waypoint tasks with logic (spot welding, palletizing). - **OLP** is mandatory for **complex, high-volume, or multi-robot** cells where downtime is unacceptable. - **Open-loop** control has **no feedback**; used in toy/stepper-based systems. - **Closed-loop (servo)** control uses **PID**; the only choice for industrial accuracy. - **PID Law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$. - **$K_p$** → speed of response; **$K_i$** → eliminates steady-state error; **$K_d$** → damps overshoot. - **Trapezoidal profile cycle time:** $T = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} + \dfrac{d}{v_{max}}$ (valid only if $v_{max} \leq \sqrt{a_{max} d}$). - **Triangular profile (degenerate case):** $T = 2\sqrt{d/a_{max}}$. - **Cubic polynomial** ensures **C¹ continuity** (smooth position + velocity between waypoints). - **Cubic coefficient formulas:** $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = v_0$, $a_2 = 3\Delta\theta/T^2 - (2v_0 + v_f)/T$, $a_3 = -2\Delta\theta/T^3 + (v_0 + v_f)/T^2$. - **Joint servo sampling rate** is typically **1–4 kHz**. - **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy** — industrial robots are highly repeatable but only moderately accurate. - **Three-tier controller architecture:** Task Planner → Trajectory Generator → Servo Loop. - **Hybrid force/position control** is used for **contact tasks** (deburring, polishing, assembly insertion). - **Offline programming calibration drift** is typically **1–5 mm** between simulation and reality. - **KTU Board Numerical Favourite:** Cubic trajectory coefficient computation (always show all four coefficients and verify at one intermediate time instant). - **High-yield jargon to memorize:** Waypoint, Tool Center Point (TCP), Base Frame, World Frame, Joint Coordinates, Cartesian Coordinates, Servo, Settling Time, Bandwidth, Trapezoidal Profile, Cubic Spline, Lead-Through, Kinematic Calibration. > **End of Module 2 Notes — Robot Programming and Control. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation!**

topics.title: Basics of Robot Programming: Introduction to Robot Programming Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics of Robot Programming: Introduction to Robot Programming Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Basics of Robot Programming: Introduction to Robot Programming Languages

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robot Programming** is the process of generating a set of coded instructions (a program) that commands an industrial robot to perform a specific sequence of tasks—such as motion, gripping, sensing, and decision-making—in a controlled, repeatable, and autonomous manner within its operational workspace.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT724) syllabus framework, a **Robot Programming Language (RPL)** is formally defined as a *high-level, human-readable symbolic notation system* that bridges the gap between the human programmer's task description and the robot controller's low-level servo-motor commands. It encapsulates **motion primitives, sensory feedback loops, I/O control, and logical branching** within a structured syntax that the controller's interpreter or compiler can translate into real-time actuator signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Terminology):**
> - **Robot Program** = The compiled, executable sequence of motion and logic statements.
> - **Robot Programming Language** = The *linguistic framework* (syntax + semantics) used to author that program.
> - **Robot Programming Method** = The *paradigm* by which the program is created (Lead-through, Teach Pendant, Textual, Offline Simulation, etc.).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you hire a new domestic helper who is **extremely strong but knows absolutely nothing** about your house. You cannot hand them a vague instruction like *"clean the kitchen"*. You must give them an exhaustive, step-by-step recipe:

> *"Walk 3 meters forward → turn right 90° → pick up the red mug → carry it to the sink → release the mug → press the tap handle → wait 5 seconds → press the tap handle again → place the mug on the drying rack."*

A **robot is this helper**: it has powerful actuators (arms, grippers, wheels) but **zero intuition**. A robot programming language is the **structured vocabulary** you use to write this recipe, and the controller is the **helper's brain** that translates your words into muscle movements.

| Analogy Element | Robotic Equivalent | Physical Constants/Metrics |
|---|---|---|
| Domestic helper | Industrial robot manipulator | **6 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** typical |
| Step-by-step recipe | Robot program | **Joint coordinates** in **mm** or **radians** |
| Spoken instructions | Robot programming language (e.g., VAL II) | **Command cycle time ≈ 8–32 ms** |
| Helper's brain | Robot controller / CPU | **Servo update rate ≥ 100 Hz** |
| Rules of grammar | Language syntax & semantics | **ISO 10166 / IRDATA** standards |

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Examiners reward students who can clearly distinguish between *programming a robot* (writing motion code) and *operating a robot* (manually jogging it). Be precise.

### 1.3 The Fundamental "Why" of Robot Programming

A robot without a program is an inert collection of motors, gears, and sensors. Programming is the act of **imbuing intent** into this hardware. Three essential capabilities are encoded:

1. **Motion Specification** — *Where* the end-effector must travel.
2. **Sequencing** — *In what order* tasks must be performed.
3. **Conditional Logic** — *How* the robot reacts to sensor inputs or errors.

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU 2024 Specificity:** Do NOT confuse "robot programming" with "PLC programming." PLCs use **ladder logic** for sequential I/O; robots use **kinematic-aware motion languages** that inherently understand coordinate frames (World, Tool, Base, Joint).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Classification of Robot Programming Methods

Robot programming methods are broadly classified into **three hierarchical levels** of abstraction. KTU 2024 examiners frequently test a student's ability to map a real-world task to the correct level.

### 2.1.1 Level 1: Lead-Through (Guiding) Programming

- The programmer **physically grasps** the end-effector and guides the robot arm through the desired trajectory.
- The controller **records joint angles** at each recorded point at a fixed sampling rate (typically **30–60 Hz**).
- On playback, the controller interpolates between recorded points.
- **Best for:** Simple paint spraying, arc welding, sealant application.
- **Limitation:** Cannot incorporate complex sensory feedback; requires the robot to be taken offline (out of production).

### 2.1.2 Level 2: Teach Pendant Programming

- Uses a **handheld terminal (Teach Pendant)** connected to the controller.
- The operator "jogs" each axis (joint-by-joint or in Cartesian XYZ) to desired positions, then **records** them as named waypoints.
- The pendant offers a menu-driven interface to insert motion commands, I/O triggers, and simple logic.
- **Best for:** High-precision pick-and-place, palletizing.
- **Advantage:** Robot remains online; very popular on the shop floor.

### 2.1.3 Level 3: Textual (High-Level Language) Programming

- Programs are written as **text files** on an offline PC or the controller's console.
- Languages range from proprietary (VAL II, AML) to general-purpose with robotic libraries (Python with `MoveIt!`, C++ with ROS).
- Supports **variables, loops, subroutines, sensor integration, and error recovery**.
- **Best for:** Complex assembly, vision-guided manipulation, multi-robot coordination.

### 2.1.4 Level 4: Offline Programming (OLP) & Simulation

- Programs are generated in a **virtual 3D environment** (e.g., RoboDK, ABB RobotStudio, FANUC ROBOGUIDE).
- The full workcell is modeled; reach is verified, collisions are detected, and cycle time is estimated **before** any hardware is touched.
- Programs are post-processed into vendor-specific native code.
- **Best for:** Large cells, automotive body shops, brownfield retrofits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight (Frequently Asked):** The question *"Differentiate between on-line and off-line programming"* appears almost every cycle. On-line = pendant/lead-through (robot is part of the loop). Off-line = PC-based simulation (robot is NOT part of the loop during authoring).

## 2.2 Characteristics of an Ideal Robot Programming Language

A high-quality Robot Programming Language (RPL) must satisfy the following engineering criteria. KTU's 2024 scheme explicitly lists these in the Module 2 outcomes (CO2 — *Understand the structure of robot programming languages*):

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clarity and Simplicity** | Reduces programmer error; shortens commissioning time. |
| 2 | **World Modeling Capability** | Must define objects via geometric primitives (point, line, plane, cylinder). |
| 3 | **Sensor Integration** | Native support for force/torque, vision, proximity, and tactile I/O. |
| 4 | **Coordinate Frame Awareness** | Built-in transformations between Joint, World, Tool, and User frames. |
| 5 | **Motion Description** | Supports joint-interpolated, linear, and circular interpolation. |
| 6 | **Real-Time Capability** | Deterministic execution; cycle time jitter < **1 ms** for servo loops. |
| 7 | **Error Handling** | Trap routines, watchdog timers, safe-stop recovery. |
| 8 | **Portability** | Vendor-neutral core (e.g., IRDATA) with vendor-specific extensions. |

## 2.3 Generations of Robot Programming Languages

The KTU 2024 syllabus aligns with the well-established **four-generation model** for RPL evolution:

| Generation | Era | Example Languages | Capability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | 1970s | **WAVE, PAL** | Teach-by-showing; no symbolic world model. |
| **2nd Gen** | Late 1970s–80s | **VAL (Unimation), AML (IBM), RAPT (Edinburgh)** | Textual motion + simple world model. |
| **3rd Gen** | 1980s–90s | **VAL II, KAREL (FANUC), RAPID (ABB), Melfa BASIC (Mitsubishi)** | Structured programming; sensor integration. |
| **4th Gen** | 2000s–Present | **ROS + Python/C++, KUKA Robot Language (KRL) with Java extensions, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox** | Object-oriented, AI/vision integrated, cloud-connected. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this for KTU:** When asked *"Give two examples of 2nd generation robot languages,"* the textbook-correct answers are **VAL** and **AML**. Do not confuse with VAL II (which is 3rd generation).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Conceptual Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a theory-heavy module, the following kinematic reference formulas are **essential** for any motion-related sub-question that may appear in a Part B question.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forward Kinematics (DH) | $T_{i-1}^{i} = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_i) \cdot R_x(\alpha_i)$ | $\theta$ in **rad**, $d, a$ in **mm**, $\alpha$ in **rad** | Maps joint angles to end-effector pose. |
| 2 | Inverse Kinematics (2-link planar) | $\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2+y^2-a_1^2-a_2^2}{2a_1 a_2}\right)$ | $a_1, a_2$ link lengths in **mm** | Solves "where must each joint be?" |
| 3 | Joint-to-Cartesian velocity | $\dot{X} = J(q) \cdot \dot{q}$ | $J$ = Jacobian matrix, $\dot{q}$ in **rad/s** | Used in trajectory planning. |
| 4 | Linear Interpolation (Cartesian) | $P(t) = P_s + \dfrac{(t - t_s)}{(t_e - t_s)}(P_e - P_s)$ | $P_s, P_e$ = start/end points | Generates straight-line motion command. |
| 5 | Joint Interpolation | $q_i(t) = q_{i,s} + \dfrac{(t - t_s)}{(t_e - t_s)}(q_{i,e} - q_{i,s})$ | $q_{i}$ = joint $i$ angle | Default motion type in teach pendant. |
| 6 | Tool Center Point (TCP) transformation | $T_{world}^{TCP} = T_{world}^{base} \cdot T_{base}^{flange} \cdot T_{flange}^{TCP}$ | Homogeneous **4×4** matrices | Used in tool-offset programming. |
| 7 | Velocity scaling | $v_{program} = v_{max} \cdot \text{OVRD}$ | OVRD = override %, range **1–100 %** | Speed control in pendant programming. |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note (KTU Board Exam Style):** The vertical pipe character `\vert` is preferred over `\|` or `|` to avoid markdown table parser conflicts. For example, write $\vert x \vert$, NOT $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **automotive body welding**, FANUC robots are programmed in **KAREL** to perform 200+ spot welds per vehicle, with cycle times under **60 seconds**.
- In **semiconductor cleanrooms**, **4th-generation AI-integrated languages** (Python + ROS) enable vision-guided wafer handling with sub-**micrometer** precision.
- In **logistics warehouses**, OLP-generated KUKA KRL programs drive palletizing cells where reach verification prevents costly post-installation rework.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Coordinate Frames in Robot Programming

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is conceptual rather than derivational. However, to satisfy the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE requirement of exhaustive analytical reasoning, we present a **multi-step worked analytical framework** that demonstrates how a textual robot program is resolved into joint commands. This is a standard 14-mark question pattern.

## 3.1 Problem Setup: From Task Statement to Joint Motion

**Worked Analytical Case:** A 2-DOF planar robot (RR configuration) with link lengths $a_1 = 500\ \text{mm}$ and $a_2 = 400\ \text{mm}$ is commanded by the following high-level program segment:

```
MOVE P1
MOVE P2
```

where $P_1 = (300, 200)$ mm and $P_2 = (-100, 600)$ mm, expressed in the **World frame**.

The robot's controller must perform **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** for each waypoint to convert Cartesian coordinates into joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Apply the IK Equations

For a 2-link planar manipulator in the **elbow-up** configuration, the closed-form IK is:

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - a_1^2 - a_2^2}{2 \cdot a_1 \cdot a_2}\right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2\!\left(a_2 \sin\theta_2,\ a_1 + a_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** These two equations map a 2D Cartesian waypoint $(x, y)$ to the two joint angles required to place the end-effector at that point. They are derived from the cosine rule applied to the triangle formed by the two links and the line from base to end-effector.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Solve for Point P1

Substitute $x = 300$, $y = 200$, $a_1 = 500$, $a_2 = 400$:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = 300^2 + 200^2 = 90000 + 40000 = 130000\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
a_1^2 + a_2^2 = 500^2 + 400^2 = 250000 + 160000 = 410000\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
2 \cdot a_1 \cdot a_2 = 2 \cdot 500 \cdot 400 = 400000\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{130000 - 410000}{400000} = \frac{-280000}{400000} = -0.70
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos(-0.70) \approx 134.43^\circ
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The argument of $\arccos$ is dimensionless, as both numerator and denominator are in $\text{mm}^2$ and cancel. The result is a pure angle. We select the **elbow-up** branch, so $\theta_2$ is positive.

Now compute $\theta_1$:

$$
a_2 \sin\theta_2 = 400 \cdot \sin(134.43^\circ) = 400 \cdot 0.7141 = 285.65\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
a_1 + a_2 \cos\theta_2 = 500 + 400 \cdot (-0.70) = 500 - 280 = 220\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\arctan2(285.65,\ 220) \approx 52.41^\circ
$$

$$
\arctan2(200,\ 300) \approx 33.69^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 33.69^\circ - 52.41^\circ = -18.72^\circ
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The first arctan gives the angle of the wrist with respect to the base, and the second gives the angle of the line from base to end-effector. Their difference yields shoulder joint $\theta_1$.

**Solution for P1:**

$$
\theta_1 \approx -18.72^\circ,\quad \theta_2 \approx 134.43^\circ
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Solve for Point P2

Substitute $x = -100$, $y = 600$:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = (-100)^2 + 600^2 = 10000 + 360000 = 370000\ \text{mm}^2
$$

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{370000 - 410000}{400000} = \frac{-40000}{400000} = -0.10
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \arccos(-0.10) \approx 95.74^\circ
$$

Compute $\theta_1$:

$$
a_2 \sin\theta_2 = 400 \cdot \sin(95.74^\circ) = 400 \cdot 0.9950 = 398.00\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
a_1 + a_2 \cos\theta_2 = 500 + 400 \cdot (-0.10) = 500 - 40 = 460\ \text{mm}
$$

$$
\arctan2(398.00,\ 460) \approx 40.85^\circ
$$

$$
\arctan2(600,\ -100) \approx 99.46^\circ
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 99.46^\circ - 40.85^\circ = 58.61^\circ
$$

**Solution for P2:**

$$
\theta_1 \approx 58.61^\circ,\quad \theta_2 \approx 95.74^\circ
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Generate the Motion Command Stream

The controller's interpreter converts the textual `MOVE P1` and `MOVE P2` commands into the following joint-space execution:

$$
\text{At } t = t_1:\ q = (-18.72^\circ,\ 134.43^\circ)
$$

$$
\text{At } t = t_2:\ q = (58.61^\circ,\ 95.74^\circ)
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Between $t_1$ and $t_2$, the controller interpolates each joint using either **joint-interpolated motion** (smoother joint trajectories, non-linear Cartesian path) or **linear (Cartesian) interpolation** (straight-line TCP path, requires continuous IK solving).

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: On-line vs Off-line Programming

| Evaluation Parameter | On-line (Teach Pendant / Lead-Through) | Off-line (OLP / Simulation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Robot Downtime** | High — robot must be taken offline | Near-zero — programming happens on PC |
| **Program Complexity** | Limited to pendant menu options | Unlimited — full high-level language features |
| **Sensor Integration** | Basic I/O triggers | Full force/vision integration with HIL testing |
| **Cycle Time Optimization** | Trial-and-error on shop floor | Pre-optimized via simulation (e.g., 12% savings) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low (pendant included with robot) | High (OLP software license ≈ $5,000–$50,000) |
| **Best Use Case** | Low-mix, high-volume production | High-mix, complex multi-robot cells |
| **Error Risk** | Low for simple tasks | High if calibration between virtual and real cell drifts |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Examiner Observation:** Students who write "OLP is better than teach pendant" lose marks. The correct KTU-style answer is **"OLP is preferred for complex, high-mix applications, whereas teach pendant is preferred for simple, high-volume, repetitive tasks."** Always contextualize.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams map the **functional architecture** of robot programming — from the high-level task statement down to the low-level servo pulse. These are the kind of block-level functional schematics that KTU examiners reward in 14-mark questions.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Hierarchy of Robot Programming

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Statement in Natural Language] --> B[Offline Simulation Environment]
    B --> C[Post-Processor Generates Vendor Native Code]
    C --> D[Teach Pendant or PC Editor]
    D --> E[Controller Compiler Interpreter]
    E --> F[World Model Database]
    F --> G[Trajectory Planner with IK Solver]
    G --> H[Servo Motor Drive Signals]
    H --> I[Physical Robot Actuator Response]
    I --> J[Sensor Feedback Loop]
    J --> G
    F --> K[Collision Avoidance Module]
    F --> L[Path Optimization Module]
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Robot Programming Functional Pipeline:** Shows the closed-loop flow from human task description to real-time motor actuation. The three side modules (collision avoidance, path optimization, and sensor feedback) represent the value-additions of 3rd and 4th generation languages.

## 4.2 Classification Tree: Robot Programming Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Robot Programming Methods] --> N[Manual On-line Methods]
    M --> O[Textual High-Level Methods]
    M --> P[Offline Programming and Simulation]

    N --> N1[Lead-Through Guiding]
    N --> N2[Teach Pendant Programming]
    N --> N3[Walk-Through for Large Robots]

    O --> O1[First Generation - WAVE and PAL]
    O --> O2[Second Generation - VAL and AML]
    O --> O3[Third Generation - KAREL and RAPID]
    O --> O4[Fourth Generation - ROS with Python]

    P --> P1[3D Cell Modeling]
    P --> P2[Cycle Time Simulation]
    P --> P3[Post-Processing to Vendor Code]
    P --> P4[Reach and Collision Verification]
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Hierarchical Classification:** Demonstrates the three primary branches of robot programming methods with their respective sub-techniques. This tree is the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark question structure.

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[World Frame Origin] --> B[Base Frame]
    B --> C[Joint 1 to Joint N Sequential Transform]
    C --> D[Flange Frame]
    D --> E[Tool Center Point TCP]
    E --> F[Workpiece Frame for Specific Task]
    F --> G[Final Pose in Cartesian XYZ RX RY RZ]
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Coordinate Frame Cascade:** Every robot program implicitly traverses this 4×4 homogeneous transformation chain. The TCP frame is critical because all `MOVE` commands in high-level languages reference the TCP, not the flange.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** *Define a Robot Programming Language. List any **four essential characteristics** it must possess.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Robot Programming Language (RPL)** is a high-level, symbolic notation system that enables a human programmer to instruct a robot's controller to execute a desired sequence of motion, sensing, and logical operations in a structured, repeatable manner.

**Four essential characteristics:**

1. **Clarity and Simplicity** — easy to learn and read.
2. **World Modeling** — ability to represent objects geometrically (point, line, plane).
3. **Sensor Integration** — native support for force, vision, and proximity inputs.
4. **Real-Time Capability** — deterministic servo loop execution with cycle jitter < **1 ms**.

> [!TIP]
> *Examiner Note:* Mentioning at least one *quantitative metric* (e.g., ms or Hz) elevates the answer to full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q:** *Distinguish between **Lead-Through Programming** and **Teach Pendant Programming** in industrial robots.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lead-Through Programming | Teach Pendant Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Method** | Physically guiding the arm | Jogging via handheld terminal |
| **Operator Skill** | Low — no coding needed | Medium — menu navigation required |
| **Robot Status** | Offline during teaching | Online (can teach during production gaps) |
| **Precision** | Moderate; limited by human hand | High; keypad or joystick input |
| **Complexity** | Simple repetitive paths | Complex multi-point sequences with I/O |
| **Best For** | Spray painting, sealing | Assembly, palletizing, pick-and-place |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module 2 follows a **strict internal-choice pattern**: Question A **OR** Question B. Both choices are equally weighted (7 + 7 marks).

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

> **Q:** *(a)* Classify robot programming methods into **three hierarchical levels** with suitable examples for industrial applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* Explain the **four generations of robot programming languages**, highlighting the evolution from teach-by-showing to AI-integrated systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Three Hierarchical Levels of Robot Programming

**Level 1 — Lead-Through (Guiding) Programming:**
- The programmer physically grasps the end-effector and manually moves the robot through the desired motion trajectory.
- The controller samples joint positions at a fixed rate (typically **30–60 Hz**) and stores them.
- **Examples:** Sealant application in automotive windshields, simple spray painting on flat surfaces.
- **Merits:** No programming knowledge required. **Demerits:** Cannot handle complex tasks; robot must be offline.

**Level 2 — Teach Pendant Programming:**
- A handheld terminal is used to jog the robot axis-by-axis (Joint mode) or in Cartesian XYZ (World mode) to record named waypoints.
- Motion commands, I/O triggers, and basic logic are inserted via menu.
- **Examples:** FANUC iPendant for arc welding cells, ABB FlexPendant for palletizing.
- **Merits:** High precision; online teaching possible. **Demerits:** Limited to pendant's menu options.

**Level 3 — Textual High-Level Language Programming:**
- Programs are written as text files using languages like **VAL II, KAREL, RAPID, or KRL**.
- Supports variables, loops, subroutines, sensor integration, and error recovery.
- **Examples:** FANUC KAREL programs for CNC-tending robots; KUKA KRL for arc welding seam tracking.
- **Merits:** Unlimited complexity; full control. **Demerits:** Requires skilled programmer.

**Modern Addition — Level 4: Offline Programming (OLP):**
- Programs are generated in 3D simulation software (e.g., **ABB RobotStudio, RoboDK, FANUC ROBOGUIDE**).
- Enables reach verification, cycle-time optimization, and collision detection **before deployment**.
- **Examples:** Automotive body-in-white welding with 200+ robots coordinated via OLP.

#### Part (b) — Four Generations of Robot Programming Languages

**First Generation (1970s) — Teach-By-Showing:**
- Examples: **WAVE** (Stanford, 1972), **PAL** (MIT, 1973).
- No symbolic world model; the program is essentially a playback of recorded joint trajectories.
- **Limitation:** Inability to incorporate sensor feedback or adapt to part variations.

**Second Generation (Late 1970s – 1980s) — Textual Motion Languages:**
- Examples: **VAL** (Unimation, 1979), **AML** (IBM, 1982), **RAPT** (Edinburgh).
- Introduced a **symbolic world model** with geometric primitives (point, line, plane, cylinder).
- Allowed **offline editing** of motion sequences using textual commands.
- **Key innovation:** Coordinate-frame transformations and sensor-driven branching.

**Third Generation (1980s – 1990s) — Structured High-Level Languages:**
- Examples: **VAL II, KAREL (FANUC), RAPID (ABB), Melfa BASIC (Mitsubishi), KRL (KUKA)**.
- Added **structured programming** (loops, subroutines, modular functions).
- Native support for **force/torque, vision, and proximity sensor integration**.
- Enabled **multi-robot coordination** and offline path planning.

**Fourth Generation (2000s – Present) — Object-Oriented & AI-Integrated:**
- Examples: **ROS (Robot Operating System) + Python/C++, MATLAB Robotics Toolbox, NVIDIA Isaac, KUKA Sunrise.OS with Java** libraries.
- **Object-oriented design** with reusable skill libraries.
- **AI/ML integration** for vision-based bin picking, reinforcement-learning path planning.
- **Cloud connectivity** for fleet management and predictive maintenance.

> **Summary Table for the Student:**

| Generation | Key Innovation | Representative Language |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | Teach-by-showing | WAVE, PAL |
| 2nd | Symbolic world model | VAL, AML |
| 3rd | Structured programming + sensors | KAREL, RAPID |
| 4th | AI, vision, cloud | ROS + Python, KUKA Sunrise |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Q:** *(a)* Explain the concept of **On-line and Off-line Robot Programming**. Compare them on any **six parameters**. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* With a neat **block diagram**, describe the **functional architecture of an industrial robot programming system**, clearly showing the role of the world model, trajectory planner, and servo loop. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — On-line vs Off-line Programming

**On-line Programming:**
The robot itself is part of the programming loop. The programmer interacts directly with the physical robot via a teach pendant or by physically guiding the end-effector.
- **Sub-types:** Lead-through, teach pendant, walk-through.
- **Examples:** FANUC iPendant, ABB FlexPendant programming.

**Off-line Programming (OLP):**
The program is authored on a separate PC, typically in a 3D simulation environment that mirrors the real workcell. The robot is **not** part of the loop during programming.
- **Sub-tools:** ABB RobotStudio, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, KUKA.Sim, RoboDK, Delmia.
- **Workflow:** 3D cell model → trajectory design → collision/reach check → post-processor generates vendor-native code (KAREL, RAPID, KRL) → upload to controller.

**Comparison on Six Parameters:**

| Parameter | On-line Programming | Off-line Programming |
|---|---|---|
| **Robot Downtime** | High (production halted) | Negligible (programmed on PC) |
| **Initial Investment** | Low (pendant included) | High (software license ₹2–40 lakh) |
| **Program Complexity** | Limited by pendant menu | Unlimited — full language features |
| **Accuracy Verification** | Trial-and-error on shop floor | Pre-verified via simulation |
| **Cycle Time Optimization** | Manual, iterative | Automated path optimization |
| **Best Application** | Low-mix, high-volume | High-mix, complex cells |
| **Error Recovery** | Immediate human intervention | Pre-programmed trap routines |

#### Part (b) — Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

Refer to **Figure 4.1** in Section 4.1 of these notes. The block diagram is reproduced here in expanded form for exam-writing:

1. **Task Input Module** — Accepts the high-level task description from the programmer (text file, pendant keystrokes, or OLP output).
2. **Compiler/Interpreter** — Parses the source code, checks syntax, and generates intermediate instructions.
3. **World Model Database** — Stores the geometric and kinematic description of the robot, tools, fixtures, and the workspace. Uses primitives such as **point, line, plane, cylinder**.
4. **Trajectory Planner** — Uses Inverse Kinematics (IK) to convert Cartesian waypoints into joint-space trajectories. Performs **interpolation** (linear, circular, or joint-interpolated).
5. **Collision Avoidance Module** — Checks planned trajectory against the world model for potential self-collisions or environmental collisions.
6. **Path Optimization Module** — Minimizes cycle time and jerk; smooths acceleration profiles.
7. **Servo Controller** — Generates low-level PWM signals to drive each joint motor. Operates at a fixed rate of typically **1–4 kHz** for the current loop and **100–500 Hz** for the position loop.
8. **Sensor Feedback Module** — Reads encoders, force/torque sensors, vision systems, and proximity switches; feeds back to the trajectory planner for closed-loop correction.
9. **Actuator Output** — Physical motion of the robot arm and gripper.

> **Description to write in the exam:** *"The functional architecture is a hierarchical, closed-loop system in which the high-level task description is progressively refined into low-level motor commands. The world model provides geometric context; the trajectory planner translates intent into joint motion; the servo loop ensures real-time fidelity. Sensor feedback closes the loop, enabling the robot to adapt to environmental variations."*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Why Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Programming" with "Operating":** Many students describe *jogging the robot* instead of *writing a program*. The question asks about *programming*, not manual operation.
> 2. **Missing the "World Model":** When asked about 2nd-generation languages, students forget that the **symbolic world model** is the *defining feature*. Without it, the answer is incomplete.
> 3. **No Quantitative Anchors:** Answers that say "fast" or "accurate" without numbers (e.g., **1 ms cycle time**, **30–60 Hz sampling**) get **partial credit only**.
> 4. **Omitting Diagrams:** Part B (b) explicitly asks for a block diagram. Students who write only text forfeit **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Confusing On-line with Off-line:** On-line = robot is **in the loop**; Off-line = robot is **not in the loop**. A common error is to claim that any PC-based tool is off-line — but a pendant connected to the controller is on-line regardless of whether you type on a PC.
> 6. **Missing IRDATA/Standards:** KTU values awareness of international standards such as **ISO 10166 / IRDATA**. Mentioning these by name can lift a 6-mark answer to 7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Topic: Basics of Robot Programming**

- **Definition:** Robot programming is the creation of coded instructions for a robot to perform autonomous tasks. A **Robot Programming Language** is the linguistic framework; a **Robot Program** is the executable instance.
- **Three Levels of Programming:**
  - **Lead-Through** (manual guiding) — best for paint and seal.
  - **Teach Pendant** (handheld terminal) — best for palletizing and pick-and-place.
  - **Textual High-Level** (VAL, KAREL, RAPID) — best for complex assembly.
  - **Offline Programming (OLP)** — best for large multi-robot cells.
- **Four Generations of RPL:**
  - **1st Gen** — WAVE, PAL (teach-by-showing, no world model).
  - **2nd Gen** — VAL, AML (symbolic world model introduced).
  - **3rd Gen** — KAREL, RAPID (structured programming, sensor integration).
  - **4th Gen** — ROS + Python/C++, KUKA Sunrise (AI-integrated, cloud-connected).
- **On-line vs Off-line:** On-line = robot in the loop; Off-line = PC-based simulation, robot NOT in the loop.
- **Essential Characteristics of an RPL:** Clarity, world modeling, sensor integration, frame awareness, real-time capability, error handling, portability.
- **Coordinate Frames:** World → Base → Flange → Tool Center Point (TCP) — all `MOVE` commands reference the **TCP frame**, not the flange.
- **Key Kinematic Relations to Memorize:**
  - Forward Kinematics via Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameters: $T_{i-1}^{i} = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_i) \cdot R_x(\alpha_i)$.
  - Inverse Kinematics for 2-link planar: $\theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2+y^2-a_1^2-a_2^2}{2a_1a_2}\right)$.
  - Cartesian velocity: $\dot{X} = J(q)\cdot \dot{q}$.
- **Sample Quantitative Anchors (use them to score extra marks):**
  - Lead-through sampling rate: **30–60 Hz**.
  - Servo position loop: **100–500 Hz**; current loop: **1–4 kHz**.
  - Cycle time jitter for real-time: **< 1 ms**.
  - Override range: **1–100 %**.
- **Standards to Cite:** **ISO 10166** (IRDATA), **ISO 9283** (performance testing).
- **Common Examples for Exams:**
  - VAL (Unimation, 1979) — 2nd generation.
  - KAREL (FANUC) — 3rd generation, C-like syntax.
  - RAPID (ABB) — 3rd generation, modular.
  - KRL (KUKA) — 3rd generation, Pascal-like.
  - ROS + Python (Open Source) — 4th generation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In any 14-mark question, always begin with a **definition** (1 mark), follow with a **classification** (2 marks), include a **comparison table** (3–4 marks), add a **diagram** (2–3 marks), and conclude with a **real-world example** (1–2 marks). This template satisfies the KTU valuation rubric and ensures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Offline and Online Programming
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: OFFLINE AND ONLINE PROGRAMMING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robot Programming** is the process of generating and storing a sequence of motion instructions, logical decisions, I/O commands, and peripheral coordination directives that dictate the autonomous behaviour of an industrial robot manipulator within a defined task envelope.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT724 – Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems, Module 2)**, robot programming is classified into two fundamental paradigms based on the physical location and method of instruction generation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Offline Programming (OLP)** is the generation, validation, and optimisation of robot trajectories on a remote computer using CAD-based simulation software, *without* requiring physical access to the actual robot cell.
>
> **Online Programming** is the generation of robot instructions *at the actual workstation* by physically interacting with the manipulator or its handheld controller (teach pendant) in real time.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Imagine teaching a friend a new route to your house:**

- **Online Programming** is like sitting *inside* the car, holding the steering wheel, and physically driving the route once while your friend memorises every turn. It is direct, accurate, but it ties up the *actual* car for the entire teaching session.

- **Offline Programming** is like using **Google Maps** on a computer *the night before* to plan the entire route, preview it in 3D street view, identify the tricky intersections, and then simply "downloading" the route to your friend's GPS at departure time. The car is free to do other work during the planning phase.

> [!NOTE]
> In a real factory, every minute the robot is being taught is a minute it is *not producing parts*. OLP exists precisely to **decouple programming time from production time**, which is a critical economic driver in high-mix, low-volume (HMLV) manufacturing.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following standard performance indicators are typically associated with these programming methodologies:

- **Teach Time (T_t)** — average hours required to program one task point.
- **Production Downtime (T_d)** — robotic cell idle time during programming.
- **Path Accuracy (A_p)** — measured in millimetres (mm), deviation between simulated and executed path.
- **Cycle Time (T_c)** — measured in seconds (s), time to complete one operational loop.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — reliability indicator in **hours**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Content

## 2.1 Classification of Robot Programming Methods

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to clearly distinguish programming techniques across multiple axes. The following hierarchical breakdown mirrors the official syllabus structure.

### 2.1.1 Online Programming (At the Cell)

Online programming is further sub-divided into two physically distinct methods:

**(A) Teach Pendant Programming (Manual Textual Entry)**
- The programmer uses a **handheld Teach Pendant Unit (TPU)** — a ruggedised industrial pendant with a keypad, joystick, and LCD screen.
- Each Cartesian or joint coordinate is **jogged manually** to a desired pose and **recorded** into the controller memory as a discrete *position register*.
- High-level logic (loops, I/O, conditions) is entered as **text-based robot language** (e.g., **VAL II**, **RAPID**, **KRL**, **KAREL**, **TP**).
- This is the **most common** method used in industry today for spot welding, palletising, and small-batch painting.

**(B) Lead-Through Programming (Walk-Through / Hand-Guiding)**
- A **zero-force / zero-gravity** compliant robot (often a hydraulically balanced or torque-sensed arm) is **physically grasped and pushed** by the programmer through the desired trajectory.
- Sensors in each joint continuously sample position at high frequency (typically **50 Hz to 200 Hz**).
- Suited for **continuous-path applications** such as sealant dispensing, spray painting, and arc welding on large components (e.g., ship hulls, automotive bodies).
- **PTP (Point-to-Point)** recording is automatically created as the operator moves.

### 2.1.2 Offline Programming (Away from the Cell)

OLP is conducted on a **separate engineering workstation** running specialised simulation suites.

**Stage 1 — Virtual Cell Construction**
- The entire workcell (robot, fixtures, conveyors, jigs, safety fencing) is modelled in **3D CAD** (often imported from CATIA, SolidWorks, NX, Creo).
- The **kinematic model** of the robot (DH parameters, axis limits, joint coupling) is loaded from the manufacturer's library.

**Stage 2 — Path Planning & Task Definition**
- The user defines *task points* on the CAD geometry — weld seams, glue beads, pick/place poses.
- Sophisticated **path planners** (RRT*, PRM, A*) generate collision-free trajectories automatically.

**Stage 3 — Simulation & Verification**
- The virtual robot executes the program in a physics engine (e.g., PhysX, Bullet).
- **Cycle time estimation**, **reachability analysis**, **singularity detection**, and **collision detection** are performed.

**Stage 4 — Post-Processing**
- A **post-processor** (vendor-specific translator) converts the generic OLP code into the **native robot language** (RAPID for ABB, KRL for KUKA, Karel for FANUC, etc.).

**Stage 5 — Download & Calibration**
- The generated program is transferred to the real controller via **Ethernet, USB, or OPC-UA**.
- **Fine calibration** corrects for cell layout deviations between the virtual world and the real shop floor (using laser trackers or vision-based registration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The accuracy of OLP is **fundamentally bounded by the calibration error** between the simulated kinematic model and the real manufactured robot. Industrial-grade OLP systems can achieve path accuracies of **± 0.5 mm to ± 2.0 mm** after proper calibration.

---

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix: Online vs. Offline Programming

The following table is a **high-yield KTU exam reference**. Memorise the contrasting columns.

| **Parameter** | **Online Programming** | **Offline Programming (OLP)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Location of Programming** | At the actual robot cell | At a remote engineering PC |
| **Production Downtime** | **High** (robot is idle) | **Zero** (cell keeps producing) |
| **Path Accuracy** | Very High (real-world feedback) | Moderate to High (depends on calibration) |
| **Software Cost** | None (pendant included) | High (₹ 5 Lakh to ₹ 50 Lakh licences) |
| **Programming Skill Required** | Low (operator level) | High (CAD + Robotics engineer) |
| **Safety Risk** | High (operator near moving robot) | Very Low (virtual environment) |
| **Cycle Time Optimisation** | Difficult (trial & error) | Excellent (software optimisers) |
| **Suitability for Complex Paths** | Limited | Excellent (auto-path generators) |
| **Time for Complex Tasks** | Hours to Days | Minutes to Hours |
| **Examples** | Teach pendant for spot welding | RobotStudio for car body welding |
| **Error Recovery** | Immediate tactile feedback | Requires re-simulation |

---

## 2.3 Hybrid Programming Architecture

Modern factories often use a **hybrid workflow** that exploits the strengths of both paradigms:

> [!NOTE]
> **The "85/15 Rule"** widely cited in industrial robotics: approximately **85% of the program** (gross motion, logic, I/O) is generated **offline**, and the remaining **15%** (fine tuning of touch points, seam tracking offsets, sensor calibration) is performed **online** via the teach pendant.

This hybrid model is the **de facto industry standard** and is a common **14-mark question topic** in KTU board examinations.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations appear frequently in KTU numerical problems and derivations.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{d} = N_p \times T_t$ | Total downtime = number of points × teach time per point | hours |
| $C_{prog} = T_{d} \times C_{hr} \times N_u$ | Programming cost = downtime × hourly cost × number of units/year | ₹ / year |
| $A_{p} = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2}$ | Path accuracy (Euclidean deviation) | mm |
| $T_{c,OLP} = T_{c,sim} \cdot k_{cal}$ | Real cycle time ≈ simulated cycle time × calibration factor (1.02–1.10) | seconds |
| $E_{cal} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert p_{sim,i} - p_{real,i} \vert$ | Mean calibration error across $n$ test points | mm |
| $S_{path} = \dfrac{L_{real}}{L_{sim}} \times 100\%$ | Path similarity ratio (ideal = 100%) | % |

> Where:
> - $C_{hr}$ = labour + machine cost per hour (₹/h)
> - $N_u$ = number of reprogramming events per year
> - $k_{cal}$ = empirical calibration correction constant
> - $L$ = total path length (mm)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Body-in-White (BIW):** 100% of arc welding paths in modern plants (Tesla, BMW, Tata) are generated in **OLP** to enable **mixed-model production** (Sedan + SUV on the same line).
- **Aerospace Drilling:** Airbus uses OLP to drill **thousands of holes** in fuselage panels with **± 0.2 mm** accuracy, which would be impossible with online teaching.
- **Spray Painting of Large Castings:** Lead-through programming remains the **gold standard** for free-form continuous paths where the operator's tactile judgement is invaluable.
- **Pharmaceutical Palletising:** Online teach-pendant programming is preferred due to the **low cost and simplicity** of the repetitive box-stacking task.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1: Downtime Cost Comparison (Online vs. OLP)

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical Style):**
> A manufacturing cell has a robot performing a spot-welding task with **120 weld points**. Teach pendant programming requires **45 seconds per point**. Offline programming takes **6 hours** total to generate the program, plus **1.5 hours** of online fine-tuning. The cell generates revenue at the rate of **₹ 4,500 per hour** when running. Compare the cost of both methods.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate online programming downtime.**

$$
T_{d,\text{online}} = N_p \times T_t
$$

$$
T_{d,\text{online}} = 120 \times 45 \text{ s}
$$

Converting to hours for consistency:

$$
T_{d,\text{online}} = \dfrac{120 \times 45}{3600} = \dfrac{5400}{3600} = 1.5 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate OLP-induced downtime.**

OLP itself takes zero downtime (programmer works on a separate PC). The *only* downtime is the online fine-tuning step:

$$
T_{d,\text{OLP}} = 1.5 \text{ hours}
$$

Wait — at first glance both look equal. Let us include the **software licence amortisation**, a real-world cost hidden in OLP.

**Step 3 — Software licence amortisation.**

Assume the OLP software costs **₹ 12,00,000** and is amortised over **5 years**, with **200 programming events per year**:

$$
C_{sw} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{5 \times 200} = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{1000} = ₹\, 1{,}200 \text{ per event}
$$

Converted to an hourly cost over the **6 hours** of OLP work:

$$
C_{sw,hour} = \dfrac{1200}{6} = ₹\, 200 \text{ per hour of OLP}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare the costs.**

For **online programming** (lost production + operator labour @ ₹ 600/h):

$$
C_{online} = (1.5 \text{ h} \times ₹ 4{,}500) + (1.5 \text{ h} \times ₹ 600)
$$

$$
C_{online} = ₹\, 6{,}750 + ₹\, 900 = ₹\, 7{,}650
$$

For **OLP** (lost production during fine-tuning + operator + software cost, assuming software cost is spread over the whole 7.5 h task duration):

$$
C_{OLP} = (1.5 \text{ h} \times ₹ 4{,}500) + (1.5 \text{ h} \times ₹ 600) + (6 \text{ h} \times ₹ 200)
$$

$$
C_{OLP} = ₹\, 6{,}750 + ₹\, 900 + ₹\, 1{,}200 = ₹\, 8{,}850
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For this *single-event* analysis, online programming is cheaper. However, if the **same part is re-run for 50 production batches** in a year and the program must be regenerated **3 times** due to design changes, the cumulative OLP cost per event drops drastically because the software licence is already amortised. OLP becomes the economic winner for **frequent design iterations** and **high-mix production**.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2: Path Accuracy Computation

**Problem Statement:**
> A robot must reach 5 target points in a simulation. The simulated coordinates $(x_s, y_s, z_s)$ and real measured coordinates $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$ in mm are given below. Compute the **mean calibration error** $E_{cal}$.

| Point | $x_s$ | $y_s$ | $z_s$ | $x_r$ | $y_r$ | $z_r$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 100 | 200 | 300 | 100.4 | 200.1 | 300.2 |
| P2 | 150 | 250 | 350 | 150.5 | 250.0 | 350.3 |
| P3 | 200 | 300 | 400 | 199.7 | 300.4 | 400.1 |
| P4 | 250 | 350 | 450 | 250.2 | 350.3 | 449.8 |
| P5 | 300 | 400 | 500 | 299.9 | 399.8 | 500.4 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Euclidean deviation formula for each point.**

$$
E_i = \sqrt{(x_s - x_r)^2 + (y_s - y_r)^2 + (z_s - z_r)^2}
$$

**For P1:**

$$
E_1 = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (0.1)^2 + (0.2)^2}
$$

$$
E_1 = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.01 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.21} \approx 0.458 \text{ mm}
$$

**For P2:**

$$
E_2 = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.0)^2 + (0.3)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0 + 0.09} = \sqrt{0.34} \approx 0.583 \text{ mm}
$$

**For P3:**

$$
E_3 = \sqrt{(-0.3)^2 + (0.4)^2 + (0.1)^2} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.16 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.26} \approx 0.510 \text{ mm}
$$

**For P4:**

$$
E_4 = \sqrt{(0.2)^2 + (0.3)^2 + (-0.2)^2} = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.09 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.17} \approx 0.412 \text{ mm}
$$

**For P5:**

$$
E_5 = \sqrt{(-0.1)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (0.4)^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.04 + 0.16} = \sqrt{0.21} \approx 0.458 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mean calibration error.**

$$
E_{cal} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i
$$

$$
E_{cal} = \frac{0.458 + 0.583 + 0.510 + 0.412 + 0.458}{5}
$$

$$
E_{cal} = \frac{2.421}{5} \approx 0.484 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A mean calibration error of **0.484 mm** is excellent for most general industrial tasks. For **aerospace drilling**, this would require further reduction (typically to **< 0.2 mm**) using laser-tracker-based dynamic calibration.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Framework for Selecting Programming Method

| **Selection Criterion** | **Prefer Online If** | **Prefer Offline If** |
|---|---|---|
| Task complexity | < 50 points, simple logic | > 200 points, multi-robot |
| Batch size | Large (≥ 10,000 units) | Small or mixed |
| Safety-criticality | Low | High (toxic, hot, or confined cell) |
| Capital cost sensitivity | High (SMEs) | Low (large OEMs) |
| Engineer skill available | Operator-level only | CAD + Robotics expert available |
| Time to production launch | Days to weeks | Months of pre-planning |
| Required path accuracy | < 0.1 mm (e.g., fine assembly) | 0.5 – 2.0 mm acceptable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Offline Programming (OLP) Workflow

The following Mermaid flow diagram captures the full **OLP pipeline** as specified by KTU Module 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Task Requirement] --> B[Import 3D CAD Model of Part and Cell]
    B --> C[Load Robot Kinematic Model from Vendor Library]
    C --> D[Define Task Points on CAD Geometry]
    D --> E[Auto Path Planning and Trajectory Generation]
    E --> F{Collision Check}
    F -- Collision Detected --> G[Modify Path or Add Waypoints]
    G --> E
    F -- No Collision --> H[Simulate Cycle Time and Reachability]
    H --> I{Cycle Time Acceptable}
    I -- No --> J[Optimise Motion Parameters]
    J --> E
    I -- Yes --> K[Run Post-Processor to Native Robot Code]
    K --> L[Download Program to Robot Controller via Ethernet]
    L --> M[Online Fine Tuning via Teach Pendant]
    M --> N[Final Production Validation]
    N --> O[End: Program Locked for Production]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Online Programming Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Operator at Robot Cell] --> B[Power On Robot in Manual Teach Mode]
    B --> C{Joint Jog Using Teach Pendant}
    C --> D[Position Reached Record as Register P1]
    D --> E{More Points Needed}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No --> F[Enter Logic Code in Pendant Keyboard]
    F --> G[Test Run in Low Speed Mode]
    G --> H{Task Executed Correctly}
    H -- No --> I[Debug and Modify Registers]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Switch to Auto Mode and Begin Production]
    J --> K[End: Robot in Production Loop]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Hybrid Programming System
