### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function from Block Diagram

**Problem Setup:** Consider a robotic-joint position-control system with:
- Forward path transfer function $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function $H(s)$
- Reference $R(s)$, Output $C(s)$, Error $E(s)$, Feedback signal $B(s)$, Control signal $U(s)$.

**Step 1:** Write the error equation at the summing junction (negative feedback convention).
$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2:** The feedback signal is the output of the feedback element.
$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute (2) into (1).
$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4:** The control signal is the error passed through the controller. For unity controller gain:
$$U(s) = E(s)$$

**Step 5:** The output of the plant is the control signal multiplied by the forward-path transfer function.
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute (3) into (5) to eliminate $E(s)$.
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot [R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)]$$

**Step 7:** Expand the right-hand side.
$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 8:** Collect all $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side.
$$C(s) + G(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 9:** Factor $C(s)$.
$$C(s) \cdot [1 + G(s) \cdot H(s)] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 10:** Divide both sides by $[1 + G(s) H(s)]$ to obtain the closed-loop transfer function.

$$\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\;}$$

This is the **master equation** of all closed-loop control theory. The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s)$ is called the **return difference** and its zero(s) are the closed-loop poles.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Error of a Unity-Feedback System for a Step Input

**Problem Setup:** A robot joint is commanded to a fixed angle (step input). The forward path is $G(s)$. Find $e_{ss}$.

**Step 1:** Step input in Laplace domain.
$$R(s) = \frac{A}{s}, \quad A = \text{step magnitude (e.g., 1 rad)}$$

**Step 2:** Error transfer function (unity feedback, $H(s) = 1$).
$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 3:** Express the error in the $s$-domain.
$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **Final Value Theorem** (valid only if the system is stable and all poles of $sE(s)$ are in the LHP).
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $s$.
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(0)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

**Step 6:** State the result.

$$\boxed{\;e_{ss} = \frac{A}{1 + K_p}\;}$$

where $K_p = G(0)$ is the **position error constant** (DC gain of forward path).

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case — Type-1 system** (one pure integrator in $G(s)$, e.g., $G(s) = K/(s(\tau s + 1))$): $G(0) \to \infty$, hence $K_p \to \infty$ and $e_{ss} \to 0$. Robot joint servos are typically Type-1, so they can track a constant command with zero error. This is why your CNC machine or industrial robot reaches the commanded position exactly (ignoring friction).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Routh-Hurwitz Stability Criterion (Algebraic Test)

**Problem Setup:** Determine the range of controller gain $K$ for which the closed-loop system with characteristic equation
$$s^{3} + 2s^{2} + 3s + K = 0$$
is stable.

**Step 1:** Construct the Routh array.

$$\begin{array}{c|cc}
s^{3} & 1 & 3 \\
s^{2} & 2 & K \\
s^{1} & b_1 & 0 \\
s^{0} & c_1 & 0 \\
\end{array}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $b_1$.
$$b_1 = \frac{(2)(3) - (1)(K)}{2} = \frac{6 - K}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $c_1$.
$$c_1 = \frac{(b_1)(K) - (2)(0)}{b_1} = K$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Routh-Hurwitz stability condition: **all first-column elements must be strictly positive**.

- Row $s^{3}$: $1 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- Row $s^{2}$: $2 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- Row $s^{1}$: $b_1 = (6 - K)/2 > 0 \implies K < 6$
- Row $s^{0}$: $c_1 = K > 0 \implies K > 0$

**Step 5:** Combine the inequalities.

$$\boxed{\;0 < K < 6\;}$$

For any gain in this range, the closed-loop system is stable. At $K = 6$, the system is **marginally stable** (sustained oscillation). For $K > 6$, the system is **unstable** (the poles cross into the right-half plane).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop

| # | Feature | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feedback path | **Absent** | **Present** (essential element) |
| 2 | Sensors on output | Not required | **Mandatory** (encoder, LVDT, camera) |
| 3 | Accuracy | Low to moderate (5–15 % typical) | High (0.01–0.1 % typical) |
| 4 | Disturbance rejection | **None** | **Strong** |
| 5 | Sensitivity to parameter variation | High | Low (feedback desensitizes) |
| 6 | Bandwidth | Generally higher (no sensor lag) | Limited by sensor & loop delay |
| 7 | Stability | Inherently stable (no loop) | Can become unstable; requires analysis |
| 8 | Cost | Low | Higher (sensors + controller) |
| 9 | Complexity | Simple | Complex (tuning, modeling) |
| 10 | Computational load | Low | Higher (real-time correction) |
| 11 | Typical actuator | Stepper motor, timer, cam | Servo motor, hydraulic servo, voice-coil |
| 12 | Example in assembly | Timed glue dispenser | Force-controlled screwdriving |
| 13 | Example in material handling | Fixed-speed conveyor | Speed-regulated conveyor with tacho |
| 14 | Example in robotics | Pre-programmed pick-and-place (no encoder) | Encoder-based articulated robot arm |
| 15 | Error correction | Impossible (no error measured) | Continuous (every sample period) |

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Error of a Robotic Joint Position Servo

**Problem Setup:** A DC servomotor-driven robot joint has the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(Js + b)}$$
where $J$ = moment of inertia of the link, $b$ = viscous friction coefficient, $K$ = motor+amplifier gain. The system is unity feedback. Find the steady-state error for (a) a step input of $\theta_{d}$ and (b) a ramp input of angular velocity $\omega_{d}$.

**Step 1 (Step input):** $R(s) = \theta_d / s$. The position error constant is
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K}{s(Js + b)} = \infty$$
since $G(s)$ contains a factor $1/s$. Hence,
$$e_{ss}(\text{step}) = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = 0$$

> **Conclusion:** The robot joint reaches the commanded angle **exactly** (no steady-state error for a step). This is the hallmark of a **Type-1 system**.

**Step 2 (Ramp input):** $R(s) = \omega_d / s^{2}$. The velocity error constant is
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s(Js + b)} = \frac{K}{b}$$

**Step 3:** Steady-state error for the ramp.
$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{\omega_{d}}{K_v} = \frac{\omega_{d} \cdot b}{K}$$

> **Conclusion:** When the joint is asked to rotate at a constant velocity, it lags the command by an angle $b\omega_d / K$. To reduce this lag, one must **increase $K$** (amplifier gain) or **decrease $b$** (use low-friction bearings). However, raising $K$ too much can cause instability — a classic control-engineering trade-off.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, OERUT724]**
> **Distinguish clearly between open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Give one example of each as applied to robotic assembly or material handling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | **No feedback path** from output to input. | **Feedback path** present; output measured and returned. |
| Accuracy | Low; affected by disturbances. | High; errors automatically corrected. |
| Example (Robotic Assembly) | A **timed pneumatic dispenser** that ejects glue for a fixed 0.5 s — no sensor checks whether glue actually flowed. | A **force-controlled gripper** with a strain-gauge sensor that regulates gripping force to hold a fragile glass part without crushing it. |
| Example (Material Handling) | A **fixed-speed belt conveyor** driven by an induction motor at 50 Hz (no speed sensor). | A **variable-speed conveyor** regulated by a PID controller with a tachogenerator feedback to maintain constant throughput. |

> **[1 mark] for definition of open-loop, [1 mark] for definition of closed-loop, [1 mark] for correct one-line example each.**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OERUT724]**
> **Define "transfer function" and "characteristic equation" of a control system. State the role of the characteristic equation in stability analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Transfer Function (1 mark):** The transfer function of an LTI system is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, assuming **zero initial conditions**. Mathematically,
$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{\text{zero ICs}}$$
It is a rational function of the complex variable $s$ and completely characterizes the input–output behavior of the system.

- **Characteristic Equation (1 mark):** For a closed-loop system with forward path $G(s)$ and feedback $H(s)$, the characteristic equation is
$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$
The roots of this equation are the **closed-loop poles** of the system.

- **Role in Stability (1 mark):** A closed-loop system is **stable if and only if all the roots of the characteristic equation lie in the left-half of the s-plane** (i.e., have negative real parts). If any root has a positive real part, the system is unstable and its output grows unbounded. The Routh-Hurwitz criterion and root-locus method both analyze the characteristic equation to determine the range of system parameters (e.g., gain $K$) for which stability is guaranteed.

### ▶ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, OERUT724, Module 2 Choice]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **closed-loop position-control system** for a robot joint, identifying the controller, actuator, plant, sensor, summing junction, error signal, reference input, and output. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the closed-loop system with forward path transfer function
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(0.5s + 1)}$$
and unity feedback $H(s) = 1$, derive the **closed-loop transfer function**, the **characteristic equation**, the **damped natural frequency** $\omega_d$, and the **steady-state error** for a unit step input. Use $K = 4$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (a)

**Step 1:** Identify the canonical elements of a closed-loop position-control system:
- **Reference input** $r(t)$: the desired joint angle (e.g., 30°).
- **Summing junction**: comparator that subtracts feedback from reference.
- **Error signal** $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
- **Controller** $G_c(s)$: typically a PID controller generating voltage command.
- **Power amplifier**: converts low-level signal to high-current drive.
- **Actuator** + **Plant**: DC servomotor + robot link (mechanical load).
- **Sensor**: incremental encoder or resolver mounted on the motor shaft.
- **Feedback element** $H(s)$: usually unity (sensor gain scaled into the loop).
- **Output** $c(t)$: actual achieved joint angle.

**Step 2:** Draw the block diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R(["R(s)\nReference Angle"]):::in --> SUM(("+ −")):::sum
    SUM --> E["E(s)\nError"]:::err
    E --> PID["G_c(s)\nPID Controller"]:::ctrl
    PID --> AMP["Power\nAmplifier"]:::actuator
    AMP --> PL["G(s)\nDC Motor + Joint"]:::plant
    PL --> Y(["C(s)\nActual Angle"]):::out
    PL -.->|"θ_actual"| ENC["Encoder\nH(s)=1"]:::sens
    ENC -.-> SUM

    classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef err fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```

**Mark distribution (a) — 7 marks:**
- Block diagram with all elements correctly drawn: **3 marks**
- Correct identification of all 8 sub-systems (reference, summing junction, error, controller, amplifier, plant, sensor, feedback path): **3 marks**
- Proper labels with arrow directions: **1 mark**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.A (b)

**Given:**
$$G(s) = \frac{K}{s(0.5s + 1)}, \quad K = 4, \quad H(s) = 1$$

**Step 1: Closed-loop transfer function.**

Using $T(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)H(s)]$:

$$T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{4}{s(0.5s + 1)}}{1 + \dfrac{4}{s(0.5s + 1)}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(0.5s + 1)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{4}{s(0.5s + 1) + 4}$$

Expand the denominator:

$$T(s) = \frac{4}{0.5s^{2} + s + 4}$$

Normalize (divide numerator and denominator by 0.5):

$$T(s) = \frac{8}{s^{2} + 2s + 8}$$

**Step 2: Characteristic equation.**

$$s^{2} + 2s + 8 = 0$$

**Step 3: Identify $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$.** Compare with the standard second-order form $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$.

$$\omega_{n}^{2} = 8 \implies \omega_{n} = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = 2 \implies \zeta = \frac{1}{\omega_{n}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.354$$

**Step 4: Damped natural frequency.**

$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1 - 0.125} = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{0.875}$$

$$\omega_{d} = 2\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{7}}{2} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Steady-state error for unit step input.** System is Type-1 (one integrator), so $K_p \to \infty$.

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

**Step 6 (optional — for partial credit): Peak overshoot.**

$$M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}} \times 100\% = e^{-0.354 \times \pi / \sqrt{0.875}} \times 100\% \approx e^{-1.190} \times 100\% \approx 30.4\%$$

**Final Answers (Q.A-b):**
- $T(s) = 8 / (s^{2} + 2s + 8)$
- Characteristic equation: $s^{2} + 2s + 8 = 0$
- $\omega_{d} \approx 3.742$ **rad/s**
- $e_{ss} = 0$

**Mark distribution (b) — 7 marks:**
- Correct closed-loop TF derivation: **2 marks**
- Characteristic equation + identification of $\omega_n, \zeta$: **2 marks**
- Damped natural frequency computation: **2 marks**
- Steady-state error using FVT: **1 mark**

### ▶ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, OERUT724, Module 2 Choice — Alternative]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **architecture and operation** of an **open-loop** and a **closed-loop** control system for a material-handling conveyor belt. Identify the elements that distinguish them. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the system shown below (block diagram with $G(s) = K / (s^{2} + 3s + 2)$ and $H(s) = 1$), determine the **range of $K$** for closed-loop stability using the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion**. Also compute the steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (a)

**Open-loop conveyor system (3 marks for diagram + 1 mark for explanation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A(["Operator Sets\nDesired Speed"]):::in --> B["Speed\nDial"]:::dial
    B --> C["VFD / Motor\nDriver"]:::drv
    C --> D["Induction\nMotor"]:::mot
    D --> E(["Conveyor Belt\nRuns at Set Speed"]):::out

    classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef dial fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    classDef mot fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
```

The operator dials a desired speed; the VFD drives the motor; the belt runs at that **assumed** speed with **no speed sensor** — hence open-loop. If the load on the belt increases, the actual speed will drop and no correction occurs.

**Closed-loop conveyor system (2 marks for diagram + 1 mark for explanation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A(["Set Point\nDesired Speed"]):::in --> SUM(("+ −")):::sum
    SUM --> E["Error\ne = r − b"]:::err
    E --> PID["PID\nController"]:::ctrl
    PID --> VFD["VFD\nDrive"]:::drv
    VFD --> MOT["Motor +\nConveyor"]:::mot
    MOT --> Y(["Actual Belt\nSpeed"]):::out
    MOT -.->|"ω_actual"| TACH["Tachogenerator\nH(s)=1"]:::sens
    TACH -.-> SUM

    classDef in fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef err fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    classDef mot fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```

The actual belt speed is measured by a tachogenerator and fed back to the summing junction. Any deviation (e.g., due to added load) produces an error that the PID controller uses to adjust the VFD frequency and restore the desired speed. **Feedback is the distinguishing element.**

**Distinguishing Elements (extra 1 mark for tabular comparison):**

| Element | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor | None | Tachogenerator / encoder |
| Error computation | Not done | Done at summing junction |
| Disturbance rejection | Absent | Present |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher |

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Q.B (b)

**Given:** $G(s) = K / (s^{2} + 3s + 2)$, $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 1: Characteristic equation.**

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \implies 1 + \frac{K}{s^{2} + 3s + 2} = 0$$

$$s^{2} + 3s + 2 + K = 0$$

$$s^{2} + 3s + (2 + K) = 0$$

**Step 2: Routh array for a 2nd-order system.**

For a second-order polynomial $s^{2} + a_{1}s + a_{0} = 0$, stability requires **both** $a_{1} > 0$ and $a_{0} > 0$.

- $a_{1} = 3 > 0$ ✓ (always)
- $a_{0} = 2 + K > 0 \implies K > -2$

**Step 3: Since $K$ is a physical gain (positive amplifier gain), the condition reduces to $K > 0$ (and indeed $2 + K > 2 > 0$ for any positive $K$).**

$$\boxed{\;\text{The closed-loop system is stable for all } K > 0.\;}$$

**Step 4: Steady-state error for a unit ramp input.** The open-loop transfer function $G(s) = K / (s^{2} + 3s + 2)$ has **no integrator** (no $1/s$ factor). It is a **Type-0 system**.

The velocity error constant is
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) \cdot H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s^{2} + 3s + 2} = 0$$

**Step 5: Steady-state error for ramp.**

$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp}) = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

**Conclusion:** A Type-0 system **cannot track a ramp input**; the error grows without bound. This is why **Type-1 (or higher) systems** are used in robotic trajectory tracking — a position servo for a robot arm must contain at least one integrator to follow a moving command with finite error.

**Mark distribution (b) — 7 marks:**
- Correct formation of characteristic equation: **2 marks**
- Routh array construction: **2 marks**
- Stability range deduction: **1 mark**
- Velocity error constant and ramp error: **2 marks**

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read before writing your exam!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include $H(s)$ in the denominator** — Students frequently write $T(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s))$ even when $H(s) \neq 1$. **Always check** the problem statement for non-unity feedback and use $T(s) = G(s) / (1 + G(s)H(s))$. **[Lose up to 2 marks per question]**
>
> 2. **Applying the Final Value Theorem without checking stability** — The FVT is valid *only* if all closed-loop poles lie in the left-half plane. If the system is unstable, $\lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ gives a **finite but meaningless** number. Always confirm stability (Routh-Hurwitz) before computing $e_{ss}$. **[Lose 1–2 marks]**
>
> 3. **Confusing the three error constants $K_p, K_v, K_a$ with the three input types** — Step uses $K_p$ (and $e_{ss} = 1/(1+K_p)$), **Ramp** uses $K_v$ ($e_{ss} = 1/K_v$), **Parabola** uses $K_a$ ($e_{ss} = 1/K_a$). A ramp is $r(t) = t$, **not** $r(t) = 1$. **[Lose 1–2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Wrong Routh array first column** — In computing $b_1 = (a_{n-1}a_{n-3} - a_n a_{n-2}) / a_{n-1}$, students often swap signs. Memorize the Routh formula and verify with one numerical example.
>
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — $\omega_n$ is in **rad/s**, $e_{ss}$ is in the **units of the output** (radians for joint angle, mm for linear position). A numerical answer without units is marked incomplete. **[Lose 0.5–1 mark]**
>
> 6. **Drawing an open-loop diagram when a closed-loop is asked (and vice versa)** — A common slip. Always **circle** the words "open-loop" or "closed-loop" in the question and re-read your diagram once before submitting.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Control (Module 2, OERUT724)**

- [ ] **Open-loop = no feedback.** Control action is a predetermined function of reference only. Cheaper, faster, but inaccurate and disturbance-blind. Example: stepper-motor pick-and-place, timed glue dispenser, fixed-speed conveyor.
- [ ] **Closed-loop = feedback present.** Sensor measures output → error computed → controller adjusts. Accurate, disturbance-rejecting, but can become unstable. Example: encoder-servoed robot joint, force-feedback gripper, speed-regulated conveyor.
- [ ] **Master closed-loop TF:** $T(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)H(s)]$ — derive this once and memorize. The denominator $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation** whose roots are the closed-loop poles.
- [ ] **Stability = all closed-loop poles in LHP.** Use Routh-Hurwitz for algebraic test; use root locus for graphical test.
- [ ] **Error constants by system type:**
  - **Type-0** (no integrator): finite $e_{ss}$ to step, **infinite** to ramp/parabola.
  - **Type-1** (one $1/s$): **zero** $e_{ss}$ to step, **finite** $e_{ss} = 1/K_v$ to ramp, **infinite** to parabola.
  - **Type-2** (two $1/s$): zero $e_{ss}$ to step & ramp, finite $e_{ss} = 1/K_a$ to parabola.
- [ ] **Most industrial robot joint servos are Type-1** (motor + integrator from armature rotation) — hence zero steady-state error to a step (position) command, finite error to a ramp (velocity) command.
- [ ] **Second-order underdamped response parameters:** $M_p$ (overshoot), $t_r$ (rise time), $t_s$ (settling time), $\omega_d$ (oscillation frequency) — all derived from $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$ using the formula sheet in Section 2.3.
- [ ] **Three golden equations for second-order step response** (for $0 < \zeta < 1$):
  - $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\%$
  - $t_s \approx 4 / (\zeta\omega_n)$ (2 % criterion)
  - $\omega_d = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}}$
- [ ] **Final Value Theorem (FVT) caveat:** valid only for stable systems. Always check stability first.
- [ ] **Mason's Gain Formula** (for multi-loop block diagrams) reduces to the simple $T(s) = G/(1 + GH)$ for a single-loop system — the case most frequently tested in KTU exams.
- [ ] **Application link:** every concept in this module underpins the design of robot joint controllers, conveyor regulators, AGV steering systems, and force-controlled grippers — the four pillars of robotic assembly and material handling.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Control**
**Course:** OERUT724 — Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems | **Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Control and Advanced Control Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PID Control and Advanced Control Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is PID Control?

**PID Control (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Control)** is a universal, feedback-based closed-loop control mechanism widely used in industrial automation and robotic systems. It continuously calculates an *error value* $e(t)$ as the difference between a desired setpoint and a measured process variable, and applies a corrective action based on three tunable terms — **Proportional (P)**, **Integral (I)**, and **Derivative (D)** — to drive the system accurately and stably to its target.

Mathematically, the control law in the **continuous-time domain** is expressed as:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$ are the tunable gains. The standard error signal is $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, with $r(t)$ being the reference and $y(t)$ the measured output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PID control forms the *backbone of robotic joint-level and trajectory-level control*. The University expects students to derive the transfer function, explain each term's effect, and apply Ziegler–Nichols (ZN) tuning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Driving a Car

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a hilly road:

| Driver Action | PID Term | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| You press the accelerator *proportional* to how far you are from 60 km/h | **P (Proportional)** | Produces a corrective effort proportional to the *current* error magnitude |
| You keep the accelerator pressed a bit longer because the speed has been *consistently* below 60 for some time | **I (Integral)** | Accumulates past errors to eliminate steady-state offset |
| You ease off the accelerator quickly as you see a downhill slope approaching (anticipating future change) | **D (Derivative)** | Reacts to the *rate of change* of error — provides damping and predicts future error |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** If P is "reacting now", I is "remembering the past", and D is "predicting the future". A well-tuned PID blends all three to give fast, accurate, and stable control — exactly what an industrial robot arm needs to track a welding path or place a component precisely.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Robotic Control

The following are the *standard performance metrics* every KTU student must memorize:

- **Rise time ($t_r$):** Time to reach 90 % (or 10 %–90 %) of the setpoint — measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Settling time ($t_s$):** Time to stay within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of the setpoint — measured in **s**.
- **Steady-state error ($e_{ss}$):** Final offset between setpoint and output — ideally **0**.
- **Maximum overshoot ($M_p$):** Peak overshoot above the setpoint, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Gain Margin (GM):** measured in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Phase Margin (PM):** measured in **degrees (°)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for Stability:** A minimum Phase Margin of **$\text{PM} \geq 45°$** and Gain Margin of **$\text{GM} \geq 6 \text{ dB}$** are conventionally accepted as stability thresholds in KTU board exam answers.

---

## 1.4 Why PID is Critical in Robotic Assembly and Material Handling

In the OERUT724 course context, PID controllers are used for:

1. **Joint position control** of robot arms (each servo motor is essentially a PID loop).
2. **Velocity control** of conveyor belts in material handling.
3. **Force/torque control** during assembly (e.g., peg-in-hole insertion).
4. **Trajectory tracking** for pick-and-place operations.
5. **End-effector stabilization** under varying payloads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the PID Controller — The Three Components

### 2.1.1 Proportional (P) Term

$$u_P(t) = K_p \, e(t)$$

- **Why:** Provides an immediate corrective action proportional to the present error.
- **How it works:** A larger error $\Rightarrow$ larger control effort.
- **Effect of increasing $K_p$:** Faster response, but **larger overshoot** and potential oscillation.
- **Limitation:** Cannot eliminate steady-state error in many systems (e.g., a Type-0 plant with step input).
- **Real-world utility in robotics:** Used in low-precision applications like conveyor belt speed regulation.

### 2.1.2 Integral (I) Term

$$u_I(t) = K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau$$

- **Why:** Eliminates the residual *steady-state error* by accumulating all past errors.
- **How it works:** Even a tiny persistent error grows the integral until the controller produces enough effort to correct it.
- **Effect of increasing $K_i$:** Eliminates $e_{ss}$, but **increases overshoot** and slows down response.
- **Limitation:** Can cause *integral wind-up* — large integral accumulation during actuator saturation.
- **Real-world utility:** Essential in joint position control where zero steady-state error is non-negotiable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Windup:** In KTU answers, always mention **clamping** or **back-calculation** as anti-windup techniques if the actuator saturates.

### 2.1.3 Derivative (D) Term

$$u_D(t) = K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

- **Why:** Provides a *predictive* damping action based on the rate of error change.
- **How it works:** Acts like a brake — slows the response near the setpoint to reduce overshoot.
- **Effect of increasing $K_d$:** Increases damping, reduces overshoot, but **amplifies high-frequency noise**.
- **Limitation:** Ineffective for systems with sudden setpoint changes (derivative of a step is an impulse).
- **Real-world utility:** Critical in robotic manipulators to dampen oscillatory modes of flexible joints.

### 2.1.4 Combined PID Output

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

In many industrial implementations, the derivative is applied only to the *measured output* (not the error) to avoid the *derivative kick* problem during setpoint changes:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau - K_d \, \frac{dy(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.2 PID Transfer Function

Taking the Laplace transform of the PID control law (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s \right) E(s)$$

$$\boxed{\,\frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s\,}$$

Combining over a common denominator:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Result:** The PID controller is a *second-order numerator* over a *first-order denominator* — making it physically unrealizable in its ideal form. Real implementations add a *pole* to the derivative term:
> $$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + \frac{K_d \, s}{1 + \tau_f s}$$
> where $\tau_f$ is a small filter time constant (typically $\tau_f = 0.01$ to $0.1$ s) used to filter measurement noise.

## 2.3 Forms of PID Controller

| Form | Equation | Use in Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard / Parallel form** | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Most common in KTU syllabus |
| **Series / Cascade form** | $u(t) = K_p \left[ \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_i s}\right)\left(1 + T_d s\right) \right] e$ | Used in industrial PLCs |
| **Ideal vs. Real (Filtered)** | Adds a pole to derivative term | Avoids noise amplification |

## 2.4 Ziegler–Nichols (ZN) Tuning — The KTU-Favorite Method

The ZN tuning rules provide empirical starting values for $K_p$, $K_i$, and $K_d$.

### 2.4.1 Method 1 — Ultimate Gain (Closed-Loop) Method

**Steps:**

1. Set $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$ (pure P-control).
2. Gradually increase $K_p$ until the system output exhibits **sustained oscillations** of constant amplitude.
3. Record the **ultimate gain $K_u$** (the $K_p$ value at sustained oscillation) and the **ultimate period $P_u$** (period of oscillation).
4. Apply the ZN table below to compute PID gains.

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $0.5 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $\frac{P_u}{1.2}$ | — |
| **PID** | $\mathbf{0.6 \, K_u}$ | $\mathbf{\frac{P_u}{2}}$ | $\mathbf{\frac{P_u}{8}}$ |

Then convert to standard form: $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ and $K_d = K_p \, T_d$.

### 2.4.2 Method 2 — Reaction Curve (Open-Loop Step Response) Method

Apply a small step input to the open-loop plant and record the S-shaped reaction curve. From the curve, identify:

- $L$ = *transport delay* (dead time)
- $T$ = *time constant* of the plant
- $K$ = *process gain* = $\frac{\Delta Y}{\Delta U}$

| Controller | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P | $\frac{T}{L}$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.9 \, \frac{T}{L}$ | $3L$ | — |
| **PID** | $\mathbf{1.2 \, \frac{T}{L}}$ | $\mathbf{2L}$ | $\mathbf{0.5L}$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$ | PID control law | N·m or V |
| 2 | $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | Controller transfer function | — |
| 3 | $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ | Combined form | — |
| 4 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Tracking error | rad, m, °C |
| 5 | $\dot{e}(t) = \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Rate of change of error | rad/s, m/s |
| 6 | $K_{cr} = K_u$ | Ultimate / critical gain | — |
| 7 | $P_{cr} = P_u = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_u}$ | Ultimate period | s |
| 8 | ZN-PID: $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u/2$, $T_d = P_u/8$ | Ziegler–Nichols tuning | — |
| 9 | $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$, $K_d = K_p T_d$ | Gain conversions | — |
| 10 | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \cdot 100\%$ | % Overshoot (2nd-order) | % |
| 11 | $\text{PM} = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ | Phase margin | degrees |
| 12 | $\text{GM} = -20 \log \vert G(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$ | Gain margin | dB |
| 13 | $K_{ff} = \dfrac{1}{G_p(s)}$ | Feedforward compensator | — |
| 14 | $u_{ff} = K_{ff} \cdot r(t)$ | Feedforward control signal | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When writing $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) inside a table row, **always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX** instead of the `|` character to avoid breaking the markdown table.

## 2.6 Advanced Control Strategies in Robotics

When a single PID loop is insufficient (e.g., for nonlinear, time-varying, or multi-input multi-output (MIMO) robotic systems), advanced strategies are employed.

### 2.6.1 Cascade Control
A **primary (master) PID** controller sets the setpoint of a **secondary (slave) PID** controller. Used when there is a measurable secondary variable that responds faster than the primary one.

- **Example in robotics:** Position loop (outer) controls a velocity loop (inner) which controls a current/torque loop.
- **Benefit:** Faster disturbance rejection.

### 2.6.2 Feedforward Control
Uses a *model-based* prediction of the disturbance to cancel it before it affects the output. The total control signal is:

$$u_{total}(t) = u_{PID}(t) + u_{ff}(t)$$

- **Example:** In a robotic arm, feedforward gravity compensation $u_{ff} = G(q)$ is added to PID output to counter gravitational torques.

### 2.6.3 Adaptive Control
The controller **automatically adjusts its gains** in real time as the plant dynamics change (e.g., a robot arm picking up varying payloads).

- **MIT rule:** $\dot{K} = -\gamma \, e \, \frac{\partial e}{\partial K}$
- **Applications:** Material handling robots handling different masses.

### 2.6.4 Fuzzy Logic Control
Uses *linguistic rules* (IF-THEN) and membership functions to handle nonlinear, uncertain systems without an explicit mathematical model.

- **Example:** Fuzzy controller for trajectory tracking of a 6-DOF arm.

### 2.6.5 Computed Torque Control (Nonlinear Feedback Linearization)
For robotic manipulators, the joint torque is computed using the known dynamic model:

$$\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + u_{PID}$$

where $M(q)$ is the inertia matrix, $C$ contains Coriolis/centrifugal terms, and $G(q)$ is the gravity vector. A PID-based outer loop then tracks the desired trajectory.

### 2.6.6 Sliding Mode Control (SMC)
A robust control method where the control law *slides* the system state along a predefined *sliding surface*, providing insensitivity to matched disturbances.

$$u = -K \, \text{sgn}(s) \quad ; \quad s = \dot{e} + \lambda e$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Chattering** is a known drawback of SMC; higher-order SMC and boundary-layer techniques are used to mitigate it.

### 2.6.7 Model Predictive Control (MPC)
Solves an **optimization problem** at every sampling instant to compute the control action, subject to constraints. Widely used in modern industrial robotics and autonomous material handling.

### 2.6.8 Impedance / Admittance Control
Regulates the *dynamic relationship* between force and position at the end-effector — essential for **assembly tasks** like peg-in-hole insertion.

$$M_d \ddot{x} + B_d \dot{x} + K_d (x - x_d) = F_{ext}$$

where $M_d$, $B_d$, $K_d$ are desired inertia, damping, and stiffness matrices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PID Transfer Function from the Control Law

**Given:**

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \, \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1: Apply the Laplace Transform to each term**

For the proportional term:

$$\mathcal{L}\{K_p \, e(t)\} = K_p \, E(s)$$

For the integral term (using $\mathcal{L}\{\int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau\} = \dfrac{E(s)}{s}$):

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau \right\} = \frac{K_i}{s} \, E(s)$$

For the derivative term (using $\mathcal{L}\{\dot e(t)\} = s E(s) - e(0)$, with $e(0) = 0$):

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{ K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right\} = K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

**Step 2: Sum the three components**

$$U(s) = K_p \, E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d \, s \, E(s)$$

**Step 3: Factor out $E(s)$**

$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 4: Compute the controller transfer function**

$$\boxed{\,G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d \, s\,}$$

**Step 5: Combine over a common denominator $s$**

$$G_c(s) = \frac{K_p \, s}{s} + \frac{K_i}{s} + \frac{K_d \, s^2}{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,G_c(s) = \frac{K_d \, s^2 + K_p \, s + K_i}{s}\,}$$

**Conversion Logic (text row):** This shows that the PID controller introduces a *zero* in the right half of the $s$-plane when $K_d$ is large — and the integrator pole at the origin provides **Type-1** behavior (zero steady-state error to a step input).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of a Unity-Feedback System with PID

**Given:** A plant with transfer function $G_p(s)$ and a PID controller $G_c(s)$ in a unity-feedback loop.

**Step 1: Write the forward-path transfer function**

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

**Step 2: Apply the standard closed-loop formula**

$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

For unity feedback ($H(s) = 1$):

$$T(s) = \frac{G_c(s) \, G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \, G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $G_c(s)$**

$$T(s) = \frac{\left(K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) G_p(s)}{1 + \left(K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 4: Multiply numerator and denominator by $s$**

$$T(s) = \frac{\left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right) G_p(s)}{s + \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 5: Substitute a typical second-order plant** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ (a common robotic joint model)

$$T(s) = \frac{\left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right) \dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}}{s + \left(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i\right) \dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(\tau s + 1)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2(\tau s + 1) + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}$$

**Step 6: Expand denominator**

$$s^2(\tau s + 1) = \tau s^3 + s^2$$

Adding the controller terms:

$$\text{Denominator} = \tau s^3 + s^2 + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i$$

$$\text{Denominator} = \tau s^3 + (1 + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i$$

$$\boxed{\,T(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{\tau s^3 + (1 + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i}\,}$$

**Conversion Logic:** For stability, all coefficients of the denominator polynomial must be **positive** (necessary but not sufficient for a 3rd-order system). The **Routh–Hurwitz criterion** is then applied.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Error Using the Final Value Theorem

**Step 1: Write the error transfer function**

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

For a **unit step input** $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$:

$$E(s) = \frac{\dfrac{1}{s}}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)} = \frac{1}{s \left[1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)\right]}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Final Value Theorem (assuming stable closed-loop)**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1}{s \left[1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)\right]} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute the PID controller** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$ and plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)}$

$$G_c(s) G_p(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s^2 (\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the limit as $s \to 0$**

As $s \to 0$, the numerator approaches $K_i$ and the denominator approaches $0^2 \cdot 1 = 0$. Hence the product $G_c(s) G_p(s) \to \infty$.

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = \frac{1}{\infty} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = 0 \text{ for a unit step input with PID controller}\,}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is why PID is so widely used — the **integrator** in the controller makes the open-loop system **Type-2** (when combined with a Type-1 plant), driving the step-error to zero.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ziegler–Nichols Ultimate Gain Tuning

**Step 1: Identify $K_u$ and $P_u$**

Suppose the sustained oscillation has a frequency $f_u = 0.5$ Hz.

$$P_u = \frac{1}{f_u} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ s}$$

Let the recorded ultimate gain be $K_u = 10$.

**Step 2: Apply the ZN-PID table**

$$K_p = 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 10 = 6$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ s}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to standard form**

$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{6}{1} = 6$$

$$K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 6 \times 0.25 = 1.5$$

$$\boxed{\,K_p = 6, \quad K_i = 6, \quad K_d = 1.5\,}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The student should always show both forms — the ZN table form ($K_p, T_i, T_d$) and the standard form ($K_p, K_i, K_d$) — for full marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stability via Routh–Hurwitz

**Given:** Closed-loop characteristic equation from §3.2:

$$\tau s^3 + (1 + K_d) s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$$

Let $\tau = 1$, $K_d = 1$, $K_p = 5$, $K_i = 2$:

$$s^3 + 2 s^2 + 5 s + 2 = 0$$

**Routh Array:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 5 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 2 | 2 | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{2 \cdot 5 - 1 \cdot 2}{2} = 4$ | 0 | 0 |
| $s^0$ | 2 | 0 | 0 |

**Step:** All first-column elements are **positive** ($1, 2, 4, 2$). Hence the system is **stable**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Advanced Strategies

| Strategy | Model Required | Handles Nonlinearity | Real-time Complexity | Typical Robotic Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PID** | No | Poor | Very Low | Joint position control |
| **Cascade PID** | Partial | Moderate | Low | Position + Velocity loops |
| **Feedforward + PID** | Yes | Moderate | Low–Medium | Gravity compensation |
| **Adaptive PID** | No | High | Medium | Varying-payload arms |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | No (rule-based) | High | Medium | Trajectory tracking |
| **Computed Torque** | Yes (full) | High | High | Manipulator dynamics |
| **Sliding Mode** | Partial | High | Medium | Robust tracking |
| **MPC** | Yes | Moderate | High | Constrained motion |
| **Impedance Control** | Partial | High | Medium | Assembly contact tasks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Unity-Feedback PID Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Setpoint r t] --> SUM1((Summing Junction +))
    SUM1 --> E[Error e t = r minus y]
    E --> PID[PID Controller Gc s]
    PID --> U[Control Signal u t]
    U --> PLANT[Robotic Plant Gp s]
    PLANT --> Y[Output y t]
    Y --> FB[Feedback H s = 1]
    FB --> SUM1
    DIST[Disturbance d t] -.-> PLANT
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- The **setpoint** $r(t)$ is the desired joint angle / end-effector position.
- The **summing junction** computes the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- The **PID controller** generates the control signal $u(t)$.
- The **plant** $G_p(s)$ represents the robotic joint dynamics.
- The **disturbance** $d(t)$ models unmeasured effects like friction or external forces.
- The **feedback path** returns the measured output for continuous error correction.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Cascade Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Position Reference] --> SUM1((Outer Sum +))
    SUM1 --> POS_PID[Position PID Outer Loop]
    POS_PID --> VSUM[Velocity Setpoint]
    VSUM --> SUM2((Inner Sum +))
    SUM2 --> VEL_PID[Velocity PID Inner Loop]
    VEL_PID --> CUR_SUM[Current Reference]
    CUR_SUM --> SUM3((Current Sum +))
    SUM3 --> CUR_PID[Current PI Inner Inner Loop]
    CUR_PID --> PWM[PWM Drive Signal]
    PWM --> MOTOR[Servo Motor]
    MOTOR --> SHAFT[Output Shaft Position]
    SHAFT --> POS_FB[Position Sensor]
    POS_FB --> SUM1
    SHAFT --> VEL_FB[Velocity Sensor]
    VEL_FB --> SUM2
    MOTOR --> CUR_FB[Current Sensor]
    CUR_FB --> SUM3
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- **Three nested loops:** Position (slowest, outer) → Velocity (middle) → Current (fastest, innermost).
- The **inner loops** reject disturbances much faster than a single position loop could.
- This is the **industry standard** architecture for industrial robotic servo drives.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Feedforward + Feedback (PID) Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Trajectory Reference r t] --> FF[Feedforward Compensator Kff]
    R --> SUM1((Feedback Sum +))
    FF --> FF_SUM((Total Control Sum +))
    SUM1 --> PID[PID Controller Gc s]
    PID --> FF_SUM
    FF_SUM --> U[Total Control Signal u t]
    U --> PLANT[Robotic Manipulator Plant]
    PLANT --> Y[Actual Output y t]
    Y --> SUM1
    DIST[Disturbance / Unmodelled Dynamics] -.-> PLANT
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- The **feedforward** term $u_{ff} = K_{ff} \cdot r(t)$ uses the model inverse $K_{ff} = G_p(s)^{-1}$ to *pre-emptively* generate the control signal.
- The **PID** term compensates for modelling errors and disturbances.
- The **sum** $u_{total} = u_{PID} + u_{ff}$ drives the plant.
- This architecture is fundamental for **Computed Torque Control** of robotic manipulators.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Adaptive Control System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Reference r t] --> SUM1((Error Junction +))
    SUM1 --> AC[Adaptive Controller with Tunable Kp Ki Kd]
    AC --> U[Control Signal u t]
    U --> PLANT[Time Varying Plant e.g. Robot with Varying Load]
    PLANT --> Y[Output y t]
    Y --> SUM1
    Y --> ID[Parameter Identifier / Estimator]
    ID --> AC_TUNE[Tuning Algorithm e.g. MIT Rule or Lyapunov Based]
    AC_TUNE --> AC
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- The **parameter identifier** estimates the changing plant dynamics (e.g., the moment of inertia as the payload changes).
- The **tuning algorithm** updates the controller gains in real time.
- This is essential for **material-handling robots** that pick up objects of different weights.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Control in Robotic Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TASK[Task Level What to do e.g. Insert Peg] --> PLAN[Trajectory Planner]
    PLAN --> TRAJ[Reference Trajectory qd qd d qd dd]
    TRAJ --> OUTER[Outer Loop Computed Torque or PID]
    OUTER --> INNER[Inner Loop Current or Velocity PI]
    INNER --> ACT[Actuator PWM Signal]
    ACT --> ROBOT[6 DOF Manipulator]
    ROBOT --> FB1[Joint Position Sensors]
    FB1 --> OUTER
    ROBOT --> FB2[Joint Velocity and Torque Sensors]
    FB2 --> INNER
    ROBOT --> FT[Force Torque Sensor at Wrist]
    FT --> IMP[Impedance or Admittance Controller]
    IMP --> OUTER
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

- The **task planner** decomposes the high-level goal into a trajectory.
- The **outer loop** tracks the trajectory using computed torque / PID.
- The **inner loop** regulates the actuator current/velocity.
- The **force/torque sensor** closes an additional **impedance control** loop, essential for compliant assembly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions are short-answer type, requiring concise, textbook-style answers of 3–4 sentences with one key equation or diagram.

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the effect of the Proportional, Integral, and Derivative terms on the transient response of a closed-loop system.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Proportional term** ($K_p$) produces an output proportional to the current error, reducing the rise time but cannot eliminate steady-state error on its own. The **Integral term** ($K_i$) accumulates past errors, eliminating steady-state error but increasing overshoot and settling time. The **Derivative term** ($K_d$) anticipates future error by acting on its rate of change, providing damping that reduces overshoot and improves stability. The combined PID law is:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is integral wind-up? Mention two techniques to prevent it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Integral wind-up** is the phenomenon where the integral term continues to accumulate error during actuator saturation, causing large overshoot and poor transient response once the actuator recovers. Two common anti-windup techniques are:
1. **Clamping (Conditional Integration):** Stops integration when the actuator output saturates.
2. **Back-calculation:** Feeds back the difference between the saturated and unsaturated control output to reduce the integral term dynamically.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### **Part (a):** Derive the transfer function of a PID controller in its parallel form. Explain the effect of each gain on system performance.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The PID control law in the time domain is:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):
$$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s E(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $E(s)$:
$$U(s) = \left( K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s \right) E(s)$$

**Step 4:** Controller transfer function:
$$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 5: Effect of each gain:**

| Gain | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | Small change | Decreases (but not zero) |
| $K_i \uparrow$ | Decreases | Increases | Increases | Eliminates $e_{ss}$ |
| $K_d \uparrow$ | Small change | Decreases | Decreases | No effect |

(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 3 for the table/explanation)

---

#### **Part (b):** A unity-feedback system has plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+4)}$. Using the Ziegler–Nichols ultimate gain method, design a PID controller. The system with pure P-control exhibits sustained oscillations at $K_u = 12$ with period $P_u = 1.5$ s.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the ZN ultimate parameters from the given data:
- Ultimate gain: $K_u = 12$
- Ultimate period: $P_u = 1.5$ s

**Step 2:** Apply ZN-PID table:
$$K_p = 0.6 \, K_u = 0.6 \times 12 = 7.2$$
$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ s}$$
$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{1.5}{8} = 0.1875 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to standard PID form:
$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{7.2}{0.75} = 9.6$$
$$K_d = K_p \times T_d = 7.2 \times 0.1875 = 1.35$$

**Step 4:** Final controller:
$$\boxed{G_c(s) = 7.2 + \frac{9.6}{s} + 1.35 \, s = \frac{1.35 s^2 + 7.2 s + 9.6}{s}}$$

**Step 5: Closed-loop verification:** Substitute into unity-feedback formula:
$$T(s) = \frac{(1.35 s^2 + 7.2 s + 9.6)/s}{s(s+4) + (1.35 s^2 + 7.2 s + 9.6)/s}$$
After multiplying by $s$, the characteristic equation becomes:
$$s^3 + 5.35 s^2 + 7.2 s + 9.6 = 0$$

Applying the Routh–Hurwitz criterion confirms stability (all first-column entries positive).

(7 marks — 3 for tuning calculations, 2 for conversion, 2 for stability check)

---

### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### **Part (a):** With neat block diagrams, explain the cascade control strategy and feedforward control strategy as applied to a robotic manipulator. How does feedforward + feedback improve performance over a standalone PID?

**Model Solution:**

**Cascade Control:**
A cascade control system has two nested loops:
- **Outer (primary) loop:** Controls the main variable (e.g., joint position).
- **Inner (secondary) loop:** Controls a faster, intermediate variable (e.g., joint velocity or motor current).

The output of the outer PID is the setpoint for the inner PID. The inner loop rejects disturbances much faster than the outer loop alone, providing superior disturbance rejection.

**Feedforward Control:**
Feedforward uses the known reference trajectory and an *inverse model* of the plant to compute a *pre-emptive* control signal:
$$u_{ff}(s) = K_{ff}(s) \cdot R(s) = \frac{1}{G_p(s)} R(s)$$

This is added to the PID feedback signal:
$$u_{total} = u_{PID} + u_{ff}$$

**Why the combination wins:**
- **Feedforward** produces the *bulk* of the control effort based on the *desired* trajectory — no waiting for the error to develop.
- **PID feedback** compensates for *modelling errors* and *unmeasured disturbances*.
- The result: **Faster response**, **reduced tracking error**, and **less burden on the PID** (so $K_p$ can be lower, reducing overshoot).

**For a robotic arm:** The feedforward term is typically the computed gravitational torque $G(q)$:
$$\tau_{total} = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d + G(q) + \tau_{PID}$$

(7 marks — 2 for cascade explanation, 2 for feedforward, 3 for the combination benefit)

---

#### **Part (b):** A robotic joint has the open-loop transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$. A PID controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ is added. Using the reaction-curve method, the dead time is $L = 0.2$ s, time constant $T = 1$ s, and process gain $K = 2$. Compute the PID gains.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recall ZN Reaction-Curve Formulas for PID:**
$$K_p = 1.2 \frac{T}{L}$$
$$T_i = 2L$$
$$T_d = 0.5L$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values:**
$$K_p = 1.2 \times \frac{1}{0.2} = 1.2 \times 5 = 6$$
$$T_i = 2 \times 0.2 = 0.4 \text{ s}$$
$$T_d = 0.5 \times 0.2 = 0.1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to standard form:**
$$K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{6}{0.4} = 15$$
$$K_d = K_p \times T_d = 6 \times 0.1 = 0.6$$

**Step 4: Final PID gains:**
$$\boxed{K_p = 6, \quad K_i = 15, \quad K_d = 0.6}$$

**Step 5: The controller transfer function becomes:**
$$G_c(s) = 6 + \frac{15}{s} + 0.6 s = \frac{0.6 s^2 + 6 s + 15}{s}$$

**Step 6: Closed-loop characteristic equation** (verification):
$$s(s+1)(s+5) + 0.6 s^2 + 6 s + 15 = 0$$
$$s^3 + 6 s^2 + 5 s + 0.6 s^2 + 6 s + 15 = 0$$
$$s^3 + 6.6 s^2 + 11 s + 15 = 0$$

Routh array confirms stability (all first-column entries positive: 1, 6.6, 11, 15).

(7 marks — 3 for formula application, 2 for conversion, 2 for stability verification)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert ZN tuning parameters:** Many students compute $K_p$, $T_i$, $T_d$ from the ZN table but forget to convert to $K_i$ and $K_d$ for the standard PID form — **2 marks lost**.
> 2. **Ideal vs. Realized PID:** Writing the ideal transfer function $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ without noting it is *not physically realizable* — examiners often deduct **1 mark** for missing this.
> 3. **Derivative on Error vs. Output:** Not mentioning that the derivative is applied to the *measured output* (not error) to avoid *derivative kick* on setpoint changes — **1–2 marks lost**.
> 4. **Steady-State Error Derivations:** Skipping the final-value theorem and the assumption of closed-loop stability — **2 marks lost**.
> 5. **Confusing $K_u$ and $K_{cr}$:** They are the same. Use the same notation as the question.
> 6. **Missing Units in Formulas:** Always write units for $K_p, K_i, K_d$ where applicable.
> 7. **No Block Diagram:** KTU awards 1–2 marks for a *neat labeled block diagram* in controller-based questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PID Law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$
- [x] **PID Transfer Function:** $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$
- [x] **P-term:** Reduces rise time, cannot eliminate $e_{ss}$.
- [x] **I-term:** Eliminates $e_{ss}$ but causes overshoot & wind-up.
- [x] **D-term:** Provides damping, reduces overshoot, amplifies noise.
- [x] **Anti-Windup Methods:** Clamping, Back-calculation.
- [x] **ZN Ultimate-Gain Method:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u/2$, $T_d = P_u/8$.
- [x] **ZN Reaction-Curve Method:** $K_p = 1.2 T/L$, $T_i = 2L$, $T_d = 0.5L$.
- [x] **Conversion Formulas:** $K_i = K_p/T_i$, $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$.
- [x] **Cascade Control:** Outer (slow) + Inner (fast) loop — better disturbance rejection.
- [x] **Feedforward Control:** $u_{ff} = G_p^{-1} r(t)$ — model-based, pre-emptive.
- [x] **Computed Torque Control:** $\tau = M(q)\ddot q + C(q,\dot q)\dot q + G(q) + \tau_{PID}$.
- [x] **Adaptive Control:** $K_p, K_i, K_d$ update in real time (MIT rule or Lyapunov-based).
- [x] **Impedance Control:** $M_d \ddot x + B_d \dot x + K_d (x - x_d) = F_{ext}$ — for assembly.
- [x] **Stability Criterion:** All Routh-array first-column entries must be positive.
- [x] **Phase Margin:** $\text{PM} \geq 45°$; **Gain Margin:** $\text{GM} \geq 6$ dB.
- [x] **Standard Performance Metrics:** $t_r$, $t_s$, $M_p$ (%), $e_{ss}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Planning and Trajectory Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motion Planning and Trajectory Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motion Planning — Formal Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme robotics, **Motion Planning** is defined as the computational process of determining a collision-free, kinematically feasible, and dynamically executable path for a robot manipulator (or mobile platform) from an initial configuration to a desired goal configuration, while respecting joint limits, velocity bounds, and task-space constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Motion planning is broadly classified into two hierarchical sub-problems:
> 1. **Path Planning** (Geometric / Spatial): "Where does the robot go?"
> 2. **Trajectory Generation** (Temporal / Dynamic): "How does the robot move along that path with respect to time?"

Mathematically, a motion plan is a continuous mapping:

$$
\pi : [0, 1] \rightarrow C_{free} \subset C
$$

where $C$ is the **Configuration Space (C-space)** of the robot, $C_{free}$ is the obstacle-free subset, and $\pi$ is a continuous, collision-free configuration curve.

## 1.2 Trajectory Generation — Formal Definition

**Trajectory Generation** is the temporal parameterization of a planned geometric path. It assigns a time history (position, velocity, and acceleration profiles) to each joint variable or to the end-effector pose, ensuring smooth, bounded, and physically realizable motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction a KTU examiner expects:**
> - A **Path** is a purely spatial curve (e.g., a straight line in 3D).
> - A **Trajectory** is a path with an associated time law $s(t)$, where $s$ is the path parameter.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from your home (start) to a mall (goal) in a city with traffic:

- **Path Planning** = Consulting Google Maps to find a road that avoids roadblocks, one-way streets, and no-entry zones. The map shows the *geometric route* but not *when* you arrive at each junction.
- **Trajectory Generation** = Deciding your speed profile — accelerate gently, cruise at 60 km/h, brake smoothly to stop at a red light. The total distance covered per second is now defined.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **robotic assembly cell**, motion planning decides *which way the gripper travels to pick a bolt from a tray*, and trajectory generation decides *how fast and how smoothly the arm moves so it doesn't drop the bolt or jerk the conveyor belt*.

## 1.4 Key Terminology for the KTU Board Exam

| Term | Formal Definition | Standard Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration Space | Set of all possible robot configurations | $C$ |
| Free C-space | Subset of $C$ with no collisions | $C_{free}$ |
| Path Parameter | Scalar progress variable along path | $s(t) \in [0, 1]$ |
| Joint Limits | Mechanical bounds on joint rotation | $q_{i}^{\min}, q_{i}^{\max}$ |
| Velocity Bound | Maximum permissible joint rate | $\dot{q}_{i}^{\max}$ |
| Singularity | Configuration where Jacobian loses rank | $\det(J(q)) = 0$ |

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Maximum joint acceleration** for industrial 6-DOF arms: typically **$\ddot{q}_{max} \le 50 \text{ rad/s}^2$**.
- **Cycle time** in assembly: typically **2 – 10 seconds**.
- **Trajectory smoothness order**: bounded up to **4th derivative (Jerk)** in modern controllers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Motion Planning

The complete motion planning stack for a robotic assembly system can be decomposed as follows:

### Level 1 — Task-Level Planning
- **Input:** High-level goal (e.g., "pick bolt from tray A, place on gearbox B").
- **Output:** Sequence of waypoints $W = \{w_0, w_1, \dots, w_n\}$.
- **Output:** A symbolic task graph (Precedence Constraints).

### Level 2 — Geometric Path Planning
- **Input:** Start pose $T_{start}$, goal pose $T_{goal}$, C-space map.
- **Output:** A continuous curve in $C_{free}$ with no collision.
- **Methods Studied in KTU Module 2:**
  - **Roadmap Methods** (Visibility Graphs, Voronoi Diagrams)
  - **Cell Decomposition** (Exact & Approximate)
  - **Sampling-Based** (Probabilistic Roadmaps — PRM, Rapidly-exploring Random Trees — RRT)
  - **Artificial Potential Fields** (Attractive + Repulsive)

### Level 3 — Trajectory Generation (Time Parameterization)
- **Input:** Geometric path + boundary conditions (position, velocity, time).
- **Output:** $\theta(t), \dot{\theta}(t), \ddot{\theta}(t)$ for each joint.
- **Methods Studied in KTU Module 2:**
  - **Joint-Space Polynomial Interpolation** (Cubic, Quintic)
  - **Cartesian-Space Straight-Line Trajectories**
  - **Trapezoidal Velocity Profile**
  - **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSBP)**

## 2.2 Why Two Coordinate Spaces?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:**
> - **Joint-Space Planning** guarantees the path is naturally feasible (no kinematic singularities), but the end-effector traces a *curved* and often *unpredictable* Cartesian arc.
> - **Cartesian-Space Planning** ensures the end-effector traces a *straight line* (essential for welding, gluing, or insertion), but may pass through a **singularity** or violate joint limits.

The mapping between them is the **Forward Kinematic Equation**:

$$
x = f(\theta) \quad \text{or} \quad T_{0}^{n}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_{i-1}^{i}(\theta_i)
$$

The differential mapping is the **Jacobian**:

$$
\dot{x} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Use Case | Key Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cubic Joint Position | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Smooth point-to-point | $\theta(0), \theta(t_f), \dot{\theta}(0)=\dot{\theta}(t_f)=0$ |
| 2 | Cubic Coefficients | $a_0=\theta_0,\; a_1=0,\; a_2=\frac{3}{t_f^2}\Delta\theta,\; a_3=-\frac{2}{t_f^3}\Delta\theta$ | Direct computation | $\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0$ |
| 3 | Quintic Joint Position | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | Acceleration-continuous | $\theta, \dot{\theta}, \ddot{\theta}$ at $0$ and $t_f$ |
| 4 | Quintic Coefficient $a_0$ | $a_0 = \theta_0$ | Initial position | — |
| 5 | Quintic Coefficient $a_1$ | $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$ | Initial velocity | — |
| 6 | Quintic Coefficient $a_2$ | $a_2 = \frac{\ddot{\theta}_0}{2}$ | Initial acceleration | — |
| 7 | Quintic Coefficient $a_3$ | $a_3 = \frac{1}{2 t_f^3} [20\Delta\theta - (8\dot{\theta}_f + 12\dot{\theta}_0) t_f - (3\ddot{\theta}_0 - \ddot{\theta}_f) t_f^2]$ | Cross-coupling term | All boundary conditions |
| 8 | Quintic Coefficient $a_4$ | $a_4 = \frac{1}{2 t_f^4} [30\Delta\theta + (14\dot{\theta}_f + 16\dot{\theta}_0) t_f + (3\ddot{\theta}_0 - 2\ddot{\theta}_f) t_f^2]$ | Higher-order coupling | All boundary conditions |
| 9 | Quintic Coefficient $a_5$ | $a_5 = \frac{1}{2 t_f^5} [12\Delta\theta - 6(\dot{\theta}_f + \dot{\theta}_0) t_f - (\ddot{\theta}_0 - \ddot{\theta}_f) t_f^2]$ | Final curvature term | All boundary conditions |
| 10 | Trapezoidal Velocity | $\dot{\theta}(t) = \begin{cases} a t, & 0 \le t \le t_a \\ v_{max}, & t_a \le t \le t_f - t_a \\ a(t_f - t), & t_f - t_a \le t \le t_f \end{cases}$ | High-speed pick-and-place | $t_a = v_{max}/a$ |
| 11 | Attractive Potential | $U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2$ | Goal seeking | Quadratic basin |
| 12 | Repulsive Potential | $U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left(\frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^2, & \rho \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho > \rho_0 \end{cases}$ | Obstacle avoidance | $\rho$ = distance to obstacle |
| 13 | Net Force in APF | $F(q) = -\nabla U_{att} - \nabla U_{rep}$ | Velocity command | $F = J^T(\theta) F_{cart}$ |
| 14 | Jacobian Velocity | $\dot{x} = J(\theta) \dot{\theta}$ | Cartesian rate from joint rate | $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}\dot{x}$ (non-singular) |
| 15 | Trapezoidal Accel Time | $t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$ | Time to reach cruise | Symmetric profile assumed |

> [!NOTE]
> All units follow SI: $\theta$ in **rad**, $\dot{\theta}$ in **rad/s**, $\ddot{\theta}$ in **rad/s²**, $t$ in **seconds**, $v_{max}$ in **rad/s**, $a_{max}$ in **rad/s²**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cubic Polynomials**: Used in CNC milling for smooth tool entry/exit; widely deployed in SCARA pick-and-place machines where motion duration is short.
- **Quintic Polynomials**: Used in collaborative robots (cobots) where **jerk limitation** is mandated by **ISO 10218** and **ISO/TS 15066** for human safety.
- **Trapezoidal Velocity Profiles**: Used in **high-speed conveyor transfer robots** in the packaging industry (e.g., delta robots in pharma packaging).
- **Artificial Potential Fields**: Used in real-time **dynamic obstacle avoidance** in mobile robots operating in warehouses (e.g., Amazon Kiva systems).
- **Sampling-Based Methods (RRT)**: Used in autonomous vehicle motion planning and humanoid robot footstep planning over rough terrain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cubic Polynomial Trajectory

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

A single joint of a robot must move from initial angle $\theta_0$ to final angle $\theta_f$ in time $t_f$. The joint must start and end with **zero velocity** (to prevent jerky starts/stops in an assembly task). Find the smooth trajectory $\theta(t)$.

### 3.1.2 Assume the General Form

The lowest-order polynomial with 4 free coefficients (needed to match 4 boundary conditions) is the **cubic**:

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

Differentiating once gives joint velocity:

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2
$$

Differentiating twice gives joint acceleration:

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t
$$

### 3.1.3 Apply the Four Boundary Conditions

**Condition 1:** Initial position at $t=0$ is $\theta_0$.

$$
\theta(0) = a_0 = \theta_0
$$

**Condition 2:** Final position at $t = t_f$ is $\theta_f$.

$$
\theta(t_f) = a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

**Condition 3:** Initial velocity is zero.

$$
\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 = 0
$$

**Condition 4:** Final velocity is zero.

$$
\dot{\theta}(t_f) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0
$$

### 3.1.4 Solve the Linear System

From Condition 1: $a_0 = \theta_0$.

From Condition 3: $a_1 = 0$.

Substituting $a_1 = 0$ into Condition 4:

$$
2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} a_3 t_f
$$

Substituting $a_1 = 0$ and $a_2$ into Condition 2:

$$
a_0 + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

$$
\theta_0 + \left(-\frac{3}{2} a_3 t_f\right) t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

$$
\theta_0 - \frac{3}{2} a_3 t_f^3 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

$$
\theta_0 - \frac{1}{2} a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{2(\theta_0 - \theta_f)}{t_f^3} = -\frac{2}{t_f^3} \Delta\theta
$$

where $\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0$.

Therefore:

$$
a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} \left(-\frac{2}{t_f^3} \Delta\theta\right) t_f = \frac{3}{t_f^2} \Delta\theta
$$

### 3.1.5 Final Cubic Trajectory

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{3 \Delta\theta}{t_f^2} t^2 - \frac{2 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} t^3
$$

This is the **standard KTU textbook form** (Spong, Hutchinson, Vidyasagar).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Quintic Polynomial Trajectory

### 3.2.1 Why Quintic?

The cubic trajectory has **continuous velocity** but exhibits a **discontinuous acceleration** at the start and end of the segment (the acceleration is non-zero at $t=0$ and $t=t_f$, dropping to zero instantaneously outside the interval). For **assembly robots** that handle delicate components, the resulting **infinite jerk** is unacceptable. We need a higher-order polynomial that also matches the **boundary accelerations**.

### 3.2.2 Assume the General Form

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

With derivatives:

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 + 4 a_4 t^3 + 5 a_5 t^4
$$

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3
$$

### 3.2.3 Apply the Six Boundary Conditions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta(0) &= \theta_0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_0 = \theta_0 \\
\dot{\theta}(0) &= \dot{\theta}_0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0 \\
\ddot{\theta}(0) &= \ddot{\theta}_0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2 = \frac{\ddot{\theta}_0}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the final time $t = t_f$, define $\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0$, $\Delta\dot{\theta} = \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0$, $\Delta\ddot{\theta} = \ddot{\theta}_f - \ddot{\theta}_0$.

After substituting and simplifying (the algebra is lengthy but standard — see the Cheat Sheet in Section 2.3), the remaining three coefficients are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_3 &= \frac{1}{2 t_f^3} \left[ 20 \Delta\theta - (8 \dot{\theta}_f + 12 \dot{\theta}_0) t_f - (3 \ddot{\theta}_0 - \ddot{\theta}_f) t_f^2 \right] \\
a_4 &= \frac{1}{2 t_f^4} \left[ 30 \Delta\theta + (14 \dot{\theta}_f + 16 \dot{\theta}_0) t_f + (3 \ddot{\theta}_0 - 2 \ddot{\theta}_f) t_f^2 \right] \\
a_5 &= \frac{1}{2 t_f^5} \left[ 12 \Delta\theta - 6 (\dot{\theta}_f + \dot{\theta}_0) t_f - (\ddot{\theta}_0 - \ddot{\theta}_f) t_f^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Special Case: All Velocities and Accelerations Zero

When $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = \ddot{\theta}_0 = \ddot{\theta}_f = 0$ (the most common KTU exam case), the coefficients reduce to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= \theta_0 \\
a_1 &= 0 \\
a_2 &= 0 \\
a_3 &= \frac{10 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} \\
a_4 &= \frac{15 \Delta\theta}{t_f^4} \\
a_5 &= \frac{6 \Delta\theta}{t_f^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the simplified quintic trajectory is:

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{10 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} t^3 - \frac{15 \Delta\theta}{t_f^4} t^4 + \frac{6 \Delta\theta}{t_f^5} t^5
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A robot joint must move from $\theta_0 = 20°$ to $\theta_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 4$ seconds with zero initial and final velocities using a **cubic polynomial**. Determine (a) the trajectory equation, (b) the maximum velocity, and (c) the maximum acceleration.

### Step 1 — Convert to Radians

$$
\theta_0 = 20° = \frac{20 \pi}{180} = 0.3491 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_f = 80° = \frac{80 \pi}{180} = 1.3963 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Displacement

$$
\Delta\theta = 1.3963 - 0.3491 = 1.0472 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Coefficients

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= \theta_0 = 0.3491 \\
a_1 &= 0 \\
a_2 &= \frac{3 \Delta\theta}{t_f^2} = \frac{3 \times 1.0472}{4^2} = \frac{3.1416}{16} = 0.19635 \\
a_3 &= -\frac{2 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} = -\frac{2 \times 1.0472}{4^3} = -\frac{2.0944}{64} = -0.032725
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Write the Trajectory

$$
\theta(t) = 0.3491 + 0.19635\, t^2 - 0.032725\, t^3 \quad \text{rad}
$$

### Step 5 — Velocity Profile

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 0.3927\, t - 0.098175\, t^2
$$

Maximum velocity occurs at the vertex of this parabola:

$$
\frac{d\dot{\theta}}{dt} = 0.3927 - 0.19635\, t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; t^* = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}_{max} = 0.3927 (2) - 0.098175 (4) = 0.7854 - 0.3927 = 0.3927 \text{ rad/s}
$$

### Step 6 — Acceleration Profile

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0.3927 - 0.19635\, t
$$

Maximum acceleration is at $t=0$ (and the maximum deceleration is at $t=4$):

$$
\ddot{\theta}_{max} = 0.3927 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** Notice that the maximum velocity occurs exactly at the midpoint of the trajectory ($t = t_f/2 = 2$ s) — this is a **property of the symmetric cubic trajectory** and is a frequently tested KTU concept.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Motion Planning Hierarchical Flow

The following block diagram shows the complete motion planning pipeline as required in a robotic assembly cell. Each block represents a transformation of information — from the abstract task description down to the voltage signals sent to the joint motors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TASK INPUT: Pick and Place Bolt] --> B[Task Level Planner]
    B --> C[Waypoint Sequence: W0 to Wn]
    C --> D[Path Planner: C-space Search]
    D --> E{Collision Free Path Exists?}
    E -- No --> F[Re-plan with Updated Map]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Geometric Path: Curvilinear in C-free]
    G --> H{Path Domain Selection}
    H --> I[Joint Space Trajectory Generator]
    H --> J[Cartesian Space Trajectory Generator]
    I --> K[Polynomial Interpolation: Cubic or Quintic]
    J --> L[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    L --> M[Convert to Joint Variables]
    M --> K
    K --> N[Time Indexed theta t, dtheta t, ddtheta t]
    N --> O[Joint Servo Controller]
    O --> P[Motor Voltage Commands]
    P --> Q[Robot Joint Motion in Workspace]
```

## 4.2 Trajectory Generation Block Architecture

This diagram focuses specifically on the trajectory generation sub-problem, showing how the boundary conditions flow into the polynomial solver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial Configuration: theta 0] --> D[Trajectory Solver Block]
    B[Final Configuration: theta f] --> D
    C[Time Duration: t f] --> D
    E[Initial Velocity: dtheta 0] --> D
    F[Final Velocity: dtheta f] --> D
    G[Initial Acceleration: ddtheta 0] --> D
    H[Final Acceleration: ddtheta f] --> D
    D --> I{Polynomial Order Selection}
    I --> J[Cubic: 4 Boundary Conditions]
    I --> K[Quintic: 6 Boundary Conditions]
    J --> L[Coefficient Vector: a 0 a 1 a 2 a 3]
    K --> M[Coefficient Vector: a 0 a 1 a 2 a 3 a 4 a 5]
    L --> N[Closed Form Position Function theta t]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Differentiation Block]
    O --> P[Velocity dtheta t and Acceleration ddtheta t]
    P --> Q[Real Time Interpolator: Output every 1 to 10 ms]
    Q --> R[Joint Servo Setpoints]
```

## 4.3 Configuration Space Obstacle Mapping

The configuration space (C-space) is the central abstraction in motion planning. The diagram below illustrates how a physical obstacle in the workspace becomes a forbidden region in the C-space.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workspace W: Physical 3D Lab] --> B[Robot Link Geometry: L1, L2]
    A --> C[Obstacles: O1, O2, O3]
    B --> D[Forward Kinematic Map f: q to x]
    C --> E[Inverse Mapping of Obstacles]
    D --> F[Configuration Space: C]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Free Space: C-free]
    F --> H[Forbidden Region: C-obstacle]
    G --> I[Path Planning Algorithm: RRT or PRM]
    I --> J[Continuous Path in C-free]
    J --> K[Trajectory Parameterization with Time Law s of t]
    K --> L[Executable Motion Command Stream]
```

## 4.4 Trapezoidal vs Triangular Velocity Profile

The trapezoidal profile is the most common in industrial assembly robots. The diagram below contrasts it with the cubic profile derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Required Displacement delta theta] --> B{Maximum Velocity Achievable?}
    B -- Yes, with margin --> C[Trapezoidal Profile]
    B -- No, vmax too high --> D[Triangular Profile]
    C --> E[Phase 1: Constant Acceleration a_max]
    E --> F[Phase 2: Constant Velocity v_max]
    F --> G[Phase 3: Constant Deceleration minus a_max]
    D --> H[Phase 1: Constant Acceleration a_max]
    H --> I[Phase 2: No Cruise, Direct Deceleration]
    I --> J[Phase 3: Constant Deceleration minus a_max]
    G --> K[Total Time t_f = t_accel + t_cruise + t_decel]
    J --> L[Total Time t_f = 2 t_accel]
    K --> M[Optimal for Long Moves]
    L --> N[Optimal for Short Moves]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common examiner question is *"For what displacement does the trapezoidal profile degenerate to a triangular profile?"* The answer is: when the required displacement is small enough that the robot never reaches $v_{max}$ before it must begin decelerating. The critical displacement is $\Delta\theta_{crit} = v_{max}^2 / a_{max}$.
