# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between Path Planning and Trajectory Generation in a robotic assembly system. Mention one method used in each category.**

**Model Answer:**

**Path Planning** is a purely *geometric* problem that determines a collision-free curve in the configuration space $C_{free}$ from start to goal configuration, without regard to time. It answers the question *"where should the robot move?"*

**Trajectory Generation** is a *temporal* problem that assigns a time history to the geometric path, producing smooth position, velocity, and acceleration profiles. It answers the question *"how does the robot move along that path with respect to time?"*

| Aspect | Path Planning | Trajectory Generation |
|---|---|---|
| Concern | Geometry / Topology | Time / Dynamics |
| Output | Curve in $C_{free}$ | $\theta(t), \dot{\theta}(t), \ddot{\theta}(t)$ |
| Example Method | Roadmap, RRT, Cell Decomposition | Cubic Polynomial, Quintic Polynomial, Trapezoidal |

> **Example methods:** Path Planning — **Probabilistic Roadmap (PRM)**; Trajectory Generation — **Cubic Polynomial Interpolation**.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**Q: State four boundary conditions typically used in a quintic polynomial trajectory and explain why a quintic is preferred over a cubic in high-precision assembly applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A quintic polynomial $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ has **six** coefficients, requiring **six boundary conditions**:

1. Initial position: $\theta(0) = \theta_0$
2. Final position: $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$
3. Initial velocity: $\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}_0$
4. Final velocity: $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f$
5. Initial acceleration: $\ddot{\theta}(0) = \ddot{\theta}_0$
6. Final acceleration: $\ddot{\theta}(t_f) = \ddot{\theta}_f$

**Why Quintic over Cubic:**
- A cubic trajectory has **discontinuous acceleration** at the start and end of motion, producing a theoretical **infinite jerk** (rate of change of acceleration).
- In high-precision assembly (e.g., inserting a flexible PCB connector, or handling glass components), infinite jerk causes vibration, overshoot, and component damage.
- A quintic trajectory provides **continuous acceleration** at the boundaries, producing **bounded jerk** and therefore smoother, safer motion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A single joint of an industrial robot must move from $\theta_0 = 15°$ to $\theta_f = 75°$ in $t_f = 3$ seconds. Using a **cubic polynomial** with zero initial and final velocities, derive the trajectory equation $\theta(t)$ and find the maximum joint velocity and acceleration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A second joint of the same robot must move from $\theta_0 = 30°$ to $\theta_f = 90°$ in $t_f = 4$ seconds with zero initial and final velocities and accelerations. Using a **quintic polynomial**, derive the trajectory equation and verify the boundary conditions.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Cubic Trajectory

**Step 1: Convert angles to radians.**

$$
\theta_0 = 15° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.2618 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_f = 75° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.3090 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Compute displacement.**

$$
\Delta\theta = 1.3090 - 0.2618 = 1.0472 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3: Write the general cubic form.**

$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

**Step 4: Apply boundary conditions.**

- $\theta(0) = a_0 = 0.2618$
- $\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 = 0$
- $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0$
- $\theta(t_f) = a_0 + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = 1.3090$

**Step 5: Solve for coefficients.**

From the velocity condition at $t_f = 3$:

$$
2 a_2 (3) + 3 a_3 (9) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 6 a_2 + 27 a_3 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2 = -4.5 a_3
$$

From the position condition at $t_f = 3$:

$$
0.2618 + 9 a_2 + 27 a_3 = 1.3090
$$

$$
9 (-4.5 a_3) + 27 a_3 = 1.3090 - 0.2618 = 1.0472
$$

$$
-40.5 a_3 + 27 a_3 = 1.0472
$$

$$
-13.5 a_3 = 1.0472
$$

$$
a_3 = -0.07757
$$

$$
a_2 = -4.5 \times (-0.07757) = 0.3491
$$

**Step 6: Final Trajectory Equation.**

$$
\theta(t) = 0.2618 + 0.3491\, t^2 - 0.07757\, t^3 \quad \text{rad}
$$

**Step 7: Velocity profile.**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = 0.6982\, t - 0.2327\, t^2
$$

Maximum at $d\dot{\theta}/dt = 0$:

$$
0.6982 - 0.4654\, t = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; t^* = 1.5 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\dot{\theta}_{max} = 0.6982(1.5) - 0.2327(2.25) = 1.0473 - 0.5236 = 0.5237 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 8: Acceleration profile.**

$$
\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0.6982 - 0.4654\, t
$$

Maximum (at $t=0$):

$$
\ddot{\theta}_{max} = 0.6982 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

> **Final Answer (a):** $\theta(t) = 0.2618 + 0.3491 t^2 - 0.07757 t^3$ rad, with $\dot{\theta}_{max} = 0.524$ rad/s and $\ddot{\theta}_{max} = 0.698$ rad/s².

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Quintic Trajectory

**Step 1: Convert angles to radians.**

$$
\theta_0 = 30° = 0.5236 \text{ rad}, \quad \theta_f = 90° = 1.5708 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the boundary conditions** $\theta(0) = \theta_0$, $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$, $\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$, $\ddot{\theta}(0) = \ddot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$.

**Step 3: Use the simplified quintic formula** (since all velocities and accelerations are zero):

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{10 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} t^3 - \frac{15 \Delta\theta}{t_f^4} t^4 + \frac{6 \Delta\theta}{t_f^5} t^5
$$

**Step 4: Compute the displacement and substitute.**

$$
\Delta\theta = 1.5708 - 0.5236 = 1.0472 \text{ rad}, \quad t_f = 4 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= 0.5236 \\
a_3 &= \frac{10 \times 1.0472}{4^3} = \frac{10.472}{64} = 0.16363 \\
a_4 &= -\frac{15 \times 1.0472}{4^4} = -\frac{15.708}{256} = -0.06136 \\
a_5 &= \frac{6 \times 1.0472}{4^5} = \frac{6.2832}{1024} = 0.006137
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Final Quintic Trajectory Equation.**

$$
\theta(t) = 0.5236 + 0.16363\, t^3 - 0.06136\, t^4 + 0.006137\, t^5 \quad \text{rad}
$$

**Step 6: Verify boundary conditions.**

- At $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = 0.5236 = \theta_0$ ✓
- $\dot{\theta}(t) = 3(0.16363) t^2 - 4(0.06136) t^3 + 5(0.006137) t^4 = 0.4909 t^2 - 0.2454 t^3 + 0.03069 t^4$
- $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ ✓
- $\dot{\theta}(4) = 0.4909(16) - 0.2454(64) + 0.03069(256) = 7.854 - 15.708 + 7.857 \approx 0$ ✓
- $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0.9818 t - 0.7363 t^2 + 0.1227 t^3$
- $\ddot{\theta}(0) = 0$ ✓
- $\ddot{\theta}(4) = 0.9818(4) - 0.7363(16) + 0.1227(64) = 3.927 - 11.781 + 7.853 \approx 0$ ✓

All six boundary conditions are satisfied. **The trajectory is verified.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method for robot motion planning in an assembly cell. Define the attractive and repulsive potential functions and derive the net force. Discuss one major limitation of the method.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 2-DOF planar robot must execute a **trapezoidal velocity trajectory** for a joint displacement of $\Delta\theta = 1.5$ rad. The maximum allowable joint velocity is $v_{max} = 1.0$ rad/s and maximum acceleration is $a_{max} = 2.0$ rad/s². Determine (i) the duration of the acceleration phase, (ii) the duration of the cruise phase, and (iii) the total trajectory time.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — Artificial Potential Field

**Definition:** The Artificial Potential Field (APF) method, introduced by **Khatib (1986)**, treats the robot as a point moving in a potential field where the goal acts as an attractor and obstacles act as repellers.

**Attractive Potential** (quadratic basin around the goal):

$$
U_{att}(q) = \frac{1}{2} k_{att} \vert q - q_{goal} \vert^2
$$

The attractive force is the negative gradient:

$$
F_{att}(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal})
$$

**Repulsive Potential** (grows near obstacles):

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2} k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^2, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the distance from the robot to the nearest obstacle and $\rho_0$ is the influence distance.

The repulsive force is:

$$
F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U_{rep}(q) = k_{rep} \left( \frac{1}{\rho(q)} - \frac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \frac{1}{\rho^2(q)} \nabla \rho(q)
$$

**Net Force (Velocity Command):**

$$
F_{total}(q) = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q) = -k_{att}(q - q_{goal}) + F_{rep}(q)
$$

The robot's commanded joint velocity is:

$$
\dot{q} = F_{total}(q)
$$

**Major Limitation — Local Minima:** A well-known failure mode is when the attractive and repulsive forces exactly cancel at a point that is **not the goal**, leaving the robot trapped. This is a common KTU exam question and a classic problem in reactive motion planning.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — Trapezoidal Velocity Trajectory

**Given Data:**

$$
\Delta\theta = 1.5 \text{ rad}, \quad v_{max} = 1.0 \text{ rad/s}, \quad a_{max} = 2.0 \text{ rad/s}^2
$$

**Step (i): Duration of the Acceleration Phase.**

$$
t_{accel} = \frac{v_{max}}{a_{max}} = \frac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

**Step (ii): Duration of the Cruise Phase.**

During acceleration, the joint covers:

$$
\Delta\theta_{accel} = \frac{1}{2} v_{max} t_{accel} = \frac{1}{2} (1.0)(0.5) = 0.25 \text{ rad}
$$

By symmetry, the deceleration phase also covers $0.25$ rad. Therefore, the cruise phase covers:

$$
\Delta\theta_{cruise} = \Delta\theta - 2 \Delta\theta_{accel} = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
t_{cruise} = \frac{\Delta\theta_{cruise}}{v_{max}} = \frac{1.0}{1.0} = 1.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Step (iii): Total Trajectory Time.**

$$
t_{total} = 2 t_{accel} + t_{cruise} = 2(0.5) + 1.0 = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **symmetrically account for both the acceleration and deceleration phases** when computing the cruise displacement. Both the ramp-up and ramp-down each consume $\frac{1}{2} v_{max} t_{accel}$ of displacement.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Assuming the cruise phase exists when in reality the profile is **triangular**. Verify by checking $\Delta\theta > v_{max}^2 / a_{max} = 0.5$ rad. Here $1.5 > 0.5$, so the trapezoidal profile is valid. If the inequality were reversed, the answer would change drastically.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting velocity in wrong units — always use **rad/s** for revolute joints and **m/s** for prismatic joints.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 — Motion Planning and Trajectory Generation**

- [ ] **Path vs Trajectory:** Path is geometric (no time); Trajectory is path + time law.
- [ ] **C-space:** The set of all robot configurations $C$; $C_{free}$ is the collision-free subset.
- [ ] **Forward Kinematics:** $x = f(\theta)$; **Inverse Kinematics:** $\theta = f^{-1}(x)$.
- [ ] **Jacobian Relation:** $\dot{x} = J(\theta) \dot{\theta}$ — invert (with care) to get joint rates from Cartesian rates.
- [ ] **Singularity:** Occurs when $\det(J(\theta)) = 0$; Jacobian loses rank and becomes non-invertible.
- [ ] **Cubic Polynomial** uses 4 boundary conditions: position at both ends, zero velocity at both ends. Yields **discontinuous acceleration** at boundaries.
- [ ] **Quintic Polynomial** uses 6 boundary conditions: position, velocity, and acceleration at both ends. Yields **continuous acceleration** and **bounded jerk**.
- [ ] **Cubic Formula:** $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = \frac{3 \Delta\theta}{t_f^2}$, $a_3 = -\frac{2 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3}$.
- [ ] **Quintic Formula (zero velocity & acceleration case):** $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = a_2 = 0$, $a_3 = \frac{10 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3}$, $a_4 = -\frac{15 \Delta\theta}{t_f^4}$, $a_5 = \frac{6 \Delta\theta}{t_f^5}$.
- [ ] **Trapezoidal Profile:** Has constant acceleration, constant velocity (cruise), and constant deceleration phases. Fastest profile for long moves under acceleration constraints.
- [ ] **Triangular Profile:** Degenerate case of trapezoidal when cruise phase has zero duration. Used for short moves.
- [ ] **Critical Displacement for Trapezoidal-to-Triangular Transition:** $\Delta\theta_{crit} = v_{max}^2 / a_{max}$.
- [ ] **Artificial Potential Field (APF):** $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$, $F = -\nabla U$. Suffers from **local minima**.
- [ ] **Sampling-Based Methods:** PRM and RRT are probabilistically complete — they find a solution if one exists given enough samples.
- [ ] **Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Planning:** Joint-space is always kinematically feasible; Cartesian-space gives straight-line end-effector motion but may encounter singularities.
- [ ] **Industrial Standard:** ISO 10218 and ISO/TS 15066 govern jerk-limited trajectories for collaborative robots.
- [ ] **Cubic Trajectory Symmetry Property:** Maximum velocity occurs exactly at $t = t_f / 2$ when the boundary velocities are zero.
- [ ] **Units Reminder:** Angles in **rad**, angular velocity in **rad/s**, angular acceleration in **rad/s²**, linear in **m, m/s, m/s²**.
- [ ] **Key Industrial Use-Cases:** Pick-and-place (cubic), collaborative robot assembly (quintic), high-speed packaging (trapezoidal), warehouse navigation (APF / RRT).

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 2, Motion Planning and Trajectory Generation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Integration and Feedback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Integration and Feedback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensor Integration and Feedback** in the context of robotic assembly and material handling refers to the systematic fusion of multiple sensing modalities (proprioceptive and exteroceptive) with closed-loop control architectures to enable a robotic manipulator to perceive its internal state, interact intelligently with an unstructured environment, and adapt its motion in real time to achieve precise assembly or handling tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 2 – Sensation & Control):**
> *"Sensor integration is the cohesive hardware–software assimilation of force/torque, tactile, vision, and position sensors into the robot controller, while feedback is the continuous comparison of the measured sensor response with a reference command to generate a corrective control action using classical/modern control laws."*

The two principal sensor categories defined by the KTU 2024 OBE (Outcome-Based Education) framework are:

| Sensor Class | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Proprioceptive** | Sensors that measure the robot's *internal* state | Encoders, resolvers, joint torque sensors, inertial measurement units (IMU) |
| **Exteroceptive** | Sensors that measure the *external* environment | Vision cameras, LiDAR, force/torque (wrist) sensors, tactile arrays, proximity sensors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Chef"

Imagine a chef asked to assemble a delicate layered cake in a completely dark kitchen. The chef has:
- **Eyes (Vision sensors)** → to locate the cream, the cake base, and the icing nozzle.
- **Fingertips (Tactile/Force sensors)** → to feel whether the cream is being squeezed too hard or too soft.
- **Inner ear (IMU/Encoders)** → to know where their own hand is without looking.
- **A reflex arc (Feedback controller)** → the moment they feel the cake tilting, they instantly correct the angle.

Without any one of these, the cake collapses. **Sensor integration** is the chef's nervous system, and **feedback** is the reflex arc. The robot, like the chef, must **sense → compare → correct**, every millisecond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects students to map the **sense–plan–act** paradigm onto **sensor → controller → actuator** in a closed loop. Memorize this triad: it appears in nearly every ESE question on Module 2.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling rate (Hz):** Industrial force/torque sensors typically operate between **1 kHz and 4 kHz**.
- **Force resolution:** Wrist F/T sensors achieve **0.1 N to 0.5 N** resolution.
- **Vision frame rate:** Machine vision systems run at **30 fps to 120 fps**.
- **Encoder resolution:** Modern optical encoders provide **20-bit (1,048,576 counts/rev)** resolution.
- **Control loop time (Cycle time):** Standard industrial robot control loops execute at **1 ms to 4 ms (250 Hz to 1 kHz)**.

## 1.4 Learning Outcomes (Aligned with KTU 2024 COs)

Upon completion of this topic, the student must be able to:

- **CO2 (Module 2 Anchor):** *Identify and integrate appropriate sensors for robotic assembly and material handling systems, and design closed-loop feedback control architectures.*
- **CO3 (Cross-Module):** Evaluate sensor fusion techniques and their influence on assembly accuracy and cycle time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Closed-Loop Feedback Architecture

A robotic feedback system consists of four canonical elements arranged in a loop:

1. **Reference (Set-point) input** $r(t)$ – the desired position/force/trajectory.
2. **Controller** $C(s)$ – typically a PID compensator computing the error.
3. **Plant (Robot + Actuator)** $G(s)$ – the manipulator dynamics.
4. **Sensor (Feedback element)** $H(s)$ – encoder, F/T sensor, or camera.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Error signal** $e(t)$ is the heart of every feedback system:
> $$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$
> where $y(t)$ is the measured (sensed) output.

## 2.2 Sensor Signal Conditioning — Operational Stages

Raw sensor outputs are never directly usable by a robot controller. The signal must pass through four mandatory stages:

- **Stage 1 — Amplification:** Weak signals (e.g., strain gauge bridge output $\sim$ mV) are amplified using **instrumentation amplifiers (INA125, AD620)** with gain typically $\mathbf{G = 100}$ to $\mathbf{G = 1000}$.
- **Stage 2 — Filtering:** Low-pass filters (Butterworth, Bessel) remove high-frequency noise. Cut-off frequency $f_c$ is chosen at $\mathbf{2\times}$ to $\mathbf{5\times}$ the control bandwidth.
- **Stage 3 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Resolution given by:
$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$
where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale voltage and $n$ is the number of bits (typically **16-bit** in industrial robots).
- **Stage 4 — Calibration & Linearization:** The raw ADC counts $N$ are mapped to engineering units using the calibration equation:
$$S_{eng} = m \cdot N + b$$
where $m$ is the slope (sensitivity) and $b$ is the offset (bias).

## 2.3 Force/Torque Sensor Working Principle

A six-axis F/T sensor (e.g., ATI Mini45, JR3) uses **strain gauges** mounted on a deformable elastomer structure (the Maltese cross). When a force $F$ or torque $\tau$ is applied, the structure deforms by a strain $\epsilon$, which changes the gauge resistance by:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \epsilon$$

where $G_f$ is the **gauge factor** (typically $\mathbf{G_f \approx 2.0}$ for constantan).

The six components of wrench $\{F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z\}$ are obtained via a **calibration matrix** $\mathbf{C}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \\ \tau_x \\ \tau_y \\ \tau_z \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{C}_{6 \times 6} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \epsilon_1 \\ \epsilon_2 \\ \vdots \\ \epsilon_8 \end{bmatrix}$$

The calibration matrix $\mathbf{C}$ is determined experimentally and stored in the robot controller's non-volatile memory.

## 2.4 Vision Sensor Integration — The Pin-hole Model

For a pin-hole camera with focal length $f$, a 3D world point $(X, Y, Z)$ projects onto the 2D image plane at pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ as:

$$u = f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$$

$$v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

where:
- $f_x = f / p_x$ and $f_y = f / p_y$ are the focal lengths expressed in **pixels** (NOT mm).
- $c_x, c_y$ are the principal point coordinates in pixels.
- $p_x, p_y$ are the physical pixel dimensions in **mm/pixel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:**
> The intrinsic parameter matrix $\mathbf{K}$ of the camera is:
> $$\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
> Students must be able to derive $\mathbf{K}$ and explain how lens distortion is corrected before vision data is fed to the feedback loop.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error signal | Varies | Feedback loop core |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law | N, Nm, V | Joint position/velocity control |
| 3 | $G_{cl}(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless | Stability analysis |
| 4 | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | ADC resolution | Volts | Sensor signal conditioning |
| 5 | $S_{eng} = m \cdot N + b$ | Linear calibration | Engineering units | Sensor calibration |
| 6 | $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \epsilon$ | Strain gauge relation | dimensionless | Force sensor |
| 7 | $u = f_x \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$ | Pin-hole projection | pixels | Vision system |
| 8 | $f_{Nyquist} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling | Hz | Sensor sampling rate selection |
| 9 | $\mathbf{J} \dot{q} = \dot{x}$ | Velocity kinematics | varies | Sensor-based Cartesian feedback |
| 10 | $\tau_{fb} = \mathbf{J}^T F_{ext}$ | Force feedback torque | Nm | Hybrid force/position control |

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Automotive Assembly (Peg-in-Hole):** Compliance from force feedback eliminates jamming; typical insertion force is $\mathbf{5 N}$ to $\mathbf{20 N}$ with positional tolerance of $\mathbf{\pm 0.1 mm}$.
- **Pick-and-Place in Warehouses:** Vision-guided grasping reduces mis-pick rates from **5% (blind) to < 0.5% (vision-guided)**.
- **Collaborative Robots (Cobots):** Force/torque sensing at $\mathbf{1 kHz}$ is mandatory for ISO/TS 15066 safety compliance, with force limits of $\mathbf{150 N}$ (quasi-static contact) and $\mathbf{320 N}$ (transient contact).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function for a Robotic Joint

**Problem Setup:**
A single revolute joint of a robot is modeled as a second-order plant driven by a DC motor. The plant transfer function from armature voltage $V_a(s)$ to joint position $\theta(s)$ is given by:

$$G(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)}$$

where:
- $K_m$ = motor torque constant (Nm/A)
- $J$ = equivalent moment of inertia at the joint (kg·m²)
- $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (Nm·s/rad)
- $L_a$ = armature inductance (H)
- $R_a$ = armature resistance ($\Omega$)

A proportional controller $C(s) = K_p$ is used, and the joint position is sensed by an optical encoder with unity feedback $H(s) = 1$.

**Derive the closed-loop transfer function $\frac{\theta(s)}{\theta_d(s)}$ and the steady-state error $e_{ss}$ for a step input.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the standard closed-loop formula.**

The general closed-loop transfer function for a unity-feedback system is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $C(s) = K_p$ and $H(s) = 1$.**

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot G(s)}{1 + K_p \cdot G(s) \cdot 1}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p G(s)}{1 + K_p G(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $G(s)$.**

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p \cdot \dfrac{K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)}}{1 + K_p \cdot \dfrac{K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)}}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)$ to clear the inner fraction.**

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_p K_m}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the denominator to get the characteristic polynomial.**

The denominator is:

$$D(s) = s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_p K_m$$

Expanding $(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)$:

$$(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) = JL_a s^2 + JR_a s + BL_a s + BR_a$$

$$= JL_a s^2 + (JR_a + BL_a) s + BR_a$$

Multiplying by $s$:

$$s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) = JL_a s^3 + (JR_a + BL_a) s^2 + BR_a s$$

Adding $K_p K_m$:

$$D(s) = JL_a s^3 + (JR_a + BL_a) s^2 + BR_a s + K_p K_m$$

**Step 6 — Write the final closed-loop transfer function.**

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p K_m}{JL_a s^3 + (JR_a + BL_a) s^2 + BR_a s + K_p K_m}$$

This is a **third-order system** because the motor inductance $L_a$ adds a pole at the high-frequency end.

**Step 7 — Determine the steady-state error $e_{ss}$ using the Final Value Theorem.**

The error transfer function for unity feedback is:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

For a unit step input $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + K_p G(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p G(0)}$$

Evaluate $G(0)$ by substituting $s = 0$:

$$G(0) = \frac{K_m}{0 \cdot (0 + B)(0 + R_a)} = \frac{K_m}{0}$$

Since $G(s)$ contains a factor of $\frac{1}{s}$ (a free integrator from the motor's mechanical dynamics), $G(0) \to \infty$. Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A proportional controller on a motor-driven joint with a free integrator yields **zero steady-state error** for a step input — this is why industrial robot joint controllers are typically **Type-1 systems** (they contain an integrator).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PID Tuning for a Material Handling Gripper

**Problem Setup:**
A parallel-jaw gripper must maintain a constant gripping force $F_g$ on a fragile object. The force control loop is governed by:

$$G(s) = \frac{1}{(0.1s + 1)(0.05s + 1)}$$

Design a **PID controller** $C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ such that:
- Damping ratio $\zeta = 0.707$
- Natural frequency $\omega_n = 20 \text{ rad/s}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the open-loop transfer function.**

$$L(s) = C(s)G(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) \cdot \frac{1}{(0.1s + 1)(0.05s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Use the Ziegler–Nichols approximation for second-order plants.**

For a plant of the form $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$, the PID gains are:

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \tau_1}{K \tau_2}, \quad K_i = \frac{K_p}{2 \tau_2}, \quad K_d = 0.5 K_p \tau_2$$

Substituting $K = 1$, $\tau_1 = 0.1$, $\tau_2 = 0.05$:

$$K_p = \frac{1.2 \times 0.1}{1 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.12}{0.05} = 2.4$$

$$K_i = \frac{2.4}{2 \times 0.05} = \frac{2.4}{0.1} = 24$$

$$K_d = 0.5 \times 2.4 \times 0.05 = 0.06$$

**Step 3 — Verify the desired closed-loop response.**

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)}$$

For the desired response, the closed-loop denominator should be:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = s^2 + 2(0.707)(20) s + (20)^2 = s^2 + 28.28 s + 400$$

**Step 4 — Express the open-loop transfer function with PID gains.**

$$C(s)G(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s(0.1s + 1)(0.05s + 1)}$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation is:

$$1 + C(s)G(s) = 0$$

$$s(0.1s + 1)(0.05s + 1) + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect terms.**

First, expand $s(0.1s + 1)(0.05s + 1)$:

$$(0.1s + 1)(0.05s + 1) = 0.005 s^2 + 0.15 s + 1$$

Multiplying by $s$:

$$0.005 s^3 + 0.15 s^2 + s$$

Adding PID terms:

$$0.005 s^3 + (0.15 + K_d) s^2 + (1 + K_p) s + K_i = 0$$

**Step 6 — Apply pole-placement matching.**

Multiply through by 200 to normalize the leading coefficient:

$$s^3 + (30 + 200 K_d) s^2 + (200 + 200 K_p) s + 200 K_i = 0$$

For dominant second-order behavior, factor out the third pole at $s = -p$ (typically $p = 5 \omega_n = 100$):

$$(s + 100)(s^2 + 28.28 s + 400) = s^3 + 128.28 s^2 + 3228 s + 40000$$

Matching coefficients:

$$30 + 200 K_d = 128.28 \implies K_d = \frac{98.28}{200} = 0.4914$$

$$200 + 200 K_p = 3228 \implies K_p = \frac{3028}{200} = 15.14$$

$$200 K_i = 40000 \implies K_i = 200$$

**Step 7 — Final PID gains.**

$$\boxed{K_p \approx 15.14, \quad K_i = 200, \quad K_d \approx 0.49}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:**
> The high $K_d$ may excite unmodeled high-frequency dynamics (sensor noise, structural modes). In practice, a **filtered derivative** is used:
> $$K_d \cdot \frac{s}{\tau_f s + 1}$$
> with $\tau_f \approx 0.01$ s.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Assembly Tasks

| Assembly Task | Primary Sensor | Secondary Sensor | Control Mode | Sampling Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Peg-in-Hole** | Wrist F/T | Vision | Hybrid force/position | 1 kHz |
| **PCB Pick-and-Place** | Vision (2D) | Vacuum pressure | Position | 30 fps |
| **Bin Picking** | 3D Vision (RGB-D) | Tactile array | Adaptive position | 30 fps |
| **Screw Driving** | F/T + Current | Vision | Force/impedance | 2 kHz |
| **Welding (Arc)** | Arc voltage/current | Seam tracking vision | Velocity + Force | 5 kHz |
| **Palletizing** | Vision (barcode) | Encoder | Position | 100 Hz |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Feedback Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[refSet-point rr] --> B[SumjunctionEe]
    B --> C[ControllerCPID]
    C --> D[RobotPlantG]
    D --> E[ActuatorandJoint]
    E --> F[OutputPositionyy]
    F --> G[SensorHEncoderFTCamera]
    G --> B
    H[NoiseDisturbanceN] --> E
    I[ReferenceTrajectory] --> A
    classDef ctrl fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef sensor fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef plant fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#1A1A1A
    class C ctrl
    class G sensor
    class D,E plant
```

## 4.2 Sensor Integration Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL[SensorLayer]
        SA[ForceTorqueWrist]
        SB[Vision2D3DRGBD]
        SC[TactileArray]
        SD[EncoderResolver]
        SE[IMUAccelerometer]
        SF[ProximityInductive]
    end
    subgraph SCL[SignalConditioningLayer]
        CA[AmplifierINAAmplifier]
        CB[LowpassFilterButterworth]
        CC[ADConverter16bit]
        CD[CalibrationMatrix]
    end
    subgraph PPL[PerceptionandProcessingLayer]
        PA[SensorFusionKalmanFilter]
        PB[FeatureExtraction]
        PC[ObjectPoseEstimation]
    end
    subgraph CCTL[ControlLayer]
        QA[PIDController]
        QB[HybridForcePosition]
        QC[AdaptiveImpedance]
    end
    subgraph AL[ActuatorLayer]
        RA[ServoDrive]
        RB[BrushlessDC]
        RC[PneumaticHydraulic]
    end
    SA --> CA
    SB --> CA
    SC --> CA
    SD --> CC
    SE --> CC
    SF --> CC
    CA --> CB
    CB --> CC
    CC --> CD
    CD --> PA
    CD --> PB
    PA --> PC
    PB --> PC
    PC --> QA
    PC --> QB
    PC --> QC
    QA --> RA
    QB --> RB
    QC --> RC
    RA --> AS[AssembledObjectOutput]
    RB --> AS
    RC --> AS
    classDef sensor fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef proc fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef ctrl fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#1A1A1A
    class SA,SB,SC,SD,SE,SF sensor
    class PA,PB,PC,CCTL proc
    class QA,QB,QC,RA,RB,RC ctrl
```

## 4.3 Force/Position Hybrid Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TaskStart] --> B{ContactDetected}
    B -- No --> C[PositionControlMode]
    B -- Yes --> D{FContactGreaterFthreshold}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[ForceControlMode]
    C --> F[TrajectoryTracking]
    E --> G[ImpedanceAdjustment]
    F --> H{TaskComplete}
    G --> H
    H -- No --> B
    H -- Yes --> I[EndEffectorRetract]
    I --> J[TaskEnd]
    classDef decision fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#1A1A1A
    classDef process fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#1A1A1A
    class B,D decision
    class C,E,F,G,H,I process
```

## 4.4 Sensor Fusion via Kalman Filter — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PredictionStepXkk1] --> B[InnovationYkYpredicted]
    C[MeasurementUpdateZksensor] --> B
    B --> D[KalmanGainKk]
    A --> E[StateUpdateXk]
    D --> E
    E --> F[ErrorCovarianceUpdatePkk]
    F --> A
    classDef kf fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#1A1A1A
    class A,B,C,D,E,F kf
```
