<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give two examples of each used in robotic assembly.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sensors that measure the robot's *internal* state (joint angles, motor currents, body acceleration). | Sensors that measure the *external* environment (object position, force, vision). |
| **Examples** | (i) Optical encoder on each joint, (ii) Motor current sensor / resolver. | (i) Wrist-mounted 6-axis force/torque sensor, (ii) RGB-D vision camera. |
| **Location** | Embedded in the robot structure (joints, links). | Mounted on the end-effector or in the workspace. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
> - 1 mark for correct definition of proprioceptive + 1 example.
> - 1 mark for correct definition of exteroceptive + 1 example.
> - 1 mark for clear distinction (location / measurement target).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Explain the role of a PID controller in the feedback loop of a robotic joint. Write the PID control law.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** computes a control signal $u(t)$ that drives the joint actuator based on the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ between the desired and measured joint position. Its roles are:

- **Proportional ($K_p e(t)$):** Provides immediate corrective action proportional to current error.
- **Integral ($K_i \int e \, dt$):** Eliminates steady-state error by accumulating past errors.
- **Derivative ($K_d \frac{de}{dt}$):** Damps overshoot by reacting to the rate of change of error.

The control law is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:**
> - 1 mark for listing the three terms and their functions.
> - 1 mark for the PID equation.
> - 1 mark for stating that PID is the most common industrial feedback compensator for robotic joints.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Closed-Loop Position Control of a Robot Joint

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop position control of a single revolute joint of an industrial robot. Define the transfer function of each block and derive the overall closed-loop transfer function assuming a proportional controller and unity feedback.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A DC-motor-driven robot joint has the following parameters: $K_m = 0.5$ Nm/A, $J = 0.02$ kg·m², $B = 0.1$ Nm·s/rad, $L_a = 0.005$ H, $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$. Determine:
(i) The open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)}$.
(ii) The steady-state error for a unit step input with $K_p = 50$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram (textual representation, 2 marks):**

```
              θ_d(s)         e(s)        V_a(s)        θ(s)
   r(s)───►(Σ)───►[ K_p ]───►[ G(s) ]───►[ H(s)=1 ]───►( )───► y(s)
            ▲(−)                                                │
            └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The plant is the DC motor + joint mechanism. The transfer function from armature voltage $V_a(s)$ to joint position $\theta(s)$ is:

$$G(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a)}$$

With proportional controller $C(s) = K_p$ and unity feedback $H(s) = 1$, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$T(s) = \frac{\theta(s)}{\theta_d(s)} = \frac{K_p G(s)}{1 + K_p G(s)}$$

Substituting $G(s)$:

$$T(s) = \frac{K_p K_m}{s(Js + B)(L_a s + R_a) + K_p K_m}$$

This is a **Type-1 system** because of the free integrator in $G(s)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Part (i) — Open-loop transfer function (3 marks):**

Substituting the given values into $G(s)$:

$$G(s) = \frac{0.5}{s(0.02s + 0.1)(0.005s + 2)}$$

Simplify the constant term in the third factor: $(0.005s + 2) = 2(0.0025s + 1)$.

$$G(s) = \frac{0.5}{s(0.02s + 0.1) \cdot 2(0.0025s + 1)}$$

$$G(s) = \frac{0.25}{s(0.02s + 0.1)(0.0025s + 1)}$$

**Part (ii) — Steady-state error (4 marks):**

For unity feedback with proportional controller $C(s) = K_p$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + K_p G(s)}$$

For unit step, $R(s) = \frac{1}{s}$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p G(0)}$$

Evaluate $G(0)$:

$$G(0) = \frac{0.25}{0 \cdot (0.02 \cdot 0 + 0.1)(0.0025 \cdot 0 + 1)} = \frac{0.25}{0} \to \infty$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0}$$

The steady-state error is **zero** because the plant contains a free integrator (Type-1 system).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Sensor Integration in Peg-in-Hole Assembly

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the principle of a 6-axis force/torque sensor used in robotic assembly. Derive the relationship between applied wrench and strain gauge output. How is the calibration matrix $\mathbf{C}$ determined experimentally?

**(b)** [7 Marks] In a peg-in-hole assembly task, the peg diameter is 19.9 mm and the hole diameter is 20.0 mm (clearance = 0.1 mm). Discuss the role of:
(i) Force feedback in RCC (Remote Center Compliance).
(ii) Hybrid force/position control.
(iii) Visual servoing for initial alignment.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A 6-axis F/T sensor uses a **Maltese-cross** deformable structure with strain gauges bonded at strategic locations. When an external force $\mathbf{F} = [F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z]^T$ is applied, the cross deforms, causing measurable strain in each gauge.

**Derivation of Gauge Equation (2 marks):**
The fractional change in resistance of a strain gauge is:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \epsilon$$

where $G_f$ is the gauge factor (typically $\approx 2.0$) and $\epsilon$ is the mechanical strain. The gauges are arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge** to convert $\Delta R / R$ into a voltage $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$$

where $V_{ex}$ is the bridge excitation voltage.

**Calibration Matrix (2 marks):**
The relationship between the 6-axis wrench and the 8 (or more) strain gauge readings is:

$$\mathbf{F}_{6 \times 1} = \mathbf{C}_{6 \times 6} \cdot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_{6 \times 1}$$

The matrix $\mathbf{C}$ is determined experimentally by applying **known reference loads** (e.g., 50 N along $+X$, 50 N along $-X$, etc.) and recording the resulting strain vector. Using a least-squares fit over many calibration trials, $\mathbf{C}$ is computed as:

$$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{F} \cdot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}^T \cdot (\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \cdot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}^T)^{-1}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Force feedback in RCC (2 marks):**
The **Remote Center Compliance (RCC)** is a passive mechanical device placed between the robot wrist and the gripper. It provides lateral and angular compliance at a virtual center located at the tip of the peg. The force feedback from the wrist F/T sensor detects lateral contact forces; the controller then commands small lateral motions (proportional to force) to align the peg with the hole without jamming.

**(ii) Hybrid force/position control (3 marks):**
In **hybrid control** (Craig & Raibert, 1981 formulation), the task space is decomposed into force-controlled directions and position-controlled directions. For peg-in-hole:
- **Along the insertion axis (Z):** Force control with a desired insertion force (e.g., 10 N).
- **Perpendicular to insertion axis (X, Y):** Position control with high stiffness.
- **Rotational axes ($\theta_x, \theta_y$):** Position control to maintain peg orientation.

The selection matrix $\mathbf{S}$ enforces this:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{S} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{S} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_{pos} \\ v_{force} \end{bmatrix}$$

**(iii) Visual servoing for initial alignment (2 marks):**
A vision system (typically eye-in-hand camera) estimates the relative pose between the peg and the hole before contact. The image-based visual servoing (IBVS) controller computes a velocity command:

$$\mathbf{v}_c = -\lambda \mathbf{L}^{+} \mathbf{e}$$

where $\mathbf{L}^{+}$ is the pseudo-inverse of the image Jacobian and $\mathbf{e}$ is the image error. The robot aligns the peg to within $\pm 0.5$ mm of the hole center, reducing the burden on the force controller.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Sensor Integration Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the calibration equation $S_{eng} = m \cdot N + b$ — $m$ has units of (eng-unit / count), $b$ has units of eng-unit. Writing bare numbers loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $G_f$ (gauge factor, dimensionless) with Young's modulus $E$ (GPa)** in strain gauge derivations.
> 3. **Misidentifying camera intrinsics** — students frequently write $f_x$ in mm instead of pixels. $f_x = f / p_x$ where $p_x$ is mm/pixel.
> 4. **Forgetting the third pole** when deriving the closed-loop transfer function of a DC-motor joint — the inductance $L_a$ adds a third pole. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 5. **Not labeling the axes** in the hybrid force/position selection matrix — students lose 1 mark for not specifying which Cartesian directions are force-controlled vs position-controlled.
> 6. **Ignoring Nyquist criterion** — when specifying sensor sampling rate, students must state $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist) and ideally $f_s \geq 5 f_{max}$ for practical control.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sensor Integration and Feedback**

- ✅ **Two sensor classes:** Proprioceptive (internal: encoder, IMU) vs Exteroceptive (external: vision, F/T, tactile).
- ✅ **Four signal-conditioning stages:** Amplification → Filtering → ADC → Calibration/Linearization.
- ✅ **Strain gauge equation:** $\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \epsilon$ with $G_f \approx 2.0$.
- ✅ **6-axis F/T sensor:** Uses Maltese-cross structure with calibration matrix $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{C} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$.
- ✅ **Pin-hole camera model:** $u = f_x \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$, $v = f_y \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$; intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ is 3×3.
- ✅ **Closed-loop transfer function:** $T(s) = \frac{C(s)G(s)}{1 + C(s)G(s)H(s)}$.
- ✅ **Error signal:** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- ✅ **PID law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$.
- ✅ **Type-1 system** (joint with free integrator) → zero steady-state error for step input.
- ✅ **Nyquist rule:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, ideally $f_s \geq 5 f_{max}$.
- ✅ **Hybrid force/position control:** Selection matrix $\mathbf{S}$ decomposes task space into force- and position-controlled directions.
- ✅ **RCC (Remote Center Compliance):** Passive mechanical device providing lateral compliance at the peg tip; works synergistically with F/T feedback.
- ✅ **Industrial sampling rates:** Force/torque at 1 kHz; vision at 30–120 fps; encoder at 20-bit; control loop at 1–4 ms.
- ✅ **ISO/TS 15066 cobot force limits:** 150 N quasi-static, 320 N transient.
- ✅ **Peg-in-hole clearance:** Typical industrial clearances are 0.1–0.5 mm; force feedback is essential for jam-free insertion.
- ✅ **Sensor fusion:** Kalman filter is the most common estimator; combines multiple noisy sensors into an optimal state estimate $\hat{x}_k$.

> **End of Module 2 — Sensor Integration and Feedback Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Sensors Used in Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of Sensors Used in Robotics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of industrial robotics and automated material handling, a **sensor** is defined as a physical transduction device that converts a physical, chemical, or biological measurand (e.g., position, force, light intensity, distance, temperature) into an equivalent electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital word) that can be processed by a robot controller for feedback, decision-making, and closed-loop control. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OERUT724 – Module 2)** classification, robotic sensors are broadly partitioned into two functional hierarchies: 1. **Internal State Sensors (Proprioceptive Sensors):** Monitor the robot's *own* internal parameters — joint angle, angular velocity, motor current, end-effector force. 2. **External State Sensors (Exteroceptive Sensors):** Monitor the robot's *environment* — object position, distance to obstacles, surface texture, visual features, and proximity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OERUT724 Module 2):** Students must be able to **classify, describe the operating principle, and state typical applications** of position, velocity, force/torque, proximity, tactile, and vision sensors used in robotic assembly and material handling. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a robot as a human worker on a factory floor performing a pick-and-place task. To do this job well, the human relies on: - **Proprioception (Body Awareness):** Knowing where their arm is, how fast it is moving, and how hard they are gripping an object → maps directly to **internal sensors** like encoders and tachometers. - **Exteroception (External Awareness):** Seeing the object, feeling its texture, hearing the conveyor belt, sensing nearby obstacles → maps directly to **external sensors** like cameras, proximity switches, and tactile arrays. > [!NOTE] > A robot without sensors is essentially **blind and numb** — it cannot adapt to part variation, recover from errors, or operate safely alongside humans. Sensors are the *nervous system* of any intelligent robotic system. ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Engineering Terms) | Metric | Symbol | Definition | |---|---|---| | **Range** | $R$ | Min/Max measurable value | | **Resolution** | $\delta$ | Smallest detectable increment | | **Accuracy** | — | Closeness to *true* value | | **Precision / Repeatability** | $\sigma$ | Closeness of repeated measurements | | **Sensitivity** | $S$ | Output change per unit input change | | **Linearity** | — | Constant ratio of output to input | | **Bandwidth** | $f_b$ | Highest frequency the sensor can track | | **Hysteresis** | $H$ | Output difference for ascending vs descending input | These standard ISO/IS terms (e.g., **ISA-37.1**, **ISO 5725**) are used as the formal evaluation vocabulary in KTU university exam answers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Robotic Sensors The KTU-recommended taxonomy (per Module 2 of OERUT724) is as follows: **A. Internal / Proprioceptive Sensors** - Position sensors — Potentiometer, LVDT, Optical Encoder (incremental & absolute), Resolver - Velocity sensors — DC Tachogenerator, Incremental Encoder (differentiation) - Acceleration sensors — Servo accelerometer, Piezoelectric accelerometer - Force/Torque sensors — Strain-gauge based joint/ wrist F/T sensors - Current/Voltage sensors — Hall-effect current transducers **B. External / Exteroceptive Sensors** - Contact sensors — Limit switches, Tactile sensor arrays - Proximity sensors — Inductive, Capacitive, Magnetic (Hall), Optical, Ultrasonic - Range finders — Laser triangulation, Time-of-Flight (ToF) LiDAR - Vision sensors — CCD/CMOS cameras, line-scan cameras - Specialized — Temperature (RTD/thermocouple), humidity, gas (for hazardous zones) ### 2.2 Operational Principles — Structured Logic **Step 1 — Potentiometer (Position):** A resistive element with a sliding wiper. The output voltage is a linear divider of the input excitation. The wiper position is proportional to the joint angle. **Step 2 — Optical Encoder (Position/Velocity):** A disc with alternating transparent/opaque slots rotates between a light source and a photodetector. Pulses generated per revolution yield angular position (count) and speed (pulse frequency). **Step 3 — LVDT (Linear Position):** A transformer with one primary and two secondaries wound on a movable ferromagnetic core. Core displacement induces differential voltage proportional to displacement. **Step 4 — Strain-Gauge Force Sensor:** A Wheatstone bridge of four foil gauges bonded to an elastic element deforms under load, changing resistance. The bridge imbalance is proportional to applied force. **Step 5 — Inductive Proximity Sensor:** An LC oscillator generates an alternating electromagnetic field. A metallic target entering the field induces eddy currents, damping the oscillation → triggers the output. **Step 6 — Capacitive Proximity Sensor:** Forms one plate of a capacitor with the target as the other. Change in distance or material dielectric constant alters capacitance → detected by an oscillator circuit. **Step 7 — Tactile Sensor Array:** A matrix of conductive/elastomeric sensing elements (piezoresistive, capacitive, or piezoelectric) whose outputs map to localized pressure distribution. **Step 8 — Vision Sensor (Camera):** A 2-D array of photosensitive pixels (CCD/CMOS) accumulates charge proportional to incident light intensity. Frame data is processed for feature extraction, edge detection, and object recognition. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Sensor / Concept | Governing Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Potentiometer output | $V_o = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_w}{R_{tot}}$ | $V_s$ = supply, $R_w$ = wiper resistance | | 2 | Optical Encoder resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ | $N$ = pulses per revolution (PPR) | | 3 | Encoder velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f_p}{N}$ | $f_p$ = pulse frequency (Hz) | | 4 | LVDT output | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $x$ = core displacement | | 5 | Strain gauge (bridge) | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor (~2.0), $\varepsilon$ = strain | | 6 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $d$ = piezoelectric coefficient | | 7 | Tachogenerator EMF | $E = K_b \cdot \omega$ | $K_b$ = back-emf constant (V·s/rad) | | 8 | Ultrasonic range | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c \approx 343$ m/s in air at **20 °C** | | 9 | Laser triangulation | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x_{img}}$ | $f$ = lens focal length, $b$ = baseline | | 10 | Inductive prox. range | Effective $S_n = S_n \cdot m$ | $m$ = correction factor (steel = 1.0) | | 11 | Sensor sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output per unit input | | 12 | Resolution | $\delta = \dfrac{FS}{2^n}$ | $n$ = ADC bits | > [!NOTE] > In KTU answer scripts, always quote the **units** and the **typical operating range** (e.g., "inductive proximity sensors detect ferrous targets at **2–10 mm**") to earn full methodology marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive assembly:** Vision-guided seam welding, force-controlled part mating. - **Palletizing & material handling:** Photoelectric proximity sensors for box detection, encoders for crane/AGV position feedback. - **Collaborative robots (cobots):** Force/torque sensors at the wrist for safe human–robot interaction. - **Pick-and-place in electronics:** Vision sensors for SMD component orientation; vacuum-pressure sensors for gripper feedback. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optical Encoder Angular Resolution The optical incremental encoder disc carries $N$ evenly spaced slots over one full rotation of $360^\circ$. Each slot transition corresponds to one increment of detectable angular position. **Step 1:** Total angular sweep = $360^\circ$ (one revolution). **Step 2:** Number of discrete pulses per revolution = $N$. **Step 3:** Therefore, the smallest angular increment (resolution) is: $$ \theta_{res} = \frac{360^\circ}{N} $$ **Step 4:** Convert to radians (KTU standard unit in control equations): $$ \theta_{res,rad} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ rad} $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical:** For a quadrature encoder with $N = 2500$ PPR, the effective count per revolution is multiplied by **4** (two channels × two edges): $$ N_{eff} = 4 \times 2500 = 10{,}000 \text{ counts/rev} $$ $$ \theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{10{,}000} = 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} \approx 0.036^\circ $$ **Engineering meaning:** Higher $N$ → finer resolution → better joint-position accuracy. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Encoder-Based Angular Velocity **Step 1:** Pulses counted in a fixed sampling time $T_s$: $$ \text{Counts} = C $$ **Step 2:** Angular displacement covered: $$ \Delta\theta = C \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N} $$ **Step 3:** Average angular velocity: $$ \omega = \frac{\Delta\theta}{T_s} = \frac{2\pi \cdot C}{N \cdot T_s} $$ **Step 4 — Alternative using pulse frequency** $f_p = C / T_s$: $$ \omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot f_p}{N} $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical:** A 500-PPR encoder produces $f_p = 2000$ Hz on a motor shaft. $$ \omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2000}{500} = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \text{ rad/s} $$ Convert to RPM: $$ n = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 2000 \cdot \frac{60}{500} = 240 \text{ RPM} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — LVDT Differential Output vs Displacement An LVDT has a primary winding and two identical secondaries connected in series opposition. Let the core be displaced by $x$ from the null (central) position. **Step 1 — Induced EMFs (assume linear coupling near null):** $$ E_{s1} = K_0 + K_1 \cdot x $$ $$ E_{s2} = K_0 - K_1 \cdot x $$ **Step 2 — Series-opposition differential output:** $$ V_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 2 \cdot K_1 \cdot x $$ **Step 3 — Define LVDT constant $K_{LVDT} = 2K_1$:** $$ V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x $$ **Step 4 — Phase polarity** indicates direction of displacement (one phase for $+x$, the opposite phase for $-x$). **Step 5 — Sensitivity:** $$ S_{LVDT} = \frac{dV_{out}}{dx} = K_{LVDT} \;\; [\text{V/mm}] $$ ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Strain-Gauge Force Sensor (Wheatstone Bridge) A quarter-bridge arrangement uses one active strain gauge with three fixed resistors $R$. **Step 1 — Bridge output (no load):** $V_o = 0$ (balanced). **Step 2 — Under load:** Active gauge resistance changes: $$ R_1 = R + \Delta R, \quad \Delta R = R \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon $$ **Step 3 — Bridge imbalance voltage** (small $\Delta R$ approximation): $$ V_o \approx V_{exc} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} = V_{exc} \cdot \frac{G \cdot \varepsilon}{4} $$ **Step 4 — Strain related to stress & force:** $$ \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A \cdot E} $$ **Step 5 — Combine:** $$ V_o = \frac{V_{exc} \cdot G \cdot F}{4 \cdot A \cdot E} $$ **Step 6 — Force sensitivity:** $$ S_F = \frac{dV_o}{dF} = \frac{V_{exc} \cdot G}{4 \cdot A \cdot E} \;\; [\text{V/N}] $$ **Step 7 — Worked Numerical:** Take $V_{exc} = 5$ V, $G = 2.0$, $A = 10^{-4}$ m², $E = 200$ GPa (steel). $$ S_F = \frac{5 \times 2.0}{4 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^{9}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V/N} $$ For a 1 kN force: $$ V_o = 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 125\;\mu\text{V} $$ This tiny signal is why **instrumentation amplifiers** (gain ≥ 1000) are essential. ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight Range **Step 1:** Sensor emits a burst; reflects from target; echo is received after time $t$. **Step 2:** The wave travels the round-trip distance $2d$ at the speed of sound $c$. $$ 2d = c \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Speed of sound in air (KTU standard reference at 20 °C):** $$ c \approx 343 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 4 — Temperature correction:** $$ c = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \;\; [\text{m/s}], \quad T \text{ in } ^\circ\text{C} $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical:** At $T = 30$ °C, $c = 331.4 + 18 = 349.4$ m/s. If $t = 12$ ms: $$ d = \frac{349.4 \times 0.012}{2} = 2.0964 \text{ m} $$ ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Selection | Selection Criterion | Best Sensor Choice | Why | |---|---|---| | Absolute joint angle at power-up | **Absolute encoder / resolver** | Retains position without homing | | High-accuracy linear displacement | **LVDT** | Frictionless, infinite resolution | | Short-range (≤10 mm) metallic target | **Inductive proximity** | Rugged, non-contact | | Transparent object detection | **Capacitive / Laser retro-reflective** | Light-based methods fail on clear objects | | Long-range 2-D mapping | **LiDAR / Scanning laser** | ToF gives precise range | | Surface texture / slip detection | **Tactile array** | Spatially distributed pressure data | | Object recognition & orientation | **Vision (CCD/CMOS)** | Provides rich 2-D feature data | | High-bandwidth force feedback | **Piezoelectric F/T sensor** | Excellent dynamic response | | Hazardous / explosive atmosphere | **Intrinsically-safe Hall sensor** | No sparking risk | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Classification of Robotic Sensors ```mermaid graph TD A[ROBOT SENSORS] --> B[PROPRIOCEPTIVE
Internal State] A --> C[EXTEROCEPTIVE
External State] B --> B1[Position] B --> B2[Velocity] B --> B3[Acceleration] B --> B4[Force and Torque] C --> C1[Contact and Tactile] C --> C2[Proximity] C --> C3[Range and Distance] C --> C4[Vision] C --> C5[Others: Temp, Humidity, Gas] B1 --> B1a[Potentiometer] B1 --> B1b[Optical Encoder] B1 --> B1c[Resolver] B1 --> B1d[LVDT] B2 --> B2a[DC Tachogenerator] B2 --> B2b[Encoder Derivative] B3 --> B3a[Piezoelectric] B3 --> B3b[Servo Accelerometer] B4 --> B4a[Strain Gauge] B4 --> B4b[Piezoelectric] C2 --> C2a[Inductive] C2 --> C2b[Capacitive] C2 --> C2c[Hall Effect] C2 --> C2d[Optical] C2 --> C2e[Ultrasonic] C3 --> C3a[Laser Triangulation] C3 --> C3b[Time of Flight LiDAR] C4 --> C4a[CCD Camera] C4 --> C4b[CMOS Camera] C4 --> C4c[Line Scan Camera] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sensor Signal Chain ```mermaid graph LR S1[Physical Measurand] --> S2[Transducer Element] S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioning
Amplification and Filtering] S3 --> S4[Analog to Digital Converter] S4 --> S5[Microprocessor or DSP] S5 --> S6[Controller / PLC] S6 --> S7[Actuator Command] S7 --> S8[Robot Motion / Gripper] S8 --> S1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Vision-Guided Pick-and-Place (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD V1[Scene Illumination] --> V2[Camera CCD or CMOS] V2 --> V3[Frame Grabber] V3 --> V4[Image Processing Unit
Edge Detection Blob Analysis] V4 --> V5[Object Pose Estimation] V5 --> V6[Robot Controller] V6 --> V7[Servo Drive] V7 --> V8[Robot Arm Trajectory] V8 --> V9[End Effector Picks Object] V9 --> V10[Tactile and Force Sensors Confirm Grip] V10 --> V6 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Feedback Loop in a Force-Compliant Robotic Assembly ```mermaid graph LR F1[Desired Force Fref] --> F2[Force Controller] F2 --> F3[Servo Amplifier] F3 --> F4[Robot Joint Motor] F4 --> F5[End Effector in Contact with Part] F5 --> F6[Wrist Force and Torque Sensor] F6 --> F7[Signal Conditioner] F7 --> F8[Analog to Digital Converter] F8 --> F9[Actual Force Fmeas] F9 --> F2 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **Q1.** *Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors used in robotics. Give two examples of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive | |---|---|---| | Monitors | Robot's *internal* state | Robot's *external* environment | | Purpose | Closed-loop joint control | Task-level perception | | Examples | Optical encoder, DC tachogenerator, LVDT | Camera, ultrasonic sensor, proximity switch | *(1 mark: clear definition of each; 1 mark: table of differences; 1 mark: two examples each.)* --- **Q2.** *Explain the working principle of an LVDT as a linear position sensor.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The LVDT consists of one primary and two secondary windings on a cylindrical former with a movable ferromagnetic core. *(1 mark)* - The primary is excited by an AC source; the two secondaries are connected in series opposition. *(1 mark)* - Core displacement produces a differential output voltage whose **magnitude is proportional to displacement** and whose **phase indicates direction**. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU End Semester Examination – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]** --- #### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an optical incremental encoder for joint position measurement. Derive the expression for its angular resolution. An encoder has 1024 pulses per revolution (PPR) and is used in quadrature mode. Determine (i) effective counts per revolution, (ii) angular resolution in degrees and radians.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain the working of an ultrasonic proximity sensor using the time-of-flight principle. A sensor is used in a warehouse at an ambient temperature of 35 °C. If the measured echo time is 8 ms, calculate the distance to the nearest object. State two industrial applications.* --- **Model Solution to Q.A (a):** **Working Principle (4 marks):** - A glass/polymer disc with $N$ radial slots rotates between an LED source and a photodetector. *(1 mark)* - Two photodetectors offset by 90° electrical (channels A and B) give **quadrature** outputs to detect direction. *(1 mark)* - An index (Z) pulse provides one reference pulse per revolution for homing. *(1 mark)* - Pulse counter / decoder in the controller converts pulses to angular position. *(1 mark)* **Derivation of Resolution (2 marks):** - One revolution = $2\pi$ rad. With $N$ pulses, each pulse represents: $$ \theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{N} \text{ rad} = \frac{360^\circ}{N} $$ - In quadrature mode, both rising and falling edges of A and B are counted, giving $N_{eff} = 4N$. **Numerical (1 mark):** - $N = 1024$, $N_{eff} = 4096$ $$ \theta_{res} = \frac{360}{4096} = 0.0879^\circ $$ $$ \theta_{res,rad} = \frac{2\pi}{4096} = 1.534 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} $$ --- **Model Solution to Q.A (b):** **Working Principle (3 marks):** - A piezoelectric transducer emits a short burst of ultrasound (~40 kHz). *(1 mark)* - The burst reflects from a target; the same or a separate transducer receives the echo. *(1 mark)* - The time $t$ between emission and reception is measured; the distance is computed using $d = c t / 2$. *(1 mark)* **Numerical (3 marks):** - $T = 35$ °C, so $c = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 35 = 352.4$ m/s. *(1 mark)* - $t = 8$ ms $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ s. *(1 mark)* $$ d = \frac{352.4 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.4096 \text{ m} $$ *(1 mark)* **Applications (1 mark):** - (i) AGV obstacle detection; (ii) bin-level measurement in storage silos. --- #### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe the construction and working of a strain-gauge based force sensor used at a robot wrist. Derive the bridge output voltage and show how the output is related to the applied force.* **(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain the working of an inductive proximity sensor and a capacitive proximity sensor. Compare their suitability for detecting (i) a steel part, (ii) a plastic bottle filled with water.* --- **Model Solution to Q.B (a):** **Construction (2 marks):** - A cross-shaped elastic element (flexure) has strain gauges bonded on four faces. - Four gauges (or two active + two dummy) form a **Wheatstone bridge** excited by a stable DC/AC voltage. **Working (2 marks):** - Force $F$ on the wrist deforms the flexure; gauges on tension arms increase in $R$, those on compression arms decrease. - The resulting bridge imbalance produces a differential voltage proportional to $F$. **Derivation (3 marks):** For a quarter-bridge with single active gauge: $$ \frac{V_o}{V_{exc}} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot G \cdot \varepsilon $$ Since $\varepsilon = F / (A \cdot E)$: $$ V_o = \frac{V_{exc} \cdot G \cdot F}{4 \cdot A \cdot E} $$ Force sensitivity: $$ S_F = \frac{V_o}{F} = \frac{V_{exc} \cdot G}{4 \cdot A \cdot E} $$ --- **Model Solution to Q.B (b):** **Inductive Proximity Sensor (2 marks):** - An LC oscillator generates an alternating magnetic field at the active face. - A metallic target entering the field induces **eddy currents**, damping the oscillation; the detector circuit then switches the output. - Detects only **ferrous and non-ferrous metals**; range typically 2–10 mm. **Capacitive Proximity Sensor (1 mark):** - The active face and the target form the two plates of a capacitor. - Change in distance or dielectric constant changes capacitance; an oscillator detects this change. - Detects **metals, plastics, liquids, wood, glass** — virtually any material. **Comparison (4 marks):** | Target | Best Choice | Justification | |---|---|---| | (i) Steel part | **Inductive** | Eddy-current damping is strong; longer range; lower cost. | | (ii) Plastic bottle with water | **Capacitive** | Water has high dielectric constant (≈ 80); capacitive sensor is unaffected by color/transparency. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Read Before You Write!** > 1. **Unit Errors:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing $d = c \cdot t$ instead of $d = c \cdot t / 2$ in ultrasonic problems — *echo travel is a round trip.* > 2. **Quadrature Confusion:** Always state $N_{eff} = 4N$ explicitly for quadrature encoders; do not forget the index pulse (Z-channel) in the diagram. > 3. **Mix-up between LVDT and RVDT:** LVDT is **linear**; RVDT (Rotary Variable Differential Transformer) measures **angular** displacement. Examiners specifically check this distinction. > 4. **Strain-gauge derivation:** Do not skip the small-signal approximation $\Delta R \ll R$ when deriving $V_o$ — the linear formula is only valid under this condition. > 5. **Proximity sensor selection:** When asked to "compare," present a clear **tabular contrast** — bullet points alone will not earn the comparison marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ Sensors are classified as **proprioceptive (internal)** and **exteroceptive (external)** — this is a guaranteed short-answer or part-(a) topic. - ✅ **Position sensors:** Potentiometer, LVDT, Optical Encoder, Resolver — each with unique accuracy, range, and ruggedness profile. - ✅ **Velocity measurement:** Either direct via tachogenerator, or computed by differentiating encoder pulses ($d\theta/dt$). - ✅ **Force/Torque sensors:** Always built on **Wheatstone-bridge strain gauges** bonded to an elastic element; small output needs high-gain amplification. - ✅ **Proximity sensors** are the most common industrial sensors; selection depends on **target material, sensing range, and environment**. - ✅ **Ultrasonic range formula** $d = c t / 2$ — speed of sound depends on temperature: $c = 331.4 + 0.6T$ m/s. - ✅ **Laser triangulation** uses $d = f \cdot b / x_{img}$ — focal length × baseline divided by image shift. - ✅ **Vision sensors** (CCD/CMOS) provide rich 2-D data; used for object recognition, pose estimation, and quality inspection. - ✅ **Tactile arrays** give distributed pressure — used in grasping delicate parts and slip detection. - ✅ Standard performance metrics to mention in every answer: **range, resolution, accuracy, sensitivity, bandwidth, hysteresis**. - ✅ Examiner keywords to include: "transduction principle," "contact vs non-contact," "active vs passive," "absolute vs incremental," "analog vs digital output."

topics.title: Sensor Fusion and Data Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor Fusion and Data Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sensor Fusion** is the systematic process of integrating data from multiple heterogeneous or homogeneous sensors to produce a unified, accurate, robust, and reliable representation of the robot's internal state and external environment that cannot be achieved by any single sensor operating independently. In the context of KTU 2024 OERUT724, sensor fusion is formally defined as the **computational framework** that combines probabilistic, statistical, or deterministic estimates from disparate sensory modalities (vision, force/torque, tactile, proximity, encoders, IMUs, etc.) using defined mathematical architectures to minimize uncertainty, resolve ambiguity, and enhance decision-making in robotic assembly and material handling tasks.

**Data Processing** in this domain refers to the sequential and parallel computational operations applied to raw sensory signals — including **signal conditioning**, **noise suppression**, **feature extraction**, **state estimation**, and **semantic interpretation** — to transform low-level sensor outputs into actionable high-level information usable by the robot controller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must distinguish between *low-level fusion* (raw data combination), *mid-level fusion* (feature fusion), and *high-level fusion* (decision fusion). This taxonomy is a **recurrent 2-mark and 7-mark exam question**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to guess the weight of a parcel inside a sealed box. You could:

1. **Shake the box** (gives you a rough idea, but very noisy — analogous to a *vibratory tactile sensor*).
2. **Look at the box size** and read the shipping label (gives structural hints — analogous to a *vision sensor*).
3. **Lift the box briefly** (gives a rough force estimate — analogous to a *force/torque sensor*).

Individually, each method is unreliable. But if you **mentally average** all three impressions, your estimate becomes remarkably accurate. This mental integration is exactly what a robot's CPU does with sensor data — and the algorithm that performs it is the **sensor fusion algorithm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition for First-Time Learners:**
> - **Sensor Fusion** = "Combining clues from many senses to know the truth."
> - **Data Processing** = "Cleaning and interpreting the clues before combining them."
> In humans, this is what your brain does when you both *see* an approaching car and *hear* its horn — your brain fuses both inputs into a single coherent understanding of danger.

---

## 1.3 Standardized Metrics & Constants

The following physical and statistical constants are referenced throughout this module:

- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)**: A measure of sensor measurement dispersion, expressed in sensor-native units.
- **Covariance ($P$)**: Joint variability between two state variables, with units of the product of those variables.
- **Process Noise Covariance ($Q$)**: Uncertainty in the system model evolution.
- **Measurement Noise Covariance ($R$)**: Uncertainty in the sensor measurement.
- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$)**: Typically **100 Hz – 1 kHz** for industrial robotic assembly loops.
- **Jensen's Inequality Constant**: $E[g(X)] \geq g(E[X])$ for convex $g$.

---

## 1.4 Why Sensor Fusion is Indispensable in Robotic Assembly

| Single-Sensor Limitation | How Fusion Solves It |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Camera occluded in peg-in-hole task | Force sensor compensates with contact feedback |
| Force sensor lacks spatial context | Vision provides pose/orientation alignment |
| IMU drifts over long durations | Vision/encoder corrects drift periodically |
| Tactile sensor has narrow spatial range | Force sensor + vision extends coverage |
| Encoder misses external perturbations | Force sensor detects external contact forces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No single sensor is sufficient for compliant assembly under uncertainty.** This statement is the philosophical backbone of Module 2 and a guaranteed short-answer question in KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomic Decomposition of Sensor Fusion

### 2.1.1 By Data Abstraction Level (KTU-Standard Taxonomy)

1. **Data-Level (Low-Level) Fusion**
   - Operates directly on raw sensor outputs.
   - Requires homogeneous sensors (same modality).
   - Example: Averaging three redundant encoders on a single joint.
   - Pros: Maximum information retention.
   - Cons: Computationally heavy, requires synchronized sampling.

2. **Feature-Level (Mid-Level) Fusion**
   - Extracts features (edges, corners, force gradients) from each sensor first.
   - Then combines the feature vectors.
   - Example: Combining SIFT features from two cameras.
   - Pros: Reduced bandwidth, robust to noise.
   - Cons: Loss of raw information.

3. **Decision-Level (High-Level) Fusion**
   - Each sensor makes an independent decision (e.g., object detected: yes/no).
   - Decisions are combined via voting or Bayesian rules.
   - Example: Multi-camera surveillance with voting.
   - Pros: Modular, fault-tolerant.
   - Cons: Throws away inter-sensor correlations.

### 2.1.2 By Sensor Relationship (Luo & Kay Classification)

- **Complementary Fusion**: Sensors cover non-overlapping regions of the state space.
  - *Example*: IMU measures orientation; GPS measures position.
- **Competitive (Redundant) Fusion**: Multiple sensors measure the *same* quantity.
  - *Example*: Three encoders on one shaft.
- **Cooperative Fusion**: Sensors combine to derive a quantity none could measure alone.
  - *Example*: Stereo cameras deriving depth from two 2-D views.

### 2.1.3 By Architectural Topology

- **Centralized Architecture**: All raw data sent to a central processor.
- **Decentralized Architecture**: Each sensor node processes locally; only estimates are shared.
- **Distributed Architecture**: Hybrid — local processing with peer-to-peer communication.

---

## 2.2 The Kalman Filter — The Workhorse of Robotic Sensor Fusion

### 2.2.1 Operational Concept

The Kalman Filter is an **optimal recursive estimator** for linear Gaussian systems. It maintains a *prediction* of the system state and corrects it using *measurements*, minimizing the mean-squared error at each step.

### 2.2.2 Two-Phase Operation

**Phase 1 — Prediction (Time Update)**:
The filter projects the current state estimate and covariance forward in time using the system model.

**Phase 2 — Correction (Measurement Update)**:
The filter incorporates a new measurement to refine the prediction, weighted by the **Kalman Gain** $K_k$.

### 2.2.3 Mathematical Statement (Discrete-Time)

**System Model:**
$$x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k, \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$$

**Measurement Model:**
$$z_k = H x_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

**Predict Step:**
$$\hat{x}_k^- = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$$
$$P_k^- = A P_{k-1} A^T + Q$$

**Update Step:**
$$K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k (z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-)$$
$$P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** The Kalman Gain $K_k$ balances trust between the model (high $Q$ → low trust) and measurement (high $R$ → low trust). When $R \to 0$, $K_k \to H^{-1}$ (full trust in measurement). When $Q \to 0$, $K_k \to 0$ (full trust in model).

---

## 2.3 The Complementary Filter (Lightweight Alternative)

Used when computational simplicity matters more than optimality (e.g., low-cost drones, mobile robots).

$$\hat{x}_k = \alpha \cdot \hat{x}_{\text{predicted}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \hat{x}_{\text{measured}}$$

where $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the blending coefficient. Typically $\alpha \in [0.95, 0.98]$ for IMU-gyroscope fusion.

In the **s-domain**, the complementary filter is a pair of complementary high-pass and low-pass filters:
$$H_{\text{low-pass}}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + \tau s}, \quad H_{\text{high-pass}}(s) = \frac{\tau s}{1 + \tau s}$$

with $H_{\text{low}} + H_{\text{high}} = 1$.

---

## 2.4 The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for Nonlinear Systems

Robotic systems are inherently nonlinear. The EKF linearizes the system around the current estimate using Jacobian matrices:

$$A_k = \left. \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \right|_{\hat{x}_{k-1}}, \quad H_k = \left. \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \right|_{\hat{x}_k^-}$$

The predict/update equations remain identical in form, but $A \to A_k$ and $H \to H_k$ at each step.

---

## 2.5 Particle Filter (For Highly Nonlinear / Non-Gaussian Systems)

The particle filter represents the posterior distribution as a set of $N$ weighted samples (particles):

$$p(x_k \mid z_{1:k}) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_k^{(i)} \, \delta(x_k - x_k^{(i)})$$

- **Resampling**: Discard low-weight particles; replicate high-weight ones.
- **Computational Cost**: $\mathcal{O}(N)$ — typically $N = 100$ to $10000$.

Used in SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) and complex assembly pose estimation.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Boundary / Units |
|---|--------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $x_k = A x_{k-1} + B u_k + w_k$ | Linear system state transition | State $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 2 | $z_k = H x_k + v_k$ | Measurement model | Measurement $z \in \mathbb{R}^m$ |
| 3 | $\hat{x}_k^- = A \hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$ | Kalman prediction (state) | — |
| 4 | $P_k^- = A P_{k-1} A^T + Q$ | Covariance prediction | Covariance symmetric PSD |
| 5 | $K_k = P_k^- H^T (H P_k^- H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | $K \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ |
| 6 | $\hat{x}_k = \hat{x}_k^- + K_k(z_k - H \hat{x}_k^-)$ | Measurement update | Innovation: $(z_k - H\hat{x}_k^-)$ |
| 7 | $P_k = (I - K_k H) P_k^-$ | Covariance update | — |
| 8 | $\hat{x}_k = \alpha \hat{x}_p + (1-\alpha)\hat{x}_m$ | Complementary filter | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |
| 9 | $\hat{x}_{\text{fused}} = \frac{\sum_i w_i \hat{x}_i}{\sum_i w_i}$ | Weighted-average fusion | $w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2$ optimal |
| 10 | $P(\omega_i \mid z) = \frac{P(z \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{P(z)}$ | Bayes' rule for decision fusion | Probabilities in [0,1] |
| 11 | $\text{Innovation} = z_k - H\hat{x}_k^-$ | Measurement residual | Should be white noise |
| 12 | $\text{MSE} = E[(x - \hat{x})^2]$ | Filter performance metric | Lower is better |
| 13 | $\tau = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c}$ | Complementary filter time constant | $f_c$: crossover frequency (Hz) |
| 14 | $w_k^{(i)} \propto w_{k-1}^{(i)} \, p(z_k \mid x_k^{(i)})$ | Particle weight update | Normalize after |
| 15 | $\text{NIS} = \nu_k^T S_k^{-1} \nu_k$ | Normalized Innovation Squared (consistency check) | Should follow $\chi^2_m$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoiding Markdown Table Breaks:** All absolute-value and conditional notations in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., `\vert x_k \vert`) to prevent rendering issues in KTU's PDF-export grading pipeline.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Robotic Assembly

| Use Case | Fusion Strategy | Payoff |
|----------|-----------------|--------|
| Peg-in-Hole Insertion | Vision (coarse) + Force (fine) | Sub-millimeter alignment under occlusion |
| Mobile Manipulator Navigation | Wheel odometry + IMU + Lidar | Drift-free localization in GPS-denied zones |
| Bin Picking | RGB + Depth + Tactile | Robust grasp on specular/jumbled objects |
| Conveyor Tracking | Encoder + Vision | Sub-cm tracking despite belt slippage |
| Human-Robot Collaboration | Vision (skeleton) + Force (collision) | Safe stop on unintended contact |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Weighted-Average Fusion

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Suppose two independent sensors measure the same scalar quantity $x$. Sensor 1 returns $\hat{x}_1$ with variance $\sigma_1^2$, and Sensor 2 returns $\hat{x}_2$ with variance $\sigma_2^2$. We wish to find the linear fusion estimator:

$$\hat{x}_f = w_1 \hat{x}_1 + w_2 \hat{x}_2, \quad w_1 + w_2 = 1$$

that minimizes the variance of the fusion error $\sigma_f^2 = E[(x - \hat{x}_f)^2]$.

### 3.1.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the fusion error.**

Let $\epsilon_i = x - \hat{x}_i$ with $E[\epsilon_i] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = \sigma_i^2$.

The fusion error is:
$$\epsilon_f = x - \hat{x}_f = x - w_1 \hat{x}_1 - w_2 \hat{x}_2$$

Substituting $\hat{x}_i = x - \epsilon_i$:
$$\epsilon_f = x - w_1(x - \epsilon_1) - w_2(x - \epsilon_2)$$
$$\epsilon_f = x(1 - w_1 - w_2) + w_1 \epsilon_1 + w_2 \epsilon_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint $w_1 + w_2 = 1$.**

The first term vanishes identically:
$$\epsilon_f = w_1 \epsilon_1 + w_2 \epsilon_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance (assuming independence, $E[\epsilon_1 \epsilon_2] = 0$).**

$$\sigma_f^2 = E[\epsilon_f^2] = E[(w_1 \epsilon_1 + w_2 \epsilon_2)^2]$$
$$\sigma_f^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2 + 2 w_1 w_2 E[\epsilon_1 \epsilon_2]$$
$$\sigma_f^2 = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + w_2^2 \sigma_2^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the constraint $w_2 = 1 - w_1$ to reduce to one variable.**

$$\sigma_f^2(w_1) = w_1^2 \sigma_1^2 + (1 - w_1)^2 \sigma_2^2$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $w_1$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\sigma_f^2}{dw_1} = 2 w_1 \sigma_1^2 - 2(1 - w_1)\sigma_2^2 = 0$$

$$w_1 \sigma_1^2 = (1 - w_1)\sigma_2^2$$
$$w_1 \sigma_1^2 + w_1 \sigma_2^2 = \sigma_2^2$$
$$w_1 = \frac{\sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $w_2$.**

$$w_2 = 1 - w_1 = \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

**Step 7 — Optimal fused estimate.**

$$\boxed{\hat{x}_f = \frac{\sigma_2^2 \, \hat{x}_1 + \sigma_1^2 \, \hat{x}_2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Minimal fused variance.**

$$\sigma_f^2 = \frac{\sigma_1^2 \, \sigma_2^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The optimal weight is *inversely* proportional to variance — the more reliable sensor (lower $\sigma_i^2$) gets the larger weight. This is the foundational formula for redundant sensor fusion and is **directly asked in KTU exams**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kalman Gain Minimizing Posterior Covariance

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

Given prior estimate $\hat{x}^-$ with covariance $P^-$, and measurement $z = Hx + v$, find the gain $K$ minimizing $\text{tr}(P)$ where:

$$\hat{x} = \hat{x}^- + K(z - H\hat{x}^-)$$
$$P = (I - KH)P^-(I - KH)^T + KRK^T$$

### 3.2.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Expand the posterior covariance.**

$$P = P^- - KHP^- - P^-H^TK^T + K(HP^-H^T + R)K^T$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $\text{tr}(P)$ with respect to $K$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{\partial \, \text{tr}(P)}{\partial K} = -2P^-H^T + 2K(HP^-H^T + R) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $K$.**

$$K(HP^-H^T + R) = P^-H^T$$
$$\boxed{K = P^-H^T(HP^-H^T + R)^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify by substituting into $P$ expression.**

Using the matrix inversion lemma (Woodbury identity):
$$(HP^-H^T + R)^{-1} = R^{-1} - R^{-1}H(HP^-H^T + R - H P^- H^T R^{-1} H P^- H^T)^{-1} H R^{-1}$$

which simplifies (after algebraic manipulation) to the Joseph form:
$$P = (I - KH)P^-(I - KH)^T + KRK^T$$

This is the numerically stable **Joseph form** of the covariance update, used in production robotic systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The matrix $S = HP^-H^T + R$ is called the **innovation covariance**. Its inverse is the precision (information) gained from the measurement. When $S$ is large (high uncertainty), $K$ becomes small (filter ignores measurement).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Filter Selection for Robotic Applications

| Application | State Vector Dim. | Linearity | Recommended Filter | Justification |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1-DOF joint position | 2 (pos, vel) | Linear | Kalman | Optimal, fast |
| IMU orientation (quaternion) | 4 | Nonlinear | EKF / Madgwick | Manifold on SO(3) |
| Visual SLAM | 6+ | Highly nonlinear | EKF-SLAM / FastSLAM | Landmarks as states |
| Peg-in-hole contact | 6 | Nonlinear | EKF with switching models | Contact mode switching |
| Bin-picking pose | 6 | Nonlinear | Particle Filter | Multi-modal distributions |
| Mobile robot localization | 3 | Nonlinear | Particle Filter / EKF | Map-based, non-Gaussian |
| Conveyor part tracking | 2 | Linear | Kalman with constant velocity | Smooth motion |
| Force-controlled insertion | 6 | Nonlinear | Complementary + EKF hybrid | Bandwidth separation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Centralized Sensor Fusion Architecture

flowchart TD

    nodeInit --> nodePredCov["Predict Covariance Pk minus A Pk minus1 AT Q"]

    nodeInnov --> nodeUpdate["Update State xk xk minus Kk nu k"]

    nodeNext --> nodePredCov
