**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Justify the **selection of a 6-DOF articulated robot** (e.g., KUKA KR 16 R1610) for the above case. Explain how the **end-effector (welding gun)** and **safety architecture (ISO 13849-1)** are integrated into the workcell.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Operator Savings:**

Original operators = 4 × 2 shifts × 300 days = 2400 operator-shifts/year (we assume same on 2 shifts; the problem simplifies to 4 per shift per day — let us take 4 per shift × 1 shift × 300 = 1200; for 2 shifts: 2400).

$$
S_L = 2400 \times 800 = \text{Rs. } 19{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Scrap Savings:**

Annual production (manual) = 240 × 300 = 72,000 side-frames
Annual production (auto) = 320 × 300 = 96,000 side-frames

Scrap before = 0.05 × 72,000 = 3,600 pieces → cost = 3,600 × 600 = ₹ 21,60,000
Scrap after = 0.02 × 96,000 = 1,920 pieces → cost = 1,920 × 600 = ₹ 11,52,000

$$
S_S = 21{,}60{,}000 - 11{,}52{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 10{,}08{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Net Savings:**

$$
S_{net} = S_L + S_S - C_{maint} = 19{,}20{,}000 + 10{,}08{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 21{,}28{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — ROI:**

$$
ROI = \frac{21{,}28{,}000}{1{,}50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 14.19 \ \%
$$

**Step 5 — Payback Period:**

$$
PP = \frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{21{,}28{,}000} = 7.05 \ \text{years}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 7-year payback is **long** — the case is borderline. The company may add a *third shift* to improve the numbers, or amortize the CapEx over a product life of 10 years.

**Model Solution for (b):**

A **6-DOF articulated robot** is justified because:

1. **Workspace complexity:** Spot welding on a side-frame involves 80+ weld points distributed across a 1.6 m × 1.4 m × 1.2 m volume. An articulated robot's spherical workspace ($V_w = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_w^3$) with $R_w = 1610$ mm naturally envelops this volume.
2. **Dexterity:** The car body has re-entrant corners and angled surfaces; the 6th axis (wrist roll) is essential to keep the welding gun perpendicular to the seam.
3. **Payload:** Welding gun + cable bundle weighs 12 kg, well within the 16 kg payload rating of the **KR 16 R1610**.
4. **Repeatability:** ±0.04 mm ensures consistent weld nugget size, critical for crash-test compliance (ECE R95).

**End-Effector Integration:**

- The **servo-electric welding gun** is mounted on the robot's ISO 9409-1 flange.
- A **force/torque sensor** at the wrist monitors electrode force (typically 3 – 5 kN).
- A **water-cooled cable bundle** is routed through the robot's hollow wrist to prevent premature wear.

**Safety Architecture (ISO 13849-1):**

- The cell achieves **Performance Level d (PLd)** by using:
  - **Dual-channel safety relays** for E-stop
  - **Light curtains** (Type 4) at the perimeter with 25 ms response
  - **Speed and separation monitoring (SSM)** that slows the robot when a human enters the collaborative zone
  - A **safety-rated PLC** (e.g., Siemens F-CPU) that interfaces with the robot's **SafeMove 3** function.

> **Marking split for (b):** Robot justification 3 marks + end-effector 2 marks + safety 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Material Handling Case Study (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* With reference to the **Amazon Kiva (now Amazon Robotics) fulfilment centre**, explain the **Goods-to-Person** material handling concept. Calculate the **fleet size** required if each drive unit carries a 450 kg pod, operates at 1.2 m/s, serves 8 picking stations, and the centre must achieve 120,000 picks/day over a 20-hour operating window with 95 % availability.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Compare **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** with **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot)** for material handling in a warehouse. Mention two real-world case studies where each was deployed.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Goods-to-Person Concept:**

In the conventional **Person-to-Goods** system, a human picker walks 12–15 km/day, retrieving items from shelves. Amazon's **Kiva drive unit** inverts this: the robot lifts the entire storage pod and brings it to a stationary picker. This **Goods-to-Person** paradigm eliminates picker walking, reduces ergonomic injury, and triples pick rates.

**Fleet Size Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Effective Operating Time:**

$$
T_{eff} = T_{window} \times A = 20 \times 0.95 = 19 \ \text{hours/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Required Picks per Hour:**

$$
\eta_{required} = \frac{120{,}000}{19} = 6315.79 \ \text{picks/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Picks per Station per Hour:**

A human picker at a station can complete ~120 picks/hour (industry benchmark for Kiva-enabled stations).

$$
\eta_{station} = 120 \ \text{picks/hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Stations Required:**

$$
N_{stations} = \frac{6315.79}{120} = 52.63 \Rightarrow 53 \ \text{stations}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Robots per Station (with redundancy):**

A 95 % availability target typically requires 1.15 robots per station (15 % spare capacity).

$$
N_{robots} = 53 \times 1.15 = 60.95 \Rightarrow 61 \ \text{robots}
$$

**Result:** A fleet of **~61 Kiva-style drive units** is needed.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | AGV | AMR |
|---|---|---|
| Navigation | Magnetic tape, QR codes, or wire-guided (fixed path) | SLAM with LiDAR and 2D cameras (dynamic path) |
| Flexibility | Low — path change requires infrastructure rework | High — re-plan path in software |
| Cost per unit | ₹ 8 – 15 Lakh | ₹ 12 – 30 Lakh |
| Best case study | **John Deere Harvester Works** — AGVs tow engine blocks along a 2 km loop. | **Kiva system at Amazon's Tracy, CA fulfilment centre** — 1000+ AMRs work in coordinated fleet. |
| Other case study | **Coca-Cola Bottling** — AGVs move raw syrup drums in Schenectady plant. | **Ocado's Ocado Smart Platform (OSP)** — 1000+ AMRs in a grid for grocery fulfilment. |

> **Marking split for (b):** Comparison table 4 marks + two real-world cases 3 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Case-Study Answers:**
> 1. **Units not stated** — Writing "ROI = 20" instead of "ROI = 20.33 %" loses 0.5 – 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing OEE with Availability** — OEE = A × P × Q; do not write OEE = 0.90 if you mean Availability = 0.90.
> 3. **Robot selection without justification** — Always state **payload, reach, repeatability, and DOF** as the four pillars of justification.
> 4. **Ignoring safety standards** — In a robotic case study, omitting **ISO 13849-1 / ISO 10218** is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Vague "industry case" mentions** — Saying "used in BMW" is weak. Saying "used at **BMW Spartanburg's body shop on the X3 line, replacing a 7-axis hydraulic robot with a FANUC R-2000iC for 23 % cycle time reduction**" is exemplary.
> 6. **Forgetting to amortize CapEx** — ROI must compare against the *full* CapEx, not just the robot arm cost.
> 7. **Mixing up SCARA with Cartesian** — SCARAs have rotary joints, not linear encoders. Cartesian = XYZ gantry.
> 8. **No mention of payload-to-reach ratio** — A robot with 16 kg payload and 1610 mm reach has a **payload-to-reach ratio of 0.0099 kg/mm**, an industry-standard sanity check.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A case study is an *evidence-based engineering audit* of a real robotic system. A practical example is a *pedagogical illustration* of a single subsystem.
- **Key Metrics to Memorize:** Takt Time, Cycle Time, Throughput (PPH), OEE, ROI, Payback Period, MTBF, Payload, Reach.
- **Robot Selection Rule of Thumb:**
  - **SCARA** → PCB, SMT, light assembly (1 – 20 kg, planar motion)
  - **Delta** → Food, pharma, packaging (sub-8 kg, 200 – 1200 PPH)
  - **6-DOF Articulated** → Welding, painting, machine tending (5 – 2300 kg)
  - **Cartesian Gantry** → Heavy palletizing, long-stroke operations (10 – 5000 kg)
- **Material Handling Paradigms:** Person-to-Goods (traditional) vs. Goods-to-Person (Kiva-style).
- **AGV vs AMR:** AGV is *fixed path, infrastructure-bound*; AMR is *SLAM-based, autonomous, dynamically routed*.
- **Famous Real-World Case Studies to Remember:**
  1. **Tesla Gigafactory Berlin** — 6-DOF body welding
  2. **BMW Spartanburg** — High-payload spot welding
  3. **Amazon Robotics (Kiva)** — AMR fulfilment
  4. **Foxconn SMT lines** — SCARA placement
  5. **Ocado OSP** — AMR grocery fulfilment
  6. **John Deere Harvester Works** — AGV in heavy industry
  7. **ABB FlexPicker in Mondelez** — Delta packaging
  8. **KUKA KR 16 R1610 in Tier-1 automotive** — Arc welding
- **Formula Recap (the 4 that appear in 80 % of KTU questions):**
  - $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D_{demand}$
  - $\eta = 3600 / T_c$
  - $OEE = A \times P \times Q$
  - $ROI = (S_{net} / C_{CapEx}) \times 100$
- **Safety Standards to Quote:** **ISO 10218-1 (Robot safety)**, **ISO 13849-1 (PL rating)**, **ISO/TS 15066 (Collaborative robots)**.
- **Industry-Standard Sanity Check Numbers:**
  - World-class OEE = **85 %**
  - Robotic cell typical ROI = **15 – 35 %/year**
  - Robotic cell typical payback = **2 – 5 years**
  - Kiva-robot pick rate = **300 – 400 picks/hour per station**
  - Delta robot peak pick rate = **1200 PPH**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:** Memorize the *Analytical Decision Matrix* (Section 3.3) and the *Formula Cheat Sheet* (Section 2.3) — together they will earn you **at least 10 of the 14 marks** in any Part-B question on this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Analysis of Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Analysis of Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design and Analysis of Robotic Systems** is the integrated engineering discipline that synthesises **mechanical architecture**, **kinematic modelling**, **dynamic modelling**, **actuation selection**, **structural analysis**, and **performance evaluation** to produce a manipulator capable of executing a specified assembly or material-handling task with desired precision, payload, speed, and reliability.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme (OERUT724), this domain is built on the following pillars:

- **Kinematic Design** – Geometric synthesis of the link-joint chain (type, sequence, lengths, offsets) to satisfy workspace and dexterity.
- **Dynamic Analysis** – Computation of joint torques/forces, accelerations, and energy requirements.
- **Static and Quasi-Static Analysis** – Evaluation of deflections, stiffness, and loading under operational payload.
- **Actuator / Drive Selection** – Matching of motors, gearheads, and transmissions to the dynamic demand.
- **End-Effector / Gripper Design** – Synthesis of the tool interface to the workpiece.
- **Performance Quantification** – Resolution, repeatability, accuracy, payload-to-weight ratio, and natural frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly couples *design intent* (specification sheet) with *analytical verification* (DH parameters, Jacobian, Euler-Lagrange). Questions in ESE typically test both dimensions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Mental Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Human Arm as a Robot**
>
> Imagine you are an engineer asked to design a "machine" that must pick up a glass of water from a table and place it on a shelf. Just as your shoulder, elbow, and wrist joints work together, a robot is a chain of rigid links connected by joints. The "design" part is deciding:
>
> - **How many joints** you need (your arm has 7, a simple pick-and-place robot may have 4).
> - **How long each link** should be (your upper arm ≈ 30 cm, forearm ≈ 25 cm).
> - **Which type of joint** at each location (shoulder rotates – *revolute*; a slider drawer uses *prismatic*).
> - **How strong the "muscles"** (motors) must be (you can lift 5 kg; a robot must be sized for the rated payload).
> - **How stiff the "bones"** are (your bones bend slightly – so does every robot arm, and the designer must compute that bending).
>
> The "analysis" part is the mathematics that **predicts** what the machine will do *before* you build it: where the gripper will land, how fast it moves, how much torque each motor needs, and whether the structure will vibrate. This is exactly analogous to a civil engineer calculating beam deflection *before* pouring concrete.

## 1.3 Design Parameters (Industry-Standard Metrics)

The following **quantitative specifications** govern every robotic design (KUKA, FANUC, ABB, Yaskawa datasheets):

| Parameter | Typical Range | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload** | **1 kg – 1500 kg** | Mass the robot can manipulate at rated performance |
| **Reach** | **0.3 m – 3.5 m** | Maximum distance from base to wrist centre |
| **Repeatability** | **±0.01 mm – ±0.5 mm** | Statistical scatter upon returning to a taught pose |
| **Number of Axes** | **4 – 7** | Independent degrees of freedom (DOF) |
| **Joint Speed** | **90°/s – 450°/s** | Maximum angular velocity per axis |
| **Cycle Time** | **0.3 s – 6 s** | Time to complete a standard pick-and-place |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** *Payload × Reach × Acceleration* determines the dominant design driver. Heavy-payload long-reach robots are dynamically dominated; light-payload high-speed SCARAs are stiffness-dominated.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Phase Design Methodology

The professional design flow taught in KTU 2024 follows a strict top-down decomposition:

1. **Task Specification Phase** – Identify cycle time, payload mass $m_p$, trajectory envelope, accuracy, and environmental constraints (temperature, dust, wash-down).
2. **Kinematic Synthesis Phase** – Choose joint type sequence (e.g., *RRR* for articulated, *PRR* for SCARA), link lengths $a_i$, and offsets $d_i$ to cover the workspace.
3. **Dynamic Modelling Phase** – Compute joint torques $\tau_i(t)$ required along the prescribed trajectory $q(t), \dot q(t), \ddot q(t)$.
4. **Actuator & Transmission Sizing Phase** – Select motor rated torque $> \tau_{i,\max}$, gear ratio $n$, and check thermal limit.
5. **Structural Verification Phase** – Compute deflection $\delta$, natural frequency $\omega_n$, and stress $\sigma$ to ensure stiffness and fatigue life.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

- **Why task specification first?** Because every subsequent decision flows from it. A 0.1 mm repeatability requirement eliminates pneumatic muscles; a 5 kg payload eliminates harmonic drives with low torque capacity.
- **Why kinematic synthesis before dynamics?** Because the *geometry* of the chain dictates the *range* of $\tau$ required. A poorly chosen arm cannot be rescued by a bigger motor.
- **Why dynamic modelling?** Quasi-static analysis alone misses inertial coupling – a phenomenon where accelerating joint 2 induces reaction torque on joint 1 even with no payload.
- **Why structural verification?** Aluminium arms (E = 70 GPa) deflect more than steel (E = 200 GPa); a long-reach arm acts like a cantilever and may have a first natural frequency dangerously close to the excitation frequency of the motion controller.

## 2.3 DH Parameterisation — The Heart of Kinematic Design

Each link is described by four parameters defined in the **Denavit–Hartenberg convention**:

- $\theta_i$ – joint angle (variable for revolute, fixed for prismatic)
- $d_i$ – link offset (fixed for revolute, variable for prismatic)
- $a_i$ – link length (common normal)
- $\alpha_i$ – link twist

The homogeneous transform from frame $\{i-1\}$ to $\{i\}$ is:

$$
^{i-1}T_i \;=\; \text{Rot}_{z,\theta_i}\,\text{Trans}_{z,d_i}\,\text{Trans}_{x,a_i}\,\text{Rot}_{x,\alpha_i}
$$

The full end-effector pose is the chain product:

$$
T_n \;=\; {}^{0}T_1 \cdot {}^{1}T_2 \cdot {}^{2}T_3 \cdots {}^{n-1}T_n
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — these are the equations that appear in KTU ESE Part B questions.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gruebler's DOF | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints |
| 2 | DH Homogeneous Transform | $^{i-1}T_i$ as in §2.3 | $4 \times 4$ matrix |
| 3 | End-effector pose | $T_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n}{}^{i-1}T_i$ | Position + orientation |
| 4 | Linear Jacobian | $J_v = \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ | $3 \times n$ matrix |
| 5 | Angular Jacobian | $J_{\omega,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (revolute) | $3 \times n$ matrix |
| 6 | End-effector velocity | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(q)\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $6 \times 1$ vector |
| 7 | Static joint torque | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J(q)^{\!T}\,\mathbf{F}$ | $\mathbf{F}$ is wrench on EE |
| 8 | Euler-Lagrange | $\tau_i = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot q_i} - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}$ | $L = T - V$ |
| 9 | Kinetic energy | $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T} M(q)\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $M(q)$ is mass matrix |
| 10 | Workspace (planar 2R) | $A = 4\pi L_1 L_2$ | $L_1,L_2$ link lengths |
| 11 | Cantilever deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$ | $E$ modulus, $I$ second moment |
| 12 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ stiffness, $m$ effective mass |
| 13 | Motor torque selection | $\tau_{rated} \geq \tau_{peak} / (n \cdot S_f)$ | $n$ gear ratio, $S_f$ safety factor $\geq 1.5$ |
| 14 | Resolution | $\Delta q = \dfrac{q_{range}}{N_{encoder}}$ | $N_{encoder}$ counts/rev |
| 15 | Repeatability vs Accuracy | $\text{Repeatability} = 3\sigma$ scatter ; $\text{Accuracy} = \vert e_{pos} \vert_{max}$ | Repeatability $\le$ Accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars in the table above use the LaTeX command `\vert` (not the ASCII pipe `\vert`) to preserve the Markdown table structure. The same applies to all subsequent tables in this document.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Design Driver | Typical Robot |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive welding | Path accuracy at high speed | 6-axis articulated (FANUC R-2000iC) |
| PCB assembly | Sub-millimetre repeatability | SCARA (Epson LS6) |
| Palletising | Heavy payload, large workspace | 4-axis articulated (KUKA KR 1000) |
| Surgery | Tremor-free precision | 7-DOF serial (da Vinci) |
| Bin picking | Complex 3D reach | 6-axis with vision (ABB IRB 1200) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are *exhaustive* — no step is skipped. KTU 2024 valuation keys award **partial marks for every correct transformation step**, so writing out every line is a deliberate exam strategy.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**Problem Setup.** A planar RR manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $q_1$ and $q_2$, both zero twist $\alpha_i = 0$, both zero offset $d_i = 0$.

**Step 1.** Write the DH parameters in a table.

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $q_1$ |
| 2 | $L_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $q_2$ |

**Step 2.** Substitute into the general DH transform:

$$
^{i-1}T_i = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\cos\theta_i \\
\sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i\cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i\sin\alpha_i & a_i\sin\theta_i \\
0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.** With $\alpha_i = 0$ and $d_i = 0$, the matrix simplifies. For link 1 ($i=1$):

$$
^{0}T_1 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos q_1 & -\sin q_1 & 0 & L_1\cos q_1 \\
\sin q_1 & \cos q_1 & 0 & L_1\sin q_1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4.** For link 2 ($i=2$), with $a_2 = L_2$ and $\theta_2 = q_2$:

$$
^{1}T_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos q_2 & -\sin q_2 & 0 & L_2\cos q_2 \\
\sin q_2 & \cos q_2 & 0 & L_2\sin q_2 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5.** Multiply $^{0}T_1 \cdot {}^{1}T_2$ to obtain the end-effector pose $T_2$. Compute the product in four blocks: rotation $R$, position $p$.

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Expanding element by element (using shorthand $c_i = \cos q_i$, $s_i = \sin q_i$):

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 & -(c_1 s_2 + s_1 c_2) & 0 \\
s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2 & -s_1 s_2 + c_1 c_2 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Apply the cosine and sine addition identities $c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2 = \cos(q_1+q_2)$ and $s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2 = \sin(q_1+q_2)$:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos(q_1+q_2) & -\sin(q_1+q_2) & 0 \\
\sin(q_1+q_2) & \cos(q_1+q_2) & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6.** Compute the position vector $p$ (4th column of $T_2$):

$$
\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1\cos q_1 \\ L_1\sin q_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} L_2\cos q_2 \\ L_2\sin q_2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
= \begin{bmatrix} L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 7.** Final homogeneous matrix:

$$
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix}
\cos(q_1+q_2) & -\sin(q_1+q_2) & 0 & L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\
\sin(q_1+q_2) & \cos(q_1+q_2) & 0 & L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the classical **2R planar arm forward-kinematic solution**, used in 90% of introductory robotics texts and KTU question papers.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jacobian for the 2R Planar Arm

**Step 1.** Differentiate the position vector $\mathbf{p}$ from §3.1 with respect to $q_1$ and $q_2$:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2.** Assemble the linear velocity Jacobian (planar, so $3 \times 2$):

$$
J_v = \begin{bmatrix}
-L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\
0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3.** The angular Jacobian for two revolute joints with axis $\mathbf{z}_0 = (0,0,1)$ and $\mathbf{z}_1 = (0,0,1)$:

$$
J_\omega = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4.** Full $3 \times 2$ Jacobian (planar, combine linear + scalar angular):

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix}
-L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\
1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Joint Torque from End-Effector Wrench

**Principle of Virtual Work:** If the end-effector applies a wrench $\mathbf{F} = [F_x, F_y, M_z]^T$ and the joints do virtual work $\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\mathbf{q}$, then:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T}\delta\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}^{T}\delta\mathbf{x}, \quad \delta\mathbf{x} = J\delta\mathbf{q}
$$

Substituting and cancelling $\delta\mathbf{q}$ (for non-zero variations):

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} = \mathbf{F}^{T}J \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}\mathbf{F}
$$

**Numerical Example.** Let $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.8\,\text{m}$, $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = -45°$, payload weight $W = 50\,\text{N}$ acting downward, $M_z = 0$.

**Step 1.** Evaluate the trigonometric terms.

$$
q_1 + q_2 = -15° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin(-15°) = -0.2588, \quad \cos(-15°) = 0.9659
$$

$$
\sin 30° = 0.5, \quad \cos 30° = 0.8660
$$

**Step 2.** Jacobian entries:

$$
J_{11} = -1(0.5) - 0.8(-0.2588) = -0.5 + 0.2071 = -0.2929
$$

$$
J_{12} = -0.8(-0.2588) = 0.2071
$$

$$
J_{21} = 1(0.8660) + 0.8(0.9659) = 0.8660 + 0.7727 = 1.6387
$$

$$
J_{22} = 0.8(0.9659) = 0.7727
$$

$$
J_{31} = 1, \quad J_{32} = 1
$$

**Step 3.** Compute $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}\mathbf{F}$ with $\mathbf{F} = [0, -50, 0]^T$:

$$
\tau_1 = J_{11}(0) + J_{21}(-50) + J_{31}(0) = 1.6387 \times (-50) = -81.94\,\text{N·m}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = J_{12}(0) + J_{22}(-50) + J_{32}(0) = 0.7727 \times (-50) = -38.63\,\text{N·m}
$$

The motor at joint 1 must counter **81.94 N·m** and joint 2 must counter **38.63 N·m** purely due to gravity. Real designs add an inertial term $\tau_{inertial} = M(q)\ddot{q}$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cantilever Deflection of a Robot Arm Link

**Model.** Treat link $i$ of length $L_i$ as a cantilever beam with payload force $F$ at the tip. The tip deflection is:

$$
\delta = \frac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}
$$

**Numerical example.** Aluminium link, $E = 70\,\text{GPa}$, rectangular cross-section $b = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $h = 0.08\,\text{m}$, $L = 0.6\,\text{m}$, $F = 100\,\text{N}$.

**Step 1.** Second moment of area:

$$
I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.08^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.000512}{12} = 2.133 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 2.** Deflection:

$$
\delta = \frac{100 \times 0.6^{3}}{3 \times 70 \times 10^{9} \times 2.133 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{21.6}{447.9 \times 10^{3}} = 4.82 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 48.2\,\mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3.** Compare to repeatability spec $\pm 0.1\,\text{mm} = 100\,\mu\text{m}$. The deflection (48.2 µm) is acceptable — within spec. This calculation is repeated for *every link* in a complete design.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Natural Frequency Constraint

To avoid resonance with the controller bandwidth (typically 10–50 Hz), the arm's first natural frequency must be **at least 10× the servo bandwidth** (Bode's rule of thumb).

For a simple lumped-parameter model:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}
$$

**Step 1.** Effective stiffness $k$ from cantilever: $k = 3EI/L^{3}$.

**Step 2.** Effective mass at tip ≈ $m_{link}/3 + m_{payload}$ (Rayleigh approximation).

**Step 3.** Continuing the numerical example, $m_{link} = \rho A L = 2700 \times 0.004 \times 0.6 = 6.48\,\text{kg}$:

$$
k = \frac{3 \times 70 \times 10^{9} \times 2.133 \times 10^{-6}}{0.6^{3}} = 2.07 \times 10^{6}\,\text{N/m}
$$

$$
m_{eff} = \frac{6.48}{3} + 5 = 7.16\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{2.07 \times 10^{6}}{7.16}} = 537.5\,\text{rad/s} = 85.5\,\text{Hz}
$$

The arm natural frequency (85.5 Hz) safely exceeds a 10 Hz servo bandwidth — the design passes the **dynamic-stiffness check**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Part A consists of compulsory 3-mark questions. Answers should be **direct, structured, and ≤ 1 page** in handwriting.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the following terms as applied to robotic manipulators:**
**(a) Repeatability, (b) Resolution, (c) Accuracy.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Repeatability** is the closeness of agreement between repeated measurements of the end-effector pose when the manipulator is commanded to return to the same location, under identical conditions. It is statistically quantified as $\pm 3\sigma$ of the scatter and **does not measure absolute error**, only consistency.
- **Resolution** is the *smallest incremental motion* the controller can command, determined by the encoder count $N$ and the gear reduction $n$:

$$
\Delta q = \frac{q_{range}}{n N}
$$

- **Accuracy** is the deviation between a *commanded* pose and the *actual achieved* pose in absolute world coordinates. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \vert \mathbf{x}_{commanded} - \mathbf{x}_{actual} \vert_{max}
$$

In industrial practice, **repeatability ≤ accuracy** is always true, because a robot can be consistent (low repeatability) yet biased (low accuracy) if not calibrated.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Using Gruebler's equation, determine the number of degrees of freedom of a 6-revolute-joint serial manipulator used in arc-welding applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $n = 6$ moving links (plus ground = 7), $j_1 = 6$ (six revolute joints, each 1-DOF), $j_2 = 0$ (no 2-DOF joints).

Gruebler's equation for planar/spatial single-loop chains:

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

Substituting:

$$
M = 3(7-1) - 2(6) - 0 = 18 - 12 = 6\,\text{DOF}
$$

This is the *classical 6R articulated robot* (FANUC, KUKA, ABB), which is the minimum DOF needed to position *and* orient an end effector arbitrarily in 3D space (3 translational + 3 rotational). It is the industry standard for arc welding, painting, and material handling.

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice. Two alternative questions (A and B) are provided below; examiners will offer one, students answer one. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the forward kinematics of a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator using Denavit–Hartenberg parameters. Use link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $q_1$ and $q_2$. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Tabulate DH parameters.

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $q_1$ |
| 2 | $L_2$ | $0$ | $0$ | $q_2$ |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write each transform $^{0}T_1$ and $^{1}T_2$ as in §3.1, step 3–4.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Multiply $^{0}T_1 \cdot {}^{1}T_2$, applying the addition identities:

$$
\cos(q_1+q_2) = c_1 c_2 - s_1 s_2, \quad \sin(q_1+q_2) = s_1 c_2 + c_1 s_2
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Arrive at the final homogeneous matrix $T_2$ (see §3.1, step 7).

The end-effector **position** is therefore:

$$
x = L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2), \quad y = L_1\sin q_1 + L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)
$$

and the end-effector **orientation** is the planar angle $q_1 + q_2$.

**(b) With reference to the Jacobian, explain how static joint torques are computed for a manipulator when a known wrench is applied at the end effector. Use a numerical example. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks): State the principle of virtual work.** From §3.3:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}\mathbf{F}
$$

where $J$ is the geometric Jacobian and $\mathbf{F}$ is the 6-vector wrench (forces + moments) at the end effector.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute the Jacobian.** For a 2R planar arm:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix}
-L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \\
1 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Evaluate numerically.** Use the same numerical example as §3.3, step 3: $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 0.8\,\text{m}$, $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = -45°$, $\mathbf{F} = [0, -50, 0]^T$ (50 N downward).

$$
\tau_1 = -81.94\,\text{N·m}, \quad \tau_2 = -38.63\,\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Conclude.** The motor at each joint must be sized to deliver a peak torque **at least equal** to this static value **plus the inertial component** $M(q)\ddot{q}$ during acceleration. A safety factor $S_f \geq 1.5$ is then applied per the formula sheet entry #13.

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the design considerations for the end-effector (gripper) of a material-handling robot. Include mechanical, sensor, and material aspects. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks): Mechanical considerations.** The gripper must provide:
- **Gripping force** $F_g \geq \mu^{-1} W$ where $W$ is payload weight, $\mu$ is the friction coefficient at the finger-payload interface.
- **Stroke (opening)** $S \geq 1.5 D_{max}$ where $D_{max}$ is the largest workpiece dimension.
- **Closing time** $t_c \leq t_{cycle}/3$ to maintain cycle time.
- **Compactness** to fit inside tight fixtures.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Actuation choice.**
- *Pneumatic*: simple, fast, low cost — best for fixed-shape workpieces.
- *Servo-electric*: precise force/position control — best for fragile or variable parts.
- *Vacuum*: for flat, smooth surfaces (glass, sheet metal).
- *Magnetic*: for ferrous parts (no surface contact needed).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks): Sensor integration.** Force/torque sensors at the wrist (e.g., ATI Mini45) provide compliance control; tactile sensors on fingers detect slip; proximity sensors confirm part presence.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks): Material aspects.**
- **Finger pads**: silicone, polyurethane, or rubber for grip and compliance.
- **Finger body**: aluminium (light) or tool steel (durable).
- **Surface finish**: knurled or patterned to maximise $\mu$.

**Conclusion:** A gripper design integrates *task spec* (workpiece, payload, cycle) with *force analysis* and *sensor feedback* to achieve reliable material handling.

**(b) Explain with a stepwise procedure how actuators and gearheads are selected for a 3-DOF articulated robot arm. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute the dynamic torque profile.** Using Euler-Lagrange (§2.3 formula #8), compute $\tau_i(t)$ for each joint along the prescribed trajectory $q(t)$. Identify the **peak torque** $\tau_{i,peak}$ and the **RMS torque** $\tau_{i,rms}$ over one cycle.

**Step 2 (1 mark): Apply the safety factor.** Required rated torque:

$$
\tau_{i,required} = S_f \cdot \tau_{i,peak}, \quad S_f = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute required speed.** Joint speed $\dot q_{i,max}$ after gear reduction:

$$
\omega_{motor} = n \cdot \dot q_{joint}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Apply the motor sizing rule.** The selected motor must satisfy **all** of:

$$
\tau_{motor,rated} \geq \tau_{i,required}, \quad \omega_{motor,rated} \geq \omega_{motor}, \quad P_{motor} \geq \tau_{rms} \cdot \omega_{rms}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Choose the gear type.**

| Gearhead Type | Backlash | Efficiency | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Harmonic drive | < 1 arc-min | 80% | Precision (medical, semi-con) |
| Planetary | 3–10 arc-min | 85–95% | General industry |
| Cycloidal | 1–3 arc-min | 75–85% | High torque |
| Spur (direct) | High | 95% | Low-cost, low precision |

**Step 6 (1 mark): Verify thermal limit.** The motor's continuous torque rating must exceed $\tau_{rms}$, not just $\tau_{peak}$ — peaks are tolerated only briefly.

**Step 7 (1 mark): Document the selection.** Record motor model, gear ratio, duty cycle, and de-rating curves in the design log book — this is the KTU 2024 "design rationale" requirement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor.** Many students size the motor to $\tau_{peak}$ alone, ignoring the 1.5–2× safety margin. Examiners deduct **at least 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Confusing accuracy with repeatability.** This appears in nearly every KTU paper; expect at least one Part-A question.
> 3. **Skipping the inertial term in joint torque.** Static $J^{T}F$ analysis alone is **incomplete**; the full Lagrangian dynamic model must be mentioned for full 14-mark credit.
> 4. **Sign errors in DH transforms.** The convention $a_i$ is along $x_i$ and $d_i$ along $z_{i-1}$ — mixing them up causes systematic 7-mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting natural frequency check.** A robot with a 30 Hz first mode operated by a 25 Hz controller **will** resonate. Examiners value this observation.
> 6. **Not specifying units.** A torque of "50" is wrong; "50 N·m" is right. KTU key awards the unit consistently.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this section. It is the single most valuable 60-second review before walking into the KTU examination hall.

- [ ] **DH convention** — $a_i$ along $x_i$, $\alpha_i$ about $x_i$, $d_i$ along $z_{i-1}$, $\theta_i$ about $z_{i-1}$.
- [ ] **Gruebler's equation** — $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ for spatial chains.
- [ ] **Jacobian definition** — Linear part from $\partial \mathbf{p} / \partial \mathbf{q}$, angular part from $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (revolute) or $\mathbf{0}$ (prismatic).
- [ ] **Static torque** — $\boldsymbol{\tau} = J^{T}\mathbf{F}$ (Principle of Virtual Work).
- [ ] **Dynamic model** — Euler-Lagrange yields $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot q)\dot q + G(q)$.
- [ ] **Repeatability** = $3\sigma$ scatter; **Accuracy** = max absolute error; **Resolution** = encoder step size.
- [ ] **Workspace (2R planar)** — Area = $4\pi L_1 L_2$ (annulus between $L_1 - L_2$ and $L_1 + L_2$).
- [ ] **Cantilever deflection** — $\delta = FL^{3}/(3EI)$; **Natural frequency** — $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$.
- [ ] **Motor sizing rule** — Rated torque ≥ $S_f \cdot \tau_{peak}$ with $S_f = 1.5$–$2.0$.
- [ ] **Stiffness vs Inertia** — Long-reach robots are *stiffness-dominated*; high-speed robots are *inertia-dominated*.
- [ ] **Grip force** — $F_g \geq W/\mu$, with $\mu$ the finger–payload friction coefficient.
- [ ] **Six-DOF rule** — A general-purpose 6R robot is the minimum to position *and* orient arbitrarily in 3D.
- [ ] **Bode rule** — Mechanical natural frequency should be at least **10× the servo bandwidth**.
- [ ] **Joint sequence mnemonics** — RRR = articulated, RRP = SCARA, RPP = Cartesian, RRR+RRR = 6R.
- [ ] **Resolution formula** — $\Delta q = q_{range} / (n \cdot N_{encoder})$.
- [ ] **Sign convention in DH** — Right-hand rule for $\alpha_i$ positive; positive $\theta_i$ = counter-clockwise about $z_{i-1}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robotic System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Robotic System Design** is the systematic, multidisciplinary engineering process of conceptualizing, analyzing, synthesizing, and integrating mechanical, electronic, control, and software subsystems to create an automated robotic workcell capable of performing a defined industrial task (such as assembly, material handling, welding, or palletizing) within specified performance, economic, and safety constraints.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OERUT724 – Module 3), Robotic System Design encompasses the structured methodology used by automation engineers to translate a manufacturing problem statement into a fully operational robotized cell. This includes kinematic selection, work envelope optimization, end-effector specification, cycle-time analysis, safety architecture, and return-on-investment (ROI) computation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Robotic System Design is *not* the design of the robot arm itself — it is the design of the **workcell in which the robot operates**. The robot is treated as a procured component; the engineer's task is to make the robot, the tooling, the fixtures, the conveyors, and the safety system work as one integrated system.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a kitchen for a chef**. The chef (robot) already has fixed height, reach, and dexterity. Your job is to:

- Decide where the chef should stand (robot base location).
- Place the ingredients within arm's reach (work envelope).
- Provide the right knives and pans (end-effector, gripper).
- Design the lighting and ventilation (sensors and safety).
- Make sure the chef can finish cooking within the lunch hour (cycle time).
- Ensure the entire kitchen renovation pays for itself through saved wages (economic justification).

That is exactly what Robotic System Design is — except the "chef" is a 6-axis articulated robot, the "ingredients" are parts on a conveyor, and the "lunch hour" is a 12-second cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Robotic System Design (the "Golden Triangle"):**
> 1. **Functional Performance** – Can the system do the job? (reach, payload, accuracy, cycle time)
> 2. **Economic Feasibility** – Should we do the job with a robot? (payback period, ROI)
> 3. **Safety & Ergonomics** – Will the system harm anyone? (guards, interlocks, risk assessment)

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard metrics are used universally and are **high-yield for KTU exams**:

- **Payload (W):** Maximum mass the robot can carry at the wrist, typically **3 kg to 800 kg** for industrial robots.
- **Reach (R):** Maximum radial distance from the robot base to the wrist center, typically **0.5 m to 3.5 m**.
- **Repeatability:** ±0.02 mm to ±0.5 mm (precision of returning to a taught point).
- **Accuracy:** Position error relative to an absolute coordinate, typically **±1 mm to ±5 mm**.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent joint motions; **6 DOF** is the industry standard for general-purpose assembly.
- **Cycle Time (T_c):** Time to complete one full operation, typically **4 s to 30 s** in material handling.
- **Work Envelope Volume:** The 3-D space swept by the wrist center; for a spherical workspace it is **V = (4/3)πR³**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Seven-Phase Design Methodology

Robotic system design follows a structured waterfall methodology. Each phase has defined deliverables and approval gates. Skipping a phase is the single most common cause of failed robotic workcells in industry.

**Phase 1 – Problem Definition & Feasibility Study**
- Identify the part, process, and production volume.
- Quantify the current manual cost (labor, scrap, ergonomic injury).
- Decide whether automation is *even appropriate* (high-volume, hazardous, or repetitive tasks are the prime candidates).
- Deliverable: **Feasibility Report**.

**Phase 2 – Task Analysis & Specification**
- Break the operation into elemental motions (pick, move, place, wait).
- Define payload, reach, accuracy, and cycle-time requirements.
- Determine the required Degrees of Freedom using the **Three-Number Rule**: DOF of part = DOF of task + DOF of approach.

**Phase 3 – Robot Selection**
- Match the specification to commercially available robots.
- Decision criteria: payload capacity, reach, repeatability, controller, vendor support.
- Use a weighted **selection matrix** (Pugh Matrix / Kepner-Tregoe analysis).

**Phase 4 – End-Effector & Tooling Design**
- Design or specify the gripper (mechanical, vacuum, magnetic, or compliant).
- Specify the tool changer if multiple tools are required.
- Compute gripping force using the friction-cone model.

**Phase 5 – Workcell Layout & Simulation**
- Place the robot, conveyors, fixtures, and safety fences in 3-D CAD.
- Simulate cycle time, reachability, and collision using offline programming software (e.g., RoboDK, DELMIA, Visual Components).
- Verify the **Robot Mounting Configuration** — floor, ceiling, wall, or rail.

**Phase 6 – Safety System Design**
- Conduct a **Risk Assessment** per ISO 10218 and ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative robots).
- Define safety zones, light curtains, area scanners, and emergency stops.
- Validate the **Safety-Rated Reduced Speed** and **Power & Force Limiting** functions.

**Phase 7 – Economic Justification & Implementation**
- Compute Capital Expenditure (CAPEX), Operating Expenditure (OPEX), and Payback Period.
- Install, commission, and validate using a **Site Acceptance Test (SAT)**.

---

### 2.2 Why Each Phase Matters — The "Why" Behind the Logic

| Phase | Engineering "Why" |
|-------|-------------------|
| 1. Feasibility | Avoids wasting capital on automation that cannot beat manual cost |
| 2. Specification | Prevents over-specifying (expensive) or under-specifying (failure) |
| 3. Robot Selection | Robot is the most expensive single item — wrong choice is costly |
| 4. End-Effector | Determines cycle time and product quality — second most expensive item |
| 5. Layout | A poorly laid-out cell has unreachable parts or collisions |
| 6. Safety | A robot cell without safety is a liability and legal non-compliance |
| 7. Economics | Management will not release funds without a payback justification |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this module.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Payback Period (years)} &= \frac{\text{Total CAPEX}}{\text{Annual Savings}} \\[6pt]
\text{ROI (\%)} &= \frac{\text{Annual Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\text{Spherical Work Envelope Volume} &= \frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \\[6pt]
\text{Gripping Force (friction cone model)} &= F_{N} \cdot \mu \geq \frac{W \cdot (g + a)}{n \cdot \cos\theta} \\[6pt]
\text{Cycle Time} &= T_{c} = T_{\text{accel}} + T_{\text{cruise}} + T_{\text{decel}} + T_{\text{process}} \\[6pt]
\text{Required DOF} &= \text{DOF}_{\text{part}} - \text{DOF}_{\text{fixture}} \\[6pt]
\text{Mean Time Between Failures (system)} &= \frac{1}{\sum_{i} \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}_{i}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | KTU Exam Relevance |
|-----------|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| Payload | $W$ | kg or N | Very High |
| Reach | $R$ | m | Very High |
| Repeatability | $\rho$ | mm | High |
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | seconds | Very High |
| Gripping Force | $F_g$ | N | High |
| Work Envelope Volume | $V$ | m³ | Medium |
| Payback Period | $P$ | years | Very High |
| Degrees of Freedom | DOF | dimensionless | Very High |
| Robot Accuracy | $\epsilon$ | mm | Medium |
| Joint Speed | $\omega$ | rad/s or °/s | Medium |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Robotic System Design is the backbone of **Industry 4.0 smart factories**. Every automotive welding line, every pharmaceutical vial-filling cell, and every e-commerce warehouse uses this exact methodology. Engineers trained in this discipline work as **Automation Engineers, Robotics Integration Engineers, and Workcell Designers** in firms such as Tata Motors, ABB, FANUC, Yaskawa, and Amazon Robotics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Minimum Gripping Force (Friction-Cone Model)

This is a classic KTU problem (14-mark questions often derive this). We will work it out in full.

**Problem Setup:** A robot gripper holds a part of weight $W$ (in Newtons) during an upward acceleration $a$. The gripper has two jaws, each providing a normal force $F_N$ at an angle $\theta$ from the horizontal. The coefficient of friction between jaw and part is $\mu$. There are $n$ contact points in total.

**Step 1 — Draw the Free-Body Diagram.**
Forces acting on the part:
- Weight: $W$ acting downward.
- Inertial force: $W \cdot (a/g)$ acting downward (D'Alembert's principle) when accelerating upward.
- Total downward force the gripper must counter:
$$
F_{\text{down}} = W + \frac{W a}{g} = W \left( 1 + \frac{a}{g} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Resolve Each Normal Force into Vertical and Horizontal Components.**
Each jaw applies a normal force $F_N$ at angle $\theta$ from the horizontal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vertical component of one jaw} &= F_N \sin\theta \\
\text{Horizontal component of one jaw} &= F_N \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Vertical Components from All $n$ Contact Points.**
The total upward lifting force provided by friction (friction = $\mu \times$ normal) is:
$$
F_{\text{lift,total}} = n \cdot \mu \cdot F_N \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Static Equilibrium.**
The part will not slip if:
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{lift,total}} &\geq F_{\text{down}} \\[4pt]
n \cdot \mu \cdot F_N \cdot \sin\theta &\geq W \left( 1 + \frac{a}{g} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Required Normal Force $F_N$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_N &\geq \frac{W \left( 1 + \frac{a}{g} \right)}{n \cdot \mu \cdot \sin\theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the jaws are horizontal ($\theta = 90°$, so $\sin\theta = 1$), the equation simplifies to the textbook horizontal-jaw form:
$$
F_N \geq \frac{W \left( 1 + \frac{a}{g} \right)}{n \cdot \mu}
$$

**Step 6 — Include a Safety Factor $S$ (typically $S = 2$ to $3$).**
$$
\boxed{F_{N,\text{design}} = S \cdot \frac{W \left( 1 + \frac{a}{g} \right)}{n \cdot \mu \cdot \sin\theta}}
$$

This is the **design gripping force** engineers use to size the pneumatic cylinder, the spring, or the servo motor that closes the gripper.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Payback Period & ROI

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 14-mark):**
A company is evaluating a robotic material-handling cell.
- Robot cost: ₹ 8,00,000
- End-effector and fixtures: ₹ 2,00,000
- Safety system and enclosure: ₹ 1,50,000
- Installation and commissioning: ₹ 50,000
- Annual labor savings (replacing 2 workers): ₹ 6,00,000 per year
- Annual maintenance: ₹ 1,00,000 per year
- Annual energy cost: ₹ 50,000 per year

**Find:** (a) Total CAPEX. (b) Annual Net Savings. (c) Payback Period. (d) ROI over 5 years.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total CAPEX:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CAPEX} &= 8{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Annual Net Savings:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Savings} &= \text{Labor Savings} - \text{Maintenance} - \text{Energy} \\[4pt]
&= 6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Payback Period:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{\text{CAPEX}}{\text{Net Savings}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{4{,}50{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 2.67 \text{ years} \quad \approx \; 2 \text{ years } 8 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (d) — 5-Year ROI:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Benefit over 5 years} &= 5 \times 4{,}50{,}000 = 22{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Profit} &= 22{,}50{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{ROI (\%)} &= \frac{10{,}50{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verdict:** A payback period under 3 years and ROI of 87.5% over 5 years is considered **excellent** by KTU valuation standards. Such a project is approved.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DOF Requirements vs. Application

This matrix is the **single most high-yield table for Part A questions** in KTU exams.

| Application | Required DOF | Typical Reach | Typical Payload |
|-------------|--------------|---------------|-----------------|
| Arc Welding | 6 | 1.5 m | 6–10 kg |
| Spot Welding | 6 | 2.0 m | 100–200 kg |
| Palletizing | 4 | 2.5 m | 100–500 kg |
| Machine Tending | 6 | 1.8 m | 20–50 kg |
| Pick & Place | 4 | 0.7 m | 3–6 kg |
| Painting | 6 | 2.0 m | 8–15 kg |
| Assembly (PCB) | 4 | 0.5 m | 2–5 kg |
| Material Handling | 4–6 | 1.5 m | 10–50 kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Trick (for exams):**
> - **P**alletizing, **P**ick-and-place, **M**aterial handling → **4 DOF is enough** (just X, Y, Z, and one wrist rotation).
> - **A**rc welding, **A**ssembly, **S**pot welding, **P**ainting → **6 DOF** is mandatory (needs full orientation control).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Workcell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator HMI Panel] --> B[Safety PLC]
    B --> C[Robot Controller]
    C --> D[Servo Drive Pack]
    D --> E[Robot Manipulator]
    E --> F[End Effector Gripper]
    F --> G[Workpiece on Fixture]
    H[Conveyor Infeed] --> G
    G --> I[Conveyor Outfeed]
    J[Vision Sensor] --> C
    K[Force Torque Sensor] --> E
    L[Area Scanner] --> B
    M[E Stop Circuit] --> B
    N[Power Supply 3 Phase] --> D
    N --> B
    N --> C

    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#8c564b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style L fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style M fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red nodes** = Safety-critical subsystem (must comply with ISO 10218 / Category 3 / PL d).
- **Blue node** = Human-Machine Interface (the operator's only safe interaction point).
- **Green node** = Robot Controller (the "brain" running the teach pendant program).
- **Purple node** = Servo Drives (the "muscles" that move the joints).
- **Orange node** = Robot Manipulator (the "arm" itself).
- **Brown node** = End Effector (the "hand").

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: The Seven-Phase Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1: Feasibility Study] --> P2[Phase 2: Task Analysis and Specification]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Robot Selection]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: End Effector Design]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Workcell Layout and Simulation]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Safety System Design]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Economic Justification]
    P7 --> P8[Implementation and SAT]

    P1 -.Rejection Gate.-> X1[STOP Project if Infeasible]
    P3 -.Rejection Gate.-> X2[Re-evaluate Specs]
    P5 -.Rejection Gate.-> X3[Re-layout Workcell]
    P6 -.Rejection Gate.-> X4[Re-engineer Safety]

    style P1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P7 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P8 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** Each phase has a **gate** that can reject the project (return to an earlier phase) or proceed. This prevents a flawed design from advancing into expensive fabrication.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Tree: Robot Selection Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Robot Selection] --> A{Application Type}
    A -->|Material Handling| B[4 DOF Articulated or SCARA]
    A -->|Welding or Painting| C[6 DOF Articulated]
    A -->|High Speed Pick Place| D[Delta or SCARA]
    A -->|Heavy Palletizing| E[4 DOF Gantry or Heavy Articulated]

    B --> F{Required Reach}
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F -->|Less than 1 m| G[Small Reach Robot 0.5 to 1.0 m]
    F -->|1 to 2 m| H[Medium Reach Robot 1.0 to 2.0 m]
    F -->|Greater than 2 m| I[Long Reach Robot 2.0 to 3.5 m]

    G --> J{Required Payload}
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J -->|Less than 10 kg| K[Light Payload Class]
    J -->|10 to 100 kg| L[Medium Payload Class]
    J -->|Greater than 100 kg| M[Heavy Payload Class]

    K --> N[Final Vendor Selection]
    L --> N
    M --> N

    style S fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style N fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **work envelope** of a robot. List any two factors that affect its shape.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **work envelope** is the three-dimensional volume in space that can be swept by the robot's wrist center or end-effector, considering all possible joint motions and mechanical limits.

Two factors affecting its shape:
1. **Robot kinematic configuration** — articulated (jointed-arm), Cartesian (gantry), SCARA, cylindrical, or spherical each produce a different envelope shape.
2. **Mechanical joint limits** — physical stops and software limits on each joint restrict the maximum and minimum angles, deforming the envelope.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two factors.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
List any **four criteria** used in the selection of an industrial robot for an assembly workcell.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Payload capacity** — must exceed the part weight + gripper weight with a safety margin.
2. **Reach** — wrist must be able to access every required point in the work envelope.
3. **Repeatability** — must be tighter than the assembly tolerance.
4. **Number of axes (DOF)** — must equal the degrees of freedom required by the task.

*(Optional fifth for extra credit: vendor support, controller capability, cost, footprint.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> When answering 14-mark Part B questions on Robotic System Design, students **commonly lose marks** by:
> 1. Skipping the **Free-Body Diagram** in the gripper-force derivation — a mandatory step (2 marks lost).
> 2. Forgetting the **Safety Factor $S$** in the final gripper design equation — examiners deduct 1 full mark.
> 3. Confusing **Repeatability** with **Accuracy** — these are *different* physical quantities.
> 4. In economic questions, students forget to **subtract OPEX** (maintenance, energy) from gross labor savings before computing ROI. This alone loses 2–3 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option A]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, explain the **seven phases** of robotic system design. State one deliverable of each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic gripper with **two parallel jaws** is required to lift a steel component of mass **5 kg**. The jaws are inclined at **30°** to the horizontal, the coefficient of friction is **0.3**, and the robot accelerates the part upward at **2 m/s²**. Determine the **minimum gripping force** required per jaw, using a safety factor of **2.5**. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (a):**

The seven phases of robotic system design are:

1. **Phase 1 – Feasibility Study:** Determine if automation is economically and technically viable. *Deliverable: Feasibility Report.*
2. **Phase 2 – Task Analysis & Specification:** Decompose the operation into elemental motions; quantify payload, reach, accuracy, and cycle time. *Deliverable: Design Specification Document.*
3. **Phase 3 – Robot Selection:** Match specification to commercially available robots using a weighted selection matrix. *Deliverable: Robot Selection Justification Report.*
4. **Phase 4 – End-Effector & Tooling Design:** Design grippers, vacuum cups, or special tools; compute gripping force. *Deliverable: End-Effector Drawing & Force Calculation.*
5. **Phase 5 – Workcell Layout & Simulation:** Use 3-D CAD/offline programming to place robot, conveyors, and fixtures; verify reachability and collision-free motion. *Deliverable: 3-D Simulation Video & Layout Drawing.*
6. **Phase 6 – Safety System Design:** Conduct risk assessment per ISO 10218; design guards, interlocks, light curtains, and emergency stops. *Deliverable: Safety Validation Report.*
7. **Phase 7 – Economic Justification & Implementation:** Compute CAPEX, OPEX, payback period, and ROI; proceed to installation and Site Acceptance Test. *Deliverable: ROI Approval Memorandum.*

*(Award 1 mark per phase = 7 marks.)*

---

**Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Given:**
- Mass of part: $W_m = 5 \text{ kg}$, so weight $W = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N}$.
- Angle of jaws from horizontal: $\theta = 30°$.
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.3$.
- Upward acceleration: $a = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- Number of contact points (two jaws): $n = 2$.
- Safety factor: $S = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute the effective downward force including inertia:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{eff}} &= W \left( 1 + \frac{a}{g} \right) \\[4pt]
&= 49.05 \times \left( 1 + \frac{2}{9.81} \right) \\[4pt]
&= 49.05 \times 1.2039 \\[4pt]
&= 59.05 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the gripping force equation:**
$$
F_N \geq \frac{W (1 + a/g)}{n \cdot \mu \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_N &\geq \frac{59.05}{2 \times 0.3 \times \sin 30°} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{59.05}{2 \times 0.3 \times 0.5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{59.05}{0.3} \\[4pt]
&= 196.83 \text{ N per jaw (without safety factor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{N,\text{design}} &= S \times F_N \\[4pt]
&= 2.5 \times 196.83 \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{492.08 \text{ N per jaw}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Award marks: 1 for free-body diagram (verbal), 1 for effective force, 1 for substitution, 1 for sin θ, 1 for numerator, 1 for denominator, 1 for final answer with safety factor.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option B]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **repeatability** and **accuracy** of an industrial robot. Why is repeatability more important than accuracy in most assembly applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is automating a welding workcell. The total capital investment is **₹ 15,00,000**. The system replaces **3 manual welders**, each earning **₹ 4,00,000 per year** including benefits. Annual maintenance cost is **₹ 1,50,000**, and the energy cost is **₹ 50,000 per year**. Calculate the **(i) payback period** and **(ii) ROI over 4 years**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (a):**

| Aspect | Repeatability | Accuracy |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| **Definition** | Ability of the robot to return to a *previously taught* position | Ability of the robot to move to a *specified absolute* position in world coordinates |
| **What it measures** | Scatter / variation of positions around the mean | Offset between *commanded* and *actual* position |
| **Typical value** | ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm | ±1 mm to ±5 mm |
| **Calibrated by** | Mechanical precision, encoder resolution, backlash | Kinematic model, calibration software |
| **Drift with time** | Stable | Drifts if arm bends or temperature changes |

**Why repeatability matters more in assembly:**
In assembly cells, the robot is *taught* each position using a teach pendant. Once a point is taught, the robot only needs to return to it — it does not need to know its absolute Cartesian coordinates. Therefore, the **scatter** around that taught point (repeatability) determines whether the part fits the assembly. Accuracy is required only for off-line programming and for large work envelopes where reach errors accumulate.

*(Award 3 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for the definition of each, 2 marks for the reasoning.)*

---

**Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Given:**
- Total CAPEX = ₹ 15,00,000
- Labor savings = 3 × ₹ 4,00,000 = ₹ 12,00,000 per year
- Annual maintenance = ₹ 1,50,000
- Annual energy = ₹ 50,000
- Analysis period = 4 years

**Step 1 — Annual Net Savings:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Savings} &= 12{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 - 50{,}000 \\[4pt]
&= 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Payback Period:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{1.5 \text{ years}} \quad \text{(Excellent payback)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — 4-Year ROI:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Savings (4 years)} &= 4 \times 10{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Net Profit} &= 40{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{ROI (\%)} &= \frac{25{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \boxed{166.67\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** A 1.5-year payback and 166.67% ROI is **highly attractive** — this project will definitely be approved by management.

*(Award marks: 1 for net savings, 1 for payback formula, 1 for payback value, 1 for total savings, 1 for net profit, 1 for ROI formula, 1 for final ROI %.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Robotic System Design**

- ✅ Robotic System Design is about the **workcell**, not the robot arm itself.
- ✅ The **Seven Phases** are: Feasibility → Specification → Robot Selection → End-Effector → Layout → Safety → Economics.
- ✅ Always start with the **Three Pillars**: Performance, Economics, Safety.
- ✅ **Repeatability** ≠ **Accuracy** — repeatability is scatter around a taught point; accuracy is offset from the commanded point.
- ✅ **Payload** must include part weight **plus** gripper weight **plus** safety margin.
- ✅ **Reach** must cover the *farthest* point in the work envelope, not the average.
- ✅ **DOF rule of thumb**: 4 DOF for material handling/palletizing; 6 DOF for welding/assembly.
- ✅ Gripping force equation: $F_N = S \cdot \frac{W (1 + a/g)}{n \cdot \mu \cdot \sin\theta}$. **Never skip the safety factor $S$.**
- ✅ **Payback Period** = CAPEX / Annual Net Savings. Below **3 years** is approval-worthy.
- ✅ **ROI** = (Total Benefit − Investment) / Investment × 100.
- ✅ **ISO 10218** is the global standard for industrial robot safety; **ISO/TS 15066** is for collaborative robots.
- ✅ **Spherical work envelope volume**: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$.
- ✅ Always perform a **Risk Assessment** before energizing the cell.
- ✅ Common mark-losing traps: confusing repeatability/accuracy, skipping the safety factor, forgetting OPEX in ROI.
- ✅ In 14-mark questions, always draw the **Free-Body Diagram** of the gripped part — examiners award 2 marks just for that.
- ✅ Selection of robot uses a **weighted matrix** with criteria like payload, reach, repeatability, cost, and vendor support.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principles and Methodologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Principles and Methodologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems*, **Design Principles and Methodologies** refers to the systematic, structured set of engineering guidelines, decision frameworks, and procedural rules used to plan, evaluate, configure, and optimize a robotic workcell or automated handling system from concept to commissioning. This encompasses product design rules, process design, workstation layout, robot selection, tooling strategy, control architecture, and economic justification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> Design Principles and Methodologies form the *bridge* between product engineering and manufacturing engineering. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to justify a *design choice* (e.g., why a SCARA robot, why a 4-axis palletizer) using a structured methodology rather than a gut feeling.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a kitchen** for a busy restaurant.

- You do **not** randomly place the stove, sink, and refrigerator. Instead, you follow a *kitchen workflow triangle* (sink–stove–fridge) to minimize the chef's walking distance.
- You select appliances based on *menu volume*, *floor space*, and *ergonomics*.
- You standardize the height of all countertops so a single chef can use them without bending awkwardly.

In the same way, **robotic workcell design** is the *kitchen design* of manufacturing:

- The **robot** is the chef.
- The **workpiece / pallet** is the dish.
- The **gripper / end-effector** is the chef's hand.
- The **fixtures and conveyors** are the countertops and storage.
- The **design methodology** is the architectural rulebook that says: *"Place the heaviest parts closest to the robot base, use modular fixtures, and follow the shortest cycle-time path."*

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Design Methodology

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU answer on this topic must acknowledge the three intertwined pillars of robotic system design:

1. **Product Design for Assembly (DFA)** – Designing the *part* to be easily assembled.
2. **Process Design** – Designing the *sequence* of operations.
3. **Workcell / System Design** – Designing the *environment* in which the robot operates.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Terminology)

A robotic design is evaluated against the following **bold, syllabus-mandated** metrics:

- **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** – Time to complete one full operation sequence, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Throughput ($\lambda$)** – Units produced per unit time, measured in **parts per hour (PPH)** or **parts per minute (PPM)**.
- **Availability ($A$)** – Fraction of scheduled time the system is operational.
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** – Product of *Availability × Performance × Quality*.
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** – Economic metric, expressed in **percentage (%)** or **years of payback**.

## 1.5 Standard Industrial Communication Protocols Referenced

> [!NOTE]
> Although Module 3 emphasizes *design*, the 2024 syllabus expects awareness of these protocols when documenting control architecture in design reports:
> - **OPC UA** (Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture)
> - **PROFIBUS / PROFINET**
> - **EtherCAT**
> - **Modbus TCP / RTU**

These protocols are the *nervous system* of a designed workcell.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of the Design Process

A KTU-compliant design methodology follows a **top-down hierarchy**:

### Step 1: Mission Statement / Design Brief
- Identify the *product*, the *production volume*, and the *quality target*.
- Define inputs (raw parts, energy) and outputs (finished assemblies, scrap).

### Step 2: Functional Decomposition
- Break the assembly task into elementary operations: *pick, place, insert, fasten, inspect, eject*.
- Each elementary operation is called an **Assembly Primitive** in KTU 2024 Module 3 terminology.

### Step 3: Concept Generation
- Generate multiple layout concepts (e.g., **inline**, **rotary index table**, **carousel**, **linear transfer**).
- Apply the **Pahl & Beitz** systematic design methodology (German VDI 2221 standard).

### Step 4: Embodiment Design
- Select the actual robot model, gripper, sensors, and fixtures.
- Convert cycle-time targets into *motion profiles* (acceleration, velocity, jerk).

### Step 5: Detailed Design
- Generate **CAD layouts**, **Pneumatic / Electrical schematics**, and **PLC ladder logic**.
- Validate via **Digital Twin** simulation (e.g., Siemens Tecnomatix, ABB RobotStudio, Visual Components).

### Step 6: Economic & Ergonomic Validation
- Calculate **ROI**, **payback period**, and perform a **Manual Handling Operations Regulations (MHOR)** style ergonomic audit for human-robot collaboration (HRC) cells.

## 2.2 Design for Manufacture and Assembly (DFMA)

**DFMA = DFM + DFA**, where:
- **DFM** = Design for Manufacture – ease of *making* individual parts.
- **DFA** = Design for Assembly – ease of *putting together* the product.

The **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Index** is a key KTU exam metric:

$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_a}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $N_m$ = theoretical minimum number of parts.
- $t_a$ = average ideal assembly time per part (typically **3 seconds** per part in Boothroyd's original work).
- $t_{total}$ = total actual assembly time observed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **DFA Index below 30%** indicates a poor design that wastes assembly time. A value **above 60%** is considered world-class.

## 2.3 Robot Selection Methodology

The KTU-recommended **decision matrix** uses weighted scoring:

$$\text{Score}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$$

Where:
- $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$ (e.g., payload = 0.30, reach = 0.25, repeatability = 0.20, cost = 0.15, footprint = 0.10). Weights **must sum to 1.0**.
- $r_{ij}$ = rating of robot $i$ on criterion $j$ (typically 1 to 5 or 1 to 10).
- $n$ = number of criteria.

The robot with the **highest composite score** is selected.

## 2.4 Cycle Time Decomposition

$$T_c = T_{index} + T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{wait}$$

Where:
- $T_{index}$ = time to index/position the incoming part (conveyor or table).
- $T_{move}$ = robot motion time (pick + place).
- $T_{process}$ = active process time (welding, screwing, gluing).
- $T_{wait}$ = synchronization / safety wait time.

> [!NOTE]
> For **synchronous assembly lines**, $T_c$ is dictated by the *longest* station time (the **bottleneck**). For **asynchronous cells**, $T_c$ is the *robot's own* cycle time.

## 2.5 Throughput Calculation

$$\lambda = \frac{3600 \cdot \eta}{T_c} \quad [\text{parts/hour}]$$

Where:
- $T_c$ = cycle time in seconds.
- $\eta$ = overall efficiency factor (**typically 0.85 to 0.90** for well-designed cells, accounting for changeovers, minor stops, and quality checks).

## 2.6 Economic Justification (Payback Period)

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{C_{capex} - C_{savings\_annual}}{C_{savings\_annual}} \times 100\%$$

Or, in years:

$$N_{pb} = \frac{C_{capex}}{\Delta C_{annual}}$$

Where:
- $C_{capex}$ = total capital expenditure (robot + gripper + fixtures + integration + safety).
- $\Delta C_{annual}$ = net annual savings (labor cost − energy − maintenance).
- A payback **less than 3 years** is the typical KTU benchmark for approval.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every Part B question on this module.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_c = T_{index} + T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{wait}$ | Cycle time decomposition | s | 5 – 60 s |
| 2 | $\lambda = 3600 \cdot \eta / T_c$ | Throughput | parts/hr | 60 – 720 |
| 3 | $\text{DFA Index} = N_m t_a / t_{total} \times 100$ | Boothroyd assembly efficiency | % | 30 – 70 |
| 4 | $\text{Score}_i = \sum w_j r_{ij}$ | Robot selection weighted score | dimensionless | 1 – 5 |
| 5 | $N_{pb} = C_{capex} / \Delta C_{annual}$ | Payback period | years | 1 – 5 |
| 6 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | % | 60 – 85 |
| 7 | $F_{grip} = m(g + a)$ | Gripper holding force | N | depends on $m$ |
| 8 | $T_{move} = 2 \sqrt{s/a} + s/v_{max}$ | Trapezoidal motion time (approx.) | s | depends on $s$ |

> **Note on notation:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions in the above table are written with the LaTeX vertical bar `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive body shops** use the cycle-time decomposition to balance 80+ robotic welding stations.
- **Electronics manufacturers** (Foxconn, Samsung) use DFMA to design smartphones that can be assembled in under 90 seconds.
- **E-commerce warehouses** (Amazon, Flipkart) use throughput formulas to size fleets of autonomous mobile robots (AMRs).
- **Pharma packaging lines** use OEE to justify $C_{capex}$ for collaborative robots (cobots) replacing manual pick-and-place.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Cycle Time from a Trapezoidal Velocity Profile

A robot joint moving a payload through a straight-line distance $s$ typically follows a *trapezoidal velocity profile*: accelerate at $a$, cruise at $v_{max}$, then decelerate at $-a$.

**Step 1 — Identify the two regimes.**

If the distance is long enough, the profile reaches the cruise velocity $v_{max}$. The "triangular-to-trapezoidal" transition occurs at a critical distance:

$$s_{crit} = \frac{v_{max}^{2}}{a}$$

**Step 2 — Case A: Trapezoidal profile ($s \ge s_{crit}$).**

Acceleration time: $t_a = v_{max} / a$.

The total time is the sum of the acceleration phase, the cruise phase, and the deceleration phase (symmetrical):

$$T_{move} = 2 \cdot \frac{v_{max}}{a} + \frac{s - s_{crit}}{v_{max}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $s_{crit}$.**

$$T_{move} = \frac{2 v_{max}}{a} + \frac{s}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a}$$

$$T_{move} = \frac{v_{max}}{a} + \frac{s}{v_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Case B: Triangular profile ($s < s_{crit}$).**

The robot never reaches $v_{max}$. Peak velocity:

$$v_{peak} = \sqrt{a \cdot s}$$

Total move time:

$$T_{move} = 2 \cdot \frac{v_{peak}}{a} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{s}{a}}$$

**Step 5 — Unified compact formula.**

For quick estimation, KTU examiners accept:

$$T_{move} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{s}{a}} + \frac{s}{v_{max}}$$

The first term dominates for *short* moves; the second term dominates for *long* moves.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Throughput from Cycle Time

Starting from the intuitive definition:

$$\lambda = \frac{\text{number of parts}}{\text{time}}$$

If $N$ parts are produced in $N$ cycles, each lasting $T_c$ seconds, the total time in seconds is $N \cdot T_c$. Converting to hours by multiplying by $3600$ (since 1 hour = 3600 s):

$$\lambda = \frac{N \cdot 3600}{N \cdot T_c} = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad [\text{parts/hr}]$$

Introducing the efficiency factor $\eta$ to account for changeovers, micro-stops, and quality rejection:

$$\lambda = \frac{3600 \cdot \eta}{T_c} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 < \eta \le 1$$

**Numerical example (KTU style):**

A pick-and-place cell has $T_{index} = 1.5\,\text{s}$, $T_{move} = 2.0\,\text{s}$, $T_{process} = 0.5\,\text{s}$, $T_{wait} = 0.5\,\text{s}$. Total cycle:

$$T_c = 1.5 + 2.0 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 4.5\,\text{s}$$

Throughput at $\eta = 0.90$:

$$\lambda = \frac{3600 \times 0.90}{4.5} = \frac{3240}{4.5} = 720 \quad [\text{parts/hr}]$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Index

**Step 1 — Theoretical minimum number of parts ($N_m$).**

A part is *necessary* if it has a *function* that cannot be obtained by combining it with another part. We count only the essential parts.

**Step 2 — Theoretical minimum time ($t_m$).**

Each essential part requires an *ideal* handling and insertion time. Boothroyd's empirical data assigns:

$$t_a = 3\,\text{s per part (handling + insertion, ideal)}$$

**Step 3 — Observed total time ($t_{total}$).**

This is measured on the prototype assembly line, including *re-orientation*, *fastening*, *obstructions*, and *operator delay*.

**Step 4 — Compute the index.**

$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_a}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$$

**Interpretation band (high-yield for exams):**

- $\text{DFA Index} < 30\% \Rightarrow$ **Poor design** (too many parts, awkward assembly).
- $30\% \le \text{DFA Index} < 60\% \Rightarrow$ **Acceptable** design.
- $\text{DFA Index} \ge 60\% \Rightarrow$ **World-class** design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Gripper Holding Force

For a payload of mass $m$ being accelerated at $a$:

**Step 1 — Identify forces on the part.**

Weight: $W = m g$, where $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

Inertial force: $F_i = m a$.

Total downward (or lateral) force the gripper must resist:

$$F_{total} = m g + m a = m (g + a)$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the safety factor $n$ (typically 1.5 to 2.0).**

$$F_{grip} = n \cdot m (g + a)$$

**Step 3 — Friction-adjusted form (for friction grippers).**

If the gripper jaws have a coefficient of friction $\mu$ and a grip force $F_n$ each, the *frictional* holding force is $2 \mu F_n$. Equating:

$$2 \mu F_n = n \cdot m (g + a) \Rightarrow F_n = \frac{n \cdot m (g + a)}{2 \mu}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Multi-Criteria Robot Selection (Weighted Scoring)

**Step 1 — Define the criteria vector.**

Let $\mathbf{w} = [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n]$ with $\sum w_j = 1$.

**Step 2 — Define the rating matrix.**

For each robot $i$ and criterion $j$, assign $r_{ij} \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the composite score.**

$$\text{Score}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij} = \mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{R}_i$$

**Step 4 — Rank robots in descending order of $\text{Score}_i$ and select the top one.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always perform a **sensitivity analysis** — vary the weights by $\pm 10\%$ to confirm the ranking is *robust* and not driven by one over-weighted criterion.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix (Example Snapshot)

Suppose three robots A, B, C are scored on 4 criteria with weights $w = [0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.25]$:
