| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Robot A $r_{Aj}$ | Robot B $r_{Bj}$ | Robot C $r_{Cj}$ |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| Payload capacity | 0.30 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Reach | 0.25 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Repeatability | 0.20 | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Cost (inverse score) | 0.25 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

Composite scores:

$$\text{Score}_A = 0.30(4) + 0.25(5) + 0.20(4) + 0.25(3) = 1.20 + 1.25 + 0.80 + 0.75 = 4.00$$

$$\text{Score}_B = 0.30(5) + 0.25(3) + 0.20(4) + 0.25(4) = 1.50 + 0.75 + 0.80 + 1.00 = 4.05$$

$$\text{Score}_C = 0.30(3) + 0.25(4) + 0.20(5) + 0.25(5) = 0.90 + 1.00 + 1.00 + 1.25 = 4.15$$

**Conclusion:** Robot C wins (4.15 > 4.05 > 4.00), but only marginally over Robot B — a perfect setup for a *sensitivity analysis* discussion in a KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Workcell Design Methodology

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *interactions* of a robotic workcell design methodology, replacing an infeasible physical drawing of an industrial shop floor with a clean functional architecture flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Design Brief and Mission Statement] --> B0[Product Analysis and DFA]
    B0 --> C0[Process Planning and Assembly Sequence]
    C0 --> D0[Concept Generation Multiple Layouts]
    D0 --> E0[Embodiment Design Robot and Gripper Selection]
    E0 --> F0[Cycle Time and Throughput Estimation]
    F0 --> G0[Workcell Layout CAD and Simulation]
    G0 --> H0[Economic Justification ROI and Payback]
    H0 --> I0[Risk and Safety Analysis]
    I0 --> J0{Design Approved by Review Board}
    J0 -->|Yes| K0[Detailed Engineering and Commissioning]
    J0 -->|No| D0
    K0 --> L0[Production Handover and OEE Tracking]
    L0 --> M0[Continuous Improvement Kaizen Loop]
    M0 -.feedback.-> A0
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Each rectangular block is a *design phase* with well-defined inputs and outputs.
- The decision diamond **J0** is the *design review gate* — a standard stage in the **Stage-Gate** process used in KTU 2024 industry-aligned projects.
- The dashed **M0 → A0** feedback line represents the *Kaizen* / continuous-improvement loop: lessons from production feed back into the next product's design brief.

## 4.2 Workcell Layout Schematics — Three Canonical Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Inline Linear Transfer Layout]
        direction LR
        IN1[Inbound Conveyor] --> S1[Station 1 Robot A] --> S2[Station 2 Robot B] --> S3[Station 3 Robot C] --> OUT1[Outbound Conveyor]
    end
    subgraph T2[Rotary Index Table Layout]
        direction LR
        IN2[Part Loader] --> RT1[Index Table 4 Stations] --> RT2[Station Robots Above] --> OUT2[Unload Chute]
    end
    subgraph T3[Carousel Layout]
        direction LR
        IN3[Hopper] --> CA1[Rotating Carousel] --> CA2[Single Robot on Linear Slide] --> OUT3[Quality Gate]
    end
```

**Selection logic (high-yield):**

| Topology | Best For | Cycle Time Profile | Footprint |
|----------|----------|--------------------|-----------|
| Inline linear | High-volume, identical parts | Long, balanced | Large |
| Rotary index | Medium-volume, multiple ops per part | Short, fixed by table | Compact |
| Carousel | Low-volume, large parts | Medium, flexible | Medium |

## 4.3 Design Methodology Iteration Loop (Pahl & Beitz Inspired)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Clarify the Task] --> P2[Conceptual Design]
    P2 --> P3[Embodiment Design]
    P3 --> P4[Detail Design]
    P4 --> P5{Validation Pass}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Release to Manufacturing]
    P5 -->|No| P3
    P6 --> P7[Field Feedback]
    P7 -.iterate.-> P1
```

**Why this matters for KTU:**

- The examiner looks for the *iteration loop* — many students forget that design is **never linear**. Drawing a feedback arrow is worth **1 to 2 marks** in a 14-mark question.

## 4.4 Robot Selection Decision-Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the payload mass?}
    Q1 -->|lt 3 kg| R1[SCARA or Delta Robot]
    Q1 -->|3 to 20 kg| R2[6 Axis Articulated Arm]
    Q1 -->|20 to 200 kg| R3[Heavy Duty Articulated Arm]
    Q1 -->|gt 200 kg| R4[Gantry or Linear Robot]
    R1 --> S1[Verify reach and repeatability]
    R2 --> S1
    R3 --> S1
    R4 --> S1
    S1 --> S2[Check cycle time target]
    S2 --> S3[Run weighted scoring matrix]
    S3 --> S4[Select final robot]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The decision tree is a *first-pass filter* — it eliminates obviously unsuitable robot categories.
- The **weighted scoring matrix** is the *final tie-breaker* between two robots in the same category.
- This two-step approach (filter + score) is the **gold standard** in KTU-evaluated design projects.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the Boothroyd-Dewhurst Design for Assembly (DFA) Index. List the three design bands used to classify a product.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Definition: The Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Index is the ratio of the *theoretical minimum assembly time* to the *actual observed assembly time*, expressed as a percentage. Formula:

$$\text{DFA Index} = \frac{N_m \cdot t_a}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$$

- **(1 mark)** Where $N_m$ is the theoretical minimum number of parts, $t_a$ is the ideal handling-and-insertion time per part (3 s), and $t_{total}$ is the total observed assembly time.
- **(1 mark)** Three bands: **Poor** (DFA < 30%), **Acceptable** (30% ≤ DFA < 60%), and **World-class** (DFA ≥ 60%).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any four design metrics that must be reported in a robotic workcell design proposal as per the KTU 2024 Scheme.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** in seconds — total time per assembly.
2. **Throughput ($\lambda$)** in parts/hour — production rate.
3. **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** in percent — availability × performance × quality.
4. **Payback Period ($N_{pb}$)** in years — economic justification.
5. *(Bonus 0.5 mark)* **DFA Index** in percent — assembly friendliness.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) Module-3 questions follow the **internal choice** pattern. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the **hierarchical design methodology** for a robotic assembly workcell. Discuss the role of the **design review gate** in this methodology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic palletizing cell must move 800 parts per hour. The conveyor index time is 1.2 s, the robot motion time is 1.8 s, the process time is 0.6 s, and synchronization wait is 0.4 s. Determine (i) the actual cycle time, (ii) the throughput at an efficiency of 0.90, and (iii) comment on whether the design meets the target. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- **(2 marks)** The hierarchical design methodology consists of six sequential phases:
  1. Mission statement / design brief
  2. Functional decomposition
  3. Concept generation
  4. Embodiment design
  5. Detailed design
  6. Economic and ergonomic validation
- **(2 marks)** Each phase produces a *deliverable* (a document, a CAD model, a simulation report) that is reviewed at the **design review gate** — see the diagram in Section 4.1 (block J0). If the gate rejects the deliverable, the design loops back to concept generation.
- **(2 marks)** The review gate enforces **Stage-Gate discipline**: cost, cycle time, safety, and quality targets are *formally* checked before the next phase is funded. This prevents *over-engineering* and *gold-plating*.
- **(1 mark)** Continuous feedback from production (Kaizen loop) re-enters the mission statement for the next-generation product.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- **(2 marks)** Cycle time:

$$T_c = T_{index} + T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{wait} = 1.2 + 1.8 + 0.6 + 0.4 = 4.0\,\text{s}$$

- **(2 marks)** Throughput:

$$\lambda = \frac{3600 \cdot \eta}{T_c} = \frac{3600 \times 0.90}{4.0} = \frac{3240}{4.0} = 810 \quad [\text{parts/hr}]$$

- **(2 marks)** Comparison with target:
  - Required: 800 parts/hour
  - Achieved: 810 parts/hour
  - **Conclusion:** The design *meets* the target with a **10 parts/hour margin** (~1.25% safety). This is a *very tight* margin — the designer should attempt to reduce $T_{wait}$ to at least 0.2 s, raising throughput to:

$$\lambda_{improved} = \frac{3600 \times 0.90}{3.8} \approx 853 \quad [\text{parts/hr}]$$

- **(1 mark)** Recommendation: shorten synchronization wait via better sensor placement, and consider upgrading the conveyor indexer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **multi-criteria weighted scoring method** for robot selection. Use a worked example with three candidate robots and four criteria to illustrate the procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SCARA robot must pick a part of mass 2 kg from a moving conveyor. The gripper jaws have a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$ and the design safety factor is $n = 1.8$. The maximum acceleration is $a = 4\,\text{m/s}^2$. Determine (i) the total inertial-plus-gravitational force on the gripper, (ii) the required normal grip force per jaw, and (iii) suggest two design changes to reduce the grip force requirement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- **(2 marks)** Introduction: The weighted scoring method assigns a *weight* $w_j$ to each design criterion (payload, reach, repeatability, cost, footprint, etc.) with $\sum w_j = 1$. Each candidate robot is rated $r_{ij}$ on a fixed scale (1 to 5). The composite score is:

$$\text{Score}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$$

- **(2 marks)** Worked example (use the same table from Section 3.6 of these notes): three robots A, B, C, four criteria with weights [0.30, 0.25, 0.20, 0.25], and ratings as tabulated. Compute the composite scores:

$$\text{Score}_A = 4.00, \quad \text{Score}_B = 4.05, \quad \text{Score}_C = 4.15$$

- **(2 marks)** Selection: Robot **C** wins with 4.15. However, the margin over Robot B (0.10) is small, so a *sensitivity analysis* is recommended.
- **(1 mark)** Sensitivity: increase the *cost* weight from 0.25 to 0.35 and recompute. If Robot C remains on top, the selection is *robust*. Otherwise, fall back to Robot B for lower integration risk.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- **(2 marks)** Total inertial-plus-gravitational force:

$$F_{total} = m (g + a) = 2 \times (9.81 + 4) = 2 \times 13.81 = 27.62\,\text{N}$$

- **(2 marks)** Required normal force per jaw:

$$F_n = \frac{n \cdot m (g + a)}{2 \mu} = \frac{1.8 \times 27.62}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{49.72}{0.8} = 62.15\,\text{N}$$

- **(2 marks)** Two design changes to reduce the grip force:
  1. **Use a *form-closure* gripper** (mechanical hooks, notches, or vacuum cup) instead of a friction gripper — this eliminates the dependence on $\mu$ and allows a much lower $F_n$.
  2. **Reduce acceleration** by smoothing the robot trajectory (S-curve profile instead of trapezoidal) and reducing the conveyor speed, thereby lowering $a$ from 4 to 1 m/s². Recomputing:

$$F_n^{new} = \frac{1.8 \times 2 \times (9.81 + 1)}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{1.8 \times 21.62}{0.8} = 48.65\,\text{N}$$

This is a **~22% reduction** in the required grip force, prolonging pneumatic seal life.

- **(1 mark)** Conclusion: with either change, the pneumatic cylinder bore size can be reduced, lowering both capex and energy consumption.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the efficiency factor $\eta$** when calculating throughput. Always multiply by 0.85 to 0.90 unless told otherwise. Students who forget $\eta$ lose **1 to 2 marks**.
> 2. **Not drawing a feedback loop** in the design methodology diagram. KTU's Stage-Gate emphasis means a *one-way* arrow diagram is incomplete. Always add a dashed feedback arrow from the last phase back to the mission statement.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — cycle time is in *seconds*, throughput is in *parts/hour*. Forgetting the 3600 conversion factor is the single most common error in numerical questions.
> 4. **Stating DFA bands incorrectly** — the standard Boothroyd bands are 30% / 60%. Some students write 25% / 50%, which is wrong and costs the *definition mark*.
> 5. **Skipping the sensitivity analysis** in the robot selection question. The 2024 scheme specifically rewards students who discuss the *robustness* of their selection.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Module 3 — Design Principles and Methodologies:**

- [ ] The three pillars of robotic system design are **Product (DFA)**, **Process (sequence)**, and **Workcell (layout)**.
- [ ] The **Boothroyd-Dewhurst DFA Index** uses $N_m$, $t_a = 3\,\text{s}$, and $t_{total}$ in its formula. Bands: **< 30% poor, 30–60% acceptable, ≥ 60% world-class**.
- [ ] The **cycle time decomposition** is $T_c = T_{index} + T_{move} + T_{process} + T_{wait}$.
- [ ] The **throughput formula** is $\lambda = 3600 \eta / T_c$, with $\eta$ typically **0.85 to 0.90**.
- [ ] The **weighted scoring method** for robot selection requires $\sum w_j = 1$, ratings on a fixed scale, and a sensitivity analysis.
- [ ] The **gripper holding force** formula is $F_n = n m (g + a) / (2 \mu)$ for a friction gripper; **form-closure** and **smoother trajectories** reduce required grip force.
- [ ] The **Pahl & Beitz methodology** has four phases: clarify task → conceptual → embodiment → detail, with iteration back to earlier phases.
- [ ] The **Stage-Gate process** is enforced at the design review gate; always include a feedback loop in your diagram.
- [ ] The **payback period** formula is $N_{pb} = C_{capex} / \Delta C_{annual}$, with a benchmark of **< 3 years** for approval.
- [ ] The **OEE** metric is the product of availability, performance, and quality — target **≥ 85%** for world-class cells.
- [ ] Standard **workcell topologies** are *inline linear*, *rotary index*, and *carousel*; selection depends on volume, part size, and flexibility needs.
- [ ] **DFMA = DFM + DFA** — design for manufacture *and* design for assembly, evaluated together.
- [ ] For trapezoidal motion, $T_{move} = 2\sqrt{s/a} + s/v_{max}$ is the unified KTU-accepted approximation.
- [ ] The **DFA Index** is the *only* KTU-graded metric that *rewards* the designer — a higher number means a *better* product.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions** (e.g., $\eta = 0.90$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$, $t_a = 3\,\text{s}$) at the top of any numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Design and Selection of Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Design and Selection of Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MECHANICAL DESIGN AND SELECTION OF COMPONENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Mechanical Design and Selection of Components** in the context of robotic assembly and material handling systems refers to the systematic engineering process of determining the geometry, kinematics, material, load-bearing capacity, and operational reliability of the structural and actuated members of a robot, while simultaneously choosing suitable off-the-shelf or custom-fabricated components (such as actuators, bearings, gears, couplings, sensors, and structural frames) that satisfy the functional, economic, and safety requirements dictated by the application.

In simple words, this topic answers the following practical questions that every robotic system designer must address **before** fabricating or purchasing anything:

1. **Will the robot arm break** when it lifts the rated payload? (Strength / Stress)
2. **Will the motor be powerful enough** to move the joint at the desired speed? (Power / Torque)
3. **Which bearing, gear, or actuator** is the most cost-effective choice for the working cycle? (Selection)
4. **How long will the components last** under continuous operation? (Fatigue / Life)
5. **Is the design safe** if the load is doubled accidentally? (Factor of Safety)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Body"**
> Think of a robotic system as a **human body lifting a heavy box**:
> - The **bones** = structural links (need strength analysis).
> - The **muscles** = actuators (need force/power selection).
> - The **joints** = bearings and revolute/prismatic pairs (need life and friction analysis).
> - The **nerves** = sensors (need signal and range selection).
> - The **doctor's prescription** = the design procedure that verifies all of the above before you commit to building the "body."

### 1.1 Scope of Mechanical Design in Robotics

The mechanical design of a robotic manipulator or material-handling system covers the following sub-domains, all of which are part of **KTU Module 3**:

| Sub-Domain | What It Decides | Example Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Design** | Geometry, cross-section, material of links | Arm links, base, column |
| **Actuator Selection** | Type, size, rating of drive element | Servo motors, stepper motors, hydraulic cylinders, pneumatic cylinders |
| **Transmission Selection** | How motion/torque is delivered | Harmonic drives, planetary gears, RV reducers, belt drives |
| **Bearing Selection** | Support for rotating/sliding members | Ball bearings, roller bearings, bushings |
| **End-Effector Design** | Gripper or tool geometry | Parallel jaw gripper, vacuum gripper, magnetic gripper |
| **Sensor Integration** | Position, force, vision feedback | Encoders, load cells, proximity sensors, cameras |
| **Material Selection** | Suitability for environment/load | Aluminium alloys, steel, composites, engineering plastics |

### 1.2 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Board-Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following standard metrics — they appear in **almost every KTU numerical**:
> - **Factor of Safety (FoS)** = $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$
> - **Density of Steel** = **7850 kg/m³**
> - **Density of Aluminium** = **2700 kg/m³**
> - **Modulus of Elasticity of Steel** = **200 GPa**
> - **Modulus of Elasticity of Aluminium** = **70 GPa**
> - **Standard gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - **Bearing Basic Life Equation (ISO 281)** uses exponent $p = 3$ for ball bearings and $p = \dfrac{10}{3}$ for roller bearings.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Stage Mechanical Design Procedure

The design of any robotic mechanical system follows a logical four-stage procedure that examiners love to test as a **5-mark short answer**:

1. **Conceptual / Functional Design** — Define what the robot must do (payload, workspace, speed, accuracy, environment).
2. **Configuration / Kinematic Design** — Choose number of DOF, type of joints (R, P), and arrangement (articulated, SCARA, Cartesian, delta, cylindrical).
3. **Component Selection and Sizing** — Select actuators, bearings, gears, sensors; size them using strength, power, life equations.
4. **Detailed / Production Design** — Finalize tolerances, surface finish, materials, manufacturing process, assembly drawings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this order matters:**
> Skipping straight to "buying a motor" without defining payload and workspace is the **#1 mistake** flagged in KTU valuation. The mark scheme always awards partial credit to students who clearly show the conceptual stage.

### 2.2 Mechanical Loads Acting on a Robot Link

A single link in a robotic arm typically experiences the following loads simultaneously:

- **Axial Force (F_a)** — due to weight of payload and downstream links.
- **Bending Moment (M_b)** — due to the moment arm of the payload.
- **Torsional Moment (T)** — due to actuation torque transmitted along the link.
- **Shear Force (V)** — transverse reaction at supports.
- **Dynamic / Inertial Loads** — during acceleration and deceleration of motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the most commonly tested combined loading case is **Bending + Torsion** on a robotic arm link. The relevant equivalent stress is given by the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Guest's Theory)** or the **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises)**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is your **one-page cheat sheet** for this module. Every equation below has appeared (or is statistically very likely to appear) in KTU University Exam papers for the 2024 Scheme.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Factor of Safety | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | dimensionless | General strength check |
| 2 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{M_b}{Z}$ | Pa | Link strength |
| 3 | Torsional Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ | Pa | Shaft strength |
| 4 | Polar Section Modulus (solid shaft) | $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | m³ | Shaft design |
| 5 | Bending Section Modulus (solid shaft) | $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{32}$ | m³ | Shaft design |
| 6 | Von Mises Equivalent Stress (bending + torsion) | $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{\,2} + 3\tau^{2}}$ | Pa | Combined loading |
| 7 | Power transmitted by rotating shaft | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | Motor selection |
| 8 | Torque from Force and Arm | $T = F \cdot r$ | N·m | Static torque check |
| 9 | Linear Actuator Force | $F = P_{line} \cdot A_{piston} = P_{line} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | N | Pneumatic / hydraulic |
| 10 | Hydraulic Cylinder Speed | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | Cycle-time check |
| 11 | Gripper Holding Force (friction) | $F_{hold} = \mu \cdot F_{grip} \cdot n_{fingers}$ | N | Gripper design |
| 12 | Gear Train Ratio | $i = \dfrac{N_{driven}}{N_{driver}} = \dfrac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}} = \dfrac{T_{out}}{T_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Speed reduction |
| 13 | ISO Bearing Basic Life (ball) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3}$ | million revs | Bearing selection |
| 14 | ISO Bearing Life (roller) | $L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{10/3}$ | million revs | Bearing selection |
| 15 | Belt / Pulley Velocity | $v = \pi d n$ | m/s | Belt drive check |
| 16 | Inertia of a Point Mass | $I = m r^2$ | kg·m² | Dynamic torque |
| 17 | Inertia of a Link (about joint) | $I = \dfrac{m L^2}{3}$ | kg·m² | Arm dynamics |
| 18 | Torque to Accelerate Inertia | $T = I \alpha$ | N·m | Motor sizing |
| 19 | Combined Stress (axial + bending) | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{F}{A} + \dfrac{M_b}{Z}$ | Pa | Column-link |
| 20 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k}$ | dimensionless | Buckling check |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Marking Trap:** In the table above, notice the **two different exponents** in the bearing life equation (3 for ball, 10/3 for roller). Writing $\dfrac{10}{3}$ as $3.33$ without showing the fraction will **cost you a half-mark** because the symbolic form is required.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Importance

The mechanical design and selection procedure is the **backbone** of every industrial robot you have ever seen — from the KUKA KR 16 welding robots in automotive assembly lines to the FANUC SCARA robots that pick-and-place chips on printed circuit boards. Without this systematic design process:

- **Automotive assembly lines** would not be able to lift car-body panels without catastrophic arm failure.
- **Palletizing robots** in warehouses would over- or under-size their drive motors, leading to energy waste or stall conditions.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots)** would not meet the strict ISO/TS 15066 safety standards for human contact.
- **Space and surgical robots** would suffer from incorrect material choice (e.g., wrong biocompatible alloy), making them unusable in sterile or vacuum environments.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gripper Holding Force from First Principles

> **Problem Setup (typical KTU numerical):**
> A parallel-jaw gripper is used to lift a payload of mass $m = 5 \text{ kg}$. The coefficient of friction between the jaw and the object is $\mu = 0.3$. The gripper has $n = 2$ fingers (one on each side). Find the minimum gripping force $F_g$ required on **each** jaw so that the payload does not slip. Take a safety factor $n_{sf} = 2$.

**Step 1 — Draw the free body diagram of the payload vertically.**

The forces acting on the payload are:
- Weight acting downward: $W = m g$
- Frictional (holding) force acting upward: $F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_{normal}$

Since the gripper has two fingers pressing on the payload, the total normal force pressing the payload is:

$$
F_{normal,total} = 2 \cdot F_g
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the static equilibrium condition (no slipping):**

For the payload to be held in place, the upward frictional force must balance the downward weight:

$$
F_{friction} = W
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $F_g$:**

$$
\mu \cdot F_{normal,total} = m g
$$

$$
\mu \cdot (2 F_g) = m g
$$

$$
F_g = \dfrac{m g}{2 \mu}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numerical values:**

$$
F_g = \dfrac{5 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.3} = \dfrac{49.05}{0.6} = 81.75 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the safety factor $n_{sf} = 2$:**

$$
F_{g,design} = n_{sf} \cdot F_g = 2 \times 81.75 = 163.5 \text{ N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (model solution):** The minimum gripping force on **each** jaw of the gripper must be $F_{g,design} = 163.5 \text{ N}$. This force must be supplied by the gripper actuator (pneumatic cylinder, servo motor with leadscrew, or spring-loaded toggle mechanism).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Torque and Power Sizing for a Vertical-Axis Joint

> **Problem Setup:**
> A robotic joint rotates at a constant speed $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$ and must lift an effective torque load of $T_{load} = 50 \text{ N·m}$. Find the rated power of the motor and the required motor-frame size.

**Step 1 — Use the power–torque–speed relation:**

$$
P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}
$$

where $N$ is in rpm, $T$ is in N·m, and $P$ comes out in Watts.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
P = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 60 \times 50}{60}
$$

$$
P = 2 \pi \times 50 = 100 \pi
$$

$$
P \approx 314.16 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a service factor $f_s = 1.5$ (typical for industrial robots, to account for acceleration, friction, and shock loads):**

$$
P_{motor,rated} = f_s \cdot P = 1.5 \times 314.16 = 471.24 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to horsepower for catalog selection (optional):**

$$
P_{hp} = \dfrac{471.24}{745.7} \approx 0.63 \text{ hp}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Catalog Selection:** From a standard motor catalog, the designer would select the next standard size up — typically a **0.75 hp (≈ 560 W)** AC servo motor or BLDC motor with a matching gear reducer (e.g., 10:1 or 20:1) depending on the desired joint speed.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shaft Diameter from Combined Bending and Torsion

> **Problem Setup:**
> A solid circular shaft of a robot's wrist joint is subjected to a bending moment $M_b = 200 \text{ N·m}$ and a torsional moment $T = 150 \text{ N·m}$. The shaft material has an ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ut} = 400 \text{ MPa}$. Using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST) with a factor of safety $n = 2$, find the minimum required shaft diameter $d$.

**Step 1 — Write the bending stress and torsional shear stress in terms of diameter $d$:**

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b}{Z} = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{T}{Z_p} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Maximum Shear Stress Theory:**

For a shaft under combined bending and torsion, the maximum shear stress is:

$$
\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}
$$

> For pure bending + pure torsion, MSST gives the equivalent **normal** stress form:
> $\sigma_{eq,MSST} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{\,2} + 4 \tau^{2}}$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions for $\sigma_b$ and $\tau$ in terms of $d$:**

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + 4 \left(\dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common term $\dfrac{1}{\pi d^{3}}$:**

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{(32 M_b)^{2} + 4(16 T)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerical value under the square root:**

$$
(32 \times 200)^{2} = (6400)^{2} = 40{,}960{,}000
$$

$$
4(16 \times 150)^{2} = 4(2400)^{2} = 4 \times 5{,}760{,}000 = 23{,}040{,}000
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 40{,}960{,}000 + 23{,}040{,}000 = 64{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\sqrt{64{,}000{,}000} = 8000
$$

So:

$$
\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{8000}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the design condition using factor of safety:**

$$
\sigma_{eq} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{n}
$$

$$
\dfrac{8000}{\pi d^{3}} \leq \dfrac{400 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 200 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $d$:**

$$
\pi d^{3} \geq \dfrac{8000}{200 \times 10^{6}} = 40 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
d^{3} \geq \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi} = 12.732 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
d \geq \sqrt[3]{12.732 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.02336 \text{ m}
$$

$$
d \geq 23.36 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8 — Round up to a standard shaft size:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer (model solution):** The minimum required shaft diameter is **24 mm** (next standard metric size above 23.36 mm). For safety against stress concentrations at keyways, designers often go to **25 mm** or **30 mm**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bearing Life Calculation (Ball Bearing)

> **Problem Setup:**
> A deep-groove ball bearing supports a radial load $P = 5 \text{ kN}$ on a robot's rotating joint. The basic dynamic load rating from the manufacturer's catalog is $C = 25 \text{ kN}$. The joint runs at $n = 1500 \text{ rpm}$ for 8 hours per day, 250 days per year. Find the expected bearing life $L_{10h}$ in hours.

**Step 1 — Use the ISO 281 basic life equation for ball bearings (exponent $p = 3$):**

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} \quad \text{(million revolutions)}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the ratio and cube it:**

$$
\dfrac{C}{P} = \dfrac{25{,}000}{5{,}000} = 5
$$

$$
L_{10} = 5^{3} = 125 \text{ million revolutions}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to hours of operation:**

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{60 \times n}
$$

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1500} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^{6}}{90{,}000} = 1388.9 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years of operation:**

$$
L_{10,years} = \dfrac{1388.9}{8 \times 250} = \dfrac{1388.9}{2000} = 0.694 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Note:** A life of less than 1 year is **unacceptable** for an industrial robot. The KTU board expects you to recommend increasing the bearing size (or reducing the radial load) so that $L_{10h}$ exceeds, say, **20,000–40,000 hours** (i.e., 5–10 years of typical industrial duty).

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Mechanical Design Procedure for a Robotic System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Design Problem] --> A1[Define Functional Requirements: Payload, Workspace, Speed, Accuracy]
    A1 --> A2[Select Robot Configuration: Articulated / SCARA / Cartesian / Delta]
    A2 --> A3[Kinematic Analysis: DH Parameters, Workspace Plot]
    A3 --> A4[Static Force and Torque Analysis at Each Joint]
    A4 --> A5[Dynamic Analysis: Inertia, Acceleration Torque]
    A5 --> B1[Structural Design of Links: Material, Cross-Section, Stress Check]
    A5 --> B2[Actuator Selection: Motor / Cylinder Sizing]
    A5 --> B3[Transmission Selection: Gearbox / Harmonic Drive / Belt]
    A5 --> B4[Bearing Selection: Life and Load Capacity]
    A5 --> B5[End Effector Design: Gripper / Tool]
    A5 --> B6[Sensor Selection: Encoder, Force, Vision]
    B1 --> C1[Integrated 3D CAD Model]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B4 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B6 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Prototype Fabrication and Testing]
    D1 --> D2[Design Validation and Iteration]
    D2 --> E1[Final Production Design]
    E1 --> F1[END: Certified Robot]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Component Selection Decision Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Component Selection Input: Load, Speed, Environment] --> S1{Is the Motion Rotary or Linear?}
    S1 -- Rotary --> S2{Is High Precision Required?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Servo Motor + Harmonic Drive Reducer]
    S2 -- No --> S4[Stepper Motor with Open Loop]
    S1 -- Linear --> S5{Force Magnitude?}
    S5 -- Low to Medium --> S6[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    S5 -- High --> S7[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    S5 -- High Precision --> S8[Servo Motor with Leadscrew or Ballscrew]
    S3 --> S9[Add Bearing: Ball Bearing for Radial, Roller for Combined Load]
    S4 --> S9
    S6 --> S9
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Add Sensor: Encoder for Position, Load Cell for Force]
    S10 --> S11[Output: Final Bill of Materials]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Combined Loading on a Robotic Arm Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Link Origin at Joint A] --> L1[Link Body of Length L]
    L1 --> L2[Payload P at End of Link]
    L0 -.Torsional Torque T around link axis.-> L1
    L0 -.Bending Moment Mb due to payload.-> L1
    L0 -.Shear Force V due to payload weight.-> L1
    L0 -.Axial Force Fa due to gravity of own link.-> L1
    L1 --> L3[Critical Cross-Section at Root of Link near Joint A]
    L3 --> L4[Check Combined Stress using Von Mises or MSST]
    L4 --> L5{Is Design Safe?}
    L5 -- No --> L6[Increase Cross-Section or Change Material]
    L5 -- Yes --> L7[Finalised Link Design]
    L6 --> L3
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — End-Effector (Gripper) Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[End Effector Subsystem] --> G1[Gripper Body: Aluminium or Steel Frame]
    G0 --> G2[Fingers / Jaws: Hardened Tool Steel, Rubber Pads]
    G0 --> G3[Actuator: Pneumatic Cylinder / Servo with Leadscrew]
    G0 --> G4[Force Transmission: Toggle Mechanism / Cam / Linkage]
    G0 --> G5[Sensors: Force Sensor on Jaw, Proximity Sensor for Object Detection]
    G0 --> G6[Mounting Flange: ISO 9409 Standard Robot Tool Flange]
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G2
    G5 --> G0
    G6 --> G0
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Explain any three important criteria used in the selection of actuators for robotic assembly systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point, with clear examples):**

1. **Load Capacity and Speed Requirements:** The actuator must be able to provide the required torque or force at the required speed. For high-precision, high-speed pick-and-place, **servo motors** are preferred; for high-force, low-speed heavy lifting, **hydraulic cylinders** are chosen. (1 mark)

2. **Control Characteristics:** The ease and accuracy of position, velocity, and force control are crucial. Servo and stepper motors offer excellent controllability, while pneumatic cylinders are typically limited to simple open-loop on/off control. (1 mark)

3. **Environmental Suitability and Maintenance:** Pneumatic actuators are clean and safe for food/pharma applications; hydraulic actuators are robust for heavy industrial use; electric motors are best for clean-room and explosion-proof environments when properly enclosed. (1 mark)

> [!TIP]
> For full marks, always add a one-line example of the **industrial application** for each criterion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
**Define factor of safety. Why is it especially important in the mechanical design of robotic manipulators?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Factor of Safety (FoS) is the ratio of the ultimate (or yield) strength of a material to the working (or allowable) stress it is subjected to in service:
$$
n = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}
$$
- **Importance in robotics (1.5 marks):**
  1. Robotic manipulators undergo **dynamic, variable, and often unpredictable loads** (acceleration, deceleration, impact) that are not present in static structures.
  2. They operate in **mixed environments** (temperature, dust, vibration) that can degrade material properties.
  3. **Human safety** is paramount — a structural failure can cause injury, loss of expensive equipment, or production downtime, so a typical FoS of **2 to 4** is recommended for robotic links, and **4 to 10** for lifting elements.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, every Part B question carries **internal choice**. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both full sets are provided for practice.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Paper]**

**A parallel-jaw gripper is used in a pick-and-place robot to handle aluminium blocks of mass 3 kg. The coefficient of friction between the rubber-padded jaws and the block surface is 0.4. The gripper is actuated by a pneumatic cylinder of bore 40 mm and supply pressure 5 bar. The cylinder drives a toggle mechanism that amplifies the gripping force by a factor of 3.**

**(a)** Calculate the minimum gripping force required per jaw, assuming a factor of safety of 2.5. Verify whether the pneumatic cylinder can develop this force. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure for selecting a deep-groove ball bearing for the wrist joint of a 6-axis robot arm. The joint rotates at 1200 rpm under a radial load of 3 kN. The available bearing has a basic dynamic load rating of 15 kN. Calculate the expected bearing life in hours. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (a) (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Payload mass $m = 3 \text{ kg}$
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$
- Pneumatic bore $D = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- Supply pressure $P_{line} = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$
- Toggle amplification factor $k = 3$
- Factor of safety $n_{sf} = 2.5$

**Step 1 — Theoretical minimum gripping force per jaw (no FoS):**

From the earlier derivation:

$$
F_g = \dfrac{m g}{2 \mu} = \dfrac{3 \times 9.81}{2 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{29.43}{0.8} = 36.79 \text{ N}
$$

**(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical substitution and answer)**

**Step 2 — Apply factor of safety:**

$$
F_{g,design} = n_{sf} \cdot F_g = 2.5 \times 36.79 = 91.97 \text{ N}
$$

**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 3 — Theoretical force developed by pneumatic cylinder:**

The piston area:

$$
A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.04)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.0016}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

The cylinder force:

$$
F_{cyl} = P_{line} \times A_{piston} = 5 \times 10^{5} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 628.3 \text{ N}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Force available at the jaw after toggle amplification:**

The toggle mechanism multiplies the cylinder force by 3, so the force that actually pushes each jaw is:

$$
F_{g,available} = k \cdot F_{cyl} = 3 \times 628.3 = 1885 \text{ N}
$$

**(0.5 mark)**

**Step 5 — Verification:**

Since $F_{g,available} = 1885 \text{ N} \gg F_{g,design} = 91.97 \text{ N}$, the pneumatic cylinder is **more than sufficient** to grip the block with the required safety margin.

**(0.5 mark for conclusion + 1 mark for writing verification statement explicitly)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.A (b) (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Speed $n = 1200 \text{ rpm}$
- Radial load $P = 3 \text{ kN} = 3000 \text{ N}$
- Basic dynamic load rating $C = 15 \text{ kN} = 15000 \text{ N}$

**Step 1 — Bearing Selection Procedure (3 marks, 1 mark per point + sketch 0.5 mark, organised as written):**

The standard procedure for selecting a deep-groove ball bearing for a robot joint is:
1. **Determine the applied loads** (radial and axial) on the bearing from static and dynamic force analysis of the joint.
2. **Compute the equivalent dynamic load $P$** using ISO 76:
$$
P = X F_r + Y F_a
$$
For a deep-groove ball bearing with negligible axial load, $X = 1, Y = 0$, so $P = F_r$.
3. **Estimate the desired design life** in hours (e.g., 20,000 hours for industrial robots) and convert to millions of revolutions:
$$
L_{10,req} = \dfrac{60 \times n \times L_{10h}}{10^{6}}
$$
4. **Calculate the required basic dynamic load rating:**
$$
C_{req} = P \cdot (L_{10,req})^{1/3}
$$
5. **Select the smallest standard bearing from the catalog** with $C \geq C_{req}$ and verify dimensions, lubrication requirements, and sealing.

**Step 2 — Apply the procedure to given numerical data (4 marks):**

- The actual life of the available bearing:

$$
L_{10} = \left(\dfrac{C}{P}\right)^{3} = \left(\dfrac{15{,}000}{3{,}000}\right)^{3} = 5^{3} = 125 \text{ million revolutions}
$$

**(1 mark)**

- Convert to hours of operation:

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{60 \times n} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^{6}}{60 \times 1200}
$$

$$
L_{10h} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^{6}}{72{,}000} = 1736.1 \text{ hours}
$$

**(1 mark)**

- The bearing life is approximately **1736 hours**.

**(0.5 mark for answer statement)**

- **Design Recommendation:** This is lower than the typical 20,000-hour industrial design life. The designer should either (a) select a bearing with a higher $C$ rating, or (b) reduce the radial load by re-distributing forces across the joint.

**(0.5 mark for recommendation — gets full credit)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a flowchart, describe the **complete mechanical design procedure** for the structural links of a 6-DOF industrial robot. Explain how material selection is integrated into this procedure. (7 marks)

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of a robot's elbow joint is subjected to a bending moment of 300 N·m and a torsional moment of 200 N·m. The shaft is made of mild steel with an ultimate tensile strength of 410 MPa. Using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory and a factor of safety of 2, determine the minimum required diameter of the shaft. State the design assumption clearly. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Mechanical Design Procedure for Robot Links (4 marks, written in points):**

1. **Define operational requirements** — payload, reach, workspace envelope, maximum joint speeds, and cycle time.
2. **Configure the kinematic chain** — number of DOF, joint types (R, P), and link lengths.
3. **Carry out static force/torque analysis** — using the Jacobian transpose method or by direct free-body analysis, compute the joint torques and link forces for the worst-case (maximum payload, maximum reach) configuration.
4. **Carry out dynamic analysis** — include inertial torques due to acceleration of the link and payload using the equation $\tau = I \alpha$ and the full Newton–Euler formulation.
5. **Propose a candidate material** — based on the requirements of strength, stiffness, weight, corrosion resistance, and cost.
6. **Propose a cross-section** — typically hollow circular or rectangular box-section to maximize stiffness-to-weight ratio.
7. **Check the combined stress** (bending + torsion + axial) at the **critical cross-section** (usually the root of the link, near the joint) using Von Mises or MSST.
8. **Check deflection and buckling** — for slender links, check Euler's buckling load and tip deflection against allowable limits.
9. **Iterate** — adjust material, cross-section, or link geometry until all design constraints are satisfied with the desired factor of safety.
10. **Document the design** in a drawing package with tolerances, surface finish, and material specification.

**Step 2 — Integration of Material Selection (2 marks):**

Material selection is integrated at **Step 5** and revisited at **Step 9**. The standard methodology uses an **Ashby chart** approach: plot stiffness (modulus) versus density, and overlay the design requirement of **maximum stiffness-to-weight ratio**. For most industrial robots, this leads to:
- **Aluminium alloys (6061, 7075)** for low-payload, high-speed arms.
- **Steel (AISI 4140, AISI 4340)** for heavy-payload arms.
- **Carbon-fibre composites** for high-performance or aerospace robots.
- **Engineering plastics (Delrin, PEEK)** for low-load, non-structural covers and cable carriers.

The choice is also constrained by:
- **Corrosion resistance** (food/pharma).
- **Thermal expansion coefficient** (precision applications).
- **Machinability and weldability** (manufacturing cost).
- **Cost and availability** (procurement).

**Step 3 — Concluding Statement (1 mark):**

A well-designed robotic link balances strength, stiffness, weight, and cost; material selection is therefore not an isolated decision but an iterative part of the entire mechanical design loop.

---

#### **Model Solution — Q.B (b) (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Bending moment $M_b = 300 \text{ N·m}$
- Torsional moment $T = 200 \text{ N·m}$
- Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ut} = 410 \text{ MPa} = 410 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
- Factor of safety $n = 2$
- Theory: Maximum Shear Stress Theory (MSST)

**Design Assumption (1 mark):**
The shaft is assumed to be **solid, circular, and of uniform diameter**, with steady (non-fatigue) loading and negligible axial and shear forces.

**Step 1 — Write the equivalent stress from MSST (1.5 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{eq,MSST} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{\,2} + 4 \tau^{2}}
$$

with:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^{3}}, \quad \tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and simplify (1.5 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{eq,MSST} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{32 \times 300}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2} + 4 \left(\dfrac{16 \times 200}{\pi d^{3}}\right)^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{eq,MSST} = \dfrac{1}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{(9600)^{2} + 4 (3200)^{2}}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{92{,}160{,}000 + 40{,}960{,}000}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{1}{\pi d^{3}} \sqrt{133{,}120{,}000}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{11{,}537.4}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the factor of safety condition (1.5 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{eq,MSST} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{n} = \dfrac{410 \times 10^{6}}{2} = 205 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\dfrac{11{,}537.4}{\pi d^{3}} \leq 205 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d$ (1 mark):**

$$
\pi d^{3} \geq \dfrac{11{,}537.4}{205 \times 10^{6}} = 5.628 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
d^{3} \geq \dfrac{5.628 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi} = 1.7914 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
d \geq \sqrt[3]{1.7914 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0262 \text{ m} = 26.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — State the final answer with proper rounding (1 mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** The minimum required shaft diameter is approximately **26.2 mm**. In practice, the designer rounds up to the next standard metric size, which is **30 mm**, to provide an additional safety margin against stress concentrations at keyways and shoulder fillets.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Where Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Bearing life exponent confusion** — using $p = 3$ for roller bearings (should be $p = 10/3$). Costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — mixing N with kN, mm with m, rpm with rad/s in the same calculation. Always convert to SI (N, m, rad/s) before plugging in.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of safety** — computing the "bare minimum" gripper force or shaft diameter and not multiplying by the design FoS. Costs up to 1 mark.
> 4. **No standard rounding** — writing $d = 26.2$ mm without mentioning that a standard size of 30 mm will be selected. Examiners expect this designer's note.
> 5. **Skipping the design assumption** — in combined loading problems, the examiner's key explicitly awards 0.5–1 mark for stating the assumption (e.g., steady loading, no stress concentration, solid circular shaft).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- [x] Factor of safety definition and typical values (2–4 for robot links, 4–10 for lifting gears).
- [x] Bending stress formula: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_b}{Z}$ and $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$.
- [x] Torsional shear stress formula: $\tau = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ and $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{16}$.
- [x] Von Mises equivalent stress for bending + torsion: $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{2} + 3\tau^{2}}$.
- [x] MSST equivalent stress for bending + torsion: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^{2} + 4\tau^{2}}$.
- [x] Motor power equation: $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (N in rpm, T in N·m, P in W).
- [x] Pneumatic cylinder force: $F = P_{line} \times \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$.
- [x] Gripper force: $F_g = \dfrac{m g}{2 \mu n_{fingers}} \times n_{sf}$.
- [x] Bearing basic life: $L_{10} = (C/P)^{3}$ for ball, $(C/P)^{10/3}$ for roller (in million revs).
- [x] Life in hours: $L_{10h} = \dfrac{L_{10} \times 10^{6}}{60 \times n}$.
- [x] ISO 9409 standard for robot tool flange.
- [x] Four-stage design procedure: Conceptual → Configuration → Sizing → Detailed.
- [x] Common materials: Aluminium 6061/7075, Steel 4140/4340, CF composites, PEEK.
- [x] Always apply service factor (1.25–1.5) to motor power to account for transients.
- [x] Always state design assumption, always round up to next standard size, always include safety factor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical and Electronic Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical and Electronic Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical and Electronic Considerations** in the context of robotic assembly and material handling systems refer to the complete spectrum of design, integration, and operational factors related to the power distribution, signal processing, control electronics, sensing circuitry, and communication interfaces that enable a robot or automated handling system to function reliably in an industrial environment. This discipline spans from the **AC mains supply** entering a control cabinet to the low-level **analog and digital signals** reaching microcontrollers, motor drives, and end-effectors.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic bridges **mechatronic system integration**, covering the electrical interfaces between the following functional blocks:
- **Power Electronics** (AC-DC conversion, DC-DC regulation, inverters)
- **Drive Electronics** (servo amplifiers, stepper drivers, VFDs)
- **Sensor Signal Conditioning** (amplification, filtering, isolation)
- **Control Logic Hardware** (PLCs, microcontrollers, IPCs, HMIs)
- **Safety and Protection Systems** (E-Stops, relays, fuses, circuit breakers)
- **Communication Buses** (EtherCAT, PROFINET, CAN, RS-485, IO-Link)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Outcome-Based Education (OBE), this topic primarily maps to **CO3: Design and integrate electrical/electronic subsystems for robotic and material handling systems**, assessed across all six cognitive levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Remember → Create).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a robotic work cell as a **human body**:

| Body Part | Robotic Equivalent | Electrical/Electronic Function |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Heart & Lungs** | Power Supply Unit (PSU) | Pumps "energy" (electricity) into the system |
| **Nervous System** | Sensors + Wiring | Carry information from periphery to brain |
| **Brain** | PLC / Controller | Processes information and issues commands |
| **Muscles** | Motors + Drives | Convert electrical signals into mechanical motion |
| **Skin Reflexes** | Safety Relays, E-Stops | Instant protective responses to hazards |
| **Spinal Cord** | Communication Bus (EtherCAT/PROFINET) | High-speed data backbone between nodes |

Just as a human cannot function if the nervous system is short-circuited or the power supply fails, a robot fails when its electrical architecture is poorly designed. The job of the electrical engineer is to ensure that **energy flows cleanly**, **signals arrive uncorrupted**, and **safety overrides always work** — even when everything else fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metrics** you must memorize:
> - Industrial supply voltage: **400 V AC, 3-phase, 50 Hz** (India/Europe)
> - Control voltage: **24 V DC** (SELV – Safety Extra-Low Voltage, per IEC 61131-2)
> - Digital I/O logic levels: **24 V DC** (sinking/sourcing) for PLCs
> - Analog I/O standards: **0–10 V** or **4–20 mA** (current loop preferred for noise immunity)
> - Encoder resolution: **10,000 to 1,000,000 pulses/revolution** (PPR/CPR)

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Examiners frequently test this module because it is **fundamentally multidisciplinary** — a student must understand physics, basic electronics, control theory, and safety standards simultaneously. Marks are awarded for:
1. Correct identification of components in a wiring diagram
2. Selection of proper signal standards (voltage vs. current loop)
3. Justification of safety circuit topology (Category 1/3/4 per ISO 13849)
4. Numerical analysis of power consumption, voltage drop, and filter design

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Architecture of a Robotic Electrical System

A complete robotic assembly cell's electrical architecture can be broken down into the following hierarchical blocks:

### Block 1 — **Power Input & Distribution**
- **Main isolator / MCCB** (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker): Provides manual lock-out capability and short-circuit protection.
- **Transformer** (e.g., 400 V → 230 V or 400 V → 110 V control transformer): Steps down voltage for control circuits.
- **SMPS / DC Power Supply** (e.g., 24 V DC, 10 A): Provides regulated DC for PLCs, sensors, and relay coils.
- **UPS / Battery Backup**: Maintains control logic during brief power loss for safe shutdown.

### Block 2 — **Drive Electronics (Power Stage)**
- **Servo Drives**: Take low-power command signals (e.g., ±10 V analog or pulse-train) and switch high-current DC to brushless/AC servo motors using **PWM at 4–16 kHz**.
- **Stepper Drives**: Convert digital step/direction pulses into current-controlled phase energization (microstepping up to 1/256 step).
- **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**: For induction motors in conveyors, used in V/f or sensorless vector control.

### Block 3 — **Controller & Logic**
- **PLC** (Programmable Logic Controller): The "brains" of material handling, executes ladder logic / FBD / ST cyclically (typical scan time 1–10 ms).
- **Safety PLC / Safety Relay**: Dedicated hardware for safety logic, certified to **SIL 3 / PL e**.

### Block 4 — **Sensors & Field Devices**
- **Digital sensors**: Inductive proximity, photoelectric, capacitive — 3-wire NPN/PNP, 24 V DC.
- **Analog sensors**: Pressure transducers, load cells, vision systems — 4–20 mA or 0–10 V output.
- **Feedback devices**: Incremental encoders (TTL/HTL), absolute encoders (SSI/BiSS), resolvers.

### Block 5 — **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**
- Touch-panel displays, pushbuttons, selector switches, indicator lamps (LED), buzzers.

## 2.2 Key Engineering Concepts

### 2.2.1 Voltage Drop in Control Wiring

Every wire has resistance. If the wire is too long or too thin, the voltage at the load (sensor, solenoid) drops below its operating threshold. This is a common KTU question.

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage drop rule (per IEC 60364):** Voltage drop in any branch circuit must not exceed **3 % for lighting** and **5 % for other loads** from the source to the load.

### 2.2.2 Current Loop (4–20 mA) vs. Voltage Signal (0–10 V)

| Property | 0–10 V Voltage Loop | 4–20 mA Current Loop |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Noise immunity** | Poor (susceptible to EMI) | Excellent |
| **Line loss effect** | Severe voltage drop | Negligible (current constant) |
| **Open-circuit detection** | Cannot detect (reads 0 V = 0 %) | Yes (0 mA = fault) |
| **Maximum distance** | < 10 m typical | Up to 1000 m+ |
| **Preferred in** | Lab, short cabinet wiring | Industrial floor, long cable runs |

### 2.2.3 Encoder Signal Quadrature

Incremental encoders output two square waves, **Channel A** and **Channel B**, phase-shifted by **90° (one quarter period)**. This phase shift allows the controller to determine both **speed** and **direction**.

### 2.2.4 PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) Basics

A servo drive switches the DC bus voltage at a high frequency (typically 8 kHz for industrial servos, 16–20 kHz for silent operation). The **duty cycle** $D$ determines the average voltage applied to the motor winding:

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{DC}$$

where $D \in [0, 1]$.

### 2.2.5 Safety Integrity Levels

- **Category B, 1, 2, 3, 4** — per **ISO 13849-1** (Performance Level PL a → PL e)
- **SIL 1 → SIL 4** — per **IEC 61508** (Safety Integrity Level)

A **dual-channel safety circuit** with cross-monitoring is required for **Category 3 / PL d** and above.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every formula you are likely to need** for numerical problems on Electrical & Electronic Considerations in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the symbol meanings, units, and typical boundary values.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units | Typical Boundary Values |
|---|--------------------|---------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Ohm's Law** | $V = I \times R$ | $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $R$ in $\Omega$ | — |
| 2 | **Power (DC)** | $P = V \times I = I^{2} R = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | $P$ in W | — |
| 3 | **3-phase AC Power** | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_{L} \times \cos\phi$ | $V_L$ line voltage, $I_L$ line current, $\cos\phi$ power factor | $\cos\phi \in [0.7, 0.95]$ |
| 4 | **Voltage Drop in 2-wire DC circuit** | $\Delta V = \dfrac{2 \times I \times L \times \rho}{A}$ | $L$ length (m), $\rho$ resistivity ($\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{mm}^{2}/\text{m}$), $A$ cross-section (mm²) | $\Delta V / V_{s} \le 5\%$ |
| 5 | **Wire Resistance** | $R = \dfrac{\rho \times L}{A}$ | Same as above | Copper: $\rho = 0.0172\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{mm}^{2}/\text{m}$ |
| 6 | **Average PWM Voltage** | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{DC}$ | $D$ duty cycle [0,1] | $f_{PWM} \in [4, 20]$ kHz |
| 7 | **RC Filter Cut-off Frequency** | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F | For 50 Hz hum rejection: $f_c \approx 10$ Hz |
| 8 | **Encoder Resolution** | $\text{Resolution} = \text{PPR} \times 4$ (quadrature) | PPR = pulses per revolution | Common: 2500 PPR → 10,000 counts/rev |
| 9 | **Motor Speed (DC)** | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | $K\Phi$ back-EMF constant, $R_a$ armature resistance | rpm |
| 10 | **Transformer Turns Ratio** | $\dfrac{V_{p}}{V_{s}} = \dfrac{N_{p}}{N_{s}} = \dfrac{I_{s}}{I_{p}}$ | Subscript $p$ primary, $s$ secondary | Ideal transformer |
| 11 | **Capacitor Energy Storage** | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $E$ in J, $C$ in F, $V$ in V | SMPS bulk caps: 470 µF / 400 V |
| 12 | **Power Supply Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ in % | SMPS: 85–95 %; Linear: 40–60 % |
| 13 | **Current Loop to Voltage Conversion** | $V_{sense} = I_{loop} \times R_{sense}$ | $R_{sense}$ typically 250 $\Omega$ → 1–5 V from 4–20 mA | — |
| 14 | **Signal Attenuation (RC low-pass)** | $\vert H(f) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^{2}}}$ | $f$ signal frequency, $f_c$ cut-off | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are used daily by control engineers sizing cable cross-sections for long conveyor runs, selecting UPS ratings for clean rooms, and tuning low-pass filters to reject VFD-induced EMI on analog sensor lines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-style numerical problems** on this topic typically fall into one of three categories. We work through each archetype below with **every algebraic step shown explicitly** — no shortcuts, no omitted arithmetic.

## 3.1 Archetype 1 — Voltage Drop & Wire Sizing Calculation

### Problem Statement (Model)
A 24 V DC inductive proximity sensor is located **35 metres** from the PLC cabinet. The sensor draws a current of **150 mA**. The cable is standard **copper** ($\rho = 0.0172\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{mm}^{2}/\text{m}$). The manufacturer specifies that the sensor must receive a minimum of **18 V** to operate reliably. Determine the **minimum cable cross-section** $A$ (in mm²) required.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum allowable voltage drop.**

The supply is 24 V; the sensor needs at least 18 V. Therefore the maximum permissible drop is:

$$\Delta V_{max} = V_{s} - V_{min} = 24 - 18 = 6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that a 2-wire DC circuit has current flowing through both the "go" and "return" conductors.**

So the total effective length of conductor carrying current is $2L$, not $L$. The resistance of the loop is:

$$R_{loop} = \dfrac{\rho \times 2L}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to express the voltage drop.**

$$\Delta V = I \times R_{loop} = I \times \dfrac{\rho \times 2L}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the cross-sectional area $A$.**

$$A = \dfrac{I \times \rho \times 2L}{\Delta V_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{(0.150\ \text{A}) \times (0.0172\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{mm}^{2}/\text{m}) \times (2 \times 35\ \text{m})}{6\ \text{V}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.150 \times 0.0172 \times 70}{6} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{0.1806}{6} \\[6pt]
&= 0.0301\ \text{mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the engineering safety margin and standard sizing.**

A calculated $0.0301\ \text{mm}^{2}$ is the **bare-minimum** (theoretically sufficient). However, the next **standard available cable size** is **0.5 mm²** (Indian/British standard: 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.5, 2.5, 4.0…). 

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** Always select the next standard size up, and apply a **1.25× to 1.5× safety factor** for derating due to ambient temperature, bundling, and future load growth. So the practical choice is **0.75 mm²** copper.

**Final Answer:** $A_{min, theoretical} = 0.0301\ \text{mm}^{2}$ → Selected size = **0.75 mm² copper** (with derating margin).

---

## 3.2 Archetype 2 — RC Filter Design for Sensor Signal Conditioning

### Problem Statement (Model)
A strain-gauge load-cell outputs a differential signal in the range of **0–30 mV full scale**, riding on top of significant **50 Hz mains pickup** from a nearby VFD. You must design a **first-order RC low-pass filter** that attenuates the 50 Hz noise by at least **20 dB** (i.e., to 10 % of original amplitude) while passing the **0–5 Hz** useful signal with at most **0.5 dB** attenuation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Translate the attenuation requirement into the filter's transfer function.**

The magnitude response of a first-order RC low-pass filter is:

$$\vert H(f) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^{2}}}$$

We require $\vert H(50) \vert \le 0.1$ (for 20 dB attenuation).

**Step 2 — Solve the inequality for $f_c$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (50/f_c)^{2}}} &\le 0.1 \\[6pt]
\sqrt{1 + (50/f_c)^{2}} &\ge 10 \\[6pt]
1 + (50/f_c)^{2} &\ge 100 \\[6pt]
(50/f_c)^{2} &\ge 99 \\[6pt]
50/f_c &\ge \sqrt{99} \approx 9.95 \\[6pt]
f_c &\le 50 / 9.95 \approx 5.025\ \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the passband constraint (0.5 dB at 5 Hz).**

The attenuation in dB is:

$$\text{Atten}_{dB} = -20 \log_{10} \vert H(f) \vert = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (f/f_c)^{2}\right]$$

For $f = 5\ \text{Hz}$ and $f_c = 5\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Atten}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[1 + (5/5)^{2}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(1 + 1) = 10 \log_{10}(2) \\[6pt]
&\approx 10 \times 0.3010 = 3.01\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **worse than the 0.5 dB specification**! So a first-order filter is insufficient. We must either:

(a) Raise the cut-off to 10 Hz → recompute the 50 Hz attenuation:
  - $\text{Atten}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}[1 + (50/10)^{2}] = 10 \log_{10}(26) \approx 14.1\ \text{dB}$ (still not 20 dB)
  
(b) Use a **second-order filter** (Sallen–Key topology) to get a steeper roll-off (−40 dB/decade).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A first-order RC filter attenuates at only **−20 dB/decade**, meaning to get 20 dB of attenuation at 50 Hz, the cut-off must be roughly 5 Hz — which then kills 5 Hz of useful signal. **Order matters.**

**Step 4 — Design a 2nd-order Butterworth filter with $f_c = 10\ \text{Hz}$.**

For a Sallen–Key low-pass with equal components, choose $C = 100\ \text{nF}$ and solve for $R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C} \\[6pt]
R &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}} \\[6pt]
R &= \dfrac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 159{,}155\ \Omega \approx 159\ \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Select the standard value $R = 160\ \text{k}\Omega$. A second-order filter at 10 Hz gives **−40 dB/decade** roll-off, so 50 Hz attenuation:

$$\text{Atten}_{dB} = 40 \times \log_{10}(50/10) = 40 \times 0.699 = 27.96\ \text{dB} \quad \checkmark\ (\text{exceeds 20 dB})$$

And 5 Hz attenuation: $\text{Atten}_{dB} = 40 \times \log_{10}\sqrt{1 + (5/10)^{2}} \approx 1.94\ \text{dB}$.

Still slightly over spec — the engineer must use a **higher-order filter** (4th order) or accept this. **Final practical design: 4th-order Butterworth with $f_c = 15\ \text{Hz}$.**

---

## 3.3 Archetype 3 — 3-Phase Power Calculation for a Robot Cell

### Problem Statement (Model)
A robotic assembly cell contains the following 3-phase AC loads:
- 1 × 6-axis robot, servo pack rated at **5 kW**, $\cos\phi = 0.85$
- 2 × conveyor induction motors, each **1.5 kW**, $\cos\phi = 0.80$
- 1 × vacuum pump, **0.75 kW**, $\cos\phi = 0.75$
- Lighting & control, **0.5 kW** (single-phase 230 V, balanced across phases)

The supply is **400 V AC, 3-phase, 50 Hz**. Calculate (a) the **total real power**, (b) the **total apparent power**, and (c) the **main circuit breaker rating**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total Real Power $P$ (sum of all loads).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= 5.0 + 2(1.5) + 0.75 + 0.50 \\[3pt]
&= 5.0 + 3.0 + 0.75 + 0.50 = 9.25\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Reactive Power $Q$ (using $Q = P \tan\phi$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{robot} &= 5.0 \times \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.85)) = 5.0 \times 0.6197 = 3.099\ \text{kVAr} \\[3pt]
Q_{conveyor,ea} &= 1.5 \times \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.80)) = 1.5 \times 0.7500 = 1.125\ \text{kVAr} \\[3pt]
Q_{vacuum} &= 0.75 \times \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.75)) = 0.75 \times 0.8819 = 0.661\ \text{kVAr} \\[3pt]
Q_{control} &= 0.50 \times \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.90)) \approx 0.50 \times 0.4843 = 0.242\ \text{kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= 3.099 + 2(1.125) + 0.661 + 0.242 \\[3pt]
&= 3.099 + 2.250 + 0.661 + 0.242 = 6.252\ \text{kVAr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Apparent Power $S$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= \sqrt{P^{2} + Q^{2}} = \sqrt{(9.25)^{2} + (6.252)^{2}} \\[3pt]
&= \sqrt{85.56 + 39.09} = \sqrt{124.65} \\[3pt]
&\approx 11.16\ \text{kVA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall Power Factor.**

$$\cos\phi_{overall} = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{9.25}{11.16} \approx 0.829$$

**Step 5 — Line Current.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_L &= \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \dfrac{11{,}160}{\sqrt{3} \times 400} \\[3pt]
&= \dfrac{11{,}160}{692.82} \approx 16.11\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Main Circuit Breaker Selection.**

Apply **1.25× safety factor** for continuous loads and 25 % spare capacity for future expansion:

$$I_{breaker} \ge 1.25 \times 16.11 = 20.14\ \text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Standard breaker ratings: 16 A, 20 A, 25 A, 32 A… → **Select 25 A, 3-pole MCCB** with adjustable thermal-magnetic trip.

**Final Answer:** $P = 9.25\ \text{kW}$, $S = 11.16\ \text{kVA}$, $I_L = 16.11\ \text{A}$ → **25 A MCCB** recommended.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Selection for Robotic Workcell

| Sensor Type | Output Type | Range | Typical Use in Robotic Cell | Pros | Cons |
|-------------|-------------|-------|----------------------------|------|------|
| **Inductive Proximity** | Digital NPN/PNP, 24 V | 2–8 mm | Part-present detection on conveyor | Rugged, low cost, no contact | Metal only |
| **Photoelectric (Diffuse)** | Digital, 24 V | 0.1–2 m | Box detection, pallet sensing | Long range, all colors | Lens contamination |
| **Capacitive Proximity** | Digital, 24 V | 2–25 mm | Plastic/glass part detection | Detects non-metals | Sensitive to humidity |
| **Strain-Gauge Load Cell** | Analog 0–30 mV | 0–500 N | Force feedback on gripper | High accuracy | Needs amplification |
| **Incremental Encoder** | Quadrature TTL/HTL | 1000–10000 PPR | Motor shaft position | High resolution | Needs homing |
| **Absolute Encoder** | SSI / BiSS / EtherCAT | Up to 25 bit | Multi-turn joint position | No homing needed | Costlier |
| **Resolver** | Analog sin/cos | – | Harsh-environment joint | Rugged, high temp | Needs RDC chip |
| **Laser Triangulation** | Analog 4–20 mA / IO-Link | 25–500 mm | Part profiling, gap measurement | High precision | Costly, eye-safety |
| **Vision Camera (2D/3D)** | GigE Vision / USB3 | – | Inspection, bin-picking | Most versatile | Lighting-sensitive, expensive |
