## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Distinguish between "Validation" and "Verification" in the context of robotic system design. Provide one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Validation (1 mark):** Confirms the design meets the *intended use* and *user needs*. It answers: *"Are we building the right robot?"* Example: Confirming that a 6-DOF arm can reach a car dashboard at 1.8 m from base within the specified 1.5 s cycle (1 mark).
- **Verification (1 mark):** Confirms the design meets the *specified engineering requirements* through testing. It answers: *"Are we building the robot right?"* Example: Hammer-test modal analysis showing the first natural frequency is 18 Hz, matching the FEA-predicted 17.6 Hz (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Pareto Optimality" in multi-objective optimization of a robot design. Why is a single "best" solution often impossible?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Pareto Optimality (1.5 marks):** A design solution $\mathbf{x}^*$ is Pareto optimal if no other feasible solution $\mathbf{x}$ exists that improves one objective without worsening at least one other objective. Formally: $\mathbf{x}^*$ is Pareto optimal if there is no $\mathbf{x}$ such that $f_k(\mathbf{x}) \le f_k(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for all $k$ and $f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^*)$ for at least one $j$.
- **Why no single "best" (1.5 marks):** Conflicting objectives (e.g., mass vs. stiffness, cost vs. accuracy) make it impossible to improve all simultaneously. Designers must choose a trade-off from the **Pareto front** based on application priorities. Reducing mass typically reduces stiffness; increasing stiffness typically increases mass/cost.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A 3-DOF planar robot has DH parameters as listed in the table below. Compute the end-effector position (x, y) when $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\theta_3 = -15°$. Validate the result by sketching the manipulator and verifying reach distance. **(7 marks)**

| Link $i$ | $a_i$ (m) | $\alpha_i$ (rad) | $d_i$ (m) | $\theta_i$ (var) |
|----------|-----------|------------------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | 0.4 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |
| 3 | 0.2 | 0 | 0 | $\theta_3$ |

**(b)** During validation, the measured end-effector position is $(x_m, y_m) = (1.025, 0.408)$ m. Compute the **position error**, **Euclidean error norm**, and comment on whether the model is validated for a tolerance of $1.0$ mm. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Build individual transforms.**
For a planar manipulator with $\alpha_i = 0$, $d_i = 0$, the DH transform reduces to:

$$
{}^{i-1}\mathbf{T}_i = \begin{bmatrix} c\theta_i & -s\theta_i & a_i c\theta_i \\ s\theta_i & c\theta_i & a_i s\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ${}^0\mathbf{T}_1$ with $\theta_1 = 30°$:**

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos 30° & -\sin 30° & 0.5 \cos 30° \\ \sin 30° & \cos 30° & 0.5 \sin 30° \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 & -0.5 & 0.4330 \\ 0.5 & 0.8660 & 0.25 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ${}^1\mathbf{T}_2$ with $\theta_2 = 45°$:**

$$
{}^{1}\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7071 & -0.7071 & 0.2828 \\ 0.7071 & 0.7071 & 0.2828 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ${}^2\mathbf{T}_3$ with $\theta_3 = -15°$:**

$$
{}^{2}\mathbf{T}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9659 & 0.2588 & 0.1932 \\ -0.2588 & 0.9659 & -0.0518 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply to get ${}^0\mathbf{T}_3$:**

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_1 \cdot {}^{1}\mathbf{T}_2
$$

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8660 \cdot 0.7071 + (-0.5) \cdot 0.7071 & 0.8660 \cdot (-0.7071) + (-0.5) \cdot 0.7071 & 0.8660 \cdot 0.2828 + (-0.5) \cdot 0.2828 + 0.4330 \\ 0.5 \cdot 0.7071 + 0.8660 \cdot 0.7071 & 0.5 \cdot (-0.7071) + 0.8660 \cdot 0.7071 & 0.5 \cdot 0.2828 + 0.8660 \cdot 0.2828 + 0.25 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2588 & -0.9659 & 0.5358 \\ 0.9659 & 0.2588 & 0.6364 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Now multiply by ${}^2\mathbf{T}_3$:

$$
{}^{0}\mathbf{T}_3 = {}^{0}\mathbf{T}_2 \cdot {}^{2}\mathbf{T}_3
$$

$$
x = (0.2588)(0.1932) + (-0.9659)(-0.0518) + 0.5358 = 0.0500 + 0.0500 + 0.5358 = 1.0248 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
y = (0.9659)(0.1932) + (0.2588)(-0.0518) + 0.6364 = 0.1866 - 0.0134 + 0.6364 = 0.4096 \; \text{m}
$$

**Predicted end-effector position:** $(x_p, y_p) = (1.0248, 0.4096)$ m. **(5 marks for derivation)**

**Step 6 — Reach distance check:**
$r = \sqrt{1.0248^2 + 0.4096^2} = \sqrt{1.0502 + 0.1678} = \sqrt{1.2180} = 1.1036$ m. The maximum theoretical reach is $a_1 + a_2 + a_3 = 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.2 = 1.1$ m. So $1.1036$ m is just below max reach — physically consistent. **(2 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Position error components (1 mark each):**

$$
e_x = x_m - x_p = 1.025 - 1.0248 = 0.0002 \; \text{m} = 0.2 \; \text{mm}
$$

$$
e_y = y_m - y_p = 0.408 - 0.4096 = -0.0016 \; \text{m} = -1.6 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Euclidean error norm (2 marks):**

$$
E = \sqrt{e_x^2 + e_y^2} = \sqrt{(0.0002)^2 + (-0.0016)^2} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{-8} + 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
E = \sqrt{2.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.001613 \; \text{m} = 1.613 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (2 marks):**
The Euclidean error $E = 1.613$ mm **exceeds the tolerance of $1.0$ mm**. Therefore, the model is **NOT validated** under the current tolerance criterion. The discrepancy likely arises from unmodeled effects such as joint compliance, backlash, sensor quantization, or DH parameter miscalibration. **Recommendation:** Refine the DH parameters via a calibration routine (e.g., least-squares minimization of $\|\mathbf{e}\|^2$ over multiple measured poses) and re-validate.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** For the manipulator of Question A, compute the **static joint torques** when the end-effector exerts a force $\mathbf{F} = [50, 30, 0]^T$ N at the position calculated in Q-A part (a). Use the **Jacobian transpose method** with the joint positions given. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Formulate a **multi-objective optimization** problem to simultaneously **minimize the total mass** of the three links and **minimize the cycle time** for a point-to-point move over 1.0 m, subject to: (i) reach $\ge 1.05$ m, (ii) max joint torque $\le 50$ N·m. Clearly state variables, objectives, and constraints. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Step 1 — Compute the 3×3 Jacobian of the planar 3-DOF arm.**
The Jacobian relates joint velocities to end-effector linear velocities. For a planar arm:

$$
\mathbf{J}_v = \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12} - a_3 s_{123} & -a_2 s_{12} - a_3 s_{123} & -a_3 s_{123} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} + a_3 c_{123} & a_2 c_{12} + a_3 c_{123} & a_3 c_{123} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $c_{123} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2+\theta_3)$, etc.

Compute sums: $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_1+\theta_2 = 75°$, $\theta_1+\theta_2+\theta_3 = 60°$.

$$
s_1 = 0.5, \; c_1 = 0.8660, \; s_{12} = 0.9659, \; c_{12} = 0.2588, \; s_{123} = 0.8660, \; c_{123} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
J_{11} = -(0.5)(0.5) - (0.4)(0.9659) - (0.2)(0.8660) = -0.25 - 0.3864 - 0.1732 = -0.8096
$$

$$
J_{12} = -(0.4)(0.9659) - (0.2)(0.8660) = -0.3864 - 0.1732 = -0.5596
$$

$$
J_{13} = -(0.2)(0.8660) = -0.1732
$$

$$
J_{21} = (0.5)(0.8660) + (0.4)(0.2588) + (0.2)(0.5) = 0.4330 + 0.1035 + 0.1 = 0.6365
$$

$$
J_{22} = (0.4)(0.2588) + (0.2)(0.5) = 0.1035 + 0.1 = 0.2035
$$

$$
J_{23} = (0.2)(0.5) = 0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Jacobian Transpose torque formula:**
Static equilibrium: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \begin{bmatrix} J_{11} & J_{21} \\ J_{12} & J_{22} \\ J_{13} & J_{23} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8096 & 0.6365 \\ -0.5596 & 0.2035 \\ -0.1732 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 50 \\ 30 \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
\tau_1 = (-0.8096)(50) + (0.6365)(30) = -40.48 + 19.10 = -21.38 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = (-0.5596)(50) + (0.2035)(30) = -27.98 + 6.11 = -21.87 \; \text{N·m}
$$

$$
\tau_3 = (-0.1732)(50) + (0.1)(30) = -8.66 + 3.0 = -5.66 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The negative signs indicate the joint motors must apply *counter-torque* to *resist* the externally applied force. Maximum magnitude is $|\tau_2| = 21.87$ N·m, well within the 50 N·m constraint.

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Identify design variables (1.5 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \\ m_1 \\ m_2 \\ m_3 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{6}
$$

where $a_i$ are link lengths and $m_i$ are link masses.

**Step 2 — Define the objective vector (2 marks):**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(\mathbf{x}) \\ f_2(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 + m_2 + m_3 \\ t_c(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $t_c$ is the point-to-point cycle time computed from the trapezoidal velocity profile:

$$
t_c = t_a + t_v + t_d, \quad \text{with} \quad t_a = t_d = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}, \quad t_v = \dfrac{d - v_{max}^2/a_{max}}{v_{max}}
$$

(Assume $v_{max} = 1$ m/s, $a_{max} = 2$ m/s² as fixed kinematic limits.)

**Step 3 — Define the constraints (2 marks):**

$$
g_1(\mathbf{x}) = a_1 + a_2 + a_3 - 1.05 \ge 0 \quad \text{(reach constraint)}
$$

$$
g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 50 - \max_i \vert \tau_i(\mathbf{x}) \vert \ge 0 \quad \text{(torque constraint)}
$$

$$
g_3(\mathbf{x}): a_i > 0, \; m_i > 0, \; i = 1, 2, 3
$$

**Step 4 — State the complete MOO problem (1.5 marks):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \quad & \begin{bmatrix} m_1 + m_2 + m_3 \\ t_c(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix} \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & a_1 + a_2 + a_3 \ge 1.05 \\
& \vert \tau_i \vert \le 50 \; \text{N·m} \quad \forall i \\
& a_i > 0, \; m_i > 0
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Solution strategy (implicit):** Apply the **Weighted Sum Method** or **NSGA-II** (Non-dominated Sorting GA-II) to generate the Pareto front. The designer then selects one Pareto-optimal point based on application priority (e.g., weight-sensitive mobile robot vs. speed-sensitive assembly line).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Validation & Optimization Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing Validation with Verification (–2 marks):** Validation checks *fitness for use*; Verification checks *conformance to specification*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting Units (–1 to –2 marks):** Always state units for all final numerical answers (mm, m, N·m, Hz, kg). A "0.5" answer with no unit is incomplete.
> 3. **Sign Error in Jacobian Transpose Method (–3 marks):** The torque equation is $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$. Students often write $\mathbf{J} \mathbf{F}$ or $\mathbf{F} \mathbf{J}^T$ — both wrong. Note that $\mathbf{F}$ is a 2×1 (or 6×1) force/wrench vector.
> 4. **Skipping the KKT Conditions (–2 marks):** When asked to "formulate" an optimization problem, students must list the Lagrangian, KKT stationarity, complementary slackness, and primal feasibility. Omitting these is a partial-solution penalty.
> 5. **Single-Objective Trap (–2 marks):** When a question says "multi-objective," do not collapse to a single weighted objective without justification. State the Pareto approach explicitly.
> 6. **DH Parameter Mistakes (–3 marks):** Always include the full 4×4 homogeneous transform; do not write only the 3×3 rotation part. Examiners check the $[0 \; 0 \; 0 \; 1]$ last row.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Validation** confirms the design *does what it should*; **Verification** confirms it *meets the spec*.
- The **design V&V cycle** is iterative — never assume the first iteration is final.
- **DH parameters** are the universal language for robot kinematic validation; always provide $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$ for every joint.
- **Repeatability** and **Accuracy** are different KPIs. Repeatability $\le \pm 0.05$ mm is typical for industrial 6-DOF arms.
- The **Jacobian transpose method** gives static joint torques as $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.
- **Singularities** occur when $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$ — the robot loses one or more DOFs and joint velocities can become infinite.
- **Multi-objective optimization** uses **Pareto fronts**, not a single "best" point. Common methods: **NSGA-II**, **SPEA2**, **MOEA/D**.
- **Topology optimization** (SIMP method) is increasingly used in robot link design — yields organic, non-intuitive geometries that beat human designs by 20–40% in stiffness-to-mass ratio.
- **First natural frequency** must be **≥ 10–15 Hz** to stay above the servo control bandwidth and avoid resonance.
- **Safety factor (SF)** ≥ **2.0** for dynamic, ≥ **1.5** for static, per ISO 10218.
- **Validation tools:** laser tracker, FARO arm, accelerometer + impact hammer, strain gauges, dynamometer, FLIR thermal camera.
- **Optimization tools:** MATLAB Optimization Toolbox, ANSYS DesignXplorer, Altair HyperWorks, Python (scipy.optimize, pymoo, geatpy).
- **Always re-validate** an optimized design — Pareto-optimality on objectives does NOT guarantee constraint satisfaction.
- **Buckling check (Euler):** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (KL)^2$ — critical for slender, lightweight robot columns.
- **Actuator sizing** must include a **1.5–2.0× safety margin** on RMS torque to handle transient peaks.
- **Material selection** trades stiffness vs. mass vs. cost: **Al 6061-T6** (general), **CFRP** (high stiffness, low mass), **Steel 4140** (high wear/joints).
- **Workspace validation** uses random sampling of 10,000+ configurations and checks for IK solvability — coverage ≥ 95% is industry standard.
- **Cycle time** is the most user-visible metric in industrial robots; 1% reduction across 1,000 robots saves thousands of hours/year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Performance Analysis** in the context of robotic assembly and material handling systems refers to the systematic, quantitative evaluation of a robotic manipulator's operational capabilities against prescribed engineering specifications and application-driven benchmarks. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this analysis is treated as a multi-criteria optimization and verification exercise that quantifies how well a robot executes its intended tasks under realistic loading, kinematic, and dynamic constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Performance analysis is the engineering process of determining, measuring, and validating key operational parameters — including **cycle time**, **throughput**, **repeatability**, **accuracy**, **payload capacity**, **workspace utilization**, **energy consumption**, and **reliability** — of a robotic system to ensure that it meets the design intent of an assembly or material handling application.

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education) requires that performance analysis be conducted using the following formally recognized performance indices:

- **Geometric Performance:** Reach, dexterity, manipulability
- **Kinematic Performance:** Joint velocities, accelerations, singularities
- **Dynamic Performance:** Drive torques, payload-to-weight ratio, settling time
- **Task-Oriented Performance:** Cycle time, throughput, mean time between failures (MTBF)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Marathon Runner" View of a Robot**
>
> Imagine you have two marathon runners (Robot A and Robot B) and you want to know which one is "better" for a particular race. You wouldn't just look at one number — you'd evaluate:
> - **How fast** they run on average (→ *Cycle Time / Speed*)
> - **How consistently** they cover the same 42.195 km route (→ *Repeatability*)
> - **How close** their actual finishing line is to the GPS-claimed finishing line (→ *Accuracy*)
> - **How much water** they can carry while still running (→ *Payload Capacity*)
> - **How many races** they can finish in a year without breaking down (→ *Reliability / MTBF*)
> - **How much oxygen** they burn per kilometer (→ *Energy Efficiency*)
>
> Performance analysis is exactly this — but for a robot arm picking parts off a conveyor and placing them into a chassis. We never trust a single number; we build a **performance profile**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **mandatory** in any KTU 2024 Scheme answer on performance analysis:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeatability | $R$ | $\text{mm}$ (or $\mu\text{m}$) | **Most cited industrial metric** |
| Accuracy | $A$ | $\text{mm}$ | Geometric deviation from commanded pose |
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $\text{s}$ | Direct driver of throughput |
| Throughput | $\dot{n}$ | $\text{parts/hour}$ | Production economics metric |
| Payload | $m_p$ | $\text{kg}$ | Mechanical + drive limit |
| Reach | $r$ | $\text{m}$ | Workspace radius |
| Resolution | $\delta$ | $\text{mm}$ | Smallest increment |
| MTBF | $T_{MTBF}$ | $\text{hours}$ | Reliability metric |
| Manipulability | $w$ | dimensionless | Yoshikawa's index |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta$ | $\text{J/cycle}$ or $\text{kWh}$ | Sustainability metric |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Heuristic:** A complete performance analysis answer *must* quote numerical values for **at least three** of the metrics above. A purely qualitative answer is considered incomplete and is capped at partial marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five Pillars of Robotic Performance

Performance analysis, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 syllabus, is structured around **five distinct analytical pillars**. Each pillar addresses a different physics layer of the robotic system.

### Pillar I — Geometric Performance
- **Workspace Volume ($V_w$):** The bounded 3-D region reachable by the end-effector's reference point (typically the Tool Center Point, TCP).
- **Reachability Test:** A pose is *reachable* if the forward kinematics solution $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ exists within the joint limits $\mathbf{q}^{-} \le \mathbf{q} \le \mathbf{q}^{+}$.
- **Dexterity:** A measure of the robot's ability to arbitrarily orient the end-effector at a given point. At singular configurations, dexterity drops to zero.

### Pillar II — Kinematic Performance
- **Singularities:** Configurations where the Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ loses rank, i.e., $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$.
- **Joint Velocity Limits:** $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\min} \le \dot{\mathbf{q}} \le \dot{\mathbf{q}}_{\max}$, with the Cartesian velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- **Yoshikawa's Manipulability Index:**
$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det\left(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\, \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\right)}$$

### Pillar III — Dynamic Performance
- **Lagrangian Equation of Motion:**
$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} - \mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$$
- **Drive Torque Limit:** Each joint actuator must satisfy $\vert \tau_i \vert \le \tau_{i,\max}$, where $\tau_{i,\max}$ is set by the motor's continuous or peak torque rating.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time for the end-effector to reach and stay within $\pm 2\%$ of the commanded pose.

### Pillar IV — Task-Oriented Performance
- **Cycle Time ($T_c$):** Total time to complete one assembly or pick-and-place cycle.
- **Throughput:**
$$\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad [\text{parts/hour}]$$
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):**
$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

### Pillar V — Reliability and Economic Performance
- **Mean Time Between Failures:**
$$T_{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$
- **Return on Investment (ROI):**
$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Net Annual Savings}}{\text{Total Capital Cost}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for Module 3 Performance Analysis. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \vert \mathbf{x}_{\text{commanded}} - \mathbf{x}_{\text{actual}} \vert$ | Accuracy | mm | Geometric, ISO 9283 |
| 2 | $R = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(\bar{\mathbf{x}} - \mathbf{x}_i\right)^2}$ | Repeatability (standard deviation) | mm | $N \ge 30$ cycles |
| 3 | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Throughput | parts/hr | Single-station model |
| 4 | $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ | Manipulability | — | Zero at singularity |
| 5 | $v_{\text{TCP}} = \Vert \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}} \Vert$ | TCP speed | m/s | Bounded by joint limits |
| 6 | $\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{q}_j + C_{ij}\dot{q}_j + g_i$ | Joint torque | N·m | $i$-th joint |
| 7 | $T_{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{op}}}{N_f}$ | Reliability | hours | $N_f$ = failure count |
| 8 | $\text{OEE} = A_v \cdot P_e \cdot Q_u$ | OEE | 0–1 or % | World-class ≥ 85% |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{input}}} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{\text{losses}}}{E_{\text{input}}}$ | Energy efficiency | dimensionless | 0 ≤ η ≤ 1 |
| 10 | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | Settling time (2% criterion) | s | Underdamped 2nd-order |
| 11 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{S_{\text{net}}}{C_{\text{cap}}} \times 100\%$ | Return on Investment | % | $S_{\text{net}}$ annual |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not isolated academic exercises. The same metrics are used by **FANUC, ABB, KUKA, and Yaskawa** in their controller diagnostics, by **ISO 9283** and **ISO 10149** standards, and by automotive OEMs (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki) for line-side acceptance testing of every robot on the shop floor.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Pillar

- **Geometric performance** answers: *"Where can the robot physically go?"*
- **Kinematic performance** answers: *"How fast and how smoothly can it move there?"*
- **Dynamic performance** answers: *"Can its motors actually deliver the forces required to move the payload at that speed?"*
- **Task-oriented performance** answers: *"How many parts per hour does this give the production manager?"*
- **Reliability performance** answers: *"Will the line be running, or will it be down, next Tuesday?"*

A robot that scores well on Pillar I but fails Pillar III is *theoretically* able to reach a point, but cannot deliver a part there under load. **All five pillars must be satisfied simultaneously** for a robot to be deemed fit for an industrial duty cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Repeatability from the Statistical Definition

The ISO 9283 standard defines repeatability as the radius of the sphere that contains a specified fraction (typically 3σ = 99.7%) of the end-effector positions when the robot repeatedly attempts the same commanded pose.

**Step 1 — Define the Sample Mean Position Vector**

For $N$ repeated attempts at the same commanded pose, the $i$-th achieved TCP position is $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)^T$. The sample mean is:

$$\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathbf{x}_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute Position Deviations**

The deviation of the $i$-th attempt from the mean is:

$$\Delta\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Variance-Covariance Matrix**

The $3 \times 3$ scatter matrix of the TCP positions is:

$$\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Delta\mathbf{x}_i \, \Delta\mathbf{x}_i^T$$

Expanding this explicitly:

$$\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\begin{bmatrix}\Delta x_i^2 & \Delta x_i \Delta y_i & \Delta x_i \Delta z_i \\ \Delta y_i \Delta x_i & \Delta y_i^2 & \Delta y_i \Delta z_i \\ \Delta z_i \Delta x_i & \Delta z_i \Delta y_i & \Delta z_i^2\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Per-Axis Standard Deviations**

The diagonal entries give the per-axis variances:

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{S_{11}}, \quad \sigma_y = \sqrt{S_{22}}, \quad \sigma_z = \sqrt{S_{33}}$$

**Step 5 — Combine into a 3σ Repeatability Index**

The most common industrial definition uses three standard deviations combined in quadrature:

$$R = 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2} = 3\sqrt{S_{11} + S_{22} + S_{33}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

A 6-axis articulated robot with $R = 0.05\,\text{mm}$ is classified as *high-precision*; one with $R = 0.5\,\text{mm}$ is *general-purpose*; SCARA arms typically achieve $R = 0.01\,\text{mm}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic (Why the $N-1$?):** The division by $N-1$ instead of $N$ is **Bessel's correction**. It removes the bias introduced by using the same data to estimate both the mean and the variance, giving an *unbiased estimator* of the population variance. KTU examiners often award bonus marks for mentioning this.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time and Throughput Relationship

Consider an automated assembly station with a single robot performing a pick-and-place operation. We want the maximum sustainable throughput.

**Step 1 — Decompose the Cycle**

The cycle time has four additive components:

$$T_c = t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{move,out}} + t_{\text{place}} + t_{\text{move,back}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Trapezoidal Velocity Profile**

Assuming a trapezoidal velocity profile for each move, the time for a move of distance $d$ with max velocity $v_{\max}$ and max acceleration $a_{\max}$ is:

$$t_{\text{move}} = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} + \frac{d}{v_{\max}} \quad \text{(if } d \ge \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_{\max}}\text{)}$$

This holds when the cruise distance dominates; otherwise the move is purely triangular.

**Step 3 — Sum All Contributions**

Substituting:

$$T_c = t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{place}} + 2\left(\frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} + \frac{d}{v_{\max}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute Throughput**

$$\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{place}} + 2\left(\frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} + \frac{d}{v_{\max}}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)**

Given: $t_{\text{pick}} = 0.4\,\text{s}$, $t_{\text{place}} = 0.4\,\text{s}$, $v_{\max} = 1.5\,\text{m/s}$, $a_{\max} = 5\,\text{m/s}^2$, $d = 0.5\,\text{m}$.

Check: $v_{\max}^2 / a_{\max} = 2.25 / 5 = 0.45\,\text{m}$. Since $d = 0.5\,\text{m} > 0.45\,\text{m}$, the trapezoidal formula applies.

$$t_{\text{move}} = \frac{1.5}{5} + \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 0.30 + 0.333 = 0.633\,\text{s}$$

$$T_c = 0.4 + 0.4 + 2(0.633) = 2.467\,\text{s}$$

$$\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{2.467} \approx 1459\,\text{parts/hour}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Yoshikawa Manipulability and the Singular Configuration

For a 2-DOF planar robot with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, the Jacobian is:

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

where $s_1 = \sin q_1$, $c_{12} = \cos(q_1 + q_2)$, etc.

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T$**

$$\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{bmatrix}$$

where

$$a = (L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12})^2 + (L_2 s_{12})^2$$

$$c = (L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12})^2 + (L_2 c_{12})^2$$

$$b = (L_1 s_1 + L_2 s_{12})(L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12}) + (L_2 s_{12})(L_2 c_{12})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Determinant**

Using the identity $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = (\det \mathbf{J})^2$:

$$\det \mathbf{J} = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2$$

Therefore:

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \vert \det \mathbf{J} \vert = L_1 L_2 \vert \sin q_2 \vert$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the Singularity**

When $q_2 = 0$ or $q_2 = \pi$ (links fully extended or fully folded), $w = 0$. The robot loses one degree of freedom in Cartesian space. This is the **boundary singularity** of a 2-DOF arm.

**Step 4 — Velocity Relationship at Singularity**

Near a singularity, even small joint velocities produce unbounded Cartesian velocities:

$$\lim_{q_2 \to 0} \Vert \mathbf{J}^{-1} \Vert = \infty \implies \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\dot{\mathbf{x}} \to \infty$$

This is the geometric reason robots must be **path-planned away from singular configurations** in high-speed material handling.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance vs. Application Suitability

| Application | Dominant Performance Metric | Typical Acceptable Value | Robot Class Best Suited |
|---|---|---|---|
| Automotive spot welding | Repeatability | $R \le 0.2\,\text{mm}$ | 6-axis articulated (heavy payload) |
| Electronics PCB assembly | Accuracy + Resolution | $A \le 0.05\,\text{mm}$ | SCARA / 4-axis |
| Palletizing (boxes) | Payload + Reach | $m_p \ge 100\,\text{kg}$ | 4-axis articulated |
| Pick-and-place (food) | Cycle time | $T_c \le 0.5\,\text{s}$ | Delta / parallel |
| Machine tending | Reach + Stiffness | $r \ge 1.5\,\text{m}$ | 6-axis articulated |
| Adhesive dispensing | Path accuracy | $A \le 0.1\,\text{mm}$ | 6-axis articulated |
| Bin picking | Reach + Repeatability | $R \le 0.3\,\text{mm}$ | 6-axis + vision |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Pillar Performance Analysis Architecture

The following Mermaid block presents a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping how the five performance pillars interact in a real industrial performance analysis workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Requirement Specification URS] --> B[Performance Analysis Engine]
    B --> C1[Pillar I Geometric]
    B --> C2[Pillar II Kinematic]
    B --> C3[Pillar III Dynamic]
    B --> C4[Pillar IV Task Oriented]
    B --> C5[Pillar V Reliability Economic]

    C1 --> D1[Workspace Volume Vw]
    C1 --> D2[Dexterity Map]
    C1 --> D3[Reachable Poses]

    C2 --> E1[Singularity Map]
    C2 --> E2[Max TCP Velocity]
    C2 --> E3[Yoshikawa Index w]

    C3 --> F1[Drive Torque Profile]
    C3 --> F2[Settling Time ts]
    C3 --> F3[Payload Margin]

    C4 --> G1[Cycle Time Tc]
    C4 --> G2[Throughput n dot]
    C4 --> G3[OEE Score]

    C5 --> H1[MTBF Tmtbf]
    C5 --> H2[ROI Percentage]
    C5 --> H3[Energy Efficiency eta]

    D1 --> I[Integrated Performance Score]
    D2 --> I
    D3 --> I
    E1 --> I
    E2 --> I
    E3 --> I
    F1 --> I
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I

    I --> J{Acceptance Test}
    J -- Pass --> K[Deploy to Production Line]
    J -- Fail --> L[Re-engineer or Re-select Robot]
```

## 4.2 Cycle Time Composition — Sub-Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Cycle Start] --> T1[Pick Part from Conveyor]
    T1 --> T2[Accelerate v max]
    T2 --> T3[Coast at v max]
    T3 --> T4[Decelerate to Place Pose]
    T4 --> T5[Place Part in Fixture]
    T5 --> T6[Accelerate Return]
    T6 --> T7[Coast Return]
    T7 --> T8[Decelerate to Pick Pose]
    T8 --> T9[Cycle End]
    T9 --> T0

    T1 -.time.-> X1[t pick]
    T2 -.time.-> X2[t acc]
    T3 -.time.-> X3[t cruise]
    T4 -.time.-> X4[t dec]
    T5 -.time.-> X5[t place]
    T6 -.time.-> X6[t acc ret]
    T7 -.time.-> X7[t cruise ret]
    T8 -.time.-> X8[t dec ret]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Robot Selection Based on Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Define Application] --> Q1{Material Handling or Assembly?}
    Q1 -- Handling --> Q2[Max Payload kg]
    Q1 -- Assembly --> Q3[Required Accuracy mm]

    Q2 --> Q4{Payload ge 50 kg}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R1[Select 4 axis Palletizer]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Throughput gt 60 parts per hour}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R2[Select Delta or SCARA]
    Q5 -- No --> R3[Select 6 axis Articulated]

    Q3 --> Q6{Accuracy le 0.05 mm}
    Q6 -- Yes --> R4[Select SCARA 4 axis]
    Q6 -- No --> Q7{Path Complexity High}
    Q7 -- Yes --> R5[Select 6 axis Articulated]
    Q7 -- No --> R6[Select Cartesian Gantry]

    R1 --> Validate[Run Performance Analysis]
    R2 --> Validate
    R3 --> Validate
    R4 --> Validate
    R5 --> Validate
    R6 --> Validate
    Validate --> End[Deploy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block is a *function* or *stage*, and each arrow is a *data flow* or *control flow*. The diagrams above are not literal physical drawings of a robot — they are **engineered representations** of how the KTU 2024 Scheme's performance analysis framework is implemented in industry. This is the same diagram style used in FANUC's *ROBOGUIDE* software and ABB's *RobotStudio* performance dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms "accuracy" and "repeatability" as applied to industrial robots. How do they differ?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy ($A$):** The ability of a robot to move its end-effector to a position that matches the *commanded* (programmed) pose. It is measured as the deviation between the commanded Cartesian position and the *mean* of the actually achieved positions. Units: $\text{mm}$. $A$ depends on **calibration** quality and kinematic modeling errors.
- **Repeatability ($R$):** The ability of a robot to return to the same *commanded* pose over many repeated cycles, under identical conditions. It is the standard deviation (or 3σ) of achieved positions about the mean. Units: $\text{mm}$. $R$ depends on **mechanical precision** (backlash, encoder resolution, joint compliance).

**Key Difference:** Accuracy measures *closeness to target*; repeatability measures *closeness to itself*. A robot can be repeatable without being accurate (e.g., consistently lands 2 mm off the commanded point every time). KTU examiners expect this distinction explicitly stated. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Yoshikawa's manipulability index? State its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

Yoshikawa's manipulability index is a scalar measure of a robot's kinematic ability to produce end-effector velocities in arbitrary Cartesian directions, given a configuration $\mathbf{q}$. It is defined as:

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det\!\left(\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q})\right)}$$

**Physical Significance:**

- $w > 0$: the robot is away from a singularity and can move in all Cartesian directions.
- $w \to 0$: the robot approaches a singular configuration and loses one or more Cartesian degrees of freedom.
- $w$ is also a measure of the *distance* of the current configuration from the nearest singularity, and is used by motion planners to keep the robot in well-conditioned configurations.

A larger $w$ implies greater dexterity, smoother motion, and lower joint velocities for a given TCP speed. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** A 6-axis articulated robot is required to perform a pick-and-place operation. The pick station is 0.6 m from the place station along a straight line. The robot has $v_{\max} = 1.2\,\text{m/s}$ and $a_{\max} = 4\,\text{m/s}^2$. The gripper open/close time at each station is 0.3 s. Calculate: (i) the time for one move, (ii) the total cycle time, and (iii) the throughput in parts per hour. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of a *singular configuration* in a 2-DOF planar robot. How does Yoshikawa's manipulability index behave at and near a singularity? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the move profile.** The cruise distance needed is

$$d_{\text{cruise}} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_{\max}} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{4} = \frac{1.44}{4} = 0.36\,\text{m}$$

Since the move distance $d = 0.6\,\text{m} > 0.36\,\text{m}$, the velocity profile is **trapezoidal**.

**Step 2 — Calculate the move time.**

$$t_{\text{move}} = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} + \frac{d}{v_{\max}} = \frac{1.2}{4} + \frac{0.6}{1.2} = 0.30 + 0.50 = 0.80\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total cycle time.**

The cycle has two gripper actions (pick and place) and two moves (out and back):

$$T_c = t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{move,out}} + t_{\text{place}} + t_{\text{move,back}}$$

$$T_c = 0.3 + 0.80 + 0.3 + 0.80 = 2.20\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the throughput.**

$$\dot{n} = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{2.20} \approx 1636\,\text{parts/hour}$$

> **Final Answer:** $t_{\text{move}} = 0.80\,\text{s}$, $T_c = 2.20\,\text{s}$, $\dot{n} \approx 1636\,\text{parts/hour}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A **singular configuration** of a 2-DOF planar robot is a joint configuration at which the robot loses one or more Cartesian degrees of freedom, i.e., the Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ becomes rank-deficient and $\det\mathbf{J} = 0$.

For a 2-DOF arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$:

$$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin q_1 - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\cos q_1 + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\det\mathbf{J} = L_1 L_2 \sin q_2$$

**Singularities occur when $q_2 = 0°$ (arm fully extended) and $q_2 = 180°$ (arm fully folded back).** The sketch is described below.

```
         y
         |
         |  Link 2 (folded back, q2=180)
         |   <----
         |   |
    -----+-----+------- x
              |
              | Link 1
              |
              
[Singularity Case 1: q2 = 0, arm fully extended along +x]
    Link 1 + Link 2 fully stretched
    TCP can only move along a circle of radius L1+L2
    Velocity control along the radial direction is LOST

[Singularity Case 2: q2 = 180 deg, arm fully folded]
    Link 2 lies back over Link 1
    TCP is at distance |L1 - L2| from base
    Velocities are indeterminate
```

**Yoshikawa's Manipulability at and Near a Singularity:**

$$w(\mathbf{q}) = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)} = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin q_2 \vert$$

- At $q_2 = 0$ or $\pi$: $w = 0$ exactly.
- As $q_2 \to 0$ or $\pi$: $w \to 0$ continuously.
- Away from these values, $w$ rises, reaching a maximum at $q_2 = 90°$ where $w = L_1 L_2$.

**Engineering Implication:** Path planners must keep the robot in regions where $w$ is large. Near a singularity, even a small Cartesian velocity command requires very large joint velocities (because $\mathbf{J}^{-1}$ becomes ill-conditioned), leading to actuator saturation, vibration, or loss of tracking. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** A SCARA robot is tested for repeatability by commanding the same pose 50 times. The standard deviations of the achieved TCP positions are $\sigma_x = 0.012\,\text{mm}$, $\sigma_y = 0.015\,\text{mm}$, $\sigma_z = 0.008\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the ISO 9283 3σ repeatability. Comment on whether the robot is suitable for electronic PCB assembly (which requires $R \le 0.05\,\text{mm}$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define *Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)*. A robotic assembly cell operates with 92% availability, 88% performance efficiency, and 99% quality rate. Calculate the OEE and comment on its industrial classification. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Recall the ISO 9283 3σ formula.**

$$R = 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$R = 3\sqrt{(0.012)^2 + (0.015)^2 + (0.008)^2}\,\text{mm}$$

Compute each term:

$$(0.012)^2 = 0.000144,\quad (0.015)^2 = 0.000225,\quad (0.008)^2 = 0.000064$$

Sum:

$$\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2 = 0.000144 + 0.000225 + 0.000064 = 0.000433\,\text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root and multiply by 3.**

$$\sqrt{0.000433} = 0.02081\,\text{mm}$$

$$R = 3 \times 0.02081 = 0.0624\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on suitability.**

The required $R$ for PCB assembly is $\le 0.05\,\text{mm}$. The computed $R = 0.0624\,\text{mm} > 0.05\,\text{mm}$, hence the robot is **NOT suitable** for high-precision electronic PCB assembly. Recommendations: (i) re-calibrate the encoders, (ii) reduce backlash through gear inspection, (iii) select a higher-precision SCARA model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition:** OEE is a composite metric that quantifies the *productive effectiveness* of a manufacturing asset (here, a robotic assembly cell). It is the product of three sub-factors:

$$\text{OEE} = A_v \times P_e \times Q_u$$

where
- $A_v$ = **Availability** = (Planned Production Time − Downtime) / Planned Production Time,
- $P_e$ = **Performance** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Operating Time,
- $Q_u$ = **Quality** = Good Pieces / Total Pieces.

**Step 1 — Substitute the given percentages.**

$$\text{OEE} = 0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.99$$

**Step 2 — Compute sequentially.**

$$0.92 \times 0.88 = 0.8096$$

$$0.8096 \times 0.99 = 0.8015$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage.**

$$\text{OEE} = 80.15\%$$

**Step 4 — Industrial classification.**

- World-class benchmark: **OEE ≥ 85%**
- Typical average plant: **60%**
- This cell at **80.15%** is *good* but **below world-class**. The limiting factor is the performance efficiency (88%) — the cell is losing 12% of theoretical throughput. Recommendations: reduce minor stoppages, optimize gripper dwell times, and inspect motion profiles for unnecessary slowdowns. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Performance Analysis Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with repeatability.** Marks are deducted for using the two terms interchangeably. Always give a clear, separate definition.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Numerical answers without $\text{mm}$, $\text{s}$, $\text{kg}$, etc. are penalized by **0.5 to 1 mark per question**.
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps.** Showing only the final numerical answer in a 7-mark sub-question is capped at 3 marks. Always show: (i) formula, (ii) substitution, (iii) evaluation.
> 4. **Ignoring Bessel's correction ($N-1$ vs $N$).** When the repeatability definition is asked, an explicit mention of $N-1$ (unbiased estimator) earns a half-mark bonus on most valuation keys.
> 5. **Not addressing singularities qualitatively.** A pure formula answer on Yoshikawa's index without explaining *why* $w=0$ at a singularity is considered incomplete.
> 6. **OEE answers that miss the benchmark interpretation.** A numerical OEE of 80% is meaningless without stating "world-class ≥ 85%". This is the most common 1-mark loss in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Performance Analysis: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Five Performance Pillars:** Geometric, Kinematic, Dynamic, Task-Oriented, Reliability/Economic. All five must be addressed for a complete analysis.
- ✅ **Accuracy ($A$):** Deviation of *mean* achieved position from *commanded* position. Depends on **calibration**.
- ✅ **Repeatability ($R$):** Standard deviation (3σ) of achieved positions about the *mean*. Depends on **mechanical precision**. **Industrial gold metric.**
- ✅ **Yoshikawa's Manipulability:** $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$. Equals $|\det \mathbf{J}|$. Zero at singularity.
- ✅ **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = t_{\text{pick}} + t_{\text{place}} + 2\!\left(\dfrac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}} + \dfrac{d}{v_{\max}}\right)$ for trapezoidal profile.
- ✅ **Throughput:** $\dot{n} = 3600 / T_c$ parts/hour (single-station, no buffering).
- ✅ **OEE:** $A_v \times P_e \times Q_u$. World-class ≥ 85%.
- ✅ **MTBF:** $T_{MTBF} = T_{\text{op}} / N_f$. Higher is better.
- ✅ **Trapezoidal vs Triangular Profile:** Switch happens at $d = v_{\max}^2 / a_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Singularity Engineering Rule:** Never command motion *through* a singularity; plan around it or use a singularity-robust inverse (e.g., damped least-squares).
- ✅ **ISO 9283:** The KTU-referenced standard for accuracy and repeatability testing. Always cite it in answers.
- ✅ **Bessel's Correction:** Use $N-1$ in the repeatability variance formula.
- ✅ **Units Checklist (must appear in every numerical answer):** $\text{mm}$, $\text{s}$, $\text{m/s}$, $\text{kg}$, $\text{N·m}$, $\text{parts/hour}$, $\%$.
- ✅ **Common Robot-Class-Performance Pairing:** SCARA → high repeatability PCB; Delta → high speed packaging; 6-axis articulated → general assembly; Palletizer → high payload; Cartesian → long-reach gantry.
- ✅ **Economic Justification:** Always pair a performance metric with its production cost impact (e.g., "1 mm increase in repeatability deviation causes 0.5% scrap rate at Maruti's weld line").

---

**End of Module 3 — Performance Analysis Notes**
*Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Metrics for Evaluating Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metrics for Evaluating Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of industrial robotics and material handling, **Metrics for Evaluating Robotic Systems** are defined as a standardized set of quantitative and qualitative performance parameters used to objectively benchmark a robot's mechanical capability, operational efficiency, reliability, and economic viability against design specifications and industrial application requirements. These metrics are essential for **robot selection, system integration, and ROI (Return on Investment)** analysis as per ISO 9283 and ANSI/RIA R15.06 industrial robot performance standards.

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes these metrics into four primary evaluation pillars:

1. **Kinematic & Static Performance** (Accuracy, Repeatability, Resolution)
2. **Dynamic Performance** (Cycle Time, Throughput, TCP Velocity, Acceleration)
3. **Reliability & Maintainability** (MTBF, MTTR, Availability)
4. **Productivity & Economic Performance** (OEE, ROI, Payback Period)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The most heavily examined metrics in Module 3 are **Repeatability vs. Accuracy**, **Resolution**, **MTBF/MTTR**, and **OEE**. A clear conceptual and numerical distinction between *accuracy* and *repeatability* is mandatory for full marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **bowler in a cricket match** asked to repeatedly hit the middle stump.

- **Repeatability** is whether you can hit the *same exact spot* on the pitch again and again, even if that spot is slightly off from the middle stump. A robot with high repeatability can return to the *same position* over many cycles.
- **Accuracy** is whether you actually hit the *middle stump* (the commanded/ideal position). A highly accurate robot hits the *exact target* commanded by the controller.
- **Resolution** is the *smallest possible step* you can adjust your bowling run-up. For a robot, it is the smallest movement increment the controller can issue.

> [!NOTE]
> A robot can be highly **repeatable** but not **accurate** (consistent but biased). It can be **accurate on average** but poorly repeatable. The ideal industrial robot is both highly accurate AND highly repeatable.

**Other Real-World Intuition:**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is like the average kilometers your car travels before a breakdown.
- **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is your factory's "health score" combining uptime, speed, and quality.
- **Payload Capacity** is the maximum "backpack weight" the robot can carry continuously without overheating its motors.
- **Cycle Time** is the "lap time" of the robot for one complete pick-and-place operation.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Benchmark Values

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| **Repeatability** | **±0.02 mm** to **±0.1 mm** (SCARA/Articulated) |
| **Accuracy** | **±0.1 mm** to **±1.0 mm** |
| **Resolution** | **0.001 mm** to **0.01 mm** |
| **MTBF** | **20,000** to **50,000 hours** |
| **Payload (Industrial)** | **3 kg** to **1000+ kg** |
| **TCP Speed** | **1 m/s** to **10 m/s** |
| **OEE World-Class** | **> 85%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Category 1: Kinematic & Static Performance Metrics

### 2.1.1 Accuracy (Absolute Positioning Accuracy)

- **Definition:** The deviation between the **commanded position** (setpoint) and the **mean of the actual attained positions** over many trials.
- **Why it matters:** Determines whether the robot hits the *true* target on the workpiece (critical in machining, laser cutting, assembly of precision parts).
- **How it is measured (ISO 9283):** The robot is commanded to a pose 30 times, the centroid of the 30 points is computed, and the distance from the centroid to the command pose is recorded.

### 2.1.2 Repeatability (Precision)

- **Definition:** The closeness of agreement among repeated measurements of the same commanded pose. It is usually expressed as ± *r*, where *r* is the **radius of the smallest sphere enclosing all attained points**.
- **Why it matters:** In high-volume manufacturing (welding, palletizing, pick-and-place), the robot only needs to return to the *same* spot — not necessarily the geometrically *correct* spot. This is why SCARA robots dominate PCB assembly despite low accuracy.
- **How it is measured:** Standard deviation of the 30 attained positions from their centroid.

### 2.1.3 Resolution

- **Definition:** The smallest incremental change in position that the robot's controller can command. Limited by the **encoder resolution, gear reduction ratio, and interpolation algorithm**.

### 2.1.4 Compliance & Stiffness

- **Compliance** is the deflection per unit applied force (mm/N). Low compliance → high stiffness → better for heavy-duty milling and deburring.

## 2.2 Category 2: Dynamic Performance Metrics

### 2.2.1 Cycle Time (T_c)

- **Definition:** Total elapsed time for the robot to complete **one full operational sequence** (e.g., pick → move → place → return).
- **Components:** Acceleration phase + constant-velocity phase + deceleration phase + dwell time (for gripper actuation).

### 2.2.2 Throughput (R)

- **Definition:** Number of parts processed (or operations completed) **per unit time**, typically per hour or per shift.

### 2.2.3 Tool Center Point (TCP) Velocity and Acceleration

- Maximum linear speed and acceleration of the end-effector along a programmed path. Governed by **jerk limits** to avoid mechanical vibration.

## 2.3 Category 3: Reliability & Maintainability Metrics

### 2.3.1 MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)

- **Definition:** Average operational time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system.

### 2.3.2 MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)

- **Definition:** Average time required to diagnose, repair, and restore the robot to working condition after a failure.

### 2.3.3 Availability (A)

- **Definition:** The fraction of total time the robot is **operationally ready** to perform its intended function.

## 2.4 Category 4: Productivity & Economic Metrics

### 2.4.1 OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

- **Definition:** A composite "world-class" manufacturing metric combining **Availability × Performance × Quality**.

### 2.4.2 ROI (Return on Investment) & Payback Period

- **Definition:** Financial metrics measuring the profitability of a robotic installation.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | $A = \vert P_{commanded} - \bar{P}_{attained} \vert$ | mm | Distance from command to centroid |
| 2 | **Repeatability** | $R = \pm 3\sigma$ (3-sigma rule) or $\pm r$ | mm | $r$ = radius of scatter sphere |
| 3 | **Resolution** | $R_{res} = \dfrac{\text{Encoder pulses/rev} \times \text{Pitch}}{4096 \times \text{Gear Ratio}}$ | mm | Limited by DAC bits |
| 4 | **Cycle Time** | $T_c = t_{acc} + t_{const} + t_{dec} + t_{dwell}$ | s | Includes gripper open/close |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{TH} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \times \eta_{sys}$ | parts/hr | $\eta_{sys}$ = system efficiency |
| 6 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum T_{uptime}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | For repairable systems |
| 7 | **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Includes diagnostics |
| 8 | **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Often expressed as % |
| 9 | **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | dimensionless (0–1) | World-class > 0.85 |
| 10 | **OEE (Expanded)** | $\text{OEE} = \dfrac{\text{Good Parts} \times \text{Ideal Cycle Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | ratio | Gold-standard form |
| 11 | **ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{(\text{Gain} - \text{Investment})}{\text{Investment}} \times 100$ | % | Time-normalized over project life |
| 12 | **Payback Period** | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Net Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | years | Simpler than ROI |
| 13 | **TCP Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{d}{t}$ | m/s | At end-effector |
| 14 | **Stiffness** | $K = \dfrac{F}{\delta}$ | N/mm | Inverse of compliance |
| 15 | **Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{idle}}$ | ratio | Motor thermal limit check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall for KTU Exams:** In the formula table, never confuse **Accuracy** (uses the *centroid* of attained points) with **Repeatability** (uses the *spread/scatter* of attained points). They are *not* the same. The KTU paper setter will deliberately test this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The 3-Sigma Repeatability Bound

The statistical basis for the industrial "$\pm 0.02$ mm" repeatability specification is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Define the attained positions.**
When a robot is commanded $N$ times to the same pose, let the attained Cartesian positions be $P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$.

**Step 2 — Compute the centroid (mean position).**
The mean of the $i$-th coordinate is:

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
$$

This gives the centroid $C = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$.

**Step 3 — Compute the standard deviation along each axis.**

$$
\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

Similarly for $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$.

**Step 4 — Apply the 3-sigma rule.**
For a normal (Gaussian) error distribution, **99.73% of all points** fall within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean. Hence the industrial repeatability is:

$$
R_{rep} = \pm 3\sigma_{3D} \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma_{3D} = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The 3-sigma bound means that out of every 1000 attempts, only **2.7 attempts** will fall *outside* the manufacturer's quoted repeatability — making it a highly reliable industrial contract.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycle Time from Trapezoidal Velocity Profile

A robot arm moves from point A to point B at distance $d$ following a **trapezoidal velocity profile** (constant acceleration $a$, constant velocity $v_{max}$, constant deceleration $a$).

**Step 1 — Compute the distance covered during acceleration.**
Let $t_1$ be the time to accelerate from $0$ to $v_{max}$. Then $t_1 = v_{max}/a$, and the distance covered is:

$$
d_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} a t_1^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{v_{max}}{a}\right)^2 = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2a}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the constant-velocity distance.**
By symmetry, deceleration also covers $d_3 = v_{max}^2 / 2a$. Hence the remaining constant-velocity distance is:

$$
d_2 = d - 2d_1 = d - \frac{v_{max}^2}{a}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the constant-velocity time.**

$$
t_2 = \frac{d_2}{v_{max}} = \frac{d}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a}
$$

**Step 4 — Total motion time.**

$$
T_{motion} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 = \frac{v_{max}}{a} + \left(\frac{d}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a}\right) + \frac{v_{max}}{a} = \frac{d}{v_{max}} + \frac{v_{max}}{a}
$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle time including dwell.**

$$
T_c = T_{motion} + t_{dwell} = \frac{d}{v_{max}} + \frac{v_{max}}{a} + t_{dwell}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Reliability Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> A six-axis articulated robot operates in a two-shift automotive welding cell. Over **one year (8760 hours)**, the robot records **4 failures** with individual repair times of **6 h, 8 h, 2 h, and 12 h**. Calculate MTBF, MTTR, and Availability.

**Step 1 — Total repair time.**

$$
\sum T_{repair} = 6 + 8 + 2 + 12 = 28 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Total uptime.**

$$
\sum T_{uptime} = 8760 - 28 = 8732 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — MTBF.**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{8732}{4} = 2183 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — MTTR.**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{28}{4} = 7 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{2183}{2183 + 7} = \frac{2183}{2190} = 0.9968 = 99.68\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** This 99.68% availability is **above world-class 95%** threshold, indicating excellent preventive maintenance.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — OEE Calculation

> A pick-and-place SCARA robot in an electronics assembly line has:
> - Planned Production Time = 480 min/day
> - Downtime (breakdowns + changeovers) = 60 min
> - Actual Cycle Time = 1.2 s, Ideal Cycle Time = 1.0 s
> - Total Parts Produced = 19,000, Defective Parts = 950
>
> **Compute OEE.**

**Step 1 — Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{480 - 60}{480} = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875
$$

**Step 2 — Performance.**

$$
P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Parts}}{\text{Operating Time}} = \frac{1.0 \times 19000}{420 \times 60} = \frac{19000}{25200} = 0.754
$$

**Step 3 — Quality.**

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts}} = \frac{19000 - 950}{19000} = \frac{18050}{19000} = 0.950
$$

**Step 4 — OEE.**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.754 \times 0.950 = 0.627 = 62.7\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** An OEE of 62.7% indicates significant improvement opportunities. **World-class is > 85%.**

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — ROI Calculation

> A manufacturing firm invests **₹50,00,000** in a robotic MIG welding cell. Annual labor savings = **₹15,00,000**, increased productivity gain = **₹6,00,000**, annual maintenance cost = **₹3,00,000**. Project life = 8 years.
> **Compute ROI and Payback Period.**

**Step 1 — Net Annual Cash Inflow.**

$$
\text{Net} = 15,00,000 + 6,00,000 - 3,00,000 = 18,00,000
$$

**Step 2 — Payback Period.**

$$
T_{pb} = \frac{50,00,000}{18,00,000} = 2.78 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — ROI over 8 years (total gain method).**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{(18,00,000 \times 8) - 50,00,000}{50,00,000} \times 100 = \frac{94,00,000}{50,00,000} \times 100 = 188\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A payback under 3 years and ROI of 188% over the project life makes this a highly viable robotic investment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Robotic System Metrics

The following Mermaid block diagram classifies all the evaluation metrics covered in Module 3, mapped against the **KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment (CO3)** framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robotic System Evaluation Metrics] --> B[Kinematic Performance]
    A --> C[Dynamic Performance]
    A --> D[Reliability Metrics]
    A --> E[Economic Metrics]

    B --> B1[Accuracy]
    B --> B2[Repeatability]
    B --> B3[Resolution]
    B --> B4[Stiffness Compliance]
    B --> B5[Workspace Reach]

    C --> C1[Cycle Time]
    C --> C2[Throughput]
    C --> C3[TCP Velocity]
    C --> C4[Acceleration Jerk]
    C --> C5[Payload Capacity]

    D --> D1[MTBF]
    D --> D2[MTTR]
    D --> D3[Availability]
    D --> D4[Failure Rate Lambda]

    E --> E1[OEE]
    E --> E2[ROI]
    E --> E3[Payback Period]
    E --> E4[Cost per Part]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Robot Performance Evaluation Workflow

This block diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of how metrics are acquired, processed, and benchmarked during a typical ISO 9283 evaluation test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Commanded Pose Setpoint] --> P2[Robot Controller]
    P2 --> P3[Drive System Motors]
    P3 --> P4[Mechanical Manipulator Linkages]
    P4 --> P5[End Effector TCP]
    P5 --> P6[External Laser Tracker Measurement]
    P6 --> P7[Data Acquisition Unit]
    P7 --> P8[Statistical Processor N=30 trials]
    P8 --> P9[Accuracy Computation]
    P8 --> P10[Repeatability 3 Sigma]
    P8 --> P11[Resolution Analysis]
    P9 --> P12[ISO 9283 Report Generation]
    P10 --> P12
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[KTU Benchmark Acceptance]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Metric Selection by Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[Application Selection] --> Q1{Is the task high-volume repetitive?}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Pick and Place or Palletizing?}
    Q1 -->|NO| Q3{Precision critical less than 0.1 mm?}

    Q2 -->|YES| R1[PRIORITIZE Repeatability Resolution Cycle Time]
    Q2 -->|NO| R2[PRIORITIZE Accuracy Reach Payload]

    Q3 -->|YES| R3[PRIORITIZE Accuracy Stiffness Low Thermal Drift]
    Q3 -->|NO| R4[PRIORITIZE ROI MTBF OEE]
```

## 4.4 OEE Composition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OEE[OEE Overall Equipment Effectiveness] --> AV[Availability]
    OEE --> PE[Performance]
    OEE --> QU[Quality]

    AV --> AV1[Uptime minus Downtime divided by Planned Time]
    PE --> PE1[Ideal Cycle Time times Total Parts divided by Operating Time]
    QU --> QU1[Good Parts divided by Total Parts]

    AV1 --> R[Multiply all three factors for final OEE]
    PE1 --> R
    QU1 --> R
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between accuracy and repeatability of an industrial robot. Which one is more critical for a pick-and-place application?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Accuracy** is the ability of the robot to move to a position that matches the *commanded* (target) position. It is measured as the deviation between the commanded pose and the centroid of the *actually attained* positions.
- **Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to return to the *same* position repeatedly over many cycles, measured as the radius of the scatter of attained positions (typically 3-sigma bound).
- For **pick-and-place** applications, **repeatability** is more critical than accuracy, because the robot only needs to return to the *same fixed location* (e.g., a feeder slot) consistently, not to an *absolute* geometric target.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define MTBF and MTTR. How are they used to compute the availability of a robotic cell?**

**Model Answer:**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operational time between two consecutive failures, given by $\text{MTBF} = \sum T_{uptime} / N_{failures}$.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time taken to restore the system after a failure, given by $\text{MTTR} = \sum T_{repair} / N_{failures}$.
- **Availability (A):** Fraction of time the system is ready for use:
$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$
A high A (e.g., 99%) indicates a well-maintained robotic cell suitable for continuous production.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **kinematic and static performance metrics** used to evaluate an industrial robot, with neat sketches of the scatter pattern for accuracy and repeatability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A six-axis robot is commanded to a target pose **30 times** for ISO 9283 testing. The Cartesian X-coordinates of the 30 attained positions have a mean of **499.85 mm** and a standard deviation of **0.015 mm**. The commanded X-coordinate is **500.00 mm**. Compute the **accuracy** and the **repeatability** in the X-direction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The kinematic and static performance metrics are:

1. **Accuracy** — Distance from the *commanded position* to the *centroid* of attained points.
2. **Repeatability** — Radius of the smallest sphere containing all attained points (3-sigma).
3. **Resolution** — Smallest programmable increment.
4. **Compliance / Stiffness** — Deflection per unit force.
5. **Workspace (Reach)** — Set of all reachable TCP points.

```
       Accuracy:                   Repeatability:
       |--- Commanded P            +   +   +
       |                           |   |   |
       + . . Centroid              +---Centroid---+
       (Scatter centered           (Scatter around
        far from target)            centroid)
```

A robot may have **good repeatability but poor accuracy** (the scatter cluster is consistent but offset from the target), or **good accuracy but poor repeatability** (the mean hits the target, but points are widely scattered). The ideal is a tight cluster **centered on the target**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**
- Commanded $X_c = 500.00$ mm
- Mean attained $\bar{X} = 499.85$ mm
- Standard deviation $\sigma_x = 0.015$ mm
- Number of trials $N = 30$

**Step 2 — Calculate Accuracy.**

$$
A_X = \vert X_c - \bar{X} \vert = \vert 500.00 - 499.85 \vert = 0.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Repeatability (3-sigma rule).**

$$
R_X = \pm 3\sigma_x = \pm 3 \times 0.015 = \pm 0.045 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
- The robot has a **systematic bias** of 0.15 mm (likely correctable through robot calibration).
- The **inherent repeatability** of ±0.045 mm is excellent — typical of a high-precision SCARA robot.
- **Conclusion:** The robot is highly *precise* (repeatable) but slightly *inaccurate*. A calibration routine can bring the centroid to 500.00 mm and recover the full 0.15 mm accuracy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Define **OEE**. List and explain its three sub-components with one industrial example each. What is the world-class OEE benchmark? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SCARA robot operates 8 hours/day. In one day, it produced **2400 parts** with an **ideal cycle time of 9.5 s**. It experienced **45 minutes of downtime** and rejected **120 parts**. Calculate the **OEE** and identify the **biggest loss category** with a one-line recommendation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a composite metric (multiplied by 0–1 or 0–100%) that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, relative to its theoretical maximum capability.

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

The three sub-components are:

| Component | Formula | Industrial Example | Typical Loss |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability (A)** | $\frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | Unplanned breakdown of welding robot | Equipment failure, setup |
| **Performance (P)** | $\frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Parts}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | Robot running at 80% of rated speed | Idling, minor stops, reduced speed |
| **Quality (Q)** | $\frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts}}$ | 95 parts out of 100 are within tolerance | Defects, rework, startup rejects |

**World-class OEE benchmark: ≥ 85%** (per SEMI E10 and industry consensus by TPM practitioners).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**
- Planned Production Time $= 8 \times 60 = 480$ min
- Downtime $= 45$ min
- Total parts $= 2400$
- Ideal cycle time $= 9.5$ s
- Rejected parts $= 120$

**Step 2 — Compute Operating Time.**

$$
T_{op} = 480 - 45 = 435 \text{ min} = 435 \times 60 = 26100 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Availability.**

$$
A = \frac{435}{480} = 0.9063
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Performance.**

$$
P = \frac{9.5 \times 2400}{26100} = \frac{22800}{26100} = 0.8736
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Quality.**

$$
Q = \frac{2400 - 120}{2400} = \frac{2280}{2400} = 0.95
$$

**Step 6 — Compute OEE.**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9063 \times 0.8736 \times 0.95 = 0.7521 = 75.21\%
$$

**Step 7 — Identify Biggest Loss.**
- Availability loss: $1 - 0.9063 = 9.37\%$
- Performance loss: $1 - 0.8736 = 12.64\%$ ← **BIGGEST**
- Quality loss: $1 - 0.9500 = 5.00\%$

**Recommendation:** The biggest loss is in *Performance* — the SCARA is running significantly slower than its ideal cycle. Conduct a **motion-profile optimization** and check for **jerk-limit / acceleration de-rating** in the controller. Tightening these could push OEE above 85%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the *mean* ($\bar{X}$) with *standard deviation* ($\sigma$) when computing accuracy vs. repeatability. The mean defines accuracy; the spread defines repeatability. **(Loss: up to 4 marks)**
> 2. **Do not** compute OEE by simply averaging the three sub-components additively. OEE is a **multiplicative** composite — never add them. **(Loss: 3 marks)**
> 3. **Do not** forget to convert minutes to seconds (or vice versa) consistently in cycle-time / OEE problems. Mixing units is the most common numerical error. **(Loss: 2 marks)**
> 4. **Do not** state "repeatability = accuracy". They are independent metrics; the answer must include the explicit distinction. **(Loss: 2 marks)**
> 5. **Do not** assume MTBF alone determines reliability. MTTR is equally important — a robot with MTBF = 5000 h but MTTR = 50 h has availability only 99%, whereas MTBF = 5000 h and MTTR = 1 h gives 99.98%. **(Loss: 2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3**

- ⭐ **Accuracy** = distance from *commanded* pose to *centroid* of attained points.
- ⭐ **Repeatability** = $\pm 3\sigma$ scatter radius of attained points — *not* the same as accuracy.
- ⭐ **Resolution** = smallest programmable increment; limited by encoder bits and gear ratio.
- ⭐ **Cycle Time** $T_c = t_{acc} + t_{const} + t_{dec} + t_{dwell}$.
- ⭐ **Throughput** $\text{TH} = 3600 \cdot \eta_{sys} / T_c$ parts/hour.
- ⭐ **MTBF** = $\sum T_{uptime} / N_{failures}$.
- ⭐ **MTTR** = $\sum T_{repair} / N_{failures}$.
- ⭐ **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — world-class ≥ 99%.
- ⭐ **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality — world-class ≥ 85%.
- ⭐ **ROI** = $(\text{Gain} - \text{Investment})/\text{Investment} \times 100$.
- ⭐ **Payback Period** = Initial Investment / Net Annual Cash Inflow.
- ⭐ For **pick-and-place** → **repeatability** dominates; for **machining/laser cutting** → **accuracy** dominates.
- ⭐ **Payload** rating is at *continuous duty*; peak payloads are typically 1.5–2× the rated value for short durations.
- ⭐ **Compliance** is the *inverse* of **stiffness** — both are critical in force-controlled assembly.
- ⭐ **Workspace** is the set of all reachable TCP positions; **dexterity** is the ability to approach a point from multiple orientations.
- ⭐ Always cite **ISO 9283** when defining accuracy/repeatability in KTU answers.
- ⭐ Always cite **SEMI E10 / TPM benchmarks** when discussing OEE world-class values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability, Precision, and Repeatability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability, Precision, and Repeatability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In industrial robotics, the three pillars that determine the **production-grade acceptability** of any robot are **Reliability, Precision, and Repeatability**. These three performance indices are the primary metrics by which a robotic manipulator is judged during KTU board examinations and by manufacturing industries during robot selection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> **Reliability** is the probability that a robot will perform its intended function under stated conditions, for a specified period of time, without failure.
> **Precision** is the ability of the robot to position its end-effector at an exact target point in space (a measure of closeness to the *intended* target).
> **Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to return to the same position in space repeatedly, under the same operating conditions, after a number of cycles.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are a **basketball player** practicing free throws:

- **Reliability**: The probability that you will successfully make a free throw every time you attempt one, every day, for the next 5 years without your shooting arm "failing." It is about *long-term dependable operation*.
- **Precision**: When you aim at the *center of the hoop* (the desired point), how close does the ball land to that center on average? If the ball lands on the rim or the backboard consistently, you are not precise.
- **Repeatability**: If you shoot the ball 50 times from the same spot, how *tightly clustered* are all the balls? Even if the cluster is on the backboard, the cluster being tight means high repeatability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** A robot can be highly **repeatable** but NOT **precise** (it consistently goes to the *wrong* point). A robot can be **precise** but have *poor repeatability* (it aims at the center on average, but the scatter is huge). Precision and Repeatability are *independent* performance metrics.

### 1.2 Companion Metrics

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Closeness of a measured/positioned value to the **true** desired value (combines precision + systematic error). |
| **Resolution** | The **smallest incremental movement** the robot's control system can command and execute. |
| **MTBF** | **Mean Time Between Failures** — a core reliability metric. |
| **Drift** | Gradual deviation of performance over time (affects long-term reliability). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often interchange "precision" and "accuracy." In KTU 2024 evaluations, **Precision = Random Error**, while **Accuracy = Total Error (Random + Systematic)**. Definitions are strictly tested for 2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reliability — Deep Analysis

Reliability is modeled as a **probabilistic function of time**. The foundational assumptions for most robotic reliability models are:

1. The system is **non-repairable** (or considered during a single mission).
2. Failures are **independent** events.
3. The failure rate is **constant** (memoryless exponential distribution).

**Why Constant Failure Rate?** Most industrial robot components (servo motors, gear reducers, encoders) follow a "useful life" period where the failure rate $\lambda$ is roughly constant (the flat region of the classic "bathtub curve").

**Mathematical Foundation:**

The reliability function is derived from the probability of surviving a time interval:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

The unreliability (probability of failure) is:

$$F(t) = 1 - R(t)$$

**System Reliability Composition:**

- For components arranged in **Series** (system fails if any one fails):

$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n$$

- For components arranged in **Parallel** (redundancy, system works if at least one works):

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

**Why this matters:** Robotic arms have many sub-systems (controller, drives, sensors) connected in **series electrically**; if any one fails, the entire robot halts. Hence, robot reliability is the **product** of sub-system reliabilities.

### 2.2 Precision and Repeatability — Deep Analysis

These are statistical measures derived from a **Positioning Test (ISO 9283 Standard)**.

In the standard test, the robot is commanded to reach a specific point $P$ in its workspace **$N$ times** (typically $N = 30$ cycles). The actual achieved points are measured, giving a cloud of points.

**Step 1 — Mean Position (Centroid of the cloud):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$$

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation along each axis:**

$$s_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

**Step 3 — Position Repeatability ($RP$):** Defined as the radius of a sphere that contains the **scatter of positions** with a certain probability. KTU/ISO uses $3\sigma$ (covers 99.7% of points under Gaussian distribution):

$$RP = \pm 3\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2}$$

**Step 4 — Precision (Distance between commanded point and mean achieved point):**

$$AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{y} - y_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{z} - z_{cmd})^2}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability at time $t$ (dimensionless) |
| $\lambda$ | $\dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Failure rate (failures/hour) |
| MTBF | $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (hours) |
| MTTR | Average repair time | Mean Time To Repair (hours) |
| Availability $A$ | $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Operational uptime ratio |
| $R_{series}$ | $\prod R_i$ | Series system reliability |
| $R_{parallel}$ | $1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ | Parallel/redundant system reliability |
| $\bar{x}$ | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum x_i$ | Mean position along $x$-axis (mm) |
| $s_x$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$ | Standard deviation along $x$ (mm) |
| $RP$ | $\pm 3\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2}$ | Position repeatability (mm) |
| $AP$ | $\sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_c)^2+(\bar{y}-y_c)^2+(\bar{z}-z_c)^2}$ | Accuracy/Precision distance (mm) |
| Resolution | Smallest commanded increment | (mm or $\mu$m) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often use $\pm 2\sigma$ (95.4%) instead of $\pm 3\sigma$ (99.7%). Always check the probability factor given in the question stem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reliability Function from Failure Rate

**Step 1 — Define the instantaneous failure rate $\lambda(t)$:**

The failure rate is the conditional probability of failure in the next small interval $dt$, given survival up to time $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

where $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ is the **probability density of failure**.

**Step 2 — Note that $R(t) + F(t) = 1$ (a part either survives or fails):**

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -f(t)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the failure rate equation:**

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $dt$ and integrate** from $0$ to $t$:
