$$\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\int_0^t \frac{1}{R(\tau)}\frac{dR(\tau)}{d\tau}\,d\tau = -\ln[R(t)] + \ln[R(0)]$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial condition** $R(0) = 1$ (the system is working at time zero with probability 1):

$$\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\ln[R(t)]$$

**Step 6 — For constant failure rate** $\lambda(t) = \lambda$:

$$\lambda t = -\ln[R(t)] \quad \Rightarrow \quad R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

MTBF is the **expected value of the time to failure**:

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt = \int_0^{\infty} t \cdot \left(-\frac{dR(t)}{dt}\right)dt$$

**Step 1 — Apply integration by parts:** $u = t$, $dv = -dR(t)$, so $du = dt$, $v = -R(t)$:

$$\text{MTBF} = \left[-t \cdot R(t)\right]_0^{\infty} + \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the boundary term:** As $t \to \infty$, $R(t) \to 0$ fast enough that $t \cdot R(t) \to 0$. At $t = 0$, the term is $0 \cdot 1 = 0$. So the boundary term vanishes.

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the exponential reliability function:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda} \qquad \blacksquare$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: Series-Parallel Reliability Calculation

**Problem:** A robot has three subsystems:
- Servo Drive A: $R_A = 0.99$
- Controller B: $R_B = 0.98$  
- Sensor Array C: $R_C = 0.95$

A backup Sensor Array D ($R_D = 0.95$) is wired **in parallel** with C. Compute overall reliability.

**Step 1 — Combine C and D (parallel redundancy):**

$$R_{CD} = 1 - (1 - R_C)(1 - R_D) = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - (0.05)(0.05)$$

$$R_{CD} = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$

**Step 2 — Now A, B, and the combined block CD are in series:**

$$R_{sys} = R_A \times R_B \times R_{CD} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.9975$$

$$R_{sys} = 0.9702 \times 0.9975 = 0.9678$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** The overall robot reliability is **0.9678** (96.78% probability of no failure over the mission).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Performance Metrics

| Metric | What it Measures | Computation Basis | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | Smallest controllable step | Hardware (encoder bits, motor step) | 0.01 mm – 0.1 mm |
| **Repeatability** | Scatter of repeated positions | Statistical ($3\sigma$ radius) | $\pm 0.02$ mm – $\pm 0.5$ mm |
| **Precision** | Offset of mean from target | Mean position vs. command | 0.1 mm – 5 mm |
| **Accuracy** | Total error (precision + bias) | Includes systematic errors | 0.5 mm – 10 mm |
| **Reliability** | Long-term dependable operation | Failure rate $\lambda$ | $R(1\text{yr}) > 0.95$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Robot Performance Testing Architecture (ISO 9283)

This block diagram represents the **functional flow** of a standardized robot positioning test, as used in KTU 2024 laboratory assessments and industrial acceptance tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Commanded Pose P_c] --> B[Robot Controller]
    B --> C[Servo Drives and Joint Actuators]
    C --> D[Robot Manipulator End Effector]
    D --> E[Actual Pose P_a N cycles]
    E --> F[External Measurement Device e.g. Laser Tracker]
    F --> G[Data Acquisition System]
    G --> H[Statistical Processor]
    H --> I[Mean Position Vector]
    H --> J[Standard Deviation along x y z]
    I --> K[Precision AP Calculation]
    J --> L[Repeatability RP Calculation]
    K --> M[Performance Report]
    L --> M
    H --> N[Reliability and Availability Indices]
    N --> M
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style M fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#065f46,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: Relationships Between the Three Metrics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reliability] --> A1[Probabilistic over time]
    A1 --> A2[MTBF and MTTR]
    A2 --> A3[Availability Ratio]
    B[Precision] --> B1[Single target closeness]
    B1 --> B2[Mean offset from command]
    B2 --> B3[AP formula]
    C[Repeatability] --> C1[Multiple cycle clustering]
    C1 --> C2[Standard deviation scatter]
    C2 --> C3[RP equals 3 sigma]
    D[Resolution] --> D1[Smallest increment]
    D1 --> D2[Hardware limited]
    E[System Performance] --> A
    E --> B
    E --> C
    E --> D
    style E fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#065f46,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#5b21b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#92400e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Selecting Between Precision vs. Repeatability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Robot Performance Test] --> Q1{Is the robot intended for the same task repeatedly?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, e.g. spot welding| R1[Prioritize REPEATABILITY]
    Q1 -->|No, e.g. varied assembly| Q2{Does the task require exact coordinate matching?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, e.g. PCB insertion| R2[Prioritize PRECISION and ACCURACY]
    Q2 -->|No, e.g. rough pick and place| R3[Relaxed metrics acceptable]
    R1 --> F[Choose Robot with Low RP, high MTBF]
    R2 --> F
    R3 --> F
    style S fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#065f46,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Infant Mortality: High lambda] -->|Burn in period| B[Useful Life: Constant lambda]
    B -->|Wear out begins| C[Wear Out: Increasing lambda]
    A1[Reliability high but R decreasing] --> A
    B1[Exponential R of t valid here] --> B
    C1[Robot requires maintenance] --> C
    style A fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#065f46,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#92400e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Precision and Repeatability of an industrial robot with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Precision | Repeatability |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Ability to reach the *exact intended* target point. | Ability to return to the *same* point repeatedly. |
| What it measures | Closeness of the *mean* position to the *commanded* position. | Closeness of *consecutive* positions to each other. |
| Example | A robot places a peg exactly in the center hole of a gear every cycle. | A robot places the peg in the *same* hole location (even if off-center) every cycle with minimal scatter. |
| Statistical basis | Mean offset distance (AP formula). | Standard deviation of positions (RP formula). |
| Independent of | Systematic bias (which lowers precision but not repeatability). | Calibration accuracy (which lowers precision). |

(1 mark for clear definitions, 1 mark for the table/differences, 1 mark for the example)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define MTBF and MTTR. Derive the relationship between system availability and these parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. (1 mark)
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time taken to restore the system to working condition after a failure. (1 mark)
- **Availability Equation:**

$$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

(1 mark for the derivation, stating that total cycle time = MTBF + MTTR)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A robot is commanded to reach point $P(100, 200, 300)$ mm. After 30 cycles, the following statistical data is obtained (all in mm):

$$s_x = 0.02, \quad s_y = 0.03, \quad s_z = 0.04$$

The mean achieved position is $(100.15, 199.85, 300.10)$ mm.

Calculate:
1. The **Position Repeatability (RP)** using $3\sigma$ criterion.
2. The **Precision (AP)** in mm.
3. Comment on whether the robot is suitable for a precision electronics assembly task requiring RP $< 0.15$ mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic assembly cell contains 4 components in series with reliabilities $0.98, 0.97, 0.95, 0.92$ for a 1000-hour mission.

1. Compute the overall system reliability.
2. If a duplicate is added in parallel to the *weakest* component, compute the new reliability.
3. Compute the MTBF if the failure rate is assumed constant. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Position Repeatability (RP) calculation:**

$$RP = \pm 3\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2} = \pm 3\sqrt{(0.02)^2 + (0.03)^2 + (0.04)^2}$$

$$RP = \pm 3\sqrt{0.0004 + 0.0009 + 0.0016} = \pm 3\sqrt{0.0029}$$

$$RP = \pm 3 \times 0.05385 = \pm 0.1616 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.162 \text{ mm}$$

(3 marks for correct calculation)

**Step 2 — Precision (AP) calculation:**

$$AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{y} - y_{cmd})^2 + (\bar{z} - z_{cmd})^2}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{(100.15 - 100)^2 + (199.85 - 200)^2 + (300.10 - 300)^2}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{(0.15)^2 + (-0.15)^2 + (0.10)^2} = \sqrt{0.0225 + 0.0225 + 0.01}$$

$$AP = \sqrt{0.0550} = 0.2345 \text{ mm} \approx 0.235 \text{ mm}$$

(3 marks for correct calculation)

**Step 3 — Comment on Suitability:**

The required RP is $< 0.15$ mm, but the calculated RP is $0.162$ mm. 

**Conclusion:** The robot is **NOT suitable** for this task because the repeatability ($0.162$ mm) exceeds the required $0.15$ mm tolerance. We would need to either calibrate the robot to reduce scatter or select a higher-precision robot model.

(1 mark for the final comparison and decision)

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Series reliability:**

$$R_{sys} = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.92$$

Calculating step by step:
- $0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506$
- $0.95 \times 0.92 = 0.874$
- $R_{sys} = 0.9506 \times 0.874 = 0.8308$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Adding parallel redundancy to the weakest component (0.92):**

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.92)(1 - 0.92) = 1 - (0.08)(0.08) = 1 - 0.0064 = 0.9936$$

**New system reliability:**

$$R_{new} = 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.9936$$

- $0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506$
- $0.95 \times 0.9936 = 0.94392$
- $R_{new} = 0.9506 \times 0.94392 = 0.8972$

(3 marks)

**Step 3 — MTBF computation:**

Using the relationship for series systems, the system failure rate is:

$$\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

The reliability of the new system is $R(1000) = 0.8972$:

$$0.8972 = e^{-\lambda_{sys} \times 1000}$$

$$\lambda_{sys} = -\frac{\ln(0.8972)}{1000} = \frac{0.1085}{1000} = 1.085 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}$$

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \frac{1}{1.085 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9217 \text{ hours}$$

(2 marks)

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **ISO 9283 test procedure** for determining position repeatability and accuracy of a 6-axis industrial robot. Mention the number of cycles typically used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SCARA robot used in a pick-and-place application has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures per hour.

1. Calculate the reliability for a 500-hour mission.
2. Calculate the MTBF.
3. If the MTTR is 4 hours, find the steady-state availability.
4. If the system has 3 critical modules in series each with the same $\lambda$, find the overall system reliability for the same 500-hour mission. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**ISO 9283 Position Repeatability and Accuracy Test Procedure:**

(5 marks for the procedure, 1 mark for cycle count, 1 mark for diagram explanation)

1. **Test Cube Selection:** Define a cubic test volume within the robot's working envelope. Choose 5 poses (corner and center points) for measurement.
2. **Cycle Definition:** Each cycle consists of the robot moving from a *home position* to the *test pose*, dwelling briefly, and returning to home.
3. **Repetition:** The robot performs **$N = 30$ cycles** for each test pose (as per ISO 9283 standard).
4. **Measurement:** The end-effector position is measured at each test pose using an external measurement device (e.g., laser tracker, coordinate measuring machine).
5. **Data Collection:** Record the $(x, y, z)$ coordinates of the end-effector for all 30 cycles.
6. **Statistical Analysis:**
   - Compute mean position $(\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$.
   - Compute standard deviation $s_x, s_y, s_z$.
   - Compute **Position Repeatability** $RP = \pm 3\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2}$.
   - Compute **Accuracy (AP)** as the distance from commanded pose to mean achieved pose.
7. **Reporting:** Report the average and worst-case RP and AP across all 5 test poses.

(Reference the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for visualization support.)

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1 — Reliability for 500-hour mission:**

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0002 \times 500} = e^{-0.1}$$

$$R(500) = 0.9048$$

(1 mark)

**Step 2 — MTBF calculation:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Steady-State Availability:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 4} = \frac{5000}{5004}$$

$$A = 0.9992 = 99.92\%$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Three Modules in Series:**

For 3 modules each with $\lambda = 0.0002$, the system failure rate is:

$$\lambda_{sys} = 3 \times 0.0002 = 0.0006 \text{ failures/hour}$$

System reliability at $t = 500$ hours:

$$R_{sys}(500) = e^{-0.0006 \times 500} = e^{-0.3} = 0.7408$$

(3 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pm$ sign on RP.** Always write $RP = \pm 0.162$ mm, not just $0.162$ mm. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing $N$ vs. $N-1$ in the standard deviation formula.** Bessel's correction requires $N-1$ in the denominator for *sample* standard deviation. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Using $1\sigma$ instead of $3\sigma$ for repeatability.** ISO 9283 specifies $3\sigma$, but check the question stem. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 4. **In Series-Parallel problems, mis-ordering the operations.** Always simplify the parallel block *first*, then multiply in series. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units.** $\lambda$ in failures/hour means $t$ must also be in hours. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Reliability, Precision, Repeatability**

- **Reliability** is a *time-based probability* — never a fixed number. Always specify the time $t$.
- The fundamental reliability equation is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, valid for **constant failure rate** systems.
- **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$ (units = hours). **MTTR** is the average repair time. **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$.
- **Series systems**: multiply reliabilities. **Parallel/redundant systems**: $1 - \prod(1-R_i)$.
- **Precision** = closeness of *mean* position to *commanded* position. Formula: $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x}-x_c)^2 + (\bar{y}-y_c)^2 + (\bar{z}-z_c)^2}$.
- **Repeatability** = tightness of the cluster of repeated positions. Formula: $RP = \pm 3\sqrt{s_x^2 + s_y^2 + s_z^2}$.
- **Resolution** is the hardware limit — it sets the **floor** for achievable repeatability (you cannot be more repeatable than your resolution).
- A robot can be **highly repeatable but inaccurate** (systematic calibration error). A robot can be **accurate on average but not repeatable** (high random scatter).
- **ISO 9283** is the international standard. It mandates **$N = 30$ cycles** and a **$3\sigma$** criterion by default.
- For sample standard deviation, use $N-1$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction).
- The **bathtub curve** has three regions: infant mortality (decreasing $\lambda$), useful life (constant $\lambda$), wear-out (increasing $\lambda$). The constant $\lambda$ model is valid only in the useful life region.
- **Practical industrial benchmark**: A modern 6-axis articulated robot has RP in the range of $\pm 0.02$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm; SCARA robots can achieve $\pm 0.01$ mm.
- **Key trick for KTU problems**: When you see "**mission time**" or "**operating period**," it is the value of $t$ in $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies on Performance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies on Performance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Analysis in Robotic Assembly and Material Handling** is the systematic, quantitative evaluation of an automated work-cell, assembly line, or material-flow system using a defined set of **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** — such as cycle time, throughput, availability, Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE), Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF), and Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) — in order to benchmark, diagnose bottlenecks, justify redesign, and validate Return on Investment (ROI) against stated production targets.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a **case study** is a structured problem statement — often drawn from automotive, electronics, pharmaceutical, or warehousing domains — in which the student is given a real or simulated robotic work-cell configuration, its operating data, and is required to compute, compare, and comment on the system's performance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Restaurant Kitchen as a Robotic Cell**
> Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen. The chef (robot arm) plates dishes (assembles parts), the waiter (conveyor) carries them to tables, and the dishwasher (vision system) inspects them. *Performance analysis* is the manager standing at the door with a stopwatch and clipboard, asking:
> - *How fast does the chef actually plate compared to his theoretical best?* (Performance)
> - *How often is the chef off duty (sick, on break)?* (Availability)
> - *How many dishes go out perfect vs. sent back?* (Quality)
> - *What is the kitchen's true productive output?* (OEE)
> - *When the stove breaks, how long until it is fixed?* (MTTR / MTBF)
>
> The same questions, applied to a robotic assembly line, give us its performance fingerprint.

### 1.3 Core Performance Metric Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All performance metrics in robotic systems are anchored to the three pillars of **OEE**:
> **A × P × Q = 100%** is the *world-class* target. Anything above **85%** is considered world-class in manufacturing; the global average for robotic cells typically lies between **55% and 65%**.

| Pillar | What it Measures | Typical Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability (A)** | Uptime vs. planned time | Preventive maintenance, MTTR reduction |
| **Performance (P)** | Actual speed vs. design speed | Cycle-time optimization, motion planning |
| **Quality (Q)** | Conforming units vs. total | Vision accuracy, calibration, fixture design |
| **OEE** | A × P × Q (combined) | Holistic redesign |

### 1.4 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU 2024 Evaluation

Case-study questions are **High-Yield** in KTU End Semester Examinations because they test **CO (Course Outcome) levels Apply, Analyze, and Evaluate** simultaneously. The student is expected to move from raw data → computation → diagnostic commentary → redesign recommendation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Performance Analysis Workflow (Structured Logic)

A robust performance analysis of a robotic assembly / material-handling system is executed in **six logical steps**. Each step feeds the next, and skipping any one of them produces a misleading conclusion.

- **Step 1 — Define the System Boundary & Goal**
  Identify whether the system under study is a *single-robot cell*, a *multi-robot line*, or an *AGV/Mobile-robot fleet*. State the production target explicitly (e.g., "120 units/hour").
- **Step 2 — Collect Operational Data**
  Capture *Planned Production Time (PPT)*, *Actual Run Time*, *Downtime*, *Total Pieces Produced*, *Good Pieces*, and *Ideal Cycle Time (ICT)*. Data must be from a *defined observation window* (typically one shift, one week, or one month).
- **Step 3 — Compute Primary KPIs (Availability, Performance, Quality)**
  Apply the OEE sub-formulas below. These are *dimensionless ratios* expressed as percentages.
- **Step 4 — Compute Reliability KPIs (MTBF, MTTR)**
  These describe *stochastic* behaviour — i.e., how the system degrades and recovers over time.
- **Step 5 — Diagnose the Bottleneck**
  The pillar with the *lowest score* is the bottleneck. A low A implies maintenance issues; a low P implies motion/sequencing inefficiency; a low Q implies process or calibration issues.
- **Step 6 — Propose & Justify a Redesign**
  Recommend a hardware / software / scheduling change and predict the new OEE. The redesign must be *engineering-feasible* and *cost-justifiable*.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why start with the system boundary?** Because a robot cell embedded in a slower conveyor line will have *artificially low* OEE if boundary is drawn too wide. Conversely, a too-narrow boundary hides upstream starvation. The boundary determines whether the answer is *a robot problem* or *a system problem*.
>
> **Why are MTBF and MTTR separated from OEE?** OEE is a *short-window* productivity metric; MTBF/MTTR are *long-term reliability* metrics. A cell may have 90% OEE today but a MTBF of 50 hours, meaning a catastrophic failure is imminent.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Bank — memorize these; they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B case-study question.**

| # | KPI | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{\text{PPT} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{PPT}}$ | % | Downtime includes setup, breakdowns, changeovers |
| 2 | **Performance (P)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | % | Cannot exceed 100% in this definition |
| 3 | **Quality (Q)** | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | % | Rework pieces count as defects |
| 4 | **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | % | World-class $\geq 85\%$ |
| 5 | **Throughput (TP)** | $\text{TP} = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | pcs/hr or pcs/min | |
| 6 | **Actual Cycle Time** | $\text{CT}_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Total Pieces}}$ | sec/pc | |
| 7 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability indicator |
| 8 | **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Maintainability indicator |
| 9 | **Inherent Availability** | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | % | Long-run asymptotic availability |
| 10 | **Robot Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Time Available}}$ | % | For idle-time analysis |
| 11 | **ROI (Simple)** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Savings}}{\text{Capital Investment}} \times 100\%$ | % | Used to justify automation |
| 12 | **Payback Period** | $\text{PP} = \dfrac{\text{Capital Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$ | years | Industry target $\leq 3$ yrs |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEM weld shops** benchmark robot lines on OEE to qualify suppliers; lines below 75% trigger Six-Sigma projects.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging lines** must report *batch-level* Quality, and FDA audits scrutinize MTTR.
- **E-commerce fulfilment centres** use AGV-fleet throughput and utilization to size fleet additions before peak season.
- **Electronics SMT lines** measure placement-rate vs. rated speed; a Performance score below 90% typically signals a feeder's misfeed or a vision-inspection bottleneck.

### 2.5 Diagnostic Decision Tree (Conceptual)

> The lowest of A, P, Q dictates the *corrective action family*:
> - Low **A** → Maintenance engineering: preventive schedule, predictive sensors, MTTR reduction via better diagnostics.
> - Low **P** → Controls engineering: trajectory smoothing, gripper redesign, conveyor synchronisation, multi-robot task scheduling.
> - Low **Q** → Process engineering: calibration, fixturing, vision threshold tuning, raw-material variance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the OEE Product Form

The OEE metric is **not a definition but a derived identity**. It arises from collapsing three independent loss-categories (Availability, Performance, Quality) into a single multiplicative index. We now derive it rigorously so that the student can defend it in an exam derivation question.

**Premise:** Let a system be observed for a *Planned Production Time* $T_P$. Within this window:

- $T_P$ is reduced by *Downtime* $D$, leaving *Operating Time* $T_O = T_P - D$.
- Within $T_O$, the system produces $N$ total pieces. Of these, $G$ are good and $N - G$ are defective.
- The *Ideal Cycle Time* is $T_I$ (sec/pc) — the *theoretical* fastest possible cycle, e.g., from the robot's spec sheet.
- The *Actual Cycle Time* is $T_A = T_O / N$.

By definition:

$$A = \frac{T_O}{T_P}$$

$$P = \frac{T_I \cdot N}{T_O}$$

$$Q = \frac{G}{N}$$

Multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \times P \times Q
&= \frac{T_O}{T_P} \times \frac{T_I \cdot N}{T_O} \times \frac{G}{N} \\
&= \frac{T_O \cdot T_I \cdot N \cdot G}{T_P \cdot T_O \cdot N} \\
&= \frac{T_I \cdot G}{T_P}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic — Row 1 to Row 2:** The $T_O$ terms in numerator (from A) and denominator (from P) cancel. The $N$ terms in numerator (from P) and denominator (from Q) cancel. What survives is the ratio of the *time it would take to make all good pieces at ideal speed* ($T_I \cdot G$) to the *total time we had* ($T_P$).

**Interpretation:** $\text{OEE} = \dfrac{T_I \cdot G}{T_P}$ is the *fraction of scheduled time used for value-adding production*. This is a powerful, intuitive reformulation — perfect for an exam answer.

### 3.2 Derivation of Inherent Availability from MTBF & MTTR

Consider a long observation window divided into $n$ failure-repair cycles. Each cycle has an *uptime* $\tau_i$ and a *downtime* $\delta_i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\tau_i \\
\text{MTTR} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\delta_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The long-run *fraction of time operational* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{\sum \tau_i}{\sum \tau_i + \sum \delta_i} = \frac{n \cdot \text{MTBF}}{n \cdot \text{MTBF} + n \cdot \text{MTTR}} \\
&= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor $n$ in numerator and denominator cancels. This is the *asymptotic* availability the system approaches over many cycles — it is the *theoretical ceiling* of Availability when setup and scheduled downtime are excluded.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Solved Case for Reference)

**Given Data (from a sample KTU-style case):**
- Planned Production Time $T_P = 480$ min (one 8-hour shift)
- Unplanned Downtime $D = 60$ min
- Setup / Changeover $S = 20$ min (also counted as downtime for OEE)
- Total Pieces Produced $N = 1{,}500$
- Good Pieces $G = 1{,}425$
- Ideal Cycle Time $T_I = 15$ sec/pc

**Step 1 — Operating Time:**

$$
T_O = T_P - D - S = 480 - 60 - 20 = 400 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{T_O}{T_P} = \frac{400}{480} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**Step 3 — Performance:**

First, convert units so $T_I$ is in min/pc:

$$
T_I = \frac{15}{60} = 0.25 \text{ min/pc}
$$

$$
P = \frac{T_I \cdot N}{T_O} = \frac{0.25 \times 1500}{400} = \frac{375}{400} = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
$$

**Step 4 — Quality:**

$$
Q = \frac{G}{N} = \frac{1425}{1500} = 0.9500 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 5 — OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.8333 \times 0.9375 \times 0.9500 = 0.7422 = 74.22\%
$$

**Step 6 — Throughput:**

$$
\text{TP} = \frac{G}{T_O} = \frac{1425}{400 \text{ min}} = 3.5625 \text{ pcs/min} = 213.75 \text{ pcs/hr}
$$

**Diagnosis:** OEE = **74.22%** is *below* the world-class 85% benchmark. The *lowest pillar* is **Availability (83.33%)**, so the bottleneck is *uptime* — predominantly the 60-min unplanned downtime. **Recommendation:** Install condition-monitoring vibration sensors on the robot's gear train to enable *predictive* maintenance and reduce MTTR by an estimated 40%.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interpreting KPI Combinations

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **rapid-diagnosis tool** KTU examiners love. Memorise the diagonal patterns.

| Pattern | A | P | Q | Likely Root Cause | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A↓, P↑, Q↑** | Low | High | High | Excessive downtime, breakdowns | Maintenance, condition monitoring |
| **A↑, P↓, Q↑** | High | Low | High | Idling, poor motion planning, starvation | Controls, line balancing |
| **A↑, P↑, Q↓** | High | High | Low | Calibration drift, fixture wear, vision mis-set | Process engineering |
| **A↓, P↓, Q↓** | All low | All low | All low | Systemic — design undersized for demand | Full redesign / capacity upgrade |
| **A↑, P↓, Q↓** | High | Low | Low | Operator-induced slowdowns + reject loops | Training, poka-yoke |
| **A↓, P↑, Q↓** | Low | High | Low | Breakdown recovery rush → poor quality | Maintenance scheduling |

> **Conversion Logic of the Matrix:** The ↓/↑ marks above are *relative to the 85% world-class benchmark*. A pillar counts as "low" if it is more than **10 percentage points** below 85%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture — Performance Analysis Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a *functional architecture flow*, not a physical drawing. It maps the logical chain from raw operational data through KPI computation to redesign recommendations — exactly the chain KTU expects a student to articulate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define System Boundary and Production Target] --> B[Step 1 Collect Operational Data]
    B --> C[Step 2 Compute Availability A]
    B --> D[Step 3 Compute Performance P]
    B --> E[Step 4 Compute Quality Q]
    C --> F[Step 5 Multiply A x P x Q equals OEE]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Step 6 Compute MTBF and MTTR]
    G --> H[Diagnostic Matrix: Identify Lowest Pillar]
    H --> I{Pillar is A?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Maintenance Lever: Predictive Sensors]
    I -->|No| K{Pillar is P?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Controls Lever: Motion Planning and Line Balancing]
    K -->|No| M{Pillar is Q?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Process Lever: Calibration and Vision Tuning]
    M -->|No| O[Systemic: Full Capacity Redesign]
    J --> P[Step 7 Predict New OEE and ROI]
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[End: Document Recommendations for Management]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Six Big Losses of OEE (Reference Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Six Big Losses** is the *SEMI E10* standard taxonomy. Mapping case-study data to these six categories is a guaranteed 7-mark answer in KTU Part-B.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Availability Losses] --> A2[1 Equipment Failure]
    A1 --> A3[2 Setup and Adjustment]
    B1[Performance Losses] --> B2[3 Idling and Minor Stops]
    B1 --> B3[4 Reduced Speed]
    C1[Quality Losses] --> C2[5 Process Defects]
    C1 --> C3[6 Reduced Yield Start-up]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Robotic Assembly Cell Performance Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Part Arrives on Conveyor] --> P2[Vision System Detects Pose]
    P2 --> P3[Robot Picks Part: Cycle Start]
    P3 --> P4[Robot Places in Fixture]
    P4 --> P5[Assembly Operation]
    P5 --> P6{Quality Check Passes?}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Good Count Increment]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Reject Count Increment]
    P7 --> P9[Update Throughput and OEE]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Log Downtime if Robot Idle or Stopped]
    P10 --> P1
```

> **Reading aid:** The loop visualises how a *single piece's journey* generates the data that *feeds back* into KPI computation. Notice that **every branch** terminates at the central KPI block — this mirrors the data flow expected in an MES (Manufacturing Execution System) database.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) and state the world-class benchmark value.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a composite performance metric that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing or robotic assembly system is utilised relative to its designed capacity. It is computed as the product of three independent ratios:
>
> $$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$
>
> The world-class benchmark value, as defined by SEMI (Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International), is **85%** or higher. Values between 60% and 75% are typical for many robotic assembly cells in the manufacturing industry.
> *(Award 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the definition of the three pillars, 1 mark for the 85% benchmark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between MTBF and MTTR. How are they combined to obtain the inherent availability?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** is the average elapsed time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. It is a measure of *reliability*.
>
> **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** is the average time taken to restore the system to a functional state after a failure. It is a measure of *maintainability*.
>
> The *inherent availability* combines them as the asymptotic fraction of time the system is operational:
>
> $$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$
>
> *(Award 1 mark each for MTBF and MTTR definitions, 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently forget to convert seconds to minutes (or vice versa) when computing Performance. This single error cascades and corrupts the OEE.
> - **Double-counting downtime:** Do *not* subtract setup time twice. In OEE, *all* non-productive time (planned + unplanned) is part of *Downtime*.
> - **Confusing Good Pieces with Total Pieces:** Throughput and OEE *both* depend on the *Good* count, not the *Total*. Writing $\text{TP} = N / T_O$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> - **Skipping the diagnostic commentary:** KTU awards up to 3 marks *purely* for identifying the bottleneck and recommending a corrective lever. A correct OEE calculation with no diagnosis is at most a 7/14.
> - **Confusing inherent availability with operational availability:** Inherent availability *excludes* scheduled downtime; operational availability *includes* it. Read the question's wording carefully.

---

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks) — *(Choose ONE from A or B)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
A robotic assembly cell in an automotive plant operates for two shifts of 8 hours each per day (total 16 hours, i.e., 960 minutes). The following operational data has been recorded over a 5-day observation window:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Planned Production Time (per day) | 960 min |
| Unplanned Downtime (5-day total) | 320 min |
| Setup / Changeover Time (5-day total) | 100 min |
| Total Pieces Produced (5-day total) | 18,000 pcs |
| Good Pieces (5-day total) | 17,100 pcs |
| Ideal Cycle Time (from robot spec) | 12 sec/pc |
| Number of Failures (5-day) | 8 |
| Total Repair Time (5-day) | 240 min |

**(a)** Calculate the Availability, Performance, Quality, and **OEE** of the cell over the 5-day period. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the MTBF, MTTR, and inherent availability. Identify the bottleneck pillar and recommend **two engineering interventions** to raise OEE above the world-class 85% benchmark. Justify each intervention quantitatively. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Solution to (a):** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Aggregate Planned Production Time:**

$$
T_P = 960 \times 5 = 4{,}800 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregate Downtime (planned + unplanned):**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 320 + 100 = 420 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Operating Time:**

$$
T_O = T_P - D_{\text{total}} = 4{,}800 - 420 = 4{,}380 \text{ min}
$$

*(Award 1 mark for correct $T_P$, 1 mark for $T_O$ calculation.)*

**Step 4 — Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{T_O}{T_P} = \frac{4{,}380}{4{,}800} = 0.9125 = 91.25\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 5 — Performance (convert $T_I$ to minutes):**

$$
T_I = \frac{12}{60} = 0.20 \text{ min/pc}
$$

$$
P = \frac{T_I \times N}{T_O} = \frac{0.20 \times 18{,}000}{4{,}380} = \frac{3{,}600}{4{,}380} = 0.8219 = 82.19\%
$$

*(2 marks — 1 for conversion, 1 for the ratio.)*

**Step 6 — Quality:**

$$
Q = \frac{G}{N} = \frac{17{,}100}{18{,}000} = 0.9500 = 95.00\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 7 — OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9125 \times 0.8219 \times 0.9500 = 0.7124 = 71.24\%
$$

*(1 mark. The cell is **23.76 percentage points** below the world-class 85% benchmark.)*

---

**Solution to (b):** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{4{,}380}{8} = 547.5 \text{ min} = 9.125 \text{ hr}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 2 — MTTR:**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime (repairs)}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{240}{8} = 30 \text{ min} = 0.5 \text{ hr}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 3 — Inherent Availability:**

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{9.125}{9.125 + 0.5} = \frac{9.125}{9.625} = 0.9481 = 94.81\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 4 — Diagnostic Commentary:** (2 marks)

The lowest pillar is **Performance (82.19%)** — well below the 85% world-class line. The robot is technically available 91% of the time, but during that time it is moving slower than its ideal cycle. This is characteristic of:
- *Minor stops and idling* (e.g., waiting for upstream conveyor, vision-recognition timeouts, gripper re-grasps).
- *Reduced speed* operation (cautious trajectories near humans, conservative motion-blend parameters).

**Step 5 — Two Engineering Interventions with Quantitative Justification:** (2 marks)

| Intervention | Lever | Quantitative Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **i. SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Dies) on the fixture changeover** | Reduce setup time by 60% | New $S = 100 \times 0.40 = 40$ min, $T_O$ rises by 60 min to 4,440 min. Performance = $(0.20 \times 18000)/4440 = 0.8108$; *but* Availability = $4440/4800 = 92.50%$, giving OEE = 0.9250 × 0.8108 × 0.95 = **71.25%** — so this addresses *A*, not the *P* bottleneck. |
| **ii. (CORRECT) Tune motion-blend parameters + install conveyor synchronisation to eliminate idle waits** | Performance lever | If minor-stop time reduces effective operating time by 8% (350 min), $T_O^{\text{new}} = 4380 + 350 = 4730$ min. New Performance = $(0.20 \times 18000)/4730 = 0.7611$ — *but* if we also raise throughput by reducing actual cycle time (more pieces in same window), say $N = 19{,}200$: $P = 0.20 \times 19200/4380 = 0.8767 = 87.67\%$. OEE = 0.9125 × 0.8767 × 0.95 = **76.0%** — significant climb. |

> The examiner will accept any *physically reasonable* intervention paired with a numerical before/after OEE comparison. The intervention targeting the **lowest pillar** scores higher.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
A pharmaceutical company has installed a 6-axis robotic pick-and-place cell for blister-pack loading. Over a one-week observation period (7 days, one 8-hour shift per day, PPT = 480 min/day), the following data is observed:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Pieces Loaded (week) | 25,200 pcs |
| Rejects (defective loading) | 504 pcs |
| Total Downtime (week) | 280 min |
| Robot Idle Time (during $T_O$) | 315 min |
| Ideal Cycle Time | 9 sec/pc |
| Number of Failures (week) | 3 |
| Cumulative Repair Time | 180 min |

**(a)** Compute the OEE of the cell, identifying each of A, P, Q. Comment on whether the cell qualifies as *world-class*. (7 marks)

**(b)** The cell must achieve an OEE of 85% to meet a regulatory throughput mandate. Propose a two-stage improvement plan: (i) a *short-term* action (≤ 1 month) and (ii) a *long-term* action (≥ 6 months). Estimate the OEE after each stage, and compute the additional daily throughput in pieces/hour gained. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Solution to (a):** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Aggregate Planned Production Time:**

$$
T_P = 480 \times 7 = 3{,}360 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Operating Time:**

$$
T_O = T_P - D = 3{,}360 - 280 = 3{,}080 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{3{,}080}{3{,}360} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 4 — Ideal Cycle Time in minutes:**

$$
T_I = \frac{9}{60} = 0.15 \text{ min/pc}
$$

**Step 5 — Performance:**

$$
P = \frac{T_I \times N}{T_O} = \frac{0.15 \times 25{,}200}{3{,}080} = \frac{3{,}780}{3{,}080} = 1.2273
$$

> *Performance > 100% is **theoretically impossible** under the strict OEE definition. It indicates that the assigned "Ideal Cycle Time" is too aggressive — the robot cannot physically meet 9 sec/pc. KTU students should note this in the answer and propose a re-baselined $T_I$.*

**Corrected approach:** Set $T_I$ to the *achievable best* of 11 sec/pc (0.1833 min/pc):

$$
P_{\text{corrected}} = \frac{0.1833 \times 25{,}200}{3{,}080} = 1.500
$$

> *Still > 100% — confirms the bottleneck is the $T_I$ baseline, not the robot. Re-baseline to 13 sec/pc (0.2167 min/pc):*

$$
P = \frac{0.2167 \times 25{,}200}{3{,}080} = 1.773
$$

> *This shows the *real* issue is that the process was over-specified. The right student insight: report $P = 100\%$ as the ceiling with a comment on the specification error.* *(2 marks for noting the anomaly and re-baselining.)*

**Step 6 — Quality:**

$$
Q = \frac{25{,}200 - 504}{25{,}200} = \frac{24{,}696}{25{,}200} = 0.9800 = 98.00\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 7 — OEE (using the 100% P ceiling after re-baselining):**

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9167 \times 1.0000 \times 0.9800 = 0.8984 = 89.84\%
$$

> *In this corrected reading, the cell is **world-class** (≥ 85%).* *(2 marks for the conclusion.)*

**Step 8 — MTBF and MTTR (carry to part b):**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{3{,}080}{3} = 1{,}026.67 \text{ min} \approx 17.1 \text{ hr}
$$

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{180}{3} = 60 \text{ min} = 1.0 \text{ hr}
$$

*(1 mark.)*

---

**Solution to (b):** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Two-Stage Improvement Plan:**

| Stage | Action | Pillar Affected | Estimated Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-term (≤1 month)** | Install a vision-system auto-calibration routine; reduce changeover-induced rejects by 50% | **Quality** | New $Q = 1 - 504 \times 0.5 / 25{,}200 = 0.9900 = 99.00\%$ |
| **Long-term (≥6 months)** | Replace ageing gripper fingers; reduce minor-stops causing 200 min/week of additional downtime | **Availability** | New $D = 280 - 200 = 80$ min, $T_O = 3{,}280$ min, $A = 3{,}280/3{,}360 = 0.9762 = 97.62\%$ |

**Step 2 — New OEE after both stages:**

$$
\text{OEE}_{\text{new}} = 0.9762 \times 1.0000 \times 0.9900 = 0.9664 = 96.64\%
$$

*(2 marks.)*

**Step 3 — Daily Throughput Comparison:**

Original daily good pieces (using actual data):

$$
G_{\text{orig}} = \frac{24{,}696}{7} = 3{,}528 \text{ pcs/day} = 441 \text{ pcs/hr} \text{ (over 8 hr)}
$$

New daily good pieces (assuming rejection rate halves and uptime rises):

$$
G_{\text{new}} = 25{,}200 \times 0.99 / 7 = 3{,}564 \text{ pcs/day}
$$

*However, with higher $T_O$ the cell can produce more — assume same $N$ but $Q$ improves, throughput per operating hour is unchanged. The real gain is in the **OEE level reached** for regulatory compliance.*

$$
\text{Additional throughput} = 3{,}564 - 3{,}528 = 36 \text{ pcs/day} \approx 4.5 \text{ pcs/hr}
$$

*(2 marks for the calculation and 1 mark for the commentary.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Final Read Before the Exam**

- **OEE = A × P × Q.** Memorise the formula and the world-class benchmark of **85%**.
- **Always convert units** before computing Performance: $T_I$ must be in the same time unit as $T_O$.
- **Quality uses Good Pieces, not Total Pieces.** This is the single most common student error in KTU valuation.
- **MTBF measures reliability; MTTR measures maintainability.** Their sum in the denominator of $A_i$ gives the *long-run fraction of time operational*.
- **Operational Availability (A)** includes *scheduled* downtime; **Inherent Availability (Aᵢ)** includes *only* failure-repair cycles. Read the question's wording.
- **The Six Big Losses** — Equipment Failure, Setup, Idling, Reduced Speed, Defects, Start-up Yield — are the SEMI E10 taxonomy. Mapping data to these six earns full diagnostic marks.
- **The lowest pillar dictates the corrective lever family:** Low A → maintenance, Low P → controls/motion, Low Q → process/calibration.
- **Throughput = Good Pieces / Operating Time.** In SI: pcs/hr or pcs/min.
- **Performance cannot exceed 100%** under the strict OEE definition. If it does, the $T_I$ baseline is wrong — re-baseline and comment.
- **Robot Utilization** is a *separate* metric from OEE: it measures busy-time vs. available-time, regardless of quality or ideal speed.
- **ROI and Payback Period** are used to *justify* automation, not to compute OEE. They appear in case studies that combine engineering and management economics.
- **Always end a case-study answer** with a bottleneck identification + a quantitative recommendation (e.g., "Reduce MTTR by 40% via vibration sensors → predicted OEE rises from 71% to 79%"). This is where 3–4 free marks live.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme values the *interpretive* sentence far more than the bare calculation. A 7-mark sub-question split is typically: **3 marks for calculation accuracy, 2 marks for numerical correctness in derived metrics, 2 marks for diagnostic commentary and redesign recommendation.** Never submit numbers without a sentence of interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety and Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Robot Safety and Standards** refers to the engineered set of design philosophies, regulatory frameworks, hardware interlocks, control-software logic, and operational procedures that collectively eliminate or reduce the risk of physical injury, equipment damage, and process failure during the operation of robotic assembly cells and material-handling systems.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the topic covers the application of internationally harmonized safety standards—principally **ISO 10218-1:2011** (industrial robot safety), **ISO 10218-2:2011** (integration of robot systems), and **ISO/TS 15066:2016** (collaborative robots)—together with the European **EN ISO 13849-1** (Performance Level / PL) and **IEC 62061** (Safety Integrity Level / SIL) frameworks, applied across the lifecycle of an industrial robot work-cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Safety in robotics is a mandatory outcome. The course outcome is mapped to: *"Apply safety standards and risk-assessment methodologies to design and operate industrial and collaborative robot work-cells."* Examiner questions typically focus on **risk-assessment tables**, **PL/SIL selection**, and the **four collaborative operation modes**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a robotic work-cell as a busy city intersection. Just as a city does not rely solely on driver skill to prevent accidents, a robot system does not rely on the operator's vigilance alone. The city paints lanes, installs traffic lights, sets speed limits, places guard-rails, and demands seat-belts — each one a **layer of protection**.

In a robot cell, those layers are:
- **Lanes** → physical fences and perimeter guards
- **Traffic lights** → emergency-stop circuits and interlocks
- **Speed limits** → Safety-Rated Monitored Speed (SRMS)
- **Seat-belts** → collaborative power-and-force-limiting (PFL) padding

If one layer fails, the next must catch the hazard. This is the essence of the **hierarchy-of-controls** philosophy.

### 1.3 Core Concepts to be Covered

> [!NOTE]
> The five mandatory concepts for KTU 2024 Module-3 evaluation:
> 1. **Risk Assessment** (ISO 12100) — Hazard identification, risk estimation, evaluation, reduction.
> 2. **Safety Integrity** — Performance Level (PL a–e) and Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1–3).
> 3. **Safeguarding Devices** — Mechanical guards, light curtains, safety mats, laser scanners, two-hand controls.
> 4. **Collaborative Operation** — Four collaborative modes of ISO/TS 15066.
> 5. **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** — Energy isolation procedures under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147 / ISO 14118.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The principal numerical metrics used in robot-safety engineering are:

- **MTTF<sub>d</sub>** (Mean Time To Dangerous Failure) — measured in **years**.
- **DC<sub>avg</sub>** (Average Diagnostic Coverage) — a percentage from **60 %** to **99 %**.
- **PFH<sub>d</sub>** (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour) — between **10⁻⁵** and **10⁻⁸** h⁻¹.
- **T<sub>M</sub>** (Mission Time) — typically **20 years**.
- **Separation Distance (S)** — a function of robot stopping time, human approach speed, and sensor resolution.

These metrics are **bold-printed** in KTU model answers because they are the vocabulary of the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Risk-Assessment Methodology (ISO 12100:2010)

The ISO 12100 framework is a **Type-B A-standard** that all Type-C standards (like ISO 10218) explicitly reference. It formalises a three-step iterative loop:

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification**
A *hazard* is any source with the potential to cause harm. The designer enumerates:
- Mechanical hazards (crushing, shearing, entrapment, stabbing)
- Electrical hazards (shock, arc-flash)
- Thermal, noise, vibration, ergonomic hazards
- Hazards introduced by the *process* (e.g., welding arc, solvent fumes)

**Step 2 — Risk Estimation**
For each hazard, three parameters are estimated:

$$R = f(S, F, P)$$

where **S** = Severity of injury (S1 = minor, S2 = serious/fatal), **F** = Frequency of exposure to the hazard, and **P** = Possibility of avoidance.

The classic ISO 12100 risk-graph yields a required **Performance Level** (PL<sub>r</sub>) from **a** to **e** for each safety function.

**Step 3 — Risk Reduction (Hierarchy of Controls)**
1. **Inherent safe design** — eliminate the hazard (highest priority).
2. **Safeguarding / protective devices** — light curtains, interlocks.
3. **Information for use** — signs, training, manuals (lowest priority).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's golden rule:** Students who *skip* the iterative re-evaluation of Step 1 after Step 3 lose 1–2 marks. Risk reduction is a **loop**, not a one-way street.

### 2.2 Performance Level and Safety Integrity Level

The two harmonized risk-reduction metrics are functionally equivalent. Mapping is given in **EN ISO 13849-1 / IEC 62061 cross-table**.

#### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Range / Unit | Mathematical Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| $PL_r$ | Required Performance Level | a, b, c, d, e | Output of risk graph |
| $PL_{achieved}$ | Achieved PL (subsystem) | a, b, c, d, e | Input from $MTTF_d$, $DC_{avg}$, Category |
| $SIL$ | Safety Integrity Level | 1, 2, 3 | Functional equivalent to PL |
| $MTTF_d$ | Mean Time To Dangerous Failure | years | Logarithm of unreliability |
| $DC_{avg}$ | Average Diagnostic Coverage | 60 – 99 % | Weighted average over channels |
| $PFH_d$ | Probability of dangerous Failure / h | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-8}$ h$^{-1}$ | PFH limit sets PL ceiling |
| $T_M$ | Mission Time (use life) | 20 years | Statistical horizon |
| $S$ | Safety separation distance | metres | $S = K \cdot (T_m + T_s + T_r) + C$ |
| $K$ | Approach speed (operator) | 1.6 m/s walking, 2.0 m/s reaching | From ISO 13855 |
| $T_m$ | Stop time measured | seconds | From robot stop test |
| $T_s$ | Stop time safety margin | seconds | Vendor default 0 %–10 % |
| $T_r$ | Response time of safeguard | seconds | From device datasheet |
| $C$ | Intrusion penetration depth | metres | $\geq C_{RO}$ or $C_{RZ}$ from Table 1 |
| $B_{10d}$ | Cycles until 10 % fail dangerously | cycles | Component-level metric |
| $n_{op}$ | Operations per year | cycles/year | $MTTF_d = B_{10d} / (0.1 \cdot n_{op})$ |

**Critical formatting note:** every column-bound separator uses ASCII text only — no vertical pipes inside math expressions; the LaTeX macros `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\Vert` are used to render absolute-value bars safely.

### 2.3 Performance Level Determination

A subsystem achieves a given PL if **all three** of the following constraints are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Category** (architecture: B, 1, 2, 3, 4) is met.
2. **DC<sub>avg</sub>** meets the minimum for the target PL.
3. **MTTF<sub>d</sub>** of each channel lies in the required range.
4. **PFH<sub>d</sub>** of the safety function is within the PL limit.

For a typical **PLe / SIL 3** zone-stop safety function:
- $PFH_d \leq 10^{-7}$ h$^{-1}$ (per hour limit)
- $DC_{avg} \geq 99\,\%$
- $MTTF_d$ of each channel $\geq 30$ years (high range)

### 2.4 Collaborative Operation Modes (ISO/TS 15066:2016)

There are **four** collaborative modes. Examiners love to ask "list and briefly explain" for 3 marks, or a 14-mark design question on a *specific* mode.

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — robot halts before operator enters the workspace.
2. **Hand Guiding** — operator physically moves the robot via an enabling device; speed limited to **250 mm/s**.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — robot speed dynamically adjusted so that minimum protective distance is never violated; separation computed from $S = K(T_m + T_s + T_r) + C$.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — quasi-static and transient contact pressures limited to biomechanical thresholds (e.g., **140 N** on hand, **280 N** on chest; **65 N/cm²** max pressure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PFL Threshold Rule (Q1 2024 KTU pattern):** The maximum permissible contact force depends on the *body region* and is set by the **biomechanical limits** of Table A.1 in ISO/TS 15066:2016. The standard free-space speed limit for PFL is **250 mm/s** when human and robot can occupy the same workspace.

### 2.5 Separation-Distance Equation Derivation

The relationship between protective-device sensitivity, robot braking dynamics, and human approach velocity is the only formula requiring derivation in the KTU pattern. Start from kinematics:

$$S = v_{operator} \cdot t_{total} + d_{intrusion}$$

where $t_{total} = T_m + T_s + T_r$.

Therefore:

$$S = K \cdot (T_m + T_s + T_r) + C$$

This is the foundational equation for **ISO 13855:2010** and appears in the KTU textbook. Students must memorise the **1.6 m/s** (walking) and **2.0 m/s** (upper-limb reach) approach constants and the **C<sub>RO</sub> = 850 mm** / **C<sub>RZ</sub> = 1 200 mm** defaults.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Risk-based engineering is the universal language of functional safety. The same PL/SIL framework that protects an operator from a 1-tonne industrial arm also protects:
- a passenger inside an autonomous vehicle (ISO 26262 — derived from IEC 61508)
- a patient in a surgical robot (IEC 60601)
- an aircraft flight-control surface (SAE ARP 4761)

KTU asks students to be able to *map* a robot safety function onto the cross-table of PL and SIL — the same skill set used in automotive and medical device industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Component MTTF<sub>d</sub> from Cycles to Failure

A standard electromechanical safety switch (e.g., a guard-door interlock) is rated by the manufacturer for **B<sub>10d</sub>** = 2 × 10⁶ cycles. The cell is operated **240 days/year**, **16 hours/day**, with **15 door-openings per hour** (i.e., one actuation per operator visit). We will derive MTTF<sub>d</sub> and the corresponding PFH<sub>d</sub>.

**Step 1 — Annual actuations**

$$n_{op} = 240 \text{ days} \times 16 \text{ h/day} \times 15 \text{ cycles/h} = 57\,600 \text{ cycles/year}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the EN ISO 13849-1 conversion formula**

The 0.1 in the denominator represents the assumption that 10 % of all failures are dangerous.

$$MTTF_d = \frac{B_{10d}}{0.1 \cdot n_{op}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numbers**

$$\begin{aligned}
MTTF_d &= \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{0.1 \times 57\,600} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{5\,760} \\
&\approx 347.2 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Classify against the EN ISO 13849-1 bands**

The bands are: Low = 3 ≤ MTTF<sub>d</sub> < 10 years; Medium = 10 ≤ MTTF<sub>d</sub> < 30 years; High = 30 ≤ MTTF<sub>d</sub> ≤ 100 years. Since 347.2 years exceeds 100 years, we *cap* at **100 years** (high range). This is the rule in §4.5.4 of the standard.

$$\boxed{MTTF_d^{\text{channel}} = 100 \text{ years (High)}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute PFH<sub>d</sub> for a single channel**

For a single-channel Category-1 / Category-B architecture, the simplified relation is:

$$PFH_d = \frac{1}{MTTF_d}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
PFH_d &= \frac{1}{100 \text{ years}} \\
&= \frac{1}{100 \times 8760 \text{ h}} \\
&= \frac{1}{876\,000 \text{ h}} \\
&\approx 1.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

This value is **one order of magnitude** worse than the PLe ceiling of $10^{-7}$ h$^{-1}$. A single-channel architecture is **insufficient** for a PLe-rated door interlock.

**Step 6 — Add a second channel and diagnostic coverage**

For a Category-3 dual-channel system with **DC<sub>avg</sub> = 90 %** (medium), the PFH<sub>d</sub> improves by roughly the channel count squared under the common-cause limit:

$$PFH_d^{\text{Cat 3}} \approx 2 \cdot \frac{(1-DC_{avg})}{MTTF_d} \cdot \frac{1}{100}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
PFH_d^{\text{Cat 3}} &\approx 2 \times \frac{0.10}{100} \times 10^{-2} \text{ h}^{-1} \\
&\approx 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

This now satisfies **PLe** ($PFH_d \leq 10^{-7}$ h$^{-1}$) and exceeds the **SIL 3** IEC 62061 ceiling. The exercise is complete.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Separation Distance for a Safety Mat

A palletiser robot's protective field is bounded by a pressure-sensitive safety mat with **C = 75 mm** intrusion tolerance. Measured robot stop time is **T<sub>m</sub> = 0.25 s**. The safety stop-time margin is **T<sub>s</sub> = 0.05 s**. The mat's own response time is **T<sub>r</sub> = 0.04 s**. Approach constant is **K = 1.6 m/s** (operator walking).

**Step 1 — Sum the times**

$$t_{total} = T_m + T_s + T_r$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= 0.25 + 0.05 + 0.04 \\
&= 0.34 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the separation equation**

$$S = K \cdot t_{total} + C$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 1.6 \text{ m/s} \times 0.34 \text{ s} + 0.075 \text{ m} \\
&= 0.544 \text{ m} + 0.075 \text{ m} \\
&= 0.619 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 100 mm / 500 mm rule**

ISO 13855 stipulates a minimum $S \geq 500$ mm. Since 619 mm > 500 mm, the design is valid. If we had computed S = 480 mm, the answer would be **rejected** and reset to 500 mm minimum.

$$\boxed{S = 619 \text{ mm}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Safeguard vs. Hazard

| Hazard Source | Most Appropriate Safeguard | Standard Reference | Typical PL<sub>r</sub> |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operator reaching into the cell | Light curtain / safety mat | ISO 13855, EN ISO 13849-1 | PL d / SIL 2 |
| Maintenance access door | Interlocked guard with guard-locking | ISO 14119, EN ISO 13849-1 | PL e / SIL 3 |
| Unexpected start-up during service | LOTO (Lockout / Tagout) | ISO 14118, OSHA 1910.147 | Administrative (informational layer) |
| Collaborative hand contact | PFL pad + software force limit | ISO/TS 15066 | PL d / SIL 2 |
| Tool change, pneumatic stored energy | Energy-isolation valve + bleed-down | ISO 14118 | PL c / SIL 1 |
| Vertical-axis free-fall (gravity) | Counter-balance + brake monitor | EN ISO 13849-1 | PL d / SIL 2 |
| Welding arc (process hazard) | Light curtain with optical filter | ISO 13855 | PL d / SIL 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **examiner-bait**. A 14-mark "design a safeguarding scheme for a robot cell" question will test exactly this mapping.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Mapping: PL ↔ SIL

The harmonisation of **EN ISO 13849-1** (PL) with **IEC 62061** (SIL) is a frequent 7-mark sub-question.

| Required Performance Level (PL) | Equivalent SIL (IEC 62061) | Equivalent PFH<sub>d</sub> range [h⁻¹] |
|---|---|---|
| a | No correspondence | $\geq 10^{-5}$ |
| b | 1 | $10^{-6} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-5}$ |
| c | 1 | $10^{-6} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-5}$ |
| d | 2 | $10^{-7} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-6}$ |
| e | 3 | $10^{-8} \leq \text{PFH} < 10^{-7}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PL a** has *no* SIL equivalent because it represents unprotected systems, which IEC 62061 does not accept as a safety-rated architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Robot-Cell Safety Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the layered functional architecture of a robot-cell safety system, from the cell-level perimeter inward to the servo-loop-level stop functions. It is the canonical drawing for any KTU 14-mark design question on safeguarding.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeH["HAZARD SOURCES\nMECHANICAL ELECTRICAL PROCESS"]
    nodeR["RISK ASSESSMENT\nISO 12100"]
    nodeS["SAFEGUARD PLAN\nHIERARCHY OF CONTROLS"]
    nodeP["PERIMETER LAYER\nFENCES INTERLOCKS"]
    nodeA["AREA SENSING LAYER\nLIGHT CURTAIN MAT SCANNER"]
    nodeC["CONTROL LAYER\nSAFETY PLC E STOP RELAY"]
    nodeB["SERVO BRAKE LAYER\nSTO SBC MONITORED"]
    nodeM["MAINTENANCE LAYER\nLOTO LOTO SIGNAGE"]
    nodeT["TRAINING LAYER\nOPERATOR MANUAL"]
    nodeV["VERIFY AND VALIDATE\nPL SIL PFH"]

    nodeH --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeP
    nodeS --> nodeA
    nodeS --> nodeC
    nodeS --> nodeB
    nodeS --> nodeM
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeP --> nodeV
    nodeA --> nodeV
    nodeC --> nodeV
    nodeB --> nodeV
    nodeM --> nodeV
    nodeT --> nodeV
```

### 4.2 State-Diagram: Emergency-Stop and Recovery Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ST01_RUN
    ST01_RUN --> ST02_ESTOP: HARD E STOP PRESSED
    ST01_RUN --> ST03_SMS: SAFEGUARD TRIPPED
    ST02_ESTOP --> ST04_RESET_REQ: MANUAL UNLATCH
    ST03_SMS --> ST05_MONITOR: GUARD RESTORED
    ST04_RESET_REQ --> ST06_PROD: RESET CONFIRMED
    ST05_MONITOR --> ST01_RUN: NO INTRUSION
    ST06_PROD --> ST01_RUN: CYCLIC RETURN
    ST02_ESTOP --> ST07_MAINT: LOTO ENABLED
    ST07_MAINT --> [*]
    ST06_PROD --> ST07_MAINT: MAINT MODE KEY
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state machine explicitly distinguishes **ST03_SMS** (Safety Monitored Stop) from **ST02_ESTOP** (catastrophic hard E-stop). Examiners reward students who do not collapse these two states.

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Risk-Reduction Iteration Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["HAZARD ID\nSTEP 1"]
    node2["RISK ESTIMATE\nSTEP 2"]
    node3["RISK EVALUATE\nPL R REQUIRED"]
    node4["RISK REDUCTION\nSTEP 3"]
    node5["RE VERIFY\nNEW RESIDUAL RISK"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node1
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Collaborative Modes Selection Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ["COLLABORATIVE TASK?"]
    nodeA["APPLY SSM\nSEPARATION DISTANCE"]
    nodeB["FREQUENT CONTACT?\nYES"]
    nodeC["PFL MODE\nLIMIT FORCE AND SPEED"]
    nodeD["NO CONTACT\nHAND GUIDE MODE"]
    nodeE["NEVER CONTACT\nSMS MODE"]

    nodeQ -->|"YES"| nodeA
    nodeQ -->|"NO"| nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|"YES"| nodeC
    nodeB -->|"NO"| nodeD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the term *Performance Level* (PL) as used in EN ISO 13849-1. List the five discrete levels and state the one with the highest risk-reduction capability.

**Model Answer:**
The Performance Level (PL) is a discrete band used to specify the reliability of a safety-related part of a control system (SRP/CS) under EN ISO 13849-1:2015. It is derived from the subsystem's $MTTF_d$, $DC_{avg}$, and architectural **Category** (B, 1, 2, 3, 4). The five levels, in increasing order, are **PL a, PL b, PL c, PL d, PL e**. **PL e** corresponds to the highest risk-reduction capability, with the most stringent limit $PFH_d \leq 10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ h$^{-1}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
List the four collaborative operation modes defined by ISO/TS 15066:2016, naming the maximum permitted robot speed in each free-space PFL operation.

**Model Answer:**
The four modes are:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)**
2. **Hand Guiding**
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)**
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)**

The maximum quasi-static robot speed in PFL collaborative work is **250 mm/s**, with transient contact forces limited by the **biomechanical thresholds** of Table A.1 (e.g., 140 N for the hand, 65 N/cm² for the abdomen).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-3 Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Risk-Assessment and PL Determination*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 (Choice 1)]**

A 6-axis industrial robot transfers 25 kg steel blanks from a feeder to a press. The cell is fenced on three sides with a light-curtain entry. The operator may reach into the cell up to twice per hour. A risk-graph analysis yields S2 (serious), F2 (frequent), P2 (avoidance possible but not certain).

**(a)** Determine the **required Performance Level (PL<sub>r</sub>)** of the light-curtain safety function using the ISO 13849-1 risk graph. Justify the choice with a sketch of the parameter reasoning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an integrated two-channel safety light curtain with $MTTF_d = 80$ years per channel, $DC_{avg} = 95\,\%$, and Category-3 architecture, verify whether the achieved PL satisfies the requirement. Compute $PFH_d$ numerically. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. The risk graph parameters: Severity = **S2** (irreversible, serious), Frequency = **F2** (frequent, > once per hour possible), Possibility of avoidance = **P2** (avoidance possible under specific conditions).
2. Reading along the risk graph branches: S2 → F2 → P2.
3. The branch terminates at the cell marked **PL e** (or alternatively, the strict reading gives **PL d** if P1 is assumed; here, since P2 is explicit, the **PL<sub>r</sub> = PL d** is the typical academic answer; **PL e** if the worst-case branch is taken).

**Conclusion:** Required Performance Level $PL_r = \text{PL d}$ (or **PL e** under the most conservative reading).

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

1. Given: $MTTF_d = 80$ years per channel, $DC_{avg} = 95\,\%$, Category = 3.
2. Classify $MTTF_d$: 30 ≤ 80 ≤ 100 → **High** band.
3. Classify $DC_{avg}$: 95 % ≥ 90 % → **Medium-to-High** diagnostic coverage.
4. The EN ISO 13849-1 Table 5 (Category 3, MTTF<sub>d</sub> High, DC<sub>avg</sub> Medium) yields an **achieved PL = PL d**.
5. Compute $PFH_d$ for Category-3:

$$PFH_d \approx 2 \cdot \frac{(1-DC_{avg})}{MTTF_d \cdot 8760}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
PFH_d &= 2 \cdot \frac{0.05}{80 \times 8760} \\
&= \frac{0.10}{700\,800} \\
&\approx 1.43 \times 10^{-7} \text{ h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

6. The PL d ceiling is $10^{-7} \leq PFH_d < 10^{-6}$ h$^{-1}$. Our value lies just above the lower edge of the band — borderline PL d.

**Conclusion:** The design is **conditionally acceptable for PL d** with a tight safety margin. If PLe were required (per part-a conservative reading), the system would **fail** and would need a Category-4 architecture with $DC_{avg} \geq 99\,\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> (i) Students routinely *skip* the unit conversion $1 \text{ year} = 8760 \text{ hours}$ and quote a wildly inflated PFH<sub>d</sub>. This costs 2 marks.
> (ii) Conflating $MTTF_d$ (a time, in years) with $PFH_d$ (a probability, per hour) is the second most common error.
> (iii) Failing to *cap* MTTF<sub>d</sub> at 100 years when the calculated value exceeds it is a subtle 1-mark deduction.
> (iv) Not stating the *boundary reference* (ISO 13849-1 Table 5) for the achieved-PL conclusion loses the final 1 mark.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Collaborative Robot and Separation Distance*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 (Choice 2)]**

A collaborative robot assembles small electronic enclosures next to a human operator on a shared bench. The robot's stop time, measured under worst-case load, is **T<sub>m</sub> = 0.30 s**. The safety-rated scanner that triggers the protective stop has a vendor-specified response time of **T<sub>r</sub> = 0.08 s** and a stop-time safety margin of **T<sub>s</sub> = 0.05 s**. The scanner's intrusion depth tolerance is **C = 100 mm**. The operator's maximum approach speed is **K = 1.6 m/s**.

**(a)** Compute the **minimum protective separation distance (S)** for the speed-and-separation-monitoring (SSM) collaborative mode. Verify against the ISO 13855 minimum of 500 mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Outline a complete **safeguarding plan** for the cell, mapping each hazard to one or more protective devices and stating the achieved Performance Level for each. Discuss why a PFL-only design would be inappropriate if the work-piece mass exceeds 5 kg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. Sum the time components:

$$T_m + T_s + T_r = 0.30 + 0.05 + 0.08 = 0.43 \text{ s}$$

2. Apply the ISO 13855 equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= K(T_m + T_s + T_r) + C \\
&= 1.6 \times 0.43 + 0.10 \\
&= 0.688 + 0.100 \\
&= 0.788 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

3. Compare with the **500 mm** floor: 788 mm > 500 mm, so the design is valid.

**Final answer:** $S = 788 \text{ mm}$.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The recommended multi-layered safeguarding plan:

| Hazard Layer | Device | Achieved PL | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perimeter | Fenced bay with interlocked door | PL e | EN ISO 13849-1, ISO 14119 |
| Approach | Safety laser scanner | PL d | EN ISO 13849-1 |
| Contact | PFL software limit + padded gripper | PL d | ISO/TS 15066 |
| Maintenance | LOTO + bleed-down valve | PL c (administrative) | OSHA 1910.147 / ISO 14118 |
| Stop | STO + SBC monitored | PL e | IEC 61800-5-2 |

**Why PFL alone fails above 5 kg:** The ISO/TS 15066 PFL thresholds (140 N quasi-static hand contact) are *human-biomechanical* limits, not robot-energy limits. A 5 kg moving payload at 250 mm/s has kinetic energy

$$\begin{aligned}
E_k &= \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 5 \times (0.25)^2 \\
&= 0.156 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

A 10 kg payload would have 0.313 J, exceeding the 0.25 J quasi-static injury threshold of the hand; at any practical clamping deceleration the contact force is several times the 140 N limit. The required PFL force limit cannot be honoured for a payload > ~3 kg at 250 mm/s, so SSM (and not PFL) must be the dominant collaborative mode for >5 kg workpieces.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> (i) **Forgetting the C term** is the most common error in separation-distance problems — students compute $K \cdot t_{total}$ and ignore intrusion depth, losing 2 marks.
> (ii) Quoting $K = 2.0$ m/s for a *walking* operator (the 2.0 m/s constant is for *reaching*, not walking). Read the scenario carefully.
> (iii) Claiming "PFL is universally safe" without computing kinetic energy is a conceptual flaw that loses 2–3 marks in part (b).
> (iv) Not stating the *floor value* (S ≥ 500 mm) and verifying against it loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The risk-assessment loop (ISO 12100)** has three steps: identify → estimate → reduce, and the loop must be **re-iterated** until residual risk is acceptable.
- **Performance Level (PL a → e)** is the output of the risk graph; achieved PL comes from $MTTF_d$, $DC_{avg}$, and **Category**.
- **PFH<sub>d</sub> ceilings** for the highest performance levels: PL d → $10^{-7}$ h⁻¹, PL e → $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ h⁻¹.
- **MTTF<sub>d</sub> bands**: Low (3–10 y), Medium (10–30 y), High (30–100 y) — values > 100 y are *capped* at 100 y.
- **Collaborative modes (ISO/TS 15066)** are four: SRMS, Hand Guiding, SSM, PFL. Free-space PFL speed cap is **250 mm/s**.
- **Separation equation** is $S = K(T_m + T_s + T_r) + C$ with ISO 13855 constants $K = 1.6$ m/s (walk) and $K = 2.0$ m/s (reach).
- **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** is *administrative* — it complements, but does not replace, hardware safeguarding.
- **Always cap** calculated MTTF<sub>d</sub> at 100 years; always verify $S \geq 500$ mm.
- **PFL cannot scale to large payloads** — biomechanical thresholds, not robot force, set the limit.
- **Cross-mapping**: PL e ↔ SIL 3, PL d ↔ SIL 2, PL b/c ↔ SIL 1, PL a ↔ no SIL equivalent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision tip:** Memorise the risk-graph branch outcomes (S2, F2, P2 → PL d) and the separation-distance equation. Together they account for ~70 % of KTU marks on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Considerations in Robotic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety Considerations in Robotic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Robotic Systems** is the integrated discipline of **hazard identification, risk assessment, safeguarding, and control** that ensures human operators, equipment, and the working environment are protected from the inherent dangers of industrial and collaborative robots during operation, programming, maintenance, and material handling tasks.

The discipline draws upon international standards issued by:
- **ISO 10218-1 & ISO 10218-2** (Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements)
- **ISO/TS 15066** (Collaborative robots — Technical specification)
- **ANSI/RIA R15.06** (American National Standard for Industrial Robots)
- **ISO 13849-1** (Safety-related parts of control systems)
- **ISO 13855** (Positioning of safeguards with respect to the approach speeds of the human body)
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** (Lockout/Tagout for U.S. industrial operations)

> [!NOTE]
> A **hazard** is any potential source of harm (e.g., a moving robot arm), whereas a **risk** is the combination of the probability of occurrence and the severity of that harm. Safety engineering is essentially a structured methodology to *reduce risk to an acceptable (tolerable) level*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a robotic work cell like a **highway construction zone** 🚧:
- The **robot** is the heavy earthmover — powerful, fast, and completely unaware of the pedestrian.
- The **safety fence with interlocked gate** is the concrete barrier that physically stops anyone from wandering onto the worksite.
- The **light curtain** is the motion sensor that automatically halts the machine if a hand crosses a forbidden line.
- The **emergency stop (E-stop)** is the red mushroom-head button — the universal "STOP EVERYTHING" command.
- The **lockout/tagout (LOTO)** is the mechanic's padlock on the disconnect switch — even if a curious person flips the switch, the lock prevents re-energization.

Just as a construction site has hard hats, designated walkways, and signalmen, a robotic cell has a layered set of protections called **defense-in-depth** — if one layer fails, the next catches the hazard.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Recall-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Safety Constants (Memorize):**
> - **Hand/arm approach speed, K = 1600 mm/s** (ISO 13855 default for hands approaching vertically)
> - **Whole-body approach speed, K = 1600 mm/s**
> - **Operator reach distance constant, C** (varies by safeguard type, e.g., 850 mm for floor-level light curtains with 14 mm resolution)
> - **Stopping time (T)** is the worst-case time from stop signal initiation to cessation of all hazardous motion
> - **Performance Level (PL)** is graded **a, b, c, d, e** (ISO 13849-1) — **PL d** is the typical minimum for robot safety functions

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Robot Safety Controls (Defense-in-Depth)

Robot safety is **layered**, never relying on a single safeguard. The hierarchy from most reliable to least:

1. **Inherent Safe Design** — Eliminating the hazard at the design stage (e.g., reducing robot kinetic energy, soft-jointed arms, rounded edges).
2. **Guarding & Physical Barriers** — Fixed perimeter fences, interlocked gates, fixed enclosures.
3. **Sensing Devices (Active Safeguarding)** — Light curtains, safety mats, area scanners, 3-D vision.
4. **Control-Logic Safeguards** — Safety-rated monitored stop (SRMS), speed & separation monitoring (SSM), power & force limiting (PFL).
5. **Administrative Controls** — Training, signage, lockout/tagout, written procedures.
6. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Safety glasses, steel-toe shoes — the *last* line of defense.

## 2.2 Types of Hazards in Robotic Systems

| Hazard Category | Examples | Typical Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Crushing, shearing, entanglement, striking | Moving arm, end-effector, gripper |
| **Electrical** | Shock, arc flash, short circuit | Servo drives, controllers, power cabling |
| **Pneumatic/Hydraulic** | Whipping hoses, injection injury, stored energy | Cylinders, accumulators |
| **Thermal** | Burns from hot end-effectors | Welders, hot-melt adhesive dispensers |
| **Chemical** | Fumes, splashes | Painting, cleaning, adhesive work |
| **Ergonomic** | Repetitive stress, awkward postures | Manual loading stations |
| **System / Control** | Unexpected motion from sensor fault, EMC, software bug | Controller, network, power glitches |
| **Operational** | Dropped payloads, tip-overs, pinch points | Material-handling motions, AGVs |

## 2.3 Risk Assessment Methodology (KTU High-Yield Framework)

The four-step procedure, identical across ISO 12100 and ISO 10218:

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Hazard Identification** | List every plausible hazard | Hazard register |
| **2. Risk Estimation** | For each hazard, evaluate **Severity (S)**, **Probability of Occurrence (O)**, and **Possibility of Avoidance (A)** | Risk score / risk graph |
