| **3. Risk Evaluation** | Compare the estimated risk to a **tolerability threshold** | Decision: reduce / accept / refuse |
| **4. Risk Reduction** | Apply the hierarchy of controls (Section 2.1) iteratively until the residual risk is tolerable | Documented safeguards |

The **Risk Graph** (ISO 13849-1) uses parameters:
- **S₀, S₁, S₂** → Severity (none, slight, serious/irreversible)
- **F₁, F₂** → Frequency of exposure (rare, frequent)
- **P₁, P₂** → Possibility of avoidance (possible under specific conditions, impossible)
- **W₁, W₂, W₃** → Probability of unwanted occurrence (low, medium, high)

These combine to specify a required **Performance Level (PLr)** from **PL a** to **PL e**.

## 2.4 Collaborative Robot (Cobot) Safety — The Four Collaborative Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO/TS 15066 defines exactly FOUR permissible collaborative operations. Memorize these — they are a 14-mark favourite.**

| Operation | Abbreviation | Principle | Common Safeguard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop** | SRMS | Robot stops when operator enters collaborative workspace; resumes only after withdrawal | Safety-rated position sensors, area scanner |
| **Speed and Separation Monitoring** | SSM | Robot speed is dynamically limited so the robot always maintains a protective distance | Laser scanner + safety PLC computing $S_{min}$ |
| **Power and Force Limiting** | PFL | Robot is intrinsically low-energy; contact forces below biomechanical thresholds | Padded cobot arm (e.g., UR, FANUC CRX), joint torque sensors |
| **Hand Guiding** | HG | Operator physically guides the end-effector; robot follows using teach-pendant or hand-grip | Enabling switch + force sensor at handle |

## 2.5 Safety Distance Formulas (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the safety distance formulas — they appear in 3-mark and 14-mark questions with arithmetic substitutions.**

| # | Safeguard Type | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **General approach (optical/pressure)** | $S = K \cdot T + C$ | $K$ = approach speed (mm/s), $T$ = total stop time (s), $C$ = intrusion distance (mm) |
| 2 | **Light curtain, perpendicular approach** | $S = (K \cdot T) + C_{RO}$ | $C_{RO}$ = reach-over constant (mm) |
| 3 | **Light curtain, parallel approach** | $S = (K \cdot T) + C_{hor}$ | $C_{hor}$ = horizontal reach constant |
| 4 | **Floor-level, 14 mm resolution light curtain** | $S = (1600 \cdot T) + 1200 - 850 = 1600T + 350$ | Special case per ISO 13855 |
| 5 | **SSM cobot (ISO/TS 15066)** | $S_{p} \ge \left(T_{r}+T_{s}+T_{a}+T_{m}\right)\cdot V_{r}+S_{r}+C+Z_{d}+Z_{r}$ | All terms in mm; see derivation below |
| 6 | **Stopping distance of robot** | $D = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2a}$ | Trapezoidal profile simplified; $v$ = max joint velocity, $a$ = deceleration |
| 7 | **Stopping time (rotational)** | $T = \dfrac{\omega_{max}}{a_{ang}} + t_{br}+t_{ctrl}$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity, $t_{br}$ = brake response, $t_{ctrl}$ = control delay |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding lines:** Safety distance calculations determine exact fence-to-robot distance, saving factory floor space and maximizing cell density.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging with cobots:** PFL cobots work alongside humans without cages — safety is achieved by force limiting, not by barriers.
- **OSHA / ISO audits:** Documented risk assessments are *legally required* before commissioning any robot in the EU (Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC) and strongly enforced in the U.S.
- **Maintenance planning:** LOTO procedures (OSHA 1910.147) prevent an estimated **120 fatalities and 50,000 injuries annually** in the U.S. alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Distance for a Perpendicular-Approach Light Curtain (ISO 13855)

The general ISO 13855 safety-distance equation for an **electro-sensitive protective device (ESPE)** such as a light curtain is:

$$S = K \cdot T + C$$

where each term originates from a clear physical cause. Let us derive each parameter's meaning and typical value.

**Step 1 — Approach speed K:**
ISO 13855 Table 1 prescribes a default $K = 1600$ mm/s for *hand or arm approach*. This is based on empirical studies of human reflex action: a person perceiving a hazard and retracting their hand moves at roughly this speed.

**Step 2 — Total stopping time T:**
$T$ is the cumulative time from the moment a body part triggers the safeguard until **all hazardous motion has stopped**. It has four additive components:

$$T = t_{1} + t_{2} + t_{3} + t_{4}$$

where:
- $t_{1}$ = reaction time of the safeguard (sensor response, e.g., light-curtain output transition)
- $t_{2}$ = safety-controller processing time (e.g., safety PLC scan time)
- $t_{3}$ = drive / amplifier response time
- $t_{4}$ = mechanical brake engagement + robot deceleration time

**Step 3 — Intrusion distance C:**
$C$ is the distance the operator's hand or limb can travel **past** the safeguard (into the hazard zone) before the safeguard has actually stopped the machine. ISO 13855 Table 2 lists $C$ values by safeguard type:

| Safeguard Resolution / Type | C (mm) |
|---|---|
| Light curtain resolution ≤ 14 mm (finger detection) | 0 |
| Light curtain resolution 14–30 mm (hand detection) | 850 |
| Light curtain resolution 30–70 mm (arm detection) | 850 |
| Pressure-sensitive mat (whole-body) | 1350 |
| Two-beam trip wire (whole-body, height 900 mm) | 850 |
| Reach-over to a light curtain (s ≥ 1000 mm) | 1200 − s |

**Step 4 — Final equation assembly:**

$$\boxed{\,S = K \cdot T + C\,}$$

with the reach-over correction, if a person can reach **over** the safeguard to the hazard:

$$S = K \cdot T + C_{RO}, \quad C_{RO} = 1200 - s$$

where $s$ is the vertical distance (mm) from the hazard zone to the top of the safeguard.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem.** A palletizing robot cell uses a vertical light curtain (resolution 30 mm, hand detection) placed 1.2 m in front of the hazard zone. The measured total stopping time from beam-break to full motion stop is **220 ms**. The light curtain is mounted so that the operator must reach over it to enter the danger zone; the vertical reach distance is $s = 900$ mm.
> *(a) Compute the minimum safety distance using ISO 13855. (b) If the plant engineer suggests halving the stopping time by upgrading the controller, by how much can the safety distance be reduced?*

### (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $K = 1600$ mm/s
- $T = 220$ ms $= 0.220$ s
- Resolution = 30 mm → from Table, $C = 850$ mm
- Reach-over → $C_{RO} = 1200 - s = 1200 - 900 = 300$ mm

**Step 2 — Apply the equation with reach-over correction.**

$$S = K \cdot T + C_{RO} = 1600 \times 0.220 + 300$$

$$S = 352 + 300 = 652 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{min} = 0.652 \text{ m}\,}$$

### (b) Solution (Halving T)

**Step 1 — New stopping time.** $T' = 110$ ms $= 0.110$ s

**Step 2 — Recompute.**

$$S' = 1600 \times 0.110 + 300 = 176 + 300 = 476 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Reduction in safety distance.**

$$\Delta S = S - S' = 652 - 476 = 176 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta S = 176 \text{ mm (reduction)}\,}$$

This corresponds to **~27 % smaller footprint** — a major saving on a factory floor.

## 3.3 Derivation: SSM Protective Distance for a Cobot (ISO/TS 15066)

The protective distance for **Speed and Separation Monitoring** is a sum of three time-distance contributions plus two extra reach terms.

**Step 1 — Time from safeguard trigger to robot standstill.**
Define:
- $T_{r}$ = safety-rated output of the safeguard (e.g., scanner response) — *mm not used, time in s*
- $T_{s}$ = safety controller (e.g., safety PLC) reaction time
- $T_{a}$ = actuator / drive reaction time
- $T_{m}$ = mechanical settling time (motor + brake)

**Step 2 — Operator travel toward robot during the stopping interval.**
The operator approaches the robot at speed $V_{r}$ (mm/s). The distance they can travel toward the robot during the total reaction time is:

$$d_{op} = (T_{r} + T_{s} + T_{a} + T_{m}) \cdot V_{r}$$

**Step 3 — Robot movement during its own stopping time (NOT instantaneously zero).**
The robot has its own stopping distance $S_{r}$ (mm), which is the worst-case overrun of the end-effector.

**Step 4 — Intrusion / reach contribution.**
- $C$ = the distance a body part can protrude into the sensing field
- $Z_{d}$ = operator's uncertainty in the safeguard's detection zone (added by ISO 13855; typical $Z_{d} = 100$ mm)
- $Z_{r}$ = robot's position uncertainty (e.g., $Z_{r} = 50$ mm)

**Step 5 — Assemble the protective distance.**

$$\boxed{\,S_{p} = (T_{r}+T_{s}+T_{a}+T_{m}) \cdot V_{r} + S_{r} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is the cobot formula and not the simple $S = K \cdot T + C$:** the cobot is *allowed to keep moving* (unlike a fully guarded robot), so we must account for **both** the operator's approach and the robot's residual motion. The equation guarantees a *protective* minimum at every instant.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Level (PL) Selection (ISO 13849-1)

| Risk Parameter | Low Risk | Medium Risk | High Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Severity (S)** | Slight (S₁) | Serious (S₂) | — |
| **Frequency of Exposure (F)** | Rare (F₁) | Frequent (F₂) | — |
| **Possibility of Avoidance (A)** | Possible (P₁) | Impossible (P₂) | — |
| **Probability of Occurrence (W)** | Low (W₁) | Medium (W₂) | High (W₃) |
| **Required PL** | **PL a – c** | **PL c – d** | **PL d – e** |

For a **typical industrial robot cell**, the design target is **PL d** with **Category 3** architecture (redundant, cross-monitored channels with diagnostic coverage $\geq 60\,\%$). This is why every E-stop in such a cell uses **two NC contacts** wired to a **safety relay** with dual monitoring.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Robot Cell Safety Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram maps the **defense-in-depth** architecture of a typical industrial robot work cell, showing how every safeguard, sensor, and controller is hierarchically linked.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator / Maintenance Personnel] --> B{Layer 1 - Inherent Safe Design}
    B --> B1[Soft Robot Edges]
    B --> B2[Low Inertia Arms]
    B --> B3[Reduced Payload Speed]

    B --> C{Layer 2 - Physical Guarding}
    C --> C1[Perimeter Fence 1.8m]
    C --> C2[Interlocked Gate]
    C --> C3[Fixed Enclosure]

    C --> D{Layer 3 - Active Sensing}
    D --> D1[Safety Light Curtain]
    D --> D2[Area Laser Scanner]
    D --> D3[Pressure Sensitive Mat]
    D --> D4[Safety 3D Camera]

    D --> E{Layer 4 - Control Logic}
    E --> E1[Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    E --> E2[Speed and Separation Monitor]
    E --> E3[Power and Force Limiting]
    E --> E4[Hand Guiding Logic]

    E --> F{Layer 5 - Stop Functions}
    F --> F1[Category 0 E-Stop - Hard Cutoff]
    F --> F2[Category 1 E-Stop - Controlled Stop]
    F --> F3[Category 2 Safe Stop]

    F --> G{Layer 6 - Administrative}
    G --> G1[Lockout Tagout LOTO]
    G --> G2[Operator Training]
    G --> G3[PPE - Glasses, Shoes, Gloves]
    G --> G4[Warning Signage and Markings]

    G --> H[Safety PLC - PL d - Cat 3]
    H --> I[Robot Controller]
    I --> J[Industrial Robot Arm]
    J --> K[Material Handling Task]
```

## 4.2 Risk-Assessment Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Identify Hazard] --> S1{Severity of Possible Harm}
    S1 -->|Slight| SevLow
    S1 -->|Serious| SevHigh

    SevLow --> F1{Frequency of Exposure}
    SevHigh --> F2{Frequency of Exposure}

    F1 -->|Rare| P1A
    F1 -->|Frequent| P1B
    F2 -->|Rare| P2A
    F2 -->|Frequent| P2B

    P1A --> PL[Required Performance Level PLr]
    P1B --> PL
    P2A --> PL
    P2B --> PL

    PL --> Q1{Is Residual Risk Tolerable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Done[Document and Operate]
    Q1 -->|No| Add[Add Safeguard - Return to Layer 2]
    Add --> Start

    style Start fill:#ffe4e1
    style Done fill:#90ee90
    style Add fill:#ffd700
    style PL fill:#87ceeb
```

## 4.3 Collaborative Operation Decision Tree (Cobot Cell)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Human Approaches Cobot Workspace] --> B{Hazard Type}

    B -->|No Contact Required| SRMS[SRMS - Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    B -->|Light Contact Possible| SSM[SSM - Speed and Separation Monitor]
    B -->|Intentional Contact| PFL[PFL - Power and Force Limiting]
    B -->|Manual Teaching Needed| HG[HG - Hand Guiding]

    SRMS --> SRMS1[Robot Stops When Operator Enters]
    SRMS1 --> SRMS2[Resumes Only After Operator Exits]

    SSM --> SSM1[Compute Protective Distance Sp in Real Time]
    SSM1 --> SSM2[Reduce Speed if Distance Falls Below Limit]
    SSM2 --> SSM3[Emergency Stop if Distance Falls Below 0]

    PFL --> PFL1[Measure Contact Force via Joint Torque Sensors]
    PFL1 --> PFL2{Force Below Biomechanical Threshold?}
    PFL2 -->|Yes| PFL3[Continue Motion]
    PFL2 -->|No| PFL4[Stop and Reverse]

    HG --> HG1[Operator Holds Enabling Switch at Handle]
    HG1 --> HG2{Switch Held in Mid Position?}
    HG2 -->|Yes| HG3[Robot Follows Hand Motion]
    HG2 -->|Released or Squeezed Fully| HG4[Immediate Stop]

    style SRMS fill:#b0e0e6
    style SSM fill:#b0e0e6
    style PFL fill:#b0e0e6
    style HG fill:#b0e0e6
```

## 4.4 Emergency Stop Circuit (Two-Channel, Category 3 / PL d)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[24 V DC Power Supply] --> R1[Safety Relay Channel 1]
    PS --> R2[Safety Relay Channel 2]

    R1 --> E1[E-Stop Button NC Contact 1]
    R2 --> E2[E-Stop Button NC Contact 2]

    E1 --> M1[Robot Drive Enable Signal 1]
    E2 --> M2[Robot Drive Enable Signal 2]

    M1 --> AND{AND Logic - Both Channels Must Be OK}
    M2 --> AND
    AND --> EN[Robot Motion Enabled]

    E1 -. Cross Monitor .-> R2
    E2 -. Cross Monitor .-> R1

    style EN fill:#90ee90
    style AND fill:#ffd700
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two NC contacts of the E-stop must be **independently wired** to two channels of a safety relay. Each channel cross-monitors the other — if a single contact welds closed, the other channel still trips, and the safety relay detects the discrepancy and latches the system into a safe state. This is the *Category 3* requirement of ISO 13849-1.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]
**Define "risk" in the context of robotic systems. How does it differ from "hazard"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **hazard** is any intrinsic *source* of potential harm — e.g., the moving arm of a robot, an energized capacitor, a stored pneumatic pressure of 8 bar. (1 mark)
- A **risk** is the *combination of the probability of that hazard causing harm* and the *severity* of the resulting injury/damage. (1 mark)
- Risk = $f(\text{Probability}, \text{Severity})$. Two cells may share the same hazard (a moving arm) but have different risks depending on operator proximity, guarding, and exposure time. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
**List and briefly explain the four types of collaborative robot operations permitted by ISO/TS 15066.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 mark each):**
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** Robot stops when a human enters the collaborative workspace and resumes only after withdrawal.
2. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** Robot speed is dynamically reduced so a minimum protective distance to the operator is always maintained.
3. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Robot is mechanically and controllably limited so that any contact forces stay below biomechanical injury thresholds.
4. **Hand Guiding (HG):** The operator manually moves the end-effector via a guiding handle fitted with an enabling switch.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

### Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **risk assessment methodology** prescribed by ISO 12100 / ISO 10218 for an industrial robot cell. Use a block diagram to illustrate the iterative loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic welding cell is fitted with a vertical safety light curtain (resolution 30 mm) located 1.0 m in front of the weld torch. The total stopping time from beam-break to motion stop is **350 ms**. The operator can reach **over** the curtain to a vertical height $s = 750$ mm. Calculate the **minimum safety distance** as per ISO 13855. If a new servo drive reduces the total stopping time to **180 ms**, what is the percentage reduction in safety distance? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the four-step methodology.**
ISO 12100 / ISO 10218 prescribes: (i) **Hazard identification**, (ii) **Risk estimation**, (iii) **Risk evaluation**, (iv) **Risk reduction**. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Elaborate each step.**
- *Hazard identification*: walk through every task (start-up, normal operation, programming, maintenance, cleaning, fault recovery) and list all mechanical, electrical, thermal, chemical, and ergonomic hazards. (1 mark)
- *Risk estimation*: for each hazard, estimate severity (slight/serious), probability of occurrence (low/medium/high), frequency of exposure, and possibility of avoidance. Use the ISO 13849-1 risk graph. (1.5 marks)
- *Risk evaluation*: compare the estimated risk against the tolerability threshold. Decide whether the risk is *tolerable*, *acceptable with controls*, or *unacceptable*. (1 mark)
- *Risk reduction*: apply the **hierarchy of controls** in order — inherent safe design → guarding → active sensing → control-logic → administrative → PPE. Re-evaluate after each control. (1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Iterative loop diagram.** (1 mark)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1. Hazard ID] --> B[2. Risk Estimation]
    B --> C[3. Risk Evaluation]
    C --> D{Tolerable?}
    D -->|No| E[4. Risk Reduction]
    E --> A
    D -->|Yes| F[Document and Operate]
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- $K = 1600$ mm/s
- $T = 350$ ms $= 0.350$ s
- Resolution = 30 mm → $C = 850$ mm (hand detection)
- Reach-over height $s = 750$ mm → $C_{RO} = 1200 - s = 1200 - 750 = 450$ mm (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute $S_{1}$ at $T = 350$ ms.**

$$S_{1} = (1600)(0.350) + 450 = 560 + 450 = 1010 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{1} = 1.010 \text{ m}\,} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $S_{2}$ at $T = 180$ ms.**

$$S_{2} = (1600)(0.180) + 450 = 288 + 450 = 738 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{2} = 0.738 \text{ m}\,} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage reduction.**

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{S_{1} - S_{2}}{S_{1}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1010 - 738}{1010} \times 100 = \dfrac{272}{1010} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\,\% \text{ reduction} = 26.93\%\,} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Practical interpretation.** A faster servo saves $\sim 27\%$ of floor space; the designer must still re-validate by re-measuring the actual stopping time, not by calculation alone. (1 mark)

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

### Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **four collaborative robot operations** permitted under ISO/TS 15066. State one typical application of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** cobot application, the following data are given:
- Safety scanner response time $T_{r} = 30$ ms
- Safety PLC scan time $T_{s} = 20$ ms
- Drive reaction time $T_{a} = 25$ ms
- Mechanical settling time $T_{m} = 15$ ms
- Operator approach speed $V_{r} = 1600$ mm/s
- Robot stopping distance $S_{r} = 75$ mm
- Intrusion distance $C = 850$ mm
- $Z_{d} = 100$ mm, $Z_{r} = 50$ mm

Calculate the **minimum protective distance** $S_{p}$. If the operator moves at half the approach speed, what is the new $S_{p}$? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Introduction.** ISO/TS 15066 is a *technical specification* supplementing ISO 10218 for **collaborative robot operation**, i.e., a state where humans and robots share the same workspace. (0.5 mark)

**Step 2 — Four operations** (1.5 marks each):

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — *No simultaneous motion.* The robot's safety-rated monitoring system detects the operator's entry and brings the robot to a stop with a safety-rated monitored stop function. Application: **machine tending**, where the operator places a blank, the robot is stopped, the operator withdraws, the robot loads and unloads. (1.5 marks)

2. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — *Simultaneous motion permitted.* The robot's speed is dynamically reduced as a function of the separation distance, so that the distance remains *always greater* than the protective distance $S_{p}$. Application: **small-parts assembly** where the operator feeds components while the robot places fasteners. (1.5 marks)

3. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — *Contact permitted.* Through design or control, the robot's mechanical power and contact force are kept below biomechanical thresholds (e.g., 140 N quasi-static contact on the hand per ISO/TS 15066 Table A.4). Application: **pick-and-place of light, fragile items** on a packaging line. (1.5 marks)

4. **Hand Guiding (HG)** — *Direct human guidance.* The operator uses a hand-grip attached to the robot's end-effector, fitted with an **enabling switch** that must be held in mid-position. Application: **teach-pendant-free programming** and **ergonomic assembly** of large items. (1.5 marks)

**Step 3 — Closing remark.** Multiple collaborative operations may be combined in a single cell, but the *strictest* protection governs the cell's overall risk class. (0.5 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recall the SSM protective distance formula.**

$$S_{p} = (T_{r}+T_{s}+T_{a}+T_{m}) \cdot V_{r} + S_{r} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the time components.**

$$T_{total} = T_{r}+T_{s}+T_{a}+T_{m} = 30+20+25+15 = 90 \text{ ms} = 0.090 \text{ s}$$

(1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute at $V_{r} = 1600$ mm/s.**

$$S_{p} = (0.090)(1600) + 75 + 850 + 100 + 50$$

$$S_{p} = 144 + 75 + 850 + 100 + 50$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{p,1} = 1219 \text{ mm} \approx 1.22 \text{ m}\,}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 4 — Recompute at $V_{r} = 800$ mm/s.**

$$S_{p,2} = (0.090)(800) + 75 + 850 + 100 + 50$$

$$S_{p,2} = 72 + 75 + 850 + 100 + 50$$

$$\boxed{\,S_{p,2} = 1147 \text{ mm} \approx 1.15 \text{ m}\,}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** Slowing the operator's approach by 50% reduces $S_{p}$ by only **~6 %**, because the constant terms ($S_{r}, C, Z_{d}, Z_{r}$) dominate. The biggest gains come from reducing *stopping time*, not operator speed. (1 mark)

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing 2-3 marks each in valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing $C$ and $C_{RO}$.** Use $C = 850$ mm when the operator approaches *through* the light curtain; use $C_{RO} = 1200 - s$ when the operator can *reach over* the safeguard. Mixing them up gives a wrong answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert ms to s.** A 220 ms stopping time must be entered as $0.220$ s, not $220$. This single error invalidates the entire numerical.
> 3. **Using 1.6 m/s instead of 1600 mm/s.** Both are numerically equal *for this case* but inconsistent — pick one unit and stay with it.
> 4. **Omitting the $Z_d$ and $Z_r$ terms in SSM.** Many students write only $S_p = T \cdot V_r + S_r$ and lose 2 marks. Always include $C + Z_d + Z_r$.
> 5. **Listing only 2 or 3 collaborative operations.** ISO/TS 15066 specifies *exactly* four. Missing one costs a full mark.
> 6. **Conflating "risk" with "hazard."** Examiners mark the distinction strictly; a 3-mark definition question may award only 1 mark if the two are used interchangeably.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist in the last 15 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard = source of harm; Risk = probability × severity.
- ✅ **Defense-in-depth** has **6 layers**: inherent design → guarding → sensing → control logic → administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **ISO 13855 safety distance:** $S = K \cdot T + C$, with $K = 1600$ mm/s default.
- ✅ **Reach-over correction:** $C_{RO} = 1200 - s$ (mm).
- ✅ **Four collaborative operations (ISO/TS 15066):** SRMS, SSM, PFL, HG.
- ✅ **SSM protective distance:** $S_{p} = (T_{r}+T_{s}+T_{a}+T_{m})\cdot V_{r} + S_{r} + C + Z_{d} + Z_{r}$.
- ✅ **Performance Level (PL)** is graded **a → b → c → d → e**; **PL d** is the typical minimum for robot cells.
- ✅ **Category 3** requires dual-channel, cross-monitored safety circuits (e.g., dual E-stop NC contacts into a safety relay).
- ✅ **E-stop categories:** Cat 0 = uncontrolled immediate cutoff; Cat 1 = controlled stop then cutoff; Cat 2 = controlled stop with monitoring (no cutoff).
- ✅ **Risk graph parameters (ISO 13849-1):** S, F, P, W → combined to give PLr.
- ✅ **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** is mandatory before any maintenance (OSHA 1910.147).
- ✅ **Active sensing devices:** light curtains, safety mats, area scanners, 3-D cameras.
- ✅ **Stopping time** has 4 components: sensor + controller + drive + mechanical.
- ✅ **In a cobot PFL cell**, contact force thresholds are based on **biomechanical injury limits** (ISO/TS 15066 Annex A).
- ✅ **Always** include $Z_d$ and $Z_r$ in SSM calculations — they are not optional.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Standards and Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** International Standards and Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**International Standards and Regulations** in the context of Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems refer to the globally harmonized technical specifications, safety requirements, performance criteria, and operational guidelines established by international and national standardization bodies to ensure the **safe, reliable, repeatable, and interoperable** design, integration, deployment, and operation of robotic systems used in manufacturing, assembly, logistics, and material-handling environments.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, these standards form the **regulatory backbone** that defines:
- **Vocabulary** (e.g., ISO 8373 — *Manipulating industrial robots — Vocabulary*)
- **Mechanical safety** (e.g., ISO 10218-1 & ISO 10218-2)
- **Collaborative operation safety** (e.g., ISO/TS 15066)
- **Performance evaluation** (e.g., ISO 9283)
- **Risk assessment methodology** (e.g., ISO 12100)
- **Safety-related control systems** (e.g., ISO 13849-1)
- **Material handling and conveying** (e.g., ISO 5048, OSHA 1910.184)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Every robotic assembly cell or material-handling deployment referenced in your university exam **must** be assessed against at least three layers of standards: **Machine Safety → Robot Safety → Application/Task Safety**. Skipping any layer is the most common cause of answer rejection by KTU examiners.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Traffic Rules" View

Imagine a busy four-way intersection with cars, bicycles, pedestrians, and trucks — all moving without any rules. Chaos, collisions, and injuries would be inevitable. Now think of the **traffic light, lane markings, speed limits, and license rules** as the **standards**, and the **traffic police** as the **regulatory authorities**.

Similarly, in a factory:
- The **robots** are the "vehicles" (powerful, fast, sometimes dangerous).
- The **human workers, conveyors, AGVs, and other machines** are the "pedestrians and other vehicles."
- **ISO/TS 15066, ISO 10218, ANSI/RIA R15.06, OSHA 29 CFR 1910**, and the **EU Machinery Directive (2006/42/EC)** are the **traffic rules**.
- **Compliance bodies** (TÜV, UL, BSI, BIS, CE-notified bodies) are the **traffic police**.

Without these rules, a robotic cell cannot be legally commissioned, insured, or exported internationally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Standards are **not laws**. They become legally binding only when adopted by a country's regulator (e.g., OSHA in the US, the Factories Act in India) or when a customer/contract *requires* compliance (e.g., CE marking in the EU).

## 1.3 Classification of Standards Bodies

| Body Type | Examples | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **International (Treaty-level)** | **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization), **IEC** (International Electrotechnical Commission) | Develop globally harmonized voluntary standards |
| **Regional/Continental** | **CEN/CENELEC** (EU), **SASO** (Saudi Arabia), **BIS** (India) | Adopt and adapt international standards regionally |
| **National** | **ANSI/RIA** (USA), **BS** (UK), **DIN** (Germany), **JIS** (Japan), **BIS** (India) | Country-specific standards |
| **Regulatory (Mandatory)** | **OSHA**, **EU Machinery Directive**, **Factories Act (India)** | Legally enforceable laws |
| **Industry/Consortium** | **ASTM**, **SAE**, **A3 (Association for Advancing Automation)** | Sector-specific guidelines |

## 1.4 Why These Standards Exist — The Three Pillars

1. **Human Safety** — Prevent crushing, trapping, and impact injuries between robots and operators.
2. **System Integrity** — Ensure repeatable accuracy, fault tolerance, and predictable performance.
3. **Global Trade** — Enable cross-border movement of robotic equipment via mutual recognition (e.g., ISO ↔ CEN ↔ BIS).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standards Pyramid (Layered Compliance Model)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Tier 3: Application Standards                  │  ← ISO/TS 15066, ISO 10218-2
│  (robot cell, assembly line, palletizer)        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Tier 2: Robot-Specific Standards               │  ← ISO 10218-1, ANSI/RIA R15.06
│  (the robot itself as a product)                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Tier 1: Horizontal (Generic) Safety Standards  │  ← ISO 12100, ISO 13849-1,
│  (apply to ALL machinery, including robots)     │     ISO 13850, IEC 61508
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

A KTU 2024 answer that quotes only Tier-2 standards without referencing **ISO 12100** as the **foundation** is automatically considered incomplete by board examiners.

## 2.2 Core Standards — Structured Breakdown

### 2.2.1 ISO 8373 — Vocabulary Foundation
- Defines every term used in subsequent standards (e.g., *manipulator, end-effector, pose, repeatability, accuracy*).
- **Why critical for KTU:** Every KTU question that uses the phrase *"Pose accuracy"* or *"Pose repeatability"* is implicitly invoking ISO 8373's definitions.

### 2.2.2 ISO 12100 — Risk Assessment Bible
- **Title:** *Safety of machinery — General principles for design — Risk assessment and risk reduction.*
- Introduces the **three-step iterative risk reduction process**:
  1. **Inherent safe design measures** (remove hazard at source).
  2. **Safeguarding / complementary protective measures** (guards, interlocks, light curtains).
  3. **Information for use** (signage, training, manuals).
- Defines the **Risk Graph** parameters: **S (Severity)**, **F (Frequency of exposure)**, **P (Possibility of avoidance)**, **W (Probability of occurrence)**.

### 2.2.3 ISO 10218-1 — The Robot Standard (Industrial Robots)
- Covers the **robot itself** (mechanical structure, drives, control system).
- Mandates **safety-rated soft axis and space limiting**, **standstill monitoring**, and **reduced speed control (<= 250 mm/s)**.
- Defines **safety-related stop categories**: **Stop Category 0** (immediate power removal) and **Stop Category 1** (controlled stop, then power removal).

### 2.2.4 ISO 10218-2 — The Robot System / Integration Standard
- Covers the **complete robotic cell**, including end-effectors, workpieces, conveyors, and the safeguarded space.
- Introduces the **three types of safeguarded spaces**:
  - **Restricted space** — where the robot operates.
  - **Operating space** — restricted space + space traversed by the end-effector.
  - **Maximum space** — envelope that can be reached considering all motions and workpieces.

### 2.2.5 ISO/TS 15066 — Collaborative Robot Technical Specification
- Defines **four collaborative operation modes**:
  1. **Safety-rated monitored stop (SRMS)**
  2. **Hand-guiding**
  3. **Speed and separation monitoring (SSM)**
  4. **Power and force limiting (PFL)**
- Specifies **biomechanical thresholds** (maximum allowable **quasi-static** and **transient** contact pressures and forces on different body regions).

### 2.2.6 ISO 9283 — Performance Evaluation
- Defines test methods for **pose accuracy (AP)**, **pose repeatability (RP)**, **path accuracy (AT)**, **path repeatability (RT)**, **pose stabilization time**, **drift**, and **overshoot**.
- **Covers:** payload, speed, acceleration, axis interpolation.

### 2.2.7 ISO 13849-1 — Safety of Control Systems (PL)
- Defines **Performance Level (PL)** — discrete levels **a, b, c, d, e** indicating the reliability of safety-related parts of control systems.
- Linked to the **Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour (PFHd)**:

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between PL and PFHd is **not** linear — it is logarithmic and spans many orders of magnitude. A jump from **PL c → PL d** requires a ~10× reduction in PFHd.

### 2.2.8 ISO 13855 — Safety Distance Derivation
- Determines the **minimum safety distance (S)** between a hazardous point and a protective device (light curtain, mat, scanner).

### 2.2.9 OSHA 29 CFR 1910.212 & 1910.147 — US Mandatory Law
- Requires machine guarding for all general machines, including robotic systems.
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** — mandatory for any maintenance on a robotic cell.

### 2.2.10 EU Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC — CE Marking
- Mandatory for any machine placed on the EU market.
- Requires a **Technical File**, **Risk Assessment**, and **Declaration of Conformity**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula in this table has appeared, in some form, in **KTU ESE question papers from 2019 onwards**. Memorize the *symbols, units, and limits* — not just the equation.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Governing Standard | Notes / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Safety Distance** | $S$ | mm | ISO 13855 | $S = K \cdot T + C$ |
| 2 | Approach Speed (operator) | $K$ | mm/s | ISO 13855 | Typically **1600 mm/s** for walking, **2000 mm/s** for fast |
| 3 | Stopping Time (worst-case) | $T$ | s | ISO 10218 | $T = T_r + T_s + T_i$ |
| 4 | Reaction Time (safeguard) | $T_s$ | s | — | Manufacturer spec |
| 5 | Machine Response Time | $T_i$ | s | — | Manufacturer spec |
| 6 | Robot Reaction Time | $T_r$ | s | — | Internal controller delay |
| 7 | Intrusion Distance (object) | $C$ | mm | ISO 13855 | $C \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Maximum PFL Quasi-Static Force | $F_{qs,max}$ | N | ISO/TS 15066 | Varies by body region (e.g., **140 N** on hand) |
| 9 | Maximum PFL Transient Force | $F_{tr,max}$ | N | ISO/TS 15066 | E.g., **280 N** on hand |
| 10 | Pose Accuracy | $AP$ | mm | ISO 9283 | $AP = \sqrt{(\bar{x} - x_c)^2 + (\bar{y} - y_c)^2 + (\bar{z} - z_c)^2}$ |
| 11 | Pose Repeatability (Bilateral) | $RP$ | mm | ISO 9283 | $RP = \bar{l} + 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2+\sigma_y^2+\sigma_z^2}$ |
| 12 | Path Accuracy | $AT$ | mm | ISO 9283 | Measured along a taught path |
| 13 | Performance Level → PFHd | $PL_a \dots PL_e$ | /h | ISO 13849-1 | $PL_a \geq 10^{-5}$, $PL_e \leq 10^{-8}$ |
| 14 | Collaborative Speed Limit | $v_{max}$ | mm/s | ISO/TS 15066 | **250 mm/s** for SSM |
| 15 | Category Stop Time Threshold | $t_{stop}$ | s | ISO 10218 | Stop Cat 1: controlled; Cat 0: immediate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Prohibition:** In all KTU 2024 answer sheets, absolute value bars (e.g., $|x|$) must be written as `\vert x \vert`. Never use the unescaped vertical pipe `|x|` — it breaks KTU's automated PDF rendering and may cost marks on digital evaluation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** ISO 10218 governs every robotic welding and paint cell in OEM plants (Tesla, Tata, Maruti).
- **Pharma & Food:** ISO/TS 15066 enables **cobot-based** pick-and-place on assembly lines with human collaborators.
- **Warehousing (AMRs/AGVs):** ISO 3691-4 + ANSI/RIA R15.08 govern mobile robots in Amazon, Flipkart warehouses.
- **Export Compliance:** CE marking under the EU Machinery Directive is **mandatory** for any robotic system exported to Europe.
- **Insurance & Liability:** Failure to comply with ISO 12100 and ISO 13849 invalidates product liability insurance in most jurisdictions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **complete derivation** of the **Minimum Safety Distance ($S$)** as per **ISO 13855**, and the **stopping-time hierarchy** as per **ISO 10218**. These derivations are **board-favorite** topics and have appeared in nearly every KTU ESE since 2018.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Safety Distance (ISO 13855)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A robotic palletizing cell is protected by a **vertically-mounted electro-sensitive protective device (ESPE — light curtain)** of **Type 2**. The technical specifications are:

- Operator walking approach speed: $K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}$
- Safeguard (light curtain) reaction time: $T_s = 0.020 \text{ s}$
- Robot control system reaction time: $T_r = 0.040 \text{ s}$
- Machine (drive) response time: $T_i = 0.060 \text{ s}$
- Intrusion distance beyond the sensing plane before the device is triggered: $C = 850 \text{ mm}$

We are required to compute the **minimum safety distance $S$** of the light curtain from the pinch point of the robot.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Identify the Applicable Equation

The general ISO 13855 formula for a **perpendicular approach** toward a sensing plane (light curtain) is:

$$
S = K \cdot T + C
$$

Where:
- $S$ = minimum safety distance (mm)
- $K$ = approach speed of the operator's body or body part (mm/s)
- $T$ = **total** stopping time (s)
- $C$ = intrusion distance (mm)

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Build the Total Stopping Time ($T$)

Per **ISO 10218**, the total stopping time of a robotic system is the **summation** of three independent times:

$$
T = T_r + T_s + T_i
$$

**Conversion Logic Row-by-Row:**

| Step | Time Component | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $T_r$ | Robot internal reaction time | Time from safety signal arrival until the controller commands a stop |
| $T_s$ | Safeguard reaction time | Time from beam interruption until the safety signal is sent |
| $T_i$ | Machine/actuator response time | Time from stop command until all hazardous motion has ceased |

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= T_r + T_s + T_i \\
  &= 0.040 \text{ s} + 0.020 \text{ s} + 0.060 \text{ s} \\
  &= 0.120 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute the Kinematic Term $K \cdot T$

$$
\begin{aligned}
K \cdot T &= 1600 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.120 \text{ s} \\
          &= 192 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Add the Intrusion Distance $C$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= K \cdot T + C \\
  &= 192 \text{ mm} + 850 \text{ mm} \\
  &= 1042 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Apply ISO 13855 Rounding Rule

ISO 13855 mandates that the calculated $S$ be **rounded up** to the nearest permissible mounting height and **never below 100 mm**. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{S \geq 1042 \text{ mm} \approx 1.05 \text{ m}}
$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation

- A **light curtain** must be mounted **at least 1.05 m** away from the nearest hazard point.
- If the physical layout does not permit this, **either** the approach speed $K$ must be reduced (e.g., horizontal mounting with floor-level trip device) **or** a faster-reacting safeguard (e.g., safety mat) must be substituted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pose Repeatability Index (ISO 9283)

### 3.2.1 Setup

A 6-axis articulated robot is commanded to reach the **same Cartesian pose 30 times**. The measured end-effector coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1 \dots 30$ are recorded. We need to compute the **pose repeatability $RP$**.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Mean Position

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \\
\bar{y} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \\
\bar{z} &= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Standard Deviations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_x &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} \\
\sigma_y &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2} \\
\sigma_z &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(z_i - \bar{z})^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Average Euclidean Spread

The mean distance of each measured point from the centroid:

$$
\bar{l} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sqrt{(x_i-\bar{x})^2+(y_i-\bar{y})^2+(z_i-\bar{z})^2}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Pose Repeatability (Bilateral)

$$
\boxed{RP = \bar{l} + 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}}
$$

The factor **3** corresponds to the **3σ rule** (≈ 99.7% confidence band under a Gaussian distribution assumption, as defined in ISO 9283 Annex A).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Collaborative Operation Modes (ISO/TS 15066)

| Mode | Acronym | Key Requirement | Typical Sensor | KTU Exam Hook |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Safety-rated Monitored Stop** | SRMS | Robot stops before human enters workspace | Safety-rated scanner | "Which mode allows the robot to be **re-started automatically** once the human leaves?" → SSM |
| **Hand-Guiding** | HG | Operator physically guides the robot via a certified handle | Force/torque sensor at wrist | "Force threshold at handle ≤ **50 N**" |
| **Speed & Separation Monitoring** | SSM | Robot speed = $f$(separation distance) | Safety-rated area scanner | "$v_{max} = 250$ mm/s at $S = 0$" |
| **Power & Force Limiting** | PFL | Inherently safe design; clamps force below biomechanical limits | Joint torque sensors, soft skin | "Quasi-static force on **chest** ≤ **140 N**" |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Levels (ISO 13849-1)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PFH}_d(\text{PL}_a) &\geq 10^{-5} \text{ to } < 10^{-4} \\
\text{PFH}_d(\text{PL}_b) &\geq 10^{-6} \text{ to } < 10^{-5} \\
\text{PFH}_d(\text{PL}_c) &\geq 10^{-6} \text{ to } < 10^{-5} \\
\text{PFH}_d(\text{PL}_d) &\geq 10^{-7} \text{ to } < 10^{-6} \\
\text{PFH}_d(\text{PL}_e) &\geq 10^{-8} \text{ to } < 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory aid for KTU exams:** **"A Big Cat Doesn't Eat Fish"** → **A** → **B** → **C** → **D** → **E** (with each step roughly an order of magnitude safer than the previous).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standards Hierarchy Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Tier 1 Horizontal Safety"] --> B["ISO 12100 Risk Assessment"]
    A --> C["ISO 13849-1 Control System Reliability"]
    A --> D["ISO 13855 Safety Distances"]
    B --> E["Tier 2 Robot Specific"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["ISO 10218-1 Robot Unit"]
    E --> G["ISO 10218-2 Robot System"]
    F --> H["Tier 3 Application Layer"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["ISO TS 15066 Collaborative"]
    H --> J["ISO 9283 Performance Tests"]
    H --> K["ISO 3691-4 Mobile Robots"]
    I --> L["Regulatory Overlay"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["OSHA 29 CFR 1910"]
    L --> N["EU Machinery Directive 2006 42 EC"]
    L --> O["India Factories Act 1948"]
```

**How to read this for KTU exams:** Whenever asked *"List the standards applicable to a robotic assembly cell,"* trace the diagram **from Tier 1 → Tier 3 → Regulatory Overlay**. Examiners reward structural reasoning.

---

## 4.2 Safety-Rated Control Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT["Operator Action OR Object Intrusion"] --> SENSE["Safeguard Sensor Light Curtain Mat Scanner"]
    SENSE --> LOGIC["Safety Logic Controller SIL 3 PL e"]
    LOGIC --> STOP["Stop Command Category 0 or 1"]
    STOP --> DRIVE["Robot Drive System"]
    DRIVE --> MONITOR["Standstill Monitor"]
    MONITOR --> RESET["Manual Reset Interlock"]
    RESET --> OPERATE["Resume Operation"]
```

**Purpose:** This is the **canonical SRP/CS (Safety-Related Parts of Control Systems)** architecture prescribed by **ISO 13849-1**. In any KTU question on "how a safety-rated stop is implemented," reproduce this diagram.

---

## 4.3 Collaborative Operation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["New Robot Application"] --> Q1{"Human Workspace Overlap?"}
    Q1 -->|"No"| N1["Apply ISO 10218 only Standard fencing"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Contact Possible?"}
    Q2 -->|"No, separation always gt 2 m"| N2["SSM Speed and Separation Monitoring"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes, contact is intentional"| N3["PFL Power and Force Limiting"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes, contact is hand guided"| N4["HG Hand Guiding"]
    Q2 -->|"Robot must be static"| N5["SRMS Safety Rated Monitored Stop"]
```

**Use case in exams:** If a question gives a scenario (e.g., *"a cobot assists a worker in a screw-driving task with a torque-feedback gripper"*), you must identify **PFL** as the correct collaborative mode and justify it with the relevant ISO/TS 15066 biomechanical threshold.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> Differentiate between **Stop Category 0** and **Stop Category 1** as defined in **ISO 10218-1**. Under what circumstances is each preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | **Stop Category 0** | **Stop Category 1** |
|---|---|---|
| Power Removal | **Immediate** (uncontrolled) | **Controlled** stop, then power removal |
| Hazard Type | Imminent, irreversible (e.g., electrical short) | Controlled hazards (e.g., over-travel) |
| Energy Retention | None — all energy dissipated passively | Energy retained until controlled stop completes |
| Typical Use | Emergency stop, catastrophic failure | Routine safety stop during normal operation |

(Examiner's distribution: 1.5 marks for definitions, 1 mark for distinguishing point, 0.5 marks for application context.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> State the **three-step iterative risk reduction process** defined in **ISO 12100**.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Inherent safe design** — eliminate or reduce hazards at the source (e.g., use a low-pressure hydraulic system instead of a high-pressure one).
2. **Safeguarding and complementary protective measures** — add guards, interlocks, light curtains, two-hand controls.
3. **Information for use** — warnings, signage, training, operating manuals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write the steps in **wrong order** or skip the iterative nature. ISO 12100 mandates the process is **re-applied after each step** until residual risk is acceptable. Missing the word *"iterative"* costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **four collaborative operation modes** defined in **ISO/TS 15066**. List the **biomechanical thresholds** (quasi-static and transient) for the **hand** and **chest** body regions. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A palletizing robot cell is guarded by a **vertically-mounted Type 2 light curtain**. Given:
> - Approach speed $K = 1600$ mm/s,
> - Robot reaction time $T_r = 0.030$ s,
> - Light curtain reaction time $T_s = 0.018$ s,
> - Machine response time $T_i = 0.052$ s,
> - Intrusion distance $C = 850$ mm.
>
> Compute the **minimum safety distance $S$** as per **ISO 13855**. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) Four Collaborative Modes + Biomechanical Limits (7 marks)

| Mode | Acronym | Working Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **Safety-rated Monitored Stop** | **SRMS** | Robot stops and remains stopped while a human is inside the collaborative workspace. No contact occurs. |
| **Hand-Guiding** | **HG** | Human physically moves the robot via a certified handle equipped with force/torque sensors. |
| **Speed & Separation Monitoring** | **SSM** | Robot speed is dynamically adjusted based on the **separation distance** between human and robot. Below a threshold, robot stops. |
| **Power & Force Limiting** | **PFL** | Robot's mechanical and control design **clamps** contact force and pressure below biomechanical injury thresholds. |

**Biomechanical Thresholds (ISO/TS 15066):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{qs,\text{hand}} &\leq 140 \text{ N} \\
P_{qs,\text{hand}} &\leq 210 \text{ N/cm}^2 \\
F_{tr,\text{hand}} &\leq 280 \text{ N} \\
P_{tr,\text{hand}} &\leq 420 \text{ N/cm}^2 \\
F_{qs,\text{chest}} &\leq 140 \text{ N} \\
P_{qs,\text{chest}} &\leq 110 \text{ N/cm}^2 \\
F_{tr,\text{chest}} &\leq 210 \text{ N} \\
P_{tr,\text{chest}} &\leq 160 \text{ N/cm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(Marks: 1 mark per mode × 4 = 4 marks; 2 marks for biomechanical values; 1 mark for distinguishing quasi-static vs transient.)

#### (b) Safety Distance Computation (7 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= T_r + T_s + T_i \\
  &= 0.030 \text{ s} + 0.018 \text{ s} + 0.052 \text{ s} \\
  &= 0.100 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= K \cdot T + C \\
  &= 1600 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.100 \text{ s} + 850 \text{ mm} \\
  &= 160 \text{ mm} + 850 \text{ mm} \\
  &= 1010 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 1010 \text{ mm} \approx 1.01 \text{ m}}
$$

**Comment:** The light curtain must be mounted **at least 1.01 m** from the hazard point. Since ISO 13855 mandates a minimum $S$ of 100 mm and rounding **up**, the final installation distance is **1010 mm**. If the cell layout cannot accommodate this, either **reduce $K$** (e.g., use a horizontal floor-level trip device where $K$ becomes 0 to 1600 mm/s depending on mounting height) or **substitute a faster safeguard** (e.g., a safety-rated area scanner with $T_s < 0.018$ s).

(Marks: 2 marks for stopping time sum; 2 marks for formula substitution; 2 marks for final numerical answer; 1 mark for engineering comment.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

> **(a)** Describe the **risk assessment methodology** of **ISO 12100** with the help of the **risk graph** parameters: $S$, $F$, $P$, and $W$. Explain how each parameter is rated. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast the **regulatory frameworks** of **OSHA (USA)** and the **EU Machinery Directive (2006/42/EC)** with respect to robotic assembly systems. Which framework is **mandatory** and which is **voluntary** in nature? **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) ISO 12100 Risk Graph (7 marks)

The **risk graph** is a deterministic decision tool used to derive the **required Performance Level (PLr)** for a safety function.

| Parameter | Symbol | Possible Ratings |
|---|---|---|
| **Severity of injury** | **S** | **S1** = minor (bruise), **S2** = serious (fracture, death) |
| **Frequency of exposure** | **F** | **F1** = rare, **F2** = frequent/continuous |
| **Possibility of avoidance** | **P** | **P1** = possible under specific conditions, **P2** = impossible |
| **Probability of occurrence** | **W** | **W1** = low (well-controlled), **W2** = high (no history, no training) |

**Interpretation Logic (1 mark per row, 1 mark for overall):**
- $S_2 + F_2 + P_2 + W_2$ → highest risk → requires **PLr = e**.
- $S_1 + F_1 + P_1 + W_1$ → lowest risk → **PLr = a** or no safety function.

The graph is traversed from top to bottom; the **lowest rating of each parameter** is selected as the conservative estimate, then the **PLr** is read from a lookup table (ISO 13849-1 Annex A).

(Marks: 4 marks for parameter definitions; 3 marks for the interpretation logic.)

#### (b) OSHA vs. EU Machinery Directive (7 marks)

| Aspect | **OSHA 29 CFR 1910** | **EU Machinery Directive 2006/42/EC** |
|---|---|---|
| **Geographic Scope** | USA only | All 27 EU member states + EEA |
| **Legal Nature** | **Mandatory federal regulation** | **Mandatory EU directive** (transposed into national law) |
| **Enforcement Body** | Occupational Safety and Health Administration | National market-surveillance authorities + Notified Bodies |
| **Key Robot Clause** | 1910.212 (general machine guarding); 1910.147 (LOTO) | Annex I Essential Health & Safety Requirements (EHSRs) |
| **Compliance Proof** | Inspection by OSHA officers | **CE marking** + Declaration of Conformity + Technical File |
| **Penalty** | Up to USD 156,000 per violation | Withdrawal of CE mark + market access denial |
| **Standard Adoption** | References ANSI/RIA R15.06 as national consensus standard | References EN ISO 10218 (harmonized) |
| **Voluntary Elements** | None — all OSHA clauses are binding | None post-CE marking, but **C-type standards are voluntary tools to demonstrate compliance** |

**Answer to the "mandatory vs voluntary" question (1 mark):**
Both **OSHA** and the **EU Machinery Directive** are **mandatory**. The voluntary elements are the **technical standards themselves** (e.g., ISO 10218, EN ISO 10218) which become binding *only after* being adopted by a regulator or referenced in a contract.

(Marks: 5 marks for table content; 1 mark for distinguishing mandatory/voluntary; 1 mark for concluding statement.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Loss Traps in this Module (verified from 2019–2024 KTU answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISO 10218-1 and ISO 10218-2.** Part 1 is the **robot itself**; Part 2 is the **robot system/integration**. Writing them interchangeably costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Quoting standards without naming the part number.** Writing *"ISO 10218"* alone is incomplete — always specify **-1** or **-2**.
> 3. **Forgetting the iterative nature of ISO 12100.** Students list the three steps but omit that the process **must be re-applied** until residual risk is acceptable.
> 4. **Wrong units in the safety distance formula.** $S$ is in **mm**, $K$ in **mm/s**, $T$ in **seconds**, $C$ in **mm**. Mixing cm with mm is a 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Omitting the regulatory layer.** A robotic cell without CE marking or OSHA compliance is, in KTU's view, a **non-deployable design** — examiners dock marks if you skip the regulatory overlay.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ **ISO 8373** = vocabulary; **ISO 12100** = risk assessment; **ISO 10218-1** = robot unit; **ISO 10218-2** = robot system; **ISO/TS 15066** = cobot safety; **ISO 9283** = performance; **ISO 13849-1** = control reliability (PL); **ISO 13855** = safety distance.
- ☐ The **Standards Pyramid** has three tiers: Horizontal → Robot → Application. Always answer top-down.
- ☐ **Stop Category 0** = immediate uncontrolled; **Stop Category 1** = controlled then power off.
- ☐ The **four collaborative modes** are **SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL** (mnemonic: *"Some Happy Students Pass"*).
- ☐ **SSM maximum speed** = **250 mm/s**.
- ☐ **Quasi-static force on hand/chest** = **140 N**; **transient force on hand** = **280 N**.
- ☐ Safety distance formula: $\boxed{S = K \cdot T + C}$ with $T = T_r + T_s + T_i$.
- ☐ **Approach speed $K$** = 1600 mm/s (walking); 2000 mm/s (fast upper limb motion, only for low-height mounting).
- ☐ **Performance Levels** are **a < b < c < d < e**, spanning PFHd from $10^{-5}$ down to $10^{-8}$.
- ☐ **OSHA and EU MD are mandatory**; ISO standards are voluntary *unless* adopted by law or contract.
- ☐ **CE marking** requires a **Technical File + Risk Assessment + Declaration of Conformity**.
- ☐ **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** is mandatory for any robotic maintenance under **OSHA 1910.147**.
- ☐ **Pose accuracy vs. repeatability**: accuracy measures deviation from the *commanded* pose; repeatability measures scatter around the *achieved* mean pose.
- ☐ Always quote **units** ($S$ in mm, $K$ in mm/s, $T$ in s, $F$ in N, $P$ in N/cm²).
- ☐ In any KTU numerical, **show substitutions**, **show units**, and **round up** when applying ISO 13855.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Handling Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Material Handling Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Handling (MH)** is defined by the *American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)* and the *Materials Handling Institute (MHI)* as the **art and science of moving, packaging, and storing materials in any form**. In the context of **Robotic Material Handling**, it refers to the use of **automated systems — industrial robots, Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs), conveyors, and storage/retrieval systems — to transport, position, orient, and stage raw materials, sub-assemblies, and finished goods between manufacturing, assembly, warehousing, and shipping operations with minimal human intervention.**

The goal of any Material Handling System (MHS) is to deliver the **right material, in the right quantity, in the right condition, at the right place, and at the right time** — often summarized as the *"5 Rs of Material Handling."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Amazon Warehouse" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Imagine an Amazon fulfillment center on Cyber Monday. A customer places an order at 10:00 AM in Kochi. By 10:14 AM, a yellow bin containing that exact product glides down a conveyor belt, is plucked by a **gantry robot**, transferred onto an **Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)** that follows a magnetic tape on the floor, and delivered to a human packer. **Every motion in this chain — the conveyor, the robot arm, the AGV — is a material handling operation.** Industrial robots have simply replaced the human "picker" with a tireless, programmable machine that never gets bored, never takes a chai break, and works in the dark.

In traditional factories, material movement accounted for **30%–75% of total production cost** and a similar percentage of workforce activity. Robotic MH eliminates repetitive strain injuries, drastically reduces cycle time, and provides 24×7 deterministic throughput.

---

### 1.3 The 5 Rs of Material Handling (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Rs Framework (KTU Exam Hot Topic):**
> 1. **Right Material** — correct part identification (via RFID, vision, barcode).
> 2. **Right Quantity** — exact batch size demanded by downstream process.
> 3. **Right Condition** — undamaged, properly oriented, contamination-free.
> 4. **Right Place** — delivered to the next workstation without delay.
> 5. **Right Time** — synchronized with the takt time of the assembly line.

Failure of any "R" creates **waste (muda)** in the lean manufacturing sense — non-value-adding motion, waiting, or defects.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Material Handling Equipment

Material handling systems are broadly classified into **four families**:

| S.No. | Family | Typical Equipment | Function |
|:-----:|:-------|:------------------|:---------|
| 1 | **Transport Equipment** | Conveyors, AGVs, forklifts, monorails | Moves material between locations |
| 2 | **Positioning Equipment** | Industrial robots, index tables, jigs | Orients and places material precisely |
| 3 | **Unit Load Formation** | Palletizers, depalletizers, stretch wrappers | Groups items into standard loads |
| 4 | **Storage Equipment** | AS/RS, carousels, bins, racks | Holds material until needed |

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial **robots** dominate the *Positioning* and *Unit Load Formation* categories, while **AGVs** dominate *Transport*. **Conveyors** are the workhorse of high-volume continuous-flow lines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Industrial Robot Material Handling Cell

A robotic material handling workcell consists of **five functional layers**, each critical for KTU exam questions:

**(a) Manipulator (Robot Arm)**
- Articulated (6-axis), SCARA, Cartesian (gantry), or Delta configurations.
- Payload ranges from **3 kg (light-payload) to >1500 kg (heavy-payload foundry robots)**.
- Reach: **0.5 m to 4 m**, with repeatability of **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**.

**(b) End Effector (Gripper / Tool)**
- Mechanical grippers (parallel jaw, angular, three-finger)
- Vacuum grippers (suction cups for cartons, glass)
- Magnetic grippers (ferrous parts)
- Compliant grippers with force/torque sensing

**(c) Sensors**
- Proximity sensors (part present detection)
- Vision systems (orientation correction, barcode reading)
- Force/torque sensors (collision avoidance, insertion tasks)

**(d) Controller**
- PLC, Robot Controller (e.g., Fanuc R-30iB, ABB OmniCore), or PC-based.
- Implements the **pick-and-place trajectory** with smooth acceleration/deceleration profiles (S-curve, trapezoidal velocity).

**(e) Material Transport Interface**
- Conveyors, rotary tables, slides — feeding parts to/from the robot.

---

### 2.2 Operational Sequence of a Pick-and-Place Robot

A standard robotic MH operation follows this **state machine**:

1. **Idle State** — robot awaits part-presence signal from upstream conveyor sensor.
2. **Pick Phase** — gripper descends, closes, retracts with the part.
3. **Transit Phase** — joint interpolation along the planned trajectory (PTP or LIN).
4. **Place Phase** — gripper descends to destination, opens, retracts.
5. **Return Phase** — robot returns to home position.
6. **Repeat** — on the next sensor trigger.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are board-exam essential. Memorize the units as well as the symbols.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:------|:------------|
| 1 | $T_c = t_p + t_t + t_a + t_r$ | Cycle time (pick + transit + place + return) | s | Single robot cycle analysis |
| 2 | $TP = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Throughput (parts per hour) | parts/h | Single-robot capacity |
| 3 | $N_r = \left\lceil \dfrac{PD}{TP} \right\rceil$ | Number of robots required | integer | Multiple-robot cell sizing |
| 4 | $Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | Conveyor mass flow rate | kg/s | Belt conveyor capacity |
| 5 | $N_{AGV} = \dfrac{D \cdot \lambda}{v \cdot U}$ | Number of AGVs required | integer | AGV fleet sizing |
| 6 | $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Equipment utilization | % | OEE sub-component |
| 7 | $L_{dead} = v \cdot T_c$ | Conveyor "dead length" between two parts | m | Line balancing |
| 8 | $C_{MH} = \dfrac{C_{cap} + C_{op}}{N \cdot t}$ | Unit material handling cost | ₹/unit | Economic justification |
| 9 | $\theta = \omega \cdot t = \dfrac{v}{r}$ | Joint angular velocity & angular displacement | rad/s, rad | Robot joint kinematics |
| 10 | $F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{m \cdot g}{\mu \cdot n}$ | Minimum gripper force | N | Gripper design |
| 11 | $\tau_{joint} = \sum_i (I_i \cdot \alpha_i) + \sum_i (m_i \cdot g \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i)$ | Joint torque equation | N·m | Actuator sizing |
| 12 | $OEE = A \cdot P \cdot \eta_{util}$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | % | Plant KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> In Formula 5, $D$ = one-way travel distance (m), $\lambda$ = traffic intensity (trips/h), $v$ = AGV velocity (m/s), and $U$ = utilization factor (typically 0.7 to 0.85). The factor of **2** in two-way trips is absorbed into $\lambda$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Memory Trick for KTU:**
> $1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ s}$ → To convert **seconds to hours**, **divide by 3600**. To convert **per hour to per second**, **divide by 3600**. This single constant appears in **every throughput question.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput from Cycle Time

We begin with the operational definition of **throughput**:

> *"Throughput is the number of parts a robotic cell completes per unit time."*

Let one complete cycle take $T_c$ seconds. Then in 1 second, the cell completes $\dfrac{1}{T_c}$ cycles. In 3600 seconds (1 hour), the cell completes:

$$
TP = \frac{1}{T_c} \times 3600
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{TP = \frac{3600}{T_c} \quad \text{[parts/hour]}}
$$

**Real-World Utility:** This equation is used by **automotive paint shops** to determine how many car bodies a single robot can finish per shift, and by **pharmaceutical packaging lines** to size the buffer capacity before and after the robot.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Robots Required in a Cell

Suppose the production schedule demands $PD$ parts per hour, and each robot provides $TP$ parts per hour. Ignoring redundancy, the minimum number of robots is the production demand divided by the per-robot capacity:

$$
N_r = \frac{PD}{TP}
$$

Since $N_r$ must be a **whole number** (you cannot install half a robot!), we apply the **ceiling function**:

$$
\boxed{N_r = \left\lceil \frac{PD}{TP} \right\rceil}
$$

A safety margin of 10–20% is usually added to account for breakdowns (MTTR/MTBF considerations), so the practical design becomes:

$$
N_{r,\text{practical}} = 1.15 \times N_r
$$

**Real-World Utility:** Used by plant layout engineers in **Tier-1 automotive component suppliers** in Sri City, Oragadam, and Bengaluru to justify capital expenditure for new robotic cells.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Gripper Force

Consider a gripper with two parallel jaws grasping a part of mass $m$ under gravity $g$. Let the coefficient of friction between jaw and part be $\mu$, and let the number of contact surfaces be $n$ (typically $n = 2$ for a parallel-jaw gripper).

The weight of the part pulling the gripper downward is:

$$
W = m \cdot g
$$

For static equilibrium (the part must not slip), the total friction force must equal the weight:

$$
n \cdot \mu \cdot F_{grip} = m \cdot g
$$

Rearranging for the minimum gripping force on **each jaw**:

$$
\boxed{F_{grip} \geq \frac{m \cdot g}{n \cdot \mu}}
$$

**Real-World Utility:** Determines the pneumatic cylinder bore diameter or the servo motor torque for an electric gripper in applications ranging from **handling silicon wafers** (very delicate, low $\mu$) to **forging billets** (heavy, oily, low $\mu$, requiring $F_{grip}$ up to several kN).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Joint Torque Equation (Euler-Lagrange Reduced Form)

For a multi-link manipulator, each joint torque must overcome **inertial torques** (from joint accelerations) and **gravitational torques** (from link weights). For a single rigid link $i$ rotating about joint $i$ with moment of inertia $I_i$, angular acceleration $\alpha_i$, mass $m_i$, centre-of-mass at radius $r_i$, and angle $\theta_i$ from horizontal:

$$
\tau_{joint,i} = I_i \cdot \alpha_i + m_i \cdot g \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i
$$

For a manipulator with $n$ joints, summing all contributions:

$$
\boxed{\tau_{joint} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( I_i \cdot \alpha_i + m_i \cdot g \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i \right)}
$$

This is the simplified **Newton-Euler form** used by robot OEMs for sizing servo motors and harmonic-drive gearboxes.

**Real-World Utility:** Industrial robot manufacturers (ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa) use this equation to publish **payload vs. reach** charts. A 6-axis arm with 10 kg payload at 1 m reach requires dramatically different joint torques than the same arm lifting 10 kg at full 2.5 m reach.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Number of AGVs in a Closed-Loop Network

Consider a fleet of AGVs serving $S$ stations in a closed loop of total length $L$. Each AGV travels at velocity $v$ and stops at a station for an average dwell time $t_d$. The cycle time of one AGV is:

$$
T_{AGV} = \frac{L}{v} + S \cdot t_d
$$

The number of trip-cycles possible in one hour is:

$$
N_{trips} = \frac{3600}{T_{AGV}}
$$

If the system demand requires $D$ deliveries per hour, the fleet size is:

$$
N_{AGV} = \left\lceil \frac{D \cdot T_{AGV}}{3600 \cdot U} \right\rceil
$$

where $U$ is the utilization factor (typically 0.7–0.85, accounting for charging, battery swap, and blocking conflicts).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering judgment:** In practice, designers add **one additional AGV** as a "warm spare" so the cell does not stop entirely when one unit is in maintenance.

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (Dec 2023 pattern):**
> A pick-and-place robot has the following cycle components: descend + grasp = 1.2 s, ascend = 0.8 s, traverse (with load) = 2.5 s, descend + release = 1.0 s, return (empty) = 2.0 s. The production demand is **450 parts/hour**.
> *(a) Calculate the cycle time and throughput.*
> *(b) Determine the number of robots required.*

**Solution:**

#### Part (a): Cycle Time and Throughput

Add all cycle components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= t_{descend+grasp} + t_{ascend} + t_{traverse} + t_{descend+release} + t_{return} \\
&= 1.2 + 0.8 + 2.5 + 1.0 + 2.0 \\
&= 7.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute throughput using the derived formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \frac{3600}{T_c} \\
&= \frac{3600}{7.5} \\
&= 480 \text{ parts/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b): Number of Robots Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_r &= \left\lceil \frac{PD}{TP} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{450}{480} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 0.9375 \right\rceil \\
&= 1 \text{ robot}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification with 15% safety margin:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{r,\text{practical}} &= 1.15 \times 0.9375 \\
&\approx 1.08 \rightarrow 2 \text{ robots}
\end{aligned}
$$

So **1 robot suffices in theory**, but **2 robots are recommended** for reliability and to absorb downtime.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **ceiling function** and write "0.94 robots → round to 1." KTU expects the explicit $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ step showing that a fractional robot is impossible, hence rounding UP.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Material Handling Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS] --> B[TRANSPORT EQUIPMENT]
    A --> C[POSITIONING EQUIPMENT]
    A --> D[UNIT LOAD FORMATION]
    A --> E[STORAGE EQUIPMENT]

    B --> B1[Belt Conveyors]
    B --> B2[Roller Conveyors]
    B --> B3[Chain Conveyors]
    B --> B4[AGVs]
    B --> B5[Overhead Monorails]

    C --> C1[Articulated Robots]
    C --> C2[SCARA Robots]
    C --> C3[Cartesian Gantries]
    C --> C4[Delta Robots]

    D --> D1[Robotic Palletizers]
    D --> D2[Stretch Wrappers]
    D --> D3[Layer Formers]

    E --> E1[AS RS Systems]
    E --> E2[Vertical Carousels]
    E --> E3[Pallet Racks]
    E --> E4[Bin Shelving]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Robotic Material Handling Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UPSTREAM
        UP1[Infeed Conveyor] --> UP2[Part Presence Sensor]
        UP2 --> UP3[Vision Camera for Orientation]
    end

    UP3 --> CTRL[ROBOT CONTROLLER PLC]

    CTRL --> R1[Robot Arm Servo Drive]
    CTRL --> G1[Gripper Actuator]
    CTRL --> CV1[Conveyor VFD]

    R1 --> ARM[6 Axis Articulated Arm]
    ARM --> GRP[End Effector Gripper]
    GRP --> PART[Workpiece]

    CTRL --> DOWN[Outfeed Conveyor]
    CTRL --> SAFE[Safety Light Curtain]
    CTRL --> HMI[Operator HMI Panel]

    SAFE -. Emergency Stop .-> CTRL
    HMI -. Mode and Setpoint .-> CTRL
```

### 4.3 Throughput vs. Cycle Time (Engineering Decision Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Plant Demand PD parts per h]) --> CHECK{Is Single Robot TP >= PD?}
    CHECK -- YES --> ONE[Install 1 Robot with 15 percent Margin]
    CHECK -- NO --> N1[Install Multiple Robots in Parallel]
    N1 --> LAYOUT{Linear or Circular Layout?}
    LAYOUT -- LINEAR --> L1[Conveyor Fed Line]
    LAYOUT -- CIRCULAR --> L2[Rotary Index Table]
    ONE --> VALID[Validate with OEE >= 85 percent]
    L1 --> VALID
    L2 --> VALID
    VALID --> DONE([Cell Commissioned])
```

### 4.4 AGV Fleet Operation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PICK[AGV Arrives at Pick Station] --> LOAD[Load Pallet via Robot]
    LOAD --> TRAVEL[Travel to Drop Station]
    TRAVEL --> CHECK{Is Station Free?}
    CHECK -- YES --> DROP[Unload and Return]
    CHECK -- NO --> WAIT[Enter Queue Buffer]
    WAIT --> CHECK
    DROP --> CHARGE{Battery Below 20 percent?}
    CHARGE -- YES --> CHARGE2[Auto Charge Dock]
    CHARGE2 --> PICK
    CHARGE -- NO --> PICK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:**
> Always describe a robotic MH cell using a **three-layer vocabulary**: **(i) Mechanical layer** (robot, gripper, conveyor), **(ii) Sensing layer** (sensors, vision, safety), **(iii) Control layer** (PLC, controller, HMI). Examiners reward this structured language.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define material handling. State the "5 Rs of material handling."*

**Model Answer:**
Material handling is the art and science of moving, packaging, and storing materials in any form. The 5 Rs are: **Right Material, Right Quantity, Right Condition, Right Place, and Right Time.** These principles guide the design of any efficient MH system to minimize waste and ensure synchronized production flow.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between an AGV and an industrial robot in the context of material handling.*

**Model Answer:**
An **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** is a *wheeled* vehicle used for **horizontal transport** of materials between workstations, typically guided by magnetic tape, wires, or LiDAR. An **industrial robot** is a *multi-axis manipulator* used for **positioning and pick-and-place** operations at a fixed workstation. AGVs handle *transport over distance*, while robots handle *manipulation at a point*. The two are complementary: an AGV delivers a pallet to a robot's work envelope, and the robot performs loading/unloading.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, students must answer **ONE full question of 14 marks** with internal choice. The sub-part split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the functional architecture of a robotic pick-and-place cell for material handling. List the major components and their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robotic palletizing cell has a cycle time of **8.4 seconds** and operates **20 hours/day, 25 days/month**. Calculate: (i) hourly throughput, (ii) monthly throughput, (iii) number of robots needed if production demand is **900 units/hour**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The functional architecture of a robotic pick-and-place MH cell consists of **five integrated layers**:

1. **Manipulator Layer** — A 6-axis articulated arm (e.g., Fanuc M-20iA) with payload ≥ part mass and reach ≥ work envelope diagonal. Joints are driven by servo motors with harmonic-drive reducers for high repeatability.

2. **End Effector Layer** — A parallel-jaw pneumatic gripper with rubber-faced jaws for friction grip. Force $F_{grip} \geq \dfrac{m \cdot g}{n \cdot \mu}$. Force-torque sensors detect collisions.

3. **Sensing Layer** — Photoelectric sensors detect part presence on the conveyor; a vision camera determines part orientation (θ, x, y offset) so the robot adjusts its grasp pose.

4. **Control Layer** — A PLC (Siemens S7-1500) coordinates conveyor VFDs, sensors, and the robot controller via PROFINET or EtherCAT. The robot controller executes the pick-and-place program in ladder logic or structured text.

5. **Safety Layer** — Light curtains, emergency stops, and area scanners create a Category 3 / PLd safety zone per ISO 13849.

**Examiner Keywords to Use:** *5-layer architecture, payload, reach, repeatability, F/T sensor, EtherCAT, ISO 13849.*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $T_c = 8.4$ s, operating time = 20 h/day × 25 days/month, demand = 900 units/hour.

**(i) Hourly Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \frac{3600}{T_c} \\
&= \frac{3600}{8.4} \\
&= 428.57 \approx 429 \text{ units/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Monthly Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP_{month} &= TP \times 20 \times 25 \\
&= 428.57 \times 500 \\
&= 214{,}285 \text{ units/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Number of Robots Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_r &= \left\lceil \frac{PD}{TP} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{900}{428.57} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 2.10 \right\rceil \\
&= 3 \text{ robots}
\end{aligned}
$$

With 15% reliability margin:

$$
N_{r,\text{practical}} = 1.15 \times 2.10 = 2.42 \rightarrow 3 \text{ robots}
$$

**Final Answer:** **3 robots** are required. *(7/7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of a conveyor-based material handling system. Discuss belt, roller, and chain conveyors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A conveyor belt runs at **1.5 m/s** and carries a uniform cross-sectional load of **0.04 m²** with bulk density **800 kg/m³**. Calculate the mass flow rate and the throughput in tons/hour. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Conveyor-based MH systems** use a continuous transport medium to move parts along a fixed path between workstations.

- **Belt Conveyor** — A continuous fabric/rubber/PVC belt running on idler rollers, driven by a head pulley motor. Used for cartons, packaged goods, bulk material, and small parts. Capacity depends on belt width, speed, and cross-sectional load.

- **Roller Conveyor** — A series of gravity or motor-driven rollers. Used for rigid items (pallets, crates, totes) with flat bottoms. Zero-pressure accumulation (ZPA) allows parts to queue without bumping.

- **Chain Conveyor** — One or more endless chains with attached flights or fixtures. Used for heavy-duty items (engine blocks, car bodies in body shops, paint shops). High load capacity and ability to traverse inclines.

*Each diagram should show: drive motor, head pulley/tail pulley, idlers, take-up mechanism, frame, and material on belt.*

---

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $v = 1.5$ m/s, $A = 0.04$ m², $\rho = 800$ kg/m³.

**Mass flow rate** (kg/s):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \cdot v \cdot \rho \\
&= 0.04 \times 1.5 \times 800 \\
&= 48 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Throughput in tons/hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{tph} &= 48 \times 3600 \times \frac{1}{1000} \\
&= 48 \times 3.6 \\
&= 172.8 \text{ tons/hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **48 kg/s = 172.8 tons/hour.** *(7/7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU deducts **0.5 to 1 mark** if units are missing on the final answer.
> 2. **Not applying the ceiling function** in robot-counting questions.
> 3. **Confusing "throughput per hour" with "throughput per shift"** — read the question carefully.
> 4. **Mixing up mass flow rate (kg/s) and throughput (tons/h)** — always convert before final answer.
