# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define material handling. List any four principles of material handling proposed by James M. Apple.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):**
> Material handling is defined as the art and science of moving, packaging, and storing of substances in any form. It involves the use of appropriate equipment, methods, and systems to ensure efficient, safe, and economical flow of materials from raw stage to finished product.
>
> **Four Principles of Apple (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Planning Principle:** All material handling activities must be planned in advance using a systematic approach.
> 2. **Systems Principle:** Material handling must be integrated into a coordinated system rather than isolated operations.
> 3. **Unit Load Principle:** Larger unit loads should be handled to reduce the number of trips and improve efficiency.
> 4. **Ergonomics Principle:** The system should be designed considering human physical and psychological capabilities.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a bridge crane and a gantry crane. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Bridge Crane (1.5 marks):**
> A bridge crane is a type of overhead crane in which the bridge (horizontal beam) runs on *overhead runways* mounted on the building structure. The trolley and hoist travel along the bridge, allowing motion in three axes (X, Y, Z). **Application:** Used inside heavy machine shops for moving machine tool components.
>
> **Gantry Crane (1.5 marks):**
> A gantry crane has a bridge supported on *legs that run on ground-level rails or wheels*, requiring no building structure. It is suitable for outdoor or temporary installations. **Application:** Used in container yards and shipyards for handling shipping containers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a belt conveyor. Derive the expression for power required to drive the belt conveyor. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A belt conveyor consists of an **endless belt** stretched between two pulleys — a **drive pulley** (powered by an electric motor through a gearbox) at the discharge end, and a **tail/idler pulley** at the loading end. The belt is supported along its length by **idlers** (rollers) — either a flat slider bed or troughed at 35°. A **take-up mechanism** (screw, gravity, or hydraulic) maintains belt tension. The motor, gearbox, and coupling form the drive unit. Loading is done through a **feed chute** or **hopper** at the tail end, and discharge is at the head pulley.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the motor rotates the drive pulley, friction between the pulley and belt causes the belt to move continuously. Material loaded onto the belt at the tail end is carried along the conveyor length and discharged at the head end. The troughed idlers contain the material in a cross-section for maximum carrying capacity.

**Power Derivation (2 marks):**
The power required to drive the belt = Effective pulling force × Belt velocity, divided by drive efficiency:

$$P = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \times v}{1000 \times \eta} \text{ [kW]}$$

where $T_1$ = tight side tension [N], $T_2$ = slack side tension [N], $v$ = belt speed [m/s], $\eta$ = drive efficiency (typically 0.85 to 0.92).

The ratio $T_1 / T_2$ is given by Euler's capstan equation:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction and $\theta$ = wrap angle at drive pulley.

---

**Q: (b) A belt conveyor of width 1200 mm is used to transport iron ore of bulk density 2.0 t/m³ at a rate of 1500 tonnes/hour. The belt speed is 2.0 m/s and the drive efficiency is 88%. The coefficient of friction is 0.3 and the wrap angle is 200°. Assume a CEMA trough angle of 35°. Determine the required motor power. Take slack side tension T₂ = 9 kN. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Verify belt width using volumetric capacity.**

$$Q = \frac{M}{\rho} = \frac{1500}{2.0} = 750 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \frac{750}{3600} = 0.2083 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A_{required} = \frac{Q}{v} = \frac{0.2083}{2.0} = 0.1042 \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{available} = 0.0686 \times W^2 = 0.0686 \times (1.2)^2 = 0.0686 \times 1.44 = 0.0988 \text{ m}^2$$

Since $A_{available} < A_{required}$, the 1200 mm belt is slightly undersized. However, we proceed with the given dimensions for this problem.

**Step 2 — Convert wrap angle to radians.**

$$\theta = 200° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 3.491 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's equation to find $T_1$.**

$$T_1 = T_2 \times e^{\mu \theta} = 9000 \times e^{0.3 \times 3.491}$$

$$0.3 \times 3.491 = 1.0473$$

$$e^{1.0473} = 2.85$$

$$T_1 = 9000 \times 2.85 = 25,650 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective pulling force.**

$$F = T_1 - T_2 = 25,650 - 9,000 = 16,650 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute motor power.**

$$P = \frac{F \times v}{1000 \times \eta} = \frac{16,650 \times 2.0}{1000 \times 0.88} = \frac{33,300}{880} = 37.84 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Apply 1.25 design safety factor.**

$$P_{design} = 37.84 \times 1.25 = 47.3 \text{ kW} \approx 50 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** A **50 kW motor** is required.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Internal Choice

**Q: (a) Explain the construction and working of an electric wire-rope hoist with a neat diagram. List four applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An electric wire-rope hoist consists of:
- **Electric motor** (3-phase induction, squirrel-cage) — provides the driving torque.
- **Gearbox** (helical or planetary) — reduces motor speed to drum speed.
- **Wire rope drum** — grooved steel cylinder on which the wire rope is wound.
- **Wire rope** — high-tensile steel rope of 6×19 or 8×19 construction.
- **Bottom block** — contains the load hook and sheaves (pulleys).
- **Top hook** — for suspending the hoist on the trolley/beam.
- **Limit switches** — upper and lower travel limits.
- **Brake** — electromagnetic disc brake, failsafe (spring-applied, electrically released).
- **Control pendant** — for operator push-button control.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the operator presses the "up" button, the motor receives current and rotates the drum through the gearbox. As the drum rotates, the wire rope winds onto the drum, lifting the bottom block and load. On pressing the "down" button, the motor reverses, unwinding the rope. The electromagnetic brake holds the load when the motor is off. The limit switch cuts off power at the top extreme position to prevent overwinding.

**Four Applications (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Machine shops for tool changing and component handling.
2. Automobile service stations for engine lifting.
3. Steel plants for ladle handling.
4. Warehouses for loading/unloading trucks via overhead gantries.

---

**Q: (b) A 5-tonne electric wire-rope hoist lifts a maximum load of 5000 kg at 8 m/min. The drum diameter is 400 mm and the overall gear train efficiency is 75%. Calculate: (i) Motor power, (ii) Drum torque, (iii) Drum RPM, and (iv) Recommended motor rating with 1.5 safety factor. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$W = 5000$ kg, $v = 8$ m/min, $D = 0.4$ m, $\eta = 0.75$.

**(i) Motor Power:**

$$P = \frac{W \times v}{6120 \times \eta} = \frac{5000 \times 8}{6120 \times 0.75} = \frac{40,000}{4590} = 8.71 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Drum Torque:**

$$T = \frac{W \times g \times D}{2 \times \eta_{gear}} = \frac{5000 \times 9.81 \times 0.4}{2 \times 0.75}$$

$$T = \frac{19,620}{1.5} = 13,080 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Drum RPM:**

$$N = \frac{v \times 60}{\pi \times D} = \frac{8 \times 60}{\pi \times 0.4} = \frac{480}{1.2566} = 381.97 \text{ RPM} \approx 382 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iv) Recommended Motor Rating (with 1.5 safety factor):**

$$P_{design} = 8.71 \times 1.5 = 13.07 \text{ kW} \approx 15 \text{ kW}$$

Select a **15 kW, 3-phase, 4-pole (1440 RPM) induction motor** with a **3.77:1 helical gear reducer**.

**Verification of motor speed to drum speed:**

$$\text{Gear ratio} = \frac{1440}{382} = 3.77 : 1 \text{ (single-stage helical or planetary)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in KTU Examinations (Module 4: CMHS):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up constants in the hoist power formula:** Students often write $P = (W \times v) / (\eta \times 60)$ instead of $P = (W \times v) / (6120 \times \eta)$. The constant **6120** correctly converts kg·m/min to kW accounting for gravity. **Lose 1 mark** if the derivation is incomplete.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the safety factor:** Always multiply by **1.25 to 1.5** for conveyor/hoist design. Examiners expect a "design" value, not a "theoretical" value.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the wrap angle in Euler's equation:** Many students use $\theta = 180°$ by default. The actual wrap angle depends on the snub pulley configuration — common values are **180°, 200°, 210°, 240°**.
>
> 4. **Misinterpreting Forklift load center:** When the actual load center exceeds the rated load center, the capacity *decreases* — not increases. This is a **conceptual trap**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units:** $v$ in the power formula must be in **m/s** (for conveyor) or **m/min** (for hoist). Mixing units is a **silent killer of marks**.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state: "Assuming CEMA standard trough of 35°" or "Neglecting windage losses" at the start of the solution. Examiners reward clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 — Final Revision Flashcard:**

✅ **Definition:** Material handling = art + science of moving, packaging, storing substances.

✅ **Apple's 10 Principles:** Memorize the mnemonic **"P-S-W-U-S-S-E-E-E-C"** (Planning, Systems, Work, Unit-load, Space, Standardization, Ergonomics, Energy, Ecology, Cost).

✅ **MHI Classification:** Transport, Positioning, Storage, Unit Load Formation — only the *conventional* subset is in Module 4.

✅ **Belt Conveyor Power Formula:** $P = (T_1 - T_2) \times v / (1000 \times \eta)$ [kW] — *always* with safety factor 1.25 to 1.5.

✅ **Euler's Capstan Equation:** $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ — applies to belt drives, rope hoists, and capstan winches.

✅ **CEMA Standard Trough Angle:** **35°** for three-roller idler. Available cross-section $A = 0.0686 W^2$.

✅ **Conveyor Throughput:** $M = Q \times \rho = A \times v \times \rho$ — units: t/h.

✅ **Hoist Power Formula Constant:** **6120** for $W$ in kg, $v$ in m/min. Drum torque $T = W \times g \times D / (2\eta)$.

✅ **Hoist Drum Speed:** $N = 60v / (\pi D)$ — used to compute gear ratio with motor.

✅ **Crane Types:** Bridge (overhead runway), Gantry (floor rails), Jib (column-mounted rotating arm), Monorail (single beam).

✅ **Forklift Stability Triangle:** 3-point contact — always verify CG remains inside the triangle during operation.

✅ **Load Center Adjustment:** $Q_{allowed} = Q_{rated} \times L_{rated} / L_{actual}$ — inverse proportionality.

✅ **Industrial Standards (India):** IS 3177 (cranes), IS 4573, IS 7194 (noise), IS 7251 (forklifts).

✅ **Design Safety Factor (Hoist):** **1.5×** for hoist service; **1.25×** for conveyor service.

✅ **Conventional vs Automated:** Conventional = human-operated mechanical equipment. Robotic AMHS = sensor-driven, programmable. CMHS forms the *baseline* for AMHS retrofit.

✅ **Typical Capacities:** Belt conveyors up to 6000 TPH, Overhead cranes up to 500 tonnes, Forklifts 1–10 tonnes typical, Wire-rope hoists up to 80 tonnes standard.

---

> 🎓 **End of Module 4 Notes — Conventional Material Handling Systems**
> *You are now prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination on this module.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Material Handling Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Material Handling Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Material Handling Robot (MHR)** is defined as a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed specifically to transport, load, unload, transfer, palletize, de-palletize, package, sort, or position raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP) components, and finished goods between defined locations within a manufacturing, warehousing, or distribution environment **without direct human assistance**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy (Course Outcome **CO4** – *Select and configure appropriate robotic systems for material handling applications*), material handling robots are classified primarily on the basis of:

1. **Geometric Configuration (Mechanical Architecture)** — the arrangement and type of joints (Prismatic `P` or Revolute `R`).
2. **Mobility Class** — Stationary (fixed-base) vs. Mobile (wheeled/AGV).
3. **Application Function** — Pick-and-Place, Palletizing, Machine Tending, AGV-based transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** *"A material handling robot is a reprogrammable, computer-controlled mechanical manipulator whose end-effector is tasked solely with the movement, positioning, or transfer of discrete or bulk material units across a defined industrial work envelope."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Warehouse Workforce

Imagine a **large Amazon-style fulfillment center** where thousands of orders must be picked from shelves, packed, sorted, and loaded onto trucks every hour.

- A human worker uses **two arms, two legs, and a brain** to walk, pick, and place.
- A **material handling robot** performs the same task, but with:
  - **A robot arm (or gantry) replacing the human arm** → precision and tireless repetition.
  - **A track or mobile base replacing the legs** → predictable, programmable navigation.
  - **A controller replacing the brain** → deterministic, sensor-driven decisions.

Just as workers are specialized (a forklift driver ≠ a shelf picker ≠ a box packer), **industrial robots are specialized by geometry** for specific material handling subtasks. A **gantry robot** acts like an overhead crane, a **SCARA robot** acts like a tabletop pick-and-place assistant, and an **AGV** acts like a self-driving forklift.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Metrics)

The following metrics are the **yardsticks** KTU examiners use to compare material handling robot types:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degrees of Freedom | DOF | Dimensionless | Number of independent motions |
| Payload Capacity | $P$ | **kg** or **N** | Maximum mass the wrist can carry |
| Reach / Work Envelope | $R$ | **m** or **mm** | Maximum extent of the end-effector |
| Repeatability | $\pm \rho$ | **mm** | Ability to return to a taught position |
| Positioning Accuracy | $\sigma$ | **mm** | Deviation from the commanded position |
| Maximum Linear Speed | $v_{max}$ | **m/s** | Throughput driver |
| Maximum Joint Speed | $\dot{\theta}_{max}$ | **rad/s** or **deg/s** | Joint-level velocity limit |
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | **s** | Time per pick-place cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When the question says *"Compare two material handling robots"*, the answer MUST be a comparison on at least **4 of the 7 metrics** above. KTU 2024 scheme typically awards **2 marks** for a structured comparison table.

### 1.4 Primary Classification Tree

```
Material Handling Robots
        │
        ├── 1. Stationary (Fixed-Base) Manipulators
        │       ├── Cartesian (Gantry) Robot       [PPP]
        │       ├── Cylindrical Robot              [RPP]
        │       ├── Spherical (Polar) Robot        [RRP]
        │       ├── Articulated (Jointed-Arm)      [RRR]
        │       ├── SCARA Robot                    [RRP]
        │       └── Delta (Parallel) Robot         [Parallel Kinematics]
        │
        ├── 2. Specialized Pick-and-Place Robots
        │       ├── High-Speed Picker
        │       ├── Palletizing / Depalletizing Robot
        │       └── Bag / Case Packer
        │
        └── 3. Mobile Material Handling Robots
                ├── Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)
                ├── Autonomous Mobile Robot (AMR)
                └── Automated Storage & Retrieval (AS/RS) Crane
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification by Mechanical Configuration — Joint Notation

The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the international standard joint notation where:
- **P** → Prismatic (Linear sliding) joint
- **R** → Revolute (Rotational) joint

The configuration is always written in the order **Base → Wrist**.

### 2.2 Detailed Anatomy of Each Type

#### A. Cartesian (Gantry) Robot — `[P, P, P]`

- **Mechanism:** Three mutually perpendicular **prismatic joints** aligned with the X, Y, and Z axes of a global Cartesian frame.
- **Work Envelope:** A perfect **rectangular cuboid**.
- **Strengths:** Highest payload-to-weight ratio; excellent for large work envelopes (moves on overhead rails); simplest kinematics (decoupled equations).
- **Weaknesses:** Requires substantial structural framing; large floor space when mounted overhead.
- **Typical Applications:** Palletizing, machine loading, large-part transfer, CNC tending, 3D printing.
- **Industry Examples:** Used in **automotive welding lines** and **PCB assembly**.

#### B. Cylindrical Robot — `[R, P, P]`

- **Mechanism:** One **revolute** base joint (rotation about a vertical axis) + two **prismatic** joints (one vertical, one radial).
- **Work Envelope:** A portion of a **cylinder** (annular cylinder).
- **Strengths:** Good for pick-and-place around a circular work area; robust design.
- **Weaknesses:** Reaches "behind itself" poorly; rare in modern greenfield plants.
- **Typical Applications:** Machine tending, die casting, foundry operations.

#### C. Spherical (Polar) Robot — `[R, R, P]`

- **Mechanism:** Two **revolute** joints (waist rotation + shoulder pitch) + one **prismatic** joint (radial extension).
- **Work Envelope:** A portion of a **sphere** (spherical sector).
- **Strengths:** Large reach with a compact base; classic geometry (e.g., Unimate).
- **Weaknesses:** Joint couplings make the inverse kinematics coupled (less trivial than Cartesian).
- **Typical Applications:** Heavy-payload forging, glass handling, forging press tending.

#### D. Articulated (Jointed-Arm) Robot — `[R, R, R, R, R, R]`

- **Mechanism:** **All revolute joints** — usually 6 in modern industrial units.
- **Work Envelope:** A complex, **asymmetric, quasi-spherical** volume.
- **Strengths:** Maximum dexterity; can reach into confined pockets; mimics the human arm.
- **Weaknesses:** Coupled, non-linear kinematics → computationally expensive controllers.
- **Typical Applications:** Universal use — welding, painting, palletizing, assembly, machine tending.
- **Industry Standard Models:** FANUC M-2000iA series (heavy payload, 2.3-ton capacity).

#### E. SCARA Robot — `[R, R, P, R]` or `[R, R, R]`

- **SCARA** = **S**elective **C**ompliance **A**ssembly **R**obot **A**rm.
- **Mechanism:** Two parallel **revolute** joints providing compliance in the X–Y plane, plus a **prismatic** vertical joint.
- **Work Envelope:** A horizontal **disc** (or "donut" with inner dead-zone).
- **Strengths:** Exceptionally high speed and stiffness for vertical pick-and-place; fast assembly.
- **Weaknesses:** Limited to vertical insertion tasks; cannot reach overhead.
- **Typical Applications:** Surface-mount device (SMD) placement, screw-driving, vertical assembly.
- **Industry Standard:** Epson SCARA, Yamaha YK series.

#### F. Delta (Parallel) Robot

- **Mechanism:** Three (or more) **parallel kinematic chains** connected to a common moving platform via parallelograms.
- **Work Envelope:** A **flat, dome-shaped** workspace directly above the base.
- **Strengths:** **Fastest** pick-and-place robot (cycle times as low as 30–50 ms).
- **Weaknesses:** Small work envelope; limited payload.
- **Typical Applications:** High-speed food packaging, pharmaceutical pick-and-place, blister packing.

#### G. Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)

- **Mechanism:** Mobile base (differential drive, steering wheel, or omni-wheels) + path-following sensor (magnetic tape, QR-code, LiDAR).
- **Work Envelope:** Entire facility floor.
- **Strengths:** Replaces human-driven forklifts; flexible routing; 24/7 operation.
- **Weaknesses:** Requires infrastructure (magnetic strips or floor maps).
- **Typical Applications:** Warehouse tote transfer, hospital logistics, line-side delivery.
- **Modern Variant:** **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot)** — uses SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), no fixed path required.

#### H. Palletizing Robot

- **Mechanism:** A high-payload (typically 50–1600 kg) **articulated arm** with a specialized end-effector (bag gripper, fork gripper, vacuum head).
- **Work Envelope:** A near-spherical volume that envelops the pallet and infeed conveyor.
- **Strengths:** Replaces back-breaking manual palletizing; pattern flexibility.
- **Typical Applications:** End-of-line packaging in food, beverage, cement, and chemical industries.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important comparison** KTU examiners expect for this topic. Memorize it.

| Property | Cartesian [PPP] | Cylindrical [RPP] | Spherical [RRP] | Articulated [RRR] | SCARA [RRP] | Delta [Parallel] | AGV [Mobile] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint Type Sequence** | L, L, L | θ, r, z | θ, φ, r | θ₁…θ₆ | θ₁, θ₂, z | Parallelogram | Differential drive |
| **Work Envelope Shape** | Rectangular cuboid | Hollow cylinder | Spherical sector | Irregular sphere | Flat disc | Dome | Full facility |
| **Reach Equation** | $X_{max} \cdot Y_{max} \cdot Z_{max}$ | $\pi R^2 H$ | $\tfrac{2}{3}\pi R^3$ | Complex (Jacobian) | $\pi R^2$ (flat) | $V_{dome}$ | Floor area |
| **Typical Payload** | 5–500 kg | 20–100 kg | 5–200 kg | 5–2300 kg | 1–20 kg | 0.5–8 kg | 50–1500 kg |
| **Typical Reach** | 0.5–10 m | 0.5–2 m | 0.5–3 m | 0.5–4.7 m | 0.2–1 m | 0.3–1.2 m | Unlimited (floor) |
| **Repeatability** | $\pm$ 0.05 mm | $\pm$ 0.1 mm | $\pm$ 0.1 mm | $\pm$ 0.05–0.2 mm | $\pm$ 0.01–0.05 mm | $\pm$ 0.05 mm | $\pm$ 10 mm |
| **Best For** | Heavy load, large envelope | Vertical machine tending | Heavy forging | Universal | High-speed assembly | Ultra-fast picking | Long-distance transport |
| **Relative Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Medium | High | High |

### 2.4 Kutzbach–Grübler Mobility Criterion (DOF Formula)

The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of any material handling robot is computed using the **Kutzbach criterion** (modified Grübler formula for spatial mechanisms):

$$
M = 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mobility (DOF) of the robot
- $n$ = Total number of links (including the ground)
- $j_1$ = Number of lower-pair joints with 1 DOF (e.g., revolute or prismatic)
- $j_2$ = Number of lower-pair joints with 2 DOF
- $j_3, j_4, j_5$ = Higher-pair joint counts

> [!NOTE]
> For a **spatial 6-DOF articulated robot**: $n = 7$ links, $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints. Applying the formula yields $M = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 36 - 30 = \mathbf{6}$, matching the practical DOF count.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of a material handling robot is governed by the **payload-to-reach ratio**, the **duty cycle**, and the **environment**.

- **Automotive Body Shop:** Articulated robots (FANUC R-2000iC) for spot welding.
- **Pharmaceutical Packaging:** Delta robots (ABB FlexPicker) for blister-pack filling.
- **E-Commerce Fulfillment:** AMRs (Kiva/Zebra) for bin transport.
- **Cement Industry:** Heavy-payload articulated palletizing robots.
- **Electronics SMT Lines:** SCARA robots for chip placement.

The **economic trade-off** is always **capital cost vs. cycle time vs. flexibility**. A Cartesian gantry is cheaper but a SCARA is faster for small parts. An AGV replaces a fleet of forklifts but requires infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Work Envelope of a Cartesian Robot

A Cartesian robot has three prismatic joints with maximum travels $X_{max}$, $Y_{max}$, $Z_{max}$.

Since each axis can move from $-X_{max}$ to $+X_{max}$ (and similarly for Y and Z), the total linear extent along each axis is $2X_{max}$, $2Y_{max}$, $2Z_{max}$.

The work envelope is therefore a **rectangular cuboid** of volume:

$$
V_{Cartesian} = (2X_{max}) \times (2Y_{max}) \times (2Z_{max}) = 8 \cdot X_{max} \cdot Y_{max} \cdot Z_{max}
$$

**Example:** If a gantry robot has travels $X_{max} = 1.5$ m, $Y_{max} = 2$ m, $Z_{max} = 0.8$ m:

$$
V_{Cartesian} = 8 \times 1.5 \times 2 \times 0.8 = 19.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We multiply the *full extents* (2X, 2Y, 2Z) because the robot arm can travel symmetrically about its home position in each direction.

### 3.2 Derivation: Work Envelope of a Cylindrical Robot

A cylindrical robot has:
- One **rotational** base joint (angle $\theta$, range $0 \le \theta \le 2\pi$)
- One **prismatic radial** joint (radius $r$, range $0 \le r \le R_{max}$)
- One **prismatic vertical** joint (height $z$, range $0 \le z \le Z_{max}$)

The work envelope is the volume swept when $r$ and $z$ vary over their full ranges while $\theta$ sweeps the full circle.

Step 1 — Differential volume element in cylindrical coordinates:

$$
dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz
$$

Step 2 — Integrate over the full angular range $[0, 2\pi]$, full radial range $[0, R_{max}]$, and full vertical range $[0, Z_{max}]$:

$$
V_{Cylindrical} = \int_{0}^{Z_{max}} \int_{0}^{R_{max}} \int_{0}^{2\pi} r \, d\theta \, dr \, dz
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate the angular integral:

$$
V_{Cylindrical} = \int_{0}^{Z_{max}} \int_{0}^{R_{max}} 2\pi r \, dr \, dz
$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the radial integral:

$$
V_{Cylindrical} = \int_{0}^{Z_{max}} \left[ \pi r^2 \right]_{0}^{R_{max}} dz = \int_{0}^{Z_{max}} \pi R_{max}^2 \, dz
$$

Step 5 — Evaluate the vertical integral:

$$
V_{Cylindrical} = \pi R_{max}^2 \cdot Z_{max}
$$

**Example:** For $R_{max} = 1.2$ m and $Z_{max} = 1.5$ m:

$$
V_{Cylindrical} = \pi \times (1.2)^2 \times 1.5 = \pi \times 1.44 \times 1.5 \approx 6.79 \text{ m}^3
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Work Envelope of a Spherical (Polar) Robot

A spherical robot uses two rotational joints (azimuth $\theta$ and elevation $\phi$) plus a prismatic radial extension $r$. The work envelope is a spherical sector with radius $R_{max}$ and an angular cone (typically $\phi \in [0, \pi/2]$ for a hemisphere).

Step 1 — Differential volume in spherical coordinates:

$$
dV = r^2 \sin\phi \, dr \, d\phi \, d\theta
$$

Step 2 — Integrate:

$$
V_{Spherical} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \int_{0}^{R_{max}} r^2 \sin\phi \, dr \, d\phi \, d\theta
$$

Step 3 — Radial integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{R_{max}} r^2 \, dr = \frac{R_{max}^3}{3}
$$

Step 4 — Polar integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \sin\phi \, d\phi = \left[ -\cos\phi \right]_{0}^{\pi/2} = 1
$$

Step 5 — Azimuthal integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi
$$

Step 6 — Combine:

$$
V_{Spherical, hemisphere} = 2\pi \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{R_{max}^3}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \pi R_{max}^3
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is exactly **half** the volume of a full sphere of radius $R_{max}$, which makes sense because the robot can only sweep the upper hemisphere (or lower) due to mechanical limits.

**Example:** For $R_{max} = 2$ m:

$$
V_{Spherical, hemisphere} = \frac{2}{3} \pi (2)^3 = \frac{16\pi}{3} \approx 16.76 \text{ m}^3
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Cycle Time of a SCARA Pick-and-Place Operation

The cycle time of a SCARA robot is the sum of:
- Acceleration time $t_a$
- Constant-velocity cruise time $t_c$
- Deceleration time $t_d$
- Pick-up dwell $t_{pick}$
- Place dwell $t_{place}$

For a trapezoidal velocity profile over a horizontal distance $d$ with peak velocity $v_{max}$ and acceleration $a$:

Step 1 — Distance covered during acceleration (and equal during deceleration):

$$
d_a = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2a}
$$

Step 2 — If $2 d_a \le d$, the profile is trapezoidal:

$$
t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a}, \quad t_d = \frac{v_{max}}{a}
$$

Step 3 — Cruise distance:

$$
d_c = d - 2 d_a
$$

Step 4 — Cruise time:

$$
t_c = \frac{d_c}{v_{max}}
$$

Step 5 — Total move time:

$$
t_{move} = t_a + t_c + t_d = \frac{2 v_{max}}{a} + \frac{d - v_{max}^2 / a}{v_{max}}
$$

Step 6 — Full cycle:

$$
T_c = t_{move} + t_{pick} + t_{place}
$$

**Example:** SCARA with $v_{max} = 2$ m/s, $a = 10$ m/s², $d = 0.4$ m, $t_{pick} = t_{place} = 0.05$ s.

Compute $d_a = (2^2)/(2 \times 10) = 0.2$ m.
Since $2 d_a = 0.4$ m equals the total distance, the profile is **triangular** (no cruise phase).

$$
t_a = t_d = 2/10 = 0.2 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_c = 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

> **Throughput Calculation:** A robot with a 0.5-second cycle delivers **2 picks/second** or **7,200 picks/hour**. This is the metric KTU examiners love to ask for "high-speed packaging" applications.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Selection Decision

| Application | Best Robot Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 2-ton engine block transfer | Articulated (heavy-payload) | High payload + large reach |
| 30,000 chocolates/hour packaging | Delta robot | Sub-second cycle time |
| PCB component insertion | SCARA | Vertical assembly, high repeatability |
| Warehouse tote transport | AGV/AMR | Mobility, no fixed path needed |
| Glass panel handling (fragile) | Cartesian with soft gripper | Predictable linear motion |
| End-of-line case palletizing | Articulated (4-axis palletizer) | Pattern flexibility |
| Bag stacking (50 kg cement bags) | Heavy articulated | High payload, high reach |
| Forging press tending (hot parts) | Spherical | Reach into a confined press |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Material Handling Robot System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supervisory Controller PLC] --> B[Robot Controller]
    B --> C1[Servo Drive X Axis]
    B --> C2[Servo Drive Y Axis]
    B --> C3[Servo Drive Z Axis]
    B --> C4[Servo Drive Theta Axis]
    C1 --> D1[Prismatic Joint X]
    C2 --> D2[Prismatic Joint Y]
    C3 --> D3[Prismatic Joint Z]
    C4 --> D4[Revolute Joint Theta]
    D1 --> E[Mechanical Arm Structure]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Wrist Assembly]
    F --> G[End Effector Gripper]
    G --> H[Workpiece to be Moved]
    I1[Vision Sensor] --> B
    I2[Force Torque Sensor] --> B
    I3[Safety Scanner] --> B
    J[Conveyor or AGV Path] --> H
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Material Handling Robot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Selection] --> Q1{What is the payload?}
    Q1 -->|Below 5 kg| P1[SCARA or Delta]
    Q1 -->|5 to 100 kg| Q2{Is workspace rectangular?}
    Q1 -->|Above 100 kg| Q3{Is path fixed?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P2[Cartesian Gantry]
    Q2 -->|No| P3[Articulated 6 Axis]
    Q3 -->|Yes| P4[Heavy Articulated Palletizer]
    Q3 -->|No| P5[Mobile Robot AGV or AMR]
    P1 --> End[Final Selection]
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End
    P5 --> End
```

```mermaid
        F2 --> F5[Lower Arm Parallelogram]

        F5 --> F6[End Effector Platform]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three classification criteria for material handling robots used in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**
Material handling robots are classified based on:
1. **Mechanical configuration / Joint architecture** — Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, or Delta. Each is defined by its joint sequence (PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR, etc.).
2. **Mobility / Mounting** — Stationary (fixed-base) manipulators vs. Mobile (AGV/AMR) units.
3. **Application function** — Pick-and-place, Palletizing/Depalletizing, Machine tending, Bulk transport.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between an AGV and an AMR with respect to navigation and flexibility.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) | AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) |
|---|---|---|
| **Navigation** | Follows a fixed path (magnetic tape, QR code, rail) | Uses SLAM, LiDAR, and onboard maps; no fixed infrastructure |
| **Flexibility** | Low — path change requires physical re-installation | High — software-defined path changes |
| **Cost** | Lower initial cost, higher infra cost | Higher robot cost, minimal infra cost |
| **Obstacle Handling** | Stops or alarms | Dynamically re-plans route |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** *With neat joint-sequence diagrams, classify the major types of material handling robots and explain the work envelope of a Cartesian and a Spherical robot.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classification (3 marks):**

| Robot Type | Joint Sequence | Work Envelope |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (Gantry) | P, P, P | Rectangular cuboid |
| Cylindrical | R, P, P | Hollow cylinder |
| Spherical (Polar) | R, R, P | Spherical sector |
| Articulated | R, R, R, R, R, R | Irregular sphere |
| SCARA | R, R, P | Flat disc |
| Delta | Parallel | Shallow dome |

**Step 2 — Cartesian Work Envelope Derivation (2 marks):**

A Cartesian robot moves along three mutually perpendicular axes (X, Y, Z), each with a maximum travel of $X_{max}$, $Y_{max}$, $Z_{max}$. The total linear extent along each axis is twice the travel. Hence:

$$
V_{Cartesian} = 2X_{max} \times 2Y_{max} \times 2Z_{max} = 8 \cdot X_{max} \cdot Y_{max} \cdot Z_{max}
$$

**Step 3 — Spherical Work Envelope Derivation (2 marks):**

A spherical robot uses two rotational joints (azimuth and elevation) and one prismatic radial joint. The volume of the hemispherical envelope of radius $R_{max}$ is:

$$
V_{Spherical,hemisphere} = \frac{2}{3} \pi R_{max}^3
$$

This is half the volume of a full sphere, reflecting the limited upper/lower hemisphere reach of typical industrial polar robots.

---

**(b)** *A 6-DOF articulated robot is required to palletize 25 kg cement bags. Justify the selection and compute the volumetric work envelope given reach $R = 3$ m and joint limits covering a hemisphere.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Justification (4 marks):**

1. **Payload margin:** 25 kg is well within the typical 100–1000 kg capacity of a heavy-payload articulated robot (e.g., FANUC M-410iB), giving a 4× safety margin against dynamic loads during bag placement.
2. **Reach flexibility:** Cement bags come off a conveyor and are stacked in patterns (column, pin-wheel, interlock). An articulated 6-DOF arm can rotate its wrist to orient the bag for any stacking pattern.
3. **Reach into the pallet:** A 3 m reach covers standard Euro-pallets (1.2 × 0.8 m) and US-pallets (1.2 × 1.0 m) at full stacking height (1.8 m).
4. **End-effector compatibility:** Vacuum or mechanical bag grippers mount easily on the robot's wrist.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Work Envelope (3 marks):**

Assuming the robot covers a full hemisphere of radius $R = 3$ m:

$$
V = \frac{2}{3} \pi R^3 = \frac{2}{3} \pi (3)^3 = \frac{2}{3} \pi (27) = 18\pi \approx 56.55 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Throughput (bonus, 1 mark):**
At a 5-second cycle (typical for heavy bag palletizing), the throughput is $3600/5 = 720$ bags/hour.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]*

**(a)** *Compare SCARA, Delta, and Cartesian robots for a high-speed electronic component pick-and-place application. Use a structured table covering joint type, work envelope, cycle time, payload, and accuracy.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | SCARA | Delta | Cartesian |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joint Sequence | R, R, P | Parallel | P, P, P |
| Work Envelope | Flat disc | Shallow dome | Rectangular cuboid |
| Typical Cycle Time | 0.3–0.5 s | 0.05–0.15 s | 0.5–1.0 s |
| Payload | 1–20 kg | 0.5–8 kg | 5–500 kg |
| Repeatability | $\pm 0.01$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ mm |
| Best for | Vertical assembly | Ultra-fast pick | Heavy part transfer |

**Best Selection:** A **Delta robot** is the optimal choice for **high-speed** pick-and-place (cycle times < 0.1 s), as the parallel kinematics drastically reduce moving mass. A **SCARA** is preferred if **vertical insertion** (e.g., through-hole components) is required. A **Cartesian** robot is unsuitable because of slower cycle time for this size of components.

---

**(b)** *A SCARA robot has a horizontal reach of 800 mm and must execute a 400 mm pick-place motion. With peak joint velocity $\dot{\theta}_{max} = 4$ rad/s and effective link length $L = 0.3$ m, compute the linear peak velocity and the theoretical minimum cycle time. Assume a triangular velocity profile.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Linear Peak Velocity (3 marks):**
For a SCARA robot, the linear end-effector velocity $v$ is related to the joint angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ and the link length $L$ by:

$$
v = L \cdot \dot{\theta}
$$

$$
v_{max} = 0.3 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ rad/s} = 1.2 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Triangular Profile Time (2 marks):**
For a triangular velocity profile (acceleration = deceleration) over distance $d$:

$$
d = v_{max} \cdot t_{total}
$$

Solving for $t_{total}$:

$$
t_{total} = \frac{d}{v_{max}} = \frac{0.4 \text{ m}}{1.2 \text{ m/s}} = 0.333 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration Phase Time (1 mark):**

$$
t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a}, \text{ where } a = \frac{v_{max}^2}{d/2} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{0.2} = 7.2 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
t_a = \frac{1.2}{7.2} = 0.167 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Minimum Cycle Time (1 mark):**
Adding 100 ms of pickup + place dwell:

$$
T_c = 0.333 + 0.10 = 0.433 \text{ s}
$$

Throughput: $\approx 8{,}300$ picks/hour.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "reach" with "work envelope"** — A robot with a 3 m reach does NOT have a $3$ m × $3$ m × $3$ m work envelope. The work envelope is a 3D volume defined by joint limits. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Wrong joint sequence notation** — Always write the sequence **Base → Wrist**, not in reverse. SCARA is `[R, R, P]`, NOT `[P, R, R]`.
> 3. **Confusing AGV and AMR** — Many students write "AGV and AMR are the same." They are NOT. AGV follows fixed paths; AMR uses SLAM. (Lose 2 marks on differentiation questions)
> 4. **Forgetting units in cycle time** — Always include "s" or "s/cycle" in cycle time answers.
> 5. **Mixing up payload with reach** — A robot can have high payload but small reach (SCARA) or large reach but low payload (Delta). The relationship is inverse in most designs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Frequency KTU 2024 Hits:**

- **Joint Notation:** P (Prismatic, linear), R (Revolute, rotational). Sequence is **Base → Wrist**.
- **Cartesian [PPP]:** Rectangular cuboid envelope; $V = 8 X_{max} Y_{max} Z_{max}$; best for heavy loads and large gantries.
- **Cylindrical [RPP]:** Hollow cylinder; $V = \pi R_{max}^2 Z_{max}$; rare in modern greenfield plants.
- **Spherical [RRP]:** Hemisphere sector; $V = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R_{max}^3$; classic (Unimate) geometry.
- **Articulated [RRR...]:** Universal, dexterous; 6-DOF standard; non-linear coupled kinematics.
- **SCARA [RRP]:** Flat disc workspace; **fastest vertical assembly** robot; poor overhead reach.
- **Delta [Parallel]:** Lowest moving mass → **fastest cycle**; shallow dome envelope; small payload.
- **AGV vs. AMR:** AGV → fixed path (tape/QR); AMR → SLAM, no infrastructure.
- **Palletizer:** A specialized high-payload articulated robot (4-axis) for stacking.
- **Key formulas to remember cold:**
  - $V_{Cartesian} = 8 X_{max} Y_{max} Z_{max}$
  - $V_{Cylindrical} = \pi R_{max}^2 Z_{max}$
  - $V_{Spherical} = \tfrac{2}{3} \pi R_{max}^3$
  - $T_c = \text{move time} + t_{pick} + t_{place}$
  - $v = L \cdot \dot{\theta}$ (link-tip linear velocity)
- **Selection Rule of Thumb:** SCARA → assembly; Delta → fast pick; Cartesian → heavy+gantry; Articulated → universal; AGV → transport.
- **Kutzbach Criterion:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - \cdots$ — for DOF verification.
- **Always include units, joint sequence, and a labeled diagram** in any 7-or-14 mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Trucks, Monorails, and Rail Guided Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Trucks, Monorails, and Rail Guided Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Industrial Trucks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Trucks** are wheeled, manually-propelled or powered vehicles used for the short-distance horizontal transportation, lifting, stacking, and order-picking of unit loads within a manufacturing plant, warehouse, or distribution centre. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, they are classified as a primary category of **intermittent-flow material handling equipment (MHE)** under the broader umbrella of *unit-load handling systems*.

Industrial trucks differ from continuous conveyors in that the load is moved in **discrete batches (intermittent motion)** rather than in a steady stream. They form the most flexible layer of an internal logistics network because they require **no fixed path** — the operator (or controller) can re-route them as production schedules change.

### 1.2 Monorails — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monorails (Monorail Hoist Systems / Overhead Monorails)** are material-handling systems in which a carrier or hoist travels along a single elevated rail (the *running rail* or *track beam*) suspended from the building structure. The load is suspended *below* or *beside* the rail, and the system can follow curves, switches, and elevation changes — making it a hybrid between an overhead crane and a fixed-path conveyor.

### 1.3 Rail Guided Vehicles (RGV) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rail Guided Vehicles (RGVs)** are driverless or driver-operated, self-propelled carriers whose travel path is constrained to a network of fixed rails laid on (or recessed into) the factory floor. They are a class of **Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV)** where the guidance mechanism is **mechanical (rail-following)** rather than magnetic-tape, laser, or wire-based. RGVs offer superior **positional accuracy** (±5 mm typical) and **load capacity** (up to **50 tonnes** in heavy industry).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy for Students:**
>
> - **Industrial Trucks** → Think of the *forklift you see in a hardware store*. Imagine a busy Kerala spice warehouse where workers move sacks from receiving to storage — the forklift is the workhorse that handles each pallet individually, like a porter carrying one suitcase at a time through a railway station.
> - **Monorails** → Picture the *Kochi Metro elevated track*. The monorail is essentially a "metro train" but for goods — instead of carrying passengers, it carries car bodies from one workstation to the next in an automobile assembly plant.
> - **Rail Guided Vehicles** → Visualise a *toy train set running on metal rails*. An RGV is the industrial-strength version — it cannot leave the rails, but because of that constraint it can carry enormous loads with pinpoint accuracy, perfect for moving engine blocks in a machine shop.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> Standard industrial parameters (memorise these for KTU exams):
>
> - **Forklift lifting capacity**: 1 tonne — 45 tonnes
> - **RGV positioning accuracy**: ±5 mm to ±10 mm
> - **Monorail rail gauge**: standard 200 mm, 250 mm, 320 mm I-beam profiles
> - **Forklift standard lift height**: 3 m — 7 m (Class I & II)
> - **RGV travel speed**: 30 m/min — 240 m/min
> - **Counterweight forklift stability angle**: typically **≥ 4°** rearward, **≥ 6°** forward

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Industrial Trucks

Industrial trucks are classified by **power source**, **operator position**, and **lifting mechanism**. The KTU syllabus follows the **Industrial Truck Association (ITA)** classification:

#### 2.1.1 By Power Source

- **Manual (Hand) Trucks** — Human-powered; e.g., pallet jack, hand truck, dolly.
- **Powered Industrial Trucks** — Electric, diesel, LPG, or CNG driven.

#### 2.1.2 By Operator Configuration (ITA Class System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield: ITA Classification Table**
>
> | ITA Class | Type | Operator Position | Typical Application |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Class I** | Electric Counterbalanced | Rider (stand-on/sit-on) | Warehouses, indoor use |
> | **Class II** | Electric Narrow-Aisle (Reach Truck) | Stand-up | Narrow-aisle racking |
> | **Class III** | Electric Hand-Rider (Walkie / Pallet Jack) | Walk-behind | Loading docks |
> | **Class IV** | Internal Combustion (IC) Cushion-Tire | Rider (sit-down) | Smooth indoor floors |
> | **Class V** | Internal Combustion (IC) Pneumatic-Tire | Rider (sit-down) | Outdoor / rough terrain |
> | **Class VI** | Electric Tractors (Tugger) | Rider | Towing multiple trailers |
> | **Class VII** | Rough-Terrain Telehandler | Rider (enclosed cab) | Construction sites |

#### 2.1.3 By Mechanical Function

- **Counterbalanced Trucks** — Load balanced by a rear counterweight; forks protrude from the front.
- **Straddle Trucks** — Outrigger arms straddle the load; legs fit between pallet openings.
- **Reach Trucks** — Forks can *reach forward* into racking; chassis has a retractable mast.
- **Order Pickers** — Operator platform elevates with the load for manual picking at height.
- **Side-Loaders** — Forks approach load from the side; useful for long loads (lumber, pipes).

### 2.2 Forklift Stability — The Stability Triangle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why stability matters (the "Why"):** A forklift is essentially a *class-1 lever* pivoting on its front wheels. When the load is raised, the centre of gravity shifts forward and upward, creating an overturning moment. Counterbalanced trucks rely on the **Stability Triangle** — the imaginary triangle formed by the two front wheels and the centre of the rear axle.

**The Three Stability Conditions:**

1. **Longitudinal Stability (Forward Tipping)** — The combined CG of truck + load must remain inside the front axle line.
2. **Lateral Stability (Sideways Tipping)** — The combined CG must remain inside the line connecting the two front-wheel contact patches.
3. **Vertical Stability (Load Slip)** — The load's CG must remain inside the wheelbase polygon to prevent sliding off the forks.

### 2.3 Monorail Systems — Structural Theory

A monorail system consists of:

- **Track (Running Rail):** Typically a hot-rolled steel I-beam or patented track section.
- **Trolley / Carriage:** Houses the wheels and drive mechanism; rides along the lower flange of the I-beam.
- **Hoist:** Chain or wire-rope hoist mounted on the trolley for vertical lifting.
- **End Trucks & Curves:** Allow direction changes; switches divert the trolley between track sections.
- **Power Supply:** Conductor bars (festoon system), cable reel, or sliding contact.

### 2.4 Rail Guided Vehicles — Operating Principle

RGVs are classified by **power pick-up method**:

- **Sliding Contact (Conductor Bar):** Power and control signals via copper bars along the track.
- **Inductive Power Transfer (IPT):** Wireless power via coils embedded in the track — modern, maintenance-free.
- **On-board Battery (Battery Rail Vehicle / BRV):** Recharged at stations; clean and flexible.

**Why RGVs over free-navigating AGVs?**

> [!NOTE]
> RGVs are preferred when: (i) extremely heavy loads are moved (>10 tonnes), (ii) very high positional accuracy is required (e.g., CNC load/unload), (iii) the path rarely changes, (iv) harsh environments preclude floor-mounted tape or lasers.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield. Memorise with units!**

**Table 2.1 — Core Engineering Formulas**

> **Note on absolute values**: To preserve markdown table integrity, all absolute-value bars are rendered as `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of the ASCII pipe symbol.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Forward Tip-Over Moment** | $M_{tip} = W_{load} \cdot d_{load}$ | $W_{load}$ = load weight (N), $d_{load}$ = horizontal distance from front axle to load CG (m) | $M_{tip} < M_{stab}$ |
| 2 | **Restoring (Stabilising) Moment** | $M_{stab} = W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}$ | $W_{truck}$ = empty truck weight (N), $d_{truck}$ = wheelbase CG distance from front axle (m) | Always positive |
| 3 | **Stability Factor (SF)** | $SF = \dfrac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip}} = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}}{W_{load} \cdot d_{load}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $SF \geq 1.5$ (industry standard) |
| 4 | **Maximum Load at Lift Height h** | $W_{load,max}(h) = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}}{d_{load}(h)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{SF_{min}}$ | $d_{load}(h)$ increases as mast is raised | Decreases with height |
| 5 | **Forklift Travel Speed (Loaded)** | $v_{L} = v_{max} \cdot \left(1 - k \cdot \dfrac{W_{load}}{W_{rated}}\right)$ | $v_{max}$ = no-load speed (m/s), $k$ = derating constant (typically 0.3) | $W_{load} \leq W_{rated}$ |
| 6 | **RGV Cycle Time** | $T_c = T_{travel} + T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{dwell}$ | All terms in seconds (s) | Sum of four operations |
| 7 | **RGV Throughput** | $\theta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \quad [\text{payloads/hour}]$ | $T_c$ in seconds | $T_c > 0$ |
| 8 | **Monorail Trolley Drive Power** | $P = \dfrac{W_{total} \cdot v_{travel}}{\eta_{drive}} \cdot f_{roll}$ | $W_{total}$ = trolley + load (N), $v_{travel}$ (m/s), $\eta_{drive}$ = drivetrain efficiency, $f_{roll}$ = rolling resistance coefficient | $f_{roll} \approx 0.015$ (steel-on-steel) |
| 9 | **RGV Acceleration Force** | $F_{acc} = m \cdot a + W_{total} \cdot f_{roll}$ | $m$ = vehicle mass (kg), $a$ = acceleration (m/s²) | $a \leq 0.5$ m/s² for safety |
| 10 | **Switching Time (Monorail)** | $T_{switch} = T_{actuate} + T_{verify}$ | $T_{actuate} \approx 1.5$ s, $T_{verify} \approx 0.5$ s | Mechanical lock required |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly** (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki plants in Pune) — Monorail systems carry car bodies on overhead conveyors through paint shops, providing floor-space savings of **~40 %** vs floor conveyors.
- **CNC Machining Cells** — RGVs load/unload CNC centres with ±5 mm accuracy, enabling **lights-out manufacturing**.
- **Port Logistics (Cochin Port, Vallarpadam ICTT)** — Reach trucks and counterbalanced forklifts handle container stuffing/de-stuffing with throughputs of **30+ pallets/hour**.
- **Cold Storage Warehouses** — Class I electric trucks operate at −25 °C without emissions, critical for frozen-food logistics in Kerala's seafood export industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forklift Stability Factor at Maximum Lift Height

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** The KTU examiner loves stability problems because they test (i) understanding of the *lever principle*, (ii) moment balance, and (iii) recognition that lift height *changes the geometry*.

**Given Scenario:**

A Class I electric counterbalanced forklift has the following specifications:

- Empty truck weight: $W_{truck} = 4500$ kgf (kilogram-force, used by Indian manufacturers)
- Wheelbase CG distance from front axle: $d_{truck} = 0.80$ m
- Rated load at standard lift: $W_{rated} = 2000$ kgf at $d_{load,0} = 0.60$ m from front axle
- When mast is raised to $h = 4.0$ m, the load CG shifts forward to $d_{load,h} = 0.85$ m

**Find:** The minimum required Stability Factor (SF) and comment on safety.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the load geometry change.
The horizontal moment arm $d_{load}$ increases from $0.60$ m (lowered) to $0.85$ m (raised) because the mast tilts forward as it elevates — a deliberate design feature for stability, but it requires engineering compensation.

$$
d_{load}(h=4.0\ \text{m}) = 0.85\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2:** Write the stability moment equation.
The stabilising moment is generated by the truck's own weight acting rearward of the front axle:

$$
M_{stab} = W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
M_{stab} = 4500\ \text{kgf} \times 0.80\ \text{m} = 3600\ \text{kgf·m}
$$

**Step 3:** Write the tip-over moment equation.
The tip-over moment is generated by the load acting forward of the front axle:

$$
M_{tip} = W_{load} \cdot d_{load}(h)
$$

For the **rated load at full lift height**:

$$
M_{tip} = 2000\ \text{kgf} \times 0.85\ \text{m} = 1700\ \text{kgf·m}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Stability Factor.

$$
SF = \dfrac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip}} = \dfrac{3600\ \text{kgf·m}}{1700\ \text{kgf·m}} = 2.117
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the industry safety standard.

$$
SF_{computed} = 2.117 \geq SF_{min} = 1.5 \quad \checkmark
$$

The forklift is **safe** at the rated load and maximum lift height, with a safety margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = \dfrac{2.117 - 1.5}{1.5} \times 100\% = 41.1\%
$$

**Step 6 (Extension):** Find the *maximum allowable load* if SF must remain ≥ 1.5 at the raised height.

$$
W_{load,max}(h=4.0\ \text{m}) = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}}{SF_{min} \cdot d_{load}(h)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
W_{load,max}(4.0\ \text{m}) = \dfrac{4500 \times 0.80}{1.5 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{3600}{1.275} = 2823.5\ \text{kgf}
$$

**Conclusion:** The truck can actually carry **2823.5 kgf** at 4 m lift height while maintaining SF = 1.5, but the manufacturer's **rated capacity is 2000 kgf** — the manufacturer has built in an additional **41 % safety margin** beyond the legal minimum. Always defer to the nameplate rating.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — RGV Throughput Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Why derive this?** Throughput is the *primary Key Performance Indicator (KPI)* for any RGV installation. KTU examiners frequently test this with 3-mark short problems.

**Given Scenario:**
An RGV operates in a manufacturing cell with the following cycle:

- Travel time between stations: $T_{travel} = 18$ s
- Load pick-up time: $T_{load} = 6$ s
- Load drop-off time: $T_{unload} = 5$ s
- Dwell time at the unload station: $T_{dwell} = 4$ s

**Find:** Cycle time and hourly throughput.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Write the cycle-time equation.

$$
T_c = T_{travel} + T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{dwell}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
T_c = 18 + 6 + 5 + 4 = 33\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert cycle time to hourly throughput.

$$
\theta = \dfrac{3600\ \text{s/hour}}{T_c\ \text{[s]}} = \dfrac{3600}{33}
$$

$$
\theta = 109.09 \approx 109\ \text{payloads/hour}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification using dimensional analysis.

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1\ \text{payload}}{33\ \text{s}} \times \dfrac{3600\ \text{s}}{1\ \text{hour}} = 109.09\ \text{payloads/hour} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Monorail Trolley Drive Power

**Given Scenario:**
A monorail trolley weighing $200$ kg carries a $1500$ kg load. The travel speed is $0.5$ m/s. Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{drive} = 0.85$ and rolling resistance coefficient $f_{roll} = 0.015$.

**Find:** The required drive motor power.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Total weight on the trolley.

$$
W_{total} = (m_{trolley} + m_{load}) \cdot g = (200 + 1500) \times 9.81 = 1700 \times 9.81
$$

$$
W_{total} = 16{,}677\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 2:** Resistance force opposing motion.

$$
F_{roll} = W_{total} \cdot f_{roll} = 16{,}677 \times 0.015 = 250.16\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3:** Power required at the wheels.

$$
P_{wheels} = F_{roll} \cdot v_{travel} = 250.16 \times 0.5 = 125.08\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 4:** Account for drivetrain losses (motor must deliver more than wheels require).

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{\eta_{drive}} = \dfrac{125.08}{0.85}
$$

$$
P_{motor} = 147.15\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply engineering safety factor (1.5× for motor selection).

$$
P_{selected} = 1.5 \times 147.15 = 220.7\ \text{W} \rightarrow \text{select a } 250\ \text{W motor}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Truck Selection Decision Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Decision Matrix — Industrial Truck Selection (must appear in design problems)**

| Selection Criterion | Recommended Truck Type | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Load $\leq$ 1.5 t, indoor smooth floor | **Class III Walkie Pallet Jack** | Lowest cost, simplest operation |
| Load 1.5–3 t, indoor warehouse | **Class I Electric Counterbalanced** | Zero emissions, quiet, low maintenance |
| Load 1.5–3 t, narrow aisles (<2.5 m) | **Class II Reach Truck** | Retractable mast saves aisle width |
| Load 1.5–3 t, outdoor yard | **Class V IC Pneumatic** | Robust tires handle rough surfaces |
| Load > 10 t, fixed path, high accuracy | **RGV (Rail Guided Vehicle)** | Mechanical guidance, ±5 mm accuracy |
| Variable path, long-distance, ergonomic | **Monorail / Overhead Hoist** | Frees floor space, automatic indexing |
| Picking individual SKUs at 4+ m height | **Order Picker (Class II variant)** | Operator elevates with platform |
| Towing 4–6 trolleys in assembly line | **Class VI Electric Tugger** | Single prime-mover for many trailers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Industrial Truck Classification Tree

The diagram below maps the complete ITA classification hierarchy from a *Logical-Functional* perspective (per KTU adaptation rule for non-physical schematics).

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Industrial Trucks]:::root --> B1[By Power Source]
    rootA --> B2[By Operator Position]
    rootA --> B3[By Mechanical Function]

    B1 --> C1A[Manual Hand Trucks]
    B1 --> C1B[Powered Industrial Trucks]

    C1A --> D1A[Pallet Jack]
    C1A --> D1B[Hand Truck Dolly]
    C1A --> D1C[Non-Powered Stacker]

    C1B --> D2A[Electric Drive]
    C1B --> D2B[Internal Combustion]
    C1B --> D2C[Hybrid]

    D2A --> E1A[Battery Electric]
    D2A --> E1B[Fuel Cell Electric]

    D2B --> E2A[Diesel Engine]
    D2B --> E2B[LPG CNG Engine]

    B2 --> F1[Class I Rider Counterbalanced]
    B2 --> F2[Class II Narrow Aisle]
    B2 --> F3[Class III Walkie Rider]
    B2 --> F4[Class IV IC Cushion Tire]
    B2 --> F5[Class V IC Pneumatic Tire]
    B2 --> F6[Class VI Electric Tractor]
    B2 --> F7[Class VII Rough Terrain]

    B3 --> G1[Counterbalanced Type]
    B3 --> G2[Straddle Type]
    B3 --> G3[Reach Type]
    B3 --> G4[Order Picker Type]
    B3 --> G5[Side Loader Type]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Forklift Stability Triangle (Functional Force-Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the *force-flow architecture* of the stability triangle rather than a literal geometric drawing. It shows how the stabilising moment $M_{stab}$ and the tip-over moment $M_{tip}$ interact at the front axle pivot.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Load Weight W_load]:::load --> nodeB[M_tip = W_load times d_load]
    nodeC[Truck Weight W_truck]:::truck --> nodeD[M_stab = W_truck times d_truck]
    nodeB --> nodeE{Pivot Point: Front Axle}
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{SF = M_stab divide M_tip}
    nodeF --> nodeG[SF greater than or equal 1.5: SAFE]
    nodeF --> nodeH[SF less than 1.5: TIP OVER HAZARD]

    classDef load fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef truck fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Monorail System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    m1[Building Structure Steel Beam]:::struct --> m2[Track Rail I-Beam 200mm]
    m2 --> m3[Trolley Carriage with Wheels]
    m3 --> m4[Hoist Mechanism Chain or Wire Rope]
    m4 --> m5[Load Hook and Sling]
    m2 --> m6[Track Switches and Curves]
    m2 --> m7[Power Supply Conductor Bar]
    m7 --> m8[Drive Motor on Trolley]
    m8 --> m3

    classDef struct fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — RGV Closed-Loop Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    r1[Load Station A] --> r2[RGV on Rail Track]
    r2 --> r3[Conveyor Pickup Interface]
    r3 --> r4[RGV Vehicle with Battery]
    r4 --> r5[Travel along Fixed Rail]
    r5 --> r6[Conveyor Dropoff Interface]
    r6 --> r7[Load Station B]
    r4 --> r8[Central PLC Controller]
    r8 --> r9[RFID Tags along Track]
    r9 --> r4

    classDef load fill:#1565c0,stroke:#000,color:#fff

```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Material-Handling Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Start: Material Handling Requirement] --> s2{Is path fixed?}
    s2 -- YES --> s3{Is accuracy critical plus or minus 10mm?}
    s2 -- NO --> s4{Is aisle less than 2.5m?}
    s3 -- YES --> s5[Choose RGV]
    s3 -- NO --> s6[Choose Monorail Conveyor]
    s4 -- YES --> s7[Choose Reach Truck Class II]
    s4 -- NO --> s8{Is load more than 10 tonnes?}
    s8 -- YES --> s9[Choose Heavy Forklift Class VII]
    s8 -- NO --> s10[Choose Counterbalanced Forklift Class I]
```
