## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define an Industrial Truck. List the ITA classification of powered industrial trucks with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An industrial truck is a wheeled, self-propelled or manually-propelled vehicle used for the horizontal transport, lifting, and stacking of unit loads in industrial settings, characterised by intermittent (batch) flow operation rather than continuous motion.

> **Classification (2 marks):** The Industrial Truck Association (ITA) classifies powered industrial trucks into seven classes:
>
> - **Class I** — Electric Counterbalanced Rider Truck (e.g., Toyota 8FBE)
> - **Class II** — Electric Narrow-Aisle Reach Truck (e.g., Crown RR 5700)
> - **Class III** — Electric Hand-Rider / Walkie (e.g., pallet jack)
> - **Class IV** — IC Engine Cushion-Tire (e.g., Caterpillar 5,000 lb LP)
> - **Class V** — IC Engine Pneumatic-Tire (e.g., Hyster H80FT)
> - **Class VI** — Electric Tractor / Tugger (e.g., Toyota 8TB50)
> - **Class VII** — Rough-Terrain Telehandler (e.g., JCB 540-170)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. Differentiate between a Monorail and a Rail Guided Vehicle (RGV) in terms of (i) load suspension, (ii) guidance mechanism, and (iii) typical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each criterion):**

> | Criterion | Monorail | Rail Guided Vehicle (RGV) |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **Load suspension** | Load hangs *below* the elevated rail (overhead) | Load is carried *on top* of the floor-level rail vehicle |
> | **Guidance mechanism** | Mechanical rolling on a single I-beam flange | Mechanical rolling on twin rails (or recessed channel) |
> | **Typical application** | Car-body transport in auto assembly, paint-shop conveyors | Heavy-payload CNC load/unload, steel-mill coil transport, port intermodal transfer |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks, Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Forklift Stability Analysis

**(a)** Explain the **Stability Triangle** concept for a counterbalanced forklift truck with a neat sketch. Discuss the three stability conditions the truck must satisfy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class I electric counterbalanced forklift has the following data:

- Empty truck weight $W_{truck} = 5000$ kgf
- Wheelbase distance from front axle to truck CG: $d_{truck} = 0.90$ m
- Load weight $W_{load} = 2500$ kgf
- Load CG distance from front axle at lowered position: $d_{load,0} = 0.55$ m
- Load CG distance at raised position $h = 4.5$ m: $d_{load,h} = 0.80$ m

Compute: (i) the **Stability Factor at the lowered position**, (ii) the **Stability Factor at the raised position**, and (iii) the **maximum permissible load** at the raised position if $SF_{min} = 1.5$. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Sketch description (2 marks):** The Stability Triangle is formed by the two front-wheel contact points (A, B) and the centre of the rear axle (C). The combined CG of (truck + load) must lie *inside* this triangle at all times.

**Three stability conditions (5 marks, ~1.5 each):**

1. **Longitudinal Forward Stability** — The truck must not tip forward about the front axle line. Condition: $M_{stab} \geq SF_{min} \cdot M_{tip}$, i.e.,

$$
W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck} \geq SF_{min} \cdot W_{load} \cdot d_{load}
$$

2. **Lateral Stability** — The truck must not tip sideways about the line connecting the two front-wheel contact patches. This is governed by the truck width $b$, and the lateral CG offset must satisfy $\vert x_{CG} \vert \leq b/2$.

3. **Vertical / Load-Slip Stability** — The load's CG must lie within the wheelbase polygon so that the load does not slide off the forks during acceleration, braking, or mast tilting.

**Additional factors affecting stability (mention for full marks):** travel speed, braking deceleration, mast tilt angle (forward/back), uneven floor, dynamic effects while turning.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) SF at lowered position:**

Step 1 — Stabilising moment.

$$
M_{stab} = W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck} = 5000 \times 0.90 = 4500\ \text{kgf·m}
$$

Step 2 — Tip-over moment.

$$
M_{tip,0} = W_{load} \cdot d_{load,0} = 2500 \times 0.55 = 1375\ \text{kgf·m}
$$

Step 3 — Stability Factor.

$$
SF_0 = \dfrac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip,0}} = \dfrac{4500}{1375} = 3.273
$$

**(ii) SF at raised position:**

Step 4 — Tip-over moment at height.

$$
M_{tip,h} = W_{load} \cdot d_{load,h} = 2500 \times 0.80 = 2000\ \text{kgf·m}
$$

Step 5 — Stability Factor at height.

$$
SF_h = \dfrac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip,h}} = \dfrac{4500}{2000} = 2.25
$$

**(iii) Maximum permissible load at raised position for $SF_{min} = 1.5$:**

Step 6 — Rearrange the SF equation.

$$
W_{load,max}(h) = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}}{SF_{min} \cdot d_{load}(h)}
$$

Step 7 — Substitute.

$$
W_{load,max}(4.5\ \text{m}) = \dfrac{5000 \times 0.90}{1.5 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{4500}{1.20} = 3750\ \text{kgf}
$$

**Final tabulated result:**

> | Quantity | Value |
> | :--- | :--- |
> | $SF_0$ (lowered) | **3.27** (safe, $>> 1.5$) |
> | $SF_h$ (raised at 4.5 m) | **2.25** (safe, $> 1.5$) |
> | $W_{load,max}$ (raised) | **3750 kgf** |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The forklift's nameplate rating of 2500 kgf at 4.5 m lift provides a safety margin of $\dfrac{3750 - 2500}{3750} \times 100\% = 33.3\%$ over the legal minimum.

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — RGV and Monorail System Design

**(a)** With a functional block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Rail Guided Vehicle (RGV) system** for a flexible manufacturing system (FMS). List four advantages of RGVs over free-navigating AGVs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RGV serves three CNC machining centres arranged along a 60-metre straight rail track. The vehicle travels at $90$ m/min, loading takes $8$ s, unloading takes $6$ s, and the dwell time at each station is $3$ s. Calculate: (i) the **cycle time** to serve one CNC centre and return, and (ii) the **hourly throughput** in loads/hour. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**RGV Architecture (4 marks):**
The RGV system consists of:

1. **Track Subsystem** — Twin steel rails embedded in or laid on the floor; switches where branch lines exist.
2. **Vehicle Subsystem** — Powered bogies with rubber or steel wheels, on-board battery, drive motor, encoder, RFID reader.
3. **Load-Handling Subsystem** — Roller conveyor, chain conveyor, or lift-table mounted on the vehicle.
4. **Control Subsystem** — Central PLC coordinating vehicle dispatch, traffic management, and station handshakes.
5. **Communication Subsystem** — Wi-Fi or inductive link for command/feedback exchange.
6. **Safety Subsystem** — Bumpers, e-stops, light curtains, anti-collision sensors.

**Four advantages of RGVs over free-navigating AGVs (3 marks, 0.75 each):**

1. **Higher positional accuracy** (±5 mm vs ±25 mm for laser-guided AGVs) due to mechanical guidance.
2. **Higher load capacity** — up to 50 tonnes, vs typical AGV limit of ~5 tonnes.
3. **Lower susceptibility to floor contamination** — dust, oil, water do not obscure guidance (since there is no tape or laser path).
4. **Predictable cycle time** — fixed path allows deterministic scheduling in FMS.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given data:**
- Track length: $L = 60$ m
- Travel speed: $v = 90$ m/min $= 1.5$ m/s
- Load time: $T_{load} = 8$ s
- Unload time: $T_{unload} = 6$ s
- Dwell time: $T_{dwell} = 3$ s

**(i) Cycle time calculation:**

Step 1 — One-way travel time.

$$
T_{travel,1} = \dfrac{L}{v} = \dfrac{60\ \text{m}}{1.5\ \text{m/s}} = 40\ \text{s}
$$

Step 2 — Round-trip travel.

$$
T_{travel,RT} = 2 \times 40 = 80\ \text{s}
$$

Step 3 — Station operation time per cycle (one load + one unload + one dwell — the simplest cycle serving one CNC).

$$
T_{station} = T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{dwell} = 8 + 6 + 3 = 17\ \text{s}
$$

Step 4 — Total cycle time.

$$
T_c = T_{travel,RT} + T_{station} = 80 + 17 = 97\ \text{s}
$$

**(ii) Hourly throughput:**

Step 5 — Apply the throughput formula.

$$
\theta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{97} = 37.11\ \text{loads/hour}
$$

Step 6 — Verification.

$$
\theta = \dfrac{1\ \text{load}}{97\ \text{s}} \times \dfrac{3600\ \text{s}}{1\ \text{hr}} = 37.11\ \text{loads/hr} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:**
> - **Cycle Time:** $T_c = 97$ s
> - **Throughput:** $\theta \approx 37$ loads/hour

**Engineering interpretation (for full marks):** If the FMS demands **45 loads/hour**, a single RGV is insufficient. Either deploy **two parallel RGVs** (combined throughput 74/hr, but with collision-avoidance logic) or **increase travel speed** to $v = 120$ m/min to lower $T_c$ to ~75 s, raising $\theta$ to 48/hr.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (lose 1–2 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the height effect** — Students often compute SF at the lowered position but ignore the shifted CG at raised position. *Always compute SF at the actual lift height specified in the problem.*
> 2. **Mixing kgf with N** — Indian manufacturer datasheets use **kgf** (kilogram-force). Do *not* convert to Newtons unless the problem explicitly asks for SI; using kgf throughout gives correct dimensionless ratios for SF.
> 3. **RGV throughput — forgetting round-trip travel** — Many students compute only the *one-way* travel, missing the return leg. Read the question carefully: *"serve one CNC and return"* → multiply travel by 2.
> 4. **Monorail power calculation — forgetting efficiency** — Always divide output power by $\eta_{drive}$ to find motor input power; the *inverse* error is the most common.
> 5. **Classifying trucks** — Students confuse Class IV (cushion-tire, indoor) with Class V (pneumatic-tire, outdoor). Remember: **Cushion = indoor smooth floor; Pneumatic = outdoor rough**.
> 6. **Stability Triangle** — Drawing it with three corners on the *rear* axle is incorrect; the triangle is formed by the **two front wheels + rear-axle centre**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**
>
> ✅ **Industrial Trucks** are intermittent-flow unit-load handlers, classified by ITA into 7 classes (I–VII).
>
> ✅ **Class I** = Electric Counterbalanced Rider; **Class V** = IC Pneumatic-Tire (outdoor).
>
> ✅ **Stability Triangle** = imaginary triangle formed by front-wheel contact patches and rear-axle centre; combined CG must remain inside.
>
> ✅ **Three stability conditions**: Longitudinal (forward tip), Lateral (sideways tip), Vertical (load slip).
>
> ✅ **Stability Factor** $SF = \dfrac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip}} = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot d_{truck}}{W_{load} \cdot d_{load}}$; minimum industry standard $SF \geq 1.5$.
>
> ✅ **As lift height increases**, $d_{load}$ increases → $M_{tip}$ increases → $SF$ decreases → rated capacity drops (read nameplate carefully).
>
> ✅ **Monorails** are overhead, single-rail systems with the load suspended below; ideal for car-body assembly and paint shops.
>
> ✅ **Rail Guided Vehicles (RGVs)** are floor-level, twin-rail, automated carriers; deliver ±5 mm accuracy and up to 50-tonne payloads.
>
> ✅ **RGV Cycle Time** $T_c = T_{travel} + T_{load} + T_{unload} + T_{dwell}$.
>
> ✅ **RGV Throughput** $\theta = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ payloads/hour.
>
> ✅ **Monorail power** $P_{motor} = \dfrac{W_{total} \cdot v \cdot f_{roll}}{\eta_{drive}}$; always multiply result by 1.5 safety factor for motor selection.
>
> ✅ **AGV vs RGV**: AGVs use electronic guidance (laser, tape, wire); RGVs use mechanical guidance (rail) — RGVs win on accuracy and payload.
>
> ✅ **Reach trucks (Class II)** for narrow aisles (<2.5 m); **Counterbalanced (Class I)** for general warehouses.
>
> ✅ **Selection rule of thumb**: Fixed path + heavy load + high accuracy → RGV; Variable path + flexible routing → Monorail or AGV.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conveyor Systems, Cranes, and Hoists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conveyor Systems, Cranes, and Hoists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Material Handling Subsystems

In the context of **Robotic Assembly and Material Handling Systems (OERUT724)**, Module 4 transitions from robot-centric manipulation to the *auxiliary* equipment that feeds, positions, and elevates workpieces. **Conveyor Systems, Cranes, and Hoists** form the **bulk material handling triad** — the silent workhorses of every assembly line, warehouse, and robotic cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Material handling equipment in a robotic assembly environment refers to the integrated set of mechanical, electromechanical, and control systems used to transport, lift, lower, and position workpieces or sub-assemblies between workstations, with or without direct robot intervention.*

---

## 1.2 Conveyor Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conveyor System:** A continuous or semi-continuous mechanical handling apparatus that transports materials (unit loads or bulk loads) along a fixed path between two or more points using a belt, chain, roller, screw, or vibrating surface, driven by a prime mover.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Moving Sidewalk"
Think of a **conveyor system** as the *moving sidewalk* you see in a large airport terminal. Instead of passengers walking, the floor itself slides and carries them from one end to the other. In a factory, the "floor" is a **belt, chain, or rollers**, and the "passengers" are **workpieces, parts, or packaged goods**. The motor (hidden below) is the *prime mover* that keeps the sidewalk — and the production line — moving at a constant, predictable pace.

### Classification of Conveyors (KTU-relevant)

| Family | Load Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Belt Conveyors** | Unit + Bulk | Mining, assembly lines |
| **Roller Conveyors** | Unit (boxes, pallets) | Packaging, sorting |
| **Chain Conveyors** | Heavy unit loads | Automotive, paint shops |
| **Screw Conveyors** | Bulk granular | Cement, food, grain |
| **Pneumatic Conveyors** | Powder/granules | Pharma, plastics |
| **Vibratory Conveyors** | Small parts | Foundries, sorting |

---

## 1.3 Cranes — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crane:** A machine equipped with a **hoisting mechanism**, mounted on a movable or fixed structure, used for vertical lifting and horizontal transportation of loads between points not necessarily in a straight line.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Giant Robotic Arm of the Yard"
A **crane** is essentially a **super-sized robot arm** without the precision encoders. Imagine a giant's hand reaching over a shipyard to lift a shipping container off a truck and place it onto a ship. The boom is the "shoulder," the hoist is the "elbow+hand," and the trolley/bridge is the "wrist." Cranes trade *precision* for **massive lifting capacity (typically 1 tonne to 1000+ tonnes)**.

### Types of Cranes
- **Overhead (Bridge) Cranes** — used in steel plants, warehouses
- **Gantry Cranes** — container terminals, shipyards
- **Jib Cranes** — workstation feeding
- **Tower Cranes** — construction
- **Mobile (Hydraulic) Cranes** — versatile field operations
- **Floating Cranes** — port operations

---

## 1.4 Hoists — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hoist:** A mechanical device specifically designed to **lift and lower** a load vertically (or near-vertically) using a drum, chain, wire rope, or hydraulic cylinder. A hoist is the *lifting component* of a crane, but can also operate as a standalone unit.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Workshop Pulley"
Imagine a simple **pulley in a garage mechanic's workshop** — pull down on a chain, and the engine block rises into the air. A **hoist** is that same pulley, industrialized: a drum wound with **wire rope** or **load chain**, driven by an **electric, pneumatic, hydraulic, or manual** power source. Rated capacities commonly range from **0.25 tonne to 50 tonnes** for industrial units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metric Highlight:**
> Standard KTU-referenced design loads include the **SWL (Safe Working Load)** — the maximum load a hoist/crane may handle under normal service, typically with a **design factor of safety of 1.5 to 4.0** depending on code (IS 3177, IS 807, ASME B30).

---

## 1.5 Why These Three Together? (Robotic Cell Context)

In a **robotic assembly cell**, the robot itself rarely moves the workpiece across the *factory* — it operates within a **station**. The flow of parts **into** and **out of** the station is handled by:
- **Conveyors** → for *in-line, continuous* transport
- **Cranes** → for *heavy, infrequent* lifting (dies, fixtures, large sub-assemblies)
- **Hoists** → for *vertical load positioning* (e.g., loading a press, feeding a CNC chuck)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "CCH":** **C**onveyors (horizontal-continuous), **C**ranes (heavy + flexible path), **H**oists (vertical-only). This trio covers **all 3 axes of motion** in a material-handling coordinate frame.

---

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Design Metrics

The following **standard reference values** are commonly used in KTU-style numerical problems:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Density of steel:** $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of water:** $\rho_w = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Standard ambient temperature for design:** $T_{amb} = 40^\circ C$
- **Design factor of safety (hoist ropes):** $FOS_{rope} = 3.5$ (typical)
- **Drive efficiency (worm-geared hoist):** $\eta = 0.7$ to $0.85$
- **Standard belt speed (assembly line):** $v = 0.25$ to $2.5 \ \text{m/s}$

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conveyor Systems — Operating Principles

A conveyor is fundamentally a **closed-loop power transmission system** where the load is carried on a moving element driven by friction or positive engagement.

### 2.1.1 Belt Conveyor — Force and Power Analysis

The primary engineering challenge in a belt conveyor is determining the **effective tension** the motor must overcome. The belt passes over a **head pulley** (drive) and a **tail pulley** (idler). The side of the belt pulling the load is the **tight side** ($T_1$), and the returning side is the **slack side** ($T_2$).

**Why tension exists:** As the belt bends over the pulleys, the material on the belt must be lifted against gravity, and friction must be overcome at the idlers and pulleys. The motor must therefore supply a net effective tension $T_e$ to keep the belt moving.

**How the motor works:** The motor torque is transmitted to the head pulley through a gearbox. The pulley applies a tangential force equal to $T_1 - T_2$ on the belt, which is the **effective tension** that moves the load.

#### Euler's Capstan Equation (Belt-Pulley Friction)
The relationship between the two belt tensions at the drive pulley is governed by friction:

$$T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$$

Where:
- $T_1$ = **Tight-side tension** (N)
- $T_2$ = **Slack-side tension** (N)
- $\mu$ = **Coefficient of friction** between belt and pulley (typically **0.25 to 0.40** for rubber-steel)
- $\theta$ = **Angle of wrap** (radians) on the drive pulley

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** For a standard drive pulley with $\theta = 180^\circ = \pi$ radians, and $\mu = 0.3$:
> $$T_1 \approx T_2 \cdot e^{0.3\pi} \approx 2.566 \ T_2$$

### 2.1.2 Power Required by the Belt Conveyor

The **power at the drive pulley** is given by:

$$P_{pulley} = T_e \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

The **motor power** must also overcome gearbox and coupling losses:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{T_e \cdot v}{\eta_{drive}}$$

Where $\eta_{drive}$ is the **overall drive efficiency** (typically **0.85 to 0.92**).

### 2.1.3 Belt Conveyor Capacity (Throughput)

The **mass flow rate** of material carried by the belt:

$$Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad [\text{tonnes/hr}]$$

Where:
- $A$ = **Cross-sectional area** of material on the belt ($\text{m}^2$)
- $v$ = **Belt speed** (m/s)
- $\rho$ = **Bulk density** of material ($\text{kg/m}^3$)

A practical formula used in industry (for troughed belts):

$$Q = 0.036 \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot \rho \quad [\text{tonnes/hr}]$$

Where $B$ is the **belt width** in meters.

---

## 2.2 Hoists — Mechanical Design Equations

### 2.2.1 Hoist Drum Design

A hoist drum is a **cylindrical spool** around which the wire rope (or chain) winds as the load is lifted. The key dimensions are:

- **Drum diameter:** $D_d$ (m)
- **Rope diameter:** $d_r$ (m)
- **Number of rope layers:** $n$
- **Total rope length to be wound:** $L$ (m)

The relationship between drum diameter and rope diameter is constrained by **fatigue life** standards:

$$D_d \geq 20 \cdot d_r \quad \text{(minimum, per IS 3177)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **mobile cranes**, the recommended ratio is $D_d \geq 30 \cdot d_r$ for higher duty cycles.

### 2.2.2 Hoist Motor Power

The **hoisting power** required to lift a load at a specified velocity is:

$$P_{hoist} = \frac{W \cdot v_{lift}}{\eta_{hoist}} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

Where:
- $W$ = **Load weight** + **weight of hook block** + **weight of rope** (N)
- $v_{lift}$ = **Lifting velocity** (m/s)
- $\eta_{hoist}$ = **Hoist mechanical efficiency** (typically **0.75 to 0.90**)

### 2.2.3 Rope / Chain Safety Factor

The **breaking strength** of the rope must exceed the maximum working load by the design factor of safety:

$$F_{break} = FOS \cdot W_{max}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 3177 Standard FOS values:**
> - Manual hoists: $FOS = 3.0$
> - Electric hoists: $FOS = 3.5$
> - Grabbing / hoisting molten metal: $FOS = 5.0+$

---

## 2.3 Cranes — Stability and Load Equations

### 2.3.1 Crane Stability (Tipping Load)

A crane mounted on wheels or outriggers can **tip over** if the load moment exceeds the stabilizing moment. The **load moment** is the load weight times its horizontal distance from the tipping edge; the **stabilizing moment** is the crane's own weight times the distance from the tipping edge to the center of gravity.

**Condition for stability (without tipping):**

$$W_{crane} \cdot x_{cg} \geq W_{load} \cdot r_{load}$$

Where:
- $W_{crane}$ = **Weight of crane** (N)
- $x_{cg}$ = **Horizontal distance from tipping edge to crane's center of gravity** (m)
- $W_{load}$ = **Lifted load** (N)
- $r_{load}$ = **Horizontal reach** of the load from tipping edge (m)

### 2.3.2 Crane Hoist Speed (Cross-Sectional Sizing)

The line pull (tension in the hoist rope) is:

$$F_{rope} = \frac{W_{load}}{n_{parts}} \quad [\text{N}]$$

Where $n_{parts}$ is the **number of rope parts** supporting the hook block (a 2-part reeving halves the rope tension but doubles the required rope travel).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Print-ready summary** — commit this table to memory before the exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Constant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euler's capstan (belt friction) | $T_1 = T_2 \cdot e^{\mu \theta}$ | N | $\mu = 0.3$ |
| 2 | Effective belt tension | $T_e = T_1 - T_2$ | N | — |
| 3 | Belt power | $P = T_e \cdot v$ | W | — |
| 4 | Motor power | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot v}{\eta_{drive}}$ | W | $\eta_{drive} = 0.88$ |
| 5 | Belt capacity | $Q = 0.036 \cdot v \cdot B^2 \cdot \rho$ | t/hr | — |
| 6 | Hoist motor power | $P_{hoist} = \dfrac{W \cdot v_{lift}}{\eta_{hoist}}$ | W | $\eta = 0.8$ |
| 7 | Drum-to-rope ratio | $D_d \geq 20 d_r$ | m | IS 3177 |
| 8 | Rope safety factor | $F_{break} = FOS \cdot W_{max}$ | N | $FOS = 3.5$ |
| 9 | Rope line pull | $F_{rope} = \dfrac{W_{load}}{n_{parts}}$ | N | — |
| 10 | Crane stability | $W_{crane} \cdot x_{cg} \geq W_{load} \cdot r_{load}$ | N·m | — |
| 11 | Lifting work | $W_{lift} = m g h$ | J | $g = 9.81$ |
| 12 | Linear load on belt | $w = m g / L$ | N/m | — |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Belt conveyors** move **40% of all mined coal, ore, and aggregate** globally — they are the arteries of the bulk-materials industry.
- **Electric wire-rope hoists** are the lifting element in **>80% of overhead bridge cranes** in automobile and steel plants.
- **Gantry cranes** in container ports handle **>25 million TEU/year** (twenty-foot equivalent units) at a single terminal.
- In **robotic cells**, conveyor indexing encoders (typically **1000 PPR** — pulses per revolution) feed position signals to the **PLC**, allowing the robot to pick parts "on-the-fly" with vision-guided correction.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Required for a Horizontal Belt Conveyor

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal belt conveyor carries **coal** of bulk density $\rho = 900 \ \text{kg/m}^3$. The belt is **800 mm wide**, the cross-section of coal forms a trough of effective area $A = 0.05 \ \text{m}^2$, and the belt speed is $v = 1.5 \ \text{m/s}$. The **tight-side tension** is $T_1 = 12000$ N, and the **slack-side tension** is $T_2 = 4500$ N. Drive efficiency is $\eta_{drive} = 0.90$. Determine the **motor power** required.

### Step 1 — Define the Effective Tension

The **effective tension** $T_e$ is the net force the drive pulley must apply to move the belt forward. It is the difference between the tight and slack side tensions:

$$T_e = T_1 - T_2$$

**Conversion logic:** This arises because on the tight side, the belt is pulling the load forward, while on the slack side, the belt is being pulled back. The net tangential force on the pulley shaft equals the difference.

**Substituting the values:**

$$T_e = 12000 - 4500 = 7500 \ \text{N}$$

### Step 2 — Power at the Drive Pulley

The **pulley power** equals the effective tension multiplied by belt speed (force × velocity):

$$P_{pulley} = T_e \cdot v$$

**Conversion logic:** Power is the rate of doing work. The belt moves at $v$ m/s while the pulley exerts a tangential force of $T_e$ N, so the work done per second is $T_e \cdot v$ Joules/second = Watts.

**Substituting the values:**

$$P_{pulley} = 7500 \ \text{N} \times 1.5 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$P_{pulley} = 11250 \ \text{W} = 11.25 \ \text{kW}$$

### Step 3 — Account for Drive Losses

The **motor power** must be greater than the pulley power because the gear reducer, couplings, and motor windings dissipate energy. The relationship is:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{pulley}}{\eta_{drive}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Efficiency is defined as $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$. Therefore, $P_{in} = P_{out} / \eta$. Since the motor is upstream (input) of the drive train, the motor must supply the pulley power plus the losses.

**Substituting the values:**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{11250}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} = 12500 \ \text{W} = 12.5 \ \text{kW}}$$

### Step 4 — Check the Material Throughput (Bonus)

Using $Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho$:

$$Q = 0.05 \times 1.5 \times 900 = 67.5 \ \text{kg/s}$$

Converting to tonnes/hour:

$$Q = 67.5 \times 3.6 = 243 \ \text{tonnes/hr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** A 12.5 kW motor driving a conveyor carrying 243 t/hr of coal is dimensionally consistent with industry practice (typical specific power consumption ≈ 0.05 kW per t/hr for horizontal conveyors).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hoist Motor Sizing for a Vertical Lift

**Problem Statement:**
An electric hoist must lift a **load of 800 kg** (including hook block) at a **speed of 6 m/min**. The hoist uses a **worm gearbox** with efficiency $\eta_{hoist} = 0.75$. The **wire rope** has a factor of safety requirement of $FOS = 3.5$. Determine (a) the **hoist motor power**, and (b) the **minimum rope breaking strength**.

### Step 3.2.1 — Convert Speed to SI Units

The lift speed is given in m/min, but power formulas require m/s:

$$v_{lift} = \frac{6 \ \text{m/min}}{60 \ \text{s/min}} = 0.1 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Conversion logic:** $1$ minute $= 60$ seconds. To convert m/min to m/s, divide by 60.

### Step 3.2.2 — Calculate the Weight Being Lifted

The total vertical force the motor must overcome is the weight of the load:

$$W = m \cdot g = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \ \text{N}$$

**Conversion logic:** Newton's second law under gravity: $W = mg$. Acceleration is $g = 9.81$ m/s² downward, so the hoist must supply an equal upward force.

### Step 3.2.3 — Hoist Motor Power (Account for Efficiency)

The motor must supply more power than the ideal $W \cdot v$ because the worm gearbox dissipates a portion as heat:

$$P_{hoist} = \frac{W \cdot v_{lift}}{\eta_{hoist}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Worm gears are non-reversible and have sliding contact, leading to significant friction losses (often 25-30% even in good units). Hence, $\eta$ is lower than for spur/helical gears.

**Substituting the values:**

$$P_{hoist} = \frac{7848 \times 0.1}{0.75} = \frac{784.8}{0.75}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hoist} = 1046.4 \ \text{W} \approx 1.05 \ \text{kW}}$$

### Step 3.2.4 — Rope Breaking Strength

The wire rope must be able to withstand $FOS$ times the working load before it fails. This protects against overload, fatigue, wear, and shock loads:

$$F_{break} = FOS \cdot W_{max}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of safety is a multiplier on the *expected* maximum load to give a *guaranteed minimum* capacity before failure. For an $FOS = 3.5$ electric hoist per IS 3177:

$$F_{break} = 3.5 \times 7848$$

$$\boxed{F_{break} = 27468 \ \text{N} \approx 27.47 \ \text{kN}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard **6×19 wire rope** with a 10 mm diameter has a typical breaking strength of ≈ 38 kN, which would satisfy the requirement with a *realized* FOS of 4.84 — above the minimum 3.5.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Crane Tipping Load

**Problem Statement:**
A mobile crane has a **total weight of 25 tonnes** (excluding load) and its **center of gravity is 1.8 m** behind the front outrigger. The crane is lifting a load at a **horizontal reach of 6 m** in front of the same outrigger. Determine the **maximum load** the crane can lift without tipping forward.

### Step 3.3.1 — Identify the Tipping Axis

The crane will tip about the **front outrigger** when the load moment exceeds the stabilizing moment from the crane's own weight.

### Step 3.3.2 — Apply the Stability Equation

**At the verge of tipping**, the moments about the outrigger are equal:

$$W_{crane} \cdot x_{cg} = W_{load} \cdot r_{load}$$

**Conversion logic:** The crane's weight creates a *restoring* moment (counterclockwise) about the tipping edge. The load creates a *destabilizing* moment (clockwise). When they are equal, the crane is on the verge of tipping.

### Step 3.3.3 — Substitute Known Values

Convert the crane weight to Newtons (using $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$W_{crane} = 25000 \ \text{kg} \times 9.81 = 245250 \ \text{N}$$

Substitute into the moment equation:

$$245250 \times 1.8 = W_{load} \times 6$$

**Solve for $W_{load}$:**

$$W_{load} = \frac{245250 \times 1.8}{6} = \frac{441450}{6}$$

$$W_{load} = 73575 \ \text{N}$$

### Step 3.3.4 — Convert to Mass

$$m_{load} = \frac{W_{load}}{g} = \frac{73575}{9.81}$$

$$\boxed{m_{load} \approx 7500 \ \text{kg} = 7.5 \ \text{tonnes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Practice:** Real cranes operate at **75% of the calculated tipping load** to provide a *stability margin* against wind, dynamic effects, and uneven outrigger settling.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Engineering Problem | Governing Principle | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Motor sizing for a belt conveyor | Belt friction + load lifting | $P = T_e \cdot v / \eta$ |
| Drum diameter for a given rope | Fatigue life of rope | $D_d \geq 20 d_r$ |
| Hoist lifting time | Power and energy balance | $t = (m g h) / P$ |
| Crane tip-over check | Moment equilibrium | $W_{crane} x_{cg} = W_{load} r_{load}$ |
| Belt capacity (tonnage/hr) | Cross-section × speed × density | $Q = 0.036 v B^2 \rho$ |
| Wire rope selection | Breaking strength & FOS | $F_{break} = FOS \cdot W$ |
| Trolley acceleration force | Newton's second law | $F = m a$ |
| Brake sizing for hoist | Energy dissipation | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ |
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
