| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $T_c = T_{pick} + T_{move} + T_{place} + T_{return}$ | Cycle Time | s | Cycle time analysis |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} \times \eta$ | Throughput (with efficiency $\eta$) | items/hr | Production rate |
| 3 | $P_{mech} = P_{payload} \times g \times (1 + SF)$ | Required mechanical actuator force | N | Gripper / wrist sizing |
| 4 | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Savings}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100\%$ | Return on Investment | % | Justification of robotics |
| 5 | $PBP = \dfrac{C_{capex} + C_{install}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Flow}}$ | Payback Period | years | Economic feasibility |
| 6 | $U = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | Utilization factor | % | Plant efficiency |
| 7 | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness (Availability × Performance × Quality) | dimensionless | World-class benchmark = **85%** |
| 8 | $N_{robots} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot t_{cycle}}{T_{available}}$ | Number of robots for demand rate $\lambda$ | robots | Sizing |
| 9 | $T_{saved} = N \cdot (T_{manual} - T_{robot})$ | Labor hours saved per year | hr/yr | Labor economics |
| 10 | $f_{grip} = \mu \cdot F_N \geq W_{payload}$ | Gripper friction condition (static hold) | N | End-effector design |
| 11 | $\tau_i = \sum_{j=i}^{n} (m_j g \cdot r_{ij})$ | Joint torque due to gravity loading | N·m | Static torque sizing |
| 12 | $E_{cycle} = \int_{0}^{T_c} P_{inst}(t)\, dt$ | Energy per cycle | J or kWh | Energy benchmarking |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING RULE:** In the formulas above, vertical bars such as $\vert x \vert$ are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the KTU markdown renderer. Example: $\vert \vec{F} \vert$ not $\vert \vec{F} \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Why these formulas matter |
|-------|---------------------------|
| **Industrial Engineering** | ROI and OEE drive make-or-buy decisions for cell automation. |
| **Mechanical Design** | Torque and gripper force equations determine motor and gripper selection. |
| **Manufacturing Mgmt** | Number-of-robots formula justifies cell expansion proposals. |
| **Energy Audit** | $E_{cycle}$ enables cost-per-part estimation against energy tariffs (e.g., ₹8/kWh in Kerala). |

---

## 2.5 Sub-Block C: Future Trends (Detailed)

### 2.5.1 Collaborative Robots (Cobots)

A **cobot** is a robot designed for **direct physical interaction with a human worker** within a defined collaborative workspace, complying with **ISO/TS 15066** and **ISO 10218**.

Four Collaborative Modes (per ISO/TS 15066):

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** Robot halts when human enters workspace.
2. **Hand Guiding:** Operator physically moves the robot end-effector.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** Robot slows as human approaches using distance sensors.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Robot's kinetic energy is capped (max **80 J** quasi-static contact, **160 N** transient force).

**Key Industry Cobot Brands:** Universal Robots (UR5e, UR10e, UR20), FANUC CRX, ABB GoFa, KUKA LBR iiwa.

**Quantitative Enabler:** A cobot's **effective dynamic mass (EDM)** must be reduced to ensure contact forces stay below biomechanical limits:
$$F_{contact} \leq F_{limit} \quad \text{where} \quad F_{limit} = k \cdot v_{robot}^{2/3}$$

where $k$ is a body-region-specific constant (e.g., **140 N·s²/m²** for the hand, per ISO/TS 15066).

---

### 2.5.2 AI-Driven Robotics

Modern assembly lines use **Machine Learning (ML)** for:
- **Bin Picking:** 6-DoF pose estimation of randomly oriented parts using **PointNet++** or **DOPE (Deep Object Pose Estimation)**.
- **Visual Quality Control:** Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) classify defects at **> 99.5% accuracy**.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** LSTM networks forecast bearing/motor failure **48–72 hours in advance** by analyzing vibration, current, and temperature signatures.

---

### 2.5.3 Digital Twins

A **Digital Twin** is a real-time virtual replica of a physical robotic cell, synchronized via **OPC-UA / MQTT** protocols. Benefits:
- **Virtual commissioning** reduces line ramp-up time by **40–60%**.
- **What-if analysis** for cycle-time optimization.
- **Closed-loop feedback** from physical to virtual for AI training (sim-to-real).

---

### 2.5.4 Mobile Manipulation (MOMRs / AMRs)

**Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs)** combine a mobile base (SLAM navigation, **LiDAR + IMU**) with a manipulator arm. Example platforms: **Fetch Robotics Freight + Arm**, **KUKA KMR iiwa**, **Omron LD-MOBILE**.

---

### 2.5.5 Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) Trends

- **Natural Language Programming:** Voice-driven robot teaching (e.g., Google SayCan).
- **AR/VR Teleoperation:** Operators control robots remotely via headsets (low-latency 5G, <20 ms).
- **Bio-inspired Grippers:** Soft robotics using **elastomeric fingers** that conform to fragile objects (fruits, IV bags, glassware).

---

### 2.5.6 Industry 5.0 — The Human-Centric Shift

| Pillar | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| Human-Centricity | Worker well-being and creativity over pure automation |
| Sustainability | Energy-efficient robots, recyclable materials, **carbon-neutral factories by 2030** |
| Resilience | Reconfigurable cells that adapt to demand shocks (e.g., post-COVID supply chain) |
| AI-Augmentation | Robots as **co-workers**, not replacements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Answer Format:** When asked *"Discuss future trends"*, students must list **at least 4 trends** (cobot, AI, digital twin, mobile manipulation) and **link each to a specific industrial application**. Generic answers without application linkage fetch **partial credit only** (max 7 of 14 marks in Part B).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section delivers **worked-out derivations and numerical examples** that the KTU examiner commonly sets. Because Module 4 is application-oriented, derivations focus on **economic justification, throughput sizing, and cobot safety analysis** — not kinematics.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Robots Required to Meet Production Demand

### Problem Statement
A manufacturing cell produces a part. Manual cycle time is **45 s**. Robot cycle time is **18 s**. The cell operates **2 shifts × 8 hours × 250 days/year**. Required annual output = **800,000 units/year**. Determine the number of robots required if the **utilization** must be **≤ 85%**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute available time per robot per year.**
$$T_{avail} = 2 \times 8 \times 250 = 4000 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 2: Compute the productive (utilized) time per robot at 85% utilization.**
$$T_{productive} = U \times T_{avail} = 0.85 \times 4000 = 3400 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 3: Compute the throughput of one robot at 18 s cycle time.**
$$Q_1 = \frac{3600 \text{ s/hr}}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{18} = 200 \text{ parts/hr}$$

**Step 4: Compute annual production per robot.**
$$Q_{annual} = Q_1 \times T_{productive} = 200 \times 3400 = 680{,}000 \text{ units/year}$$

**Step 5: Compute the number of robots required.**
$$N = \frac{\text{Demand}}{Q_{annual}} = \frac{800{,}000}{680{,}000} = 1.176$$

Since a robot is an integer, **N = 2 robots** must be deployed.

**Step 6: Verify utilization on 2 robots.**
$$U_{actual} = \frac{800{,}000}{2 \times 680{,}000} = 0.588 = 58.8\%$$

This is below the 85% cap ✓ — the design is feasible.

### Final Answer
**2 robots** with an actual utilization of **58.8%** (leaves headroom for surges).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ROI and Payback Period for a Robotic Cell

### Problem Statement
A palletizing robot costs:
- Robot + gripper: **₹25,00,000**
- Safety fencing, conveyor integration, installation: **₹5,00,000**
- Total CapEx: **₹30,00,000**

Annual savings:
- Labor displaced: 2 workers × ₹4,00,000/yr = **₹8,00,000**
- Scrap reduction: **₹1,50,000**
- Productivity gain: **₹2,50,000**
- **Total annual savings: ₹12,00,000**

Annual operating cost (power, maintenance): **₹2,00,000**.

Compute **(a) Net annual cash flow, (b) Simple ROI, (c) Payback period.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Net annual cash flow.**
$$\text{Net CF} = \text{Savings} - \text{OpEx} = 12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2: Simple ROI.**
$$ROI = \frac{\text{Net CF}}{\text{Total Investment}} \times 100\% = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 33.33\%$$

**Step 3: Payback period.**
$$PBP = \frac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Net CF}} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ years}$$

### Final Answer
- **Net CF: ₹10 Lakh/yr**
- **ROI: 33.33%/yr** (Excellent — above KTU's typical 20% benchmark)
- **PBP: 3 years** (Acceptable — industry target is ≤ 4 years)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cobot Contact Force Verification (ISO/TS 15066)

### Problem Statement
A UR10e cobot (mass **33 kg**) moves at **v = 250 mm/s** toward a stationary operator's hand. Determine whether the **quasi-static contact force** at impact satisfies the **140 N** hand-region limit per **ISO/TS 15066**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the effective dynamic mass of the moving system.**
For a cobot, the effective mass moving toward the operator is the **payload + a fraction of the arm inertia**. Conservatively:
$$m_{eff} = m_{robot,equivalent} + m_{payload}$$

Assume $m_{payload} = 5$ kg, $m_{robot,equivalent} = 10$ kg (manufacturer data).
$$m_{eff} = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Compute the kinetic energy at impact.**
$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 15 \times (0.25)^2 = 0.46875 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Apply the energy-based contact-force model (simplified).**
Using $F_{max} \approx \sqrt{2 \cdot KE \cdot k}$ where $k$ is the effective contact stiffness. For a soft cushion pad on the robot, $k \approx 50{,}000$ N/m.
$$F_{max} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.46875 \times 50{,}000} = \sqrt{46{,}875} = 216.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Compare to limit.**
$$F_{max} = 216.5 \text{ N} > F_{limit} = 140 \text{ N} \quad \text{✗ NOT SAFE}$$

**Step 5: Reduce velocity to meet the limit.**
Set $F_{max} = 140$ N and solve for $v$:
$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot KE}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times \left(\frac{F_{limit}^2}{2k}\right)}{m_{eff}}} = \sqrt{\frac{F_{limit}^2}{m_{eff} \cdot k}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{(140)^2}{15 \times 50{,}000}} = \sqrt{\frac{19{,}600}{750{,}000}} = \sqrt{0.02613} = 0.1617 \text{ m/s} = 161.7 \text{ mm/s}$$

### Final Answer
The robot must operate at or below **161.7 mm/s** to comply with the **ISO/TS 15066** hand-region limit. The original 250 mm/s speed is **non-compliant**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Robot Selection by Application

| Application | Robot Type | DoF | Payload | Reach | Key Reason |
|-------------|-----------|-----|---------|-------|------------|
| SMT Pick-and-Place | Delta | 3–4 | 1–5 kg | 500–1200 mm | Speed, low inertia |
| Spot Welding (Auto BIW) | Articulated | 6 | 100–250 kg | 2500–3500 mm | Heavy transformer, large reach |
| Palletizing | Articulated (4-axis) | 4 | 100–1200 kg | 2500–3500 mm | High Z-axis, high payload |
| Bin Picking | Articulated + Vision | 6 | 5–30 kg | 1400–2000 mm | 6-DoF pose correction |
| Machine Tending | Articulated | 6 | 20–500 kg | 1500–3000 mm | Door opening, dexterity |
| Collaborative Assembly | Cobot (UR/FANUC/ABB) | 6 | 5–35 kg | 500–1300 mm | Safety, flexibility |
| AGV Transport | AMR | 0 (mobile) | 100–1500 kg | N/A | Logistics, indoor nav |
| Aerospace Drilling | Gantry / PKM | 5–6 | 50–200 kg | 10,000+ mm | Large work envelope, stiffness |
| Wafer Handling | Cleanroom Articulated | 6 | 2–5 kg | 600–900 mm | ISO Class 3 cleanliness |
| Surgical Assistance | Specialty 7-DoF | 7 | <2 kg | 600–800 mm | Precision, sterility |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not write generic "future trends" essays.** The examiner awards marks only when you **link a trend to a specific industrial application** (e.g., "Cobots in small-batch electronics assembly where changeover is frequent").
> 2. **Always quantify when possible.** Saying "robots save money" scores 1 mark; saying "ROI = 33% over a 3-year payback" scores 3 marks.
> 3. **Cite standards by number** (e.g., ISO/TS 15066, ISO 10218, RIA 15.06). Examiners reward this.
> 4. **Use block diagrams in 14-mark answers.** A well-labelled Mermaid-style ASCII diagram is worth 2–3 extra marks.
> 5. **Common trap question:** *"Differentiate cobot from industrial robot."* Students often confuse payload and safety — remember: **a cobot is defined by its collaborative operating mode, not by payload.**

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a collaborative robot (cobot). List any two collaborative operating modes as per ISO/TS 15066.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **collaborative robot (cobot)** is a robot designed for direct physical interaction with a human within a defined collaborative workspace, in compliance with **ISO/TS 15066** and **ISO 10218** safety standards.

Two collaborative operating modes:
1. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** The robot dynamically adjusts its speed based on the distance to the operator, slowing down as the human approaches and stopping if the minimum protective distance is breached.
2. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** The robot's actuators and mechanical design inherently limit contact forces and pressures to biomechanically safe values, capping transient contact force at **≤ 160 N** and quasi-static contact at **≤ 140 N** for the hand region.

*(Valid alternatives: Safety-Rated Monitored Stop, Hand Guiding — any 2 = full marks.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between an AGV and an AMR in the context of modern material handling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) | AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Navigation | Follows fixed paths (magnetic tape, QR codes, inductive wire) | Uses **SLAM** with **LiDAR** and cameras — no fixed infrastructure |
| Flexibility | Low — path change requires physical re-installation | High — software-defined dynamic path planning |
| Obstacle Handling | Stops on obstacle; cannot reroute | Detects, plans around, and reroutes autonomously |
| Typical Use | High-volume, fixed-route pallet transport | Mixed-SKU warehouses, dynamic order fulfillment |

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **industrial applications of robotic assembly systems** in the **automotive** and **electronics** sectors. For each sector, mention **robot type, payload, repeatability, and a representative cycle time**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company plans to install a **robotic palletizing cell** to replace 3 manual workers. CapEx = **₹45,00,000** (robot + gripper + safety + installation). Annual labor savings = **₹18,00,000**. Annual operating cost = **₹3,00,000**. Compute the **Net Annual Cash Flow, ROI (%), and Payback Period (years)**. Comment on the investment decision. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Q-A(a): (7 marks)**

**Automotive Sector Applications:**

1. **Body-in-White (BIW) Spot Welding:** 6-axis articulated robots (e.g., FANUC R-2000iC, payload **100–250 kg**, reach **3 m**, repeatability **±0.1 mm**, cycle time **60–90 spots/minute**) weld stamped panels to form the car body shell.
2. **Powertrain Assembly:** Engine block and transmission subassemblies use force-controlled compliant assembly robots (KUKA KR 1000, payload **up to 1000 kg**, force/torque sensor for ±10 N mating precision).
3. **Final Assembly:** Door, windshield, bumper, and wheel fitment with vision-guided SCARA/articulated robots (cycle time **40–80 s/station**).

**Electronics Sector Applications:**

1. **SMT Pick-and-Place:** High-speed delta or SCARA robots place SMD components onto PCBs at **30,000–80,000 CPH** with **±0.01 mm** placement accuracy.
2. **PCB Soldering & Inspection:** Selective soldering robots with vision alignment and AOI (Automated Optical Inspection) integration.
3. **Semiconductor Wafer Handling:** Cleanroom robots (ISO Class 3, payload **2–5 kg**, reach **600–900 mm**) transport 300 mm wafers between process chambers with **±0.05 mm** precision.

*(Any 2 sectors × 2 examples each, with the required four parameters, scores full 7 marks.)*

---

### **Model Answer to Q-A(b): (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Total CapEx ($C_{capex}$) = ₹45,00,000
- Annual labor savings = ₹18,00,000
- Annual OpEx = ₹3,00,000

**Step 1: Net Annual Cash Flow.**
$$\text{Net CF} = \text{Savings} - \text{OpEx} = 18{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = ₹15{,}00{,}000/\text{yr}$$

**Step 2: ROI.**
$$ROI = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{45{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 33.33\%$$

**Step 3: Payback Period.**
$$PBP = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4: Comment.**
A **ROI of 33.33% per annum** and a **payback period of 3 years** are **excellent** by industry standards (benchmark PBP ≤ 4 years, ROI ≥ 20%). The investment is **economically justified** and should be approved. Additional qualitative benefits (24/7 operation, consistent quality, reduced workplace injuries in heavy lifting) further strengthen the case.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **four collaborative operating modes** defined by **ISO/TS 15066**, with a one-line example application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UR10e cobot (effective moving mass **15 kg**) must operate alongside a human operator's hand. The protective padding on the robot reduces effective contact stiffness to **k = 75,000 N/m**. The biomechanical limit for the hand region per ISO/TS 15066 is **F_limit = 140 N**. Compute the **maximum permissible velocity** $v_{max}$ for safe operation. If the originally programmed speed is **500 mm/s**, what action is required? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Q-B(a): (7 marks)**

The **four collaborative operating modes** as per **ISO/TS 15066** are:

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** The robot operates at full speed when no human is in the workspace; it halts in a safety-rated stop when a human enters. *Example: A cobot tending a CNC machine while the operator loads blanks intermittently.*

2. **Hand Guiding (HG):** The operator physically leads the robot's end-effector to teach trajectories or perform tasks requiring human dexterity. *Example: Manual loading of a heavy casting into a fixture with hand-guided robot positioning.*

3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** The robot continuously adjusts its speed as a function of the operator's distance, computed from safety-rated laser scanners or vision systems. *Example: A mobile manipulator navigating a warehouse aisle alongside pickers.*

4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** The robot's design intrinsically limits contact forces and pressures below biomechanical thresholds, using rounded surfaces, compliant padding, and current/torque monitoring. *Example: A cobot performing screw-driving in a small-batch electronics assembly where contact with the operator's hand is plausible.*

---

### **Model Answer to Q-B(b): (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Set up the impact force model.**
For a quasi-elastic contact:
$$F_{max} = \sqrt{2 \cdot KE \cdot k} = \sqrt{m_{eff} \cdot v^2 \cdot k}$$

**Step 2: Equate to the limit and solve for v.**
$$F_{limit} = \sqrt{m_{eff} \cdot v_{max}^2 \cdot k}$$
$$v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{F_{limit}^2}{m_{eff} \cdot k}} = \frac{F_{limit}}{\sqrt{m_{eff} \cdot k}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**
- $F_{limit} = 140$ N
- $m_{eff} = 15$ kg
- $k = 75{,}000$ N/m

$$v_{max} = \frac{140}{\sqrt{15 \times 75{,}000}} = \frac{140}{\sqrt{1{,}125{,}000}} = \frac{140}{1060.66}$$

$$v_{max} = 0.1320 \text{ m/s} = 132.0 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4: Compare with the programmed speed of 500 mm/s.**
$$v_{programmed} = 500 \text{ mm/s} > v_{max} = 132.0 \text{ mm/s}$$

The programmed speed is **non-compliant** with ISO/TS 15066.

**Step 5: Required action.**
1. **Reduce the programmed TCP speed** to ≤ **132 mm/s** in collaborative mode.
2. **Implement Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** so the robot automatically slows further as the operator approaches.
3. **Re-validate** the risk assessment per **ISO 10218-1/-2** and **ISO 12100**.
4. **Document** the new speed and the validation report in the cell's technical file.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points (must memorize before exam):**

- [ ] Applications are driven by the **4 D's**: Dull, Dirty, Dangerous, Delicate.
- [ ] **SMT pick-and-place** → Delta robot; **Spot welding** → 6-axis articulated; **Palletizing** → 4-axis high-payload articulated.
- [ ] **Cobot** = robot designed for collaboration; **defined by operating mode, not payload**.
- [ ] **ISO/TS 15066** = cobot safety standard; **4 collaborative modes**: SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL.
- [ ] Hand-region biomechanical limit: **140 N (quasi-static), 160 N (transient)**.
- [ ] **AGV** = fixed path; **AMR** = SLAM-based dynamic navigation.
- [ ] **Digital Twin** = real-time virtual replica synchronized via **OPC-UA / MQTT**.
- [ ] **OEE world-class benchmark** = **85%** (Availability × Performance × Quality).
- [ ] **Industry 4.0** = connectivity and automation; **Industry 5.0** = human-centric + sustainable + resilient.
- [ ] Always compute **ROI > 20%** and **PBP < 4 years** to justify cell automation.
- [ ] Cite **standards by number**: ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 12100, RIA 15.06.
- [ ] In 14-mark answers, **include a block diagram** (mermaid / ASCII) — worth 2–3 bonus marks.
- [ ] Common trap: confusing **repeatability** (±0.02 mm) with **accuracy** (larger band).
- [ ] **Soft robotics** = elastomeric / compliant grippers for delicate items (food, pharma).
- [ ] **Predictive maintenance** uses LSTM/ML on vibration, current, temperature data.
- [ ] **Sim-to-real** transfer of AI models from digital twin to physical cell is a KTU buzzword.
- [ ] **5G teleoperation** latency target: **< 20 ms** for responsive HRI.
- [ ] **MNPs (Mobile Manipulators)** combine AMR base + manipulator arm; payload 100–1500 kg.
- [ ] Always quantify: "cycle time 18 s" beats "fast robot".
- [ ] Kerala-specific data: industrial electricity tariff ~ **₹8/kWh**; use this for energy cost calculations.

---

**End of Module 4 — Topic: Applications and Future Trends | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Advanced Robotic Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERUT724 - ROBOTIC ASSEMBLY AND MATERIAL HANDLING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Robotic Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Advanced Robotic Applications** refer to the deployment of multi-axis, sensor-integrated, reprogrammable robotic manipulators and mobile platforms in industrial, medical, and service domains beyond the scope of conventional pick-and-place operations. As per the KTU 2024 OERUT724 syllabus (Module 4), these applications encompass robotic welding, painting, machining, assembly, palletizing, and inspection tasks where the robot acts as a *flexible automation agent* capable of executing complex trajectories, force-controlled interactions, and coordinated multi-robot operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** *An advanced robotic application is a programmed, sensor-augmented robotic work-cell in which a manipulator, mobile platform, or hybrid system performs non-trivial tasks (e.g., arc welding, assembly insertion, painting) with closed-loop control, environmental awareness, and adaptive decision-making capability.*

The three pillars that elevate a robotic application from "basic" to "advanced" are:

1. **Sensor Fusion** — integration of vision, force/torque, proximity, and tactile feedback.
2. **Adaptive Control** — real-time trajectory and force adjustment based on sensory input.
3. **System Integration** — networking with CNC, PLC, MES, and IoT layers (Industry 4.0 compliance).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a basic industrial robot as a **trained circus elephant** — strong, reliable, but it follows the same memorized path every day. An **advanced robotic application**, on the other hand, is like a **skilled artisan with sensory organs and a brain**. 

Consider robotic arc welding:
- The *basic* version is a robot blindly tracing a pre-taught seam, hoping the part is in exactly the right place.
- The *advanced* version uses **laser seam tracking**, **through-the-arc sensing**, and **adaptive voltage/current control** to "see" the joint, "feel" the gap, and "decide" how much filler metal to deposit — just as a human welder would visually follow the puddle and adjust amperage by ear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Advanced applications move the robot from *blind execution* (open-loop, position-controlled) to *intelligent execution* (closed-loop, sensor-driven, adaptive). This is the heart of Module 4.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Parameters

The performance of advanced robotic applications is benchmarked by:

- **Repeatability:** $\pm 0.02 \text{ mm}$ to $\pm 0.1 \text{ mm}$ for high-precision applications.
- **Payload-to-Weight Ratio:** Modern cobots achieve **1:1** (e.g., 5 kg payload for a 5 kg arm).
- **Cycle Time:** Measured in **seconds per operation**; KTU problems often quote **30–60 s** for a typical welding or palletizing cycle.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Industrial benchmark is **> 60,000 hours** for 6-axis arms.
- **TCP (Tool Center Point) Accuracy:** Typically **$\pm 0.05 \text{ mm}$** in well-calibrated cells.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Standard weld travel speed range: $0.5 \text{ m/min}$ to $2.0 \text{ m/min}$
> - Typical assembly insertion force tolerance: $5 \text{ N}$ to $50 \text{ N}$ for compliant parts

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Advanced Robotic Applications

KTU 2024 Module 4 groups advanced applications into **six functional clusters**. Each cluster has unique control, sensing, and programming demands.

### 2.1.1 Robotic Welding (Arc, Spot, Laser)

**Operating Principle:** The robot moves a welding torch or laser head along a pre-defined seam while maintaining **constant TCP velocity**, **constant stand-off distance**, and **constant torch orientation** (often called the *work angle* and *travel angle*).

**Critical Control Parameters:**
- **Wire Feed Rate (WFR):** in m/min
- **Travel Speed ($V_t$):** in mm/s
- **Heat Input ($HI$):** the energy deposited per unit length of weld.

**The "Why" of Sensor Integration in Welding:**
- Arc welding produces intense EM interference, so vision-based tracking is preferred over simple encoders.
- Through-the-arc sensing measures the *dynamic resistance* of the arc; if the torch drifts, the arc length changes, the resistance changes, and the controller corrects the path in real time.

### 2.1.2 Robotic Painting and Coating

**Operating Principle:** Atomized paint or powder is sprayed through a nozzle attached to the robot's end-effector. The robot moves the nozzle at a **constant stand-off distance** (typically $150\text{–}300 \text{ mm}$) and **constant velocity** to ensure uniform film thickness.

**Why Constant Velocity Matters:**
Paint film thickness $t$ is governed by:

$$ t = \frac{Q}{V_t \cdot w} $$

where $Q$ is volumetric flow rate, $V_t$ is TCP velocity, and $w$ is spray fan width. Any variation in $V_t$ produces visible *orange peel* or *sags* — hence the need for **Constant Velocity Path Planning** (CVPP).

### 2.1.3 Robotic Machining (Drilling, Milling, Deburring)

**Operating Principle:** A high-speed spindle is mounted on the robot's flange. The robot provides 6-DoF positioning while a **force/torque sensor** at the wrist maintains constant cutting force.

**Compliance Control:** The robot's effective stiffness is reduced along the tool axis so that small geometric errors do not translate into huge force errors (which would break the tool or workpiece).

### 2.1.4 Robotic Assembly

**Operating Principle:** Two main sub-tasks — *parts mating* (e.g., peg-in-hole) and *fastener driving* (e.g., screw insertion). Compliance is achieved via:
- **RCC (Remote Center Compliance) devices** — passive mechanical compliance.
- **Active force control** — wrist-mounted F/T sensor + admittance control.

### 2.1.5 Robotic Palletizing and Packaging

**Operating Principle:** A heavy-payload robot (typically $100\text{–}500 \text{ kg}$) picks cartons from a conveyor and stacks them in user-defined patterns on a pallet. The "trick" lies in:
- **Pattern generation algorithms** (column-stacked, pinwheel, interlocked).
- **Stability scoring** based on center-of-gravity and interlocking friction.

### 2.1.6 Robotic Inspection and Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)

**Operating Principle:** Robots carry ultrasonic probes, eddy-current sensors, or cameras to scan parts. **Path accuracy** and **probe orientation** are the critical control variables.

## 2.2 The Six "Whys" Behind Advanced Applications

| # | Question | Engineering Answer |
|---|----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Why use robots for painting? | Human exposure to VOC fumes → OSHA limits; robots give uniform film and 24/7 operation. |
| 2 | Why force control in assembly? | Parts have $\pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ positional tolerance; rigid robots jam if no compliance. |
| 3 | Why seam tracking in welding? | Thermal distortion moves the joint by $1\text{–}3 \text{ mm}$ during welding. |
| 4 | Why vision in inspection? | Sub-mm defects invisible to human eye in production volumes. |
| 5 | Why mobile robots? | Fixed manipulators cover $\sim 5\%$ of factory floor; AMRs cover 100\%. |
| 6 | Why cobots? | Safe human-robot collaboration eliminates safety cages, saves floor space. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Topic | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|-------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Weld Heat Input | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{V_t \cdot 1000}$ | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (0.6–0.85), $V$ = voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $V_t$ = travel speed (mm/s), $HI$ in kJ/mm | Robotic arc welding |
| 2 | Paint Film Thickness | $t = \dfrac{Q}{V_t \cdot w}$ | $Q$ = flow rate (mm³/s), $V_t$ = TCP velocity (mm/s), $w$ = fan width (mm), $t$ in mm | Painting/coating |
| 3 | Pallet Stability Score | $S = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{h}{H_{max}} + \beta \cdot \dfrac{I_{friction}}{I_{max}} + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{d_{cg}}{d_{base}}$ | $\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1$ (weights), $h$ = stack height, $d_{cg}$ = CG offset | Palletizing |
| 4 | Assembly Insertion Force (Peg-in-Hole) | $F_{ins} = \mu \cdot N + k \cdot \delta$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff., $N$ = normal force, $k$ = contact stiffness, $\delta$ = misalignment | Assembly |
| 5 | Cycle Time | $T_c = T_{motion} + T_{process} + T_{idle}$ | All in seconds | Cell design |
| 6 | Robot Reach Constraint | $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} \leq R_{max}$ | $R_{max}$ = max reach (m) | Work envelope |
| 7 | TCP Velocity Bound | $V_{tcp} = \omega \cdot r_{link}$ | $\omega$ = joint angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = link length (m) | Trajectory planning |
| 8 | Payload Inertia (Parallel Axis) | $I_{payload} = I_{cm} + m \cdot d^2$ | $I_{cm}$ = inertia at CG, $m$ = mass, $d$ = offset (m) | Wrist design |
| 9 | Arc Seam Tracking Error | $e(t) = K_p \cdot \Delta R(t) + K_i \int \Delta R(t) \, dt$ | $\Delta R$ = arc resistance change, $K_p, K_i$ = PI gains | Welding control |
| 10 | Energy Consumption | $E = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \tau_i \cdot \theta_i$ | $\tau_i$ = joint torque (Nm), $\theta_i$ = joint angle (rad) | Optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In all the above formulas, absolute-value notation is written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ — never using the bare pipe character inside tables, to preserve KTU markdown formatting integrity.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These advanced applications are not academic curiosities — they are the **economic backbone of modern manufacturing**:

- A single robotic welding cell in an automotive body shop replaces **3–4 human welders** and runs **24/7** with consistent quality.
- Robotic painting reduces VOC emissions by **30%** through precision control (no over-spray).
- Palletizing robots handle **$1000+$ cycles/hour** versus $150$ for a human.
- Inspection robots with machine vision achieve **$99.97\%$ defect detection** versus $90\%$ for human inspectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weld Heat Input (High-Yield for KTU 2024)

The **heat input** is the most frequently asked derivation in robotic welding questions. Here is the full, exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — Start with the definition of electrical power

The arc welding power source delivers electrical power $P$ to the arc:

$$ P = V \cdot I $$

where $V$ is the arc voltage (in volts) and $I$ is the welding current (in amperes). The unit of $P$ is therefore **watts (W)**, which equals **joules per second (J/s)**.

### Step 2 — Account for arc transfer efficiency

Not all electrical power is converted into useful heat at the workpiece. A fraction $\eta$ (the **arc efficiency** or **heat transfer efficiency**) is deposited into the joint:

$$ P_{heat} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I $$

Typical values: $\eta \approx 0.6$ for GMAW (MIG/MAG), $\eta \approx 0.85$ for SAW (submerged arc).

### Step 3 — Compute the energy delivered over a unit length of weld

The robot moves the torch at a travel speed $V_t$ (in mm/s). The time to traverse **1 mm** of weld is:

$$ t_{1mm} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{V_t \text{ mm/s}} = \frac{1}{V_t} \text{ s} $$

Energy deposited per millimetre is therefore:

$$ E_{1mm} = P_{heat} \cdot t_{1mm} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{V_t} $$

### Step 4 — Convert watts × seconds to kilojoules

Since $1 \text{ W} \cdot 1 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ J}$, and $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$:

$$ HI = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{V_t \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kJ/mm]} $$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for heat input.

### Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** A robotic MIG welding cell operates at $V = 28 \text{ V}$, $I = 220 \text{ A}$, $V_t = 6 \text{ mm/s}$, and $\eta = 0.8$.
**Find:** Heat input in kJ/mm.

**Solution:**

$$ HI = \frac{0.8 \cdot 28 \cdot 220}{6 \cdot 1000} $$

$$ HI = \frac{0.8 \cdot 6160}{6000} $$

$$ HI = \frac{4928}{6000} $$

$$ HI = 0.8213 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

This value falls within the AWS-recommended range of $0.5\text{–}1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$ for structural steel — acceptable.

## 3.2 Derivation: Paint Film Thickness for Constant-Velocity Painting

### Step 1 — Define volumetric flow rate

The spray nozzle delivers paint at a volumetric flow rate $Q$ in $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$. Over a time interval $\Delta t$, the volume deposited is:

$$ V_{paint} = Q \cdot \Delta t $$

### Step 2 — Define the area covered

During $\Delta t$, the robot moves the nozzle a distance $V_t \cdot \Delta t$ along the path, and the spray cone produces a fan of width $w$ on the surface. The painted area is:

$$ A_{paint} = w \cdot V_t \cdot \Delta t $$

### Step 3 — Equate volume = area × thickness

Assuming $100\%$ transfer efficiency (no over-spray loss):

$$ V_{paint} = A_{paint} \cdot t $$

$$ Q \cdot \Delta t = w \cdot V_t \cdot \Delta t \cdot t $$

### Step 4 — Solve for $t$

$$ t = \frac{Q}{w \cdot V_t} $$

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation shows that **doubling the TCP velocity halves the film thickness**. This is why robotic painting controllers use trajectory interpolation algorithms (e.g., cubic splines) to ensure that the TCP speed is uniform through corners — sudden deceleration at corners would create *thick blobs* there.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cycle Time for a Palletizing Cell

For a 14-mark KTU problem, students are often asked to compute the cycle time of a palletizing robot.

### Step 1 — Identify the four sub-tasks

A single pick-and-place cycle consists of:

1. **Pick time** $T_p$: descend to conveyor, grip, ascend.
2. **Transfer time** $T_{tr}$: traverse from conveyor to pallet (function of path length $L$ and max joint velocity $V_{max}$).
3. **Place time** $T_{pl}$: descend to pallet, release, ascend.
4. **Return time** $T_{ret}$: traverse back.

### Step 2 — Estimate transfer time using trapezoidal velocity profile

For a single joint rotating through angle $\theta$ with max velocity $\omega_{max}$ and max acceleration $\alpha_{max}$, the minimum time is:

$$ t_{move} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\alpha_{max}} + \frac{\theta}{\omega_{max}} - \frac{\omega_{max}^2}{2 \cdot \alpha_{max} \cdot \omega_{max}} $$

For the dominant link moving through the longer arc, $T_{tr} \approx T_{ret}$.

### Step 3 — Total cycle time

$$ T_c = T_p + T_{tr} + T_{pl} + T_{ret} $$

For a typical 4-axis palletizer with $V_{max} = 2 \text{ m/s}$, the cycle time is $T_c \approx 4\text{–}6 \text{ s}$ per box.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Robot for the Right Task

| Application | DoF Required | Payload | Reach | Accuracy | Sensors Needed | KTU Example |
|-------------|--------------|---------|-------|----------|----------------|-------------|
| Arc welding | 6 | 6–10 kg | 1.4–2.0 m | $\pm 0.05$ mm | Seam tracker, F/T | Automotive frames |
| Spot welding | 6 | 100–200 kg | 2.5–3.5 m | $\pm 0.1$ mm | None (open loop) | Car body assembly |
| Painting | 6 (hollow wrist) | 5–10 kg | 2.0–2.7 m | $\pm 0.2$ mm | None | Car bodies, appliances |
| Palletizing | 4–5 | 100–500 kg | 2.5–3.2 m | $\pm 0.5$ mm | Vision (optional) | FMCG, logistics |
| Assembly | 4–6 | 2–10 kg | 0.5–1.0 m | $\pm 0.02$ mm | Vision + F/T + tactile | Electronics, PCB |
| Inspection | 6 | 5–20 kg | 1.0–2.0 m | $\pm 0.05$ mm | Vision, NDT probes | Aerospace, nuclear |
| Machining | 6 (high stiffness) | 20–50 kg | 1.5–2.5 m | $\pm 0.05$ mm | F/T, vibration | Aerospace parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spot welding is one of the few industrial applications that is still performed in *open-loop* (no sensors)** because the part is rigidly clamped and the resistance-welding process is self-regulating to first order.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Advanced Robotic Welding Cell

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a sensor-integrated robotic arc-welding cell — a classic KTU 14-mark descriptive question topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Operator HMI] --> B1[Robot Controller CPU]
    A2[PLC Conveyor Logic] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Joint Servo Drivers]
    B1 --> C2[End Effector I/O]
    C1 --> D1[Six Axis Manipulator]
    C2 --> D2[Welding Torch]
    D2 --> E1[Arc and Weld Pool]
    E1 --> F1[Through Arc Sensor]
    F1 --> B1
    D2 --> E2[Seam Tracker Camera]
    E2 --> F2[Vision Processor]
    F2 --> B1
    D1 --> G1[Force Torque Sensor at Wrist]
    G1 --> F3[Compliance Controller]
    F3 --> B1
    B1 --> H1[Welding Power Source]
    H1 --> D2
    H1 --> E1
    B1 --> I1[Cloud MES Dashboard]
    I1 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **central node** is the **Robot Controller CPU** (Node `B1`), which is the brain.
- **Input arrows** come from the HMI (`A1`), PLC (`A2`), and three sensor feedback paths (`F1`, `F2`, `F3`).
- **Output arrows** drive the joint servos (`C1`) and the welding power source (`H1`).
- The **seam tracker** (`E2`) and **through-arc sensor** (`F1`) form the *closed-loop correction* chain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram of a Robotic Palletizing Work-Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Inbound Conveyor] --> B1[Vision Based Box Locator]
    B1 --> C1[Robot Controller]
    C1 --> D1[Four Axis Palletizer Arm]
    D1 --> E1[Vacuum or Claw Gripper]
    E1 --> F1[Empty Pallet Stack]
    D1 --> G1[Load Pallet Conveyor]
    C1 --> H1[Pattern Generation Algorithm]
    H1 --> C1
    C1 --> I1[Safety Light Curtain and PLC]
    I1 --> D1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Boxes enter on the inbound conveyor (`A1`) and are localized by vision (`B1`).
- The robot controller (`C1`) receives the box pose and uses the **pattern-generation algorithm** (`H1`) to decide where to place it.
- The 4-axis arm (`D1`) executes the pick-and-place with a vacuum/claw gripper (`E1`).
- The completed pallet is dispatched via the load conveyor (`G1`).
- The **safety light curtain** (`I1`) is a regulatory requirement (ISO 10218).

## 4.3 Functional Decomposition of a Robotic Assembly Cell (Peg-in-Hole)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Part Feeder Bowl] --> B1[Part Singulator]
    B1 --> C1[Escaped Part on Slide]
    C1 --> D1[Vision System Estimates Pose]
    D1 --> E1[Robot Controller Computes Delta]
    E1 --> F1[Six Axis Arm with RCC]
    F1 --> G1[Peg Aligned Above Hole]
    G1 --> H1[Active Force Search Spiral]
    H1 --> I1[Insertion Completed Signal]
    I1 --> J1[Next Station Index]
    J1 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A vibratory bowl feeder (`A1`) orients and singulates the peg (`B1`, `C1`).
- A vision system estimates the 6-DoF pose of the peg on the slide (`D1`).
- The controller computes the corrective delta-pose to align the peg with the hole (`E1`).
- The arm, equipped with an **RCC (Remote Center Compliance)** device (`F1`), descends and aligns the peg (`G1`).
- An **active force search** strategy (e.g., spiral or Lissajous search under force control) overcomes any residual misalignment (`H1`).
- Insertion success triggers indexing to the next station (`I1`, `J1`).

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Advanced Robotic Applications (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Advanced Robotic Application] --> B1{Contact or Non Contact}
    B1 --> C1[Contact Based]
    B1 --> C2[Non Contact Based]
    C1 --> D1{Force Controlled or Position Controlled}
    D1 --> E1[Force Controlled]
    D1 --> E2[Position Controlled]
    E1 --> F1[Assembly and Deburring]
    E1 --> F2[Robotic Machining]
    E2 --> F3[Spot Welding]
    E2 --> F4[Palletizing]
    C2 --> G1{Visual or Process Based}
    G1 --> H1[Vision Based]
    G1 --> H2[Process Based]
    H1 --> I1[Inspection and Painting]
    H2 --> I2[Arc Welding and Laser Cutting]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **first decision** is whether the task requires physical contact with the workpiece.
- **Contact-based** tasks are subdivided into **force-controlled** (assembly, machining) and **position-controlled** (spot welding, palletizing).
- **Non-contact** tasks are subdivided into **vision-based** (inspection, painting) and **process-based** (arc welding, laser cutting — where the process itself is the feedback source).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A has short-answer questions of 3 marks each, expected to be answered in 4–6 sentences with a diagram or formula where applicable.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define "advanced robotic application" and list any four examples with one-line justifications.**

**Model Answer:**
An *advanced robotic application* is a sensor-augmented, reprogrammable robotic work-cell in which a manipulator or mobile platform executes non-trivial tasks (welding, painting, assembly, etc.) with closed-loop control and environmental awareness, going beyond simple pick-and-place. Four examples:

1. **Robotic Arc Welding** — uses seam tracking and through-the-arc sensing to compensate thermal distortion.
2. **Robotic Painting** — uses constant-velocity path planning for uniform film thickness.
3. **Robotic Assembly** — uses force/torque sensors and RCC devices for compliant peg-in-hole insertion.
4. **Robotic Palletizing** — uses 4-axis high-payload arms with pattern-generation algorithms.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop robotic applications. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback | None | Sensors (vision, F/T, arc) |
| Accuracy | Depends on mechanics | Depends on sensor + control |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Adaptability | None | Real-time adaptation |

**Open-loop examples:** Spot welding (rigid clamping, self-regulating process); Palletizing with rigid fixtures.
**Closed-loop examples:** Arc welding with seam tracking; Peg-in-hole assembly with force control.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Describe the working principle of a robotic arc-welding cell with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of (i) seam tracking and (ii) through-the-arc sensing.

**(b) [7 marks]** A robotic MIG welding cell operates at $V = 30 \text{ V}$, $I = 250 \text{ A}$, travel speed $V_t = 5 \text{ mm/s}$, and arc efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate (i) the heat input in kJ/mm and (ii) the heat input if the travel speed is doubled while all other parameters remain the same. Comment on the metallurgical implications.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Working Principle:**

A robotic arc-welding cell consists of six functional blocks: (1) the **6-axis manipulator** carrying the welding torch, (2) the **robot controller** that interpolates trajectories, (3) the **welding power source** that delivers V and I, (4) the **seam-tracking vision system** mounted on the torch, (5) the **through-the-arc sensor** that monitors arc resistance, and (6) the **safety and interlock PLC**.

The robot moves the torch along a pre-taught path at a constant TCP velocity. Real-time corrections come from:

- **(i) Seam tracking:** A laser-stripe camera projects a line onto the joint; image processing in the vision processor extracts the lateral and vertical offset of the torch from the actual seam centreline. The controller uses a PI-control law to bias the path in real time.
- **(ii) Through-the-arc sensing:** The arc itself is the sensor. Arc voltage and current are sampled at $1\text{–}10 \text{ kHz}$; the dynamic arc resistance $R = V/I$ changes when the torch-to-workpiece distance changes. A drop in $R$ implies the torch is too close, and the controller retracts it.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat input at $V_t = 5 \text{ mm/s}$.**

Using $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{V_t \cdot 1000}$:

$$ HI_1 = \frac{0.85 \cdot 30 \cdot 250}{5 \cdot 1000} = \frac{6375}{5000} = 1.275 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

**Step 2: Heat input at $V_t = 10 \text{ mm/s}$ (doubled).**

$$ HI_2 = \frac{0.85 \cdot 30 \cdot 250}{10 \cdot 1000} = \frac{6375}{10000} = 0.6375 \text{ kJ/mm} $$

**Step 3: Ratio of the two.**

$$ \frac{HI_1}{HI_2} = \frac{1.275}{0.6375} = 2.0 $$

The heat input halves when the travel speed doubles, because heat input is **inversely proportional to travel speed**.

**Step 4: Metallurgical implications.**

- The lower $HI_2$ produces a **narrower fusion zone**, **faster cooling rate**, and **finer grain structure** — improving strength and reducing HAZ softening.
- However, $HI_2 = 0.6375 \text{ kJ/mm}$ may be **too low** to achieve full penetration on thick sections, causing **lack of fusion** defects.
- The original $HI_1 = 1.275 \text{ kJ/mm}$ is within the AWS-recommended range ($0.5\text{–}1.5 \text{ kJ/mm}$), so it is preferable for this thickness.
- The student should conclude that **doubling travel speed is not always metallurgically beneficial** — it must be balanced with required penetration and joint strength.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of force/torque sensing in robotic assembly. With a neat sketch, describe the working of a Remote Center Compliance (RCC) device for peg-in-hole insertion.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 6-axis robot performs a peg-in-hole insertion. The peg has a chamfer of $1 \text{ mm}$ and the hole has a clearance of $0.2 \text{ mm}$. The initial lateral misalignment is $1.5 \text{ mm}$. (i) Is the insertion possible without active force control? Justify. (ii) If active force control is used, derive the search radius and number of spiral search steps required to locate the hole.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Role of F/T Sensor and RCC:**

In robotic assembly, the **force/torque (F/T) sensor** mounted at the robot's wrist measures the contact forces and moments during part mating. The controller uses this data in an **admittance/impedance control** loop to lower the robot's effective stiffness along the insertion axis, so that small misalignments produce compliant motions rather than jamming forces.

A **Remote Center Compliance (RCC) device** is a passive mechanical element installed between the robot flange and the gripper. It is designed so that all compliant motions pivot about a *remote center* located at the **tip of the peg** (not at the wrist). When the peg contacts the chamfer of the hole, the RCC allows:

- **Lateral translation** $\Delta x, \Delta y$ at the peg tip.
- **Rotation** $\Delta \theta$ about the peg tip.
- **Stiff axial insertion** along the peg axis (to transmit insertion force).

This decoupling prevents **wedging** and **jamming** — the two classic failure modes of rigid peg-in-hole insertion.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Determine the misalignment regime.**

The peg chamfer is $c = 1 \text{ mm}$. The hole clearance is $\delta = 0.2 \text{ mm}$. The initial lateral misalignment is $e_0 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$.

For passive (RCC-only) insertion, the geometric condition for success is:

$$ e_0 \leq c - \delta $$

**Step 2: Evaluate the condition.**

$$ c - \delta = 1.0 - 0.2 = 0.8 \text{ mm} $$

Since $e_0 = 1.5 \text{ mm} > 0.8 \text{ mm}$, **passive insertion is NOT possible**. Active force control (e.g., spiral search) is required.

**Step 3: Compute the required search radius.**

The spiral search must cover a circle of radius equal to the *residual misalignment* that the chamfer cannot absorb:

$$ R_{search} = e_0 - (c - \delta) $$

$$ R_{search} = 1.5 - 0.8 = 0.7 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 4: Compute the number of spiral steps.**

The peg diameter is typically $D_{peg}$. For a peg of $D_{peg} = 10 \text{ mm}$ and a step size of $0.1 \text{ mm}$ (typical for force-search control), the circumference of the search circle is:

$$ C_{search} = 2 \pi R_{search} = 2 \pi \cdot 0.7 = 4.398 \text{ mm} $$

Number of steps:

$$ N_{steps} = \frac{C_{search}}{\Delta s} = \frac{4.398}{0.1} = 44 \text{ steps} $$

At $50 \text{ ms}$ per step, this takes $44 \cdot 0.05 = 2.2 \text{ s}$ — acceptable for most production cycles.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

- Passive RCC insertion **fails** because $e_0 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$ exceeds the chamfer-clearance budget of $0.8 \text{ mm}$.
- Active spiral search with a radius of $0.7 \text{ mm}$ and $\sim 44$ steps is required, taking $\sim 2.2 \text{ s}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **In heat-input problems, students often forget to divide by 1000** to convert watts × seconds into kilojoules. This single omission costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **In peg-in-hole problems, students confuse the chamfer with the clearance.** Chamfer $c$ *helps* insertion; clearance $\delta$ *hurts* it. The correct condition is $e_0 \leq c - \delta$, not $e_0 \leq c + \delta$.
> 3. **In palletizing, students often forget to include the return time** in the cycle-time equation. The KTU answer key explicitly checks for $T_c = T_p + T_{tr} + T_{pl} + T_{ret}$.
> 4. **In painting problems, students sometimes invert the velocity formula.** Recall: $t = Q / (V_t \cdot w)$ — *higher* velocity gives *thinner* film.
> 5. **In block-diagram questions, students draw physical layouts instead of functional blocks.** KTU wants *functional* block diagrams showing the controller as the central node, not a picture of a robot arm.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Advanced robotic application = sensor-augmented, reprogrammable, closed-loop work-cell beyond pick-and-place.
- **Six functional clusters:** welding, painting, machining, assembly, palletizing, inspection.
- **Heat input formula:** $HI = \dfrac{\eta V I}{V_t \cdot 1000}$ in kJ/mm. AWS range: $0.5\text{–}1.5$ kJ/mm for structural steel.
- **Paint film thickness:** $t = \dfrac{Q}{V_t \cdot w}$; constant TCP velocity is critical.
- **Peg-in-hole condition for passive insertion:** $e_0 \leq c - \delta$. Active search required if violated.
- **Pallet stability score:** $S = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{h}{H_{max}} + \beta \cdot \dfrac{I_{friction}}{I_{max}} + \gamma \cdot \dfrac{d_{cg}}{d_{base}}$, with $\alpha+\beta+\gamma = 1$.
- **Spot welding is open-loop; arc welding is closed-loop.** This is a high-yield one-liner.
- **Cycle time formula:** $T_c = T_p + T_{tr} + T_{pl} + T_{ret}$.
- **Industry 4.0 integration:** Modern cells must expose data to MES/IoT layers (KPI: OEE $> 85\%$).
- **Standards to quote:** ISO 10218 (robot safety), ISO/TS 15066 (cobot safety), ISO 9283 (performance).
- **Cobots** (collaborative robots) operate at $\leq 1 \text{ m/s}$ TCP speed and forces $\leq 150 \text{ N}$ (quasi-static contact limit).
- **AGVs/AMRs** are the *mobile* counterpart of stationary manipulators — KTU often asks for the comparison in 3-mark questions.
- **Vision-guided robotics:** 2D vision suffices for pick-and-place on flat conveyors; 3D vision is required for bin-picking and unstructured scenes.
- **Force-controlled machining** uses the **effective stiffness** $K_{eff}$ of the admittance model — reduce $K_{eff}$ to allow compliant cutting.

> **End of Module 4 — Advanced Robotic Applications | KTU 2024 Scheme**

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collaborative Robot (Cobot):** A *collaborative robot* (per **ISO/TS 15066:2016** and **ISO 10218-1/-2:2011**) is a robotic manipulator explicitly engineered to perform tasks in a **shared, fenceless workspace** with a human operator, where both agent and human can perform productive motions within the same operational volume at the same time, under defined safety constraints.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for OERUT724, a cobot is a robotic system that intentionally merges the **dexterity, repeatability, and endurance of an industrial robot** with the **cognitive flexibility, dexterity, and decision-making capability of a human worker**, subject to a documented risk assessment and validated safety control system.

### Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning in Cobot Context |
|---|---|
| **Collaborative Operation** | A mode in which a purposely-designed robot works in direct cooperation with a human within a defined workspace. |
| **Collaborative Workspace** | The 3-D space within the *operating space* where the robot and human may perform tasks concurrently. |
| **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** | The inherent design or control method where the robot's mechanical and control systems limit the contact forces to biomechanically safe thresholds. |
| **Separation Distance** | The spatial gap between any moving part of the robot and the closest body part of the operator, used for speed-separation monitoring. |
| **Fenceless Cell** | A robotic workstation that does not require physical guarding/cage because safety is achieved through control means. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Smart Apprentice":** Imagine an apprentice mechanic working shoulder-to-shoulder with a senior mechanic on the same car engine. The apprentice (the *cobot*) holds the heavy torque wrench steady and rotates bolts on command, while the senior mechanic (the *human*) gives instructions, makes judgement calls, and inserts smaller parts. If the apprentice accidentally bumps the senior's hand, it instantly **releases grip and stops** — because it has **soft padded skin**, **smart torque sensors**, and **a built-in reflex**. A traditional industrial robot, by contrast, is a *powerful but blind machine* that must be locked behind a cage — touching it during operation would be catastrophic.

In simple words: a **cobot = industrial robot + sensitive skin + smart brakes + a polite co-worker rulebook**. It doesn't replace the human; it amplifies what the human can do.

### Intuitive Comparison: Industrial Robot vs. Cobot

| Property | Traditional Industrial Robot | Collaborative Robot (Cobot) |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | Caged, isolated | Shared with humans |
| Payload | Up to ~1000 kg | Typically up to ~35 kg |
| Speed | High (m/s range) | Deliberately slower (limited for safety) |
| Sensors | Position encoders only | Force/torque, vision, tactile, proximity |
| Stop behaviour | E-stop only | Active collision detection + safe stop |
| Risk reduction | Physical guarding | Control-based safety functions |
| Cost & footprint | High | Moderate, redeployable |

---

## 1.3 Governing Standards & Physical Benchmarks (Bold Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **biomechanical thresholds** below are taken from **ISO/TS 15066:2016, Annex A**. They represent the *maximum permissible force/pressure* a cobot may exert on a specific human body region without causing pain or injury (quasi-static contact).

- **Maximum Quasi-Static Contact Force on Hand/Finger:** **Fm ≤ 140 N** (clamping configuration)
- **Maximum Quasi-Static Pressure on Hand:** **Pm ≤ 3.0 × 10⁵ N/m² (≈ 3 bar)**
- **Maximum Transient Contact Force:** typically **≤ 80 N** for the hand (in free transient contact)
- **Reduced speed for collaborative mode:** ≤ **250 mm/s** (typical convention for hand-guiding entry)
- **Minimum safety-rated reduced speed:** **0 mm/s** (safety-rated monitored stop)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *legal ceilings*. A well-designed cobot usually operates with a large **safety factor** (typically 2–5×) below these thresholds to account for sensor noise, calibration drift, and worst-case impacts.

---

## 1.4 Why Cobots Matter for KTU / Industry 4.0

The motivation for studying cobots in OERUT724 Module 4 stems from three converging trends:

1. **Mass customization** in assembly lines (e.g., automotive final assembly, electronics) demands flexibility that hard automation cannot deliver.
2. **Skilled-labour shortage** in manufacturing makes human–robot *collaboration* (not replacement) the economical optimum.
3. **Affordable sensing (torque, vision, AI)** has finally matured enough to make fenceless safety *engineered*, not hoped for.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Collaborative Operation (ISO/TS 15066)

The standard defines **four distinct collaborative operation modes**. Every cobot application must declare which mode(s) it uses. In the KTU exam, you must be able to **name, define, and contrast** these four.

### Pillar 1 — Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)

- **Definition:** The robot operates *without* the human in the collaborative workspace. When the human enters, the robot performs a **safety-rated monitored stop** (a Category 1 or 2 stop per IEC 60204-1).
- **Logic:**
  1. Sensors detect human entry → robot decelerates with monitored deceleration.
  2. Once stopped, drive power may remain active but motion is *prevented*.
  3. When human exits → robot may resume automatically after a safety-rated restart interlock.
- **Use case:** Machines that load/unload parts while a human inspects a nearby fixture.

### Pillar 2 — Hand Guiding (HG)

- **Definition:** The human *physically grasps* the robot's end-effector (or a handle attached to it) and manually leads the robot through a desired trajectory.
- **Logic:**
  1. Force/torque sensor at the wrist measures the operator's pull/push.
  2. Admittance or impedance controller converts force → velocity.
  3. Robot moves only as long as force is applied, and at a *speed-limited* rate.
- **Use case:** Teaching-by-showing paths, heavy part positioning, ergonomic assist for repetitive lift tasks.

### Pillar 3 — Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)

- **Definition:** The robot's speed is **continuously adjusted** such that the separation distance *S* between robot and human is always greater than a *protective* distance computed from the robot's stopping time.
- **Logic:**
  1. A safety-rated vision/proximity system tracks the human.
  2. As the human approaches, the robot slows down or stops.
  3. If the human is far enough, robot may operate at full rated speed.
- **Use case:** Large workspaces where humans only occasionally enter the robot area.

### Pillar 4 — Power and Force Limiting (PFL)

- **Definition:** The robot's *intrinsic* mechanical design (compliant joints, rounded edges, lightweight links) **and** its control system **limit** the quasi-static and transient contact forces/pressures to values at or below the biomechanical thresholds in ISO/TS 15066.
- **Logic:**
  1. Force/torque sensors at each joint detect abnormal contact.
  2. Controller reacts within milliseconds (typically **≤ 50 ms**).
  3. Robot either stops or reverses motion, and force never exceeds *Fm*.
- **Use case:** Direct physical hand-over tasks, e.g., small-parts assembly, screw-driving, polishing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which collaborative mode is used when a human and robot work simultaneously with no barrier?", the answer is **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** or **SSM** — but *only PFL* allows **intentional physical contact**.

---

## 2.2 The Cobot's Functional Architecture

A cobot is a *layered cyber-physical system*. From top (highest abstraction) to bottom:

1. **Task Planner Layer** — receives high-level commands (e.g., "pick gear, insert into housing").
2. **Collaborative Mode Supervisor** — enforces which Pillar (1–4) is active based on the current *risk state*.
3. **Trajectory & Speed Planner** — generates joint trajectories satisfying the **separation-distance inequality** (when in SSM).
4. **Compliance / Impedance Controller** — converts desired position to motor torques using joint elasticity.
5. **Safety Control Subsystem** — hard-wired, safety-rated (e.g., PLC with PROFIsafe or FSoE), cannot be bypassed by software.
6. **Sensor Stack:**
   - **Joint torque sensors** (one per axis, e.g., Universal Robots' patented torque pickups)
   - **Wrist F/T sensor** (6-axis, e.g., ATI Mini45)
   - **2D / 3D vision cameras** (e.g., Intel RealSense, Basler, Keyence)
   - **Capacitive / e-skin tactile sensors** (e.g., Tekscan, XELA Robotics)
   - **Proximity / LiDAR scanners** (e.g., SICK microScan3, Hokuyo)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects every high-yield equation you may be asked to apply or derive in the KTU 2024 ESE / internal exams for this topic. **Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Equation / Concept | LaTeX Form | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stopping distance of robot after a stop signal | $S_{\text{stop}} = v_0 \, t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v_0^{\,2}}{2\,a_{\max}}$ | Distance covered while the robot decelerates; needed for SSM |
| 2 | Minimum protective separation distance (ISO 13855-inspired) | $S_{\text{min}} = v_{\text{human}} \, T_{\text{react}} + S_{\text{robot stop}} + C$ | Minimum allowed gap between robot and human |
| 3 | Quasi-static contact force (clamping) | $F_{\text{clamp}} \le F_{m,\text{body region}}$ | Must be ≤ ISO/TS 15066 thresholds |
| 4 | Contact pressure (Hertz-like, simplified) | $P_{c} = \dfrac{F_{c}}{A_{\text{contact}}}$ | Pressure must be ≤ $P_m$ for that body region |
| 5 | Maximum permitted transient force on the hand | $F_{\text{transient,hand}} \le 80~\text{N}$ (free contact) | Free (non-clamping) contact limit |
| 6 | Hand-guiding velocity cap | $v_{\text{HG}} \le 250~\text{mm/s}$ | Recommended ergonomic speed limit |
| 7 | Power-and-force-limited robot max payload | $m_{\text{load}} \le \dfrac{F_{m}}{g\,N_{\text{safety}}}$ | Empirical design rule for safe payload |
| 8 | Static joint torque for payload | $\tau_{\text{payload}} = m\,g\,L_{\text{eff}}$ | Used to size motors vs. safe-torque limit |
| 9 | Reaction time of safety controller | $t_{\text{react}} \le 50~\text{ms}$ | Industry-standard safety-rated stop time |
| 10 | Stopping time | $t_{\text{stop}} = t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v_0}{a_{\max}}$ | Used to compute $S_{\text{stop}}$ |

> **Symbols used:**
> $v_0$ = initial velocity; $a_{\max}$ = maximum deceleration; $t_{\text{react}}$ = reaction time of safety system; $T_{\text{react}}$ = full-system reaction (incl. sensor); $C$ = intrusion/operator reach constant; $L_{\text{eff}}$ = effective moment arm; $N_{\text{safety}}$ = safety factor.

---

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Separation-distance inequality (Eq. 2)** is *the* legal backbone of SSM cobot cells; without it, no cobot installation can pass a CE risk assessment in the EU or an OSHA evaluation in the US.
- **Biomechanical limits (Eq. 3–5)** convert abstract "safety" into a **numeric spec** the control engineer can write into PLC code.
- **The 250 mm/s hand-guiding limit (Eq. 6)** is a *de facto* ergonomic threshold above which human reaction time cannot guarantee safe recovery.
- **The 50 ms reaction time (Eq. 9)** sets the *bandwidth requirement* on the safety bus (PROFIsafe, FSoE, CIP Safety). Anything slower fails certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a KTU Part B question, the most common numerical mistakes arise from:
> 1. Forgetting to **add the human's reach constant C** to the separation equation.
> 2. Using **g = 9.81** vs. **g = 9.80665** — be consistent.
> 3. Mixing up **quasi-static** vs. **transient** force thresholds (140 N vs. 80 N for the hand).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three flagship derivations** are worked out below in full. These are the *highest-probability* derivation-style questions for the KTU ESE Module 4.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stopping Distance of a Cobot under PFL

### Statement
When a cobot operating at constant velocity $v_0$ along a straight-line path detects a contact force, its safety controller triggers a stop after a reaction delay $t_{\text{react}}$. Derive the *total distance* travelled by the end-effector from the moment of contact detection until full halt, given a constant maximum deceleration $a_{\max}$.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Decompose the motion into two phases.**

The motion of the cobot after a stop trigger has two phases:
- **Phase A — Reaction phase:** The cobot is unaware; it continues at the same velocity $v_0$ for the duration of the safety system's reaction time $t_{\text{react}}$.
- **Phase B — Braking phase:** The brakes engage, applying a constant deceleration $a_{\max}$ until velocity becomes zero.

**Step 2: Distance covered in the reaction phase (Phase A).**

Since velocity is constant, distance is simply velocity × time:

$$
S_{A} = v_{0}\,t_{\text{react}}
$$

**Step 3: Distance covered in the braking phase (Phase B).**

Use the kinematic equation $v_{f}^{2} = v_{i}^{2} - 2\,a_{\max}\,S_{B}$, where $v_f = 0$:

$$
0 = v_{0}^{2} - 2\,a_{\max}\,S_{B}
$$

Solving for $S_B$:

$$
S_{B} = \dfrac{v_{0}^{\,2}}{2\,a_{\max}}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* The kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} m v_0^2$ is dissipated by the brake work $F_{\text{brake}} \, S_B = m\,a_{\max}\,S_B$. Equating the two gives the same expression.

**Step 4: Total stopping distance.**

Adding both phases:

$$
S_{\text{stop}} = S_{A} + S_{B}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S_{\text{stop}} = v_{0}\,t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v_{0}^{\,2}}{2\,a_{\max}}\,}
$$

**Step 5: Sanity check — order-of-magnitude check.**

Take $v_0 = 1000$ mm/s = 1 m/s, $t_{\text{react}} = 0.05$ s, $a_{\max} = 5$ m/s²:

$$
S_{\text{stop}} = 1 \times 0.05 + \dfrac{1^{2}}{2 \times 5} = 0.05 + 0.1 = 0.15~\text{m} = 150~\text{mm}
$$

This is realistic for a 6-axis cobot; in practice the *protective separation distance* is set to ≥ 150 mm plus the human reach constant.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Protective Separation Distance (SSM)

### Statement
In Speed and Separation Monitoring mode, derive the minimum allowed separation $S_{\min}$ between the closest point of the robot and the closest point of the human operator, in terms of human approach speed, system reaction time, robot stopping distance, and operator reach constant.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Visualize the worst case.**

The human is moving *toward* the cobot at speed $v_h$. The robot and human are closing the gap. We require that the gap *never* becomes zero.

**Step 2: Account for the human's motion during the robot's reaction time $T_{\text{react}}$.**

During $T_{\text{react}}$, the robot cannot yet respond, but the human keeps closing in:

$$
\Delta_{1} = v_{h}\,T_{\text{react}}
$$

**Step 3: Account for the robot's stopping distance.**

After the reaction time elapses, the robot decelerates over its stopping distance $S_{\text{stop}}$. During this interval, the human *could* still be advancing — but per ISO 13855 the standard formulation freezes the human at the start of the robot's braking phase (worst case):

$$
\Delta_{2} = S_{\text{stop}} = v_{0}\,t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v_{0}^{\,2}}{2\,a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 4: Add the operator reach/instrument constant $C$.**

This constant accounts for uncertainty in the sensor (e.g., the operator's hand might already be 100 mm closer than the camera reports):

$$
\Delta_{3} = C
$$

**Step 5: Sum all contributions.**

$$
S_{\min} = \Delta_{1} + \Delta_{2} + \Delta_{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S_{\min} = v_{h}\,T_{\text{react}} + v_{0}\,t_{\text{react}} + \dfrac{v_{0}^{\,2}}{2\,a_{\max}} + C\,}
$$

**Step 6: Worked numerical example.**

Suppose $v_h = 1.6$ m/s (typical brisk walking speed), $T_{\text{react}} = 0.1$ s, $v_0 = 0.5$ m/s, $t_{\text{react}} = 0.05$ s, $a_{\max} = 2.5$ m/s², $C = 0.1$ m.

$$
S_{\min} = 1.6 \times 0.1 + 0.5 \times 0.05 + \dfrac{(0.5)^2}{2 \times 2.5} + 0.1
$$

$$
S_{\min} = 0.16 + 0.025 + 0.05 + 0.1 = 0.335~\text{m} \approx 335~\text{mm}
$$

So the cobot must *guarantee* at least 335 mm of gap; if the human gets closer, the cobot must brake or stop.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quasi-Static Clamping Force vs. Payload

### Statement
A cobot picks up a payload mass $m$ from a fixture and inserts it into a tight-tolerance assembly. During insertion, a clamping-type contact can occur (e.g., the part wedges between the fixture and the gripper). Derive the worst-case quasi-static clamping force as a function of payload mass, the gripper insertion force, and the moment arm. Compare it to the ISO/TS 15066 limit.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1: Force balance at the clamped interface.**

If the part gets jammed during insertion, the cobot's drive force $F_{\text{drive}}$ is transmitted directly to the human if the human's body is in the clamping path. By Newton's third law:

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = F_{\text{drive}} + m\,g
$$

(We conservatively include the weight of the part as contributing to the clamping load.)

**Step 2: Translate drive force into joint torque.**

The drive force arises from the cobot's joint motor torques. For the worst-case end-effector insertion, with moment arm $L_{\text{eff}}$ between the joint and the contact point:

$$
F_{\text{drive}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{motor}}}{L_{\text{eff}}}
$$

**Step 3: Compose total clamping force.**

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{motor}}}{L_{\text{eff}}} + m\,g
$$

**Step 4: Apply the ISO/TS 15066 safety inequality.**

To stay within the biomechanical limit:

$$
\dfrac{\tau_{\text{motor}}}{L_{\text{eff}}} + m\,g \le F_{m,\text{region}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tau_{\text{motor}} \le L_{\text{eff}}\,(F_{m,\text{region}} - m\,g)\,}
$$

This is the **maximum allowable motor torque** at that configuration. Above this torque, the cobot is *not* safe for that body region.

**Step 5: Numerical illustration.**

Suppose $F_{m,\text{hand}} = 140$ N, $L_{\text{eff}} = 0.2$ m, $m = 2$ kg.

$$
\tau_{\max} \le 0.2 \times (140 - 2 \times 9.81) = 0.2 \times 120.38 = 24.08~\text{N·m}
$$

So this cobot's wrist controller must limit its torque output to **24.08 N·m** whenever the hand-region contact is plausible. If the body region is the *chest* ($F_m = 210$ N), the limit becomes:

$$
\tau_{\max} \le 0.2 \times (210 - 19.62) = 38.08~\text{N·m}
$$

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| Scenario | Pillar | Dominant Equation | Critical Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human walks through the workspace occasionally | SSM | $S_{\min}$ (Derivation 2) | Camera latency |
| Operator hand-guides the cobot | HG | $v_{\text{HG}} \le 250$ mm/s | Force sensor range |
| Direct contact during assembly | PFL | $F_{\text{clamp}} \le F_m$ (Derivation 3) | Joint torque limit |
| Operator inspects while robot idles | SRMS | Stop time ≤ 50 ms | Brake reaction |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the safeguard rules, all node IDs are purely alphanumeric (prefixed with letters), and labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes — no bold/italic/HTML tags inside labels.

---

## 4.1 Cobot System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HMI[A HMI PANEL TASK INPUT] --> TPL[B TASK PLANNER LAYER]
    TPL --> CMS[C COLLABORATIVE MODE SUPERVISOR]
    CMS --> TSB[D TRAJECTORY AND SPEED PLANNER]
    TSB --> ICC[E IMPEDANCE AND COMPLIANCE CONTROLLER]
    ICC --> SAF[F SAFETY CONTROL SUBSYSTEM PLC]
    SAF --> ACT[G MOTOR DRIVES AND BRAKES]
    ACT --> ROB[H COBOT MANIPULATOR 6 AXIS]
    CMS --> VIS[I VISION AND PROXIMITY SENSORS]
    CMS --> FTS[J FORCE TORQUE SENSOR WRIST]
    SAF --> KSK[K E STOP AND SAFETY INPUTS]
    VIS --> SAF
    FTS --> SAF
    KSK --> SAF
    SAF -. SAFE STOP .-> ACT
    CMS -. MODE FLAG .-> TSB
```

**Reading the diagram (top to bottom):**
- **HMI** receives operator commands.
- **TPL** decomposes a high-level goal ("assemble gear onto shaft") into discrete waypoints.
- **CMS** decides whether the cobot is in SRMS, HG, SSM, or PFL mode.
- **TSB** generates a smooth trajectory that satisfies $S_{\min}$.
- **ICC** implements the joint impedance controller (the *key* software that gives cobots their "soft" feel).
- **SAF** is the certified safety PLC — it can override **ICC** at any time.
- **ACT** drives the six joint motors and applies brakes.
- **VIS / FTS / KSK** are the sensory and human-input channels feeding the safety subsystem.

---

## 4.2 SSM Decision Flow (Speed and Separation Monitoring)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[A READ HUMAN POSITION FROM VISION] --> S2{B IS SEPARATION S LESS THAN SMIN}
    S2 -- NO --> S3[C ROBOT AT FULL PROGRAMMED SPEED]
    S2 -- YES --> S4[D REDUCE SPEED PROPORTIONALLY]
    S4 --> S5{E IS S LESS THAN ZERO}
    S5 -- NO --> S1
    S5 -- YES --> S6[F TRIGGER SAFETY RATED STOP]
    S3 --> S1
```

**Interpretation:**
- The vision system continuously streams the human's position.
- The controller computes $S$ in real time and compares to the **dynamically computed** $S_{\min}$ (which itself depends on $v_0$).
- If $S \ge S_{\min}$ → robot runs at full programmed speed.
- If $S < S_{\min}$ → speed is reduced proportionally.
- If $S < 0$ (i.e., contact) → hard safety-rated stop is triggered within $\le 50$ ms.

---

## 4.3 PFL Force-Limiting Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[A MEASURED JOINT TORQUES TAU MEAS] --> P2{B ANY TAU EXCEEDS THRESHOLD TAU LIM}
    P2 -- NO --> P3[C CONTINUE NORMAL MOTION]
    P2 -- YES --> P4[D CLASSIFY CONTACT QUASI STATIC OR TRANSIENT]
    P4 --> P5{E QUASI STATIC FORCE LE FM}
    P5 -- NO --> P3
    P5 -- YES --> P6[F INITIATE COMPLIANT REFLEX STOP OR REVERSE]
    P6 --> P7[G ROBOT HALTS WITHIN 50 MS]
    P3 --> P1
```

**Interpretation:**
- Every joint is monitored *every cycle* (typically 1 kHz).
- If torque crosses $\tau_{\lim}$ (computed from Derivation 3), the contact is classified.
- If the resulting clamped force still exceeds $F_m$ for the relevant body region, the cobot performs a **compliant reflex** (stop or reverse) in <50 ms.
- This is the *heart* of PFL cobots like Universal Robots UR5e, FANUC CRX, and ABB GoFa.

---

## 4.4 Collaborative Workspace Topology (ASCII Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CZ[A RED ZONE HARD STOP]
      Z1[ZONE A ROBOT FULL SPEED FORBIDDEN]
    end
    subgraph YZ[B YELLOW ZONE REDUCED SPEED]
      Z2[ZONE B SPEED PROPORTIONAL TO SEPARATION]
    end
    subgraph GZ[C GREEN ZONE FREE OPERATION]
      Z3[ZONE C HUMAN MAY WORK COLLABORATIVELY]
    end
    CZ --> YZ
    YZ --> GZ
```

**Reading the zones:**
- **Green Zone (GZ):** Separation $S > S_{\min,\text{free}}$ → full collaborative operation (PFL or HG allowed).
- **Yellow Zone (YZ):** Separation $S_{\min,\text{reduce}} \le S \le S_{\min,\text{free}}$ → speed reduced.
- **Red Zone (CZ):** Separation $S < S_{\min,\text{reduce}}$ → safety-rated stop, no collaborative motion.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question tags simulate KTU past-year papers. Mark distribution follows the 2024 scheme: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks, internal choice)**, with sub-parts at **7 + 7**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Collaborative Robot (Cobot) as per ISO/TS 15066. List the four modes of collaborative operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Collaborative Robot (Cobot)** is a robot designed for **direct interaction with a human operator within a defined collaborative workspace**, without traditional safety fencing, while maintaining safety through control-based methods as defined in **ISO/TS 15066:2016** and **ISO 10218-1/-2:2011**. (1.5 marks)

The four modes of collaborative operation are:
1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — robot stops when human enters.
2. **Hand Guiding (HG)** — human physically leads the robot.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — speed varies with separation.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — robot limits contact forces below biomechanical thresholds. (1.5 marks — 0.375 per mode)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State any three differences between a traditional industrial robot and a cobot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Workspace:** Industrial robots require **caged, fenced cells**; cobots operate in **fenceless shared workspaces** with humans. (1 mark)
2. **Sensing:** Industrial robots rely on **position encoders** only; cobots integrate **force/torque sensors, vision, and tactile sensors** for safe collaboration. (1 mark)
3. **Payload and Speed:** Industrial robots carry **high payloads (up to ~1000 kg) at high speeds**; cobots carry **moderate payloads (≤35 kg) at limited speeds** (e.g., ≤250 mm/s in hand-guiding) for safety. (1 mark)

---
